incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Museum of History
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Museum of History's posting for a Manager, Accounting Services encourages applications based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation and sex, and declares the museum subscribes to the federal employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: A job posting from the Canadian Museum of History for a Manager, Accounting Services position includes language encouraging applications based on race, skin colour, disability, sexual orientation, and sex. The posting states: "We encourage applications from all qualified individuals, including candidates from all cultures, racialized communities, all abilities, all sexual identities and genders, and anyone else who can contribute to the diversity of ideas." The posting also explicitly states that "The Canadian Museum of History subscribes to the Employment Equity Act," tying the hiring process to the federal government's employment equity program, which categorizes workers by designated group membership. Rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals on the basis of their qualifications, experience, and abilities, the museum's posting signals that demographic characteristics — skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, sexual orientation, and sex — are factors in its hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a public institution signals that it values candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, or sexual orientation, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as unique persons based on their character and abilities. Candidates who belong to the named categories are invited to consider their demographic traits as relevant to their application, while those who do not belong are implicitly told the institution views applicants partly through a lens of group identity. This practice undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual and that advancement should reflect merit and individual circumstance alone. The framing encourages people to see themselves and others as members of competing demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals sharing a common workplace and civic life.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Capital Commission
incidents.short_description: The National Capital Commission's job posting for a Director of Audit, Research, Evaluation and Ethics encourages applicants to voluntarily indicate if they belong to categories defined by sex, ethnicity, or disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Capital Commission posted an indeterminate, full-time position for a Director, Audit, Research, Evaluation and Ethics (AREE) and Chief Audit Executive (CAE), classified at the RE-08 level ($154,301 to $170,710 per annum) and based in Ottawa, Ontario. The posting closes June 24, 2026. As part of its hiring process, the NCC states it promotes employment equity and encourages candidates to voluntarily disclose in their application whether they are a woman, an indigenous person, a person with a disability, or a member of a visible minority group. By inviting candidates to sort themselves into demographic categories — defined by sex, ethnicity, and disability — the NCC signals that these characteristics are relevant to the hiring process, not merely a candidate's qualifications, experience, or individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution asks job seekers to identify themselves by sex, ethnicity, or disability, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as a distinct individual with unique skills and character. A qualified candidate who does not belong to a preferred category is disadvantaged not because of any shortcoming of their own, but because of who they are by birth or circumstance. Equally, a candidate who does fall into a preferred category risks being advanced for reasons unrelated to their individual abilities. The practice normalizes the idea that demographic characteristics are a legitimate factor in assessing a person's suitability for a role, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be seen and judged on their own merits. Repeated across the federal government in nearly every posting, this pattern entrenches a system where Canadians are routinely sorted and ranked by immutable traits, eroding the shared civic space in which every person is treated as an individual of singular worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's posting for a Data Analyst, Regulatory Reporting role strongly encourages candidates to self-identify as members of federal 'employment equity' designated groups, categorising applicants based on ethnicity, disability, and sex.
incidents.description_of_event: The Bank of Canada posted a two-year term position for a Data Analyst, Regulatory Reporting (official title: Financial Data Analyst) in Ottawa, with a closing date of June 23, 2026. The posting includes an 'Equity, Diversity & Inclusion' section that frames workforce composition in terms of demographic categories, stating that the Bank is committed to a workforce that reflects diversity across race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, and sexual orientation. Beyond this broad statement, the posting explicitly invites all candidates to self-identify if they belong to what the Bank calls an 'Employment Equity designated group,' listing the specific categories as indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, and racialized/visible minorities. The self-identification request is presented as a strong encouragement, with the posting stating the Bank 'strongly encourage[s] candidates to self-identify.'
incidents.expected_harm: By asking candidates to declare whether they belong to categories defined by ethnicity, disability, or sex, the Bank of Canada reduces applicants to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their skills, experience, and character. A candidate who self-identifies as belonging to one of the listed groups will be assessed partly through the lens of that demographic category, not solely on what they bring as a person. This practice signals to applicants that their immutable traits are relevant data points in a hiring process, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be recognised for their unique qualities and abilities. Repeating this approach across virtually every posting normalises the idea that demographic category membership is a legitimate factor in employment decisions, entrenching institutional categorisation of individuals and working against the shared civic belonging the Coalition believes hiring should foster.

quotes.text: We invite you to join an organization where differences are seen as strengths and are recognized, valued and respected. We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities).
quotes.rationale: This is the core self-identification encouragement tied directly to federal 'employment equity' designated groups, enumerating indigenous peoples (ethnicity), persons with disabilities (disability), women (sex), and racialized/visible minorities (race or ethnicity). This matches trigger pattern 2 (self-declaration tied to designated groups) and trigger pattern 1 (EE encouragement boilerplate).

quotes.text: The Bank is committed to achieving a workforce that reflects the diversity of our country and is representative and inclusive of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, socio-economic background or lived experience. By living our values and prioritizing our actions to enhance equity, diversity and inclusion, the Bank ensures broader discussions, better decisions, and a more engaging workplace.
quotes.rationale: This is the Bank's 'Equity, Diversity & Inclusion' policy statement enumerating demographic categories including race, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, reflecting trigger pattern 4 (reference to a diversity/equity policy framing).

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's posting for a Senior Quantitative Analyst (Financial Markets) role encourages applicants to self-identify based on their ethnicity, sex, disability status, and other traits under the federal 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Bank of Canada posted a job advertisement for a Senior Quantitative Analyst (Financial Markets) position, open to applicants in Ottawa or Calgary. Within the posting, under the heading 'Equity, Diversity & Inclusion,' the Bank states it is committed to a workforce representative of Canadians "regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, socio-economic background or lived experience." The posting goes further and actively solicits candidates to self-identify if they belong to a federal 'employment equity' designated group — specifically naming "Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities." Candidates are "strongly encouraged" to self-identify membership in these categories as part of the application process. This is the standard language the Bank of Canada attaches to its postings, and its consistent use across postings is the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: By asking candidates to label themselves according to skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability, the Bank of Canada reduces applicants to demographic categories at the very first step of the hiring process, before any individual assessment of their character, abilities, or qualifications has occurred. A candidate's ethnicity, sex, or disability status is irrelevant to their capacity to build quantitative financial models or collaborate with financial market specialists, yet the Bank formally invites applicants to declare these traits and signals that doing so is relevant to their candidacy. This practice teaches both applicants and hiring staff to view people through demographic lenses rather than as distinct individuals. The cumulative effect — replicated across many federal postings — is the normalisation of sorting Canadians by immutable traits as a routine step in government hiring, undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation based on their own demonstrated abilities and merit.

quotes.text: The Bank is committed to achieving a workforce that reflects the diversity of our country and is representative and inclusive of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, socio-economic background or lived experience. quotes.rationale: Policy statement enumerating identity categories (race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression) as the basis for the Bank's workforce representation commitment — references the institution's 'equity, diversity and inclusion' framing.

This is the primary qualifying language for the incident.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada is recruiting Principal Analysts for its Consumer-Driven Banking team and invites candidates to self-identify as members of federal 'employment equity' designated groups, categorising applicants by ethnicity, disability, and sex.
incidents.description_of_event: The Bank of Canada posted a job listing for Principal Analysts in Data Security and Technical Standards within its Consumer-Driven Banking division, based in Ottawa or Toronto. The posting includes an Equity, Diversity and Inclusion section that invites all candidates to self-identify if they belong to one of the federal government's 'Employment Equity designated groups,' which it names as Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, and racialized or visible minorities. The posting states: "We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities)." The Bank frames this invitation under a commitment to building a workforce that "reflects the diversity of our country," directing candidates to label themselves by ethnicity, disability status, and sex as part of the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: Asking candidates to label themselves by ethnicity, disability, and sex in a hiring process reduces individual applicants to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their skills, experience, and character. A person's ethnicity, whether they have a disability, and their sex are irrelevant to their ability to carry out technical and analytical work. When the Bank of Canada formally solicits this information from applicants, it embeds demographic sorting into the recruitment process, signalling that a candidate's immutable traits form part of the basis on which they will be assessed or selected. This undermines the principle that each person deserves to be seen and judged as an individual. It also places candidates in a position where disclosing personal characteristics they may consider private feels necessary to compete fairly, eroding their autonomy. Over time, institutionalising this kind of demographic categorisation normalises the idea that people are best understood through group labels rather than as distinct individuals with unique abilities and contributions.

Repeated across nearly every Bank of Canada posting, it normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories and embeds group-based thinking into the foundation of the federal employment relationship.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's posting for a Senior Economist - Model Development role encourages candidates to self-identify based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and other traits under the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Bank of Canada posted a job for a Senior Economist in its Model Development Division in Ottawa, closing June 15, 2026. The posting includes a dedicated 'Equity, Diversity & Inclusion' section that frames workforce goals in terms of demographic representation. Most notably, the posting states: "We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities)." This is a direct invitation for applicants to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability — categories grouped under the federal government's 'employment equity' program — as part of the application process. The posting also states that the Bank is "committed to achieving a workforce that reflects the diversity of our country and is representative and inclusive of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression," situating hiring decisions within a framework of demographic group representation rather than individual qualification alone.
incidents.expected_harm: When applicants are asked to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, or disability as part of a job application, the hiring institution reduces people to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Candidates are prompted to define themselves by immutable traits, and those traits are then made visible to the institution at the point of application. This process treats a person's skin colour, ethnicity, sex, or disability as relevant information in a hiring context where it has no bearing on anyone's ability to develop macroeconomic models or conduct economic research. Every applicant deserves to be assessed on their own merits — their education, their analytical skills, their experience — rather than on the demographic groups they belong to. The practice also signals to all candidates that group membership is a factor in how applications are received, which undermines confidence in the integrity of the process and the singular worth of each individual who competes for the role.

quotes.text: We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities).
quotes.rationale: This is the core self-identification/EE encouragement trigger. It explicitly names the four employment equity designated groups (women → sex; indigenous peoples → race_or_ethnicity; persons with disabilities → disability; racialized/visible minorities → race_or_ethnicity) and invites applicants to label themselves by these traits.

incidents.short_description: The University of Guelph's job posting for an Assistant Professor position explicitly encourages applications from people based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting treats demographic group membership as a relevant factor in hiring rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications and individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Guelph posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Marketing and Consumer Studies on June 15, 2026. The posting outlines qualifications related to research, teaching, and expertise in artificial intelligence and business. However, the posting also explicitly states that the university 'strongly welcome[s] and encourage[s] applications from members of designated groups in employment, including Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, women, and 2SLGBTQIA+ persons.' This language identifies specific demographic categories and signals that membership in these groups is a consideration in the hiring process. The posting frames this approach as part of the university's commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion, and references its strategic plan's goal of recruiting a 'diverse student, faculty and staff body.' By singling out applicants based on skin colour, disability, sex, and sexual orientation as preferred groups, the university treats demographic characteristics as relevant hiring criteria alongside academic qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed in part based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and potential contributions. Candidates who do not belong to the named demographic groups receive less encouragement to apply and face a process in which their demographic characteristics work against them. This reduces the university's ability to identify and appoint the most capable individual for the role, and sends a message to all prospective applicants that their demographic identity is a factor in how their application will be received. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people by immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits.

The posting includes an equity, diversity and inclusion statement asserting that the Bank is committed to a workforce that reflects the diversity of Canada and is representative of all Canadians across a range of demographic characteristics. The posting then explicitly invites candidates to self-identify as members of an employment equity designated group, naming the four categories as Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, and racialized or visible minorities. The request to self-identify is framed as encouragement, but it expressly singles out candidates based on ethnicity, disability, and sex as categories of special interest to the hiring institution. This is a standard pattern applied broadly across Bank of Canada job postings.
incidents.expected_harm: When a hiring institution asks candidates to declare their ethnicity, disability status, or sex as part of the application process, it signals that those demographic characteristics are relevant to hiring decisions. This reduces each applicant from a distinct individual — with their own skills, experience, and character — to a representative of a demographic category. Candidates who belong to the named groups are identified and tracked not for what they can uniquely contribute, but for what demographic box they fill. Candidates who do not belong to those groups receive a clear signal that the institution views some applicants through a demographic lens, which undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their individual merits. The practice normalises the sorting of people by immutable traits at the very point of entry into public institutions.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's posting for a Security Solutions Architect invites candidates to self-identify as members of federal 'employment equity' designated groups — categorising applicants by ethnicity, disability, and sex — and frames demographic representation as a core institutional commitment.
incidents.description_of_event: The Bank of Canada posted a Security Solutions Architect position (term until December 31, 2028, closing June 23, 2026) and included two identity-categorisation elements. First, under an 'Equity, Diversity & Inclusion' heading, the Bank states it is committed to a workforce that 'reflects the diversity of our country' and is 'representative and inclusive of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression.' Second, the posting explicitly invites candidates to self-identify if they belong to an 'Employment Equity designated group,' naming those groups as 'Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities.' This amounts to an institutional request that applicants label themselves by ethnicity, sex, and disability status as part of the hiring process, under the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.expected_harm: Asking candidates to label themselves by ethnicity, sex, and disability reduces each person to a set of demographic categories rather than recognising them as an individual with unique abilities, experience, and character. When an employer structures hiring around demographic representation — and invites self-identification on that basis — the message sent to every applicant is that group membership is a relevant qualification. Candidates who belong to listed categories face the implicit pressure of being evaluated partly as representatives of a group rather than as individuals. Candidates who do not belong to those categories receive the message that their demographic profile counts against them. This undermines the principle that each person possesses singular worth and deserves to be assessed on their own merits, and it normalises the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable traits rather than engaging with them as distinct human beings.

quotes.text: The Bank is committed to achieving a workforce that reflects the diversity of our country and is representative and inclusive of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, socio-economic background or lived experience. incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's posting for a Senior Media Relations Specialist invites applicants to self-identify based on ethnicity, disability, sex, and other demographic categories under the federal 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Bank of Canada posted a permanent position for a Senior Media Relations Specialist, Regulatory Mandates, based in Ottawa. The posting then explicitly invites candidates to self-identify if they belong to what the Bank calls an 'Employment Equity designated group,' listing indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, and racialized/visible minorities as the named categories. Candidates are 'strongly encouraged' to self-identify in this way. The posting frames categorizing applicants by these demographic traits as a strength and as part of how the Bank evaluates and recruits its workforce.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates applying to the Bank of Canada are asked to label themselves by ethnicity, disability status, and sex rather than being considered solely on the basis of their qualifications, experience, and individual character. When an institution collects and acts on demographic self-identification during recruitment, it evaluates people at least in part as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals. This reduces the candidate to a set of immutable traits and introduces group membership as a factor in hiring — exactly the kind of categorization the Coalition holds should have no place in evaluating a person's worth or suitability. The practice sends applicants the message that their demographic background is a relevant credential, which undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits and unique qualities, not on the group they happen to belong to.

The posting states: "We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities)." This self-identification request sorts job applicants into categories based on sex, disability, ethnicity, and skin colour as part of the Bank's hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution asks job applicants to identify themselves by sex, disability, ethnicity, and skin colour, it reduces individuals to demographic labels rather than evaluating them on their abilities, experience, and character. Each person who applies for this role is a distinct individual whose value cannot be captured by membership in any demographic group. Asking applicants to declare which group they belong to signals that the institution views candidates partly through the lens of their immutable traits, not solely on what they know and what they can do. This practice undermines the principle that every person deserves to be recognised for their unique qualities. It creates a hiring environment where demographic category membership becomes a relevant factor alongside individual merit, which works against the equal dignity owed to every applicant.

incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's posting for a Senior Operational Resiliency Analyst strongly encourages candidates to self-identify as members of the federal government's 'employment equity' designated groups, categorizing applicants by sex, ethnicity, and disability.

Within the posting's 'Equity, Diversity & Inclusion' section, the Bank states it is 'committed to achieving a workforce that reflects the diversity of our country' and explicitly invites candidates to self-identify if they belong to what the federal government calls 'Employment Equity designated groups' — namely indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, and racialized or visible minorities. The posting frames this self-identification as a welcomed and encouraged step in the application process, asking candidates to sort themselves into demographic categories defined by sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.expected_harm: When the Bank of Canada asks candidates to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status, it reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their character, skills, and experience. A person applying for a technically demanding role in operational resilience is assessed not solely on their analytical ability or professional record, but also on which demographic box they occupy. This practice signals to all candidates — those who self-identify and those who do not — that their demographic category is a relevant factor in hiring decisions. It normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable traits, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be seen and evaluated as a distinct individual. Over time, this framing teaches both applicants and hiring managers to view the workforce through a lens of group membership rather than individual worth.

It also pressures candidates to disclose personal characteristics that should be irrelevant to whether they are suited for a technical role involving SharePoint and Microsoft 365 collaboration tools. The cumulative effect, reproduced across thousands of federal postings, normalises the institutional habit of sorting people by demographic proxies rather than treating each applicant as a distinct individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Senate of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Senate of Canada posted a full-stack developer position that encourages applicants to identify as members of so-called 'designated groups' under the federal government's employment equity program, sorting candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The Senate of Canada posted a job competition for a Developer (full-stack) position in its Information Services Directorate, closing July 5, 2026. The posting includes a statement declaring that the Senate 'promotes employment equity' and actively encourages applicants 'to indicate if you belong to one of the designated groups when you apply.' This language — drawn from the federal government's employment equity framework — invites candidates to label themselves based on demographic characteristics including sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting applies this standard encouragement to self-identify based on these traits as part of the application process, regardless of individual qualifications or circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: When a hiring institution asks candidates to declare their membership in groups defined by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, it reduces people to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. Applicants are prompted to present themselves through the lens of immutable traits, and the institution signals that these characteristics are relevant to the selection process. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their character, abilities, and demonstrated experience. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories are treated as less deserving of encouragement to apply, and all candidates are led to understand that their demographic identity is a factor alongside their professional qualifications. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by demographic category and erodes the expectation that hiring decisions will rest solely on what a person can do and who they are as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Capital Commission
incidents.short_description: The National Capital Commission encourages applicants for a Branch Services Manager role to identify themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status, treating demographic categories as relevant to the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Capital Commission (NCC) posted a job for a Branch Services Manager (RE-05, $77,281–$102,535) in Ottawa, closing June 19, 2026. The posting includes a standard statement in which the NCC declares it promotes its federal "employment equity" program and asks candidates to voluntarily indicate in their application whether they are a woman, an indigenous person, a person with a disability, or a member of a visible minority group. The NCC frames this as building a "skilled, diverse workforce reflective of Canadian society." By inviting candidates to self-identify based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, the posting treats these demographic characteristics as relevant factors in the hiring process, rather than evaluating applicants solely on their qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer asks job seekers to identify themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability, it treats applicants as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. A person's sex, ethnic background, or disability status has no bearing on their ability to manage administrative services, analyze financial data, or liaise with procurement functions — yet the posting signals that these traits are considered relevant. Applicants who share their demographic details will be assessed in part through the lens of group membership. This reduces each person's singular worth to a label, undermining the principle that hiring should reflect individual character, ability, and demonstrated merit. It also sends a message to applicants from all backgrounds that demographic sorting is a normal and acceptable part of public service hiring.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Capital Commission
incidents.short_description: The National Capital Commission is hiring a Branch Services Administrator and encourages candidates to declare their sex, ethnicity, and disability status, sorting applicants into demographic categories as part of its hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Capital Commission posted a job for a Branch Services Administrator in Ottawa, closing June 19, 2026. In the posting, the NCC states it "promotes employment equity and encourages candidates to indicate voluntarily in their application if they are a woman, an Indigenous person, a person with a disability or a member of a visible minority group." This language invites applicants to self-identify based on sex, ethnicity, and disability — categories drawn from the federal government's employment equity program — and factors demographic membership into the hiring process alongside individual qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: By inviting applicants to declare their sex, ethnicity, and disability status, the NCC treats demographic categories as relevant hiring criteria. Candidates are nudged to present themselves not simply as individuals with relevant skills and experience but as representatives of particular demographic groups. This reduces people to immutable characteristics rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and the individual merits they bring to the role. Those who do not belong to a named category, or who decline to self-identify, are implicitly disadvantaged relative to those who do. The practice teaches applicants that federal institutions regard demographic traits as hiring factors, normalising the sorting of individuals into categories and eroding the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own qualities alone.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Capital Commission
incidents.short_description: The National Capital Commission's job posting for a Project Management Office Assistant encourages candidates to voluntarily identify based on sex, ethnicity, and disability as part of the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Capital Commission posted a one-year term position for a Project Management Office Assistant based in Ottawa, Ontario. The posting states that the NCC "promotes employment equity and encourages candidates to indicate voluntarily in their application if they are a woman, an Indigenous person, a person with a disability or a member of a visible minority group." This is the standard encouragement language used under the federal government's employment equity program, which sorts candidates into demographic categories based on sex, ethnicity, and disability. The posting invites self-identification along these lines as part of the application process, treating membership in demographic groups as a relevant factor in hiring.
incidents.expected_harm: By asking candidates to identify themselves based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, the National Capital Commission treats applicants as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals to be evaluated on their character, abilities, and experience. This categorization reduces a person's identity to immutable traits and introduces demographic proxies into a process that should assess each applicant solely on what they bring as an individual. Candidates who do self-identify are sorted into group categories that can influence hiring decisions, meaning the outcome of their application is shaped in part by traits they were born with rather than by their demonstrated qualifications. This undermines the principle that every person possesses singular worth and deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's job posting for a Specialist, Employee Relations encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, reducing candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a job for a Specialist, Employee Relations (Job Requisition ID: 12103), a temporary full-time position open across multiple cities in Canada. The posting includes an employment equity encouragement statement under the heading 'Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion,' which reads: 'We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply.' This statement explicitly sorts prospective candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability status, religion, and sexual orientation, inviting applications on the basis of those demographic traits rather than solely on the basis of each applicant's individual qualifications, character, and abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: By publicly signalling that candidates are encouraged to apply based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, CMHC reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as a distinct individual with unique skills and experience. This practice embeds the assumption that a person's immutable traits are relevant to their suitability for employment, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. When an employer signals preference based on demographic categories, it also sends a message to applicants who do not belong to those categories that they are less welcome, fragmenting the workforce along identity lines rather than uniting it around shared purpose and common humanity.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada
incidents.short_description: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada's posting for a PM-01 Client Support Centre Agent states that hiring preference may be given to applicants based on their ethnicity, disability, and sex, and asks applicants to self-declare their membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) posted a PM-01 Client Support Centre Agent position (Selection Process Number 2025-IMC-EA-53628) based in Montreal. Under the heading 'Organizational Needs,' the posting states that "preference may be given to under-represented Employment Equity Groups" and lists four categories: indigenous people, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, and women. Applicants who identify under any of these categories are asked to complete a self-declaration section in their application. The posting also links to IRCC's anti-racism commitments and references the federal government's 'employment equity' program throughout. This means candidates are assessed not only on their qualifications, education, and behavioural competencies, but also on their ethnicity, disability status, and sex.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer signals that hiring preference will follow from a person's ethnicity, disability, or sex, it reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. Applicants who do not belong to the preferred categories are disadvantaged not because of any shortcoming in their qualifications or conduct, but solely because of who they are by birth or circumstance. This undermines the principle that every person possesses singular worth as an individual. It also pressures applicants to label and declare themselves by immutable traits in order to compete on equal footing, treating identity disclosure as a transactional step in a hiring process rather than a private matter. The cumulative effect, repeated across virtually every federal posting, normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic groups and making consequential decisions about their careers on that basis.

incidents.short_description: Humber Polytechnic's job posting for a Vice-President role states that it actively seeks candidates from specific demographic groups, evaluating applicants based on their group identity rather than solely on individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Humber Polytechnic posted a job listing on June 12, 2026 for a Vice-President, Strategic Growth, Productivity & Innovation. The posting states that the institution 'actively seeks Indigenous Peoples and individuals from equity-deserving groups with demonstrated skills and knowledge to deal with all aspects of equity, diversity and inclusion.' This language signals that candidates are sorted and preferred based on their demographic identity — including their ancestry and membership in designated group categories — rather than being evaluated solely as individuals on the basis of their qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities. The posting explicitly frames demographic group membership as a factor in recruitment, directing the institution's active recruitment efforts toward certain people based on their immutable characteristics.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution signals it is actively seeking candidates based on demographic group membership, individual applicants are evaluated at least in part through the lens of their identity rather than their unique qualifications and abilities. This reduces people to demographic proxies, undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as an individual, and discourages qualified candidates who do not belong to the named groups from applying with full confidence that they will be judged on merit alone. It normalises institutional sorting of people by identity categories, entrenching the habit of viewing individuals primarily through demographic labels rather than as distinct persons with their own capabilities and circumstances.

incidents.short_description: First Nations University of Canada posted a senior leadership position that gives hiring preference to applicants based on their Indigenous status, explicitly invoking a human rights code exemption to do so.
incidents.description_of_event: First Nations University of Canada (FNUniv), a federated college of the University of Regina with campuses across Saskatchewan, posted a job listing on June 12, 2026 for the role of Vice President, University Relations. The posting explicitly states that the university relies on section 56 of The Saskatchewan Human Rights Code to give preference to people based on their Indigenous status when filling this position. Applicants are directed to indicate their status in their cover letter or resume. The preference applies to a senior executive role responsible for external relations, brand management, communications, student recruitment, and fundraising. The job posting thus formally and institutionally sorts applicants into categories based on ancestry and ethnicity, making this a determining factor in who is considered for the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are screened and ranked based on their ancestry and ethnic background rather than solely on their individual character, abilities, and demonstrated experience. This practice reduces people to demographic categories at the point of hiring, denying individuals the opportunity to be evaluated purely on their merits. It normalises institutional decision-making based on a person's background, entrenching the habit of sorting people by ancestry in the workplace. Individuals who do not identify as Indigenous are placed at a formal disadvantage regardless of how well-qualified they may be, and all applicants receive the message that their ethnic identity is a material factor in how institutions assess their worth.

incidents.institution_name: King's University College at Western University
incidents.short_description: King's University College posted a faculty position that gives preferential treatment to applicants based on demographic characteristics, including ethnicity and disability, and requires candidates to explain how they will advance equity, diversity, inclusion, and decolonization as part of the application process.
incidents.description_of_event: King's University College at Western University posted a 2-year limited-term Assistant Professor position in the Department of Childhood and Youth Studies on June 12, 2026. The application requires candidates to include in their cover letter an explanation of how they will contribute to equity, diversity, inclusion, and decolonization. Beyond this ideological commitment requirement, the posting states that the college specifically welcomes applications from 'persons of all genders and sexual orientations, persons with disabilities, Indigenous persons, and racialized people,' signalling preferential consideration for candidates based on their demographic characteristics. Rather than evaluating applicants purely on their scholarly qualifications, teaching record, and research expertise, the college has built demographic and ideological criteria into the formal application process. All qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, but Canadian citizens and permanent residents are given priority, which is a standard practice the Coalition does not consider problematic.
incidents.expected_harm: Requiring applicants to demonstrate a commitment to a particular ideological framework as a condition of employment restricts who can realistically compete for the position and pressures academics to profess views that may not reflect their actual beliefs. Candidates who do not embrace this framework, regardless of their academic qualifications, are effectively disadvantaged. Additionally, signalling preference for applicants based on demographic characteristics means that some individuals are evaluated not solely on their abilities and scholarly merit but on immutable traits, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as an individual. This sends a message to prospective applicants that their demographic background is a relevant qualification, reducing people to group membership rather than recognizing them as unique individuals.

incidents.short_description: Université Laval posted a faculty position in which it explicitly operates an equal opportunity employment program that gives preferential hiring treatment to candidates based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability. The posting treats demographic group membership as a factor in employment decisions rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications and abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 12, 2026, Université Laval posted a tenure-track faculty position in the Department of Biology for a role in cell biology, molecular biology, or genetics. The posting states that the university operates an equal opportunity employment program for women, members of visible or ethnic minorities, indigenous peoples, and people with disabilities. The posting goes further by explicitly encouraging applications from people belonging to a visible minority, women, first nations peoples, people with disabilities, ethnic minorities, members of the 2SLGBTQIA+ community, and others categorized by demographic characteristics. This approach means the university formally categorizes job applicants by demographic group and uses those categories as a factor in hiring decisions, rather than evaluating candidates purely on their scholarly qualifications, research potential, and teaching abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and character. This means qualified applicants who do not belong to the designated groups face a disadvantage in the selection process that has nothing to do with their merits. It also reduces every applicant to a set of demographic labels, undermining their recognition as distinct individuals. The institutional practice normalizes sorting people by group membership as a legitimate tool in employment, eroding the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own terms.

incidents.short_description: Université Laval's biology department job posting for a professor position explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation, and subscribes to an employment equity programme that grants preferential access based on these characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: Université Laval posted a tenure-track professor position in its Biology Department on June 12, 2026, covering molecular biology, cellular biology, or genetics. The posting's eligibility and selection language includes a section encouraging applications specifically from people categorised by their skin colour or visible minority status, sex, indigenous identity, disability, ethnicity, and membership in 2ELGBTQIA+ communities. Beyond encouragement, the university states it subscribes to an employment equity programme that formally applies preferential treatment in hiring for women, visible or ethnic minority members, indigenous persons, and people with disabilities. The posting thus moves beyond welcoming all qualified candidates to instituting a structured system in which demographic categories shape hiring outcomes. The position's substantive selection criteria — a doctorate in a relevant field, a record of peer-reviewed publications, demonstrated teaching ability, and capacity to communicate in French — are otherwise based on professional qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and ranked in part on the basis of immutable personal characteristics such as skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation rather than solely on their character, abilities, and demonstrated professional qualifications. Qualified individuals who do not belong to the designated demographic groups face a structurally disadvantaged competition for a public academic position. This institutionalises the practice of sorting people into demographic categories and awarding or withholding opportunity on that basis, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as a distinct individual. It also signals to all prospective applicants — and to current students and faculty — that the university views people primarily through demographic lenses rather than as individuals with unique capabilities and contributions.

incidents.institution_name: Université de Moncton, Campus de Moncton
incidents.short_description: The Université de Moncton is soliciting demographic self-identification from job applicants — including information about sex, disability, indigenous status, skin colour, and sexual orientation — and explicitly using this information as a factor in hiring decisions for a faculty position in the Faculty of Education.
incidents.description_of_event: The Université de Moncton, Campus de Moncton advertised a three-year temporary assistant professor position in career-life counselling within the Faculty of Education. As part of the application process, candidates are invited to complete a voluntary self-identification form that collects information about their sex, disability status, indigenous identity, skin colour (framed as 'visible minority' membership), and sexual orientation (via LGBTQ2+ identification). The job posting states that the university encourages applications from indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, men, gender minorities, members of LGBTQ2+ communities, and members of racialised communities. The self-identification form states directly that the responses will be used both for statistical purposes and 'pour la prise en considération de votre candidature à des fins de recrutement' — that is, as a consideration in evaluating candidates for hiring. Candidates are asked to submit the completed form alongside their application materials. This means demographic characteristics — skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, indigenous status, and disability — are formally built into the candidate evaluation process, rather than hiring being based solely on qualifications, research record, teaching ability, and professional experience.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated not only on their individual merits, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities, but also on demographic characteristics such as skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and indigenous identity. This reduces individuals to group membership categories, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own character, abilities, and experience. Qualified candidates who do not belong to preferred demographic groups face a structural disadvantage regardless of the strength of their application. The practice entrenches the habit of sorting people into demographic categories as a normal part of institutional decision-making, normalizing the idea that immutable traits are relevant to professional advancement. It also pressures applicants to disclose deeply personal information about themselves — their identity, their body, their family background — in order to compete on equal footing, eroding individual privacy and autonomy.

quotes.text: L'Universit\'e de Moncton souscrit à l'équité en matière d'emploi et encourage les candidatures de toutes personnes qualifiées : Autochtones, personnes en situation de handicap, femmes, hommes, personnes membres de minorités de genre, des communautés LGBTQ2+ ou de minorités racialisées.
quotes.rationale: The posting explicitly lists demographic categories — including skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and indigenous identity — as bases on which the university encourages and distinguishes candidates, establishing the demographic preference framework.

quotes.text: 10) Le formulaire d'auto-identification relatif à l'équité en matière d'emploi, le cas échéant.
quotes.rationale: The self-identification form is a required submission item in the application package, showing the process is formally integrated into candidate evaluation rather than being purely voluntary and separate.

quotes.text: Les réponses que vous inscrirez sur ce questionnaire demeureront confidentielles et seront utilisées tant à des fins de statistiques que pour la prise en considération de votre candidature à des fins de recrutement.
quotes.rationale: This is the most direct confirmation that demographic self-identification data is used as a factor in assessing candidates for hiring, not merely for aggregate statistics. This establishes that group membership influences individual hiring outcomes.

quotes.text: Les membres des minorités visibles sont des personnes autres que les Autochtones, qui ne sont pas de race blanche ou qui n'ont pas la peau blanche, quel que soit leur lieu de naissance.
quotes.rationale: The form explicitly categorizes candidates by skin colour as a hiring consideration, directly reducing individuals to a demographic proxy rather than evaluating their individual qualifications.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Correctional Service Canada
incidents.short_description: Correctional Service Canada posted a heating and cooling mechanic position at Port-Cartier, QC, that invites applicants to self-identify as members of groups defined by disability, ethnicity, and other traits, and lists targeted support programs organized around those same demographic categories.
incidents.description_of_event: Correctional Service Canada posted a job for a heating and cooling mechanic (with handyman/woman duties) at its Port-Cartier, QC location on June 11, 2026. The posting includes an 'Employment groups' section stating that the employer promotes equal employment opportunities for all applicants 'including those self-identifying as a member of these groups,' and that applicants who self-identify as a member of any employment group are encouraged to indicate it in their application. The posting then lists structured support programs broken down by demographic category: persons with disabilities, Veterans, indigenous people (described by the posting using ethnicity as a category), and people categorised by skin colour as 'visible minorities.' Each category is accompanied by a list of targeted initiatives — mentorship, coaching, networking, awareness training, and targeted hiring — that treat applicants differently based on these traits. The posting thereby formally invites applicants to declare their demographic category and signals that membership in those categories is a relevant consideration in how the employer engages with them.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer formally asks applicants to declare their skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, or other immutable traits, it reduces each person to a demographic label rather than evaluating them as an individual with unique abilities and character. Applicants are sorted into categories at the very first point of contact with the employer, signalling that their demographic profile — not their skills or individual merit — shapes how they will be treated. This categorisation entrenches the habit of seeing people as members of groups first and individuals second, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on what they can do and who they are. It also creates a two-tiered hiring environment where those who declare membership in a favoured category receive structured institutional advantages, while those who do not are implicitly treated as the unmarked default — a division that fragments the workforce along demographic lines rather than building a common workplace grounded in shared contribution and individual worth.

quotes.text: This employer promotes equal employment opportunities for all job applicants, including those self-identifying as a member of these groups:
quotes.rationale: Core self-identification trigger: the employer explicitly invites applicants to self-identify as members of designated demographic groups, which is one of the enumerated qualifying patterns.

quotes.text: If you self-identify as a member of any employment group, you are encouraged to indicate it in your application.
quotes.rationale: Explicit self-declaration request tied to the federal 'employment equity' program groups — a direct trigger for the self-identification pattern.

quotes.text:
Support for Indigenous people

Develops and maintains relationships with indigenous communities, indigenous-owned businesses and organizations
Provides cultural competency training and/or awareness training to all employees to create a welcoming work environment for Indigenous workers
Facilitates access to Elders who can offer support and guidance to Indigenous workers
quotes.rationale: Lists targeted employer supports organized around ethnicity (indigenous status), a designated employment equity group.

Applies hiring policies that discourage discrimination against members of visible minorities (for example: anonymizing the hiring process, etc.)

Provides diversity and cross-cultural training to create a welcoming work environment for members of visible minorities
quotes.rationale: Lists targeted supports organized around skin colour/ethnicity ('visible minorities'), a designated employment equity group.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Correctional Service Canada
incidents.short_description: Correctional Service Canada posted a plumber position in Donnacona, QC, and explicitly invites applicants to self-identify as members of demographic groups — including based on ethnicity, disability, and skin colour — as part of its hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: Correctional Service Canada posted a plumber vacancy (Job Bank #3598091) in Donnacona, QC on June 11, 2026. The posting includes an 'Employment groups' section stating that the employer 'promotes equal employment opportunities for all job applicants, including those self-identifying as a member of these groups.' It then lists targeted support programs organized by demographic category: persons with disabilities, Veterans, indigenous people, and visible minorities. Candidates are told: 'If you self-identify as a member of any employment group, you are encouraged to indicate it in your application.' The posting thereby invites applicants to label themselves by disability status, ethnicity, and skin colour as part of submitting a job application to a federal government employer.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer invites applicants to label themselves by ethnicity, skin colour, and disability status, it signals that those immutable traits are relevant to the hiring process. Each applicant who self-identifies is reduced to a demographic category rather than being evaluated purely on their skills, experience, character, and demonstrated needs. This practice conditions both applicants and hiring managers to see people as representatives of demographic groups first and individuals second. Over time it normalises the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable traits, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits.

quotes.rationale: Core self-identification trigger: employer explicitly frames the employment groups section around self-identification by group membership.

quotes.rationale: Direct self-declaration invitation: candidates are explicitly encouraged to self-identify their demographic group membership in the application, the clearest trigger for this incident type.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Correctional Service Canada
incidents.short_description: Correctional Service Canada posted a plumber position at Port-Cartier, QC, and invited applicants to self-identify as members of demographic categories — including ethnicity, disability, and others — as part of its hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: Correctional Service Canada posted a plumber vacancy (Job Bank #3598093) on June 11, 2026, located in Port-Cartier, QC. The posting includes an 'Employment groups' section stating that the employer promotes equal employment opportunities 'including those self-identifying as a member of these groups,' and encourages applicants to indicate their membership in one of those groups in their application. The groups enumerated are: persons with disabilities, veterans, indigenous people (categorised by ethnicity), and visible minorities (categorised by skin colour and ethnicity). The posting further lists specific initiatives targeted at each of these demographic categories, such as mentorship programs for people based on skin colour, cultural competency training for indigenous workers, and targeted hiring initiatives for veterans. Applicants are explicitly invited to disclose which demographic category they belong to as part of the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: By inviting applicants to declare which demographic category they belong to, the employer reduces individuals to labels based on immutable traits — skin colour, ethnicity, and disability — rather than evaluating each person on their character, abilities, and individual circumstances. A candidate who discloses their demographic group will be assessed, at least in part, through that demographic lens rather than purely on merit. This categorisation treats people as representatives of groups rather than as distinct individuals, undermining each person's singular worth. The practice normalises the idea that immutable characteristics are relevant qualifications for employment, which erodes the principle that advancement should reflect individual capability and demonstrated need alone.

quotes.text: This employer promotes equal employment opportunities for all job applicants, including those self-identifying as a member of these groups:
quotes.rationale: Core self-identification trigger: the employer explicitly frames the employment groups section around applicants self-identifying as members of demographic categories, which is one of the enumerated qualifying patterns.

quotes.text: If you self-identify as a member of any employment group, you are encouraged to indicate it in your application.
quotes.rationale: Explicit invitation to self-declare demographic group membership as part of the application — directly matches the self-declaration trigger pattern.

incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba's Department of French, Spanish and Italian posted a faculty position that explicitly invites applications based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting asks candidates to disclose personal identity characteristics as part of the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba's Department of French, Spanish and Italian posted a two-year term Instructor I position in Spanish, with a posting date of June 11, 2026. The job posting states that the department 'particularly invite[s] application from' candidates based on demographic categories including indigenous peoples, women, persons based on skin colour, persons with disabilities, and persons of particular sexual orientations and gender identities. The posting further encourages candidates to 'self-identify aspects of your identity' that position them to bring 'under-represented viewpoints' to the role. Additionally, the teaching dossier requirement asks applicants to include a statement about 'contributions or potential contribution to advancing a culture of equity and inclusion,' effectively making ideological alignment with a specific institutional framework a formal part of the application. The University's broader institutional statement reinforces this approach by framing certain demographic groups as having been 'excluded from full participation' and committing to preferential consideration in hiring and promotion for those groups.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated not solely on their teaching ability, scholarly qualifications, and individual merit, but also on their demographic characteristics and willingness to self-disclose personal identity traits. This reduces individuals to their demographic categories rather than recognising them as distinct persons with unique abilities and experience. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic groups, or who are unwilling to frame themselves in those terms, face a disadvantage unrelated to their fitness for the role. The requirement to declare ideological commitments as part of the teaching dossier further narrows who can meaningfully compete for the position, excluding those who may disagree with or feel uncomfortable subscribing to a particular institutional framework. This practice normalises the sorting of job applicants by immutable characteristics and signals to the broader academic community that demographic identity is a legitimate criterion for employment decisions.

incidents.institution_name: Wilfrid Laurier University – Lazaridis School of Business & Economics
incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University's Lazaridis School of Business & Economics posted a lecturer hiring advertisement that invites applicants from designated demographic groups and requires candidates from those groups to self-identify to be considered for employment equity. The posting signals that demographic category membership is a factor in the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 11, 2026, Wilfrid Laurier University's Lazaridis School of Business & Economics posted two one-year limited term lecturer positions in Finance on the University Affairs jobs board. The posting states that the university is committed to increasing the diversity of faculty and staff and welcomes applicants from members of what it calls 'equity-deserving groups.' It directs candidates from these groups to self-identify in confidence to a named administrative officer in order to be considered for employment equity. The posting also provides separate contact points for indigenous candidates and candidates from other designated groups to learn about equity programming. The language of the posting makes membership in specific demographic categories a relevant factor in the hiring process, alongside professional qualifications, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their individual qualifications, teaching experience, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a university signals that demographic group membership is a factor in hiring, applicants are evaluated in part on the basis of immutable traits rather than purely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and character. Candidates who do not belong to designated groups are disadvantaged relative to those who do, regardless of their individual merit. This practice normalizes the institutional sorting of people into demographic categories and conditions faculty hiring on identity rather than demonstrated excellence, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Simon Fraser University – School of Criminology
incidents.short_description: Simon Fraser University's School of Criminology posted a term lecturer position that explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories including sex, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and disability status. The posting signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in the hiring process rather than evaluating candidates purely on their qualifications and abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 11, 2026, Simon Fraser University's School of Criminology posted a job advertisement for a Term Lecturer position in Criminology on University Affairs. The posting is for a 0.75 FTE, eight-month appointment beginning September 1, 2026. While outlining standard academic qualifications — a Ph.D. in Criminology or a related discipline, teaching effectiveness, and relevant subject expertise — the posting also states that the university "strongly encourages applications from all qualified individuals including women, Indigenous Peoples, racialized persons, people of all sexual orientations and gender identities, persons with disabilities and others who may contribute to the further diversification of the university." The posting frames demographic characteristics as relevant factors in the hiring process. Candidates are also asked to demonstrate experience with "inclusive teaching and mentoring" and to discuss how they would "meet the needs of equity-deserving students," embedding demographic considerations further into the assessment criteria.
incidents.expected_harm: When a post-secondary institution signals that demographic characteristics — including sex, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and disability status — are relevant to hiring decisions, it reduces candidates to their demographic profile rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their abilities, qualifications, and character. Applicants who do not belong to the listed demographic groups receive the message that they are less welcome or less competitive regardless of their merit. This practice normalises the sorting of people into demographic categories as a basis for institutional decisions, undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation on their own individual qualities.

incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary posted an academic position in which it explicitly encouraged applications from people based on demographic characteristics including sex, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting signals that demographic identity is a factor in the hiring process rather than individual merit and qualifications alone.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Calgary posted a job advertisement on June 11, 2026 for an Assistant Professor position in Stroke Neurology within the Cumming School of Medicine. The posting includes a standard equity statement that explicitly names demographic categories — women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities, and people of diverse sexual orientation and gender identities — and singles them out as especially encouraged to apply. The posting frames these demographic categories under the heading of "equity-deserving groups" and states the university is committed to removing barriers "historically encountered by some people in our society." By explicitly listing demographic characteristics and stating a preference for applicants who belong to them, the university signals that demographic identity is a relevant consideration in recruitment, rather than limiting selection to individual qualifications, research accomplishments, and demonstrated abilities. The posting also states that the university will "support their academic and professional success while they are here," suggesting differentiated institutional treatment based on demographic group membership rather than individual need or circumstance.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated in part on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. Candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic categories receive a weaker institutional signal of welcome and may reasonably understand themselves to be at a disadvantage. This normalises the institutional practice of sorting job applicants into demographic categories and making hiring decisions influenced by those categories, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed as an individual. It also entrenches the idea that institutions owe differentiated support to people based on group membership rather than individual circumstances, eroding a shared standard of merit and dignity that applies equally to all candidates.

incidents.short_description: Thompson Rivers University restricted applications for a tenure-track Social Work professor position exclusively to candidates of certain skin colours and ethnicities, barring all others from consideration. This is a hiring restriction based on immutable personal characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: Thompson Rivers University's School of Social Work and Human Service posted a tenure-track Assistant or Associate Professor position in the Bachelor of Social Work program, designated exclusively for candidates who identify as Black, indigenous, or racialized — a category the posting defines as people socially categorised as non-white. The posting explicitly states the position is restricted to these applicants and requires candidates to self-declare their identity in application materials, including a narrative of engagement with communities defined by skin colour and ethnicity. All other candidates, regardless of their qualifications, teaching experience, or scholarly expertise, are ineligible to apply. The restriction is framed as correcting historic underrepresentation and as part of the university's commitment to decolonisation and reconciliation. The posting was published on 10 June 2026 with a closing date of 23 June 2026.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from a publicly advertised academic position solely on the basis of their skin colour or ethnicity. This practice reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and professional achievements. It normalises the institutional sorting of people by immutable traits and entrenches the view that a person's value and suitability for a role is determined by their demographic background. Candidates who do not identify within the designated categories are denied equal access to employment at a public university, regardless of their expertise or their capacity to contribute to the program's stated goals.

incidents.short_description: Thompson Rivers University restricted a tenure-track faculty position exclusively to applicants who identify as indigenous, barring all other qualified candidates from consideration based on their ancestry.
incidents.description_of_event: Thompson Rivers University posted a tenure-track Assistant or Associate Teaching Professor position in its School of Social Work and Human Service on June 10, 2026, with a closing date of June 23, 2026. The posting explicitly states that the position is restricted to applicants who identify as indigenous (First Nations, Métis, and Inuit), meaning no other candidate, regardless of qualifications, teaching experience, or ability to contribute to the program's goals, will be considered. Applicants are required to self-declare their indigenous identity in their cover letter and provide a narrative of community engagement with indigenous communities. The university frames this restriction as part of its commitment to decolonization and reconciliation and states that the school aims to correct historic underrepresentation. The job posting makes clear that candidates who do not identify as indigenous will not advance through the selection process, regardless of how well they meet the academic and professional qualifications listed.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from a publicly funded university position solely on the basis of their ancestry and ethnic identity, rather than their individual character, abilities, or demonstrated professional competencies.
