The practice also signals to the academic community that demographic identity is a legitimate and expected factor in employment decisions, normalising the institutional sorting of people by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other personal characteristics.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's Faculty of Dentistry posted a faculty position that explicitly invites applicants to disclose their membership in designated demographic groups, signalling that demographic identity is a factor in the hiring process rather than relying solely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On 27 May 2026, McGill University's Faculty of Dentistry and Oral Health Sciences posted a tenure-track assistant professor position in oral pathology and oral medicine. The posting states that McGill welcomes applications from racialized persons, women, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity. It further states that McGill has an employment equity programme and invites members of designated groups to indicate their group membership in their application. Applicants are explicitly encouraged to disclose demographic characteristics as part of their candidacy package. This means that demographic identity becomes a named and encouraged factor in the evaluation of candidates for an academic post, rather than the position being filled purely on the basis of individual qualifications, research excellence, teaching ability, and demonstrated experience.
incidents.expected_harm: When a university signals that demographic group membership is a relevant and encouraged element of a job application, candidates are evaluated — at least in part — on immutable personal characteristics rather than solely on their individual abilities, achievements, and character. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as an individual. It creates a process in which some candidates gain an advantage or disadvantage based on who they are rather than what they have accomplished, eroding confidence in the fairness and integrity of academic hiring. It also pressures applicants to categorise and disclose personal information about themselves that should be irrelevant to their professional suitability, reducing individuals to demographic proxies rather than recognising their singular worth.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's job posting for an academic position encourages applications from people based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting also states that an employment equity program is in place and asks members of designated groups to self-identify.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a position for an Academic Associate in Food Science (Food Safety) on May 27, 2026. The posting states that the university "welcomes and encourages applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities." It further states that McGill "implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify." In practice, this means the university is collecting information about applicants' demographic characteristics and using that information as part of the hiring process. Rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their qualifications, skills, and demonstrated abilities, the institution explicitly sorts and categorizes applicants by skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation. Applicants are invited to label themselves according to these categories, and the institution signals that membership in certain demographic groups is a factor it weighs in selecting candidates.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution evaluates candidates based on demographic categories rather than solely on individual merit, qualifications, and abilities, every applicant is reduced to a set of immutable characteristics. Candidates who do not belong to listed groups receive a signal that their application is less valued regardless of their individual qualities. Candidates who do belong to listed groups receive a signal that their demographic identity, rather than their unique abilities and achievements, is a factor in their favour. This undermines the principle that every person deserves recognition as an individual and that advancement should reflect character and demonstrated ability. It also normalises institutional categorisation of people by demographic proxies, entrenching the habit of sorting individuals into groups rather than assessing them on their own terms.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's Faculty of Dental Medicine and Oral Health Sciences posted a tenure-track position that encourages applications from candidates based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, sex, and sexual orientation, and implements an employment equity program requiring members of designated groups to self-identify.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in its Faculty of Dental Medicine and Oral Health Sciences on May 27, 2026. The posting includes a statement that the university welcomes and encourages applications from people sorted into categories based on skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. The university further states it implements an employment equity program and encourages members of these designated groups to self-identify during the application process. Rather than evaluating all candidates solely on their qualifications, research record, clinical expertise, and teaching ability, the posting signals that demographic characteristics are a relevant consideration in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and sorted by demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and merit. This institutional practice reduces applicants to proxies for their skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability status, or sexual orientation, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as a distinct individual. Qualified candidates who do not belong to designated demographic groups face a disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their individual merits. The requirement for designated group members to self-identify normalises the institutional practice of categorising employees and applicants by immutable traits, entrenching a framework in which demographic identity, rather than individual worth, shapes professional opportunity.

incidents.short_description: Queen's University posted a faculty position in its School of Computing that requires applicants to submit a personal statement demonstrating commitment to anti-racism and indigenization, effectively making ideological conformity a condition of candidacy. The posting also explicitly encourages applications from named demographic groups.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 27, 2026, Queen's University advertised a faculty position at the Assistant or Associate Professor level in Computational Intelligence within its School of Computing. The posting sets out standard academic qualifications — a PhD, a record of research output, and teaching experience — but also includes two requirements that go beyond academic merit. First, applicants must submit, as part of their application package, a formal statement of their "experience with, and commitment to, facilitation and promotion of Indigenization, equity, diversity, inclusion, anti-racism, and accessibility." This statement is a mandatory document alongside the CV and research and teaching statements, meaning candidates who decline to provide it or who cannot demonstrate the required ideological commitments are effectively disqualified. Second, the posting states that Queen's "encourages applications from Black, racialized/visible minority, Indigenous people, women, persons with disabilities, and 2SLGBTQ+ persons," directing the institution's recruitment efforts toward applicants based on demographic characteristics rather than individual merit alone. The posting also embeds a commitment to these principles into the ongoing responsibilities of the role, requiring the successful candidate to demonstrate "a demonstrated commitment to the principles of Indigenization, equity, diversity, inclusion, anti-racism, and accessibility (I-EDIAA) in the teaching and research environment."
incidents.expected_harm: Requiring candidates to submit a statement of personal ideological commitment as a mandatory component of a job application functions as a screening mechanism that filters out individuals based on their views rather than their scholarly qualifications. Academics who hold different views on how to address inequality, or who object to framing their work through a particular ideological lens, are placed at a disadvantage regardless of their teaching ability or research excellence. This reduces the diversity of thought within the institution and undermines the open intellectual environment that universities are meant to foster. Encouraging applications from people based on their skin colour, sex, or sexual orientation — rather than treating every applicant as an individual to be assessed on their merits — further entrenches the practice of sorting people into demographic categories. Together, these requirements send a message that advancement at the institution depends not only on scholarly achievement but on demonstrated alignment with a specific set of political and social commitments.

incidents.institution_name: University of Manitoba, Department of Computer Science
incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba's Department of Computer Science posted a job listing that explicitly invites applications based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, sex, disability, and sexuality, rather than evaluating all candidates purely on their qualifications and abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba's Department of Computer Science posted a job advertisement on May 27, 2026, for a full-time Instructor I position. The posting states that the department 'particularly invite[s] applications' from candidates belonging to specific demographic groups, including indigenous peoples, women, people of certain skin colours, people with disabilities, and people of certain sexualities and genders. The posting also encourages applicants to 'self-identify aspects of your identity' that position them as under-represented. Rather than assessing all applicants based solely on their teaching ability, academic qualifications, and potential contributions to the department, the institution explicitly signals that demographic characteristics will factor into the selection process. The posting frames this as creating 'role models for a diverse population of students,' linking hiring decisions to the demographic identity of prospective employees rather than to their individual merit and demonstrated capabilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics — skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexuality — rather than solely on their qualifications, teaching ability, and character. This reduces individuals to demographic proxies and undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. Highly qualified applicants who do not belong to the preferred demographic categories face a disadvantage in the hiring process through no fault of their own. The practice also normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic categories, reinforcing the idea that a person's identity group is a relevant factor in professional evaluation. Students and staff alike receive the message that demographic representation, rather than individual excellence, is a primary institutional value.

incidents.short_description: UQAM's job posting for a nursing professor position explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on their sex, indigeneity, visible minority status, and disability, and asks those individuals to self-identify during the application process.
incidents.description_of_event: The Université du Québec à Montréal posted a position for a nursing professor in its Faculty of Health Sciences on 26 May 2026. The posting includes a statement indicating that the university wishes to reflect Québec society and invites all qualified individuals to apply, but singles out women, indigenous peoples, members of visible and ethnic minorities, and persons with disabilities for particular encouragement, in accordance with its equal access to employment programme. Candidates from these groups are specifically asked to self-identify at the time of application by completing an identification questionnaire and sending it to a designated email address. The posting thus institutionalises the categorisation of applicants by demographic characteristics as part of the hiring process, rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications, experience, and individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are sorted and tracked according to demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, indigeneity, and disability — rather than being assessed purely as individuals. This practice embeds the habit of evaluating people through group membership into institutional hiring, undermining the principle that each person deserves recognition based on their unique abilities and character. Candidates who do not belong to the named groups receive a lower level of institutional encouragement, signalling that their applications are less valued based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to perform the role. Over time, this normalises the idea that demographic proxies are a legitimate basis for institutional decision-making, eroding the commitment to singular worth and individual dignity.

incidents.short_description: Université Sainte-Anne's job posting for a faculty position explicitly encourages applications from women, indigenous peoples, visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and people of various sexual orientations and gender identities, signalling that demographic characteristics factor into hiring rather than individual merit alone.
incidents.description_of_event: Université Sainte-Anne, the only francophone university in Nova Scotia, posted a faculty position in education sciences on 26 May 2026. The posting includes a statement inviting applications from all qualified persons but then lists specific demographic groups — women, North American indigenous peoples, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression — and encourages them to apply in order to achieve greater diversification of ideas and of the university. This language signals that the institution considers demographic characteristics as a factor in the hiring process alongside qualifications. Candidates are evaluated not solely on their individual academic credentials, teaching experience, and research contributions, but partly through the lens of which demographic categories they belong to.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants who do not belong to the listed demographic groups are disadvantaged in the hiring process regardless of the quality of their individual qualifications, experience, or ideas. The institution reduces candidates to their demographic categories rather than evaluating them purely as individuals with distinct abilities and character. This practice sends a message to all staff and students that demographic identity is a legitimate basis on which to sort and favour people, normalising institutional classification of individuals by immutable traits. It also undermines trust in the fairness of academic hiring and in the credentials of those ultimately appointed, as colleagues and students cannot be certain whether appointments reflect individual merit or demographic considerations.

incidents.short_description: Université Sainte-Anne posted a faculty position in education sciences that explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories including sex, indigeneity, visible minority status, disability, and sexual orientation or gender identity. The posting signals that the institution evaluates applicants partly through the lens of demographic group membership rather than solely on individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event:
Université Sainte-Anne, the only francophone university in Nova Scotia, advertised a 12-month term position for a professor in education sciences specialising in inclusive education and learner diversity. The posting was listed on University Affairs on 26 May 2026, with a closing date of 12 June 2026.

In the application section, the university states that it is committed to fostering an equitable, diverse, and inclusive work environment, and goes on to explicitly encourage applications from women, indigenous peoples of North America, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression, and anyone who can contribute to a greater diversification of ideas and the university. This language signals to candidates and hiring committees that demographic characteristics are relevant considerations in the selection process, beyond individual qualifications, skills, and experience.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution publicly signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in hiring, candidates are evaluated not solely as individuals with distinct qualifications and abilities but as members of demographic categories. This undermines the principle that each person deserves assessment based on their own merits and circumstances. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the listed groups receive an implicit message that their application is less valued, while candidates from listed groups are defined in part by their demographic traits rather than their individual accomplishments. Over time, this normalises the practice of sorting people by immutable characteristics in institutional decision-making, eroding the expectation that advancement should reflect individual character, ability, and demonstrated need.

incidents.short_description: Université Sainte-Anne posted a faculty position in administrative sciences that explicitly encourages applications from women, indigenous peoples, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity, signalling that hiring decisions will be influenced by demographic characteristics rather than individual qualifications alone.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 26, 2026, Université Sainte-Anne posted a tenure-track faculty position in administrative sciences on University Affairs. The posting includes a standard equal-opportunity statement but goes further by explicitly encouraging applications from women, indigenous peoples, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity. The institution states its commitment to fostering an equitable, diverse, and inclusive workplace and frames the encouragement of applications from these groups as a means of contributing to a greater diversification of ideas. This approach signals that candidates' demographic characteristics — including sex, ancestry, physical appearance, disability status, and sexual orientation — are factors the institution intends to consider in evaluating applicants, rather than assessing each candidate solely on their character, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic groups are placed at a disadvantage in the hiring process, as the institution has signalled that demographic characteristics factor into its evaluation of applicants. This reduces the likelihood that hiring decisions reflect individual merit, qualifications, and demonstrated ability. Qualified individuals outside the preferred groups are treated as less desirable candidates based on immutable traits rather than what they bring as individuals. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people into demographic categories and entrenches the idea that a person's group membership — rather than their unique qualities — is a legitimate basis for employment decisions.

incidents.short_description: Acadia University's School of Education posted a faculty position requiring applicants to demonstrate ideological commitments as a job qualification, and made completion of a demographic self-identification form mandatory on pain of disqualification.
incidents.description_of_event: Acadia University advertised a one-year term position of Assistant Professor or Lecturer in Science Education, posted on May 26, 2026. The job posting sets out several requirements that raise concerns under the Coalition's principles. First, candidates are required to have "proven experience teaching science from a culturally responsive pedagogical standpoint, grounded in indigeneity, and decolonizing, equity, social justice, and antiracist frameworks." These are ideological commitments presented as mandatory professional qualifications, not as neutral pedagogical competencies. The posting further states that the School is seeking candidates "committed to contributing to Acadia's priorities of decolonization, antiracism, equity, inclusion, and diversity," and that cover letters must include "evidence of the applicant's commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion." This means an ideological litmus test is explicitly built into the selection process. Second, all applicants are required to submit an Employment Equity Voluntary Self-Identification Form, and the posting states that "failure to complete the form by the closing date will result in the disqualification of your application." Although the form is described as voluntary in name, the penalty for non-completion is elimination from consideration, making it compulsory in practice. The posting additionally lists specific demographic groups — including people identified by skin colour, ethnicity, disability, sexuality, and gender — as groups whose applications the university encourages, framing the hiring process around demographic categories rather than individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part on their adherence to a specific ideological framework rather than solely on their teaching ability, subject knowledge, and professional experience. This reduces academic hiring to a values screen that excludes qualified individuals who do not subscribe to or are unwilling to perform allegiance to particular political positions. The mandatory self-identification requirement, despite being labelled voluntary, compels applicants to disclose personal demographic information under threat of disqualification, violating the principle that individuals deserve privacy and should not be reduced to demographic categories. The combination of ideological requirements and demographic preferences signals that advancement in this institution reflects conformity to a prescribed worldview and demographic background rather than individual merit and demonstrated ability. This harms the integrity of the academy and the students who are taught by faculty selected partly on ideological rather than educational grounds.

incidents.short_description: The Université de l'Ontario français posted a job advertisement for its rector position that gives hiring priority to candidates based on demographic categories including indigeneity, disability status, and membership in visible minorities, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 26, 2026, the Université de l'Ontario français (UOF) in Toronto posted a job advertisement for the position of rector on University Affairs. The posting states that the UOF subscribes to the principle of employment equity and encourages applications from indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities. While the posting notes that all qualified persons are invited to apply, it signals that candidates will be assessed in part based on their membership in designated demographic groups. The posting also lists equity, diversity, and inclusion among the institution's core values, and indicates the ideal candidate should be able to demonstrate experience in addressing systemic inequalities. Priority in hiring is framed in part around demographic group membership rather than being grounded exclusively in the individual's character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for the rector position are evaluated and sorted in part based on their membership in demographic categories — such as indigeneity, disability status, or being a member of a visible minority — rather than exclusively on their individual qualifications, experience, and character. This means that equally or more qualified individuals who do not belong to these designated groups are disadvantaged in the hiring process through no fault of their own. The practice reduces people to demographic proxies rather than treating them as distinct individuals, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits.

incidents.institution_name: University of British Columbia, Department of Urologic Sciences
incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia's Department of Urologic Sciences posted a faculty job listing requiring applicants to submit a statement detailing their personal contributions to anti-racism and related ideological frameworks. The posting also explicitly invites applications on the basis of demographic group membership rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event:
The University of British Columbia's Department of Urologic Sciences posted a job listing on May 26, 2026, for a full-time Associate Professor position. As part of the mandatory application package, candidates are required to include a statement of their current or previous contributions to advancing anti-racism, equity, diversity, decolonization, indigenization, and inclusion. This requirement is listed alongside standard academic materials such as a curriculum vitae, publications record, and teaching summary — placing an ideological commitment statement on the same footing as scholarly qualifications.

The posting also explicitly invites applications from individuals belonging to named demographic groups, including people categorized by skin colour, disability status, sex, sexual orientation, and indigenous identity. The framing signals that membership in these groups is a factor in the hiring process rather than restricting selection entirely to individual qualifications.

The posting states that UBC 'hires on the basis of merit' while simultaneously requiring applicants to demonstrate alignment with a specific ideological framework and signalling preference for candidates based on demographic characteristics. These elements are in tension with one another and with the principle that individuals should be evaluated on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Requiring a statement of personal contributions to a specific ideological framework as part of a mandatory application package pressures applicants to demonstrate ideological conformity in order to be considered for an academic position. Applicants who hold different views on how to address social concerns, or who have not engaged in activism aligned with these frameworks, face disadvantage regardless of their scholarly qualifications. This chills intellectual diversity and academic freedom within the institution. Explicitly inviting applications on the basis of demographic group membership signals that candidates are being evaluated in part as representatives of demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique abilities and accomplishments. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be recognized and advanced based on their individual merit and circumstances.

incidents.short_description: Acadia University's job posting for a kinesiology instructor requires all applicants to complete an Employment Equity Voluntary Self-Identification Form, with disqualification threatened for non-completion. The posting also encourages applications from people based on skin colour, ethnicity, and other demographic characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: Acadia University posted a 12-month term instructor position in its School of Kinesiology on May 26, 2026. The posting includes a requirement that all applicants complete an Employment Equity Voluntary Self-Identification Form as part of their application package. The posting states that failure to complete the form by the closing date results in disqualification of the application. While the form offers an option to not self-identify, the threat of disqualification for not submitting the form at all creates meaningful pressure on applicants to disclose personal demographic information as a condition of being considered. The posting also explicitly encourages applications from people categorised by skin colour, ancestry, disability, and sexual and gender identity, framing these demographic characteristics as relevant to the selection process. Candidates are told that only those who have submitted a self-identification form can be considered as members of designated equity-seeking groups, suggesting that demographic categorisation plays a role in how candidates are evaluated and selected.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are pressured to disclose personal demographic information — including skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual or gender identity — as a condition of having their application considered. This compromises individual privacy and autonomy, and treats demographic characteristics as relevant hiring criteria rather than evaluating people on their qualifications, abilities, and character. Candidates who prefer not to be categorised by demographic labels face a formal barrier to employment: disqualification for not submitting the form. The structure of the process normalises institutional sorting of individuals into demographic groups, reducing people to categories rather than recognising them as distinct individuals. It also creates a hiring environment in which certain candidates receive preferential treatment based on immutable traits, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect merit and demonstrated ability.

incidents.short_description: Acadia University's School of Education posted a faculty position that explicitly favours candidates based on personal demographic characteristics, including their identity and lived experiences tied to those characteristics. Completing a mandatory self-identification form is required, and failure to do so results in disqualification.
incidents.description_of_event: Acadia University's School of Education posted a 12-month contractually limited term position at the rank of Assistant Professor or Lecturer in Foundations and Inclusive Education, advertised on May 26, 2026. The posting states that preference will be given to applicants with 'lived experience of marginalized identities,' treating personal demographic characteristics as a job qualification relevant to the position. The posting encourages applications from a list of specific demographic groups defined by skin colour, ethnicity, disability, sexuality, and gender identity. All applicants are required to complete an Employment Equity Voluntary Self-Identification Form as part of their application, with a warning that failure to complete it by the closing date will result in the disqualification of their application. The posting frames demographic identity as 'fundamental knowledge' and as a credential alongside academic qualifications, rather than evaluating candidates on their scholarly expertise, teaching ability, and professional accomplishments alone.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part on the basis of their personal demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual abilities, qualifications, and teaching record. The mandatory self-identification form, combined with the explicit preference given to specific demographic groups, creates pressure on applicants to disclose personal information about their identity to gain a competitive advantage. Candidates who decline to self-identify face disqualification, meaning their application receives no consideration regardless of their academic qualifications. This approach reduces individuals to their demographic categories, undermining the principle that people deserve to be assessed on their character, abilities, and demonstrated work. It establishes a hiring process that sorts applicants by group membership rather than individual merit, embedding demographic categorisation as an institutional norm within the university's recruitment practices.

incidents.institution_name: Université d'Ottawa — École de gestion Telfer
incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa's Telfer School of Management posted a tenure-track accounting faculty position that explicitly singles out specific demographic groups — including indigenous peoples, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women — for preferential encouragement to apply, directing them to complete a self-identification questionnaire.
incidents.description_of_event: The Telfer School of Management at the University of Ottawa posted a tenure-track accounting faculty position (assistant or associate professor) on 26 May 2026, with an anticipated start date of January or July 2027. The posting describes qualifications based on academic credentials, research output, and teaching ability, which are all appropriate measures of individual merit. However, the posting concludes with a section stating that the university 'strongly encourages' specific named demographic groups — indigenous peoples of Canada, members of visible minorities (racialised persons), persons with disabilities, and women — to apply, and asks members of these groups to complete a self-identification questionnaire when submitting their application. The posting frames this preferential encouragement as a commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion, and situates it within an institutional culture that explicitly values these categories as criteria relevant to recruitment. By naming demographic groups and asking candidates to self-identify, the institution introduces demographic categorisation as a factor in the hiring process rather than evaluating all candidates solely on the basis of their individual character, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated not only on their individual merits — qualifications, research record, and teaching ability — but also on their membership in designated demographic categories. This reduces individuals to demographic proxies and undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own unique qualities. Candidates who do not belong to the named groups are disadvantaged in the competition, not because of any deficiency in their abilities, but because of immutable characteristics. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by demographic categories and signals to the broader academic community that demographic identity is a relevant credential for employment. Over time, this erodes trust in hiring processes, raises questions about whether appointments reflect individual excellence, and entrenches the habit of viewing colleagues through the lens of group membership rather than as distinct individuals.

incidents.institution_name: University of Ottawa, Department of Biology
incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa posted a biology professor position that explicitly encourages applications from people in designated demographic groups and asks all applicants to complete a self-identification questionnaire sorted by demographic category. The hiring process evaluates candidates partly on the basis of immutable traits rather than solely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Ottawa's Department of Biology posted a replacement professor position on May 26, 2026, with a start date of July 1, 2026. The posting requires candidates to submit a statement explaining how equity, diversity and inclusion principles will be incorporated into their teaching and research. It explicitly encourages applications from individuals belonging to four designated groups under the Employment Equity Act: aboriginal peoples, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women. All applicants are invited to complete a self-identification questionnaire that sorts candidates into these demographic categories. The posting states that the university is committed to providing a leadership environment where equity, diversity and inclusion prevail, and frames the institution's selection process around group membership alongside individual qualifications. Candidates are effectively evaluated in part based on which demographic categories they belong to, rather than solely on their academic credentials, teaching record, and research experience.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed based on their demographic group membership rather than purely on their individual character, qualifications, and abilities. This reduces applicants to demographic categories, undermining their recognition as distinct individuals. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the designated groups face a structural disadvantage in the selection process regardless of their merit. The requirement for applicants to explain how they will incorporate identity-based principles into their teaching pressures faculty candidates to adopt and promote a particular ideological framework as a condition of employment. Collectively, these practices normalise institutional sorting of people by immutable traits and embed demographic categorisation into the fabric of academic hiring.

incidents.short_description: The Université d'Ottawa is recruiting a replacement professor in biology while explicitly encouraging applications from members of designated demographic groups and requiring candidates to describe how they will incorporate equity, diversity, and inclusion principles into their teaching.
incidents.description_of_event: The Université d'Ottawa's Department of Biology posted a replacement professor position on 26 May 2026, with a start date of 1 July 2026. The posting states that the university strongly encourages applications from indigenous peoples of Canada, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women. Applicants belonging to at least one of these groups are invited to complete a confidential self-identification questionnaire when submitting their application. Beyond encouraging applications from certain demographic categories, the posting requires candidates to submit a statement describing how the principles of equity, diversity, and inclusion will be incorporated into teaching within research groups. This makes adherence to a particular ideological framework a formal qualification for the position. The posting also notes that priority will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents, which is a standard government requirement and not a concern of the Coalition.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed and sorted in part based on their demographic characteristics — such as ethnicity, disability status, and sex — rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching excellence, and research ability. This reduces people to group membership rather than recognising them as individuals with distinct skills and merits. The mandatory statement on equity, diversity, and inclusion as a teaching framework imposes an ideological requirement on applicants, signalling that those who do not adopt this framework are less competitive. Together, these practices undermine fair evaluation of individuals on their own merits and introduce demographic criteria into what should be a process grounded in academic excellence and individual achievement.

incidents.institution_name: University of Ottawa – Telfer School of Management
incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa's Telfer School of Management posted a faculty position in accounting that explicitly encourages applications from and gives hiring preference to candidates based on their demographic group membership, including skin colour, disability status, and sex, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The Telfer School of Management at the University of Ottawa posted a tenure-track faculty position in accounting on May 26, 2026. The posting includes a statement that the university strongly encourages applications from specific demographic groups — identified by skin colour, disability, and sex among other categories — and instructs applicants who wish to be considered as members of one or more of these designated groups to complete a Self-Identification Questionnaire. This signals that demographic group membership is a factor in the hiring process. The university thus invites candidates to be assessed not only on their academic qualifications, research record, and teaching ability, but also on which demographic categories they belong to. This institutional practice reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them as unique persons based solely on their abilities, character, and demonstrated achievements.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated and ranked in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than purely on their individual abilities and merit. This denies each applicant the recognition they deserve as a distinct individual. Candidates who do not belong to named demographic groups face a structural disadvantage regardless of their qualifications. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by immutable traits, reinforcing the idea that a person's demographic category defines their worth or relevance to an institution. Over time this undermines the principle that every person deserves to be judged on their own qualities, and erodes trust in the fairness and integrity of academic hiring.

incidents.short_description: Thompson Rivers University's Faculty of Law posted a tenure-track position that encourages applications from candidates based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation. While the posting claims to hire based on merit, it signals that certain demographic identities are treated as hiring advantages.
incidents.description_of_event: Thompson Rivers University's Faculty of Law posted a tenure-track or tenured faculty position on May 25, 2026, for a role beginning July 1, 2027. The posting's diversity and inclusion statement declares the university is committed to hiring based on merit but then specifically names demographic categories — women, indigenous people, black people, people of colour, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity — as groups whose applications are especially welcomed for the purpose of further diversifying staff and faculty. This framing signals that membership in these categories is treated as a relevant consideration in the hiring process, going beyond simply welcoming all qualified applicants and instead attaching institutional weight to applicants' demographic identities. The same diversity statement appears twice in the posting, reinforcing its prominence. The posting is for the Faculty of Law in Kamloops, British Columbia.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution signals that demographic characteristics influence hiring decisions, candidates are evaluated not solely on their individual character, abilities, and qualifications but on their membership in designated groups. This reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than recognizing their singular worth. Candidates who do not belong to the named groups face a disadvantage despite potentially equal or superior qualifications, and all candidates — including those from the named groups — are left uncertain whether outcomes reflect genuine individual merit or demographic considerations. This practice normalises institutional categorisation of people by immutable traits and undermines trust in the fairness and integrity of academic hiring.

incidents.institution_name: Western University (Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry)
incidents.short_description: Western University restricted applications for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair position to applicants who self-identify as women only, explicitly excluding all other candidates from consideration. This bars qualified individuals from competing for a prestigious academic role solely on the basis of sex.
incidents.description_of_event: The Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry at Western University posted a job advertisement on May 25, 2026, for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair (CRC) in Mass Spectrometry 'Omics for Novel Therapeutics, a tenured Associate Professor or Professor appointment. The posting explicitly states that, pursuant to Section 14 of the Ontario Human Rights Code and citing underrepresentation of women among its CRC holders, only applicants who self-identify as women will be considered for the position. The restriction extends to individuals who self-identify as transgender, gender-fluid, non-binary, or Two-Spirit under what the posting describes as a "Gender Equity-Seeking Groups" designation. Applicants are required to disclose their self-identification to the appointments committee. All other qualified candidates — regardless of their research excellence, publications, funding record, or leadership — are categorically excluded from consideration before their qualifications are even reviewed.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are denied the opportunity to compete for a prestigious, tenured academic position and the associated research funding, infrastructure, and career advancement solely because of their sex. This practice reduces individuals to a demographic category rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated research merit. It sends a clear institutional message that sex is a determining factor in who deserves access to senior academic roles, undermining the principle that people should be assessed as individuals. It also deprives the institution and the broader research community of contributions from talented scientists who are excluded before their qualifications are considered.

incidents.short_description: UBC's Department of Urologic Sciences posted a faculty position that requires applicants to submit a statement on contributions to anti-racism and explicitly invites applications preferentially from candidates based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, sex, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia's Department of Urologic Sciences posted a job advertisement on 25 May 2026 for an Assistant Professor position in computational oncology. The posting requires applicants to include, as a mandatory component of their application package, a written statement describing their "previous, current or envisioned contributions to advancing anti-racism, equity, diversity, decolonization, Indigenization and inclusion." This requirement makes adherence to a particular ideological framework a formal criterion for employment consideration, evaluated alongside scholarly and teaching qualifications. The posting also explicitly states that UBC "welcome[s] applications from candidates belonging to historically, persistently, or systemically marginalized groups," naming specific demographic categories including indigenous peoples, racialized persons, persons with disabilities, women, and people defined by their sexual orientation or gender identity. By singling out these groups for preferential invitation, the institution signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in candidate evaluation rather than assessing all individuals purely on their academic qualifications, abilities, and potential.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not belong to the named demographic groups, or who hold different views on the ideological frameworks named in the required statement, face a structural disadvantage in the hiring process. The mandatory anti-racism statement requirement effectively screens candidates for conformity to a specific set of political and ideological positions, undermining the principle that academic positions should be awarded on the basis of scholarly merit, teaching ability, and individual capability. This erodes open intellectual inquiry and academic freedom by embedding ideological conformity as a hiring criterion. The preferential invitation to specific demographic groups reinforces the practice of evaluating people by demographic category rather than as distinct individuals, normalising institutional sorting of people by immutable characteristics.

incidents.institution_name: Wilfrid Laurier University – Lazaridis School of Business & Economics
incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University's Lazaridis School of Business & Economics posted a faculty hiring advertisement that explicitly invites and prioritises applications from candidates based on demographic categories including skin colour, disability, and sexual and gender identity. The posting requires members of designated groups to self-identify to be considered for employment equity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Lazaridis School of Business & Economics at Wilfrid Laurier University posted a job advertisement on May 25, 2026, seeking two tenure-track Assistant Professors in Organizational Behaviour and Human Resource Management. The posting explicitly states that the university is committed to increasing faculty diversity and welcomes applications from candidates who identify as indigenous, racialized, having disabilities, and from persons of any minority sexual and gender identities. Candidates belonging to these designated groups are directed to self-identify to the Senior Administrative Officer to be considered for employment equity. The posting instructs that members of these designated groups must self-identify to be considered under the university's employment equity program. Separate contact points are provided for indigenous candidates and for candidates from other equity-seeking groups, channelling applicants into different processes based on their demographic characteristics. The posting evaluates prospective faculty members not solely on their research potential, teaching ability, and academic qualifications, but also on whether they belong to particular demographic categories, treating group membership as a relevant criterion in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed and sorted based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated merit. This approach reduces individuals to group membership categories rather than evaluating them as unique persons with distinct skills and potential. Qualified applicants who do not belong to the designated demographic groups face a disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their individual merits. The requirement that candidates self-identify their demographic characteristics to be considered for employment equity normalises institutional categorisation of individuals by immutable traits. Over time, this practice entrenches the idea that a person's demographic identity is a legitimate and relevant factor in professional evaluation, undermining the principle that people should be assessed on their character, abilities, and demonstrated contributions.

incidents.short_description: Nipissing University's job posting for a postdoctoral research fellowship strongly encourages applications from people based on demographic characteristics including sex, sexual orientation, disability status, and ethnicity, and asks applicants to self-identify by these categories in their cover letters.
incidents.description_of_event: Nipissing University posted two postdoctoral research fellowship positions within its Rare Dementia Support Canada unit on May 25, 2026. The posting included a statement declaring the university is "committed to employment equity" and explicitly encouraged applications from individuals who belong to named demographic groups — specifically indigenous peoples, women, persons with disabilities, persons who identify as 2SLGBTQI+, and racialized minorities. Applicants were invited to self-identify membership in these groups within their cover letter as part of the application process. This approach directs applicants to disclose personal demographic characteristics as part of the hiring process and signals that group membership is a relevant consideration in hiring decisions, rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications, research experience, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are assessed in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual merits, qualifications, and potential. Applicants who do not belong to the named groups are placed at a disadvantage regardless of the quality of their work or the strength of their candidacy. The invitation to self-identify demographic characteristics in a cover letter introduces personal information about sex, sexual orientation, disability, and ethnicity into the early stages of the hiring process, where it can influence selection decisions in ways unrelated to job performance. This normalises the practice of categorising and sorting job candidates by immutable traits, undermining the principle that each person should be evaluated as an individual based on their character and abilities.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto, Department of Germanic Languages and Literatures
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto posted a faculty position in which it explicitly encouraged applications from candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, and other demographic characteristics, and indicated that these traits factor into the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Toronto's Department of Germanic Languages and Literatures posted a job advertisement on May 25, 2026, for an Assistant Professor, Teaching Stream (Contractually Limited Term Appointment) in German Language Pedagogy. The posting stated that the university 'strongly welcome[s] and encourage[s] candidates' from specific groups defined by skin colour and ethnicity, citing historically experienced inequities as the justification. The posting's Diversity Statement went further, explicitly directing the university to 'strongly encourage applications' from people sorted into categories based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation. Candidates were required to complete a Diversity Survey as part of their application, in which they would classify themselves according to these demographic categories. The posting also indicated that the university values and seeks candidates who have 'demonstrated a commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion,' making ideological alignment with these institutional frameworks a stated criterion for hiring alongside academic qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for this academic position are evaluated not solely on their individual qualifications, teaching excellence, and scholarly merit, but on their demographic characteristics — skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation. This means qualified individuals who do not belong to the listed demographic groups face a disadvantage in the hiring process based on traits entirely unrelated to their ability to teach German or conduct pedagogical research. The practice normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories and treating those categories as relevant to professional evaluation. It undermines the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their character, abilities, and demonstrated record, and entrenches a system where immutable personal traits, rather than individual merit, shape professional opportunity.

incidents.short_description: Dalhousie University's job posting for Assistant Vice-President, Student Recruitment & Marketing explicitly states that preference will be given to candidates who identify as members of designated demographic groups. The posting formally disadvantages applicants who do not belong to those groups.
incidents.description_of_event: Dalhousie University posted a senior leadership position — Assistant Vice-President, Student Recruitment & Marketing — on May 25, 2026, with an application deadline of June 24, 2026. The posting states that the university will give hiring preference to candidates who self-identify as members of one or more designated groups, including indigenous peoples, persons of black/african descent, members of other racialized groups, persons with disabilities, women, and persons identifying as members of 2SLGBTQIA+ communities. This preference is described as being applied in accordance with the university's Employment Equity Policy. The role is a senior executive position reporting to the Vice-President, Strategic Engagement and carrying significant institutional responsibility for recruitment, admissions, marketing, analytics, and creative services. By embedding demographic preference directly into the hiring process for this role, the university evaluates candidates not solely on their abilities, experience, and character, but on whether they belong to particular demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are passed over for a senior leadership position based on demographic characteristics rather than their individual abilities, experience, and merit. The policy reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than recognising them as individuals with unique skills and contributions. This practice embeds institutional discrimination into the hiring process, normalising the idea that demographic group membership is a legitimate and relevant criterion for employment decisions. It also undermines public trust in the university's leadership appointments, since the best-qualified person for the role is not guaranteed to be selected.

incidents.short_description: The University of Guelph is recruiting a faculty member while explicitly encouraging applications from people based on their demographic characteristics and requiring all applicants to submit a statement demonstrating commitment to a particular ideological framework as a condition of the application.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Guelph's College of Engineering posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in the Department of Civil, Environmental and Water Resources Engineering on May 22, 2026. The posting states that the university 'strongly welcomes and encourages applications' from people sorted into specific demographic categories, including indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, people identified by their skin colour, women, and people identified by their sexual orientation. The posting also requires all applicants to submit a statement providing 'evidence of commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion (EDI)' as a mandatory component of the application package. This means that candidates are evaluated in part on their adherence to a particular ideological position, in addition to their academic and professional qualifications. The university's strategic plan, cited in the posting, frames human resources practices around recruiting a 'diverse' student, faculty, and staff body defined by demographic categories rather than individual qualities.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are disadvantaged in the hiring process based on their demographic characteristics rather than their abilities, expertise, and potential contributions. The mandatory EDI statement requirement means applicants are assessed on ideological conformity, creating pressure to adopt and perform particular beliefs as a condition of employment consideration. This reduces the position to one where demographic characteristics and ideological alignment weigh alongside or against demonstrated academic merit, undermining the principle that individuals deserve to be evaluated on their unique qualities, character, and abilities. It also signals to the broader academic community that advancement in this institution depends in part on demographic identity and ideological commitment rather than individual excellence.

incidents.institution_name: University of Saskatchewan – Western College of Veterinary Medicine
incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan's Western College of Veterinary Medicine posted a faculty position that encourages applications from people based on demographic categories and establishes a separate verification process for applicants who identify as indigenous. The posting frames demographic group membership as a relevant qualification for an academic role.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 22, 2026, the University of Saskatchewan advertised a two-year term Lecturer position in Food Animal Theriogenology at the Western College of Veterinary Medicine. The job posting states that 'applications from equity-deserving groups are encouraged' as part of the university's ongoing efforts to reflect community diversity, signalling a preference for candidates based on their membership in designated demographic categories rather than solely on qualifications and experience. Additionally, the posting describes a formal process by which candidates who assert indigenous membership or citizenship are asked to undergo a separate verification procedure governed by a specific university policy and a Standing Committee. This creates a two-track system in which applicants are assessed not only on professional qualifications — such as a DVM degree, an approved residency in theriogenology, and board certification — but also on their demographic identity and ethnic background. The posting links to an 'EDI Framework for Action' as the basis for these practices.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part on the basis of demographic group membership rather than solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and character. This practice disadvantages applicants who do not belong to designated groups, regardless of their professional merit. The separate verification process for indigenous identity creates an unequal application experience, sorting candidates by ethnicity. Over time, institutional hiring practices that emphasise demographic categories normalise the idea that a person's group identity is a relevant credential, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed on their own abilities and demonstrated qualities. This approach also sends a signal to students and staff that demographic characteristics are professionally significant attributes, which erodes a shared sense of common institutional belonging.

incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia posted a Research Associate position that explicitly encourages applications from people based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, religion, disability, and Indigenous identity. The posting treats group membership as a relevant factor in hiring rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications and individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia's School of Public Policy and Global Affairs posted a full-time Research Associate position on May 22, 2026, for a two-year term beginning July 16, 2026. The role involves research on copper exploration and mining decision-making frameworks. The posting states that the university encourages applications from people belonging to groups it identifies as marginalized, listing specific demographic categories including sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, skin colour, disability, political belief, religion, marital or family status, age, and Indigenous identity. Rather than evaluating all candidates purely on their research qualifications, skills, and demonstrated abilities, the posting signals that demographic group membership is a relevant and desirable characteristic for applicants. This approach frames candidates through the lens of their immutable or personal characteristics rather than their individual merit and ability to perform the role.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and sorted based on demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique qualifications, abilities, and potential. This approach signals to all applicants that group membership influences hiring decisions, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. Highly qualified individuals who do not belong to the listed groups face a disadvantage unrelated to their ability to do the work. At the same time, individuals from listed groups are implicitly treated as representatives of a demographic category rather than as distinct people whose strengths stand on their own. This normalises institutional sorting of people by immutable traits and erodes confidence that academic hiring is conducted on the basis of individual excellence.

incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary posted a job listing for Dean of the Faculty of Arts that explicitly encourages applications from people based on their sex, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation, treating demographic characteristics as relevant criteria for a senior academic leadership role.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Calgary advertised the position of Dean of the Faculty of Arts on May 22, 2026, through University Affairs. The posting lists extensive and legitimate academic and leadership qualifications for the role. However, the application materials explicitly state that the university encourages members of specific demographic groups to apply, listing women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities, and people of diverse sexual orientations and gender identities as preferred applicant categories. The posting frames this as a commitment to removing barriers and enriching the campus community, but the practical effect is that the institution signals it evaluates candidates in part based on immutable or personal characteristics rather than treating every applicant purely as an individual. The posting also references an institution-wide indigenous strategy and broader equity, diversity, inclusion and accessibility commitments as context for the hiring approach.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution explicitly encourages applications based on demographic characteristics such as sex, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation, it sends a signal that these traits are relevant criteria for selection. Candidates are evaluated not solely on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications but at least in part on which demographic categories they belong to. This undermines the principle that each person deserves to be assessed as an individual. It also creates an environment where some candidates understand themselves to be preferred or disadvantaged based on immutable traits, rather than being assessed on the merits of their academic and leadership record.

incidents.institution_name: Wilfrid Laurier University – Lazaridis School of Business & Economics
incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University's Lazaridis School of Business & Economics posted a lecturer position that explicitly signals preference for candidates from demographic groups defined by immutable characteristics, asking applicants to self-identify their membership in those groups to be considered for employment equity benefits.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 21, 2026, Wilfrid Laurier University posted a one-year lecturer position in Accounting at the Lazaridis School of Business & Economics. The posting states the university is "committed to increasing the diversity of faculty and staff" and "welcomes applicants from qualified members of the equity-deserving groups." Candidates are directed to self-identify, in confidence, as members of "designated groups" in order to be considered under the university's employment equity framework. The posting thus creates a parallel track in the hiring process where candidates are evaluated not only on their qualifications, character, and abilities, but also on their membership in demographic categories defined by race, sex, disability, and similar characteristics. The institution asks candidates to declare demographic group membership as a formal step in the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are sorted and evaluated based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching ability, and professional merit. This reduces individuals to proxies for group membership, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own character and abilities. Candidates who do not belong to designated demographic groups face a structural disadvantage in the competition, regardless of their qualifications. The requirement to self-declare group membership also pressures applicants to label themselves by immutable traits as a condition of full participation in the hiring process, treating personal identity as an institutional instrument rather than respecting individual autonomy and privacy.

incidents.institution_name: Wilfrid Laurier University – Lazaridis School of Business & Economics
incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University's Lazaridis School of Business & Economics posted a Lecturer position in Accounting that instructs applicants belonging to designated demographic groups to self-identify for employment equity consideration, indicating hiring preferences based on demographic categories rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 21, 2026, Wilfrid Laurier University advertised a one-year Limited Term Lecturer position in the Accounting area at the Lazaridis School of Business & Economics. The posting states that the university is 'committed to increasing the diversity of faculty and staff and welcomes applicants from qualified members of the equity-deserving groups.' Candidates are directed to self-identify as members of designated groups in order to be considered for employment equity. The posting instructs members of these designated groups to contact specific university offices and to self-identify in confidence to a named Senior Administrative Officer. This structure signals that membership in particular demographic categories is a factor in the hiring process, alongside — or potentially above — individual qualifications, experience, and merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated not only on their individual qualifications, abilities, and experience but also on their membership in designated demographic categories. This reduces individuals to proxies for group identity rather than recognising them as unique candidates with distinct skills and circumstances. Qualified applicants who do not belong to designated groups face a disadvantaged process, while those who do belong are assessed in part through a demographic lens rather than purely as individuals. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by demographic characteristics and entrenches the idea that group membership is a relevant criterion for employment decisions in an academic setting.

quotes.text: Laurier is committed to increasing the diversity of faculty and staff and welcomes applicants from qualified members of the equity-deserving groups.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly states a preference for applicants based on membership in designated demographic groups, signalling that group identity is a criterion in the hiring process.

quotes.text: Members of the designated groups must self-identify to be considered for employment equity. Candidates may self-identify, in confidence, to Michael Lisetto-Smith, Senior Administrative Officer.
quotes.rationale: Requires candidates to declare demographic group membership as a formal step in the hiring process, confirming that group identity influences employment decisions rather than individual merit alone.

quotes.text: Indigenous candidates who would like to learn more about equity and inclusive programming at Laurier are welcome to contact the Office of Indigenous Initiatives. Candidates from other equity-deserving groups who would like to learn more about equity and inclusive programming at Laurier are welcomed to contact Equity and Accessibility.
quotes.rationale: Separates candidates by demographic category and directs them to different institutional offices based on group membership, reinforcing demographic categorisation as a structural element of the hiring process.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University's job posting for an Associate Vice-President of Information Technology explicitly encourages applications from people based on their demographic characteristics, including skin colour, indigeneity, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting also provides a formal language-requirement exemption tied to self-identified indigenous ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: Laurentian University posted a job for an Associate Vice-President, Information Technology on May 21, 2026. The posting states that applications are encouraged from members of what it calls 'equity-seeking communities,' listing women, people classified by skin colour, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities. Rather than evaluating candidates purely on their qualifications, abilities, and character, the university explicitly signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in the hiring process. The posting also contains a provision in its bilingualism policy that grants an exemption from language requirements specifically for applicants who self-identify as First Nations, Métis or Inuit, making a formal institutional accommodation available on the basis of ethnic self-identification rather than individual circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed in part on demographic characteristics rather than on their individual qualifications, abilities, and character. This sends a clear signal that a person's skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, or sexual orientation influences their chances of being hired, which diminishes the dignity of all applicants by reducing them to demographic proxies. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the listed groups are disadvantaged, while candidates from listed groups face the suggestion that their selection reflects demographics rather than merit. The language-exemption tied to ethnic self-identification creates a formal institutional policy that grants different treatment based on ancestry, normalising the idea that ethnicity is a legitimate basis for institutional decision-making.

quotes.text: Applications are encouraged from members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions.
quotes.rationale: This passage directly demonstrates the institution encouraging hiring on the basis of demographic categories including skin colour, indigeneity, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals.

quotes.text: Laurentian University's bilingualism policy provides a provision regarding the language requirement for persons self-identifying as First Nations, Métis or Inuit.
quotes.rationale: This passage reveals a formal policy that grants a language-requirement exemption based solely on ethnic self-identification, meaning the institution applies different standards to applicants based on their ancestry.

incidents.short_description: The University of New Brunswick posted a faculty position that explicitly grants hiring preference to applicants based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates solely on their qualifications and individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of New Brunswick advertised a two-year term Assistant Teaching Professor position in Technology and Skilled Trades Education, posted on May 21, 2026. The job posting states that the university "welcomes and encourages applications from all qualified individuals" but then specifies that preference will be given to candidates from named demographic groups: women, visible minorities, Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression. This means candidates are not evaluated purely on their qualifications, teaching experience, or demonstrated abilities — their sex, skin colour, disability status, and sexual orientation are used as criteria that can determine which qualified applicants are preferred for the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are ranked and preferred not solely on the basis of their individual abilities, experience, and character, but on the basis of their membership in demographic categories. This reduces individuals to group identities rather than recognising their singular worth. Applicants who do not belong to the preferred demographic groups are disadvantaged regardless of their qualifications, undermining a merit-based hiring process. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by immutable traits and entrenches the idea that demographic categories are a legitimate basis for evaluating individuals in professional contexts.

quotes.text: The University welcomes and encourages applications from all qualified individuals who will help us achieve our goals, including women, visible minorities, Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression. Preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada.
quotes.rationale: This passage establishes that the university grants preference based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates purely on individual qualifications and merit.
