incidents.short_description: The University of New Brunswick posted a job listing for a nursing faculty position that explicitly lists demographic categories — including sex, skin colour, and sexual orientation — as bases for preferential consideration in hiring.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of New Brunswick (UNB) posted a job advertisement on May 21, 2026 for a one-year term Assistant Teaching Professor position in the Faculty of Nursing and Health Sciences at its Saint John campus. The posting states that the university encourages applications from and gives preference to candidates based on demographic characteristics including sex (women), skin colour (visible minorities), indigenous identity (Aboriginal persons), disability, sexual orientation, gender identity, and gender expression. The posting frames this approach as a commitment to employment equity and fostering diversity. Rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated fit for the role, the institution has stated it will give preferential consideration based on a candidate's membership in designated demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated and ranked based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual abilities, qualifications, and merit. This means some applicants receive an advantage or disadvantage in the hiring process based on immutable traits, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed as an individual. The practice normalizes the institutional sorting of people into demographic categories and signals that a person's group identity is a relevant factor in professional advancement, eroding the idea that character and ability are what matter.

quotes.text: The University of New Brunswick is committed to employment equity and fostering diversity within our community and developing an inclusive workplace that reflects the richness of the broader community that we serve. The University welcomes and encourages applications from all qualified individuals who will help us achieve our goals, including women, visible minorities, Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression. Preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada.
quotes.rationale: This passage directly states the institution's policy of giving preferential consideration to candidates based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, indigenous status, disability, and sexual orientation. It is the core evidence of a hiring preference based on group identity rather than individual merit.

incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University's Department of Retail posted a faculty position that explicitly prioritises applications from candidates based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability, sex, and sexual orientation. The posting treats demographic identity as a relevant hiring criterion rather than evaluating candidates as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Toronto Metropolitan University advertised a one-year Limited Term Faculty position at the rank of Assistant Professor in its Department of Retail, posted on May 21, 2026. The posting stated that the university is 'particularly interested in applications' from a list of named demographic groups, including people identified by skin colour, ethnicity, indigeneity, disability, sex, and sexual orientation. The posting further requires applicants to submit an 'EDI statement' as part of their application dossier. Candidates who belong to these designated groups are directed to contact a separate university office, and indigenous-identified candidates are subjected to a distinct verification process requiring documentation and interviews with the university's Indigenous Human Resources Lead. The job posting frames demographic group membership as a qualification relevant to the hiring decision, rather than evaluating candidates solely on their academic qualifications, teaching ability, and professional experience.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are assessed not only on their individual merits — teaching ability, scholarly accomplishment, and professional experience — but on whether they belong to designated demographic categories. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups face a systematic disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their qualifications. The requirement for an EDI statement further embeds ideological conformity as a hiring criterion. The separate verification process imposed on indigenous-identified candidates singles out a group of applicants for additional scrutiny based on their stated identity, treating them differently from all other applicants. This approach reinforces the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories rather than recognising each person's singular worth and evaluating them as an individual.

quotes.text: we are particularly interested in applications from members of historically disadvantaged and marginalized groups including: First Nations, Métis and Inuit peoples*, Indigenous peoples of North America, Black-identified persons, other racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and those who identify as women and/or 2SLGBTQ+.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly names demographic groups that the university gives preferential consideration to, making skin colour, ethnicity, disability, sex, and sexual orientation relevant factors in the hiring decision rather than treating all candidates as individuals.

quotes.text: Applicants who self-identify as First Nations, Métis, Inuit peoples, or Indigenous peoples of North America will be invited to complete an affirmation process at TMU. This process involves candidates providing relevant documentation to support the claim of Indigenous identity and participating in discussions with the University's Indigenous Human Resources Lead in order to share relevant information about their lived experience.
quotes.rationale: This passage reveals that a subset of applicants identified by ethnicity is subjected to a separate, more burdensome vetting process not required of other candidates, treating them differently based on demographic identity.

quotes.text: Candidates who belong to one or more of the equity-deserving groups recognized at TMU are welcome to connect with Debbie Thompson, Executive Director, Office of the Vice-President, Equity and Community Inclusion.
quotes.rationale: The posting directs applicants from designated demographic groups to a separate institutional contact, reinforcing the two-tier approach to candidate engagement based on group membership.

incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa's job posting for a Canada Research Chair in Neuromuscular Disease states that applications from members of designated demographic groups will be prioritised and assessed first, ahead of other qualified candidates.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Ottawa posted a job advertisement on May 20, 2026 for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Neuromuscular Disease, based in the Faculty of Medicine's Department of Medicine. The posting explicitly establishes a preferential hiring process in which applications from members of four designated groups — described as indigenous peoples, racialized individuals, persons with disabilities, and women and gender equity-seeking groups — are to be assessed before applications from all other candidates. The posting states this process is intended to increase the representation of members of these groups in Canada Research Chair positions. All applicants are required to complete a self-identification questionnaire to facilitate this sorting. The result is a structured two-tier application process in which a candidate's demographic background, rather than their qualifications and ability alone, determines the order and priority in which their application is reviewed.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are placed at a formal disadvantage in the hiring process based solely on their demographic characteristics. The institution evaluates applicants not as individuals with distinct abilities and research records, but first through the lens of which demographic category they belong to. This practice treats people as representatives of groups rather than as individuals of singular worth, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect merit and demonstrated ability. Candidates who do not belong to the designated groups receive less favourable treatment regardless of the quality of their application, which normalises the use of demographic proxies as a basis for institutional decision-making.

quotes.text: As part of this preferential hiring process, applications from members of the four designated groups will be prioritized and assessed first for the vacancy. The purpose of this measure is to increase the representation and contribution of members of the four designated groups in the allocation of Canada Research Chair positions.
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement establishing that demographic group membership determines the order in which applications are assessed, directly placing some candidates ahead of others on the basis of identity rather than qualifications.

quotes.text: we strongly encourage applications from skilled and experienced members of the four designated groups such as Indigenous Peoples, racialized individuals, persons with disabilities, women and gender equity-seeking groups, as defined in the CRC Program method for establishing equity targets and the Employment Equity Act
quotes.rationale: Identifies the specific demographic categories used to classify applicants and trigger the preferential assessment process.

quotes.text: All candidates are asked to complete the confidential self-identification questionnaire during the application process online.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that applicants are formally required to disclose their demographic identity as part of the application, enabling the institution to sort candidates by group membership.

incidents.short_description: The Université d'Ottawa posted a Canada Research Chair position with an explicit preferential hiring policy that prioritises candidates from designated groups — including people sorted by ethnicity, disability status, and sex — over other equally or more qualified applicants.
incidents.description_of_event: On 20 May 2026, the Université d'Ottawa posted a job listing for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Neuromuscular Diseases. The posting includes an explicit preferential hiring provision stating that applications from members of four designated groups — indigenous peoples, people categorised as racialised, people with disabilities, and women and gender-equity-seeking groups — will be prioritised and evaluated first for the position. The stated purpose is to increase the representation of these groups among Canada Research Chair holders. All candidates are required to complete a confidential self-identification questionnaire at the time of application. The posting makes clear that belonging to one of these demographic categories is a decisive factor in how candidacies are ordered and evaluated, placing demographic identity ahead of individual merit in the initial screening process.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not belong to the designated groups are placed at a structural disadvantage in the hiring process, regardless of their individual abilities, research record, or demonstrated merit. The university evaluates applicants first through the lens of demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique qualifications. This sends a clear institutional message that a person's group membership determines their standing in a competition that should be decided on the strength of each individual's contributions. It entrenches the practice of sorting people into demographic categories as a precondition for fair consideration, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits. Highly qualified researchers who fall outside the designated groups are passed over in favour of candidates evaluated later only if no suitable designated-group candidate is found, regardless of comparative individual excellence.

quotes.text: Dans le cadre de ce processus d'embauche préférentielle, les candidatures des membres des quatre groupes désignés seront priorisées et évaluées en premier pour le poste à combler. Le but de cette mesure est d'augmenter la représentation et la contribution des membres des quatre groupes désignés dans l'attribution de postes de Chaires de recherche du Canada.
quotes.rationale: This passage is the core of the incident: it explicitly states that candidates from designated demographic groups will be prioritised and evaluated first, with the explicit goal of increasing group representation. This is the preferential hiring mechanism that elevates demographic category over individual merit.

quotes.text: nous encourageons fortement les candidatures des membres compétents et expérimentés des quatre groupes désignés (tels que les Autochtones, les personnes racisées, les personnes en situation de handicap, les femmes et les groupes en quête d'équité de genre, telles que définies dans la méthode d'établissement des cibles en matière d'équité et la Loi sur l'équité en matière d'emploi)
quotes.rationale: This passage defines the specific demographic categories used to sort and prioritise candidates, making explicit which immutable or protected characteristics determine a candidate's priority status in the process.

quotes.text: Toutes les personnes qui souhaitent être considérées pour ce poste doivent remplir le questionnaire confidentiel d'auto-identification au moment de soumettre leur candidature en ligne.
quotes.rationale: All applicants are required to self-identify by demographic category as a compulsory step in the application. This operationalises the demographic sorting mechanism described elsewhere in the posting.

incidents.short_description: The University of Victoria's job posting for an Assistant Professor in Terrestrial Plant Ecology requires applicants to submit a diversity and inclusion statement, and actively encourages applications from people based on their membership in demographic groups rather than their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Victoria posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Terrestrial Plant Ecology on May 20, 2026. The posting requires each applicant to submit a one-page statement on diversity and inclusion describing their experience and plans for working with a diverse group of students, staff, and faculty. This statement is listed as a mandatory component of the application package alongside the CV and research statement, meaning all candidates are screened in part on their attitudes toward demographic classification. The posting also explicitly encourages applications from people based on their membership in groups defined by demographic characteristics, framing this as an institutional value. Candidates are additionally expected to demonstrate the ability to 'address equity, diversity and inclusion' as part of their qualifications. These requirements embed demographic considerations into the evaluation of individual candidates for an academic science position.
incidents.expected_harm: Requiring all candidates to submit a statement on their commitment to demographic classification as a condition of employment screens out individuals who may be outstanding scientists but who do not share or wish to publicly affirm the institution's ideological framing. Candidates are evaluated not solely on their scientific ability, teaching skill, and research potential, but also on their expressed views about sorting people into demographic categories. This reduces academic hiring to something other than a pure assessment of individual merit and ability, and sends a signal to prospective applicants that adherence to a particular ideological framework is a prerequisite for employment at the university.

incidents.institution_name: BC Ministry of Environment and Parks
incidents.short_description: The BC provincial government announced closure dates for Joffre Lakes Park that are shorter than those requested by the Lil'wat Nation, restricting public access for certain periods while denying the Nation's fuller closure requests. The dispute highlights how governments manage park access based on group membership and ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: Pipi7íyekw/Joffre Lakes Park in British Columbia has been subject to annual closure periods that restrict public access to allow the Lil'wat Nation and N'Quatqua First Nation to carry out traditional activities on land they describe as unceded territory. In 2026, the province announced the park would be closed to recreational visitors for 31 days, covering June 20–27 and September 8–30. However, the Lil'wat Nation had announced its own closure dates totalling 75 days, spanning April 19 to May 11, June 20 to June 27, and August 23 to October 5. The province confirmed that the requested spring closure dates, which had been honoured in the two previous years, would not be implemented in 2026. The province stated through Minister Tamara Davidson on May 19 in the BC Legislature that it had sought to balance different interests, including public access and time for cultural activities. The Lil'wat Nation's political chief, Dean Nelson, stated that the closure dates were not requests but required reconnection periods, and that the province had announced its shorter dates without any engagement or discussion with the Nation. The Nation has called on the province to immediately recognize and implement its dates, and requested a response by June 2. In 2025, a similar dispute led to a ceremony blocking entry on Highway 99 near Pemberton. Access to the park for ordinary members of the public is determined, in part, by decisions made on behalf of specific ethnic and national groups rather than on the basis of individual circumstances or needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Members of the public are denied access to a popular provincial park based on decisions made on behalf of specific ethnic and national groups, rather than on individual needs or circumstances. During closure periods, all Canadians regardless of their own background are excluded from the park, while access is reserved for members of particular groups. This creates a system of group-based access to public land. The dispute also reflects ongoing institutional tension between the provincial government and First Nation governments over authority to manage public spaces, which is resolved through group-membership frameworks rather than individual rights. Citizens who wish to access the park have no individual recourse — their access is determined entirely by their demographic group membership or exclusion from a designated group.

quotes.text: These dates were not requests. They are the reconnection periods that Líl̓wat requires so our people can harvest, hold ceremony, teach our children on the land, and carry out our stewardship responsibilities in Pipi7íyekw.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that the Lil'wat Nation's position is that access to the park should be exclusively reserved for members of their group during certain periods, framing this as a group entitlement rather than an individual or universal public interest.

quotes.text: We had collaborated an agreement that balanced the different interests that we're looking at providing public access to the parks by the public and then also providing time for the land to regenerate, and also for the two nations to have time to do cultural activities.
quotes.rationale: The minister's statement confirms the provincial government is managing public park access partly by reserving periods for specific ethnic and national groups, reflecting a policy of group-based access to public resources.

incidents.institution_name: Wilfrid Laurier University – Lazaridis School of Business & Economics
incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University's Lazaridis School of Business & Economics posted a faculty job listing that directs candidates to self-identify based on demographic categories — including race, disability, and sexual and gender identity — in order to receive preferential consideration under an employment equity policy.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 19, 2026, Wilfrid Laurier University's Lazaridis School of Business & Economics advertised two one-year term positions at the rank of Lecturer or Assistant Professor in Organizational Behaviour and Human Resource Management. The job posting states that the university is committed to increasing the diversity of faculty and staff, and specifically welcomes applications from candidates who identify as indigenous, racialized, having disabilities, and from persons of any minority sexual and gender identities. The posting further states that members of the designated groups must self-identify to be considered for employment equity, and directs candidates to do so confidentially to a named university official. Candidates are sorted into distinct categories based on immutable or personal identity characteristics, and those who fall into designated groups are given preferential consideration in the hiring process. The posting evaluates applicants not solely on their qualifications, teaching record, and abilities, but on whether they belong to particular demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated not solely on their individual merits — their teaching record, academic qualifications, and demonstrated abilities — but on whether they belong to designated demographic categories. This means applicants who do not self-identify within those categories receive less favourable consideration regardless of their individual qualifications. The practice reduces people to demographic labels, treating their identity group membership as a criterion for employment rather than recognising them as distinct individuals whose abilities and character should determine their candidacy. It also creates a hiring environment in which employees are appointed partly on the basis of demographic proxies rather than their capacity to perform the role, undermining institutional trust and the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's job posting for a senior academic and clinical leadership position encourages applications from candidates based on demographic characteristics, and implements an employment equity program that asks members of designated groups to self-identify.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a position for Associate or Full Professor (Clinical) and Division Director of the Harvey E. Beardmore Division of General Surgery in the Department of Pediatric Surgery, advertised on May 19, 2026. The posting includes a diversity and inclusion section that explicitly lists demographic categories — including skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — as characteristics the university specifically seeks in applicants. The university also states it implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated groups to self-identify during the application process. These practices direct the evaluation of candidates, at least in part, toward demographic characteristics rather than purely on individual qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated experience.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution asks candidates to self-identify based on demographic characteristics and signals that membership in designated groups is a factor in hiring, it reduces individuals to their demographic category rather than evaluating them solely on their character, abilities, and experience. Candidates who do not belong to listed groups are disadvantaged not because of any deficiency in their qualifications but because of their immutable traits. This practice normalises the institutional sorting of individuals by demographic proxies and undermines the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits and unique qualities.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's job posting for a senior academic position in paediatric surgery explicitly invites applicants to declare membership in designated groups, signalling preferential consideration based on demographic characteristics rather than individual merit alone.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a full-time faculty position on 19 May 2026 for an Associate or Full Professor (Clinical) and Division Director of the Harvey E. Beardmore Division of General Surgery within the Department of Paediatric Surgery, based at the Montreal Children's Hospital. The posting includes a standard equity, diversity and inclusion section in which the university states it operates an employment equity programme and encourages members of designated groups — specifically listing people who are racialised or visible minorities, women, indigenous persons, people with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and people of any sexual orientation or gender identity — to indicate their membership in these groups in their application. The posting further states that McGill "strongly welcomes" applications from these groups. This signals to applicants and the hiring committee that demographic characteristics are a factor in the evaluation of candidates, rather than assessing each person solely on their qualifications, clinical expertise, research record, and leadership abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed not only on their individual abilities, clinical qualifications, and leadership record but also on which demographic categories they belong to. This reduces people to proxies for group membership rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the listed groups face a structural disadvantage. The practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable traits and conditions advancement in academic medicine on demographic self-identification, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be recognised and evaluated on their own merits.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University's job posting for a French Language Services Librarian explicitly solicits applications from named demographic groups and includes a language-requirement exemption that applies only to people of certain ethnic backgrounds. The posting treats applicants differently based on their demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event:
On May 19, 2026, Laurentian University posted a one-year limited-term librarian position on University Affairs. The posting states that applications are "encouraged from members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions." This language explicitly invites applicants based on sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than treating each applicant as an individual evaluated on their qualifications, character, and abilities.

Additionally, the posting references Laurentian University's bilingualism policy (Section 7.3.b), which provides a specific provision regarding the French-language requirement for persons who self-identify as First Nations, Métis or Inuit. This means the core language qualification for the role is applied differently depending on an applicant's ethnic background, creating a formal distinction in how candidates are assessed based on their ancestry rather than their individual circumstances or demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are sorted and encouraged or discouraged based on their demographic characteristics — skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation — rather than being evaluated purely on their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. Applicants who do not belong to the named groups receive an implicit signal that their applications are less welcome, reducing their opportunity to compete on equal footing. The language-requirement exemption tied to ethnic self-identification means two candidates with identical language abilities are assessed under different standards depending on their ancestry, undermining the principle that individuals deserve evaluation based on their own merits. This institutional practice normalizes the categorization of people by demographic proxies and reinforces the idea that a person's immutable traits — rather than their unique qualities — determine their standing in a hiring process.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University's job posting for a French-language librarian position explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories including sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 19, 2026, Laurentian University posted a one-year limited-term librarian position on University Affairs, providing French-language library services for the Faculty of Education and Health and the Faculty of Arts. The posting states that Laurentian University encourages applications from members of communities seeking equity, specifically naming women, racialized persons, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and people of all sexual orientations and gender identities. The university's bilingualism policy also contains a separate provision that modifies the language requirement specifically for persons who identify as First Nations, Métis, or Inuit. By structuring the application process around these demographic categories, the institution signals that membership in a particular group — defined by sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, or sexual orientation — is a relevant consideration in hiring, rather than evaluating each candidate purely on their individual abilities, qualifications, and demonstrated experience.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual merits, abilities, and qualifications. This approach reduces people to group membership rather than recognising their singular worth as individuals. It creates a hiring environment where a person's skin colour, sex, disability status, or sexual orientation influences institutional decisions, which undermines the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own character and demonstrated abilities. It also signals to all prospective applicants — including those who do not belong to the named groups — that demographic identity is a factor in how the institution will regard them, undermining a fair and impartial process.

incidents.short_description: The University of Victoria posted a senior research chair position that actively encourages applications based on group membership rather than individual merit alone. The posting signals that demographic background is a factor in the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Victoria's Faculty of Health posted a job listing on May 19, 2026 for an Aspiration Research Chair in Mental Health, a senior academic and research leadership position. The posting includes an equity statement that declares UVic 'actively encourages applications from members of historically and systemically marginalized groups.' This framing directs the institution's recruitment approach toward selecting candidates partly on the basis of their membership in demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research record, and professional merit. The position itself carries significant responsibilities including leading internationally recognized research, building large-scale partnerships, and mentoring faculty and students. The posting does not restrict the position to any specific demographic group, but the explicit encouragement of applications based on group membership signals that demographic identity is treated as a relevant factor in the selection process.
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions signal that demographic group membership is a relevant consideration in hiring, they evaluate candidates as representatives of categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualifications and abilities. Researchers who belong to groups not named as preferred face a disadvantaged position regardless of their individual merit. This practice also undermines confidence in the appointee, as colleagues and the public have reason to question whether the successful candidate was selected purely on the strength of their research record and leadership capacity. It normalizes the use of immutable characteristics as proxies for evaluating a person's suitability, rather than assessing each candidate on their demonstrated excellence and individual circumstances.

incidents.short_description: Université de Montréal's job posting for a veterinary medicine professor explicitly invites applications from people based on their sex, indigeneity, and other demographic categories under its Equal Access to Employment Program, giving these groups preferential consideration in hiring.
incidents.description_of_event: Université de Montréal posted a position for an Assistant or Associate Professor in Veterinary Medicine (Care for Small Animals) at its Rimouski campus on May 15, 2026. The posting's diversity and inclusion section states that through its Equal Access to Employment Program, the university invites women, indigenous peoples, visible minorities, ethnic minorities, and people with disabilities to self-identify and apply, signalling that membership in these demographic categories is a factor in the recruitment process. Applicants are encouraged to complete a self-identification questionnaire. The framing of the program makes clear that demographic characteristics are being used as a basis for preferential consideration, rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications, abilities, and individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications and abilities. This reduces the hiring process to one where a person's sex, ancestry, or other immutable traits influence their chances of being selected, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. Qualified individuals who do not belong to the named groups face a disadvantage that has nothing to do with their competence or suitability for the role. The practice also signals to all applicants that the institution views people primarily through demographic categories, normalizing the reduction of individuals to group identities in professional contexts.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan's job posting for an Assistant Professor in Soil Science explicitly prioritizes candidates based on indigenous ancestry, and includes a formal institutional process to verify and sort applicants by this demographic characteristic.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Saskatchewan posted a job advertisement on May 15, 2026 for an Assistant Professor and Chair position in the Department of Soil Science. Within the posting, the university states that it is 'proud to support career opportunities for Indigenous peoples to reflect the community we serve.' The posting further states that the university 'values the unique perspective that Indigenous employees provide' and describes a formal institutional verification process — governed by the deybwewin | taapwaywin | tapwewin: Indigenous Truth policy and Standing Committee — through which candidates who assert indigenous ancestry must submit documentation to confirm membership or citizenship status. This verification process is a formal mechanism by which the institution categorizes and distinguishes applicants based on ancestry and heritage. The posting also requires applicants to submit an Equity, Diversity and Inclusion statement as part of their application package. While the posting states that all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, the explicit institutional commitment to preferring candidates based on ancestral background, combined with a formal bureaucratic process for verifying that ancestry, signals that an applicant's demographic background is treated as a meaningful factor in the hiring decision.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed and sorted in part based on their ancestry rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and character. Candidates who do not belong to the preferred ancestral group face a structural disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their individual merit. The formal verification mechanism normalizes institutional categorization of people by ancestry, embedding demographic sorting into the university's standard employment processes. This reduces individuals to their heritage rather than evaluating them as unique people with distinct skills and contributions to offer.

incidents.short_description: McMaster University's job posting for a tenure-track professor position explicitly lists preferred demographic groups and requires applicants to submit a statement on advancing equity, diversity and inclusion, giving hiring advantages based on personal characteristics rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: McMaster University posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in the Department of Psychology, Neuroscience & Behaviour on May 15, 2026. The posting states that the university "especially welcomes applications" from people belonging to specific groups defined by their ancestry, skin colour, disability status, sex, and sexual orientation. Applicants are also required to submit a two-page statement of experience and plans for advancing equity, diversity and inclusion as a mandatory component of the application package. The posting frames the diversity of the workforce as central to the institution's mission and frames the hiring process explicitly around expanding representation across demographic categories. The requirement that applicants demonstrate commitment to and experience with demographic equity initiatives means that candidates are evaluated not only on their scientific qualifications but on their orientation toward sorting people by group membership.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed in part based on their demographic background and their willingness to advance group-based categorization, rather than solely on their scientific qualifications, teaching ability, and individual merit. This approach disadvantages qualified researchers who do not belong to the preferred demographic groups or who do not share the institutional framing around demographic categorization, regardless of their actual abilities. It embeds demographic sorting into the hiring process, normalizing the idea that a person's group membership is a relevant factor in professional evaluation. The mandatory equity statement requirement further compels applicants to signal ideological alignment as a condition of being considered, which restricts open intellectual discourse and penalizes those who may hold different views on how to address inequality.

incidents.institution_name: Université de Montréal – Faculté de médecine vétérinaire
incidents.short_description: The Université de Montréal's Faculty of Veterinary Medicine posted a faculty position that explicitly invites candidates to identify by sex, ethnicity, and disability status as part of an employment equity programme, giving preference in hiring to people based on these demographic categories rather than evaluating all candidates solely on their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The Université de Montréal's Faculty of Veterinary Medicine posted a faculty position (Assistant or Associate Professor, companion animal care) on 15 May 2026, to be filled at the Rimouski campus. The posting includes an equity, diversity and inclusion section that formally invites women, indigenous peoples, visible minorities, ethnic minorities, and persons with disabilities to self-identify and submit their candidacy through the university's employment equity programme (Programme d'accès à l'égalité en emploi – PAÉE). Candidates are encouraged to complete a voluntary self-identification questionnaire. The university states that the personal information collected will be used to improve its practices. By structuring its recruitment process around these demographic categories, the institution signals that a candidate's demographic identity is a relevant and positive factor in the selection process, rather than evaluating each applicant purely on their skills, experience, and scholarly merit.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution formally channels certain applicants into a preferred category based on their sex, ancestry, or disability status, it reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than assessing them on their unique qualifications and character. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the designated groups face a structural disadvantage that has nothing to do with their abilities. Candidates who do belong to those groups are implicitly defined by their demographic characteristics rather than their individual merits, which undermines the recognition of each person's singular worth. The practice normalises the sorting of people into demographic categories as a routine institutional function, entrenching division rather than fostering a shared academic community where advancement reflects demonstrated ability and individual circumstances.

incidents.short_description: Université TÉLUQ's job posting for a labour law professor position explicitly singles out candidates based on sex, indigenous identity, visible minority status, ethnic minority status, and disability, signalling that these demographic characteristics are factors in the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: Université TÉLUQ posted a regular faculty position in labour law on May 15, 2026, advertised through University Affairs. The posting includes a standard equity clause that goes beyond encouraging all qualified applicants and instead explicitly names demographic categories — women, indigenous people, members of visible minorities, members of ethnic minorities, and people with disabilities — as groups the university particularly invites to apply. The university states that it subscribes to employment equity and equal access programs, and declares its commitment to becoming a leader in diversity, equity, and inclusion. The posting frames these commitments as central to its pursuit of research excellence. Rather than evaluating candidates purely on qualifications, the posting signals that demographic identity is a relevant factor in the recruitment process.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution publicly identifies certain demographic groups as preferred candidates, it introduces demographic categorisation into what should be an individual merit-based evaluation. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the named groups receive a clear signal that their applications are less valued, not because of their abilities or character, but because of who they are. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their individual merits and reduces people to proxies for demographic categories rather than treating them as distinct individuals. It also sets a precedent within the institution that sorting people by immutable traits is consistent with the pursuit of excellence.

incidents.short_description: Athabasca University's job posting for an accounting faculty position explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic characteristics including sex, disability, sexual orientation, and gender identity, rather than treating all applicants as individuals evaluated on their qualifications alone.
incidents.description_of_event: Athabasca University posted a tenure-track Assistant/Associate Professor position in Accounting on May 15, 2026, through University Affairs. The posting includes a section titled 'Considerations in applying' that explicitly encourages applications from people defined by their sex, disability status, sexual orientation, gender identity and expression, and membership in so-called equity-deserving groups. The posting singles out these demographic categories as the basis for encouraging participation, framing candidates through group membership rather than their individual qualifications, abilities, or character. The posting also notes that "life circumstances such as illness, disability, family and community responsibilities" should be taken into consideration during the hiring process, which in itself is a reasonable accommodation provision. However, the solicitation of applicants by demographic category signals that the university applies group-based considerations to its hiring decisions rather than evaluating each person solely as an individual.
incidents.expected_harm: When a university publicly signals that it encourages applications from people based on demographic categories, it communicates to all candidates — those in the listed groups and those not — that group membership is a relevant factor in hiring. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their character, abilities, and demonstrated potential. Candidates not belonging to the listed groups receive the implicit message that they are less welcome or less preferred. Candidates who do belong to the listed groups may have their accomplishments overshadowed by their demographic identity. The practice normalizes the institutional habit of sorting individuals into categories rather than recognising each person's singular worth, and it sets a precedent that advancement in academic careers can be influenced by immutable or personal characteristics rather than merit.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University's job posting for a Veterinary Physiology faculty position states a preference for candidates based on their demographic identity and lived experience as a member of designated groups, rather than evaluating applicants solely on their qualifications, abilities, and character.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University posted a tenure-track or tenured faculty position in Veterinary Physiology on May 14, 2026, for its newly established Department of Veterinary Science at the Thunder Bay campus. The posting included standard academic qualifications such as a PhD, postdoctoral experience, a record of publication, and teaching ability. However, it also explicitly stated a preference for candidates based on their demographic identity and membership in designated groups. The posting stated that the university prefers candidates with 'knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience' in relation to being a member of demographic categories, and that 'lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred.' Candidates were encouraged to self-identify as members of under-represented groups. The posting further listed experience working with indigenous or racialized communities, or members of other equity-deserving groups, as a 'strong asset.' In this way, the institution incorporated demographic identity as a positive qualification factor, treating membership in certain groups as a credential that tips the scales in favour of a candidate, independent of their individual abilities, achievements, or professional merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed in part on their demographic identity and membership in designated groups rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups, or who choose not to self-identify, face a structural disadvantage in the hiring process despite potentially being the most qualified. This approach reduces individuals to proxies for their demographic category, undermining the principle that each person deserves evaluation based on their unique merits. It also normalises institutional sorting of applicants by immutable characteristics, entrenching the practice of treating demographic identity as a qualification rather than recognising the singular worth of each candidate.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University's job posting for a Veterinary Comparative Anatomy faculty position explicitly prefers candidates based on their membership in demographic groups and lived experience tied to immutable characteristics, rather than evaluating all applicants on their qualifications and abilities alone.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University posted a tenure-track or tenured faculty position in Veterinary Comparative Anatomy at its Thunder Bay campus on May 14, 2026. The posting states that the university prefers candidates with 'knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience' associated with being from under-represented groups, and that 'a lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred.' Candidates are encouraged to self-identify based on demographic characteristics including being a woman, a 'racialized person,' an indigenous person, or a person with a disability. The posting further states that experience working with indigenous or 'racialized communities' and members of other 'equity-deserving groups' is considered 'a strong asset.' This framing effectively treats a candidate's demographic identity and associated lived experience as a qualification in its own right, placing applicants who belong to designated demographic groups in a preferred position over equally or better qualified candidates who do not. The posting also requires applicants to submit a statement outlining their 'commitment to developing an equitable, diverse, and inclusive teaching and research environment,' making ideological alignment a formal requirement of the application.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are disadvantaged in the hiring process based on their demographic characteristics rather than their professional abilities, expertise, and record of achievement. The practice reduces individuals to their demographic group membership, treating immutable traits as qualifications. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their character, skills, and demonstrated abilities. Candidates who do not belong to designated groups face a structurally inferior position in the competition regardless of the quality of their work, while candidates who do belong to those groups are assessed in part on characteristics they were born with rather than what they have accomplished. The requirement to affirm a specific ideological commitment further narrows the field by excluding candidates who may hold different but reasonable views on how to foster a respectful academic environment.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University's job posting for a veterinary science faculty position states a preference for candidates based on demographic background and lived experience tied to identity categories, treating these characteristics as hiring assets rather than evaluating candidates purely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University posted a tenure-track or tenured faculty position in Veterinary Anatomic Pathology on May 14, 2026. The posting states that the university 'prefers candidates with the knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience' associated with being from an under-represented group. It further states that 'a lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred,' referring to identity-based demographic categories including being a woman, a person with a disability, or a member of a racialized or indigenous community. Candidates are encouraged to self-identify their demographic membership. The posting also requires applicants to submit a one-page statement outlining their 'commitment to developing an equitable, diverse, and inclusive teaching and research environment,' making ideological alignment with a particular institutional framework a formal requirement of the application. While the posting notes that all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, the explicit preference for candidates based on demographic identity characteristics means that two equally qualified candidates are treated differently based on who they are rather than what they can do.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated in part based on their demographic identity rather than solely on their character, qualifications, and professional abilities. Candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic categories, or who decline to self-identify, face a structural disadvantage in the hiring process despite being fully qualified. This approach reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than recognising them as distinct people with unique skills and experiences. It also requires candidates to signal ideological alignment with a specific institutional framework as a condition of being considered, constraining open and honest professional expression. Over time, hiring practices that treat demographic membership as a qualification skew academic environments away from the principle that people deserve to be judged on their individual merits.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University posted a faculty position in its Department of Veterinary Science that explicitly prefers candidates from certain demographic groups and states a preference for candidates whose qualifications derive from their demographic background rather than solely from their professional abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University posted a tenure-track or tenured faculty position in Large Animal and Rural Community Veterinary Practice on May 14, 2026. The posting includes a section indicating that the university prefers candidates with 'knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience' connected to membership in designated demographic groups, including people categorised by skin colour, indigenous identity, disability, and sex. The posting also states that experience working with people categorised by these demographic characteristics 'is a strong asset,' and that 'a lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred.' This means the university is treating a candidate's demographic background as a qualification in itself, placing it on par with or above professional credentials, research output, and teaching ability. The posting invites candidates to self-identify as members of designated groups, and frames demographic background as a source of preferred professional competencies. This approach evaluates candidates in part based on their demographic identity rather than solely on their individual abilities, experience, and character.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not belong to the designated demographic groups are placed at a disadvantage in the hiring process despite their individual merits. The institution sends a message that a person's demographic background determines part of their professional value, reducing individuals to their group membership rather than recognising them for their unique abilities and achievements. This approach normalises the practice of sorting job applicants by demographic categories and treating those categories as proxies for desirable competencies, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It also risks discouraging highly qualified individuals from applying if they do not see themselves reflected in the preferred demographic categories described in the posting.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University's job posting for a faculty position in veterinary science states a preference for candidates based on their membership in demographic groups, favouring applicants whose qualifications include lived experience as a member of a categorised group rather than evaluating all candidates solely on professional merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University posted a tenure-track or tenured faculty position in Food Animal Veterinary Practice and Herd Health within its newly established Department of Veterinary Science at the Thunder Bay campus. The posting, dated May 14, 2026, included standard academic qualifications such as a veterinary degree, doctoral-level post-graduate training, and teaching and research experience. However, the posting also stated that the university prefers candidates with 'knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience' as a member of an under-represented group, and explicitly states that 'a lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred.' The posting encourages candidates to self-identify if they belong to a designated demographic category — including women, racialized persons, indigenous people, and persons with disabilities — and treats this self-identification as an asset in the hiring process. This means that two candidates with otherwise equivalent professional qualifications are evaluated differently based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual abilities, expertise, and demonstrated performance.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for this faculty position are assessed in part on the basis of their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications and professional merit. This means that a person's skin colour, sex, disability status, or other immutable traits influence hiring decisions, reducing individuals to demographic proxies. Qualified candidates who do not belong to a designated group face a structural disadvantage that has nothing to do with their ability to teach veterinary medicine or conduct research. The practice normalises the institutional habit of categorising and sorting people based on group membership, which undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It also sends a message to students and staff that demographic identity is a relevant criterion for professional advancement in academia.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University's job posting for a veterinary faculty position explicitly preferences candidates based on demographic characteristics, including lived experience tied to identity, and encourages self-identification to receive preferential treatment in hiring.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University posted a tenure-track or tenured faculty position in Companion Animal Veterinary Practice on May 14, 2026. The posting states the university "prefers candidates with the knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience" associated with being from an under-represented group. It explicitly encourages applicants to self-identify if they belong to demographic categories — including women, people of particular ethnic backgrounds, indigenous people, and persons with disabilities — and states that "lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred." Applicants are also required to submit a written statement outlining their "understanding and commitment to developing an equitable, diverse, and inclusive teaching and research environment," making ideological alignment a formal component of the application. The posting further characterizes experience working with particular communities, defined by their demographic identity, as "a strong asset." Together, these requirements and stated preferences treat demographic background and identity affiliation as substantive hiring qualifications rather than evaluating candidates solely on their academic credentials, research ability, and teaching effectiveness.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are disadvantaged or excluded from consideration based on their demographic characteristics rather than their individual abilities, qualifications, and merit. This approach reduces applicants to group membership rather than evaluating them as individuals, undermining the principle that people deserve to be assessed on their character, competencies, and demonstrated achievements. The requirement that applicants articulate commitment to a specific institutional ideology as part of the application process introduces a conformity test that restricts open intellectual inquiry and independent thought. Candidates who do not belong to favoured demographic groups face a structural disadvantage regardless of their professional qualifications, while those who do belong to such groups are evaluated in part on their identity rather than their individual accomplishments.

incidents.institution_name: University of Calgary – Haskayne School of Business
incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary's Haskayne School of Business posted a faculty position that explicitly encourages applications from people based on demographic categories and requires all applicants to submit a statement on equity, diversity and inclusion. This practice treats demographic characteristics as relevant hiring criteria and imposes an ideological requirement on candidates.
incidents.description_of_event: The Haskayne School of Business at the University of Calgary posted a job for an Assistant Professor (Teaching) in Real Estate, with a posting date of May 14, 2026. The application package requires all candidates to submit a statement on equity, diversity and inclusion alongside their cover letter, curriculum vitae, and teaching philosophy. The posting also explicitly singles out applicants who belong to designated demographic groups — women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible or racialized minorities, and people of diverse sexual orientations and gender identities — and encourages them to apply. This framing treats demographic characteristics as factors relevant to the hiring process rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications, teaching ability, and professional experience. The requirement that all candidates produce a written statement on equity, diversity and inclusion functions as an ideological screening tool, as candidates whose views do not align with the institution's stated commitments face a structural disadvantage in the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics and their willingness to affirm a particular ideological framework, rather than solely on their individual abilities, teaching effectiveness, and professional expertise. The mandatory equity, diversity and inclusion statement requirement filters out candidates based on their views, undermining open and reasoned discourse and the principle that people should be assessed on their individual merits. This practice reduces individuals to demographic proxies and entrenches the idea that a person's immutable characteristics are relevant qualifications for academic employment.

incidents.institution_name: University of Victoria, School of Public Health and Social Policy
incidents.short_description: The University of Victoria posted a faculty position requiring applicants to demonstrate 'anti-racist approaches to teaching' and actively encouraged applications based on demographic background rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Victoria's School of Public Health and Social Policy posted a job advertisement on May 14, 2026, for a three-year term Assistant Teaching Professor position. The posting lists among its formal requirements that candidates demonstrate 'evidence of social justice and/or anti-racist approaches to teaching and learning.' Applicants are also asked to submit a 'statement of diversity knowledge, experience and skills' as part of their application package. The posting states that the university 'actively encourages applications from members of historically and systemically marginalized groups,' framing the hiring process in terms of demographic group membership rather than individual qualifications, abilities, and character. These requirements combine ideological screening criteria with demographic preferences in the formal hiring process for a publicly funded academic institution.
incidents.expected_harm: Requiring applicants to demonstrate adherence to particular ideological frameworks — such as anti-racist approaches — as a formal job requirement excludes candidates based on their beliefs and viewpoints rather than their teaching ability or scholarly competence. This narrows the intellectual environment of the institution and compromises open and reasoned academic discourse. Actively encouraging applications based on demographic group membership rather than individual merit means qualified candidates are evaluated, at least in part, through the lens of their background rather than their unique abilities and character. Together, these practices undermine the principle that individuals deserve to be assessed on their own merits, and entrench a hiring culture in which demographic categories and ideological conformity take precedence over individual excellence.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University's job posting for a faculty position in Natural Resources Management explicitly prefers candidates based on demographic categories including skin colour and ethnicity, and states that lived experience as a member of certain groups is preferred over other qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Forest Operations and Forest Management at its Thunder Bay Campus on May 14, 2026. The job posting contained language stating that the university prefers candidates with 'knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience' tied to membership in specific demographic groups, and that 'lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred.' The posting explicitly categorises prospective applicants by skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sex, and expresses a preference for candidates whose qualifications are understood through those demographic lenses. The university also states it has an Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Action Plan with a goal to increase 'representation of under-represented groups' and encourages candidates to self-identify based on demographic characteristics. Rather than evaluating applicants based on their academic qualifications, teaching ability, and professional experience alone, the posting makes demographic group membership a stated hiring preference.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are disadvantaged in the hiring process because the university evaluates candidates partly on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual abilities, qualifications, and demonstrated performance. This reduces individuals to representatives of demographic categories rather than recognising them as unique people with distinct skills and experience. Candidates who do not belong to the preferred demographic groups face a structural disadvantage regardless of their professional merit. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by immutable traits and entrenches the idea that a person's demographic background is a meaningful proxy for the value they bring to an academic role.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University's Faculty of Natural Resources Management posted a tenure-track position that explicitly ranks applicants by Indigenous identity, directing that only people identified as first nations, métis, or inuit from Canada will be considered as high-ranking candidates, with all others evaluated only if no suitable Indigenous candidate is found.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in the Faculty of Natural Resources Management on May 14, 2026, with an application deadline of May 29, 2026. The posting explicitly states that high-ranking applicants will be first nations, métis, and inuit from Canada, citing the Employment Equity Act as justification. It further states that applications from other indigenous nations will only be considered if no suitable Canadian applicant from those groups is found, and that all other applicants are implicitly ranked below candidates meeting this ethnic identity criterion. The posting also states a preference for candidates whose knowledge and competencies derive from lived experience associated with Indigenous or other designated communities. The position's research focus — Indigenous land-based knowledge and resource stewardship — does not inherently require that the candidate hold a particular ethnic identity, as the posting itself acknowledges that research experience may include relationships built in Canada or elsewhere. The institution is formally sorting applicants into tiers based on their ethnic and ancestral background rather than evaluating each person on their individual qualifications, skills, and demonstrated research capabilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are excluded from fair consideration for a publicly funded academic position based on their ethnic and ancestral identity rather than their individual abilities, knowledge, and experience. A person's capacity to conduct rigorous research on land stewardship, resource governance, and Indigenous knowledge systems is assessed not on its own merits but filtered first through the lens of the applicant's background. This institutional practice normalises the sorting of individuals into hierarchies based on immutable characteristics, sending the message that a person's ethnic heritage determines their professional worth and suitability. It forecloses opportunities for individuals who may possess deep expertise, strong community relationships, and genuine commitment to the subject matter, simply because of who they are by birth. It also undermines the integrity of the academic hiring process by substituting demographic proxies for genuine evaluation of each candidate's unique qualities and demonstrated contributions.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta's job posting for a faculty position in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic characteristics including sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates solely on their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta posted a faculty position in Engineering Education, Communication, and AI-Enabled Learning within the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering on May 14, 2026. The posting, advertised on University Affairs, is open to candidates at the rank of Assistant, Associate, or Full Professor. While the posting outlines substantive qualifications — including a PhD in a relevant field, evidence of scholarly achievement, and experience with pedagogical innovation — it concludes with a statement explicitly encouraging applications from women, first nations, métis and inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression, and others who may contribute to the diversification of ideas. This language signals to prospective applicants that their demographic characteristics are a factor the university considers in its hiring process, rather than evaluating all candidates purely on their individual abilities, character, and demonstrated qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic categories receive a clear signal that the institution views their application less favourably, regardless of their individual qualifications and merit. This practice reduces people to their demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them as individuals. It also sends a message to all applicants — including those in the encouraged groups — that group membership is a relevant credential, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own abilities and achievements. Over time, institutional hiring practices that sort candidates by demographic category entrench the idea that people's immutable traits are legitimate proxies for the value they bring, eroding a culture of individual worth and fair evaluation.

incidents.short_description: Western University is filling a Canada Research Chair position open exclusively to applicants of indigenous ancestry. All other candidates are ineligible regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University posted a job advertisement on May 14, 2026 for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Indigenous Sovereignty and Self-Determination, to be appointed in the Faculty of Law, Department of Political Science, or Department of History. The position carries a salary of $125,000–$175,000 and is a tenure-track or tenured academic appointment. The posting explicitly restricts eligibility to applicants who identify as First Nations, Métis, or Inuit, citing Section 14 of the Ontario Human Rights Code as justification. Candidates who self-identify as indigenous are also required, if shortlisted, to have that identification verified through a "relational accountability process" run by Western's Office of Indigenous Initiatives. All other applicants — regardless of their scholarly qualifications, research record, or expertise in the advertised subject areas — are excluded from consideration on the basis of their ancestry and ethnic background.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified scholars are barred from competing for a publicly funded academic position solely because of their ancestry. This reduces access to a prestigious research role and the career advancement it represents to a subset of candidates defined by descent rather than by their individual abilities, knowledge, or demonstrated excellence. The practice institutionalises the evaluation of people based on demographic categories rather than individual merit, normalising the idea that a person's ethnic background determines what roles they may hold. It also sends a signal across the academic community that ancestry is a legitimate criterion for distributing professional opportunity, entrenching division rather than fostering a shared scholarly community where individuals are recognised for their unique contributions.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan posted a senior academic leadership position that explicitly names preferred demographic groups for hiring, sorting candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating each applicant as an individual.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Saskatchewan advertised the position of Associate Vice-President Research, Medical and Health Science on May 14, 2026. The job posting states that the university is 'proud to support career opportunities for Indigenous peoples, women, persons with disabilities, members of visible minority groups, and members of 2SLGBTQIA+ communities.' A second statement in the posting reinforces a preference specifically for indigenous peoples, noting the university is 'proud to support career opportunities for Indigenous peoples to reflect the community it serves.' By explicitly naming specific demographic groups as preferred candidates, the posting frames the hiring process around demographic categories rather than the individual qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities of each applicant. All qualified candidates are nominally encouraged to apply, but the preference statements signal that certain demographic characteristics carry weight in the selection process beyond a candidate's individual merits.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for this senior role are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics — including their sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation — rather than solely on their individual qualifications and abilities. This reduces people to categorical labels at the point of assessment, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be judged on their own merits. Highly qualified individuals who do not belong to the named groups face a disadvantage that has nothing to do with their competence or character. The posting also normalises the institutional practice of sorting applicants by immutable traits, reinforcing a culture in which demographic identity, rather than individual worth, shapes access to senior leadership positions in publicly funded universities.

incidents.short_description: Thompson Rivers University's job posting for a Field Education Coordinator position explicitly identifies women, indigenous people, people categorized by skin colour, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity as preferred candidates, prioritizing demographic characteristics alongside merit in the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: Thompson Rivers University posted a tenure-track Field Education Coordinator position in its School of Social Work and Human Service on May 13, 2026, with a closing date of May 27, 2026. The position is based in Kamloops, British Columbia, and carries a salary range of $73,665.93 to $187,352.80 annually. The posting includes a diversity and inclusion commitment statement that, while framing the institution's approach as merit-based, explicitly names specific demographic groups — women, indigenous people, people categorized by skin colour (described as "Black and People of Colour"), persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity — as candidates whose applications are particularly welcomed on the basis of their demographic characteristics. The posting signals to applicants that belonging to one or more of these demographic groups is a factor in the hiring process, not merely their qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities. The substantive qualifications listed are extensive and professionally rigorous, yet the diversity commitment statement appended to the posting introduces demographic categorization as an additional layer of candidate evaluation.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated not solely as individuals with distinct qualifications, skills, and experiences, but also through the lens of demographic categories. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups receive an implicit signal that their applications are less valued, regardless of their individual merit. This approach reduces people to demographic proxies rather than recognizing their singular worth as individuals.
