It also undermines confidence in the institution's hiring process by introducing criteria that are unrelated to an individual's ability to perform the role, and normalizes the practice of sorting job applicants by immutable characteristics in a publicly funded university setting.

incidents.institution_name: University of New Brunswick (Saint John Campus)
incidents.short_description: The University of New Brunswick advertised a faculty position in youth mental health while explicitly stating its commitment to advancing applications from scholars categorised by their ethnic and racial background. The posting signals that demographic background will be a factor in candidate selection.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of New Brunswick Saint John posted a job listing on May 13, 2026 for a tenure-track Medavie Chair in Youth Mental Health at the rank of assistant or associate professor. The posting was published on the University Affairs jobs board. Within the posting, the university stated: "We are committed to advancing applications from Indigenous and racialized scholars within equity-deserving groups." This language signals that candidates will be evaluated or prioritised based on their ethnic or racial background, rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research record, and demonstrated abilities. The posting otherwise describes standard academic qualifications and a research focus on youth mental health services in New Brunswick.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are assessed through the lens of their ethnic or racial background rather than as individuals whose merit, research abilities, and character stand on their own. This sends a clear message to all applicants that their demographic characteristics will influence their chances, undermining the principle that people deserve to be evaluated based on what they do and who they are as individuals. Candidates from groups not identified in the preference language face structural disadvantage regardless of the quality of their work. The practice normalises institutions sorting people by demographic categories and entrenches the idea that a person's background, rather than their individual contributions, is a relevant criterion for academic hiring.

incidents.institution_name: University of Calgary, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine
incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary's Faculty of Veterinary Medicine explicitly encourages applications from members of designated demographic groups when hiring two assistant professors in veterinary anatomy, and requires all applicants to submit a Statement on Equity, Diversity and Inclusion.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Calgary's Faculty of Veterinary Medicine posted two full-time tenure-track Assistant Professor (Teaching) positions in Veterinary Anatomy on May 13, 2026. In the application requirements, the university states it "encourages members of the designated groups (women, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible/racialized minorities, and diverse sexual orientation and gender identities) to apply," signalling that demographic group membership is a factor in the hiring process. All applicants are required to submit a Statement on Equity, Diversity and Inclusion as one of only four permitted application attachments, making demographic orientation a formal and mandatory element of the application. The university frames this approach as removing historical barriers and enriching the campus through diversity, but the effect is to sort applicants by demographic category rather than evaluating them purely on their individual qualifications, teaching effectiveness, and scholarly contributions.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated in part on their membership in designated demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching ability, and scholarly merit. This introduces a formal preference based on sex, ancestry, disability, and sexual orientation into a competitive academic hiring process, meaning equally or more qualified candidates outside designated groups face a structural disadvantage. The mandatory Statement on Equity, Diversity and Inclusion further requires all candidates to demonstrate alignment with a particular ideological framework as a condition of consideration, regardless of their teaching expertise or scholarly record. This practice reduces individuals to demographic proxies and undermines the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their own character, abilities, and demonstrated competence.

incidents.institution_name: University of Alberta – Alberta School of Business
incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta's Alberta School of Business posted a faculty job listing that explicitly encourages applications from people based on their sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation, treating demographic categories as relevant criteria for academic hiring.
incidents.description_of_event: The Alberta School of Business at the University of Alberta posted three tenure-track faculty positions in its Department of Strategy, Entrepreneurship, and Management on May 12, 2026. The posting lists qualifications centred on academic credentials, research potential, and teaching excellence. However, the closing statement explicitly singles out applicants by demographic category — including women, first nations, métis and inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression — and encourages them to apply on the basis of these characteristics. The stated rationale is to contribute to the "further diversification of ideas" and of the university. This language frames demographic identity as a relevant factor in academic hiring, separate from the candidate's individual qualifications, research record, or teaching ability.
incidents.expected_harm: Framing demographic characteristics as relevant hiring considerations signals to all applicants that they are evaluated in part based on immutable traits rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups receive an implicit message that the institution values their demographic profile less. Candidates who do belong to listed groups face the suggestion that their identity, rather than their merit alone, may influence the outcome — undermining recognition of their achievements as purely their own. This practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories, eroding the principle that each person deserves to be assessed as a distinct individual.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's job posting for a faculty position in soil biogeochemistry invites applicants to declare membership in designated demographic groups and signals that belonging to those groups is an advantage in the hiring process. The posting evaluates candidates in part based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Natural Resource Sciences posted a tenure-track faculty position in environmental soil biogeochemistry on May 12, 2026. The posting states that McGill welcomes applications from racialized persons and visible minorities, women, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of any sexual orientation and gender identity. Beyond a general statement of welcome, the posting goes further by operating an employment equity programme and explicitly inviting members of designated groups to indicate their group membership in their application materials. This means that demographic information — including skin colour, sex, disability status, ethnicity, and sexual orientation — becomes part of the evaluated application package. The posting frames this as a commitment to equity and diversity, but the practical effect is that the institution is collecting and weighing applicants' demographic characteristics as part of the selection process, rather than evaluating each candidate solely on their research record, teaching ability, and individual qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution asks applicants to declare demographic group membership and incorporates that information into evaluation, candidates are assessed in part on immutable personal characteristics rather than solely on their abilities and achievements. This means some candidates receive an advantage — and others a disadvantage — based on who they are rather than what they can do. The practice reduces individuals to demographic proxies, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be recognised for their unique qualities and capabilities. It also normalises the sorting of people into labelled groups within academic hiring, reinforcing institutional habits of categorising individuals by skin colour, sex, ethnicity, and other personal characteristics rather than treating each applicant as a distinct person.

incidents.short_description: Carleton University's posting for a physics postdoctoral fellowship explicitly signals preference for candidates from named demographic groups, framing diversity as a hiring criterion alongside qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Carleton University posted a postdoctoral fellowship in its Department of Physics on May 12, 2026. The posting includes a statement that the university welcomes those who would contribute to the 'further diversification' of the university, listing specific demographic categories: women, visible minorities, first nations, inuit and métis peoples, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity and/or expression. This language signals institutional preference for candidates belonging to these named groups, going beyond a neutral commitment to accessibility or accommodation. The posting otherwise describes a research role focused on the ATLAS experiment at CERN, with qualifications centred on experimental particle physics expertise. The diversity preference statement is positioned as part of the hiring criteria, meaning candidates are assessed not only on their research credentials but also on whether their demographic characteristics align with the university's diversification goals.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated research potential. This practice reduces applicants to representatives of group categories rather than treating them as distinct individuals. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the named demographic groups face a structural disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their merit. The approach normalises institutional decision-making based on immutable personal traits and entrenches the idea that demographic composition is a legitimate criterion for academic appointments.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's job posting for an Environmental Soil Biogeochemistry professor encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories and operates an employment equity program that asks candidates to self-identify according to those same categories.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a tenure-track faculty position in Environmental Soil Biogeochemistry on May 12, 2026. The posting's equity section explicitly encourages applications from people grouped by skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation or gender identity. The university states it implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify during the application process. The posting also states that a demonstrated commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion is expected of the successful candidate. Rather than assessing all candidates purely on their qualifications, research excellence, and teaching ability, the posting introduces demographic self-identification as a component of the hiring process and frames certain demographic groups as preferred.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and sorted according to demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research excellence, and teaching ability. Applicants who do not belong to the listed demographic groups receive a less favourable standing in the process, meaning their individual merits are discounted because of who they are rather than what they can contribute. This practice signals to faculty and students that demographic identity is a legitimate basis for differential treatment in a university setting, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own character and abilities. It also pressures candidates to disclose personal characteristics — such as sexual orientation or disability status — to gain a competitive advantage, eroding individual privacy.

incidents.short_description: Acadia University restricted applications for a full-time librarian position to candidates of specific skin colours and ethnicities, excluding all other qualified applicants on that basis.
incidents.description_of_event: Acadia University posted a full-time, continuing appointment librarian position (Librarian I, II, or III) at the Vaughan Memorial Library on May 12, 2026, with a closing date of May 24, 2026. The posting explicitly designated the role as a "focused hire" open only to candidates who self-identify as black or indigenous. Applicants were required to include in their cover letter a statement of self-identification in relation to these designated groups, and to complete an Employment Equity Self-Identification Form; failure to complete the form results in automatic disqualification. Candidates who do not belong to these groups, regardless of their qualifications, experience, or professional merit, are effectively excluded from consideration. The position involves liaison responsibilities, information literacy instruction, research consultations, and collections development across academic departments.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified librarians are denied consideration for a permanent academic position solely on the basis of their skin colour or ethnicity, not their abilities, character, or demonstrated competence. This reduces individuals to demographic categories and treats immutable traits as a legitimate reason to bar people from employment. The practice normalizes the institutional sorting of people by ancestry and skin colour in hiring, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their individual merits. It sends a message to all applicants and staff that demographic identity, rather than professional excellence, determines access to opportunity.

incidents.short_description: Acadia University posted a librarian position that requires all applicants to complete an Employment Equity Self-Identification Form, with disqualification for failure to do so, while explicitly encouraging applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Acadia University advertised a 12-month term Librarian I or II position at the Vaughan Memorial Library, posted on May 12, 2026, with a closing date of May 18, 2026. The posting explicitly encourages applications from people categorised by skin colour, ethnicity, indigeneity, disability, sexuality, and gender identity. The posting goes further than an expression of preference: it mandates that all applicants complete an Employment Equity Self-Identification Form as a required part of the application package. The form asks applicants to self-identify based on these demographic categories. The posting states that failure to complete the form by the closing date will result in the disqualification of the application entirely. This means that any candidate who declines to categorise themselves by demographic identity is excluded from consideration, regardless of their qualifications for the role.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates who decline to self-identify by demographic category are disqualified from consideration for the position, regardless of their qualifications, character, or abilities. This treats demographic categorisation as a prerequisite for employment rather than an individual's merits, and compels applicants to submit personal information about their identity as a condition of being evaluated. The practice reinforces institutional sorting of people by immutable traits rather than individual worth, and sends a message that belonging to particular demographic groups affects one's standing as a job applicant. It undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated based on their abilities and demonstrated needs alone.

incidents.short_description: Acadia University's job posting for a kinesiology instructor position requires all applicants to submit an Employment Equity Self-Identification Form, with disqualification threatened for non-compliance, while actively preferring candidates based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and other demographic characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: Acadia University posted a one-year term instructor position in the School of Kinesiology on May 12, 2026. As part of the application process, all candidates are required to submit an Employment Equity Voluntary Self-Identification Form. Despite being described as voluntary, the posting states that failure to complete the form by the closing date will result in the disqualification of the application. The posting also explicitly encourages applications from individuals identified by skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, sexual orientation, gender identity, and other demographic categories, framing these characteristics as relevant factors in the hiring process. Candidates who self-identify as members of these designated groups are treated as a distinct class of applicants. The job posting does not indicate that these demographic preferences are tied to any individual's assessed needs or abilities relevant to the teaching role.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and sorted based on their demographic characteristics rather than their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. The mandatory self-identification requirement — where refusal results in disqualification — strips applicants of a genuine choice about whether to disclose sensitive personal information such as their skin colour, sexual orientation, or disability status to an institution. Candidates who do not belong to the favoured demographic groups face a structural disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their teaching qualifications or merit. This practice normalizes the use of immutable characteristics as hiring criteria, undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation on their own terms. It also pressures applicants to define themselves through demographic categories as a condition of being considered for employment.

incidents.short_description: Yorkville University's job posting for a Faculty of Education Chair explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation, sorting applicants into demographic categories rather than evaluating them purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Yorkville University posted a job advertisement on 12 May 2026 for the position of Chair, Faculty of Education. The posting included a diversity statement that explicitly encouraged applications from candidates identified by their skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, gender identity, and disability status. The statement listed preferred demographic groups including women, persons with disabilities, people described as Black, indigenous and People of Colour, and members of the LGBTQ2SIA+ community. By directing its recruitment appeal to people on the basis of these demographic characteristics, the institution signals that demographic identity is a relevant factor in evaluating candidates for the role, rather than focusing solely on each individual's qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and sorted based on demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique qualifications and abilities. This approach communicates to all applicants that their skin colour, ethnicity, or sexual orientation is a relevant criterion for selection, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. Qualified individuals who do not belong to the listed demographic groups receive a clear signal that they are less preferred, regardless of their individual abilities or achievements. The practice normalises institutional categorisation of people by immutable traits and entrenches the idea that demographic group membership is a legitimate basis for employment decisions.

incidents.short_description: Western University's job posting for a Commercial Aviation Management position explicitly invites applications based on demographic categories including sex, race, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates solely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University posted a limited-term faculty position in its DAN Department of Management and Organizational Studies on May 11, 2026. The posting for an Assistant Professor or Lecturer in Commercial Aviation Management includes a statement that the university "welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression." This language signals preferential consideration based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating all applicants equally as individuals on the basis of their qualifications, experience, and ability. Separately, the posting includes an "Affirmation Policy Statement" describing a process by which candidates who declare indigenous citizenship or membership may be required to have that declaration verified through a "relational accountability process" led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives — indicating that indigenous identity is used as a material factor in hiring decisions at the institution.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed not only on their qualifications and demonstrated abilities but also on whether they belong to specified demographic groups. This reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than recognising their unique qualities and capabilities. Qualified applicants who do not belong to the listed groups receive less institutional encouragement to apply, and the process normalises the sorting of people by immutable characteristics when making employment decisions. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual, and entrenches institutional practices that divide rather than unite people around shared standards of merit and excellence.

incidents.short_description: Western University's job posting requires applicants who declare indigenous identity to undergo a separate institutional verification process not required of any other applicants. This subjects a specific group of candidates to additional scrutiny based on their declared identity.
incidents.description_of_event: A May 2026 job posting from Western University's School of Kinesiology for an Assistant Professor position includes an 'Affirmation Policy Statement' that applies exclusively to candidates who declare indigenous citizenship or membership. Under this policy, candidates who make such a declaration for any material advantage — including where it is required or preferred for the position — must have that declaration affirmed through a 'relational accountability process' led by Western's Office of Indigenous Initiatives. This additional verification step is triggered solely by a candidate's declaration of a particular identity and is not applied to any other group of applicants. The posting frames this as moving 'beyond sole reliance upon Indigenous self-identification' in order to guard against 'incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information.' No equivalent verification requirement exists for any other demographic or identity declaration made by applicants during the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants who declare indigenous identity are subjected to an additional institutional scrutiny process that other candidates do not face. This creates a two-tiered hiring process in which a person's declared identity determines the level of institutional examination they receive, treating individuals differently based on their background rather than their qualifications, character, or demonstrated abilities. The policy sends a signal that declarations made by this particular group of people are inherently less trustworthy and require external institutional validation. This undermines the dignity of individuals who identify in this way and normalises the practice of evaluating people through the lens of demographic categories rather than as individuals.

incidents.short_description: York University's School of Nursing posted a faculty position that grants hiring preference to applicants based on their demographic characteristics, including sex, skin colour, and sexual orientation, through the university's Affirmative Action program.
incidents.description_of_event: York University's School of Nursing posted five Sessional Assistant Professor, Teaching Stream positions on May 11, 2026. The job posting states that York is an Affirmative Action employer and that the program specifically advantages women, members of racialized groups, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and those who identify as 2SLGBTQ+. The posting directs applicants to provide self-identification information for Affirmative Action purposes as part of the online application. Rather than evaluating all candidates solely on their qualifications, demonstrated abilities, and experience, the hiring process formally incorporates demographic categories — including skin colour, sex, and sexual orientation — as factors that determine preferential consideration. The posting requires candidates to submit a teaching philosophy statement that addresses equity, diversity, inclusivity and decolonization, embedding ideological frameworks as a formal assessment criterion alongside academic qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for these positions are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and experience. This means qualified individuals who do not belong to the listed groups are disadvantaged in the hiring process due to their immutable traits. The institutionalization of demographic preferences in hiring signals that a person's skin colour, sex, or sexual orientation is a legitimate basis for differential treatment in employment. Requiring candidates to affirm commitment to specific ideological frameworks as a formal hiring criterion restricts the range of perspectives that may be considered, and imposes conformity on applicants who may otherwise bring valuable approaches to teaching and scholarship.

incidents.institution_name: University of British Columbia School of Nursing
incidents.short_description: The UBC School of Nursing requires job applicants to submit a statement demonstrating alignment with the university's anti-racism framework, functioning as an ideological test for a faculty position. The posting also encourages applications on the basis of demographic characteristics rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia School of Nursing posted a Lecturer position on May 11, 2026, for a full-time non-tenure track faculty role in its undergraduate nursing program. Alongside standard professional qualifications — such as registration with the BC College of Nurses and Midwives, a Master's or PhD in nursing, and relevant teaching experience — the posting requires applicants to include a statement explaining how their experiences and knowledge will support the School's commitments to anti-racism and indigenous cultural safety. Applicants are directed to two dedicated institutional websites outlining these frameworks. This requirement functions as an ideological screening tool: candidates must demonstrate alignment with specific institutional frameworks as a condition of being considered for the position, regardless of their qualifications as nursing educators. The posting additionally encourages applications from individuals belonging to groups defined by enumerated demographic characteristics — including skin colour, sexual orientation, gender identity, disability, religion, and indigeneity — framing institutional diversity goals in terms of group membership rather than individual circumstances or merit.
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions require job applicants to demonstrate ideological alignment with particular frameworks as a condition of employment, they exclude individuals who may be highly qualified educators but who do not subscribe to those frameworks or are unwilling to express a prescribed position. This restricts the range of perspectives within the institution and pressures applicants to conform publicly to a specific worldview rather than be evaluated on their abilities and character. Encouraging applications on the basis of group membership signals to applicants that their demographic identity — not their individual qualifications — is a relevant factor in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that people deserve to be evaluated as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The Université de Sherbrooke posted a faculty position that explicitly directs hiring priority toward candidates based on their sex, visible minority status, ethnicity, indigenous identity, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than solely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 11, 2026, the Université de Sherbrooke posted a full-time regular faculty position in biochemistry and omic sciences within its Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences. The posting's equity, diversity and inclusion section states that while all qualified candidates are invited to apply, priority must be given to women, members of visible and ethnic minorities, indigenous peoples, and persons with disabilities under the university's employment equity access program (Programme d'accès à l'égalité en emploi). The posting also encourages applications from people of all sexual orientations and gender identities. The effect of this language is that the university commits to evaluating candidates based in part on demographic characteristics — sex, skin colour, ethnicity, indigenous identity, disability, and sexual orientation — rather than assessing each applicant purely on their qualifications, abilities, and individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are disadvantaged in the selection process because of their demographic characteristics rather than their abilities or demonstrated merit. This institutional practice reduces individuals to demographic proxies, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their unique qualities and capabilities. Candidates who do not belong to the designated groups face a structurally unequal process, while those who do belong to designated groups may have their professional achievements overshadowed by their demographic identity. The practice normalises the sorting of individuals into demographic categories as a core institutional function, eroding the expectation that academic hiring is grounded in individual merit and intellectual achievement.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan's job posting for a term professorship includes a formal verification process specifically for candidates who assert indigenous membership or citizenship, and explicitly encourages applications from certain demographic groups. The posting treats applicants differently based on their ancestral or ethnic identity.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Saskatchewan posted a term professorship in Agricultural Economics on May 11, 2026. The posting includes two provisions that sort applicants by demographic identity. First, it encourages applications from 'equity-deserving groups,' a category defined by demographic characteristics rather than individual qualifications or demonstrated need. Second, it singles out candidates who assert indigenous membership or citizenship for a distinct verification process governed by a specific university policy and standing committee. Candidates in this category are required to submit documentation to confirm their identity before their application can be fully considered. No comparable verification requirement applies to other applicants. The posting frames these measures as part of the university's commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion, but in practice the hiring process evaluates and tracks candidates based on ethnic and ancestral identity rather than treating every applicant as an individual.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are sorted and evaluated in part based on their ethnic or ancestral identity rather than solely on their qualifications, abilities, and character. This creates a two-tier application process in which some individuals must prove their demographic identity to receive the treatment the posting implies they will receive. Applicants who do not belong to favoured demographic categories are placed at a disadvantage regardless of their individual merit. The practice normalises the institutional habit of categorising people by immutable characteristics and signals that the university views applicants first through the lens of demographic membership rather than as distinct individuals. Over time this erodes the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own terms.

incidents.institution_name: Western University – Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry
incidents.short_description: Western University's job posting for a transplant anesthesiologist position explicitly lists demographic characteristics as preferred hiring criteria, evaluating candidates in part based on group membership rather than individual qualifications. The posting also introduces a formal verification process for candidates who declare indigenous identity, adding an extra procedural hurdle based on ancestry.
incidents.description_of_event:
Western University's Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry posted a faculty position for a transplant anesthesiologist on May 11, 2026. The posting states that the university "welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression," signalling that these demographic characteristics are considered in the hiring process rather than evaluating all candidates purely on qualifications, abilities, and experience.

The posting also includes a section titled "Affirmation Policy Statement" describing a process by which candidates who declare indigenous citizenship or membership will be subject to an additional verification step. Candidates who have made such a declaration "for material advantage" and are shortlisted will be required to have that declaration affirmed through a "relational accountability process" led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives. This process applies specifically to candidates who have declared an indigenous identity in the context where it provides a hiring benefit, adding a procedural requirement tied to ancestry that other candidates do not face.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed in part on the basis of demographic categories — skin colour, sex, disability, sexual orientation, and ancestry — rather than solely on their individual qualifications, skills, and demonstrated clinical and academic abilities. This reduces each candidate to a set of group memberships rather than recognising them as distinct individuals. Candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic groups face a structurally disadvantaged application process despite potentially being equally or better qualified. The additional verification step for those declaring indigenous identity singles out a group of applicants for heightened scrutiny tied to their ancestry, creating an unequal experience within the hiring process itself. Over time, these practices normalise the institutional habit of sorting and evaluating people by immutable traits, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated ability.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta's job posting for a research associate position explicitly encourages applications from people based on their sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation. The posting treats demographic characteristics as relevant hiring considerations rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications and abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta posted a job listing for a Research Associate position in the Faculty of Engineering on May 8, 2026, with an application deadline of June 20, 2026. The posting includes a statement at the end that, beyond welcoming applications from all qualified persons, specifically names and encourages applications from women, first nations, métis and inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression. The rationale given is that such persons may contribute to the 'further diversification of ideas and the University.' This framing signals to applicants and the institution's own hiring process that demographic characteristics are a relevant consideration, moving beyond a commitment to accessible hiring toward actively directing recruitment based on immutable traits and group membership rather than individual qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed not purely on their individual qualifications, skills, and demonstrated abilities, but on whether they belong to named demographic groups. This reduces individuals to their demographic categories, undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation based on their character and abilities alone. Applicants who do not belong to the named groups receive an implicit signal that they are less preferred, regardless of their merits. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by group identity and embeds demographic categorisation into hiring, eroding the idea that each person's singular worth and individual qualities should guide how institutions treat them.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan's job posting for a veterinary medicine lecturer position actively encourages applications from 'equity-deserving groups,' signalling that demographic characteristics play a role in hiring rather than evaluating each candidate solely on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Saskatchewan posted a Lecturer position in Elective Surgery within the Western College of Veterinary Medicine on May 8, 2026. The posting states that the university 'actively seek[s] to create a welcoming environment where all individuals feel empowered to thrive, contribute, and grow' and that 'applications from equity-deserving groups are encouraged as part of our ongoing efforts to reflect the diversity of the communities we serve.' The posting also singles out indigenous applicants specifically, noting that the university 'value[s] the unique perspective that Indigenous employees provide' and that successful candidates asserting indigenous membership or citizenship must complete a formal verification process. These statements indicate that the university uses demographic categories — rather than individual qualifications, character, and demonstrated ability — as factors in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a university signals that applications from certain demographic groups are preferred, it introduces demographic categorisation into what should be an evaluation of individual merit, qualifications, and ability. Candidates are assessed not purely as individuals but as members of groups, reducing their singular worth to their demographic identity. This also sends a message to all applicants that group membership, rather than individual excellence, influences the outcome — discouraging some qualified individuals from applying and undermining confidence in the fairness of the process. The institutional practice of sorting job applicants by demographic categories normalises the idea that people's immutable traits are relevant proxies for their value as employees, weakening the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as a unique individual.

incidents.institution_name: University of Victoria, Faculty of Law
incidents.short_description: The University of Victoria's Faculty of Law posted a senior academic chair position giving explicit hiring preference to candidates based on their ethnicity, excluding all others from the preferential candidate pool. Applicants are also required to formally prove their ethnic identity to advance in the process.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Victoria's Faculty of Law posted a job listing on 8 May 2026 for a Strategic Framework Impact Chair in International Indigenous Law, a senior tenured academic role offering a salary of up to $244,000 plus $50,000 annually in research funding. The posting states that, pursuant to Section 42 of the BC Human Rights Code and the university's Equity Plan, preference will be given to candidates who identify as indigenous persons. Candidates from this group are instructed to self-identify in their cover letter to qualify for preferential consideration, with the committee reviewing other applicants only if no suitable candidate is found in the preferential pool. Beyond self-identification, shortlisted candidates claiming indigenous identity must submit a formal declaration and supporting documentation through the university's Indigenous Citizenship Declaration (ICD) Policy portal to verify their claimed ethnicity before proceeding through the hiring process. The position is open only to candidates external to UVic.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are filtered and ranked based on their ethnic background rather than their individual abilities, scholarly record, or demonstrated expertise. Non-indigenous applicants are placed in a secondary candidate pool and are considered only as a last resort, regardless of their qualifications. This arrangement reduces individuals to demographic categories at the point of institutional evaluation, directly contradicting the principle that people deserve to be assessed on their character, abilities, and individual merits. The formal verification requirement — requiring candidates to prove their ethnic identity through a government-style declaration process — entrenches institutional practices of categorising and sorting people by ancestry. This normalises the idea that a person's ethnic background is a legitimate and primary basis for determining access to prestigious, well-compensated academic positions, and signals to the broader academic community that demographic identity outweighs individual achievement in hiring decisions.

incidents.short_description: Acadia University's job posting for a Physics professor requires all applicants to complete a demographic self-identification form as a mandatory condition of their application, with disqualification threatened for non-compliance. The form is used to determine whether applicants can be considered members of designated groups, building demographic categorisation into the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: Acadia University posted a 10-month term position for an Assistant Professor in Physics on May 8, 2026, with a closing date of May 27, 2026. As part of the application requirements, all candidates are required to submit an Employment Equity Voluntary Self-Identification Form. Despite being described as voluntary in terms of what an applicant chooses to disclose, completion of the form itself is mandatory: the posting explicitly states that failure to complete the form by the closing date will result in the disqualification of the application. The form is used to determine whether applicants can be classified as members of designated groups, with only those who have submitted the form being considered in that capacity. The posting also lists specific demographic groups the university encourages to apply, including individuals categorised by skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. The hiring process therefore formally incorporates demographic categorisation as a structural element, with administrative consequences for those who do not participate in the self-identification process.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who object to being categorised by demographic characteristics are disqualified from consideration entirely, denying them access to employment on the basis of their unwillingness to submit to demographic labelling. This coerces applicants into self-identifying by demographic category as a condition of being evaluated for a position, subordinating individual dignity and privacy to institutional demographic management. The process reduces applicants to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them solely as individuals based on their abilities, teaching experience, and scholarly contributions. It entrenches the institutional practice of sorting people into designated groups, normalising the idea that demographic identity is a relevant and required element of professional evaluation.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's job posting for an Assistant Professor position explicitly encourages applications from people based on their demographic characteristics and operates an employment equity program that asks candidates to self-identify by designated group membership.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a position for an Assistant Professor (Clinical) in the Department of Medicine, Division of Medical Biochemistry on May 8, 2026. The posting states that the university "welcomes and encourages applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities." The posting further states that McGill "implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify." The institution frames candidate evaluation around demographic categories rather than solely on individual qualifications, abilities, and character. The posting also requires a "demonstrated commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion" as part of the expected professional conduct of applicants.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and sorted based on their membership in demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated merit. The requirement that applicants demonstrate commitment to a particular ideological framework as a condition of employment restricts access to the position for those who may not share or publicly endorse those views. Encouraging self-identification by demographic group normalises the institutional practice of categorising individuals by immutable traits, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be recognised and assessed as a unique individual.

incidents.short_description: Western University's job posting for a paediatric orthopaedic surgeon includes a policy that subjects candidates who declare indigenous ancestry to an additional verification process not required of other applicants, singling them out based on their ethnic background.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University posted a position for a paediatric orthopaedic surgeon on May 8, 2026. Embedded in the posting is an 'Affirmation Policy Statement' that applies exclusively to candidates who declare indigenous citizenship or membership. Under this policy, candidates who make such a declaration — particularly where it is used as a material advantage in the hiring process — are required to have that declaration verified through a separate process led by the university's Office of Indigenous Initiatives. This affirmation process is described as being consistent with 'indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing.' No equivalent verification requirement exists for any other group listed in the university's employment equity statement, which also names women, members of racialized groups, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation. The policy creates a two-tiered application experience in which a specific group of candidates faces additional procedural requirements tied directly to their ethnic or ancestral identity.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are subjected to differential treatment during hiring based on their ethnic ancestry or membership in an indigenous community. This singles out one group for a verification process unavailable to others, reducing individuals to their demographic identity at a decisive moment in their evaluation. The policy normalises institutional scrutiny of a person's ancestral or ethnic credentials, treating group membership as a material factor in employment decisions rather than assessing each candidate as an individual based on their qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities. This approach entrenches the practice of evaluating people through demographic categories and undermines the principle that every person deserves assessment on their own merits.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's job posting for an assistant professor position in medical biochemistry invites applicants to declare membership in designated demographic groups, signalling that hiring decisions are influenced by applicants' immutable characteristics rather than solely by their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a full-time contractual faculty position in the Department of Medicine, Division of Medical Biochemistry, at the McGill University Health Centre (MUHC), advertised on 8 May 2026. The posting lists rigorous academic and clinical qualifications — an MD and PhD, Royal College certification in medical biochemistry, Quebec licensing eligibility, bilingual competency in French and English, and an active funded research programme. However, the posting also states that McGill has an employment equity programme and explicitly invites applicants identified as belonging to certain demographic categories — including those described as racialized persons and visible minorities, women, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity — to indicate that membership in their application. The institution frames this as a commitment to equity and diversity. The practical effect is that applicants are sorted and evaluated in part based on immutable personal characteristics, and that belonging to specified demographic groups is treated as a relevant factor in hiring.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated not purely on their individual character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications but also on the demographic categories they belong to. This reduces people to group membership rather than recognizing them as distinct individuals. Qualified applicants who do not belong to the designated groups face a disadvantage in the selection process regardless of merit. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people by immutable traits and undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. It also pressures applicants to disclose sensitive personal information — regarding ethnicity, disability, or sexual orientation — in order to compete on equal footing, eroding individual privacy.

incidents.short_description: Yorkville University's job posting for a Dean of Business position explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on their skin colour, sexual orientation, and other demographic characteristics, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Yorkville University posted a job listing on May 8, 2026, seeking a Dean of Business for its British Columbia campus. The posting sets out a range of professional and academic qualifications for the role. However, the application materials explicitly encourage applications from people based on their demographic characteristics, including skin colour, disability status, and sexual orientation, listing specific identity categories rather than inviting all qualified individuals to apply on the basis of their abilities and experience alone. The posting uses the acronym "BIPOC" to categorize people by skin colour and ethnicity, and lists membership in the "LGBTQ2SIA+" community as a basis for encouragement. This framing signals that demographic characteristics are considered relevant factors in the selection process, rather than an approach that evaluates every candidate purely as an individual.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution signals that demographic identity is a relevant consideration in hiring, it introduces non-merit factors into what should be an evaluation of individual qualifications, character, and ability. Candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic categories receive a different and lesser invitation to apply, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as an individual. This approach also reduces those in the favoured categories to their demographic characteristics rather than recognizing them for their unique professional qualities. Over time, normalizing demographic preferences in academic leadership hiring erodes the principle that advancement should reflect merit and individual capability.

incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba posted a senior research leadership position that explicitly encourages applications from 'systemically marginalized groups,' signalling that demographic characteristics are a factor in hiring rather than individual merit and qualifications alone.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba advertised the role of Associate Vice-President (Research) on May 8, 2026, via University Affairs. The posting describes a senior administrative leadership role responsible for research policy, integrity, compliance, and institutional strategy. At the close of the posting, the university states it 'is committed to equity, diversity, and inclusion and encourages applications from systemically marginalized groups.' This language signals to prospective applicants that demographic group membership is a consideration in the hiring process, rather than evaluating each candidate solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities. The posting does not define which groups are considered 'systemically marginalized,' leaving the scope of the preference undefined and broad.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for this senior role are evaluated in part through the lens of their demographic group membership rather than purely on their individual qualifications and abilities. This reduces every applicant to a set of demographic categories rather than recognizing them as individuals with distinct skills, character, and experience. Qualified individuals who do not belong to preferred demographic groups face a disadvantage regardless of their merit, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual ability. The practice also sends a message that group identity is a legitimate basis for institutional decision-making, normalizing the categorization of people by immutable traits in professional settings.

incidents.short_description: Yorkville University's job posting for an Associate Dean of Business position explicitly encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and other demographic categories. The posting signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in hiring decisions rather than individual merit and qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Yorkville University posted a job listing on May 8, 2026 for an Associate Dean of Business in Ontario, advertising a salary range of $130,000 to $155,000. The posting included a statement encouraging applications specifically from people sorted into named demographic categories, including women, persons with disabilities, people identified by skin colour and ethnicity, and people identified by their sexual orientation. By naming and listing these demographic categories as groups whose applications are especially encouraged, the institution signals that demographic identity is a relevant consideration in the hiring process. This approach treats candidates as members of demographic categories rather than as individuals whose qualifications, character, and abilities are the sole relevant factors in the assessment process.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated, at least in part, based on their membership in demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications and abilities. This sends a message to all applicants that demographic characteristics are a relevant credential for senior academic leadership. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups receive a signal that they are less valued by the institution. The practice normalizes the use of immutable personal traits as hiring criteria, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed as a distinct individual. It also reduces trust in hiring outcomes, as colleagues and the broader community cannot be certain that selection was based entirely on merit.

incidents.short_description: Dalhousie University's job posting for a Landscape Architecture Instructor states that preference will be given in hiring to candidates who self-identify as members of designated demographic groups. Candidates are evaluated not only on their qualifications but on whether they belong to particular categories defined by skin colour, ancestry, disability, sex, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Dalhousie University posted a one-year limited-term Instructor position in Landscape Architecture on May 8, 2026, within the Department of Plant, Food, and Environmental Sciences at the Faculty of Agriculture. The posting states that, in accordance with the university's Employment Equity Policy, preference will be given to candidates who self-identify as members of one or more specified demographic groups. These groups are defined by skin colour and descent (specifically naming people of African descent and indigenous peoples), disability, sex, and sexual orientation. Applicants are required to submit an EDIA Statement as part of their application package, which functions as a mechanism for candidates to declare their membership in these groups. The posting makes explicit that this self-identification directly influences hiring decisions, meaning candidates are evaluated and ranked in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and ability to perform the role.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are disadvantaged in the hiring process based on characteristics that have nothing to do with their ability to teach landscape architecture. The policy reduces individuals to demographic categories and treats group membership as a credential, undermining the principle that people deserve to be evaluated on their character, abilities, and demonstrated merit. Candidates who do not belong to the preferred groups face a structurally lower chance of being selected regardless of their qualifications, sending the message that their individual worth is secondary to their demographic profile. This practice normalises institutional sorting of people by immutable traits and embeds the assumption that demographic representation, rather than individual excellence, is the primary hiring criterion. It also pressures applicants to self-label and disclose personal characteristics in order to compete fairly for a public academic position.

incidents.short_description: Dalhousie University's job posting for an English instructor states that hiring preference will be given to candidates who identify as members of designated demographic groups. Applicants are evaluated partly on the basis of demographic self-identification rather than solely on their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Dalhousie University posted a job listing on May 7, 2026, for a full-time, limited-term English instructor position in the Faculty of Agriculture, located in Truro, Nova Scotia. The posting explicitly states that, in accordance with the university's Employment Equity Policy, preference in hiring will be given to candidates who self-identify as members of one or more designated groups. The groups listed include indigenous peoples, persons of a particular skin colour or descent, members of other groups defined by their ethnicity or skin colour, persons with disabilities, women, and persons identifying as members of sexual orientation or gender identity communities. The posting frames this preferential treatment as contributing to diversity, but the operative effect is that candidates are ranked and selected in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their abilities, qualifications, and demonstrated needs. The position itself requires a graduate degree in English or a related field and experience teaching at a post-secondary level, and candidates are also asked to provide a teaching statement and referees — criteria that assess individual merit. However, the explicit preference policy means that demographic identity is used as a tiebreaker or advantage in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not belong to any of the listed demographic groups are placed at a disadvantage in the hiring process based solely on immutable or personal characteristics unrelated to their ability to perform the role. This approach reduces individuals to their demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and experience. It undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as an individual, and normalizes the institutional practice of sorting and ranking people based on group membership. Candidates who are passed over because of this policy experience a direct and concrete harm to their careers and livelihoods.

incidents.short_description: The Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières posted a faculty position in management accounting that explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories including sex, indigeneity, visible minority status, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates solely on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières (UQTR) posted a tenure-track faculty position in the Department of Accounting Sciences (management accounting) on May 7, 2026, with a closing date of June 7, 2026. The posting outlines standard academic qualifications — a doctorate or master's degree in accounting or a related discipline, membership in the Ordre des comptables professionnels agréés (CPA) du Québec, and at least two years of relevant professional experience. However, the posting closes with a statement that the university particularly encourages applications from women, indigenous persons, members of visible or ethnic minorities, persons with disabilities, and persons from LGBTQ+ communities. The posting explicitly states that the university subscribes to an employment equity access programme in accordance with the law. While all qualified persons are invited to apply, the institution has publicly signalled that it will give preferential weight to candidates based on demographic categories rather than evaluating each applicant solely as an individual on the basis of their character, skills, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a university signals that certain demographic groups are particularly encouraged to apply and that hiring is governed by an employment equity programme, it introduces demographic categories as a factor in evaluating candidates. This reduces individuals to proxies for group membership rather than assessing them on their unique qualifications and potential. Candidates not belonging to the listed groups are disadvantaged in the competition regardless of their individual merits, and candidates who do belong to these groups may have their accomplishments attributed to their demographic identity rather than their abilities. The practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories and making decisions on that basis, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be recognised for their individual worth.

incidents.institution_name: BC Parks / Ministry of Environment and Parks
incidents.short_description: The BC government closes a public provincial park to the general public on two occasions each year, restricting access based on ethnicity, to provide exclusive use to members of specific First Nations. In 2026, these closures span up to 23 days in total.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 7, 2026, the BC Ministry of Environment and Parks announced that Pipi7íyekw/Joffre Lakes Park will be temporarily closed to the general public on two separate occasions during the 2026 season. The first closure runs from June 20 to 27, 2026, and the second from September 8 to 30, 2026 — a combined total of roughly 30 days. During these periods, access to the park is reserved for members of the Lil'wat Nation and N'Quatqua, with all other members of the public excluded. The closures are described by the government as providing time and space for those groups to reconnect with the land and participate in cultural and harvesting practices. The government notes this arrangement has been in place since 2018 and is part of a jointly managed visitor use strategy. General visitors to the park — regardless of their individual connection to the land, their recreational needs, or any other personal circumstance — are denied access solely on the basis of their ancestry and ethnic background.
incidents.expected_harm: Members of the public are denied access to a publicly funded provincial park based solely on their ethnic background and ancestry. A person's individual circumstances, interests, or connection to the natural environment are irrelevant — what determines their access is their ethnicity. This establishes a government-operated system in which public land is allocated on the basis of demographic categories rather than individual merit or need. The practice normalizes the use of ethnicity as a criterion for access to public spaces and resources, reinforcing division rather than shared civic belonging. It sends the message that a park ostensibly managed for all park users is in fact closed to the majority of the public for extended periods each year based on who they are by birth.

incidents.short_description: Western University's job posting for an Assistant Professor in Visual Arts explicitly solicits applications by demographic category rather than evaluating all candidates as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University posted a two-year limited-term Assistant Professor position in painting practices in its Department of Visual Arts on May 7, 2026. The posting requires applicants to submit, as a mandatory component of their application package, a statement of experience working with a diverse student body and contributions to advancing a culture of equity and inclusion. This requirement functions as an ideological screen, compelling applicants to affirm a particular institutional framework as a condition of being considered. The posting also explicitly invites applications sorted by demographic categories — women, members of racialized groups, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or expression — signalling that candidates are evaluated in part through the lens of their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their qualifications, artistic practice, and teaching ability.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants who do not align with or wish to perform commitment to the institution's equity and inclusion framework are screened out before their qualifications are assessed, regardless of their teaching excellence or artistic merit. This reduces hiring to a process that rewards ideological conformity alongside professional ability, rather than evaluating each candidate as a distinct individual. Candidates are also sorted and solicited based on demographic characteristics, reinforcing institutional habit of categorising people by immutable traits rather than treating them as individuals with unique abilities and character. Over time, this narrows the range of perspectives present in academic departments and signals to all candidates and students that demographic identity is a legitimate basis for institutional decision-making.

incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba's Department of Statistics posted a faculty position that explicitly invites applications from people based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, disability status, and sexual identity. Applicants are also required to submit a written statement of commitment to a specific ideological framework as part of their application.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba's Department of Statistics advertised a continuing Instructor I position in Statistics, posted on May 7, 2026, with a closing date of June 15, 2026. The posting states that the department 'particularly invite[s] applications' from individuals belonging to named demographic groups, including indigenous peoples, women, people categorised by skin colour, persons with disabilities, and persons of particular sexual identities and genders. This language signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in the hiring process, treating applicants as representatives of group categories rather than as individuals assessed on their qualifications, teaching ability, and character. In addition, the posting requires all applicants to submit a 'statement of commitment to Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion in teaching' as a mandatory component of the application package. This requirement functions as an ideological screen, compelling candidates to demonstrate adherence to a particular framework as a condition of being considered for an academic role.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching ability, and merit. This disadvantages individuals who do not belong to the named demographic groups, reducing their prospects regardless of their professional capabilities. The mandatory EDI statement requirement compels applicants to express allegiance to a specific ideological position as a condition of employment, chilling intellectual diversity and excluding those who hold different but legitimate views on how to approach fairness in teaching. Together these practices undermine the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their own abilities and character, and they normalise the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories rather than recognising their singular worth as individuals.

incidents.short_description: Brock University's job posting for a nursing assistant professor position explicitly encourages applications from people based on their demographic characteristics and asks candidates to self-identify as members of designated groups. This practice reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their abilities and qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Brock University posted a probationary tenure-track Assistant Professor position in the Department of Nursing on May 7, 2026. The posting includes a section titled 'Our Commitment' that explicitly encourages applications from people on the basis of their sex, ancestry, skin colour, disability status, and sexual orientation. The posting asks candidates to voluntarily self-identify as members of designated groups by completing a Self-Identification Form and including it with their application. The posting also specifies that research involving 'equity-deserving populations' would be considered an asset, embedding demographic preferences into the academic qualifications criteria. By soliciting self-identification by demographic category and treating group membership as an asset in the hiring process, the university signals that candidates' immutable characteristics are a relevant factor in how their applications will be evaluated.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated partly on the basis of demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated merit. The practice of soliciting self-identification by designated group membership introduces demographic sorting into the hiring process, reducing applicants to representatives of categories rather than distinct individuals. This undermines the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own character and abilities. Qualified candidates who do not belong to designated groups face a structural disadvantage, and the process normalises institutional classification of people by immutable traits.

incidents.short_description: Université de Montréal's job posting for a Full Professor in Health System Analysis invites applicants to self-identify based on demographic categories including sex, ethnicity, and disability status as part of its Equal Access to Employment Program, signalling that hiring decisions are shaped by these characteristics rather than solely by individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Université de Montréal posted a Full Professor position in Health System Analysis at its School of Public Health on May 7, 2026. The posting included an Equal Access to Employment Program section that explicitly invites women, indigenous peoples, visible minorities, ethnic minorities, and people with disabilities to self-identify and apply. Applicants are directed to complete a voluntary self-identification questionnaire, with the stated purpose of supporting a diverse and inclusive working and learning environment and improving institutional practices. The posting indicates that this demographic information is collected and used to inform hiring practices. While the posting states that all qualified individuals are encouraged to apply regardless of their characteristics, the explicit invitation to certain demographic groups through a formal program signals that applicants' demographic characteristics are a factor in the recruitment process, rather than limiting evaluation to individual qualifications, abilities, and merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants for this academic position are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, expertise, and abilities. This approach reduces candidates to their membership in demographic categories, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own merits and unique qualities. Candidates who do not belong to the named demographic groups face a structural disadvantage in the hiring process despite potentially possessing equal or superior qualifications. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of individuals by immutable traits, reinforcing the idea that demographic categories are a legitimate basis for making decisions about people.

incidents.short_description: Brock University's job posting for a Head of User Services & Engagement position explicitly encourages applications from people based on demographic characteristics and asks candidates to self-identify as members of designated groups. People not belonging to these groups are treated as less preferred applicants.
incidents.description_of_event: Brock University posted a job listing on May 7, 2026 for a full-time, permanent Head of User Services & Engagement position in its library. In the application instructions, the university explicitly named several demographic categories and stated that people belonging to those groups are encouraged to apply and to voluntarily self-identify as a member of a designated group as part of their application. Candidates wishing to be considered as members of one or more designated groups were directed to complete a Self-Identification Form and include it with their application. The posting makes clear that membership in these demographic categories is a factor the institution considers in hiring decisions. All applicants are evaluated not solely on their qualifications, skills, and demonstrated abilities, but also on which demographic categories they belong to.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed not purely on their individual qualifications, character, and abilities, but also on their membership in demographic categories. This reduces people to their demographic characteristics rather than recognising them as distinct individuals. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic groups face a systemic disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their merit. The practice normalises institutional sorting of individuals by demographic proxies and signals that demographic identity is a legitimate and valued basis for employment decisions at a publicly funded university.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's job posting for a Chinese-language lecturer explicitly invites applications from people based on their demographic characteristics and requires self-identification as a member of a designated group as part of the employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of East Asian Studies posted a full-time lecturer position in Chinese language on 7 May 2026. The posting states that the university welcomes applications from people described as racialised persons and visible minorities, women, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of any sexual orientation and gender identity. Beyond a general welcome, the university states it has an employment equity program and explicitly invites members of these designated groups to indicate their group membership in their application.
