The Faculty of Education also requires candidates to demonstrate a commitment to social justice goals as a qualification for the role, embedding a particular ideological orientation as a hiring criterion.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates who do not belong to the preferred demographic categories are placed at a disadvantage in the hiring process despite possessing equal or superior qualifications, skills, and experience. The institution reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their unique character and demonstrated abilities. This practice signals to all applicants — and to students and staff — that group identity is a legitimate and institutionally sanctioned basis on which to differentiate between people, normalizing the sorting of individuals by immutable characteristics. It also excludes from fair consideration anyone who does not wish to self-identify by demographic category.

incidents.short_description: York University's Faculty of Education posted a faculty position that explicitly gives hiring preference to candidates based on their demographic characteristics, including skin colour, indigeneity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates solely on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: York University posted a job advertisement on May 1, 2026 for a Sessional Assistant Professor, Teaching Stream in Deaf and Hard of Hearing Education within its Faculty of Education. The posting stated that York is an Affirmative Action employer and that the affirmative action program applies specifically to women, members of racialized groups, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and those who identify as 2SLGBTQ+. Candidates were directed to provide self-identification information as part of the application process for affirmative action purposes. The posting makes clear that membership in these designated demographic groups is a formal factor in the hiring process, meaning candidates are evaluated not only on their qualifications, experience, and teaching record, but also on their membership in particular demographic categories. The position is a three-year contractually limited appointment commencing July 1, 2026, with a salary range of $116,000–$126,000.
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions make demographic characteristics a formal criterion in hiring, they reduce individuals to the groups they belong to rather than evaluating them as unique people with distinct abilities, qualifications, and character. Candidates who are well qualified for the role are disadvantaged based solely on immutable traits unrelated to their ability to teach or lead the Deaf and Hard of Hearing Teacher Education program. At the same time, candidates from preferred demographic groups face the indignity of having their achievements viewed through the lens of group membership. This practice normalises the institutional sorting of people by demographic category and undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits.

incidents.short_description: Sault College's job posting for its President position explicitly invites applications from people based on their sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation. This signals that demographic characteristics factor into hiring consideration rather than individual merit alone.
incidents.description_of_event: Sault College posted a job advertisement dated May 1, 2026, for the position of President and Chief Executive Officer. The posting included a statement inviting applications from people identified by demographic categories including sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation. The posting simultaneously stated that the college fairly hires the best candidates based on merit, creating a direct tension with the demographic-based solicitation. By explicitly listing and targeting applicants based on immutable personal characteristics, the posting signals that a candidate's demographic identity is a relevant consideration in the hiring process. This approach reduces individuals to group membership rather than evaluating them solely on character, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for a senior leadership role are evaluated in part based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications and abilities. This undermines the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic groups receive a signal that their applications are less welcome, while those who do belong to those groups face the indignity of uncertainty about whether they were selected on the basis of their abilities or their demographic characteristics. The practice normalizes the institutional habit of sorting and categorizing people by immutable traits, entrenching a culture where demographic identity shapes professional opportunity.

incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba posted multiple research associate positions that explicitly signal hiring preferences based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual identity. Applicants are evaluated not purely on individual merit but on membership in listed groups.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba advertised multiple research associate positions on May 1, 2026, open through October 31, 2026. The posting states that the university is committed to "promoting opportunities in hiring, promotion and tenure" for a list of specific demographic groups, naming indigenous peoples, women, racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and those who identify as 2SLGBTQIA+. The posting explicitly encourages all qualified candidates to apply while simultaneously signalling that people belonging to these demographic categories are preferred. This means candidates are assessed not only on their doctoral qualifications and experience but on whether they belong to one of the listed groups. The posting frames this as a commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion, but in practice it institutionalises the practice of evaluating prospective researchers based on demographic characteristics rather than purely on individual ability, experience, and the merits of their work.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic categories are disadvantaged in the hiring process based on characteristics they cannot change. This reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than recognising them as an individual whose abilities, knowledge, and potential should be the basis for employment decisions. The practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories and assigning different treatment accordingly, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits. Candidates from outside the preferred groups receive a clear message that the institution values their demographic profile alongside — or above — their individual qualifications, eroding trust in the fairness of academic hiring and the integrity of the institution.

incidents.institution_name: Wilfrid Laurier University – Lazaridis School of Business & Economics
incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University's Lazaridis School of Business & Economics is hiring two assistant professors in Marketing and explicitly prioritises candidates from designated demographic groups. Applicants are asked to self-identify their demographic characteristics in order to be considered under employment equity provisions.
incidents.description_of_event: Wilfrid Laurier University posted two tenure-stream assistant professor positions in the Marketing area at the Lazaridis School of Business & Economics on May 1, 2026, with a deadline of July 7, 2026. The posting states that the university is committed to increasing the diversity of faculty and staff and welcomes applicants from members of so-called equity-seeking groups. Candidates are instructed to self-identify their membership in designated groups to a named administrative officer in order to be considered for employment equity. The posting directs candidates from these groups to contact specific university offices. This approach means that applicants' demographic characteristics — such as skin colour, sex, disability status, or other identity markers — are formally solicited and factored into the hiring process alongside qualifications. The university frames this as a commitment to equity, but it results in candidates being evaluated, at least in part, based on their demographic group membership rather than solely on their individual character, research output, teaching ability, and professional merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated not only on their individual qualifications, abilities, and achievements but also on their membership in designated demographic categories. This reduces applicants to demographic proxies rather than recognising them as distinct individuals. Qualified candidates who do not belong to preferred groups face a disadvantage that is unrelated to their ability to perform the role. The practice institutionalises the sorting of people by demographic labels, normalising the idea that a person's skin colour, sex, or other immutable traits are relevant factors in professional hiring. This undermines a merit-based culture where each person's singular worth and demonstrated capabilities are the basis for professional advancement.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto, Department of Philosophy
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Department of Philosophy explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation for a faculty position, rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Toronto's Department of Philosophy posted a one-year contractually limited term appointment for an Assistant Professor in Logic, advertised on May 1, 2026, with a closing date of June 4, 2026. The posting includes a diversity statement that explicitly singles out specific groups — defined by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation — and "strongly encourages" their applications above others. The institution frames this preferential recruitment as integral to building a "culture of belonging" and states that "diverse perspectives, experiences, and expertise are essential to strengthening our academic mission." Applicants are also asked to complete a diversity survey as part of the application process, and candidates are assessed in part based on a "demonstrated commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion." This means a candidate's demographic characteristics and ideological commitments, rather than solely their teaching ability and scholarly qualifications, are formally embedded in the hiring criteria.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic groups are treated as less desirable applicants regardless of their qualifications, teaching ability, or individual merit. The institution reduces individuals to their demographic characteristics — skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation — rather than evaluating them on their unique abilities and accomplishments. This approach entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories and assigning different value to them accordingly. Requiring a demonstrated commitment to a specific ideological framework as a hiring criterion further excludes individuals whose professional philosophy may differ, chilling open intellectual inquiry in an academic setting. The diversity survey, while described as voluntary, signals to applicants that their demographic identity is a relevant factor in the process.

incidents.short_description: McMaster University's job posting for a lecturer or assistant professor position explicitly solicits applications from candidates based on demographic categories and requires applicants to submit a statement demonstrating commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion ideology as a condition of the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: McMaster University posted a one-year contractually limited academic appointment in the Department of Health, Aging & Society on May 1, 2026. The posting requires all applicants to submit a statement describing their contributions to advancing equity, diversity and inclusion or 'inclusive excellence' in teaching, research, or service. This functions as an ideological conformity screen, requiring candidates to demonstrate alignment with a particular institutional framework as part of their application. The posting also explicitly invites applications from people identified by demographic categories including indigenous peoples, members of racialized communities, persons with disabilities, women, and persons who identify as 2SLGBTQ+. In addition, applicants are asked to complete a Diversity Survey collecting information about their demographic background. Together, these requirements establish a hiring process in which applicants are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics and their expressed commitment to a specific ideological framework rather than solely on their academic qualifications, teaching ability, and individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated based on their demographic characteristics and their willingness to publicly profess commitment to a specific ideological framework, rather than solely on their individual qualifications and merit. This reduces candidates to demographic proxies and ideological labels rather than recognising them as individuals with unique abilities and character. Candidates who do not belong to named demographic groups face a structural disadvantage regardless of their qualifications. Candidates who hold sincere but different views on how to promote fairness and inclusion are screened out not because of their academic ability but because of their beliefs. This normalises the institutional practice of sorting people by immutable traits and demanding ideological conformity, undermining open intellectual discourse and the principle that each person deserves evaluation based on their own merits.

incidents.short_description: Athabasca University's job posting for Dean of its Faculty of Business explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic characteristics including sex, indigeneity, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity, and visible minority status, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their qualifications and individual merits.
incidents.description_of_event: Athabasca University posted a job listing on May 1, 2026 for the role of Dean, Faculty of Business. The posting describes the ideal candidate in terms of academic leadership, strategic acumen, and commitment to accessible education. However, the posting also explicitly states that the university encourages applications from women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, persons of any gender identity and expression, and members of visible minorities. This language signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in the hiring process, directing certain groups to apply in a manner that goes beyond welcoming all individuals. The posting further describes the ideal candidate as a champion of equity, diversity, inclusion, indigenization, and reconciliation, embedding ideological commitments into the core qualifications for the role. These elements together indicate that demographic identity is treated as a relevant criterion in candidate evaluation rather than focusing solely on each individual's qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed partly through the lens of their demographic characteristics rather than purely on individual merit, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated based on their abilities, character, and experience. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic groups receive a less encouraged and potentially disadvantaged path through the process. This approach normalizes the institutional practice of sorting job seekers by immutable traits, reinforcing a hiring culture where identity categories shape outcomes rather than individual excellence.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto, Dalla Lana School of Public Health
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Dalla Lana School of Public Health posted a tenure-stream faculty position that explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation. The posting treats demographic characteristics as relevant qualifications for a faculty role.
incidents.description_of_event: The Dalla Lana School of Public Health at the University of Toronto posted a job advertisement on May 1, 2026, for a full-time tenure-stream Assistant Professor position in Bioethics. The posting includes a Diversity Statement that explicitly singles out and encourages applications from individuals based on their skin colour, indigeneity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting also requires applicants to submit a "statement of contributions to equity and diversity" as a mandatory component of their application, making demographic commitments a formal part of the evaluation process. Applicants are additionally asked to complete a voluntary Diversity Survey during the application process, under which personal demographic information is collected. The institution frames demographic characteristics as markers of value, stating that "diverse perspectives, experiences, and expertise are essential to strengthening our academic mission," linking demographic identity directly to academic worth.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research excellence, and teaching ability. This reduces individuals to representatives of demographic categories rather than recognizing them as distinct people with unique skills and accomplishments. Candidates who do not belong to the named demographic groups are disadvantaged in the hiring process through no fault of their own. The mandatory equity statement requirement means all applicants must signal commitment to a particular ideological framework to be considered, constraining the range of scholarly perspectives that can enter the academy. The collection of demographic data through the Diversity Survey, even if described as voluntary, creates conditions where applicants' immutable characteristics become part of the institutional record surrounding their application.

incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba's College of Community and Global Health advertised a faculty position stating it particularly invites applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, sex, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba's College of Community and Global Health advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position, posted on May 1, 2026, with an application deadline of May 31, 2026. The job posting stated that to enhance diversity and create role models, the college particularly invites applications from indigenous peoples, women, racially marginalized persons, persons with disabilities, persons of marginalized sexualities and genders, and those committed to a diverse environment. The Rady Faculty of Health Sciences, which includes the college, described its commitment to promoting opportunities in hiring for systemically marginalized groups including indigenous peoples, communities categorized by skin colour, disabled persons, and those who identify based on sexual orientation and gender identity. The posting indicated the institution takes proactive measures including implicit bias training for hiring panels and strives for diversity throughout the hiring process. The position itself focused on health equity and social justice research, with preference given to candidates with a PhD in health or social sciences. The advertisement explicitly framed hiring decisions around demographic categories rather than focusing solely on candidates' individual qualifications, research excellence, and teaching abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on immutable characteristics such as skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, sex, and sexual orientation rather than being assessed primarily as distinct individuals based on their scholarly merit, research potential, and teaching capabilities. This approach normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups during hiring processes, signaling that applicants' identities based on immutable traits are relevant factors in employment decisions. The practice undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated abilities, instead introducing demographic considerations into what should be an assessment of each candidate's unique qualifications and potential contributions to scholarship and teaching. Applicants who do not fit the specified demographic categories may perceive that their applications receive different consideration, while those who do fit these categories may question whether their selection was based on their individual accomplishments or their demographic characteristics. The institutional framing reduces people to members of groups rather than recognizing each candidate's inherent dignity as an individual with distinct experiences, perspectives, and capabilities that cannot be assumed from demographic proxies.

quotes.text: To enhance our College and create role models for a diverse population of students, we particularly invite application from those who can support and enhance our diversity, including Indigenous peoples, women, racially marginalized persons, persons with disabilities, persons of marginalized sexualities and genders, and those committed to a diverse environment.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly indicates the institution particularly invites applications based on demographic categories including ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, demonstrating how hiring preferences are framed around immutable characteristics rather than individual merit alone.

quotes.text: The Rady Faculty of Health Sciences is committed to the social justice principles of equity, access & participation and to promoting opportunities in hiring, promotion and tenure (where applicable) for systemically marginalized groups who have been excluded from full participation at the University and the larger community including Indigenous Peoples, Black, racially marginalized communities, disabled persons and those who identify as 2SLGBTQIA+
quotes.rationale: This passage describes the broader faculty commitment to promoting hiring opportunities based on demographic group membership, contextualizing the specific job posting within an institutional framework that categorizes candidates by immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: To address the Rady Faculty of Health Sciences commitment to equity, access and participation, and in recognition of the underrepresentation of members of historically and currently excluded groups, we take proactive measures including implicit bias training for all hiring panels. We strive for diversity and cultural safety throughout the hiring process (hiring panels, short-list of candidates, interviews).
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates the institution's systematic approach to incorporating demographic considerations throughout the hiring process, from panel composition to candidate evaluation, rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications and merit.

incidents.short_description: Trent University's Department of Sociology posted a faculty position that grants explicit hiring preference to candidates based on sex, skin colour, indigeneity, disability, and sexual orientation. Qualified applicants who do not belong to these listed demographic categories are placed at a structural disadvantage regardless of their individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 30, 2026, Trent University advertised a one-year limited term Assistant Professor position in the Department of Sociology, specialising in gender and sexualities. The posting states that preference will be given to candidates from a list of demographic categories: women, indigenous people (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities or racialized groups, and LGBTQ2+ people. The posting makes this preference explicit and formal, meaning that a candidate's demographic characteristics — rather than solely their qualifications, scholarship, and teaching ability — become a decisive factor in the hiring process. The position is otherwise open to all qualified applicants, but those outside the listed categories are disadvantaged at the point of selection.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated partly on the basis of immutable or personal characteristics — skin colour, sex, indigeneity, disability, and sexual orientation — rather than solely on their individual abilities, scholarship, and demonstrated teaching effectiveness. This preference system reduces individuals to demographic proxies, denying each applicant the right to be assessed purely on their own merits and unique qualities. Highly qualified individuals who fall outside the preferred categories are passed over not because of anything they have done or achieved, but because of who they are. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by demographic categories and undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.short_description: Trent University's job posting for an assistant professor position in psychology explicitly states that hiring preference will be given to candidates based on their sex, skin colour, indigeneity, disability, and sexual orientation. This reduces applicants to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 30, 2026, Trent University (Peterborough campus) posted a job advertisement for a 12-month limited term Assistant Professor position in Cognitive Psychology. The posting states that preference will be given to candidates from designated groups, specifically women, indigenous people, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities or racialized groups, and LGBTQ2+ people. The university also requests that applicants submit a self-identification form as part of their application package. Rather than selecting the most qualified candidate based on their academic credentials, teaching ability, and demonstrated merit, the university's stated hiring practice is to sort applicants by demographic categories and grant preference based on immutable personal characteristics.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are passed over for an academic position not because of any shortcoming in their abilities, teaching record, or scholarly contributions, but because of who they are demographically. This practice reduces individuals to proxies for group membership, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits and unique qualities. It also signals to the academic community that demographic characteristics, rather than individual achievement, are the deciding factor in career advancement at the institution.

incidents.institution_name: University of British Columbia, Department of Urologic Sciences
incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia posted a faculty position in urologic sciences that explicitly invites applications preferentially from candidates belonging to named demographic groups and requires applicants to submit a statement on ideological commitments as part of their application package.
incidents.description_of_event:
The University of British Columbia's Department of Urologic Sciences posted a full-time Assistant Professor, Grant Tenure Track position in kidney cancer, advertised on April 30, 2026. The job posting explicitly invited applications preferentially from candidates belonging to specific demographic categories — including indigenous peoples, people categorised by skin colour, people with disabilities, women, and people categorised by sexual orientation or gender identity — framing these demographic characteristics as relevant to the hiring process.

The application package required candidates to include, as part of their curriculum vitae letter, "a brief statement of your current, previous or envisioned contributions to advancing anti-racism, equity, diversity, decolonization, Indigenization and inclusion in an academic, professional, or community context." This requirement asks candidates to demonstrate alignment with a particular ideological framework as a condition of being considered for an academic medical position. The posting also stated that the successful candidate must demonstrate "a commitment to enhancing one's own awareness, knowledge, and skills related to equity, diversity, and inclusion."

The position is a clinical and research role in urologic oncology with clearly defined professional qualifications — including Royal College certification, fellowship training in urologic oncology, and laboratory expertise in genomics and metabolomics. The addition of ideological commitment statements and demographic preferences sits alongside these professional criteria as part of the formal hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated not solely on their professional expertise, clinical skills, and scholarly record, but also on their adherence to a prescribed ideological framework and their membership in designated demographic groups. This means candidates who hold different views, or who do not belong to the listed demographic categories, face a structurally disadvantaged application process regardless of their individual merit, character, or ability. The requirement to submit a statement of ideological contributions functions as a screening mechanism that favours those who publicly affirm particular political commitments, suppressing intellectual diversity and open discourse within the university. Patients who depend on the eventual appointment of the most qualified urologic oncologist are also affected, as hiring decisions influenced by demographic preferences and ideological conformity risk deprioritising clinical and research excellence. The practice normalises the institutional habit of categorising and sorting individuals by demographic characteristics rather than evaluating each person on their unique qualifications and demonstrated abilities.

incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary posted a faculty position that explicitly encourages applications from people based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting signals that membership in these groups is a consideration in the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Calgary advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in the Department of Oncology, posted on April 30, 2026. The job posting includes language stating that the university is committed to removing barriers historically encountered by 'some people in our society' and explicitly encourages members of named demographic groups — women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities, and people of diverse sexual orientation and gender identities — to apply. The posting frames membership in these groups as a relevant factor in the recruitment process, stating the university will 'support their academic and professional success' as a specific outcome of their demographic identity. This signals to applicants and selection committees that demographic characteristics are a consideration in evaluating candidates, rather than restricting assessment solely to qualifications, research productivity, and individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated in part based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research record, and abilities. This practice reduces people to group membership rather than recognising them as distinct individuals. Candidates who do not belong to the named demographic groups receive an implicit signal that the institution views their application less favourably, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own character and demonstrated abilities. It also entrenches the institutional habit of categorising people by immutable traits, normalising the idea that demographic identity is a legitimate basis for differential treatment in a professional hiring context.

incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary's job posting for an Assistant Professor in Oncology explicitly encourages applications from people belonging to specific demographic groups, signalling that hiring decisions are influenced by candidates' immutable characteristics rather than solely by their qualifications and abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Calgary posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in the Department of Oncology on April 30, 2026. The posting included a statement indicating that the university is committed to recruiting individuals who will enhance its 'diversity,' and explicitly encouraged members of designated demographic groups — including women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities, and people of diverse sexual orientation and gender identities — to apply. This language signals to candidates that membership in these demographic categories is a factor in the hiring process, rather than evaluating all individuals solely on their character, qualifications, research record, and demonstrated abilities. The posting frames certain candidates as preferred based on immutable or personal characteristics unrelated to the academic and research requirements of the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not belong to the named demographic groups receive a signal that they are less preferred, meaning their individual merits are discounted in favour of demographic characteristics. This reduces the likelihood that the best-qualified individual is selected for the role, undermining the integrity of the hiring process. Candidates from the named groups also face a situation where their selection is potentially linked to group membership rather than individual achievement, which diminishes recognition of their own abilities and efforts. The practice normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories and assigning different value to their applications based on immutable traits, eroding the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as a unique individual.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto (Dalla Lana School of Public Health – Institute of Health Policy, Management, and Evaluation)
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto posted a faculty position that explicitly encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, treating these demographic traits as relevant criteria in hiring.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Toronto's Dalla Lana School of Public Health advertised a tenure-stream Assistant Professor position in Health Informatics Systems and Policy Research, posted April 30, 2026. The job posting includes a diversity statement that explicitly singles out certain groups by skin colour, ancestry, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, and states that the university 'strongly encourages applications' from these groups. The posting further states that 'diverse perspectives, experiences, and expertise are essential to strengthening our academic mission,' framing demographic identity as an academic asset rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications, research record, and teaching ability. The university also collects voluntary demographic survey data from applicants, which, while stated to be confidential and aggregated, is part of an institutional framework that categorises applicants by demographic characteristics.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution signals that certain demographic identities are valued and encouraged in a hiring process, it introduces demographic sorting into what should be an evaluation of individual merit, ability, and character. Candidates are assessed, at least in part, through the lens of their demographic group membership rather than solely on their qualifications. This reduces every applicant to a set of immutable traits and sends a message that who a person is matters more than what they can do. It also undermines confidence in the fairness of the selection process for all applicants, as those who do not belong to the listed groups understand they are not equally encouraged to apply. The practice normalises institutional categorisation of individuals by demographic proxies and works against a hiring culture grounded in individual worth and demonstrated ability.

incidents.short_description: The University of Guelph posted four Department Chair positions in its College of Engineering that explicitly encourage applications from people based on demographic characteristics, and require all applicants to submit a statement on equity, diversity and inclusion.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Guelph advertised four Department Chair positions in its College of Engineering on April 30, 2026. The posting requires all applicants to submit a statement on equity, diversity and inclusion as a mandatory component of the application package, alongside a cover letter, curriculum vitae, and references. The posting explicitly states that the university "strongly welcomes and encourages applications from members of designated groups in employment, including indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, women, and 2SLGBTQIA+ persons." Candidates are also expected to demonstrate "commitment to Indigenization, equity, diversity, inclusion, and decolonial practices" as part of the stated qualifications. The university frames the hiring process around demographic categories rather than evaluating candidates solely on their academic credentials, leadership experience, and individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for these positions are assessed in part through the lens of their demographic characteristics rather than purely on their individual qualifications, scholarship, and leadership ability. Requiring all applicants to submit a statement on equity, diversity and inclusion introduces a values-based screening mechanism that can disadvantage those whose views differ from the university's preferred ideological framework, regardless of their professional excellence. Signalling strong preference for applicants from named demographic groups reduces the likelihood that all qualified individuals are evaluated on equal footing. This approach normalises the institutional practice of sorting people into demographic categories and treating membership in those categories as a qualification, undermining the principle that individuals deserve to be recognised for their unique abilities and character.

incidents.short_description: Western University's job posting for Dean of the Faculty of Social Science explicitly solicits applications from candidates based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, and sexual orientation, rather than inviting all qualified individuals to apply on equal footing.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University posted a job listing on April 30, 2026 for the position of Dean, Faculty of Social Science. The posting outlines a wide range of academic and leadership qualifications expected of the successful candidate. However, the posting also explicitly states that the university 'welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all gender identities and sexual orientations.' This language singles out applicants by demographic characteristics — sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — as part of the formal recruitment communication. Rather than simply affirming that all qualified candidates will be evaluated on their individual merits, the posting treats demographic identity as a relevant factor in the application process, directing certain groups to apply in a way that signals demographic background is being weighed alongside qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions signal in job postings that certain demographic characteristics are relevant to hiring, they undermine the principle that every candidate deserves to be evaluated as an individual based on their abilities, character, and experience. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups receive the implicit message that their applications are less welcome or will carry less institutional weight. This practice normalises the use of immutable traits as sorting criteria in hiring, eroding the expectation of equal individual consideration. It also reduces candidates from listed groups to representatives of a demographic category rather than recognising them for their unique qualifications. Over time, this approach entrenches the habit of evaluating people through the lens of group identity rather than singular worth, which runs counter to a merit-based and individually-focused institution.

incidents.institution_name: Western University (Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry)
incidents.short_description: Western University's job posting for a Department Chair includes a policy requiring candidates who identify as indigenous to have that identity verified through a university-controlled process as a condition of receiving any hiring advantage. Candidates are subject to scrutiny of their ancestry that other candidates are not.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University, in partnership with London Health Sciences Centre and St. Joseph's Health Care London, posted a position for Chair and Head of the Department of Anesthesia & Perioperative Medicine on April 30, 2026. Embedded in the posting is a policy statement titled 'Affirmation Policy Statement,' which describes a process whereby candidates who declare indigenous citizenship or membership for any material advantage in the hiring process — including where such identity is listed as required or preferred — must have that declaration verified through a 'relational accountability process' administered by the university's Office of Indigenous Initiatives. The policy is framed as a safeguard against incorrect or misleading declarations. Candidates who have self-identified and are shortlisted or invited to interview are required to submit to this affirmation process. The policy explicitly applies in circumstances where indigenous identity confers a material advantage in hiring. This means the university has established a hiring stream in which candidates are evaluated, at least in part, on the basis of their ancestry, and then subjected to institutional scrutiny of whether that ancestry is authentic enough to qualify.
incidents.expected_harm: The policy embeds ancestry-based criteria into hiring decisions, meaning candidates are evaluated not solely on their qualifications, character, and abilities, but on their demographic identity. Individuals who do not belong to the designated ancestral group are categorically excluded from any preference conferred under this scheme. Those who do claim such identity are subjected to a verification process — an institutional judgment about the authenticity of their ancestry — that no other candidates must undergo. This treats people as representatives of demographic categories rather than as individuals. It normalizes the institutional practice of sorting job applicants by ancestry and making advancement conditional on group membership, which undermines the principle that people deserve to be evaluated on their individual merits.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University's job posting for a Canada Impact+ Research Chair invites applicants to identify as members of demographic groups, with the implicit suggestion that group membership will be considered in the selection process. The posting frames individuals primarily through demographic categories rather than as individuals evaluated on their merits.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University of Newfoundland posted a Canada Impact+ Research Chair position in Critical Minerals and Energy Transition on April 30, 2026. The posting invites applicants to identify themselves as members of so-called equity-deserving groups and requires them to complete a demographic survey. The posting explicitly names a range of demographic categories — including sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, ethnicity, and disability status — as characteristics relevant to the application process. Candidates are told they cannot be considered as a member of these groups unless they complete the survey, and a link to the survey is issued by email after submission with a seven-day expiry. By building demographic self-identification into the formal application process in this way, the university signals that group membership is a factor in candidate assessment alongside qualifications and experience, rather than evaluating each candidate purely on individual merit, research excellence, and demonstrated ability.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed in part based on their membership in demographic groups rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and character. This reduces people to categorical labels and undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as a distinct individual. Candidates who do not identify with any named demographic group face a structural disadvantage unrelated to their qualifications or the quality of their research. The process normalises the institutional practice of sorting applicants by immutable traits, reinforcing the idea that group identity is a legitimate basis for professional advancement in an academic setting.

incidents.short_description: Western University's job posting for an assistant professor position includes a policy requiring candidates who have declared indigenous identity to have that declaration verified through a separate institutional process. The posting also lists demographic characteristics as factors in its employment equity approach.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University posted a three-year limited-term position in Financial/Actuarial Analytics on April 29, 2026. The posting includes an 'Affirmation Policy Statement' that singles out candidates who have declared indigenous citizenship or membership for an additional verification step not required of other applicants. Candidates who have made such a declaration for 'material advantage' — including where it was listed as a required or preferred qualification — must have their declaration affirmed through a process led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives. The policy is described as a safeguard against incorrect or misleading declarations. Additionally, the posting's employment equity section lists specific demographic groups — women, members of racialized groups, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity — as groups whose applications are particularly welcomed, reflecting the university's broader practice of evaluating candidates in part based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on individual merit, qualifications, and demonstrated ability.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and processed differently based on their declared identity, with one group singled out for an additional institutional vetting process not applied to others. This creates a two-tier application experience based on a person's background rather than their qualifications. The employment equity framing further embeds the practice of sorting applicants by demographic category, reinforcing the institutional habit of viewing people through the lens of group membership rather than as individuals with unique qualifications, experience, and character.

incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba posted a job listing for an instructor in biological anthropology that explicitly invites applications from candidates based on their demographic characteristics, including skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation. Qualified candidates are effectively ranked in part by their membership in preferred demographic groups rather than solely on their individual abilities and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba's Department of Anthropology posted a two-year full-time term position for an Instructor I in Biological Anthropology, with a posting date of April 29, 2026. The posting outlines teaching responsibilities and required qualifications, including a Ph.D. in Anthropology and demonstrated excellence or potential in teaching. However, the posting also explicitly states that the university 'particularly invite[s] applications' from candidates based on their skin colour, indigeneity, sex, disability, sexual orientation, and gender identity, framing this preference as a means to 'create role models for a diverse population of students.' The university's institutional statement further reinforces this approach by committing to promoting hiring, promotion, and tenure opportunities specifically for listed demographic groups. Candidates are therefore evaluated not only on their academic qualifications and teaching ability, but on their membership in particular demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed partly on the basis of immutable personal characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching ability, and professional merit. This practice disadvantages qualified candidates who do not belong to the preferred demographic groups, reducing individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their unique abilities and character. The framing that certain groups are needed as 'role models' implies that students can only be inspired or served by instructors who share their demographic characteristics, which is itself a reductive view of both students and faculty. This normalises the institutional habit of sorting and ranking people by group membership, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.short_description: Athabasca University's job posting for two counselling psychology faculty positions explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic characteristics including sex, disability, and sexual orientation, among others, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their qualifications and individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Athabasca University (AU) posted two tenure-track Assistant/Associate Professor positions in its Master of Counselling program, advertised on April 29, 2026. The posting includes a section titled 'Considerations in applying' that explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on a list of demographic categories: women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, persons of any gender identity and expression, and members of equity-deserving groups. The posting frames the university as an inclusive workplace and references its strategic plan's commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion. Rather than assessing all candidates on the basis of their qualifications, research record, and teaching effectiveness alone, the posting singles out particular demographic groups for explicit encouragement, signalling that group membership is a relevant consideration in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution signals that demographic group membership is a relevant factor in hiring, it shifts the evaluation of candidates away from their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. Candidates who do not belong to the named groups receive an implicit message that the institution views them less favourably, not because of anything they have done or achieved, but because of immutable or personal characteristics. This reduces every applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than recognizing them as distinct individuals. Over time, this approach normalises the idea that institutions should sort and preference people based on group identity, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits and circumstances.

incidents.short_description: McMaster University's job posting for an Assistant Professor in Mathematics explicitly invites applications from people based on their demographic characteristics, and requires all applicants to submit a statement on their personal commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion as a condition of a complete application.
incidents.description_of_event: McMaster University posted a two-year Contractually Limited Appointment for an Assistant Professor in Mathematics on April 29, 2026. The posting states that the university "especially welcomes applications" from candidates identified by specific demographic categories, including first nations, métis and inuit peoples, members of racialized communities, persons with disabilities, women, and persons who identify as 2SLGBTQ+. This language signals that candidates' demographic characteristics are a factor in how their applications are received, rather than evaluating each applicant purely on their academic qualifications, teaching ability, and professional record. In addition, the posting requires all applicants to submit a statement of personal experience and plans for advancing equity, diversity and inclusion in post-secondary or professional settings as a mandatory component of a complete application. Applicants who do not provide this statement are deemed to have submitted an incomplete application. A Diversity Survey is also solicited from all applicants through the submission portal, with the stated purpose of broadening the diversity of the applicant pool.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are disadvantaged in the application process based on their demographic characteristics rather than their abilities and professional record. The mandatory EDI statement requirement functions as an ideological screen, compelling applicants to demonstrate alignment with a particular institutional viewpoint as a condition of being considered. This reduces hiring decisions to a process that weighs demographic identity and ideological conformity alongside — or above — professional merit, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. Candidates who do not belong to the listed demographic groups, or who hold different perspectives on EDI approaches, face structural disadvantage regardless of their qualifications. Over time, such practices erode the norm that academic appointments should be made on the basis of knowledge, skill, and demonstrated ability.

incidents.short_description: NOSM University's job posting for a Human Anatomy Lecturer explicitly invites applications from candidates based on demographic categories including sex, visible minority status, indigeneity, disability, and sexual orientation or gender identity. This reflects an institutional practice of evaluating applicants through demographic lenses rather than purely on individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: NOSM University posted a tenure-track Lecturer position in Human Anatomy on April 29, 2026, advertised through University Affairs. The posting describes qualifications related to anatomy teaching, cadaveric experience, and scholarly engagement. However, the posting includes a statement that the university is committed to employment equity and specifically welcomes applications from women, visible minorities, indigenous people, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity. This language signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in the hiring process, meaning applicants are not being evaluated purely as individuals based on their qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated needs. The institution categorizes prospective employees by sex, appearance, ancestry, disability status, and sexual orientation as part of its recruitment approach.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed in part through the lens of demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and character. This practice reduces people to group membership and signals that belonging to certain demographic categories carries weight in hiring decisions. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups are disadvantaged not because of any shortcoming in their abilities or character, but because of immutable or personal traits. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people by demographic proxies, undermining a merit-based hiring process and the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta's Faculty of Engineering job posting for a Geoenvironmental Engineering faculty position explicitly encourages applications from people based on their sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation, signalling that demographic characteristics are a factor in the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta posted a tenure-track faculty position in Geoenvironmental Engineering on April 29, 2026, advertised through University Affairs. The posting describes a cluster hiring initiative within the Faculty of Engineering. Near the end of the posting, the University states that the positions are part of a priority to "build a diverse faculty" and that "candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply." A separate boilerplate statement goes further, explicitly naming women, first nations, métis and inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression as groups the University specifically encourages to apply. Rather than evaluating all candidates solely on their academic qualifications, research record, and teaching ability, the posting frames demographic characteristics as relevant factors in who should seek the position. The stated purpose is the "further diversification of ideas," but the mechanism used is to sort and direct candidates by their demographic traits.
incidents.expected_harm: When a university signals that certain demographic groups are preferred or more welcome to apply, it communicates to all candidates that their demographic identity is a factor in how they will be assessed, not just their qualifications, research potential, and teaching ability. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups receive the message that they are less preferred. Candidates who do belong to the listed groups face the possibility that their success will be attributed to their demographic identity rather than their individual merits, undermining their professional standing. This approach reduces each applicant to a set of demographic categories rather than recognising them as a distinct individual with unique abilities and accomplishments. It normalises institutional sorting of people by immutable characteristics and embeds demographic preferences into hiring structures at publicly funded universities.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta's Faculty of Engineering posted a faculty position that explicitly encourages applications from people based on demographic characteristics including sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. The posting signals that hiring decisions are influenced by these immutable traits rather than solely by individual merit and qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta's Faculty of Engineering posted a tenure-track faculty position in Water Resources Engineering on April 29, 2026. The posting describes a cluster of five positions and states that the positions are part of the university's priority to build a 'diverse faculty,' explicitly noting that 'candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply.' A separate statement at the close of the posting goes further, explicitly naming women, first nations, métis and inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression as groups the university encourages to apply. This language signals that an applicant's demographic characteristics — their sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation — are factors considered in the hiring process, rather than limiting consideration strictly to each candidate's qualifications, abilities, research potential, and teaching effectiveness.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated in part based on their membership in demographic categories rather than purely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and potential. This approach reduces people to proxies for group identity and undermines the principle that each person deserves to be considered on their own merits. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the named groups receive a less encouraging signal that their applications are welcome, creating a two-tiered recruitment environment. The practice normalizes the idea that institutions should sort and prefer individuals based on immutable traits, eroding the standard that advancement should reflect individual character and demonstrated ability.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University posted a faculty position that explicitly prefers candidates based on their demographic identity and lived experience tied to group membership, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on qualifications and ability.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Mechatronics Engineering at its Barrie STEM Hub, posted on April 29, 2026. The job posting states that the university 'prefer[s] candidates with the knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience' associated with designated demographic groups, and that 'a lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred.' The posting also encourages candidates to self-identify if they belong to an 'under-represented group,' and frames demographic group membership itself as a qualification. While the posting lists rigorous academic and professional qualifications — including a PhD, research excellence, engineering licensure eligibility, and teaching effectiveness — it layers on top of these a stated preference for candidates based on their identity characteristics and the experiences assumed to flow from those characteristics. This treats demographic group membership as a hiring asset, directing the institution to evaluate candidates in part based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, or other immutable traits rather than purely on their individual merits, abilities, and demonstrated competencies.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are disadvantaged in a competitive hiring process based on their demographic characteristics rather than their individual abilities, qualifications, or the actual merit of their work. The institution signals that a person's value as a prospective colleague is partly determined by which demographic group they belong to, reducing individuals to proxies for group identity. This undermines the principle that people deserve to be evaluated on their character, skills, and demonstrated contributions. It also sets a precedent within the university that demographic categorisation is an appropriate and desirable lens through which to assess individuals, normalising the practice of sorting people by immutable traits in institutional decision-making.

incidents.short_description: Brock University's Department of Dramatic Arts posted a faculty position that requires candidates to demonstrate ideological commitments as an essential qualification and encourages applicants to self-identify by demographic group as part of the application process.
incidents.description_of_event:
Brock University posted a one-year Assistant Professor position in Performance Scenography in its Department of Dramatic Arts, advertised on April 29, 2026. The posting requires candidates to provide, as an essential qualification, demonstrated expertise in 'anti-supremacist approaches to teaching theatre.' Applicants are required to submit a teaching dossier that includes evidence of this ideological orientation alongside standard evidence of teaching quality. The department's stated identity frames its institutional mission around an 'intersectional, anti-supremacist understanding of equity' and a ten-year process of revising hiring, curriculum, and programming practices according to this framework.

The posting also invites applicants from a list of named demographic groups — including women, indigenous peoples, black people, people with disabilities, members of visible minorities, and individuals identified by sexual orientation and gender identity — and encourages them to voluntarily self-identify using a designated form submitted as part of the application. The university explicitly describes these groups as 'designated groups,' signalling that membership in one or more of them is a relevant consideration in the selection process. Candidates are evaluated not solely on their professional qualifications and individual merit, but on the basis of demographic characteristics and adherence to a prescribed ideological framework.
incidents.expected_harm:
Requiring candidates to demonstrate adherence to a specific ideological framework as an essential qualification functions as a political or ideological test for employment. Applicants who hold different views about how to approach theatre education — even if they are otherwise highly qualified — are screened out not on the basis of their abilities or professional excellence, but because they do not conform to a particular set of ideological commitments. This narrows the intellectual environment of the institution and restricts the diversity of perspectives available to students.

Encouraging applicants to self-identify by demographic group as part of the application process introduces demographic categorization into hiring decisions. Qualified candidates who do not belong to, or who choose not to identify with, named demographic groups are disadvantaged relative to those who do. The practice reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and professional contributions. Over time, institutionalizing demographic-based hiring criteria erodes the principle that each person deserves recognition as an individual and that advancement should reflect merit and demonstrated skill.

incidents.short_description: The University of Winnipeg posted a Dean of Science position that encourages applicants from specific demographic groups to self-identify and signals preferential consideration based on immutable characteristics rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Winnipeg posted a job listing for Dean of the Faculty of Science on April 29, 2026. The posting includes an employment equity section that names specific demographic groups — women, racialized persons, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and 2SLGBTQ+ persons — and explicitly encourages members of these groups to self-identify at the time of application. By singling out applicants for preferential treatment based on demographic categories such as skin colour, sex, and sexual orientation, the institution signals that demographic characteristics are a factor in assessing candidates alongside their qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities. The posting also lists a commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion as a core responsibility of the role, and asks the successful candidate to advance these frameworks across teaching, research, and faculty operations.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated in part based on their demographic characteristics — such as skin colour, sex, or sexual orientation — rather than solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and merit. This creates a system in which immutable traits influence hiring outcomes, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own abilities and character. Candidates who do not belong to the named demographic groups face a structural disadvantage regardless of their qualifications. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people into demographic categories and reinforces the idea that group membership is a relevant credential, eroding the standard that employment decisions should reflect individual worth and demonstrated capability.

incidents.short_description: Vancouver International Airport and Air Canada publicly celebrated a flight operated by a crew selected entirely on the basis of ethnicity, framing the practice as reconciliation and industry representation.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 29, 2026, Vancouver International Airport (YVR) posted on X to celebrate an Air Canada flight operated by what was described as an all-indigenous crew. The post was made in partnership with the Musqueam Nation and the First Nations Major Projects Coalition. YVR framed the arrangement as part of walking "the path towards reconciliation and representation" in the aviation industry. The post treated crew composition based on ethnicity as a cause for celebration and a model for the industry, signalling institutional approval for staffing decisions made on the basis of ethnic background rather than individual merit, qualifications, or demonstrated need.
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions publicly celebrate staffing arrangements based on ethnicity, they normalise the practice of evaluating and selecting individuals based on their demographic background rather than their character, abilities, or qualifications. Aviation crew members not of the favoured ethnicity are excluded from such arrangements, and the public celebration sends a message that ethnicity is a legitimate and praiseworthy basis for crew composition. This reinforces the division of workers into demographic categories rather than recognising each person as an individual deserving equal consideration on their own merits. It also sets a precedent for other institutions to adopt similar practices, normalising ethnic sorting in professional settings and undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

quotes.text: Today, we're proud to celebrate an @AirCanada flight operated by an all-Indigenous crew.
quotes.rationale: This is the central claim of the post — YVR and Air Canada explicitly celebrated a flight crew composed entirely on the basis of ethnicity, framing ethnic sorting as a positive institutional achievement.

incidents.institution_name: University of Saskatchewan - Western College of Veterinary Medicine
incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan's Western College of Veterinary Medicine posted a job advertisement that explicitly encouraged applications from candidates based on their ethnicity and race, rather than evaluating all candidates as individuals based on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Saskatchewan's Western College of Veterinary Medicine posted a job advertisement on April 28, 2026, for a full-time, 3-year term Lecturer position in Large Animal Internal Medicine. The posting included detailed information about the position's requirements, including a DVM degree, completion of an approved residency, and eligibility for professional certification. The advertisement contained a statement indicating that the university 'aspires to be what the world needs and embraces equity, diversity and inclusion as foundational to excellence and innovation' and that 'applications from equity-deserving groups are encouraged.' The posting further stated that the university continues to grow partnerships with indigenous communities and 'value[s] the unique perspective that Indigenous employees provide.' The advertisement included a verification process where successful candidates 'that assert Indigenous membership/citizenship will be asked to complete the verification process of Indigenous membership/citizenship with documentation.' These statements indicated that the institution was encouraging applications based on demographic categories and ethnicity rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities relevant to the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to identify themselves by demographic categories and ethnicity rather than being evaluated purely as individuals based on their veterinary qualifications, teaching abilities, and professional accomplishments. The institutional message suggests that demographic characteristics are relevant factors in the hiring process, normalizing the practice of categorizing candidates by immutable traits rather than their unique merits. Candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic groups receive a signal that the institution considers group membership as a valued attribute, potentially undermining their sense that they will be evaluated fairly based on individual qualifications. The practice entrenches the notion that institutions should sort and evaluate people according to demographic proxies rather than assessing each person's distinct capabilities, experience, and potential contributions. The verification process for indigenous membership creates a system where candidates are required to prove demographic credentials, further institutionalizing the practice of treating demographic categories as relevant employment qualifications rather than irrelevant characteristics that should not factor into assessments of individual merit.

incidents.short_description: McGill University posted a job opening for Assistant Professor that encouraged applications from candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and other immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Neurology and Neurosurgery posted a job advertisement on April 28, 2026, for a Contract Academic Staff position at the rank of Assistant Professor (Research) in Metabolism and Brain. The posting was advertised until May 28, 2026. The job description included standard academic qualifications and research expectations. However, the posting stated that McGill is 'committed to equity and diversity' and explicitly welcomed and encouraged applications from people based on immutable characteristics including skin colour ('racialized persons/visible minorities'), sex ('women'), ethnicity ('Indigenous persons', 'ethnic minorities'), disability status ('persons with disabilities'), sexual orientation ('persons of minority sexual orientations'), and gender identity ('persons of minority gender identities'). The institution framed this encouragement as part of its commitment to equity and diversity, stating it 'values academic rigour and excellence' while simultaneously categorizing potential applicants by demographic characteristics. The posting also indicated that McGill 'implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify.' This represented an institutional practice of evaluating and sorting candidates based on demographic categories rather than solely on individual merit, qualifications, and ability to perform the academic role.
incidents.expected_harm: The hiring practice sends a message that the institution evaluates candidates partly through the lens of their immutable characteristics rather than exclusively as individuals with unique qualifications, experiences, and capabilities. Candidates are encouraged to view themselves and be viewed by the institution through demographic categories, undermining recognition of their singular worth as distinct individuals. This categorization normalizes the practice of institutions sorting people into groups based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to conduct research, supervise students, or contribute to academic excellence. The explicit encouragement of applications based on demographic characteristics creates a system where individuals may feel pressure to identify themselves by immutable traits to receive favourable consideration, compromising their privacy and autonomy. The practice reinforces the idea that demographic proxies should play a role in professional advancement rather than character, demonstrated abilities, and individual merit. It establishes a precedent where institutions publicly signal that they make hiring decisions with reference to demographic categories, eroding confidence that all candidates will be evaluated fairly based on their individual qualifications and potential contributions to the academic mission.

incidents.institution_name: McGill University, Department of Neurology and Neurosurgery
incidents.short_description: McGill University's Department of Neurology and Neurosurgery advertised a research faculty position stating the institution welcomes applications from individuals based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating all candidates equally on merit.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Neurology and Neurosurgery posted a job advertisement for an Assistant Professor (Research) position in metabolism and the brain on April 28, 2026. The posting was advertised until May 28, 2026. While the position description outlined standard academic requirements including a PhD in neurosciences, postdoctoral training, research productivity, and ability to secure competitive research funding, the advertisement included institutional language stating that McGill 'welcomes applications from racialized persons and visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities.' This framing presents certain demographic categories as particularly welcome or favoured in the application process, rather than stating that all qualified candidates will be evaluated equally based on their individual qualifications, research record, and potential contributions.
