The posting treats demographic characteristics as relevant factors in fostering academic excellence rather than focusing exclusively on individual merit, abilities, and demonstrated teaching qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Prospective instructors are encouraged to present themselves to the institution through demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching excellence, and professional accomplishments. This approach normalizes the practice of institutions considering applicants' immutable characteristics as relevant factors in hiring decisions, even when claiming merit-based selection. Candidates learn that their demographic identity may be viewed as contributing to institutional goals of diversity, rather than being evaluated purely as distinct individuals with unique skills and experiences. The framing undermines the principle that people should be assessed based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated competencies alone. By linking demographic categories to academic excellence, the institution suggests that certain immutable traits have inherent value for achieving educational goals, rather than treating all applicants with equal dignity regardless of their background. This creates an environment where individuals may feel their demographic identity is being weighed alongside or instead of their professional qualifications, compromising the integrity of merit-based evaluation and the recognition of each person's singular worth.

quotes.text: We encourage applications from members of groups that have been marginalized on any grounds enumerated under the B.C. Human Rights Code, including sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, racialization, disability, political belief, religion, marital or family status, age, and/or status as a First Nation, Métis, Inuit, or Indigenous person.
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement that explicitly encourages applications based on demographic and immutable characteristics, treating candidates as members of groups rather than as distinct individuals.

incidents.short_description: Saint Mary's University posted a job for an assistant professor position stating that while all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, preference will be given to applicants based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Saint Mary's University advertised a tenure-track assistant professor position in sociology with a posting date of April 23, 2026, and application deadline of October 1, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is committed to fostering an environment of inclusion and respect and welcomed applications from specific demographic groups. The posting explicitly stated: 'All qualified candidates are encouraged to apply; however, preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents.' This was immediately preceded by a statement welcoming applications from women, indigenous peoples, racialized persons/visible minorities, persons with disabilities, persons of minority sexual orientation or gender identity and others who might contribute to the growth and enrichment of the community. The juxtaposition of these statements creates ambiguity about whether hiring preference extends beyond citizenship status to include the demographic categories listed. The framing suggests the university evaluates candidates not solely on their individual qualifications for teaching sociology, research expertise, and ability to engage students, but also considers demographic characteristics as factors in hiring decisions.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated based on demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching ability, research merit, and capacity to serve students. This approach signals that institutions may weigh immutable characteristics in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated abilities. Applicants who do not fall into the specified demographic categories may reasonably perceive that their applications will receive different consideration based on characteristics unrelated to their scholarly work or teaching effectiveness. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of individuals into demographic groups rather than recognizing each candidate's unique qualities, experiences, and potential contributions. This framing fails to evaluate people as distinct individuals and instead reduces them to membership in categories defined by immutable traits, contradicting the principle that institutions should assess people based on character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications.

quotes.text: At Saint Mary's University equity and diversity are integral to excellence and enrich our community. As an institution committed to fostering an environment of inclusion and respect, we welcome applications from women, Indigenous peoples, racialized persons/visible minorities, persons with disabilities, persons of minority sexual orientation or gender identity and others who might contribute to the growth and enrichment of our community. All qualified candidates are encouraged to apply; however, preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents.
quotes.rationale: This statement demonstrates the institution's practice of categorizing applicants by demographic characteristics and contains language about preferences in hiring. The juxtaposition of welcoming specific demographic groups with the preference statement creates ambiguity about whether preferences extend beyond citizenship.

incidents.short_description: Acadia University's Vaughan Memorial Library posted a job advertisement stating that candidates from certain demographic categories would be prioritized in hiring based on their immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 23, 2026, Acadia University posted a job advertisement for a 12-month half-time Librarian position at the Vaughan Memorial Library. The posting stated that while the university invites applications from all qualified individuals, 'candidates eligible to work in Canada shall be given priority.' The advertisement then specified that the university 'encourages applications from Mi'kmaq and other Indigenous Peoples; African Canadians; African Nova Scotians; persons with disabilities; visible minorities; women; persons of any minority sexuality, minority gender identity, or minority gender expression; and all intersections of these identities.' The posting framed this encouragement within a broader institutional commitment to 'equity, diversity, and inclusion' and described these principles as 'fundamental in creating an expansive academic environment.' The job advertisement required all applicants to complete an 'Employment Equity Self-Identification Form' and stated that failure to complete this form by the closing date would result in disqualification of the application. This requirement meant that applicants were compelled to categorize themselves by demographic characteristics as a condition of having their application considered. The position itself involved liaison responsibilities for the Department of Economics and coordination of outreach and events at the library.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and sorted by the institution based on their immutable characteristics rather than solely on their qualifications, abilities, and merit for the librarian position. The mandatory self-identification requirement forces individuals to categorize themselves by demographic characteristics and disqualifies those who decline to do so, regardless of their professional qualifications. This practice normalizes institutional evaluation of people through demographic proxies rather than their individual capabilities, experience, and character. The explicit encouragement of applications based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status sends a message that these immutable traits are relevant factors in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed based on what they can contribute to the role. Qualified candidates who do not fit the encouraged categories may perceive that their applications will receive less consideration, discouraging them from applying and creating a system where access to employment opportunities is influenced by demographic categorization. The approach divides applicants into preferred and non-preferred groups based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to perform the duties of a librarian, rather than fostering a hiring process that recognizes the singular worth of each candidate.

quotes.text: Acadia University invites applications from all qualified individuals; however, candidates eligible to work in Canada shall be given priority. The University encourages applications from Mi'kmaq and other Indigenous Peoples; African Canadians; African Nova Scotians; persons with disabilities; visible minorities; women; persons of any minority sexuality, minority gender identity, or minority gender expression; and all intersections of these identities.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly states that the university encourages applications based on demographic categories including ethnicity, skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status, demonstrating institutional prioritization of applicants based on immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: All applicants are required to submit an online Employment Equity Self-Identification Form as part of their application package. The form provides the option of self-identifying based on the applicant's comfort level. Failure to complete the form by the closing date will result in the disqualification of your application.
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates that applicants are compelled to categorize themselves by demographic characteristics as a mandatory condition of application, with disqualification as the consequence for non-completion, forcing individuals to submit to demographic categorization.

incidents.institution_name: Territorial Court of the Northwest Territories
incidents.short_description: A Northwest Territories judge overrode a defendant's stated self-identification and ordered a sentencing report specifically designated for people classified as indigenous, insisting the man's background made him subject to a different sentencing process than he himself claimed applied to him.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 22, 2026, Judge Robert David Gorin of the Territorial Court of the Northwest Territories issued a decision in the sentencing of Jeremy Kuneyuna, who had pleaded guilty to breaking into a hotel. Kuneyuna, who has approximately 90 prior criminal convictions, identified himself in court as Caucasian. The judge rejected this self-identification, stating in his written decision that the defendant "is plainly not Caucasian" and that his background is indigenous, partly on the basis of his surname and prior dealings with the accused in previous court proceedings. The judge ordered the production of a Gladue report — a form of pre-sentence report used specifically for offenders classified as indigenous — before proceeding with sentencing. The judge grounded his order in the Supreme Court of Canada decisions in Gladue and Ipeelee, which require sentencing judges to consider circumstances specific to indigenous offenders, including the impacts of colonialism and intergenerational trauma. Although both Kuneyuna and the Crown had jointly submitted a recommended six-month sentence, the judge declined to accept that submission without first obtaining the ethnicity-specific report. Sentencing was adjourned to June 5, 2026, with Kuneyuna remanded in custody in the interim.
incidents.expected_harm: The court's approach illustrates how a legal framework built around classifying people by ancestry and ethnicity places judges in the position of determining a person's background against that person's own stated identity. The individual is denied the ability to define himself, and instead has an ethnic classification imposed upon him by an institution. While the Gladue framework is intended to be beneficial, the process of a court overriding a person's self-stated identity and assigning him to an ethnically designated category treats ethnicity as an objective, court-determinable fact rather than as a dimension of individual identity. This normalises institutional sorting of individuals into demographic categories as a prerequisite for legal proceedings, and entrenches a sentencing system in which outcomes are shaped not only by individual circumstances but by assigned group membership. It also keeps the accused in custody for an extended period pending production of the ethnicity-specific report, meaning the classification process itself has immediate consequences for his liberty.

quotes.text: Mr. Kuneyuna states that he is 'Caucasian' and on that basis has provided no information relating to case specific factors relating to his background as an indigenous offender.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the defendant identified himself as Caucasian and that the court nonetheless required him to be treated as falling under an ethnicity-specific legal category.

quotes.text: When I asked him about why he had an Inuit surname, he provided no meaningful response. Also, he is plainly not Caucasian. Rather, his background is indigenous. I will say that I know this from dealings I have had with Mr. Kuneyuna in previous court proceedings.
quotes.rationale: Shows the judge asserting an ethnic classification of the defendant over the defendant's own stated identity, based on his surname and the judge's personal impressions from prior proceedings.

quotes.text: his erroneous position as to his heritage does not amount to a waiver of the need for this court to make the inquiry as to his background factors that are required by the cases of Gladue and Ipeelee
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the court treating the defendant's self-identification as legally irrelevant and requiring ethnicity-specific proceedings regardless of his wishes.

quotes.text: I require more information than he is prepared to provide me. While I do not require a complete pre-sentence report, I do require a report that provides me with his background factors as an indigenous offender.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the court's order for an ethnicity-specific report, reinforcing that sentencing is conditioned on ethnic classification.

incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia's job posting for Dean of Health and Social Development states that applications from people categorized by demographic characteristics including sex, sexual orientation, skin colour, disability, religion, and other immutable traits are encouraged, while noting Canadians and permanent residents receive priority.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia posted a position for Dean, Faculty of Health and Social Development on April 22, 2026, advertised until May 22, 2026. The posting included a statement encouraging applications from people based on demographic categories enumerated under the B.C. Human Rights Code, including sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, skin colour, disability, political belief, religion, marital or family status, age, and status as first nations, métis, inuit, or indigenous person. The statement described these groups as having been 'marginalized' and framed the hiring preference as essential to academic excellence. The posting stated that 'equity and diversity are essential to academic excellence' and that 'an open and diverse community fosters the inclusion of voices that have been underrepresented or discouraged.' While all qualified candidates were stated to be encouraged to apply, the posting explicitly noted that Canadians and permanent residents would be given priority. The institution's approach signals to applicants that their demographic characteristics are relevant factors in the hiring process, rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications, scholarly record, leadership capabilities, and fit for the specific role.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants learn that the institution evaluates candidates through the lens of demographic categories rather than solely as individuals with unique qualifications and potential contributions. The message sent is that immutable characteristics are relevant to academic hiring decisions, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit, character, and demonstrated abilities. Candidates who do not fall into the enumerated categories may reasonably conclude their applications face additional scrutiny or reduced likelihood of success, regardless of their individual qualifications. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people by demographic proxies, reinforcing the idea that individuals should be understood primarily through group membership rather than their distinct qualities. The statement creates an environment where applicants' immutable traits become salient in a professional context where individual scholarly achievement, leadership vision, and ability to advance the faculty's mission should be the evaluative criteria. This approach fails to recognize singular worth by making demographic categorization a stated institutional consideration in evaluating candidates for senior academic leadership.

quotes.text: Equity and diversity are essential to academic excellence. An open and diverse community fosters the inclusion of voices that have been underrepresented or discouraged. We encourage applications from members of groups that have been marginalized on any grounds enumerated under the B.C. Human Rights Code, including sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, racialization, disability, political belief, religion, marital or family status, age, and/or status as a First Nation, Metis, Inuit, or Indigenous person.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories and immutable characteristics, making these traits relevant to the hiring process rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications and merit. It frames demographic categorization as essential to academic excellence.

incidents.short_description: The University of Guelph posted a faculty position stating that while all qualified individuals may apply, priority will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents, and the university strongly encourages applications from members of designated demographic groups.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Guelph posted a job advertisement on April 22, 2026, for an Assistant or Associate Professor position in Supply Chain Management within the School of Hospitality, Food and Tourism Management. The posting stated that the university invites applications from all qualified individuals but explicitly notes that Canadians and permanent residents will be given priority. The advertisement also stated that the university strongly welcomes and encourages applications from members of designated groups in employment, specifically listing indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, women, and 2SLGBTQIA+ persons. The job posting framed this approach within the university's commitment to fostering inclusive excellence through promoting Indigenization, equity, diversity, inclusion, and accessibility. The advertisement presented these hiring preferences as part of the university's strategic plan to recruit a diverse student, faculty and staff body and create equitable work environments.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and merit for the academic position. The explicit prioritization of applications from certain demographic groups signals that immutable characteristics factor into hiring decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed based on their character, scholarly achievements, and demonstrated capabilities. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the preferred demographic categories receive the message that their applications are less welcomed or valued, regardless of their individual merit or potential contributions to the institution. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic categories and treating these categories as relevant factors in employment decisions, rather than recognizing each applicant's singular worth as an individual scholar. This approach entrenches the idea that institutions should evaluate people through the lens of group membership rather than individual achievement, potentially excluding excellent candidates based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to fulfill the position's responsibilities in teaching, research, and service.

quotes.text: The University of Guelph invites applications from all qualified individuals; however, Canadians and permanent residents will be given priority. We strongly welcome and encourage applications from members of designated groups in employment, including Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, women, and 2SLGBTQIA+ persons.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly states the university's hiring preferences, indicating that while all may apply, priority is given to certain groups. It demonstrates institutional categorization of applicants by demographic characteristics and signals preferential consideration based on these categories.

quotes.text: The University of Guelph commits to fostering inclusive excellence through promoting Indigenization, equity, diversity, inclusion, and accessibility. Inclusion is a core pillar of Our Time, our strategic plan, which pledges to 'transform our human resources, governance and student services and practices across the University to enable us to recruit a diverse student, faculty and staff body and support them in achieving their personal, professional and scholarly goals.'
quotes.rationale: This passage provides context for the institutional framework driving the hiring preferences, showing how the university frames recruitment through demographic diversity goals rather than purely merit-based individual assessment.

incidents.short_description: Carleton University posted a tenure-track nursing faculty position restricted to applicants based on their ethnicity, excluding qualified candidates based on immutable characteristics rather than evaluating individuals on merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Carleton University advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Indigenous Nursing within the Faculty of Science Nursing Program, posted on April 22, 2026. The position was for a preliminary (tenure-track) appointment beginning July 1, 2026. The posting explicitly stated that the selection would be limited to applicants based on their ethnicity: 'In accordance with the University's Employment Equity Program and pursuant to the Ontario Human Rights Code Section 14, this selection will be limited to Indigenous peoples.' The restriction appeared in a section describing the university's commitment to diversity, alongside a statement that the position is 'an Indigenous-specific position limited to First Nations, Métis, and Inuit applicants.' The university restricted eligibility for this faculty position based on ethnic background rather than evaluating candidates as individuals based on their qualifications, teaching excellence, clinical experience, and demonstrated ability to contribute to nursing education. The posting outlined extensive qualifications including an active nursing license, PhD or equivalent, evidence of research excellence in Indigenous Health, and demonstrated teaching excellence, but made ethnic background a threshold requirement that superseded all other qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified nursing educators are excluded from employment opportunities based on their ethnicity rather than being evaluated as individuals on their character, abilities, clinical expertise, and teaching qualifications. This practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into ethnic categories and makes immutable characteristics the primary basis for professional opportunity, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect merit and individual qualities. The restriction sends a message that institutions may properly evaluate candidates first by demographic category rather than by their unique qualifications and potential contributions. Nursing students are denied the opportunity to learn from the most qualified educators, as the hiring process excludes candidates based on ethnic background rather than assembling faculty based on teaching excellence and expertise. The practice undermines shared civic belonging within academic communities by institutionalizing the view that certain positions belong to certain ethnic groups, rather than fostering an environment where all individuals compete and collaborate based on their distinct qualities and contributions.

quotes.text: In accordance with the University's Employment Equity Program and pursuant to the Ontario Human Rights Code Section 14, this selection will be limited to Indigenous peoples.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly restricts the hiring process to candidates based on ethnic background, demonstrating that the institution is evaluating applicants by demographic category rather than individual merit.

quotes.text: This is an Indigenous-specific position limited to First Nations, Métis, and Inuit applicants.
quotes.rationale: This reinforces that the position excludes candidates based on ethnicity, making ethnic background a threshold requirement that supersedes all other qualifications.

quotes.text: Applicants will be subject to review under the Hiring Policy for Positions Limited to Indigenous (First Nation, Inuit, and Métis) Candidates.
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates that there is an institutional policy framework that enables and governs ethnicity-based hiring restrictions, showing this is not an isolated incident but part of broader institutional practice.

incidents.short_description: The University of New Brunswick posted a job advertisement for President and Vice Chancellor stating that preference would be given to applicants based on demographic categories including skin colour and ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of New Brunswick advertised a position for President and Vice Chancellor on April 22, 2026, through University Affairs. The job posting described the university's commitment to employment equity and diversity, stating the institution welcomes applications from all qualified individuals including women, visible minorities, aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression. The posting concluded with a preference statement: "Preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada." However, the equity statement's framing suggested institutional evaluation of candidates through demographic categories. The posting explicitly listed demographic groups by immutable characteristics such as skin colour (visible minorities), ethnicity (aboriginal persons), disability status, and sexual orientation, indicating these categories would factor into hiring considerations. This represented an institutional approach to recruitment that categorized candidates by demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them solely as individuals based on their qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities to lead the university.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for leadership positions are evaluated and potentially preferred based on demographic categories rather than being assessed solely as individuals based on their character, qualifications, leadership abilities, and demonstrated accomplishments. This practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into groups defined by immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that each person deserves recognition for their unique qualities and individual merit. The approach signals that demographic category membership is a relevant factor in determining who should lead the institution, rather than focusing exclusively on individual capability, experience, and vision. Qualified candidates may be disadvantaged or advantaged based on how institutions categorize them demographically rather than on their individual strengths. The practice reinforces the idea that institutions should track and make decisions based on demographic proxies, moving away from evaluation systems where advancement reflects individual merit and demonstrated qualifications.

quotes.text: The University of New Brunswick is committed to employment equity and fostering diversity within our community and developing an inclusive workplace that reflects the richness of the broader community that we serve. The University welcomes and encourages applications from all qualified individuals who will help us achieve our goals, including women, visible minorities, Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression. Preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly lists demographic categories by which the university welcomes applications, framing the hiring process through the lens of demographic group membership rather than purely individual qualifications. The categorization by skin colour (visible minorities), ethnicity (aboriginal persons), disability, and sexual orientation indicates institutional evaluation through demographic proxies.

incidents.institution_name: Unity Health Toronto - St. Michael's Hospital
incidents.short_description: Unity Health Toronto advertised a senior research position with a strong preference for candidates based on their skin colour and ethnicity, rather than evaluating applicants as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Unity Health Toronto, St. Michael's Hospital posted a job advertisement on April 22, 2026 for a Scientist II/III position holding the Cassaday-Downe Chair in Anti-Racism and Health Equity. The posting explicitly stated that the institution especially welcomes applicants who are indigenous, or based on their skin colour, among other categories. The job description emphasized that candidates should have 'demonstrated knowledge and lived experience of health challenges facing' people categorized by skin colour and ethnicity. The position required experience designing interventions focused on addressing racism through the lens of demographic categories rather than treating discrimination as a violation affecting individuals. The research program was to be guided by partnerships with communities defined by skin colour and ethnicity, and measured by effectiveness in 'improving the health and well-being' of people sorted into these demographic groups. The institution framed health disparities primarily through the lens of demographic categories rather than recognizing that any individual, regardless of background, deserves healthcare evaluated on their unique needs and circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are advantaged or disadvantaged in hiring based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than their individual qualifications, character, abilities, and demonstrated expertise. The institution normalizes sorting people into demographic categories and evaluating them through these proxies rather than as distinct individuals. Applicants learn that institutions value them primarily for immutable characteristics rather than their unique contributions, skills, and experiences. The approach reinforces the practice of institutional categorization by demographic groups, undermining recognition of singular worth where people are evaluated based on their individual merits. The framework entrenches division by emphasizing differences based on skin colour and ethnicity in addressing health challenges, rather than building understanding that mistreatment of any patient based on assumptions about their background violates human dignity. The hiring preference sends a message that people's immutable traits define their capacity to contribute to solving healthcare problems, rather than their intellect, compassion, innovation, and commitment to treating all patients with dignity.

quotes.text: Unity Health encourages applications from qualified candidates and we especially welcome applicants who are Indigenous, Black or Racialized, persons with disabilities, people who identify as 2SLGBTQIA+, and all others who may contribute to the further diversification of ideas.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly indicates institutional preference for candidates based on demographic categories including skin colour and ethnicity, rather than evaluating all applicants equally as individuals based on their qualifications.

quotes.text: Demonstrated knowledge and lived experience of health challenges facing Black, Indigenous, and/or racialized Canadians.
quotes.rationale: This qualification requirement explicitly ties candidate evaluation to demographic categories and lived experience based on skin colour and ethnicity, rather than focusing solely on professional expertise and individual capabilities.

quotes.text: Racism is a key driver of inequitable health outcomes for Canadians. Racialized people access the health system at a lower rate, from primary care to specialized acute care. Racialized people also generally have a worse experience than their white counterparts in healthcare institutions
quotes.rationale: This passage demonstrates the institutional framework of categorizing people by skin colour rather than treating discrimination as something that can affect any individual based on unfair assumptions.

quotes.text: The success of this program will be measured by its effectiveness in contributing meaningfully to improving the health and well-being of Black and racialized groups
quotes.rationale: This establishes that institutional success is measured through outcomes for demographic groups rather than ensuring all individuals receive dignified, effective healthcare regardless of background.

quotes.text: Established collaborative relationships and partnerships with racialized communities and non-profit/ community-based organizations.
quotes.rationale: This qualification emphasizes partnerships with communities defined by skin colour and ethnicity, reinforcing institutional categorization by demographic groups.

quotes.text: Demonstrated experience with the design, implementation and evaluation of interventions to address anti- Black racism/ racism, taking into account the social determinants of health at multiple levels.
quotes.rationale: This requirement frames the work through demographic categories and group-based interventions rather than addressing discrimination as a violation of individual dignity that can affect any person.

incidents.short_description: McMaster University posted a job opening that explicitly states preference for candidates based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: McMaster University's Department of Mathematics & Statistics posted a job advertisement on April 22, 2026, for a two-year Assistant Professor position in Probability and Statistics. The posting explicitly states that the university 'especially welcomes applications from First Nations, Métis and Inuit peoples, members of racialized communities ("visible minorities"), persons with disabilities, women, persons who identify as 2SLGBTQ+.' This language signals institutional preference for candidates based on demographic categories rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications, character, and abilities. The job posting requires applicants to submit 'a statement of experience and plans for advancing equity, diversity and inclusion in post-secondary education, community-based or other professional settings (2-page maximum)' as part of the complete application, making commitment to demographic-based frameworks a condition of candidacy. The university also asks applicants to complete a voluntary Diversity Survey to 'support efforts to broaden the diversity of the applicant pool,' institutionalizing the collection and consideration of demographic information in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated based on their immutable characteristics rather than solely on their individual merit, teaching ability, and research qualifications. The explicit statement of preference signals to applicants that their demographic category will be a factor in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed based on their unique abilities and demonstrated accomplishments. The required statement on advancing demographic-based frameworks makes adherence to a particular ideological approach a condition of employment, compelling candidates to express commitment to categorizing people by immutable traits regardless of their own views on how best to treat individuals with dignity. Candidates who believe in evaluating each person as a distinct individual may feel pressured to misrepresent their views or self-select out of the application process. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic categories and sends a message that an applicant's value to the university depends partly on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, or disability status rather than exclusively on what they can contribute as teachers and scholars.

quotes.text: The University seeks qualified candidates who share our commitment to equity and inclusion, who will contribute to the diversification of ideas and perspectives, and especially welcomes applications from First Nations, Métis and Inuit peoples, members of racialized communities ("visible minorities"), persons with disabilities, women, persons who identify as 2SLGBTQ+.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly signals institutional preference for candidates based on demographic categories including ethnicity, skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status, rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications and merit.

quotes.text: We invite all applicants to complete a brief Diversity Survey, which takes approximately two minutes to complete, through McMaster's application submission portal. All questions are voluntary, with an option to decline to answer. All information collected is confidential and will be used to support efforts to broaden the diversity of the applicant pool and to promote a fair, equitable and inclusive talent acquisition process.
quotes.rationale: The institution collects demographic data explicitly to influence the composition of the applicant pool, institutionalizing consideration of demographic categories in the hiring process rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta's Faculty of Engineering posted a faculty position that explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic characteristics including sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their individual qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta's Faculty of Engineering posted a tenure-track faculty position in Mining Engineering (Geostatistics) on April 21, 2026. The posting describes a cluster hiring initiative and states that "these positions are part of the University of Alberta's priority to build a diverse faculty; thus, candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply." At the close of the posting, the university further encourages applications specifically from women, first nations, métis and inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression. By singling out individuals based on sex, ancestry, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation as preferred groups of applicants, the university frames its faculty hiring around demographic categories rather than the individual character, abilities, and scholarly achievements of each candidate.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed through the lens of demographic membership rather than as individuals with unique qualifications, abilities, and potential contributions. Candidates who do not belong to the named groups receive an implicit signal that their application is less valued by the institution regardless of their individual merit. The practice embeds demographic categorisation into faculty hiring, normalising the treatment of people as representatives of groups rather than as distinct individuals. This undermines the integrity of academic hiring and erodes trust in the university's commitment to evaluating each person on their own qualities and achievements.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta posted a faculty position in Environmental Engineering that explicitly encourages applications from people based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. This approach directs hiring preferences toward group membership rather than evaluating each candidate purely on individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta's Faculty of Engineering posted a tenure-track faculty position in Environmental Engineering on April 21, 2026, as part of a cluster of five coordinated hires. The posting states that these positions are "part of the University of Alberta's priority to build a diverse faculty" and that "candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply." The closing section of the posting lists specific demographic groups — women, first nations, Métis and Inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression — and singles them out as especially encouraged to apply. This framing directs applicants to present and be evaluated partly based on their demographic identity rather than solely on their qualifications, research record, and teaching potential. The posting thereby incorporates demographic group membership as a relevant consideration in the hiring process rather than treating every candidate as an individual to be assessed on their own merits.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are assessed in part based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual abilities, achievements, and potential. This reduces each applicant to a set of group identities rather than recognising their singular worth as an individual. Candidates who do not belong to the listed groups face a disadvantage not related to their qualifications. The practice normalises the institutional habit of categorising people by immutable traits, reinforcing a hiring culture where demographic proxies substitute for genuine individual evaluation. It also signals to faculty and students that group identity is a legitimate lens through which the university evaluates people, undermining the principle that each person deserves recognition based on their character and demonstrated abilities.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta's Faculty of Engineering posted a job advertisement for a cybersecurity faculty position that encouraged applications based on demographic categories rather than solely on individual qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta's Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering advertised a tenure-track Assistant or Associate Professor position in cybersecurity, posted on April 21, 2026. The job posting included standard qualifications related to educational credentials, research experience, teaching ability, and professional licensure. However, the advertisement concluded with explicit encouragement for applications based on demographic characteristics. The posting stated that the university encourages applications from women, First Nations, Métis and Inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression, and all those who may contribute to the further diversification of ideas. This language framed demographic categories as relevant considerations in the hiring process, rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications, research excellence, teaching effectiveness, and professional accomplishments. The institution presented demographic diversity as an institutional goal to be advanced through hiring decisions, signaling that applicants would be evaluated in part through the lens of their membership in particular demographic groups rather than solely as distinct individuals with unique skills, experiences, and potential contributions to the academic field.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to present themselves through demographic categories rather than solely as individuals with unique qualifications, experiences, and achievements. This approach normalizes institutional sorting of candidates by immutable characteristics, suggesting that demographic group membership is a relevant factor in evaluating academic merit. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should assess people based on their individual character, abilities, and demonstrated accomplishments. Candidates who do not fit preferred demographic categories receive a message that their applications are less encouraged, regardless of their individual qualifications or potential contributions to the field. The framing creates pressure for applicants to identify themselves by demographic characteristics and positions these characteristics as relevant to professional evaluation, rather than treating each candidate as a distinct individual whose worth derives from their unique combination of skills, knowledge, research potential, and teaching ability. This approach to hiring fails to build a community united by shared commitment to academic excellence and instead reinforces the practice of categorizing people by immutable traits.

incidents.short_description: The University of Windsor posted a job advertisement for a tenure-track accounting professor that encouraged applications based on demographic categories rather than merit alone, and required candidates to demonstrate commitment to equity frameworks.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Windsor's Odette School of Business advertised a tenure-track Teaching Intensive Assistant Professor position in accounting on April 21, 2026. The job posting stated that in pursuit of the university's commitment to employment equity, members from designated groups including women, indigenous peoples, persons based on skin colour, persons with disabilities, and persons of minority sexual orientation and/or gender identity were encouraged to apply and to self-identify. The posting required applicants to submit a one-page statement of verifiable commitment to Equity, Diversity, Inclusion, Decolonization and Indigenization as part of the application requirements. Candidates were also required to possess a strong and verifiable commitment to advancing these frameworks through meaningful engagement and practice. The advertisement presented the university's approach to hiring as aligned with its strategic plan priorities, including building what it described as a just, equitable, diverse, and inclusive university, and noted the establishment of the Black Scholars Institute supported by a cohort hiring initiative. The position required substantial professional qualifications including a PhD in accounting with five years' senior management experience or a CPA with over 15 years' experience, along with teaching effectiveness, practical accounting expertise, and technical proficiency.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and encouraged to apply based on demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, and professional accomplishments. The requirement to demonstrate commitment to specific institutional frameworks as a condition of employment creates pressure on applicants to align with particular ideological positions rather than being assessed primarily on their teaching ability, professional expertise, and capacity to treat students as distinct individuals. The practice of encouraging self-identification by demographic category signals that the institution views people through the lens of group membership rather than individual merit. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic categories may perceive that their applications receive different consideration, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual ability and demonstrated achievement. The explicit framing of hiring around demographic representation normalizes the practice of sorting people by immutable characteristics in employment decisions, rather than focusing on each candidate's unique qualifications and potential contributions to student learning and the accounting profession.

quotes.text: In pursuit of the University's commitment to employment equity, members from the designated groups including women, Indigenous Peoples (First Nations, Métis, Inuit), racialized persons/visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and persons of a minority sexual orientation and/or gender identity are encouraged to apply and to self-identify.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories and requests self-identification, demonstrating institutional practice of categorizing candidates by immutable characteristics rather than individual qualifications alone.

quotes.text: Driven by the University's commitment to anti-racism, the Black Scholars Institute was established in 2023 supported by a historic cohort hiring initiative that has brought thirteen Black scholars to UWindsor across several of its faculties.
quotes.rationale: This statement reveals the university's practice of cohort hiring based on demographic categories, providing context for the institutional approach to faculty recruitment that emphasizes group identity.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University's School of Business Administration posted a faculty position stating preference for applicants from demographic categories including women, racialized people, indigenous people, disabled people, and people of all sexual orientations and gender identities.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 21, 2026, Laurentian University's School of Business Administration advertised a two-year limited-term assistant or associate professor position in business analytics or artificial intelligence. The job posting was published on the University Affairs job board with an application deadline of May 21, 2026. The position required teaching in English with French ability considered an asset. In the application requirements section, the university stated that it is an inclusive and welcoming community and encourages applications from members of equity-seeking communities, specifically naming women, racialized people, indigenous people, disabled people, and people of all sexual orientations and gender identities and expressions. This statement framed certain demographic categories as preferred for consideration, rather than evaluating all applicants equally based on their individual qualifications, teaching ability, research potential, and character. The institution explicitly signaled that applicants would be assessed in part through the lens of demographic group membership rather than solely on individual merit and demonstrated capability for the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to view themselves and are viewed by the institution through demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. This practice normalizes sorting people into groups based on immutable characteristics, undermining recognition of singular worth. Qualified candidates who do not fit the specified demographic categories may perceive that their applications will receive less favorable consideration, regardless of their individual qualifications, teaching excellence, or research contributions. The institutional messaging signals that demographic group membership is a relevant factor in hiring decisions, rather than focusing exclusively on individual character, abilities, and demonstrated professional achievement. This approach entrenches the practice of evaluating people through demographic proxies rather than assessing each person's unique qualities and contributions. It undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate individuals based on their merit and capabilities, fostering division rather than building a community united by shared professional standards and academic excellence.

quotes.text: L'Université Laurentienne est une communauté englobante et accueillante et encourage les candidatures de membres de communautés qui recherchent l'équité, y compris les femmes et les personnes racialisées, autochtones, handicapées et de toutes orientations sexuelles et identités et expressions sexuelles.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications from specific demographic categories, framing the hiring process around group membership rather than individual evaluation. It demonstrates institutional categorization of applicants by immutable characteristics.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University posted a faculty position explicitly encouraging applications from people based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: Laurentian University's School of Business Administration advertised a two-year limited-term faculty position for Assistant or Associate Professor in Business Analytics or Artificial Intelligence, posted on April 21, 2026, with applications due May 21, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is "committed to employment equity" and explicitly encouraged applications from "members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions." This language signals that the institution evaluates and sorts candidates based on demographic categories rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications, character, and abilities. The posting describes the university as "inclusive and welcoming" while simultaneously categorizing potential applicants by immutable characteristics. The job description includes legitimate academic qualifications such as PhD completion, teaching ability, research potential, and commitment to pedagogical excellence, but frames the hiring process through the lens of demographic group membership rather than purely individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to identify themselves by demographic categories, signaling that institutions evaluate candidates partly based on group membership rather than solely on individual character, abilities, and qualifications. This practice normalizes the sorting of people into demographic categories and suggests that immutable characteristics are relevant factors in employment decisions. Candidates may feel pressured to disclose personal information about their identity that should be irrelevant to their academic qualifications and teaching abilities. The framing undermines the principle that people should be assessed as individuals and advances based on merit, not demographic proxies. It creates an environment where some candidates may question whether they are being evaluated fairly based on their unique qualities and demonstrated abilities, or whether institutional decisions reflect categorical thinking. The practice entrenches the idea that institutions should track and respond to people's demographic characteristics rather than creating truly merit-based systems that evaluate each person's individual circumstances, needs, and contributions.

quotes.text: Laurentian University is an inclusive and welcoming community committed to employment equity. Applications are encouraged from members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, demonstrating institutional sorting of people by group membership rather than individual merit.

incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University posted a job advertisement for a Canada Research Chair position that expressed a preference for hiring candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, gender identity, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: Wilfrid Laurier University advertised a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair position in Cold Regions Ecosystem Science on April 21, 2026, with an application deadline of May 22, 2026. The job posting outlined qualifications and responsibilities for directing the Trail Valley Creek Research Station and conducting Arctic ecosystem research. The advertisement stated that the university is committed to increasing the diversity of all employees and welcomed applications from candidates who identify based on specific demographic categories including indigeneity, skin colour, disability status, and sexual orientation or gender identity. The posting framed these hiring preferences as part of the university's core values and strategic commitments to equity, diversity and inclusion. Applicants were required to complete an equity survey as part of the recruitment process, in accordance with Canada Research Chair program requirements. The advertisement treated demographic characteristics as relevant factors in evaluating candidates for a scientific research position, rather than focusing exclusively on research qualifications, scientific merit, and individual capabilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated by the institution based on immutable characteristics and demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research accomplishments, and scientific merit. This approach signals that demographic identity is a relevant consideration in hiring decisions for academic research positions, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of people by demographic proxies, treating these characteristics as meaningful factors in determining who should hold research positions rather than irrelevant to scientific capability. Qualified candidates who do not fit preferred demographic categories may question whether they are welcome to apply or whether their applications will receive equal consideration. The requirement to complete an equity survey as part of the application process reinforces the message that demographic identity matters to the hiring decision. This approach fails to recognize the singular worth of each candidate as a distinct individual whose value as a researcher stems from their unique contributions to knowledge, not from demographic group membership.

quotes.text: As part of this work, Laurier is committed to increasing the diversity of all employees. We welcome applications from candidates who identify as Indigenous, racialized, having disabilities, and from persons of all minority sexual and/or gender identities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly expresses preference for candidates based on demographic categories including indigeneity, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation or gender identity, treating these immutable characteristics as relevant to hiring decisions.

quotes.text: In keeping with Laurier's best practices in recruitment, and in accordance with the CRC's Requirements for Recruitment Guidelines, applicants are required to complete the applicant equity survey.
quotes.rationale: This requirement demonstrates that demographic categorization is built into the application process, signaling to candidates that their demographic identity is treated as relevant information for the hiring decision.

quotes.text: Core values at Laurier are equity, diversity and creating a culture of inclusion, as is our commitment to moving towards pathways for reconciliation, Indigenization and decolonization.
quotes.rationale: This statement frames demographic-based hiring preferences as institutional core values, indicating that the practice is embedded in the university's institutional culture and strategy rather than being based on individual merit.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's Faculty of Medicine posted a job advertisement that explicitly encouraged applications from candidates based on demographic categories rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications and abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Pediatric Surgery, Division of Pediatric Otolaryngology - Head and Neck Surgery, posted a job advertisement on April 21, 2026, for an Assistant or Associate Professor (Clinical) position. The advertisement was for a full-time, three-year renewable contract position that does not lead to tenure. The posting described clinical, research, and teaching responsibilities in pediatric otolaryngology at the Montreal Children's Hospital. While the position required standard qualifications including an MD, eligibility for registration with the Collège des médecins du Québec, Royal College certification in otolaryngology, and postdoctoral training in pediatric otolaryngology, the advertisement included language that explicitly invited applications based on demographic characteristics. The posting stated that McGill "welcomes applications from racialized persons and visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities." This framing presented demographic categories as factors in the application process, rather than focusing exclusively on the individual qualifications, clinical skills, research potential, and teaching abilities that would enable someone to excel in the position. The advertisement emphasized the university's commitment to equity and diversity principles and encouraged candidates to indicate membership in designated groups as part of their application materials.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated with explicit reference to demographic categories rather than solely on their individual merit, clinical excellence, research vision, and teaching capabilities. This approach signals that institutions consider immutable characteristics as relevant factors in academic hiring decisions, undermining the principle that people should be assessed based on their unique qualifications and demonstrated abilities. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of individuals by demographic proxies, sending a message that a person's skin colour, sex, ancestry, or other immutable traits are appropriate considerations in professional evaluation. Prospective faculty members learn that institutions view them partially through demographic lenses rather than exclusively as individuals with distinct experiences, skills, and potential contributions. This framing entrenches the practice of sorting people into identity categories in professional contexts, rather than building a culture where each candidate is recognized for their singular worth and individual capacity to advance clinical care, research, and education. The approach prevents institutions from fully embracing a merit-based evaluation system where advancement reflects individual character, abilities, and achievements independent of demographic characteristics.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill s'engage fermement à respecter les principes d'équité et de diversité au sein de sa communauté, tout en valorisant la rigueur et l'excellence académiques. Elle accueille favorablement les demandes d'emploi des personnes racialisées et des minorités visibles, des femmes, des personnes autochtones, des personnes en situation de handicap, des minorités ethniques, des personnes de toute orientation sexuelle et identité de genre, ainsi que toute personne possédant les aptitudes et les connaissances nécessaires pour interagir au sein de groupes diversifiés.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly welcomes applications based on demographic categories including race, sex, disability, sexual orientation, and gender identity, demonstrating that the institution frames hiring through the lens of demographic characteristics rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill dispose d'un programme d'équité en matière d'emploi et invite les membres des groupes visés à indiquer leur appartenance à ces derniers dans leur dossier de candidature.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly invites candidates to indicate membership in designated demographic groups as part of their application, institutionalizing the practice of evaluating candidates with reference to immutable characteristics.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Department of Classics job posting included institutional statements encouraging applications based on demographic categories and requiring completion of a diversity survey as part of the application process.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Toronto posted a job opening for an Assistant Professor position in Greek and Roman Literature and Cultural History on April 21, 2026. The posting included a diversity statement declaring that the university 'strongly encourage[s] applications from Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities.' The statement framed this encouragement around the institution's goal of 'building a culture of belonging' and increasing capacity to serve its global community. The posting also informed applicants that they would be required to complete a 'brief Diversity Survey' as part of their application, though participation was described as voluntary. The university stated that information from this survey would be aggregated for institutional planning purposes, with assurances that individual responses would remain confidential from search committees and human resources staff. The job posting presented these demographic categories as relevant considerations in the hiring process, rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications, teaching excellence, and research merit. The posting emphasized that 'diverse perspectives, experiences, and expertise are essential to strengthening our academic mission,' linking the institution's academic goals to demographic characteristics of applicants.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to identify themselves to the institution based on immutable characteristics rather than being evaluated solely as individuals with unique qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated excellence in teaching and research. The practice of soliciting demographic information through a mandatory survey step, even if completion is voluntary, signals to candidates that the institution categorizes people by these characteristics and considers them relevant to hiring decisions. This undermines the principle that individuals should be assessed based on their character, capabilities, and achievements rather than demographic proxies. The institutional framing creates pressure for applicants to define themselves by group membership rather than their individual merits, and may lead qualified candidates to question whether they are being evaluated fairly based on their scholarly work and teaching abilities. The approach normalizes the practice of institutions sorting people into demographic categories and treating these categories as meaningful factors in academic hiring, rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications and fit for the position.

quotes.text: The University of Toronto embraces Diversity and is building a culture of belonging that increases our capacity to effectively address and serve the interests of our global community. We strongly encourage applications from Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including ethnicity, skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications and merit.

quotes.text: As part of your application, you will be asked to complete a brief Diversity Survey. This survey is voluntary. Any information directly related to you is confidential and cannot be accessed by search committees or human resources staff. Results will be aggregated for institutional planning purposes.
quotes.rationale: This requires applicants to encounter and respond to a demographic survey as part of the application process, signaling that the institution collects and uses demographic data in its hiring and planning processes.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Department of Classics posted a job advertisement for an Assistant Professor position that explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour and ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 21, 2026, the University of Toronto's Department of Classics advertised a one-year contractually limited-term appointment for an Assistant Professor in Greek Language and Literature. The job posting included a diversity statement that explicitly encourages applications from people based on demographic categories. The statement declares that the university 'strongly encourage[s] applications from Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities.' The posting frames diversity as essential to academic excellence and states that diverse perspectives and experiences are essential to strengthening the academic mission. The university signals that it evaluates and categorizes applicants based on immutable characteristics such as skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation, rather than focusing solely on individual merit, qualifications, and demonstrated ability to excel in the specific academic role. The diversity statement appears alongside standard qualification requirements including a PhD in Classics, excellence in research and teaching, and ability to teach Greek language and literature courses.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation rather than being assessed solely as individuals based on their unique qualifications, research excellence, and teaching abilities. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic categories, undermining recognition of each person's singular worth and individual merit. Candidates who do not fit the encouraged demographic categories receive a signal that their applications are less welcome or valued, regardless of their individual excellence in the field of Classics. The approach reinforces the practice of institutions making assumptions about candidates based on immutable characteristics rather than evaluating each person's character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. Academic hiring becomes framed around demographic representation rather than the pursuit of excellence through fair evaluation of individual candidates, potentially affecting who feels encouraged to apply and how selection decisions are perceived and made.

quotes.text:
Diversity Statement
The University of Toronto embraces Diversity and is building a culture of belonging that increases our capacity to effectively address and serve the interests of our global community. We strongly encourage applications from Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly categorizes and encourages applications based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation, demonstrating the institution's practice of sorting candidates by immutable traits rather than evaluating them solely as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto advertised a faculty position stating that the institution strongly encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Toronto posted a job advertisement on April 21, 2026, for a tenure-stream Associate Professor position jointly appointed between the Faculty of Information and the Department of Italian, Spanish, Portuguese & Latin American Studies. The posting included a diversity statement declaring that the university strongly encourages applications from specific demographic categories based on immutable characteristics. The statement identified categories including indigenous peoples, people based on their skin colour, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities. The job posting framed this encouragement within the context of building a culture of belonging and strengthening the academic mission through diverse perspectives. The position required candidates to submit a statement of contributions to equity and diversity as part of their application materials. The advertisement also required applicants to complete a voluntary diversity survey, with results to be aggregated for institutional planning purposes. The job posting presented institutional categorization of applicants by demographic characteristics as aligned with the university's values and commitment to excellence.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to identify themselves and be evaluated based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other immutable characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research excellence, and teaching abilities. This practice normalizes the institutional sorting of people into demographic categories, sending a message that certain immutable traits are relevant factors in academic hiring decisions. The requirement for a diversity statement pressures candidates to frame their professional accomplishments through the lens of demographic identity rather than focusing on their individual contributions to scholarship, teaching, and academic leadership. The diversity survey, even if voluntary, reinforces the institutional practice of collecting and categorizing people by immutable characteristics for planning purposes. This approach undermines the principle that individuals should be evaluated based on their unique character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements, and instead establishes demographic categorization as a legitimate institutional practice in faculty recruitment and hiring.

quotes.text: The University of Toronto embraces Diversity and is building a culture of belonging that increases our capacity to effectively address and serve the interests of our global community. We strongly encourage applications from Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly identifies the demographic categories the institution is encouraging to apply, demonstrating institutional categorization of applicants based on immutable characteristics rather than individual merit alone.

quotes.text: As part of your application, you will be asked to complete a brief Diversity Survey. This survey is voluntary. Any information directly related to you is confidential and cannot be accessed by search committees or human resources staff. Results will be aggregated for institutional planning purposes.
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates the institutional practice of collecting demographic data on applicants, reinforcing categorization of individuals by immutable characteristics for planning purposes.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's Department of Pediatric Surgery posted a job opening stating it welcomes applications from people based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating candidates as individuals based on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Pediatric Surgery, Division of Pediatric Otolaryngology – Head & Neck Surgery posted a full-time faculty position for Assistant or Associate Professor (Clinical) on April 21, 2026. The job posting outlined qualifications including an MD degree, Royal College certification in Otolaryngology, fellowship training in Pediatric Otolaryngology, and clinical excellence. The posting stated that McGill University 'welcomes and encourages applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities.' This statement explicitly categorized and invited applicants based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than focusing solely on the professional qualifications, clinical expertise, and individual merit required for the position. The university framed this categorization as part of its commitment to equity and diversity, implementing an employment equity program that encourages members of 'designated equity groups' to self-identify. The approach directed institutional attention toward applicants' immutable characteristics and demographic categories rather than exclusively emphasizing their unique qualifications, scholarly potential, research vision, and clinical abilities as individual candidates.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than being assessed exclusively as distinct individuals with unique qualifications, clinical expertise, and professional accomplishments. This practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups, undermining recognition of each candidate's singular worth and individual merit.
