Applicants learn that the institution views them partly through the lens of immutable characteristics, rather than focusing entirely on their character, abilities, clinical skills, research potential, and demonstrated achievements. The explicit encouragement of applications based on demographic categories sends a message that these characteristics are relevant factors in the hiring process, rather than treating all candidates with equal dignity based solely on their professional qualifications. This approach entrenches the practice of labelling and dividing people by immutable traits in professional settings, normalizing demographic categorization as an acceptable institutional practice. Candidates who do not fit into the named categories may perceive themselves as less welcomed or valued, undermining shared civic belonging within the academic medical community. The focus on demographic proxies rather than individual circumstances prevents the institution from fully honoring the principle that every person deserves evaluation based on their unique qualities, professional excellence, and potential contributions to patient care, research, and education.

quotes.text: McGill University is committed to equity and diversity within its community and values academic rigour and excellence. We welcome and encourage applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities, as well as from all qualified candidates with the skills and knowledge to engage productively with diverse communities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly welcomes and encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications and merit. This is the core evidence of the hiring preference based on immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: McGill implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify.
quotes.rationale: This confirms that McGill has an institutional program that categorizes applicants into demographic groups and encourages self-identification based on these categories, demonstrating that the categorization is part of systematic institutional practice rather than incidental language.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University advertised a tenure-track sociology position stating preference for candidates from demographic categories the institution identifies as seeking equity, including women, people based on skin colour, indigenous people, people with disabilities, and people based on sexual orientation and gender identity.
incidents.description_of_event: Laurentian University's School of Social Sciences posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in sociology with specialization in criminology on April 21, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is an inclusive and welcoming community and encourages applications from members of communities seeking equity, explicitly listing women, people based on skin colour, indigenous people, people with disabilities, and people of all sexual orientations and gender identities and expressions. This represents an institutional practice of evaluating candidates through demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and merit. The university framed this preference as part of being an inclusive community, institutionalizing the categorization of applicants by immutable characteristics. The posting was advertised until May 21, 2026, and distributed through University Affairs, reaching the broader Canadian academic community.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and sorted by institutions based on demographic categories rather than being assessed purely as individuals based on their unique qualifications, research potential, teaching ability, and character. This approach normalizes institutional categorization of people by immutable characteristics, sending a message that demographic group membership is a relevant factor in academic hiring decisions. The practice undermines the principle that individuals should advance based on their demonstrated abilities and merit, instead treating demographic characteristics as qualifying factors. It creates a system where some candidates receive preferential consideration based on traits unrelated to their capacity to perform the job, while others face disadvantage based on those same immutable characteristics. This institutionalizes division among candidates and faculty, emphasizing demographic differences rather than fostering a community united by shared academic values and commitment to excellence. The approach fails to recognize each applicant's singular worth and instead reduces individuals to representatives of demographic categories.

quotes.text: L'Université Laurentienne est une communauté englobante et accueillante et encourage les candidatures de membres de communautés qui recherchent l'équité, y compris les femmes et les personnes racialisées, autochtones, handicapées et de toutes orientations sexuelles et identités et expressions sexuelles.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour/ethnicity, indigenous identity, disability status, and sexual orientation/gender identity, demonstrating institutional preference based on immutable characteristics rather than individual merit alone.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University posted a tenure-track sociology position stating that preference would be given to candidates based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation rather than individual qualifications alone.
incidents.description_of_event: Laurentian University's School of Social Sciences advertised a tenure-track assistant professor position in sociology specializing in equity, diversity, and human rights, posted on April 21, 2026, with applications due May 21, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is an inclusive and welcoming community and encourages applications from members of equity-seeking communities, including women and people who are racialized, indigenous, disabled, and of all sexual orientations and gender identities and expressions. This language indicates that the institution evaluates candidates based on demographic categories rather than solely on individual merit, qualifications, and demonstrated ability to fulfill the position's requirements. The posting establishes that certain applicants receive preference based on immutable characteristics and group membership rather than their unique skills, experience, and potential contributions as individuals. While the position requires a doctorate in sociology or equity, diversity and human rights, teaching experience, and research capacity, the preferential consideration based on demographic categories introduces criteria unrelated to individual qualification or character.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated by the institution based on demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualifications and potential. This practice normalizes sorting candidates into groups based on immutable characteristics, undermining recognition of each person's singular worth. Qualified candidates who do not belong to preferred demographic categories may be disadvantaged regardless of their individual merit, abilities, or demonstrated potential to excel in the role. The approach teaches that institutions should categorize people by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation when making hiring decisions, rather than evaluating each candidate's character, qualifications, and fit for the position. This undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and circumstances. The practice also places candidates within preferred categories in a position where their hiring may be viewed through the lens of demographic characteristics rather than their individual accomplishments, potentially undermining their professional recognition and the respect they deserve as individuals who earned their positions through their own efforts and qualifications.

quotes.text: L'Université Laurentienne est une communauté englobante et accueillante et encourage les candidatures de membres de communautés qui recherchent l'équité, y compris les femmes et les personnes racialisées, autochtones, handicapées et de toutes orientations sexuelles et identités et expressions sexuelles.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly indicates that the university encourages applications from and shows preference to candidates based on demographic categories including sex (women), skin colour and ethnicity (racialized persons, indigenous persons), disability status, and sexual orientation and gender identity, rather than evaluating all candidates equally based on individual qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University posted a tenure-track faculty position that explicitly encourages applications from people categorized into demographic groups labeled as equity-seeking communities, rather than evaluating all candidates as individuals based on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 21, 2026, Laurentian University advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Sociology with a specialization in Criminology through University Affairs. The job posting outlined academic qualifications including a PhD in Sociology, Criminology, or related field, teaching ability, research experience, and commitment to pedagogical excellence. The position required teaching, research, student supervision, and participation in program administration. The posting stated that Laurentian University is committed to employment equity and explicitly encouraged applications from members of what it termed equity-seeking communities, specifically listing women, racialized and indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions. This language signals that the institution organizes and evaluates candidates based on demographic categories rather than treating each applicant as a distinct individual to be assessed on their character, qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated potential. The posting prioritized immutable characteristics as relevant factors in the application process, rather than focusing solely on academic merit, teaching effectiveness, research capability, and individual suitability for the role.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and sorted by institutions based on demographic categories rather than their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. This approach normalizes the practice of organizing people according to immutable characteristics, undermining recognition of each person's singular worth and unique qualities. Applicants learn that institutions view them through the lens of demographic groups rather than as individuals deserving evaluation based on their own merits and achievements. The framing reinforces division among candidates by emphasizing differences based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than fostering an environment where all applicants are assessed on equal terms according to their demonstrated abilities and potential contributions. Qualified candidates who do not fall into the specified categories receive a signal that their applications may be valued differently, not because of their individual capabilities but because of demographic characteristics beyond their control. The practice entrenches institutional habits of categorizing people by immutable traits rather than building systems where advancement reflects merit and individual circumstances. This undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people as individuals and support a society where recognition is based on character and demonstrated ability rather than demographic proxies.

quotes.text: Laurentian University is an inclusive and welcoming community committed to employment equity. Applications are encouraged from members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories rather than individual qualifications, demonstrating institutional sorting of candidates by immutable characteristics. The job posting included a statement that applications are encouraged from members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions. The posting presents the institution as evaluating candidates in part based on demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. The position requires a PhD in Sociology, Equity, Diversity, and Human Rights, or a related field, with teaching and research experience. The job posting emphasizes commitment to pedagogical excellence, promoting a safe and inclusive learning environment, and advancing the university's bilingual and tricultural mandates. The successful candidate will teach undergraduate and graduate courses, participate in program administration, supervise students, and engage in research and governance activities. The position was advertised with a deadline of May 21, 2026.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are signaled that the institution considers demographic categories as factors in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be evaluated based on their character, abilities, and qualifications. The statement encourages candidates to identify themselves by immutable characteristics, normalizing the practice of sorting people into demographic groups rather than treating them as distinct individuals. This approach sends a message to all applicants that the institution values demographic representation alongside or potentially in place of merit-based assessment. The practice establishes a framework where individuals may feel pressure to emphasize demographic identity rather than unique qualifications and contributions. The institutional communication reinforces the idea that people should be categorized and evaluated based on immutable traits, undermining recognition of singular worth and the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated abilities.

quotes.text: Applications are encouraged from members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, demonstrating institutional prioritization of immutable characteristics in the hiring process.

incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa advertised a Canada Research Chair position with a preferential hiring process that prioritizes applications based on candidates' skin colour, sex, ethnicity, and disability status rather than evaluating all applicants equally on merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Ottawa posted a job advertisement on April 20, 2026, for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Applied AI Innovation in Public Health. The posting included a section titled 'Preferential Hiring' that explicitly states the university will prioritize and assess first applications from members of four designated groups: indigenous peoples, individuals categorized by skin colour, persons with disabilities, and women and gender equity-seeking groups. The advertisement explains that 'applications from members of the four designated groups will be prioritized and assessed first for the vacancy' and that 'the purpose of this measure is to increase the representation and contribution of members of the four designated groups in the allocation of Canada Research Chair positions.' The position is a tenure-track faculty appointment with significant responsibilities including teaching, research, and graduate student supervision. All candidates are required to complete a confidential self-identification questionnaire during the application process. The preferential hiring process means that candidates are sorted and evaluated based on their demographic categories rather than having all applications assessed together on the basis of qualifications, research achievements, and potential contributions to the field.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants who do not belong to the designated demographic categories are evaluated differently, with their applications considered only after others have been assessed based on immutable characteristics. This process establishes a two-tier system where individuals are sorted by demographic proxies rather than evaluated equally as distinct persons with unique qualifications and potential. Qualified candidates may be excluded from full consideration based on characteristics unrelated to their scholarly abilities, research achievements, or capacity to advance knowledge in their field. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of individuals by demographic characteristics in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that people should be assessed based on their individual merits, character, and demonstrated abilities. Academic hiring that prioritizes demographic categories over individual achievement signals to all applicants that institutions view them primarily through the lens of group membership rather than as unique scholars with distinct contributions to offer. This approach fails to recognize the singular worth of each candidate and instead reduces individuals to demographic representations, creating a system where professional opportunities depend partly on immutable traits rather than solely on qualifications and potential.

quotes.text: The University of Ottawa is committed to ensuring equity, diversity and inclusion in the scholarly and leadership environments of our students, staff, and faculty. Accordingly, we strongly encourage applications from skilled and experienced members of the four designated groups such as Indigenous Peoples, racialized individuals, persons with disabilities, women and gender equity-seeking groups, as defined in the CRC Program method for establishing equity targets and the Employment Equity Act, as well as from all qualified candidates with the skills and knowledge to productively implement equity measures that ensure and retain diverse teams and promote inclusive practices for research team members. As part of this preferential hiring process, applications from members of the four designated groups will be prioritized and assessed first for the vacancy.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly establishes that the university will prioritize applications based on demographic group membership, creating a hiring process that evaluates candidates differently based on their immutable characteristics rather than treating all applicants equally.

quotes.text: All candidates are asked to complete the confidential self-identification questionnaire during the application process online.
quotes.rationale: This requirement shows that demographic categorization is built into the application process itself, enabling the sorting of candidates by group membership for preferential assessment.

quotes.text: The purpose of this measure is to increase the representation and contribution of members of the four designated groups in the allocation of Canada Research Chair positions.
quotes.rationale: This statement confirms the institutional intent to allocate positions based on demographic representation rather than solely on individual qualifications, demonstrating that group membership is a factor in hiring decisions.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University posted two faculty positions in sports administration with hiring preferences based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating candidates as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Laurentian University's School of Sports Administration in the Faculty of Management posted two faculty positions on April 20, 2026: one tenure-track assistant professor position and one limited-term lecturer position. The job posting stated that the university is an inclusive and welcoming community and encourages applications from members of equity-seeking communities, including women and people who are racialized, indigenous, disabled, and of all sexual orientations and gender identities and expressions. This statement framed certain demographic categories as preferred characteristics in the hiring process. The posting emphasized these demographic categories as relevant factors in application evaluation, rather than focusing solely on candidates' qualifications, teaching ability, research potential, and demonstrated commitment to pedagogical excellence. The positions required expertise in sports administration with one role emphasizing analytics, finance, or innovation and technology. Both positions required commitment to fostering a safe, inclusive learning environment and advancing the university's bilingual and tricultural mandate. The institution organized its hiring process around demographic categorization of applicants rather than treating each candidate as a distinct individual to be evaluated on merit, experience, and fit for the specific academic role.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated by the institution based on demographic categories rather than their individual qualifications, teaching effectiveness, research potential, and unique contributions to the academic program. The hiring preference signals that certain immutable characteristics are considered advantages in the selection process, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed based on their abilities, character, and demonstrated achievements. Candidates who do not fit the preferred demographic categories may perceive that their applications receive less favourable consideration regardless of their merit, discouraging qualified individuals from applying or undermining their confidence in fair evaluation. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups during hiring, reinforcing the idea that immutable traits define individuals rather than their professional accomplishments and potential. Faculty hiring decisions that emphasize demographic categories over individual merit compromise the institution's ability to select the best candidates for educational and research roles, affecting the quality of instruction and scholarship available to students. Students encounter an academic environment where institutional decisions model demographic categorization rather than individual evaluation, learning that organizations view people primarily through group identity rather than as distinct persons with unique capabilities and perspectives.

quotes.text: L'Université Laurentienne est une communauté englobante et accueillante et encourage les candidatures de membres de communautés qui recherchent l'équité, y compris les femmes et les personnes racialisées, autochtones, handicapées et de toutes orientations sexuelles et identités et expressions sexuelles.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications from people categorized by demographic characteristics including sex, skin colour/ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, treating these immutable traits as relevant hiring factors rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals based on their qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Nipissing University posted a job advertisement for Associate Dean that explicitly encouraged applicants to self-identify based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: Nipissing University advertised a position for Associate Dean, School of Business on April 20, 2026, with an application deadline of May 15, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is committed to employment equity and strongly encourages applications from individuals categorized by demographic characteristics. The posting requested that applicants may self-identify in a cover letter based on several immutable traits. The university explicitly listed categories including indigenous peoples, women, persons with disabilities, persons who identify based on sexual orientation, and people categorized by skin colour or ethnicity. This represented an institutional practice of soliciting demographic information from job applicants and signaling that the university evaluates candidates through the lens of these demographic categories rather than solely on individual merit, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities for the specific role.
incidents.expected_harm: Job applicants are prompted to categorize themselves and disclose immutable characteristics that have no bearing on their qualifications, abilities, or suitability for an academic leadership position. This practice normalizes institutional evaluation of candidates based on demographic proxies rather than individual merit and professional accomplishments. Applicants face pressure to self-identify according to categories the institution has prioritized, creating uncertainty about whether their application will be evaluated primarily on their leadership capabilities, scholarly achievements, and administrative experience, or on their membership in demographic groups. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should assess individuals based on their character, demonstrated abilities, and qualifications for the specific role. It signals that the university views candidates through the lens of immutable characteristics rather than recognizing each applicant's unique professional trajectory and individual qualities. This approach reinforces division by emphasizing differences based on demographic categories rather than uniting the academic community around shared standards of excellence and individual evaluation.

quotes.text: Nipissing University is committed to employment equity and strongly encourages applications, which may include self-identification in a cover letter, from Indigenous peoples, women, persons with disabilities, persons who identify as 2SLGBTQI+ and racialized minorities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly requests applicants to self-identify based on demographic categories in their application materials, demonstrating that the institution is soliciting and potentially evaluating candidates based on immutable characteristics rather than solely on individual merit.

incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa implemented a preferential hiring process for a research chair position that prioritizes candidates based on demographic categories rather than evaluating all qualified applicants equally.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Ottawa posted a Canada Research Chair Level 2 position in AI Innovation Applied to Public Health on April 20, 2026. The job posting explicitly states that applications from members of four designated groups (Indigenous peoples, racialized persons, persons with disabilities, women and gender equity-seeking groups) will be prioritized and evaluated first for the position. The posting describes this as a "preferential hiring" process designed to increase representation and contribution of members of these designated groups in Canada Research Chair positions. All applicants are required to complete a confidential self-identification questionnaire when submitting their online application. The university frames this approach as part of its commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion in research and leadership environments. The posting states that while all qualified persons are invited to apply, applications from members of the four designated groups will be given priority in the selection process. The position is tenure-track in the School of Epidemiology and Public Health in the Faculty of Medicine, with scientific appointment at the University of Ottawa Heart Institute.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are evaluated and prioritized for academic positions based on demographic categories rather than their individual qualifications, research excellence, and potential contributions to the field. This institutional practice treats candidates primarily as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals with unique abilities and accomplishments. The preferential hiring process sends a message that the university values and assesses researchers according to immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated abilities. Researchers who do not belong to the designated demographic categories face explicit disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their qualifications or research potential. The requirement for all applicants to complete demographic self-identification questionnaires as part of the application process pressures individuals to categorize themselves by immutable traits in order to be considered for the position. This approach normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups and evaluating them differently based on these categories, rather than fostering an academic environment where each person is recognized for their singular worth and individual achievements.

quotes.text: Embauche préférentielle : L'Université d'Ottawa est engagée à assurer l'équité, la diversité et l'inclusion dans les environnements de recherche et de leadership de nos étudiantes et nos étudiants, de notre personnel et de notre corps professoral. Par conséquent, nous encourageons fortement les candidatures des membres compétents et expérimentés des quatre groupes désignés (tels que les Autochtones, les personnes raciséees, les personnes en situation de handicap, les femmes et les groupes en quête d'équité de genre, telles que définies dans la méthode d'établissement des cibles en matière d'équité et la Loi sur l'équité en matière d'emploi), ainsi que toutes les personnes qualifiées possédant les compétences et les connaissances requises pour mettre en œuvre de façon productive des mesures d'équité qui assurent et maintiennent en poste des équipes diversifiées et favorisent des pratiques inclusives pour les membres des équipes de recherche.
quotes.rationale: This passage establishes that the university has implemented a preferential hiring process that explicitly encourages applications from members of four designated demographic groups based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sex.

quotes.text: Dans le cadre de ce processus d'embauche préférentielle, les candidatures des membres des quatre groupes désignés seront priorisées et évaluées en premier pour le poste à combler. incidents.short_description: McGill University's Department of Surgery posted a job opening that explicitly welcomes applications based on applicants' immutable characteristics including skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates as distinct individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences advertised an Assistant or Associate Professor position in cardiac surgery on April 20, 2026. The job posting for a clinical academic position in the Division of Cardiac Surgery included standard qualifications related to medical credentials, surgical training, research capabilities, and clinical expertise. However, the posting concluded with language that explicitly categorizes and prioritizes candidates based on demographic characteristics. The institution stated it 'welcomes applications' from people it categorizes as racialized persons and visible minorities, women, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of any sexual orientation and gender identity. This approach frames candidates through demographic categories rather than treating each applicant as a distinct individual to be evaluated on their qualifications, character, abilities, and demonstrated clinical and research capabilities. The posting embeds this demographic framing within statements about the institution's commitment to equity and diversity principles, presenting the categorization of applicants by immutable traits as aligned with academic rigour and excellence.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on demographic proxies rather than being assessed solely as individuals with unique qualifications, experiences, and capabilities. This practice normalizes the idea that institutions should sort candidates into groups based on immutable characteristics, undermining recognition of each person's singular worth. The explicit welcoming of applications based on skin colour, sex, and other demographic traits signals that these characteristics factor into institutional evaluation, rather than character, merit, and demonstrated abilities being the sole criteria. Candidates learn that the institution views them partially through the lens of demographic categories, which may influence how they present themselves and how hiring committees assess applications. This approach entrenches the practice of labelling and dividing people based on immutable traits in the academic hiring context, missing an opportunity to unite the academic community around shared principles that every candidate deserves evaluation based on their individual qualities and professional accomplishments. The framing undermines the principle that advancement should reflect merit and individual capability, instead introducing demographic categorization as a relevant factor in professional evaluation.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill s'engage fermement à respecter les principes d'équité et de diversité au sein de sa communauté, tout en valorisant la rigueur et l'excellence académiques. Elle accueille favorablement les demandes d'emploi des personnes racialisées et des minorités visibles, des femmes, des personnes autochtones, des personnes en situation de handicap, des minorités ethniques, des personnes de toute orientation sexuelle et identité de genre, ainsi que toute personne possédant les aptitudes et les connaissances nécessaires pour interagir au sein de groupes diversifiés.

incidents.short_description: Algoma University posted a job opening for an Assistant Professor position that stated preference would be given to candidates from groups categorized by skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: Algoma University advertised a two-year contract Assistant Professor position in Psychology at its Brampton campus, with applications open from April 20, 2026 until May 20, 2026. The job posting included standard qualifications and expectations for the role. The posting also contained institutional statements about diversity, equity, and inclusion that framed hiring through demographic categories. The university stated it invites and encourages applications from candidates categorized as indigenous peoples, persons based on skin colour, women, persons with disabilities, and persons based on sexual orientation. The posting indicated that preference would be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents, which is a standard citizenship requirement not related to demographic characteristics. However, the broader institutional framing presented in the posting emphasized evaluating and encouraging candidates based on demographic group membership rather than solely on individual qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities for the teaching and research position.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are encouraged to understand themselves primarily through demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualifications and experiences. The institutional messaging normalizes the practice of sorting people into groups based on immutable characteristics, suggesting that demographic category membership is relevant to academic merit and teaching ability. Prospective faculty members encounter a framework that emphasizes identity categories over individual character, achievements, and pedagogical skills. This approach undermines recognition of singular worth by foregrounding demographic classifications in the hiring context rather than focusing evaluation purely on teaching philosophy, research capability, and ability to mentor students as individuals. The framing may lead candidates to believe their demographic category is a relevant factor in assessment rather than their unique contributions to student learning and scholarship. The institutional culture signals that people should be viewed through the lens of group membership, potentially affecting how faculty members later evaluate and interact with students and colleagues.

quotes.text: Algoma University is committed to undoing systemic and institutional discrimination and being publicly transparent and accountable. Diversity, equity, and inclusion are fundamental to our Special Mission. In keeping with the Seven Grandfather teachings that are the core values that inform our decisions as an institution, we are committed to creating a welcoming, inclusive, respectful, and safe environment where everyone belongs. These values, commitments, and practices are highlighted in our institutional strategies, including the Strategic Plan; Academic Plan; Research Plan, and EDI Plan. We live these values through the strength and richness that diversity brings to our workforce and welcome contributors from equity-deserving groups including Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, Persons with Disabilities, 2 Spirit, Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Transgender, and Queer persons.
quotes.rationale: This passage establishes the institutional framework that emphasizes demographic categories and explicitly lists groups based on ethnicity, skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation as relevant to hiring decisions. It demonstrates how the institution frames workforce composition through identity categories rather than individual merit.

quotes.text: Algoma University is strongly committed to fostering diversity and inclusivity within our community and is an equal-opportunity employer. The university invites and encourages applications from all qualified individuals who would contribute to the further diversification of our Institution, including equity-deserving groups that are traditionally underrepresented in employment (Indigenous peoples, racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and 2SLGBTQQIPA+ persons).
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly states that the university invites and encourages applications from candidates based on specific demographic categories, directly framing the hiring process around group membership rather than purely individual qualifications. The language of 'further diversification' emphasizes demographic composition as an institutional goal.

incidents.short_description: McGill University posted a job opening for a cardiac surgery professor that encourages applicants to self-identify based on skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation as part of an employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Surgery posted a job opening on April 20, 2026, for an Assistant or Associate Professor position in Cardiac Surgery. The job posting includes standard position requirements related to medical qualifications, surgical training, and clinical expertise. However, the posting also contains language encouraging applicants to self-identify based on demographic categories. The posting states that McGill 'welcomes and encourages applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities.' The posting further notes that 'McGill implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify.' This approach frames the hiring process around demographic categorization, asking candidates to identify themselves based on immutable characteristics rather than evaluating them solely as individual professionals with unique qualifications, skills, and experience. The institution presents this demographic sorting as part of its commitment to equity and diversity.
incidents.expected_harm: Job candidates are encouraged to categorize themselves based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation rather than being evaluated purely as distinct individuals with unique professional qualifications and demonstrated abilities. This practice normalizes institutional assessment of people through demographic proxies, undermining recognition of singular worth where advancement reflects individual merit and character. Candidates learn that institutions view them primarily through the lens of demographic categories, potentially influencing hiring decisions based on immutable characteristics rather than professional competence alone. The self-identification requirement reinforces division among applicants by emphasizing demographic differences rather than shared professional standards and common commitment to clinical excellence. This approach fails to honour each candidate's autonomy and privacy regarding personal characteristics unrelated to their ability to perform the duties of cardiac surgeon and medical educator. The practice entrenches the expectation that institutions will sort and evaluate individuals based on group membership, preventing a hiring process focused on the unique strengths, experience, and potential contributions each candidate brings as an individual professional.

quotes.text: McGill University is committed to equity and diversity within its community and values academic rigour and excellence. We welcome and encourage applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities, as well as from all qualified candidates with the skills and knowledge to engage productively with diverse communities.
quotes.rationale: This quote demonstrates the institution's explicit encouragement of applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, framing hiring through these demographic lenses rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications.

quotes.text: McGill implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify.
quotes.rationale: This quote confirms the institutional practice of encouraging applicants to self-identify based on demographic categories as part of an employment equity program, demonstrating the systematic nature of categorizing candidates by immutable characteristics.

incidents.short_description: Western University posted a faculty position in paediatric neurology that requires candidates who claim membership in certain demographic categories to undergo an institutional verification process before being considered for the role.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University, through its Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry, posted a full-time faculty position for a Paediatric Neurology physician on April 20, 2026. The job posting included an 'Affirmation Policy Statement' requiring candidates who declare indigenous citizenship or membership to undergo a verification process led by the Office of indigenous Initiatives. The policy applies when such declarations are made 'for material advantage at Western, including where required or preferred for the position.' The posting states that candidates invited for interviews or who are short-listed must have their declarations affirmed through a 'relational accountability process' consistent with what the institution describes as indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing. The policy emerged from what Western characterizes as a move 'beyond sole reliance upon indigenous self-identification in its hiring processes' to guard against 'incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information.' This represents an institutional practice of categorizing candidates by demographic characteristics and subjecting those who identify with particular groups to additional scrutiny and verification processes not applied to all applicants.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and processed differently based on their claimed membership in demographic categories rather than being assessed uniformly as individuals based on their qualifications, character, and abilities. The policy creates a system where institutions sort applicants into groups and apply different procedural requirements depending on demographic claims, normalizing the practice of making immutable characteristics central to hiring processes. Candidates who declare membership in certain groups face additional verification burdens and institutional scrutiny of their identity claims, while others do not. This undermines individual dignity by making demographic categorization a formal gate in the hiring process. The approach reinforces the notion that people should be understood primarily through demographic lenses rather than as distinct individuals, and it entrenches institutional practices of dividing candidates into categories based on ancestry and heritage. The policy fails to recognize candidates simply as individuals with unique qualifications and instead treats demographic declarations as matters requiring institutional validation, creating different pathways through the hiring process based on identity claims.

quotes.text: Western, like many postsecondary institutions in Canada, is moving beyond sole reliance upon indigenous self-identification in its hiring processes. This is to safeguard against the use of incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information in circumstances in which a candidate has made a declaration of indigenous citizenship or membership.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the institution has implemented a policy specifically targeting candidates who declare indigenous citizenship or membership, creating differential treatment in hiring processes based on demographic categories.

quotes.text: Candidates who are invited for an interview or who are short-listed, and who have made a declaration of indigenous citizenship or membership for material advantage at Western, including where required or preferred for the position, will be asked to have their declaration of indigenous citizenship or membership affirmed through a relational accountability process, led by the Office of indigenous Initiatives, that is consistent with indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing.
quotes.rationale: Describes the specific verification requirement applied only to candidates who declare membership in particular demographic categories, demonstrating that the hiring process treats candidates differently based on their identity claims.

quotes.text: The University invites applications from all qualified individuals. Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the institution's practice of categorizing and labeling candidates by demographic characteristics as part of its hiring approach, while simultaneously stating it welcomes all qualified individuals.

incidents.short_description: Western University advertised a Physician Team Lead position stating the university welcomes applications based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, sexual orientation, and disability status.

The statement frames institutional commitment through demographic categories rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications, merit, and the unique circumstances of each applicant. The job posting emphasizes that Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace, explicitly naming demographic groups in the context of welcoming applications.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on demographic characteristics rather than being assessed purely as distinct individuals with unique qualifications, experience, and abilities. This approach normalizes institutional sorting of individuals into demographic groups, teaching both applicants and the broader university community that people's worth and consideration are tied to categorical identities rather than individual merit. The practice reinforces division among potential applicants by emphasizing differences based on immutable characteristics, rather than uniting candidates around shared professional standards and the common goal of delivering excellent healthcare services. Well-qualified candidates may question whether their individual accomplishments and capabilities are the primary basis for evaluation, or whether demographic categorization plays a role in institutional decision-making.

quotes.text: The University invites applications from all qualified individuals. Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups/visible minorities, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.

It demonstrates the institution's practice of naming and highlighting demographic categories in the hiring process rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications and merit.

incidents.short_description: Trent University advertised a faculty position stating that preference will be given to candidates from specific demographic groups including women, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities or racialized groups, and LGBTQ2+ people.
incidents.description_of_event: Trent University's Department of Psychology at the Durham GTA campus posted a job advertisement on April 20, 2026, for a one-year limited term Assistant Professor position. The posting stated that while all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, it stated that preference will be given to candidates from underrepresented groups including women, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities or racialized groups, and LGBTQ2+ people. The institution framed this approach as part of its commitment to creating a diverse and inclusive campus community. The position required teaching undergraduate psychology courses in research methods, social and personality, health, and learning and behaviour, with an appointment commencing August 15, 2026.

Applicants are sorted into preferred and non-preferred groups based on immutable characteristics including sex, ancestry, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than being assessed purely as individuals with unique abilities and accomplishments. This approach signals to all applicants that the institution considers demographic characteristics as relevant factors in employment decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be evaluated based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. The preference system creates a hierarchy among candidates that is partially determined by factors beyond their control or effort, rather than by their individual merit and potential contributions to students and the academic community. Qualified candidates who do not fall into preferred demographic categories may be passed over despite superior qualifications, while the institution's stated commitment to diversity and inclusion is pursued through methods that categorize and differentiate people based on immutable traits rather than fostering a community united by shared academic values and individual worth.

quotes.text: Trent University is committed to creating a diverse and inclusive campus community. All qualified candidates are encouraged to apply; however, Canadian citizens and permanent residents will be given top priority. Preference will be given to candidates from underrepresented groups including women, Indigenous People (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities, or racialized groups and LGBTQ2+ people.

incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated by the institution based on demographic categories rather than purely on their individual merit, qualifications, and character.
