The posting also stated that the university is engaged in a process leading toward a fully equitable, diversified and inclusive university. It specifically encouraged women, indigenous persons, members of visible or ethnic minorities, persons with disabilities, and persons from LGBTQ+ communities to submit their applications. The institution framed these hiring preferences as part of its commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion, and indicated it subscribes to an employment equity access program in accordance with the law. The advertisement organized and categorized potential candidates based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them solely on their individual qualifications, research accomplishments, and teaching abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and encouraged to apply based on demographic categories rather than being assessed solely as individuals with unique qualifications and merit. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of people according to sex, ethnicity, sexual orientation, disability status, and indigeneity, rather than focusing exclusively on their scholarly achievements, teaching abilities, and potential contributions to the academic community. Candidates learn that institutions view them through the lens of demographic characteristics, potentially undermining recognition of their individual worth and accomplishments. The approach reinforces the idea that immutable characteristics should factor into hiring decisions, rather than exclusively considering each person's character, abilities, and demonstrated academic excellence. This creates a system where individuals may question whether their selection or rejection was based on their merit or their demographic profile, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual achievement. The practice also categorizes and divides potential applicants into demographic groups, rather than uniting them around shared academic values and common pursuit of knowledge.

quotes.text: L'Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières est engagée dans une démarche menant vers une université pleinement équitable, diversifiée et inclusive (ÉDI). En ce sens, elle porte un regard sur ses pratiques et souhaite être un acteur engagé dans son milieu et au sein de la société. Elle valorise la diversité au sein de son personnel et reconnaît le caractère pluriel des identités. Elle encourage particulièrement les femmes, les personnes autochtones, les membres de minorités visibles ou ethniques, les personnes ayant des limitations ainsi que les personnes issues des communautés LGBTQ+ à soumettre leur candidature.
quotes.rationale: This quote demonstrates the institution explicitly encouraging applications based on demographic categories including sex, ethnicity, indigeneity, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications and merit.

incidents.short_description: Trent University advertised a faculty position stating that preference will be given to candidates from specific demographic groups including women, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities and LGBTQ2+ people.
incidents.description_of_event: Trent University posted a job advertisement on April 10, 2026, for a one-year limited term Assistant Professor position in the Ancient Greek & Roman Studies Program at the Peterborough Campus. The posting stated that while all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, Canadian citizens and permanent residents will be given priority. The advertisement then explicitly stated that preference will be given to candidates from underrepresented groups including women, Indigenous People (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities or racialized groups, and LGBTQ2+ people. The job posting framed this preferential treatment as part of the university's commitment to creating a diverse and inclusive campus community. The position involved teaching ancient languages and literature, with specific course assignments detailed in the posting. Applicants were invited to submit a self-identification form as part of their application package, suggesting that demographic information would be collected and used in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated based on demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, abilities, and merit relevant to teaching ancient languages and literature. The explicit preference system communicates that immutable characteristics will influence hiring decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed as unique persons based on their demonstrated capabilities and achievements. Applicants learn that institutions categorize people into demographic groups and use these categories as factors in determining who receives opportunities, normalizing the practice of sorting individuals by immutable traits. The policy reduces candidates to representatives of demographic categories rather than recognizing each person's distinct qualities, experiences, and potential contributions. Candidates who do not fall within the preferred categories face disadvantage in the hiring process based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to teach, conduct research, or serve the university community. The practice entrenches the institutional habit of viewing people through the lens of demographic proxies rather than as individuals deserving evaluation on their own merits, perpetuating a system where access to academic positions depends partly on characteristics beyond individual control or achievement.

quotes.text: Trent University is committed to creating a diverse and inclusive campus community. All qualified candidates are encouraged to apply; however, Canadian citizens and permanent residents will be given priority. Preference will be given to candidates from underrepresented groups including women, Indigenous People (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities or racialized groups and LGBTQ2+ people.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly states the university's policy of giving preference to candidates based on demographic categories including sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation, which is the core of the incident and demonstrates institutional hiring practices that evaluate individuals based on group membership rather than individual merit.

quotes.text: Applicants may also submit a self-identification form as part of their application package.
quotes.rationale: This statement indicates that the university collects demographic information from applicants, supporting the inference that such information is used in hiring decisions in accordance with the stated preference policy.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's Department of Family Medicine posted a tenure-track position restricted to applicants based on their ethnicity, limiting eligibility to candidates who self-identify as First Nations, Inuit, or Métis.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Family Medicine advertised a full-time tenure-track position at the Associate or Full Professor level in conjunction with a Canada Research Chair Tier 1, posted on April 10, 2026, with applications open until May 25, 2026. The position was explicitly restricted based on ethnicity. The job posting stated this was 'a special priority call for First Nations, Inuit, and Métis scholars' and clarified that 'for the purposes of a nomination for a Canada Research Chair through this Open Call, we will select qualified applicants who self-identify as First Nations, Inuit or Métis.' The posting further specified that shortlisted applicants would be required to validate their self-identification in accordance with McGill's Policy on Indigenous Membership/Citizenship Validation. Required application materials included a 'statement of your ties to Indigenous communities and your Indigenous identity.' The position restricted access to employment opportunity based on demographic categorization rather than evaluating candidates as individuals based on their qualifications, research excellence, teaching ability, and potential contributions to the department's mission. While the posting described desired expertise in areas relevant to Indigenous health and mentioned welcoming applications from scholars with certain methodological approaches and knowledge bases, the eligibility restriction was based on the applicant's ethnicity rather than their demonstrated expertise, research record, or community relationships.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified scholars are excluded from employment consideration based on their ethnicity rather than being evaluated as individuals on their academic merit, research excellence, teaching effectiveness, and potential contributions to the institution. This practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic categories, treating ethnicity as a qualifying credential rather than assessing each candidate's unique qualifications, expertise, and demonstrated commitment to the position's goals. The approach undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. Candidates learn that universities view eligibility for certain positions through the lens of immutable characteristics rather than individual merit and accomplishment. The restriction prevents talented researchers from diverse backgrounds who may have developed relevant expertise, community relationships, and commitment to Indigenous health from being considered, limiting the pool of potential contributors to the research mission. The practice entrenches the idea that certain types of work or research areas belong to people of particular demographic categories, rather than being open to any scholar who has developed the necessary knowledge, relationships, and capabilities through their work and dedication.

quotes.text: In alignment with our commitment to fostering Indigenous leadership in health sciences and academic excellence, this is a special priority call for First Nations, Inuit, and Métis scholars.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the position is restricted to applicants of specific ethnic backgrounds, demonstrating hiring restriction based on demographic category.

quotes.text:
For the purposes of a nomination for a Canada Research Chair through this Open Call, we will select qualified applicants who self-identify as First Nations, Inuit or M
éfis.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly states that selection will be limited to candidates who self-identify with specific ethnic categories, confirming the hiring restriction.

quotes.text: Applicants who are shortlisted will be asked to validate such self-identification in accordance with McGill's Policy on Indigenous Membership/Citizenship Validation.
quotes.rationale: Shows that candidates must prove their ethnic background through institutional validation procedures, reinforcing that eligibility is based on demographic categorization.

quotes.text: Statement of your ties to Indigenous communities and your Indigenous identity (1 page)
quotes.rationale: Required application material focused on ethnic identity rather than solely on research qualifications or expertise, supporting the finding that ethnicity is treated as a credential.

quotes.text: This special call welcomes both external scholars and internal candidates from McGill University who are First Nations, Inuit or Métis.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that both external and internal applications are limited to specific ethnic categories, demonstrating the scope of the restriction.

incidents.short_description: McGill University posted a job opening for a Canada Research Chair position that restricts eligibility based on applicants' ethnicity, prioritizing those who identify as First Nations, Inuit or Métis.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Family Medicine advertised a tenure-track faculty position at the associate or full professor level, coupled with a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair, on April 10, 2026. The job posting explicitly states this is a special call giving priority to scholars who are First Nations, Inuit or Métis. The posting describes this restriction as part of McGill's commitment to advancing excellence and leadership in health sciences and academic spheres. The university requires shortlisted candidates to validate their membership in these groups according to McGill's Policy on Validation of Indigenous Citizenship and Membership. The posting frames the position as seeking scholars with lived experience and expertise in knowledge systems, epistemologies, methodologies, experiences, traditions, languages or legal and governance systems. McGill justifies this approach by referencing the Final Report of the Provost's Task Force on Indigenous Studies and Indigenous Education (2017) and the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's Calls to Action, setting a target of appointing at least 35 professors to tenure or tenure-track positions by 2032. The job description includes standard academic responsibilities such as research, teaching, supervision and service, but explicitly connects the hire to expanding departmental capacity for sustainable community health through perspectives the institution categorizes as essential based on demographic group membership.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated for academic positions based on their ethnicity rather than their individual qualifications, research excellence, teaching ability and demonstrated expertise. This institutional practice normalizes sorting candidates into demographic categories and granting preferential access to employment opportunities based on immutable characteristics. Scholars who do not belong to the specified ethnic groups are explicitly deprioritized or excluded from consideration regardless of their individual merit, research accomplishments, or ability to contribute to family medicine and primary care research. The hiring approach sends a message that institutional advancement depends on group membership rather than individual character, abilities and achievements. It undermines the principle that people should be recognized for their unique qualities and evaluated as distinct individuals. The practice establishes a precedent where academic positions are allocated based on demographic proxies rather than assessment of each candidate's specific contributions, expertise and potential. This approach divides the academic community along ethnic lines rather than fostering shared understanding and common intellectual pursuit across all members of the university community.

quotes.text: Cet appel spécial accordant une priorité aux universitaires des Premières Nations, inuits ou métis s'inscrit dans le cadre de notre engagement à favoriser l'excellence et le leadership autochtones dans les sciences de la santé et la sphère universitaire.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes that the job posting explicitly prioritizes candidates based on their ethnicity (First Nations, Inuit or Métis), which is the core hiring preference that violates the principle of evaluating individuals based on their character and abilities rather than demographic categories.

quotes.text: Aux fins de l'attribution d'une chaire de recherche du Canada dans le cadre de cet appel de candidatures, nous sélectionnerons les personnes candidates qualifiées qui indiquent leur appartenance aux Premières Nations, aux Inuits ou aux Métis. Les personnes candidates présélectionnées devront valider cette appartenance, conformément à la Politique sur la validation de la citoyenneté et de l'appartenance autochtones de l'Université McGill.
quotes.rationale: This quote confirms the hiring restriction mechanism, requiring candidates to indicate and validate their membership in specific ethnic groups, demonstrating how the institution sorts applicants by demographic category rather than individual merit.

incidents.short_description: Saint Paul University advertised a tenure-track professor position stating that applications are invited from all qualified individuals but indicating the announcement is intended primarily for certain demographic categories.
incidents.description_of_event: Saint Paul University posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in the School of Conflict Studies on April 10, 2026, advertised until May 6, 2026. The job posting included standard qualifications such as a doctorate in conflict studies, strong publication record, significant teaching experience, and bilingualism in French and English. The posting concluded with a statement indicating that the announcement is intended primarily, but not exclusively, for Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada, followed by an invitation for applications from all qualified individuals including women, members of visible and ethnic minorities, Indigenous people, and persons with disabilities. This framing suggests that while the position is technically open to all, there is an institutional preference for evaluating candidates through demographic categories rather than solely on individual merit, character, and abilities. The university explicitly names demographic groups in its hiring communications, signaling that applicants' immutable characteristics are relevant considerations in the employment process.
incidents.expected_harm: Prospective candidates are encouraged to view themselves and be viewed by the institution through the lens of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals evaluated solely on their qualifications, experience, and potential contributions. The institutional framing normalizes the practice of sorting applicants by immutable characteristics such as sex, ethnicity, and disability status, undermining recognition of each person's singular worth. Qualified candidates who do not fall within the named demographic categories receive a message that their applications may be given lesser consideration, regardless of their individual merit or fit for the position. The approach reinforces division among applicants by emphasizing demographic differences rather than fostering a shared understanding that hiring decisions should reflect individual character, demonstrated abilities, and scholarly excellence. The practice trains academic communities to accept that institutions categorize people by immutable traits as a standard employment practice, eroding the principle that advancement should reflect merit and individual circumstances rather than demographic proxies.

quotes.text: This announcement is intended primarily, but not exclusively, for Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada. Saint Paul University invites applications from all qualified individuals, including women, members of visible and ethnic minorities, Indigenous people, and persons with disabilities.
quotes.rationale: This statement reveals the institutional approach to hiring by explicitly naming demographic categories and indicating a primary intention toward certain groups, suggesting that immutable characteristics are relevant considerations in the hiring process rather than individual merit alone.

incidents.short_description: Nipissing University advertised a faculty position for Director of Community Planning that explicitly preferred candidates whose work demonstrates a decolonizing focus and integrates indigenous and marginalized perspectives, evaluating applicants based on demographic categories rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Nipissing University posted a job advertisement on April 10, 2026, for an inaugural Director position in the Community Planning Program at the Associate or Full Professor rank. The posting described the position as a tenure-track or tenured appointment with responsibilities including academic leadership, curriculum development, and leading the program toward accreditation. The advertisement explicitly stated that the university was particularly interested in candidates whose work and approach demonstrates a decolonizing focus, challenging dominant Western-centric frameworks and integrating indigenous and marginalized perspectives into planning processes. The posting also indicated that knowledge of indigenous cultures, perspectives and reconciliation was preferred. The job advertisement further stated that Nipissing University is committed to employment equity and strongly encourages applications from indigenous peoples, women, persons with disabilities, persons who identify as 2SLGBTQI+ and people based on skin colour. This approach evaluated candidates based on their alignment with specific ideological frameworks and demographic categories rather than solely on individual qualifications, teaching excellence, research accomplishments, and ability to lead an academic program.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated based on their adherence to specific ideological perspectives and demographic categories rather than being assessed as individuals based on their unique qualifications, teaching abilities, research contributions, and leadership potential. The explicit preference for candidates demonstrating a decolonizing focus creates a framework where academic merit becomes secondary to alignment with particular theoretical perspectives, undermining the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated accomplishments. Candidates who do not subscribe to or center these specific frameworks in their work face disadvantage regardless of their individual expertise in community planning, their record of successful teaching, or their capacity to lead an academic program. The approach normalizes the practice of sorting and preferring candidates based on demographic characteristics and ideological commitments rather than individual merit. This creates divisions within the academic community by establishing hierarchies based on immutable traits and theoretical alignment rather than fostering understanding that excellence in planning education can emerge from diverse perspectives and approaches. The institutional emphasis on demographic categories in hiring undermines shared civic belonging within the university by signaling that certain groups are preferred over others, rather than building a community united by commitment to rigorous scholarship and excellent teaching that serves all students.

quotes.text: As the planning field continues to respond to evolving social, environmental, economic, and regional challenges, we are particularly interested in candidates whose work and approach demonstrates a decolonizing focus, challenging dominant Western-centric frameworks and integrating Indigenous and marginalized perspectives into planning processes.
quotes.rationale: This statement establishes an explicit preference for candidates based on ideological framework rather than individual qualifications and merit, creating a hiring criterion that evaluates applicants based on alignment with specific theoretical perspectives.

quotes.text: Nipissing University is committed to employment equity and strongly encourages applications, which may include self-identification in a cover letter from Indigenous peoples, women, persons with disabilities, persons who identify as 2SLGBTQI+ and racialized minorities.
quotes.rationale: This statement demonstrates institutional practice of categorizing and encouraging applications based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals based on their unique qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University posted a job for an economics professor stating preference for candidates based on skin colour, sex, disability status, and ethnicity rather than merit alone.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University advertised a multi-year limited term Assistant Professor position in Economics on April 10, 2026, with an application deadline of May 31, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is committed to recruiting and retaining a diverse workforce as measured by increasing representation of under-represented groups among applicants, candidates and hires. The posting explicitly stated that candidates are encouraged to self-identify if they are from an under-represented group, and that the university prefers candidates with knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience. The posting further indicated that lived experience or worked experience working with indigenous or racialized communities and members of other equity-deserving groups is a strong asset, and that such lived or worked experience is preferred. This approach evaluates candidates based on demographic categories and lived experiences tied to immutable characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching ability, research potential, and character as economists and educators.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and preferred based on their demographic characteristics rather than being assessed solely as individuals based on their academic qualifications, teaching effectiveness, and research potential. The explicit preference for candidates from certain demographic groups signals that the institution values group membership over individual merit in hiring decisions. Qualified candidates who do not belong to preferred demographic categories face disadvantage in the application process regardless of their individual abilities or contributions to economics education and research. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic categories, undermining recognition of each person's singular worth and unique qualifications. The approach reduces candidates to representatives of demographic groups rather than recognizing them as distinct individuals with their own experiences, perspectives, and scholarly contributions. This framing prevents the institution from building a faculty based on shared commitment to academic excellence and student success, instead dividing the academic community along demographic lines.

quotes.text: Lakehead University has an Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Action Plan 2019-2024 with a goal to recruit and retain a diverse workforce as measured by increasing representation of under-represented groups among applicants, candidates and hires.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the university explicitly measures hiring success by demographic representation rather than individual merit.

quotes.text: We encourage candidates to self-identify, if you are from an under-represented group, and prefer candidates with the knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates explicit preference for candidates based on demographic group membership and lived experience tied to that membership.

quotes.text: Lakehead University is committed to creating a diverse and inclusive environment and welcomes applications from all qualified individuals including women, racialized persons, Indigenous people, persons with disabilities and other equity-seeking groups.
quotes.rationale: Identifies the specific demographic categories the institution uses to evaluate and categorize candidates.

incidents.institution_name: McGill University - Desautels Faculty of Management
incidents.short_description: McGill University's Desautels Faculty of Management advertised a tenure-track marketing professor position expressing particular interest in candidates focusing on equity, diversity and inclusion, while also stating commitment to prioritizing applications from people based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 10, 2026, McGill University's Desautels Faculty of Management advertised a tenure-track Assistant or Advanced Assistant Professor position in marketing with a start date of August 1, 2027. The job posting stated the faculty had particular interest in candidates with research and teaching focus in equity, diversity and inclusion, among other priority areas, though noting all research areas were welcome. The posting also included institutional statements expressing commitment to equity and diversity, stating the university welcomes and encourages applications from people categorized as racialized persons or visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities. The university implemented an employment equity program and encouraged members of designated equity groups to self-identify. This approach evaluated candidates through demographic categories rather than solely on their individual scholarly merit, research quality, and teaching effectiveness. The job advertisement framed institutional priorities around demographic representation and identity-based research areas, signaling that certain immutable characteristics and research focused on demographic categories may be viewed favourably in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated by the institution based on their immutable characteristics and demographic categories rather than exclusively on their individual scholarly merit, research quality, and teaching potential. The explicit encouragement of applications from people based on skin colour, ancestry, sex, disability, and sexual orientation signals that these characteristics factor into hiring considerations, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed on their unique abilities and qualifications. Scholars pursuing research in areas unrelated to demographic categorization receive a signal that their work may be less valued than research focused on equity, diversity and inclusion topics, regardless of the quality or impact of their scholarship. The practice of encouraging self-identification based on demographic categories normalizes institutional sorting of people into groups defined by immutable traits, rather than recognizing each candidate's distinct contributions and potential. Academic hiring processes are shaped by considerations beyond scholarly excellence and fit with institutional needs, introducing factors unrelated to an individual's capacity to advance knowledge, teach effectively, and contribute to the academic community. The framing establishes a precedent where institutions signal preferences based on demographic proxies, potentially influencing which candidates apply and how they present themselves, rather than fostering evaluation based purely on intellectual merit and individual achievement.

quotes.text: McGill University is committed to equity and diversity within its community and values academic rigour and excellence. We welcome and encourage applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities, as well as from all qualified candidates with the skills and knowledge to engage productively with diverse communities.
quotes.rationale: This quote demonstrates the institution explicitly encouraging applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability, ancestry, and sexual orientation, treating these immutable characteristics as relevant factors in the hiring process rather than focusing exclusively on individual scholarly merit.

quotes.text: McGill implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify.
quotes.rationale: This quote confirms the institution operates a formal equity program that encourages candidates to categorize themselves based on demographic characteristics, institutionalizing the practice of evaluating people through group identity rather than individual merit.

incidents.institution_name: McGill University - Desautels Faculty of Management
incidents.short_description: McGill University's Desautels Faculty of Management posted a tenure-track marketing professor position that explicitly welcomes applications based on candidates' skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their academic qualifications and teaching abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 10, 2026, McGill University's Desautels Faculty of Management advertised a tenure-track assistant or advanced assistant professor position in marketing with an anticipated start date of August 1, 2027. The job posting stated that the university strongly commits to principles of equity and diversity within its community while valuing academic rigour and excellence. The posting explicitly welcomed applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, stating that the university 'accueille favorablement les demandes d'emploi des personnes racialisées et des minorités visibles, des femmes, des personnes autochtones, des personnes en situation de handicap, des minorités ethniques, des personnes de toute orientation sexuelle et identité de genre.' The university framed this approach as part of its employment equity program and invited members of designated groups to indicate their membership in these groups in their application files. While the position requires a doctorate and seeks candidates with strong research programs and teaching effectiveness, the institution chose to organize its hiring process around demographic categories rather than focusing exclusively on individual candidates' scholarly merit, research potential, and teaching capabilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Academic job candidates are evaluated and welcomed to apply based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other immutable characteristics rather than being assessed solely as individuals based on their research quality, teaching effectiveness, and scholarly contributions. This approach signals that the institution considers demographic categories as relevant factors in faculty hiring decisions, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect merit and individual qualifications alone. Prospective faculty members learn that institutions may categorize them by demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them exclusively on their unique scholarly achievements and potential. The practice normalizes sorting applicants into demographic groups in academic hiring, suggesting that individuals are defined by immutable traits rather than their character, abilities, and demonstrated accomplishments. By explicitly inviting applications based on demographic categories, the institution creates a hiring environment where candidates may question whether they are valued primarily for their individual scholarly merit or their demographic characteristics. This undermines confidence in the fairness of evaluation processes and fails to advance a system where all candidates are recognized for their singular worth as scholars and teachers.

quotes.text: accueille favorablement les demandes d'emploi des personnes racialisées et des minorités visibles, des femmes, des personnes autochtones, des personnes en situation de handicap, des minorités ethniques, des personnes de toute orientation sexuelle et identité de genre
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly states that McGill welcomes applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, demonstrating the institution's practice of organizing hiring around demographic categories rather than individual merit alone.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill dispose d'un programme d'équité en matière d'emploi et invite les membres des groupes visés à indiquer leur appartenance à ces derniers dans leur dossier de candidature.
quotes.rationale: This confirms that McGill has an employment equity program and explicitly asks candidates to identify their membership in designated demographic groups in their applications, showing how the institution categorizes applicants by demographic characteristics.

incidents.short_description: Western University's faculty job posting requires candidates who declare Indigenous citizenship to undergo an affirmation process through the Office of Indigenous Initiatives, evaluating applicants based on ancestry rather than individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University posted a faculty position in the Division of Urology on April 10, 2026, advertised through University Affairs. The job posting includes an Affirmation Policy Statement that applies to candidates who declare Indigenous citizenship or membership. According to the posting, Western has moved beyond relying solely on Indigenous self-identification in hiring processes. Candidates who declare Indigenous citizenship or membership and are interviewed or short-listed face an additional requirement: they must have their declaration affirmed through a relational accountability process led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives. The policy explicitly states this affirmation process applies when such declarations provide material advantage at Western, including where Indigenous status is required or preferred for the position. The posting directs candidates to contact the Office of Indigenous Initiatives for details on affirmation processes and references Policy 1.58. This represents an institutional practice of subjecting candidates to scrutiny based on their ancestry and ethnic background, requiring them to prove their membership in a particular demographic category through processes administered by university offices, rather than evaluating all candidates uniformly based on their individual qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities for the faculty position.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are sorted and evaluated based on their ancestry and ethnic background rather than being assessed as individuals based on their qualifications, expertise, and demonstrated abilities. The institution creates a separate procedural track for certain applicants, requiring them to undergo additional verification processes tied to their demographic status rather than their professional credentials. This practice normalizes the concept that people's ethnic and ancestral categories are relevant factors in employment decisions, and that institutions should investigate and verify these demographic characteristics as part of hiring. The policy signals that the university views certain positions through the lens of demographic preferences, treating candidates differently based on their background rather than their individual merit. This undermines the principle that all applicants deserve evaluation based on their unique qualifications and character, and it entrenches the practice of categorizing people by immutable traits in academic hiring.

quotes.text: Western, like many postsecondary institutions in Canada, is moving beyond sole reliance upon Indigenous self-identification in its hiring processes. This is to safeguard against the use of incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information in circumstances in which a candidate has made a declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that Western University has implemented a policy requiring verification of Indigenous status beyond self-identification, indicating institutional focus on demographic categorization in hiring.

quotes.text: Candidates who are invited for an interview or who are short-listed, and who have made a declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership for material advantage at Western, including where required or preferred for the position, will be asked to have their declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership affirmed through a relational accountability process, led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives (OII), that is consistent with Indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that candidates claiming Indigenous status must undergo additional verification procedures, creating a separate evaluation track based on demographic characteristics rather than uniform assessment based on qualifications.

incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba advertised a tenure-track Statistics position with a hiring preference for candidates from demographic categories the institution considers underrepresented, rather than evaluating all applicants equally based on merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba Department of Statistics posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position on April 10, 2026, advertised until May 15, 2026. The job posting explicitly stated that to enhance departmental diversity and create role models, the university particularly invited applications from those who can support and enhance diversity, including women, indigenous peoples, other visible minorities, and those committed to an inclusive environment. The posting encouraged candidates to self-identify aspects of their identity that position them to bring currently underrepresented viewpoints, expertise and forms of excellence to the role. The institution framed this hiring approach as addressing underrepresentation and creating role models for a diverse student population. The position required a PhD in Statistics, Biostatistics, Computer Science or related area, excellence in teaching and research, and ability to establish an externally funded research program. The university stated commitment to principles of equity, diversity and inclusion and to promoting opportunities in hiring for systemically marginalized groups including indigenous peoples, women, racialized persons, persons with disabilities and those who identify as 2SLGBTQIA+.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and considered for academic positions based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research capabilities, teaching potential, and professional accomplishments. The institution signals that certain candidates receive preferential consideration based on immutable traits, undermining the principle that all individuals deserve recognition for their unique qualities and demonstrated abilities. This approach reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals with their own character, skills, and scholarly contributions. The practice normalizes institutional decision-making that categorizes and sorts people based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other immutable characteristics, rather than assessing each person's merit and potential contributions to the academic community. Candidates who do not fit preferred demographic categories may perceive that their applications receive different consideration regardless of their qualifications, while candidates from preferred categories may face assumptions about why they were hired. The institutional framing suggests that diversity of demographic characteristics inherently produces diversity of viewpoints and forms of excellence, conflating immutable traits with intellectual perspectives and professional capabilities.

quotes.text: To enhance our department and create role models for a diverse population of students, we particularly invite applications from those who can support and enhance our diversity, including women, Indigenous peoples, other visible minorities, and those committed to an inclusive environment.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly indicates that the institution is giving particular invitation to applicants based on demographic categories rather than evaluating all candidates equally based on their individual qualifications and merit.

quotes.text: We encourage candidates to self-identity aspects of their identity that position them to bring currently under-represented viewpoints, expertise and forms of excellence to this role.
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates the institution conflating demographic identity with viewpoints and forms of excellence, and requesting candidates identify themselves by demographic categories as part of the hiring process.

quotes.text: The University of Manitoba is committed to the principles of equity, diversity & inclusion and to promoting opportunities in hiring, promotion and tenure (where applicable) for systemically marginalized groups who have been excluded from full participation at the University and the larger community including Indigenous Peoples, women, racialized persons, persons with disabilities and those who identify as 2SLGBTQIA+
quotes.rationale: This reveals the institutional commitment to promoting opportunities in hiring based on demographic categories, indicating that hiring decisions will consider group membership rather than solely individual merit.

incidents.short_description: Université Saint-Paul posted a job advertisement that stated applications were solicited from all qualified persons, but emphasized priority would be given to candidates based on demographic categories including women, visible minorities, ethnic minorities, indigenous nations, and persons with disabilities.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 10, 2026, Université Saint-Paul's Faculty of Human Sciences advertised a tenure-track assistant professor position in Conflict Studies. The job posting was advertised until May 6, 2026, with a start date of July 1, 2026. The advertisement included standard qualifications related to doctoral credentials, publication record, teaching experience, and bilingual capability. The posting stated that applications were primarily, but not exclusively, directed to Canadian citizens and permanent residents. The advertisement then explicitly stated that the university solicited applications from all qualified persons, including women, members of visible and ethnic minorities, indigenous nations, and persons with disabilities. This language framed certain demographic categories as distinct groups deserving special mention in the hiring process, rather than evaluating all applicants equally as individuals based on their qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities for the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are signaled that the institution evaluates candidates through the lens of demographic categories rather than solely on individual merit, qualifications, and fit for the role. The explicit naming of demographic groups in the job posting suggests that membership in these categories is a relevant consideration in hiring decisions, potentially influencing how applications are assessed and undermining the principle that people should be evaluated based on their individual abilities and character. Qualified candidates who do not fall into the named demographic categories may perceive that their applications will be assessed differently, creating doubt about whether the institution truly evaluates all individuals equally. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people by immutable characteristics rather than focusing exclusively on each candidate's unique qualifications, research contributions, teaching effectiveness, and potential to contribute to the academic community. This approach diminishes shared understanding that hiring should be based on individual merit and fosters division by emphasizing demographic differences rather than common professional standards and academic excellence that unite all scholars.

quotes.text: L'Université Saint-Paul sollicite des demandes de toutes les personnes qualifiées, y compris les femmes, les membres de minorités visibles et ethniques, les nations autochtones et les personnes handicapées.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly names demographic categories in the context of soliciting applications, indicating that the institution views candidates through these categorical lenses rather than purely as individuals with unique qualifications.

incidents.short_description: St. Francis Xavier University advertised a psychology faculty position stating that candidates who self-identify based on skin colour or ethnicity will be given preferential consideration in hiring.
incidents.description_of_event: St. Francis Xavier University's Department of Psychology posted a job advertisement on April 9, 2026, for a 9-month limited term faculty appointment beginning August 17, 2026. The posting outlined that the university strongly encourages applications from scholars who self-identify based on their skin colour or ethnicity, specifically listing categories of people. The advertisement stated that qualified candidates will be provided the opportunity to self-identify through a process with People & Culture, and that the successful candidate may be required to provide additional self-identification information prior to hiring. The posting explicitly stated that self-identification will be used to support recruitment practices and that evaluation will be based on both academic excellence and demonstrated contributions to equity and inclusion. The university framed this approach as addressing historic and ongoing underrepresentation, while claiming all qualified candidates will receive full consideration. The position required teaching courses in research methods, cognitive psychology, and sensation and perception, with candidates expected to have or be nearing completion of a PhD in Psychology.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching abilities, and scholarly merit. The hiring process explicitly asks candidates to disclose demographic information about immutable characteristics and uses this information as a factor in recruitment decisions, treating people as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. This practice normalizes institutional evaluation of people based on immutable traits, sending a message that demographic characteristics are relevant to professional competence and hiring decisions. Candidates who do not fall into preferred demographic categories face disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their individual qualifications, experience, or ability to contribute to student learning. The requirement to self-identify and potentially provide additional demographic information before hiring pressures individuals to categorize themselves according to institutional demographic frameworks, reducing them to group memberships rather than recognizing their singular worth. The approach undermines merit-based evaluation and fosters division among candidates by emphasizing demographic differences rather than shared commitment to academic excellence and student success.

quotes.text: In alignment with our institutional commitment to equity, diversity, inclusion, and reconciliation, we strongly encourage applications from scholars who self-identify as Black (especially African Nova Scotians), Indigenous (especially Mi'kmaq), and/or People of Colour.
quotes.rationale: This statement demonstrates the university's explicit preference for candidates based on skin colour and ethnicity, categorizing applicants by demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them solely as individuals with unique qualifications.

quotes.text: Qualified candidates will be provided the opportunity to self-identify through a process with People & Culture. The successful candidate may be required to provide additional self-identification information prior to hiring.
quotes.rationale: This shows the institutional process of collecting demographic information about immutable characteristics and requiring candidates to categorize themselves, treating people as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals.

quotes.text: Self-identification is voluntary and will be used to support recruitment practices aimed at addressing historic and ongoing underrepresentation. All qualified candidates will receive full consideration, and evaluation will be based on both academic excellence and demonstrated contributions to equity and inclusion in teaching, research, mentoring, and/or service.
quotes.rationale: This reveals that demographic information is used in recruitment decisions and that evaluation criteria include factors beyond individual academic merit, while claiming all candidates receive full consideration despite explicitly encouraging applications based on demographic categories.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University restricted a Canada Research Chair competition to current employees who identify as members of specific demographic categories, excluding other qualified candidates based on immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University's Department of Anthropology announced a competition for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Indigenous Knowledge, Youth and Digital Technology on April 9, 2026. The university explicitly restricted eligibility to applicants who are current Memorial University employees and who identify as members of one or more employment equity groups. The job posting stated that equity groups include women, 2SLGBTQIA+ people, indigenous peoples, racialized persons, and persons with disabilities. This restriction was authorized by the Human Rights Commission pursuant to Section 8 of the Human Rights Act. The position offered a five-year appointment worth $100,000 per year with an additional $20,000 annual research stipend, renewable for another five years. The university required applicants to complete an employment equity survey to be considered as members of equity-deserving groups, making demographic categorization a mandatory condition of application consideration. Current Memorial University employees who did not identify as members of these specified demographic categories were ineligible to apply regardless of their qualifications, research excellence, or suitability for the research focus.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are excluded from career advancement opportunities based on their immutable characteristics rather than their individual merit, abilities, and research qualifications. The institution evaluates candidates through demographic categories instead of assessing each person's unique scholarly contributions, research potential, and capacity to advance knowledge in the field. Researchers learn that institutions value them primarily for demographic attributes rather than their individual accomplishments, expertise, and potential to contribute to scholarship and student mentorship. This approach normalizes the practice of sorting academics into groups based on sex, sexual orientation, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability status, undermining recognition of each scholar's distinct worth. The restriction prevents the university from considering all qualified researchers on equal terms and creates a system where institutional opportunities depend on demographic proxies rather than demonstrated research excellence. Colleagues and students observe that access to prestigious research positions is granted based on immutable traits, sending a message that individual achievement and merit are secondary to demographic categorization in academic advancement.

quotes.text: This competition is open only to applicants who: are current employees of Memorial University; and identify as members of one or more employment equity groups. As per approval granted by the Human Rights Commission pursuant to Section 8 of the Human Rights Act, these equity groups include women, 2SLGBTQIA+ people, Indigenous peoples, racialized persons, and persons with disabilities
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the explicit restriction of the competition to individuals who identify with specific demographic categories, demonstrating the institutional policy of evaluating candidates based on immutable characteristics rather than individual merit.

quotes.text: Please note that applicants cannot be considered as a member of an equity-deserving group(s) unless they complete an employment equity survey.
quotes.rationale: This statement confirms that demographic categorization through the survey is mandatory for eligibility, illustrating how the institution requires individuals to classify themselves by demographic groups as a condition of consideration.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University posted a job opening for a librarian position that explicitly encouraged applications from individuals based on immutable characteristics including skin colour and ethnicity, rather than evaluating all candidates equally on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Laurentian University advertised a two-year limited term position for an Access and User Services Librarian, posted on April 9, 2026, with an application deadline of May 1, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is committed to employment equity and explicitly encouraged applications from members of what it termed 'equity-seeking communities,' defined as women, individuals based on skin colour and ethnicity, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities. By actively soliciting applications based on these demographic categories, the institution signaled that it evaluates candidates through the lens of immutable characteristics rather than solely on individual qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities. The posting prioritized group identity categories over individual merit in its recruitment messaging, framing certain demographic characteristics as relevant factors in the application process. While the position required specific qualifications including a Master's degree in Library/Information Science and experience with indigenization principles, the university's explicit call for applications based on demographic categories communicated that membership in certain groups was a consideration alongside professional credentials.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are encouraged to identify themselves by immutable characteristics rather than being evaluated purely as individuals with unique qualifications and experiences. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of people based on demographic proxies, undermining recognition of each person's singular worth and distinct capabilities. Applicants who do not belong to the specified demographic categories receive a message that their applications may be valued differently based on characteristics unrelated to their professional competence or character. The approach entrenches the concept that institutions should sort and evaluate people according to group membership rather than individual merit, abilities, and demonstrated expertise. This undermines a system where advancement reflects merit and individual circumstances, replacing it with one where demographic category membership becomes a relevant factor in employment decisions. The practice also reinforces division among potential applicants by emphasizing demographic differences rather than shared professional standards and common dedication to librarianship and service excellence.

quotes.text: Laurentian University is an inclusive and welcoming community committed to employment equity. Applications are encouraged from members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions.
quotes.rationale: This statement demonstrates the institution's explicit encouragement of applications based on demographic categories rather than individual merit alone. It shows the university actively soliciting candidates based on immutable characteristics including skin colour and ethnicity.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto posted a job opening for an Assistant Professor position that stated the institution "strongly welcome[s] and encourage[s] applicants" from certain demographic groups based on skin colour and ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 9, 2026, the University of Toronto's Department of Statistical Sciences posted a job advertisement for a three-year contractually-limited term appointment at the rank of Assistant Professor, Teaching Stream, with an anticipated start date of August 1, 2026. The advertisement included a statement indicating the search aligned with the university's commitment to promote diversity among community members. The posting explicitly stated: "Recognizing that Black, Indigenous, and other Racialized communities have experienced inequities that have developed historically and are ongoing, we strongly welcome and encourage applicants from those communities to apply." This statement directed preferential encouragement toward applicants based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals based on their qualifications, teaching abilities, and scholarly merit. The job posting also included standard equity and diversity language emphasizing that diverse perspectives and experiences are essential to the academic mission, and encouraged applications from various demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.expected_harm: Prospective applicants are evaluated and encouraged differently by the institution based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than being assessed solely as individuals with unique qualifications and abilities. This approach signals to applicants that the institution categorizes candidates by demographic characteristics and applies different levels of encouragement based on these immutable traits. Candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic groups receive the message that they are less welcomed or encouraged to apply, regardless of their individual merit, teaching excellence, or potential contributions to the academic community. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of individuals into demographic categories and treating people differently based on these classifications rather than recognizing each person's singular worth. This undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications, and instead reinforces a system where demographic proxies influence institutional communications about opportunities. The approach divides the academic community along demographic lines rather than fostering shared civic belonging where all individuals are equally welcomed to contribute based on their individual merits and capabilities.

quotes.text: Recognizing that Black, Indigenous, and other Racialized communities have experienced inequities that have developed historically and are ongoing, we strongly welcome and encourage applicants from those communities to apply.
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement that demonstrates the institution's practice of directing different levels of encouragement to applicants based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than treating all applicants equally as individuals.

quotes.text: This search aligns with the University's commitment to strategically and proactively promote diversity among our community members (Statement on Equity, Diversity & Excellence).
quotes.rationale: This contextualizes the preferential encouragement statement as part of an institutional policy framework, showing this is not an isolated incident but reflects broader institutional practice.

quotes.text: We strongly encourage applications from Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities.
quotes.rationale: This diversity statement reinforces the institution's practice of categorizing and encouraging applicants based on demographic characteristics including skin colour and ethnicity.

incidents.short_description: Mount Royal University restricted a senior leadership position to candidates based on indigenous identity, evaluating applicants by demographic category rather than individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Mount Royal University posted a job advertisement on April 9, 2026, for the position of Associate Vice President, Indigenization and Decolonization. This senior leadership role reports to the Provost and Vice-President, Academic, and is responsible for guiding the university's indigenization and decolonization efforts. The position requires providing transformative vision, strategy, leadership and direction to advance institutional commitments in these areas. The job posting explicitly restricted eligibility to candidates based on indigenous identity, stating that the ideal candidate is an indigenous leader who is First Nations, Métis, or Inuit. This restriction appeared in the qualifications section describing the ideal candidate's attributes. The position carries significant institutional authority, including membership in senior academic leadership, oversight of the Office of Indigenization and Decolonization and its staff, championing curriculum changes, advising on policy and procedures, and collaborating across the university and with external partners. The university evaluated candidates for this leadership role based on their demographic category rather than solely on their character, abilities, leadership experience, subject matter expertise, and capacity to advance the institution's stated goals.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are excluded from consideration for a senior leadership position based on their indigenous identity status rather than being evaluated as distinct individuals based on their leadership abilities, experience, knowledge, and capacity to perform the role's responsibilities. This practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic categories and treats immutable characteristics as job qualifications, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit, demonstrated competence, and ability to contribute. The restriction sends a message that certain leadership roles are reserved for people based on demographic categories rather than being open to all individuals who possess the necessary skills, experience, and vision. It entrenches the practice of institutions evaluating people through the lens of group membership rather than recognizing each person's unique qualities and capabilities. This approach prevents the university community from understanding leadership and expertise as qualities that can be developed and demonstrated by any individual regardless of background, and it forecloses the possibility that the best-qualified person for advancing the university's goals might be someone from any background who has developed deep knowledge, relationships, and commitment to the work through their individual journey and choices.

quotes.text: The ideal candidate is an Indigenous leader (First Nations, Métis, or Inuit) who brings lived experience, cultural knowledge, and strong, respectful relationships with Indigenous communities
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly restricts the candidate pool based on indigenous identity, requiring applicants to belong to specific demographic categories rather than evaluating all individuals based on their qualifications, experience, and abilities.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University posted a job opening for a librarian position that explicitly encourages applications from individuals based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, sexual orientation, and ethnicity rather than evaluating candidates as distinct individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Laurentian University advertised a two-year term position for a librarian for access and user services, posted on April 9, 2026, with an application deadline of May 1, 2026. The job posting explicitly states that the university is an inclusive and welcoming community and encourages applications from members of equity-seeking communities, including women and people who are racialized, indigenous, disabled, and of all sexual orientations and sexual identities and expressions. The posting frames hiring through demographic categories, signaling that the institution evaluates candidates based on immutable characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and demonstrated abilities. The job requirements include a master's degree in library and information science from an ALA-recognized program, at least two years of library experience, proven knowledge of access and user services, experience integrating principles of indigenization into library practice, current knowledge of library and online learning trends and technologies, and strong communication skills in both oral and written form, with mastery of both English and French considered an asset. The position involves coordinating circulation, interlibrary loan, and reference services across Laurentian University's library system.
incidents.expected_harm: Job applicants are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, sexual orientation, and ethnicity rather than being assessed solely on their character, abilities, qualifications, and professional experience. This approach normalizes the practice of sorting candidates into demographic groups, undermining recognition of each person's inherent dignity as a unique individual. Qualified candidates learn that institutions view them primarily through demographic categories rather than their individual merits, professional accomplishments, and potential contributions to the role. The explicit encouragement of applications based on immutable characteristics signals that demographic identity factors into hiring considerations, creating a system where access to employment opportunities is influenced by group membership rather than individual qualifications alone. This framing entrenches division among job seekers by emphasizing differences based on demographic characteristics rather than fostering a shared understanding that all candidates deserve evaluation based on their unique abilities and demonstrated competencies. The practice fails to advance a merit-based system where employment decisions reflect individual character, skills, and professional achievements, missing an opportunity to unite the academic community around shared principles that recognize singular worth and evaluate people as distinct individuals.

quotes.text: L'Université Laurentienne est une communauté englobante et accueillante et encourage les candidatures de membres de communautés qui recherchent l'équité, y compris les femmes et les personnes racialisées, autochtones, handicapées et de toutes orientations sexuelles et identités et expressions sexuelles.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including sex (women), skin colour and ethnicity (racialized persons, indigenous), disability status, and sexual orientation and identity, demonstrating that the institution frames hiring through immutable characteristics rather than individual merit alone.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University restricted eligibility for a Canada Research Chair position to current employees who identify as members of specific demographic groups, excluding other qualified candidates based on immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University's Department of Anthropology posted a Canada Research Chair, Tier 2 position in Indigenous Knowledge, Youth and Digital Technology on April 9, 2026, with a closing date of May 8, 2026. The position was explicitly restricted to applicants who meet two criteria: being current Memorial University employees and identifying as members of one or more designated employment equity groups. The job posting stated that the equity groups include women, 2SLGBTQIA+ people, Indigenous peoples, racialized persons, and persons with disabilities, citing approval from the Human Rights Commission pursuant to Section 8 of the Human Rights Act. This dual restriction meant that qualified researchers who were not current Memorial employees, or current employees who did not identify with the specified demographic categories, were ineligible to apply regardless of their scholarly qualifications, research achievements, or suitability for the research focus. The university required applicants to complete an employment equity survey to be considered as members of equity-deserving groups, making demographic self-identification a gatekeeping mechanism for position eligibility. The posting emphasized Memorial's commitment to employment equity, diversity, inclusion and anti-racism while simultaneously restricting the candidate pool based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating all candidates as individuals based on their merit, research accomplishments, and potential contributions to the field.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are excluded from career opportunities based on whether they identify with specific demographic categories, rather than being evaluated as individuals on their scholarly merit, research achievements, and potential contributions to knowledge. This creates a system where institutions sort people into categories based on immutable characteristics and grant or deny access to positions accordingly, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed based on their unique qualifications, character, and abilities. Researchers learn that their demographic identity, rather than their individual scholarly accomplishments, determines their eligibility for certain positions, normalizing the practice of institutional categorization by demographic proxies. The restriction prevents the university from considering the full pool of qualified candidates and finding the individual best suited to advance the research program, regardless of their background. Current employees who do not identify with the specified groups are told their individual merits are insufficient for consideration, experiencing exclusion based on characteristics unrelated to their research capabilities. The approach entrenches the idea that people should be defined and evaluated primarily through demographic group membership rather than their distinct individual qualities, working against shared understanding and common academic community.

quotes.text: This competition is open only to applicants who: are current employees of Memorial University; and identify as members of one or more employment equity As per approval granted by the Human Rights Commission pursuant to Section 8 of the Human Rights Act, these equity groups include women, 2SLGBTQIA+ people, Indigenous peoples, racialized persons, and persons with disabilities
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the explicit dual restriction on eligibility: candidates must both be current employees AND identify with specific demographic groups, demonstrating institutional gatekeeping based on immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: All applicants will receive the employment equity survey link by email after submitting their application. If you do not see the email in your inbox, please check your junk or spam folder. The survey link will remain active until 11:59 p.m. NST, May 8, 2026. If you do not receive the survey or have any questions about it, please contact Mandy Penney, Employment Equity Officer
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates that demographic self-identification through a survey is a required procedural step, making group membership a gatekeeping mechanism rather than evaluating candidates solely on individual merit.

quotes.text: Please note that applicants cannot be considered as a member of an equity-deserving group(s) unless they complete an employment equity survey.
quotes.rationale: This quote explicitly states that demographic self-identification is mandatory for consideration under the restricted competition, reinforcing that group membership rather than individual qualifications determines eligibility.

quotes.text: Memorial University is committed to employment equity, diversity, inclusion and anti-racism, and encourages applications from all qualified candidates, including: women; people of any sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender expression; Indigenous Peoples; visible minorities, and racialized people; and people with disabilities.
quotes.rationale: This statement appears to encourage broad applications while the actual competition restricts eligibility to specific groups, illustrating the contradiction between stated openness to 'all qualified candidates' and the actual demographic-based restrictions.

incidents.institution_name: University of Manitoba, Max Rady College of Medicine
incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba advertised a department head position stating that preference would be given to applicants based on their skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status rather than solely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba's Max Rady College of Medicine posted a job advertisement on April 9, 2026, for the position of Head, Department of Ophthalmology. The posting stated that the position required substantial achievement in ophthalmology, extensive experience in teaching, research, administration, and leadership ability. Despite these merit-based requirements, the advertisement explicitly stated that preference would be given to applicants based on demographic categories. The posting indicated that pursuant to the Manitoba Human Rights Commission Policy I-7, preference would be given to applicants who self-identify as women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of racialized communities, and those who identify as 2SLBGTQ+. The advertisement described this preference as addressing underrepresentation of members of historically underrepresented groups. The institution framed the hiring preference as part of commitments to equity, diversity, and inclusion, and to social justice principles. Applicants were required to submit a personal statement including their positionality and demonstrated commitment to anti-racism and social justice principles in teaching, research, and service. The position involved leading a department responsible for undergraduate teaching, post-graduate training, and serving as the provincial lead advisor on ophthalmology care across Manitoba.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants will be evaluated based on demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, experience, character, and demonstrated abilities to lead the ophthalmology department and serve patients across the province. The explicit preference system signals that institutions categorize and evaluate individuals through the lens of immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that people deserve recognition based on their unique qualities and merit. Applicants who do not fit the preferred demographic categories face disadvantage regardless of their individual circumstances, achievements, or capability to excel in the role.
