The practice normalizes sorting people into groups based on skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than treating each candidate as a distinct individual. Patients and students will be served by a department head selected partly on demographic considerations rather than purely on who possesses the best qualifications and leadership ability for this consequential medical role. The requirement to submit statements on positionality and commitment to particular ideological frameworks creates pressure on applicants to conform to institutional viewpoints, potentially compromising intellectual honesty and open discourse. The system entrenches the idea that demographic characteristics are central to professional identity and evaluation, moving away from a society where advancement reflects individual merit and institutions respect each person's autonomy.

quotes.text: preference will be given to applicants who self-identify as women, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of racialized communities, as well as other historically underrepresented groups such as 2SLBGTQ+
quotes.rationale: This quote directly establishes the hiring preference based on demographic categories rather than individual merit, which is the core concern of this incident.

quotes.text: a personal statement including your positionality, demonstrated commitment and approach to issues pertaining to anti-racism and social justice (including principles of equity, access, and participation) in the following areas: teaching, research, and service and/or other experiences
quotes.rationale: This requirement shows that applicants must demonstrate commitment to particular ideological frameworks, creating pressure to conform to institutional viewpoints.

quotes.text: The Rady Faculty of Health Sciences is committed to the social justice principles of equity, access & participation and to promoting opportunities in hiring, promotion and tenure (where applicable) for systemically marginalized groups who have been excluded from full participation
quotes.rationale: This contextualizes the institutional framing of the hiring preference as part of social justice commitments rather than individual merit evaluation.

incidents.short_description: Trent University posted a job opening for an Assistant Professor position that explicitly stated preference would be given to candidates based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Trent University advertised a one-year limited term Assistant Professor position in the Chanie Wenjack School for Indigenous Studies, posted on April 9, 2026, with an anticipated start date of August 15, 2026. The position was located at the Peterborough Campus and involved teaching seven half courses within the Indigenous Course Requirement Program. While the posting stated that all qualified candidates were encouraged to apply and that Canadian citizens and permanent residents would be given priority, it explicitly declared that preference would be given to candidates from designated groups. The posting identified these groups as women, indigenous people, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities or racialized groups, and LGBTQ2+ people. This represented an institutional hiring practice that evaluated candidates based on demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, abilities, and demonstrated capacity to perform the role. The university also provided applicants the option to submit a self-identification form as part of their application package, creating a formal mechanism for candidates to declare membership in preferred demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and granted hiring preference based on immutable characteristics rather than being assessed solely as individuals based on their qualifications, experience, teaching ability, and potential contributions to the institution. This practice institutionalizes the categorization of people by demographic proxies, undermining the principle that each person deserves recognition for their unique qualities and individual merit. Qualified candidates who do not belong to preferred demographic categories face disadvantage in the hiring process based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to excel in the role, violating their individual dignity and the principle of fair evaluation. The requirement that applicants may submit self-identification forms creates pressure for candidates to declare and emphasize demographic characteristics rather than focusing hiring decisions on individual capabilities and achievements. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups, teaching that institutions should make decisions about individuals based on assumptions about group membership rather than evaluating each person's distinct circumstances, qualifications, and character. This approach prevents the university from building a community united by shared commitment to excellence and common academic purpose, instead dividing faculty and applicants along demographic lines.

quotes.text: Trent University is committed to creating a diverse and inclusive campus community. All qualified candidates are encouraged to apply; however, Canadian citizens and permanent residents will be given priority. Preference will be given to candidates from underrepresented groups including women, Indigenous People (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities or racialized groups and LGBTQ2+ people.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly states the university's policy of granting hiring preference based on demographic categories rather than individual merit alone, directly demonstrating the discriminatory hiring practice that is the core of this incident.

quotes.text: Applicants may also submit a self-identification form as part of their application package.
quotes.rationale: This statement reveals the institutional mechanism through which candidates are asked to declare membership in demographic categories, supporting the claim that the university has formalized a process for categorizing applicants by immutable characteristics.

incidents.short_description: McGill University posted a job advertisement for Director of Urology that explicitly encouraged applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University advertised a full-time Contract Academic Staff position for Director of the McGill University Division of Urology and Director of the Division of Urology at the McGill University Health Centre on April 8, 2026. The position was advertised at the rank of Associate or Full Professor (Clinical). The job posting stated that McGill is committed to equity and diversity and welcomes and encourages applications from specific demographic groups: persons based on skin colour (described as racialized persons/visible minorities), women, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities. The posting framed these demographic categories as qualification considerations alongside skills and knowledge. McGill further implemented an employment equity program and encouraged members of designated equity groups to self-identify during the application process. This approach evaluated candidates through demographic proxies rather than treating each applicant as a distinct individual assessed on their unique qualifications, experience, clinical expertise, leadership ability, and demonstrated commitment to patient care and academic excellence.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and sorted by institutions based on demographic categories rather than their individual merits, character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. This practice normalizes institutional categorization of people by immutable characteristics, undermining recognition of each person's singular worth. Qualified candidates who do not fit preferred demographic categories receive the message that their individual achievements and capabilities matter less than demographic proxies. The institution reinforces the notion that people should be defined and assessed based on skin colour, sex, disability status, ethnicity, and sexual orientation rather than their unique qualities as individuals. This approach prevents evaluation based on the actual skills, knowledge, clinical expertise, leadership experience, and commitment to excellence that determine whether someone will succeed as Director of Urology. The practice entrenches division among candidates by emphasizing demographic differences rather than shared professional standards and common commitment to patient care and academic mission. Medical students, residents, and faculty learn that institutions prioritize demographic categorization over individual merit, normalizing this approach to professional advancement in healthcare and academia.

quotes.text: McGill University is committed to equity and diversity within its community and values academic rigour and excellence. We welcome and encourage applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities, as well as from all qualified candidates with the skills and knowledge to engage productively with diverse communities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability, ethnicity, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals based on their unique qualifications and abilities.

quotes.text: McGill implements an employment equity program and encourages members of designated equity groups to self-identify.
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates the institutional practice of categorizing applicants by demographic groups and encouraging self-identification based on these categories rather than individual characteristics.

incidents.short_description: McGill University posted a job opening for Director of the Division of Urology that encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation rather than solely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Surgery posted a job advertisement on April 8, 2026, for a full-time Associate or Full Professor position to serve as Director of the McGill University Division of Urology and Director of the Division of Urology at the McGill University Health Centre. The posting includes standard qualifications such as medical degree, licensure, fellowship certification, current associate or full professor rank, clinical expertise, and demonstrated administrative leadership. However, the advertisement explicitly states that the university welcomes applications from people based on demographic categories. The posting encourages applications from individuals based on their skin colour, sex, indigenous status, disability status, ethnic background, sexual orientation, and gender identity. This approach frames hiring preferences around immutable characteristics and demographic group membership rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications, clinical excellence, leadership ability, teaching effectiveness, and research achievements. The institution presents this demographic-based encouragement as part of its commitment to equity and diversity principles, signaling that applicants will be evaluated with consideration of their membership in designated demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to identify themselves by demographic categories, signaling that the institution evaluates candidates partly through the lens of immutable characteristics rather than solely as individuals with unique qualifications and demonstrated abilities. This practice normalizes the categorization of people by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation in professional advancement decisions, undermining recognition of singular worth based on character, skills, clinical expertise, and leadership capacity. The explicit invitation for demographic self-identification creates pressure for candidates to present themselves as members of designated groups rather than solely on their professional merits and individual accomplishments. The approach sends a message that institutional decisions about leadership positions will be influenced by demographic proxies rather than exclusively by assessment of each candidate's unique capabilities, vision, clinical excellence, research contributions, teaching effectiveness, and leadership experience. This undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated achievement, potentially leading qualified candidates to question whether they are being evaluated primarily as individuals or as representatives of demographic categories. The practice also creates an environment where colleagues and patients may perceive the selected candidate's appointment as influenced by demographic considerations rather than solely by their individual qualifications and proven leadership ability.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill s'engage fermement à respecter les principes d'équité et de diversité au sein de sa communauté, tout en valorisant la rigueur et l'excellence académiques. Elle accueille favorablement les demandes d'emploi des personnes raciséés et des minorités visibles, des femmes, des personnes autochtones, des personnes en situation de handicap, des minorités ethniques, des personnes de toute orientation sexuelle et identité de genre, ainsi que toute personne possédant les aptitudes et les connaissances nécessaires pour interagir au sein de groupes diversifiés.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly welcomes applications based on demographic categories including racialized persons, visible minorities, women, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities. This demonstrates the institution's practice of encouraging applications based on immutable characteristics rather than solely individual qualifications.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill dispose d'un programme d'équité en matière d'emploi et invite les membres des groupes visés à indiquer leur appartenance à ces derniers dans leur dossier de candidature.
quotes.rationale: This statement invites members of designated groups to indicate their membership in these groups in their application, creating explicit pressure for applicants to self-identify by demographic categories and signaling that such identification may influence evaluation.

incidents.short_description: The University of Prince Edward Island advertised a tenure-track position in Educational Leadership stating it encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates as individuals based on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Prince Edward Island posted a tenure-track faculty position in Educational Leadership on April 8, 2026, with a closing date of May 1, 2026. The position was in the Faculty of Education and required a doctoral degree in education or related field, significant educational leadership experience, and expertise in areas including artificial intelligence in education and educational leadership research. The job posting included a statement that UPEI "encourages applications from underrepresented groups including women, Indigenous peoples, visible minorities, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity." This language explicitly identifies and categorizes potential applicants based on immutable characteristics and demographic categories rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications, abilities, and character. The institution framed its commitment to equity by naming specific demographic groups it wishes to receive applications from, signaling that it evaluates candidates through the lens of demographic categories. While the posting states that Canadian citizens and permanent residents will be given priority and that the university believes in "providing a positive learning and working environment where every person feels empowered to contribute," the specific encouragement directed toward certain demographic groups contradicts the principle of evaluating all candidates as distinct individuals based on their merit and suitability for the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than being assessed purely as individuals with unique qualifications and abilities. The explicit encouragement of applications from certain demographic groups signals that the institution views candidates through the framework of demographic categories, undermining the recognition of each person's singular worth. Qualified candidates who do not fit into the named demographic categories receive the message that their applications are less encouraged or valued, regardless of their individual merit, experience, or potential contributions to educational leadership. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups and reinforces the idea that immutable characteristics should factor into hiring considerations rather than focusing exclusively on character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. This approach fails to build a hiring culture grounded in individual dignity and merit-based evaluation, instead creating a system where demographic proxies influence how candidates are viewed and potentially assessed. The institutional statement fails to unite the academic community around shared principles of evaluating each person based on their unique qualities and contributions.

quotes.text: UPEI is committed to equity, diversity, inclusion, and reconciliation and believes in providing a positive learning and working environment where every person feels empowered to contribute. UPEI is committed to the principle of equity in employment and encourages applications from underrepresented groups including women, Indigenous peoples, visible minorities, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity, and others with the skills and knowledge to productively engage with diverse communities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly names demographic categories and encourages applications based on these immutable characteristics, demonstrating the institution's practice of evaluating and categorizing candidates by demographic groups rather than purely as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The University of Windsor posted a job opening for Head Coach of Women's Hockey requiring applicants to submit a statement of commitment to equity, diversity, inclusion, decolonization and Indigenization, and encouraged applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Windsor advertised a position for Head Coach of Women's Hockey on April 8, 2026, with applications open until May 8, 2026. The job posting required all applicants to submit a one-page statement of demonstrated commitment to equity, diversity, inclusion, decolonization and Indigenization as part of their application materials. The posting also stated that in pursuit of the university's commitment to employment equity, members from designated groups including women, indigenous peoples, people categorized by skin colour or ethnicity, persons with disabilities, and persons of a minority sexual orientation and/or gender identity were encouraged to apply and to self-identify. The position requirements focused on coaching qualifications, including an undergraduate degree, NCCP certification, strong hockey coaching background, and communication skills. The university framed this approach within its strategic plan emphasizing anti-racism, a Black Scholars Initiative, and building a just, equitable, diverse and inclusive university. The job posting made the statement on equity, diversity, inclusion, decolonization and Indigenization a mandatory application requirement alongside traditional qualifications like curriculum vitae, cover letter, and proof of coaching certifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are required to demonstrate commitment to institutional ideological frameworks as a condition of employment consideration, regardless of their coaching abilities or qualifications. This requirement evaluates candidates based on their adherence to specific institutional perspectives rather than solely on their merit, character, and demonstrated coaching competence. The mandatory statement compels applicants to affirm particular viewpoints about social categories and institutional approaches, potentially excluding qualified coaches who may have different perspectives on how to treat individuals with dignity and fairness. The encouragement for applicants to self-identify based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation and disability status signals that the institution will evaluate candidates through these demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. This creates a hiring environment where people are sorted and assessed based on immutable characteristics and group membership rather than their unique qualifications, experience, and individual merit. Qualified coaches may be disadvantaged or advantaged in the hiring process based on their demographic categorization rather than their ability to lead, develop athletes, and build a successful hockey program. The practice normalizes institutional evaluation of individuals through demographic proxies, undermining the principle that people should be assessed based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements.

quotes.text: In pursuit of the University's commitment to employment equity, members from the designated groups including women, Indigenous Peoples (First Nations, Métis, Inuit), racialized persons/visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and persons of a minority sexual orientation and/or gender identity are encouraged to apply and to self-identify.
quotes.rationale: This quote demonstrates that the university explicitly encourages applicants to identify themselves based on demographic categories including sex, ethnicity, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation, signaling that these characteristics will be considered in the hiring process rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals.

quotes.text: Driven by the University's commitment to anti-racism, the Black Scholars Institute was established in 2023 supported by a historic cohort hiring initiative that has brought thirteen Black scholars to UWindsor across several of our faculties.
quotes.rationale: This quote reveals the university's broader pattern of organizing hiring and institutional initiatives around demographic categories, providing additional context for the approach taken in this job posting.

incidents.short_description: The University of Windsor posted a job opening for Head Coach of Women's Volleyball that encouraged applicants to self-identify based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 8, 2026, the University of Windsor's Department of Athletics and Recreation advertised a three-year limited-term position for Head Coach of the Lancers Women's Volleyball team, commencing July 1, 2026. The job posting explicitly encouraged applications from individuals based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, and disability status. The posting stated that the university was pursuing employment equity and that members from designated groups including women, indigenous peoples, people based on their skin colour, persons with disabilities, and persons of a minority sexual orientation and gender identity were encouraged to apply and to self-identify. This directive required applicants to categorize themselves by immutable characteristics as part of the application process. The job posting also required a one-page statement of demonstrated commitment to equity, diversity, inclusion, decolonization and indigenization as part of the application requirements. The position involved coaching responsibilities for the women's varsity volleyball program, including organization, administration, fundraising, coaching and recruitment duties.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and sorted by the institution based on demographic categories rather than their individual coaching qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities. The requirement to self-identify based on immutable characteristics pressures candidates to reduce themselves to demographic labels in order to be considered for the position, undermining their recognition as distinct individuals with unique skills and experiences. Qualified coaches are sent the message that their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, or disability status matters more than their coaching record, leadership abilities, and capacity to develop student athletes. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of people by demographic proxies, teaching that these immutable traits define individuals rather than their professional accomplishments and personal qualities. Coaches who do not fit the preferred demographic categories understand they are disadvantaged in the hiring process regardless of their merit, creating a system where advancement reflects demographic status rather than individual capability. The institution fails to evaluate candidates based on their character, coaching philosophy, strategic thinking, communication skills, and proven results, instead prioritizing demographic classification in the selection process.

quotes.text: In pursuit of the University's commitment to employment equity, members from the designated groups including women, Indigenous Peoples (First Nations, Métis, Inuit), racialized persons/visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and persons of a minority sexual orientation and/or gender identity are encouraged to apply and to self-identify.
quotes.rationale: This passage demonstrates the institution's explicit encouragement of applicants to categorize and identify themselves based on immutable demographic characteristics rather than individual qualifications, directly supporting the incident classification as hiring_preference and the targeting_basis categories.

quotes.text: Driven by the University's commitment to anti-racism, the Black Scholars Institute was established in 2023 supported by a historic cohort hiring initiative that has brought thirteen Black scholars to UWindsor across several of our faculties.
quotes.rationale: Provides context for the university's broader institutional approach of categorizing and hiring individuals based on demographic characteristics, showing this is part of a systematic institutional practice rather than an isolated incident.

incidents.short_description: The University of Lethbridge advertised a term instructor position stating that candidates who identify based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sex are particularly encouraged to apply.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Lethbridge, Faculty of Fine Arts, Department of New Media posted a job advertisement on April 8, 2026, for a limited-term Instructor position in Critical Media Studies. The position is a 9-month appointment from August 1, 2026 to April 30, 2027. The job posting explicitly states that candidates who identify as members of groups historically underrepresented in post-secondary education, including indigenous peoples, people categorized by skin colour, persons with disabilities, and women and gender minorities are particularly encouraged to apply for this position. The department describes itself as committed to promoting indigenous resurgence, decolonization, and anti-racism, and states that successful candidates must demonstrate they share these commitments. The posting also indicates that the committee recognizes certain demographic groups have been historically underrepresented in graduate education and is open to various forms of evidence demonstrating qualifications. The university's broader diversity statement emphasizes recruitment and support of individuals who enhance diversity, framing diversity as fundamental to excellence in teaching, learning, and research.
incidents.expected_harm: The job posting signals that the institution evaluates candidates in part based on their membership in demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. Applicants learn that their immutable characteristics are considered relevant factors in the hiring process, undermining the principle that institutions should assess people as distinct individuals. The explicit encouragement of applications from people based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sex creates a hiring environment where demographic categories are foregrounded rather than individual merit and demonstrated teaching effectiveness. This approach normalizes institutional categorization of people by demographic proxies, sending a message that individuals are valued in part for their demographic characteristics rather than their unique qualities and contributions. The practice divides potential applicants into preferred and non-preferred groups based on immutable traits, rather than fostering a hiring process focused on finding the candidate best able to serve students through excellent teaching and scholarship. Candidates who do not belong to the encouraged demographic categories may reasonably conclude they face disadvantage in the hiring process based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to perform the job duties.

quotes.text: Candidates who identify as members of groups historically underrepresented in post-secondary education, including Indigenous peoples, racialized individuals, persons with disabilities, and women and gender minorities are particularly encouraged to apply for this position.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sex, demonstrating institutional practice of categorizing and evaluating candidates based on immutable characteristics rather than individual qualifications alone.

quotes.text: The committee recognizes that racialized and Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and women and gender minorities have been historically underrepresented in graduate education and is open to a variety of forms of evidence demonstrating that a candidate meets the below requirements
quotes.rationale: This statement shows the hiring committee frames qualifications assessment through the lens of demographic categories, suggesting different evidentiary standards may apply based on candidates' demographic characteristics.

quotes.text: The Department of New Media strives to promote Indigenous resurgence, decolonization, and anti-racism. It is committed to accessibility, inclusion, and equity. Successful candidates must demonstrate that they share these commitments and that their teaching practice will contribute to their realization.
quotes.rationale: This requirement mandates that candidates demonstrate commitment to specific ideological frameworks, potentially creating pressure for candidates to express particular viewpoints rather than being evaluated on teaching effectiveness and subject matter expertise.

incidents.short_description: The University of Guelph posted a faculty position encouraging applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, evaluating candidates through demographic categories rather than solely on individual qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Guelph's Department of Geography, Environment & Geomatics posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in GeoAI with an advertised date of April 8, 2026, and application deadline of June 6, 2026. The job posting stated that the university 'strongly welcome[s] and encourage[s] applications from members of designated groups in employment, including Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, women, and 2SLGBTQIA+ persons.' This language explicitly encouraged applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation. The posting prioritized demographic group membership in its solicitation of applicants rather than focusing exclusively on the academic qualifications, research capabilities, and teaching excellence required for the position. The university framed this approach as part of its commitment to 'fostering inclusive excellence through promoting Indigenization, equity, diversity, inclusion, and accessibility' and referenced its strategic plan pledging to 'recruit a diverse student, faculty and staff body.' The institution positioned demographic categorization as central to its hiring practices, signaling that applicants' immutable characteristics factored into the university's recruitment priorities alongside their individual scholarly merit and potential contributions to the department's academic mission.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to view themselves and be viewed by the institution primarily through demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique qualifications, research interests, and scholarly potential. This approach normalizes the practice of institutions evaluating people based on immutable characteristics such as skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than focusing exclusively on individual merit, academic excellence, and potential contributions to the field. The messaging signals to all applicants that demographic group membership is a consideration in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be evaluated based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. Candidates are implicitly sorted into demographic categories, with some groups receiving explicit encouragement based on characteristics unrelated to their scholarly qualifications. This creates a system where institutions prioritize demographic proxies over individual assessment, failing to recognize the singular worth of each candidate. The practice entrenches division within the academic community by emphasizing differences based on immutable traits rather than fostering shared civic belonging around common academic values and pursuit of knowledge. It teaches future scholars that institutions view demographic categorization as legitimate in professional advancement, normalizing this framework for the next generation of academics and students.

quotes.text: The University of Guelph invites applications from all qualified individuals; however, Canadians and permanent residents will be given priority. We strongly welcome and encourage applications from members of designated groups in employment, including Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, women, and 2SLGBTQIA+ persons.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, demonstrating the institution's practice of categorizing applicants by immutable characteristics rather than evaluating them solely as individuals.

quotes.text: We invite and encourage applications from all qualified individuals, including from groups that are underrepresented or historically marginalized in higher education.
quotes.rationale: This departmental statement reinforces the practice of categorizing applicants into demographic groups and explicitly encouraging applications based on group membership rather than individual merit alone.

incidents.short_description: The University of Winnipeg advertised a faculty position that encouraged applicants to self-identify based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, evaluating candidates through demographic categories rather than solely on individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Winnipeg's Department of Business & Administration posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Organizational Behaviour and Human Resources Management on April 7, 2026, with a closing date of May 7, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is committed to equity, diversity and inclusion and that a diverse staff and faculty benefits the institution. The posting explicitly encouraged applications from individuals based on demographic categories, stating: 'We welcome applications from all qualified individuals and encourage women, racialized persons, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and 2SLGBTQ+ persons to confidentially self-identify at time of application.' This approach frames candidates through immutable characteristics and demographic labels rather than evaluating them solely as individuals based on their qualifications, research potential, teaching ability, and character. The institution requested that applicants identify themselves by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation as part of the application process, reducing individuals to demographic categories in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on immutable characteristics rather than being assessed purely as individuals with unique qualifications, abilities, and potential contributions. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups, sending a message that these categories are relevant to professional evaluation rather than focusing on character, demonstrated skills, research capabilities, and teaching effectiveness. Candidates learn that institutions view them through the lens of demographic labels, undermining recognition of their singular worth as distinct individuals. The self-identification request creates pressure on applicants to categorize themselves by immutable traits, potentially influencing hiring decisions based on demographic considerations rather than merit alone. This approach reinforces division among candidates by emphasizing demographic differences rather than fostering a hiring process that unites applicants around shared professional standards and individual achievement. The practice fails to advance a system where people are evaluated based on their demonstrated abilities and individual circumstances, instead entrenching institutional reliance on demographic proxies.

quotes.text: The University of Winnipeg is committed to equity, diversity and inclusion and recognizes that a diverse staff and faculty benefits and enriches the work, learning and research environments, and is essential to academic and institutional excellence. We welcome applications from all qualified individuals and encourage women, racialized persons, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and 2SLGBTQ+ persons to confidentially self-identify at time of application.
quotes.rationale: This passage demonstrates the institution's explicit encouragement of applicants to self-identify based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour/ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation during the hiring process, which represents evaluation through demographic lenses rather than purely individual merit.

incidents.short_description: Saint Mary's University posted a job opening for Dean of Arts stating that while all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, preference will be given based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, sex, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: Saint Mary's University advertised a Dean of Arts position on April 7, 2026, with an application deadline of May 7, 2026. The job posting included language stating that the university welcomes applications from specific demographic categories including women, indigenous peoples, racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of minority sexual orientation or gender identity. The posting then explicitly stated that while all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents. The posting framed institutional commitment to equity and diversity as integral to excellence and described the goal of enriching the community through demographic representation. The Dean position is a senior academic leadership role reporting to the Provost, responsible for strategic leadership, faculty development, operational oversight, advocacy, and advancing equity, diversity, inclusion, and accessibility principles. Required qualifications included holding associate or full professor rank, significant academic leadership experience, and demonstrated commitment to EDIA with examples of fostering inclusion. The posting described Saint Mary's as a student-centered institution with over 5,500 students from over 115 countries, emphasizing its commitment to equity, diversity, inclusion, and accessibility.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated and selected for senior academic leadership positions based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, abilities, and demonstrated leadership capacity. The explicit statement of hiring preference based on immutable traits signals that the institution views applicants through the lens of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. This practice normalizes the categorization of people by skin colour, sex, disability status, sexual orientation, and other characteristics in employment decisions, undermining the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on merit and individual qualities. Candidates who do not fall within the preferred demographic categories face institutional disadvantage regardless of their qualifications, experience, or potential contributions to the Faculty of Arts. The approach entrenches the idea that demographic representation is a legitimate criterion for selecting leaders, rather than focusing on each candidate's unique strengths, vision, and ability to advance the academic mission. This creates a system where advancement reflects demographic proxies rather than individual merit, and where applicants learn that their immutable characteristics will factor into institutional evaluation of their worth as candidates.

quotes.text: At Saint Mary's University equity and diversity are integral to excellence and enrich our community. As an institution committed to fostering an environment of inclusion and respect, we welcome applications from women, Indigenous peoples, racialized persons/visible minorities, persons with disabilities, persons of minority sexual orientation or gender identity and others who might contribute to the growth and enrichment of our community. All qualified candidates are encouraged to apply; however, preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents.
quotes.rationale: This passage establishes the institutional framing that categorizes applicants by demographic characteristics and states hiring preferences, though the preference mentioned here is for citizenship status rather than the demographic categories listed.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan advertised a tenure-track librarian position restricted to candidates based on their ethnicity, excluding applicants who do not meet specific ancestry requirements.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Saskatchewan posted a job advertisement on April 7, 2026, for a full-time, tenure-track Indigenous Studies Librarian position. The posting explicitly restricted eligibility to candidates based on their ethnicity and ancestry. The advertisement stated that "only Indigenous candidates will be considered for this position" and required verification of Indigenous membership or citizenship as a condition of employment. The position included liaison services to the Department of Indigenous Studies, outreach programming with Indigenous communities, and coordination of the Indigenous Storyteller in Residence Program. The job posting framed the restriction as part of the university's commitment to employment equity and explained that the verification process would be "led and determined by the deybwewin | taapwaywin | tapwewin: Indigenous Truth policy." The university required successful candidates to maintain verification of their Indigenous membership or citizenship throughout their employment. This represented an institutional decision to evaluate job applicants based on ancestry and ethnicity rather than their individual qualifications, experience, knowledge, and demonstrated ability to perform the role's responsibilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from employment opportunities based on their ancestry and ethnicity rather than being evaluated as individuals based on their skills, knowledge, experience, and ability to contribute to the role. This practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into categories based on immutable characteristics, treating demographic classification as a legitimate basis for determining who deserves access to career opportunities. The restriction sends a message that certain work can only be performed by people of particular ancestries, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed on their character, abilities, and demonstrated competencies. Applicants who possess relevant knowledge of Indigenous worldviews, histories, cultures, and decolonization work, and who have strong relationships with Indigenous communities, are prevented from even being considered if they do not meet ancestry requirements. The policy entrenches the idea that institutions should evaluate people through demographic proxies rather than recognizing each person's unique qualities and contributions, and it diminishes the dignity of both those excluded and those hired under such restrictions by reducing individuals to their ancestry rather than honouring their full humanity and individual merit.

quotes.text: Indigenous candidates are invited to apply for a full-time, tenure track librarian position with the University Library.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly restricts eligibility for the position to candidates based on their ethnicity, establishing the demographic restriction at the outset of the job posting.

quotes.text: Only Indigenous candidates will be considered for this position. Verification of Indigenous Membership/Citizenship at the University of Saskatchewan is led and determined by the deybweyin | taapwaywin | tapwewin: Indigenous Truth policy and Standing Committee in accordance with the processes developed to enact the policy. Verification of Indigenous Membership/Citizenship with documentation is a condition of employment and a requirement to hold this position and the successful candidate must maintain those conditions throughout their employment.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the absolute restriction based on ethnicity and ancestry, establishes verification requirements, and makes clear that demographic classification is a condition of both hiring and continued employment.

quotes.text: The University is committed to employment equity, diversity, and inclusion, and is proud to support career opportunities for Indigenous Peoples to reflect the community we serve. We continue to grow our partnerships with Indigenous communities across the province, nationally, and internationally and value the unique perspective that Indigenous employees provide to strengthen these relationships.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates how the institution frames the hiring restriction as part of equity commitments, illustrating the institutional rationale for categorizing and restricting opportunities based on demographic characteristics.

incidents.short_description: Dalhousie University advertised a faculty position stating that preference will be given to candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating all applicants equally on merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Dalhousie University's School of Occupational Therapy posted a job advertisement for a full-time, probationary tenure-track position at the rank of Assistant or Associate Professor, advertised from April 7, 2026 until June 6, 2026. The position was posted through University Affairs and included standard academic qualifications including a completed Ph.D., research productivity, and teaching experience. The job posting explicitly stated that the university applies a preference system in hiring based on demographic categories. The posting declared that all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, but then specified that in accordance with the university's Employment Equity Policy, preference will be given in hiring processes to candidates who self-identify as members of certain groups defined by immutable characteristics. These groups were listed as Indigenous Peoples, persons based on skin colour or descent, persons with disabilities, women, and persons identifying based on sexual orientation or gender identity. The institution framed this preference system as part of its commitment to equity, diversity, inclusion, and accessibility, and directed candidates to visit a website for more information about definitions of what it termed equity-deserving groups. The posting required applicants to submit an Equity, Diversity, Inclusion, Accessibility and Decolonization Statement describing commitments to anti-oppressive and anti-colonial work as part of the application package.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated by the institution based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than solely on their academic qualifications, research potential, and teaching abilities. This preference system signals that the institution does not evaluate applicants as distinct individuals deserving assessment based on their unique character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Applicants learn that their immutable characteristics will factor into hiring decisions, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit. The requirement to submit a statement on equity, diversity, inclusion, accessibility and decolonization as part of the application creates pressure on candidates to demonstrate alignment with particular ideological frameworks rather than focusing on their scholarly contributions and teaching effectiveness. Candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic categories face explicit disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their qualifications or potential contributions to the academic community. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of individuals by demographic proxies and entrenches the idea that people should be sorted and evaluated based on group membership rather than recognized for their singular worth as individuals.

quotes.text: In accordance with our Employment Equity Policy, preference will be given in hiring processes to candidates who self-identify as members of one or more of the equity-deserving groups listed above.
quotes.rationale: This quote explicitly states that the institution applies a hiring preference based on demographic category membership rather than evaluating all candidates equally on individual merit.

quotes.text: The university encourages applications from Indigenous Peoples of Turtle Island (especially Mi'kmaq), persons of Black/African descent (especially African Nova Scotians), and members of other racialized groups, persons with disabilities, women, persons identifying as members of 2SLGBTQIA+ communities, and all candidates who would contribute to the diversity of our community.
quotes.rationale: This passage identifies the specific demographic categories that will receive preference in hiring, demonstrating that the institution categorizes applicants based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.

incidents.short_description: Mount Saint Vincent University posted a faculty job opening that prioritizes candidates based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all applicants equally on their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Mount Saint Vincent University's Faculty of Education posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Child Clinical Psychology on April 7, 2026, with applications being reviewed starting April 21, 2026. The posting sought up to two faculty members to support the Master of Arts in School Psychology program and a new Doctor of Psychology program launching in fall 2026. While the position outlined specific academic and professional qualifications, the posting stated that the university encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories including women, persons of sexual orientations and gender identities, indigenous persons, individuals characterized by skin colour, persons with disabilities, and other groups. The posting explicitly stated that priority would be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents after noting these demographic preferences. The institution framed this approach as part of its commitment to equity, diversity, inclusion, and accessibility, and encouraged applicants to include information supporting their commitment to these concepts in their application materials. The posting evaluated and invited candidates based on immutable characteristics rather than focusing solely on individual merit, qualifications, clinical skills, research capabilities, and teaching abilities that were also outlined in the position requirements.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated by the institution based on demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualifications, skills, and experiences. This creates a system where demographic characteristics become relevant factors in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that people should be assessed based on their individual character, abilities, and demonstrated professional achievements. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of candidates by immutable traits, sending a message that these characteristics matter in determining who receives opportunities. Qualified candidates who do not fit preferred demographic categories face disadvantage regardless of their individual merit, clinical expertise, research accomplishments, or teaching abilities. The approach fails to recognize the singular worth of each applicant and instead reduces individuals to representatives of demographic groups. Students and colleagues learn that the institution values demographic representation over individual capability, which undermines trust that advancement reflects merit and that each person will be evaluated fairly based on their own qualities. The practice entrenches division by emphasizing demographic differences rather than building understanding around shared professional standards and common commitment to excellence in psychology education and practice.

quotes.text: Mount Saint Vincent University is strongly committed to fostering diversity and inclusion within our community and encourages applications from all qualified candidates including women, persons of any sexual orientations and gender identities and/or expressions, Indigenous persons, African Canadians, other racialized groups, persons with disabilities, and other groups that would contribute to the diversification of our campus.
quotes.rationale: This statement demonstrates the institution's practice of encouraging applications based on demographic categories rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications. It explicitly lists demographic groups and frames diversification of campus as a goal, which prioritizes immutable characteristics in the hiring process.

incidents.short_description: The University of Manitoba required applicants for a Canada Impact+ Research Chair position to submit a personal statement on their approach to anti-racism and social justice, evaluating candidates based on their views on these ideological frameworks rather than solely on their research excellence and ability to advance scientific knowledge.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Manitoba advertised a Canada Impact+ Research Chair position in Space Systems Design, posted on April 7, 2026. The position is one of the most prestigious international research awards, providing up to $8 million over eight years. Among the required application materials, candidates were instructed to submit a personal statement reflecting on their positionality, demonstrated commitment, and approach to issues pertaining to anti-racism and social justice, including principles of equity, access, and participation in teaching, research, and service. This requirement asks applicants to demonstrate adherence to specific ideological frameworks as a condition of consideration for a research position. The job posting also emphasized that equity, diversity and inclusion are embedded as foundational principles in the program's objectives, expected outcomes and reporting requirements, and stated that the selection committee would receive equity training on recognizing and combating various forms of bias. The university committed to promoting opportunities for what it described as systemically marginalized groups including people categorized by immutable characteristics such as skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated not solely on their research excellence, teaching ability, and contributions to knowledge, but on their adherence to specific ideological frameworks regarding anti-racism and social justice. This introduces a criterion unrelated to scientific merit or individual capability, potentially excluding qualified researchers who may approach human dignity and fairness through different philosophical or practical frameworks. The requirement to submit a statement on positionality and commitment to particular social theories pressures applicants to demonstrate conformity with institutional ideology as a condition of employment. Researchers who believe in evaluating individuals based on character and accomplishments rather than demographic categories may find themselves at a disadvantage or forced to misrepresent their views. The emphasis on categorizing people by immutable characteristics in hiring practices normalizes institutional sorting of individuals into demographic groups rather than recognizing each person's unique qualifications and worth. This approach undermines the principle that academic positions should be awarded based on intellectual merit, research potential, and demonstrated ability to advance knowledge and train students, rather than on applicants' expressed views about contested social and political theories.

quotes.text: UM is committed to these principles and to promoting opportunities in hiring, promotion and tenure (where applicable) for systemically marginalized groups who have been excluded from full participation at UM and the larger community including Indigenous Peoples, women, racialized persons, persons with disabilities and those who identify as 2SLGBTQIA+
quotes.rationale: This quote shows the institution's commitment to categorizing and evaluating people based on demographic characteristics, treating individuals as members of groups defined by immutable traits rather than as distinct persons.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University's job posting for a Canada Impact+ Research Chair position requires applicants to identify as members of equity-deserving groups and complete an employment equity survey to be considered for such status, with responses shared with the search committee.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University posted a faculty position for a Canada Impact+ Research Chair in Trustworthy, Quantum-Resilient AI for Cyber-Physical Infrastructure on April 6, 2026. The posting states that the university is committed to employment equity, diversity, inclusion and anti-racism, and encourages applications from qualified candidates identifying as women, people of any sexual orientation or gender identity or expression, Indigenous Peoples, visible minorities and racialized people, and people with disabilities. The posting instructs all applicants to identify themselves as members of equity-deserving groups as appropriate. To be considered an equity-deserving group member, applicants must complete an employment equity survey and provide permission to share their survey responses with the search committee. After submitting applications, candidates receive the survey via email and have seven days to complete it. The institution explicitly sorts and categorizes applicants based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status, making membership in demographic categories a factor evaluated by the search committee rather than focusing solely on candidates' individual qualifications, research excellence, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated based on their demographic categories rather than solely on their individual merit, qualifications, and research accomplishments. The requirement to identify and be categorized by immutable characteristics normalizes institutional sorting of people into groups based on sex, skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and disability status. This undermines the recognition of each candidate's singular worth and unique abilities. Candidates who decline to categorize themselves by these demographic proxies face disadvantage in the hiring process, as the system explicitly distinguishes between those who complete the equity survey and those who do not. The practice sends a message that institutions will evaluate people through the lens of demographic group membership rather than treating them as distinct individuals with their own character, abilities, and achievements. This approach entrenches division among applicants by emphasizing immutable characteristics over shared standards of research excellence and professional capability, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated ability.

quotes.text: Memorial University is committed to employment equity, diversity, inclusion and anti-racism, and encourages applications from all qualified candidates, including: women; people of any sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender expression; Indigenous Peoples; visible minorities, and racialized people; and people with disabilities. All applicants are invited to identify themselves as a member of an equity-deserving group(s) as appropriate.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the institution's explicit categorization of applicants by demographic characteristics including sex, sexual orientation, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability status, with invitation for self-identification into these categories.

quotes.text: To be considered an equity-deserving group member, applicants must complete the employment equity (EE) survey, and provide permission to share their survey responses with the search committee. Once applicants submit their application, they will receive the EE survey via email, and have 7 days to complete it.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the institutional mechanism for categorizing applicants by demographic group membership and sharing this information with decision-makers, making demographic categorization an explicit factor in the hiring process.

quotes.text: All applicants are invited to identify themselves as a member of an equity-deserving group(s) as appropriate.
quotes.rationale: Shows that the institution requests all applicants to categorize themselves by demographic group membership, making this identification part of the application process.

incidents.institution_name: Western University - Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry, London Health Sciences Centre, and St. Joseph's Health Care London
incidents.short_description: Western University, London Health Sciences Centre, and St. Joseph's Health Care London posted a job advertisement for Chair/Chief/Department Head of Surgery that explicitly invites applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, and other immutable characteristics rather than solely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 3, 2026, Western University's Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry, London Health Sciences Centre, and St. Joseph's Health Care London posted a job advertisement for the position of Chair/Chief/Department Head of the Department of Surgery. The advertisement described extensive qualifications and responsibilities for leading a department of over 100 surgeons and scientists with eight divisions and 160 trainees. Near the end of the posting, the institutions included language stating that they 'invite applications from all qualified individuals' followed by a commitment to 'employment equity and diversity in the workplace' and explicit welcoming of 'applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.' This language categorizes potential applicants based on demographic characteristics including sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than focusing exclusively on the professional qualifications, clinical expertise, research accomplishments, and leadership capabilities required for the position. The job posting emphasized demographic categories in its invitation to apply, signaling that the institutions evaluate candidates through the lens of immutable characteristics rather than treating each applicant as a distinct individual to be assessed solely on merit, qualifications, and ability to fulfill the role's substantial academic, clinical, and administrative responsibilities.
incidents.expected_harm: The institutional communication signals to potential applicants that demographic categories factor into how candidates are viewed and evaluated, rather than being assessed purely as individuals based on their qualifications, experience, and demonstrated capabilities. This undermines the principle that people should be evaluated based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. Qualified candidates may question whether they are being considered as individuals or as representatives of demographic groups. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of people by immutable characteristics in professional contexts, teaching that demographic proxies matter in determining who is invited or welcomed to apply for leadership positions. This messaging divides rather than unites the academic and medical community, emphasizing differences based on skin colour, sex, and other immutable traits instead of shared professional standards, common dedication to patient care, teaching excellence, and research advancement that should unite all qualified candidates regardless of their backgrounds. The approach fails to recognize that discrimination harms any individual who experiences it, and that commitment to fair evaluation benefits everyone when institutions focus on individual merit rather than demographic representation.

quotes.text: The University invites applications from all qualified individuals. Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.
quotes.rationale: This passage demonstrates the institutional practice of explicitly categorizing and inviting applicants based on demographic characteristics including sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications and merit for the position.

incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia restricted a Canada Research Chair faculty position to applicants who identify as having a disability, excluding candidates based on this immutable characteristic.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia's Faculty of Forestry & Environmental Stewardship posted a tenure-stream faculty position for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair on April 2, 2026. The position was advertised for an assistant or associate professor with an application deadline of May 14, 2026, and a desired start date of July 1, 2027. The posting explicitly restricted eligibility to applicants who identify as having a disability, stating that applicants must self-identify as disabled to be considered for the position. The restriction was implemented pursuant to Section 42 of the BC Human Rights Code and in accordance with UBC's CRC Equity, Diversity, & Inclusion Action Plan. Candidates were required to complete a mandatory equity survey to confirm their disability status, with the definition of disability taken from the Accessible Canada Act encompassing physical, mental, intellectual, cognitive, learning, communication, or sensory impairments. The institution indicated that personal information would be collected to determine whether applicants qualified for participation in this restricted process. While the posting welcomed applications from disabled scholars who were also members of other federally designated groups, the fundamental requirement was self-identification as disabled. Non-disabled candidates, regardless of their qualifications, research excellence, or potential contributions to the field, were excluded from consideration.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are evaluated and excluded from employment opportunities based on whether they identify as having a disability rather than on their individual merit, research capabilities, or potential contributions to the field. The institution normalizes sorting candidates into categories based on immutable characteristics, treating disability status as a relevant criterion for academic appointment rather than focusing on each person's unique qualifications, research program, and abilities. Candidates learn that institutions view them primarily through demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals deserving evaluation based on their character, skills, and demonstrated achievements. The practice undermines the principle that academic positions should be awarded based on research excellence, teaching ability, and scholarly potential, sending a message that demographic classification takes precedence over individual accomplishment. Non-disabled researchers who may have exceptional qualifications, innovative research programs, or outstanding teaching records are categorically barred from competing, regardless of their individual circumstances or contributions. The approach entrenches institutional practices of demographic categorization in hiring, normalizing the idea that employment decisions should be made based on whether individuals fit predetermined demographic criteria rather than on rigorous assessment of each candidate's merit and potential.

quotes.text: For this position, applicants must identify as having a disability (please see below for more details).
quotes.rationale: Explicitly states the hiring restriction that excludes candidates who do not identify as having a disability, making disability status a mandatory requirement regardless of qualifications.

quotes.text: In accordance with UBC's CRC Equity, Diversity, & Inclusion Action Plan, and pursuant to Section 42 of the BC Human Rights code, this search is restricted to people with disabilities.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the institutional policy framework for the restriction and that the search is limited to people with disabilities, excluding others from consideration.

quotes.text: To be eligible for this CRC position, applicants must self-identify as disabled in the above survey with the definition of 'disability' taken from the Accessible Canada Act.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that self-identification as disabled is a mandatory eligibility requirement, creating a categorical barrier based on disability status.

quotes.text: Applicants to CRC positions are required to complete this equity survey as part of the application, and disabled candidates must self-identify to be considered for the position.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that the demographic categorization is mandatory for consideration, making disability status the primary screening criterion before any evaluation of individual merit.

quotes.text: The information you provide will be used to determine whether you qualify for participation in this restricted process, and to advance accessibility, equity, and fair adjudication in this process.
quotes.rationale: Shows that qualification for the hiring process itself depends on demographic categorization rather than research excellence or individual capabilities.

incidents.short_description: McGill University posted a job opening that explicitly stated preference for candidates based on skin colour, sex, disability status, ethnicity, and sexual orientation rather than solely on qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a job opening for Head of Liaison Services (Associate or Assistant Librarian) on April 2, 2026, with an application deadline of May 2, 2026. The posting included an equity statement declaring that the university "firmly adheres to principles of equity and diversity" and "welcomes applications from racialized persons and visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities." The statement indicates institutional practice of categorizing applicants by immutable characteristics including skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation. The posting encouraged candidates belonging to designated groups to indicate their membership in these categories within their application materials. This represents an institutional approach to hiring that explicitly organizes and evaluates candidates based on demographic classifications rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications, experience, and ability to perform the role. The position itself involves managing a team of eleven librarians and providing strategic leadership for open science initiatives, roles requiring professional competence and leadership skills that have no inherent connection to demographic characteristics.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and sorted by the institution based on immutable characteristics rather than being assessed solely as individuals with unique qualifications, skills, and experiences. The practice signals to all applicants that the university views them primarily through demographic categories, undermining recognition of their singular worth and individual merit. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic groups may reasonably perceive that their applications receive different consideration based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to perform the job. The institutional message normalizes the practice of categorizing people by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation in professional contexts, teaching that demographic classification is an appropriate basis for institutional decisions. This approach forecloses the possibility of a hiring process focused purely on evaluating each candidate's character, professional abilities, demonstrated competence, and potential to contribute to the library's mission. The practice divides the applicant pool into demographic categories rather than building common understanding that every qualified person deserves fair consideration based on their individual merits.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill s'engage fermement à respecter les principes d'équité et de diversité au sein de sa communauté, tout en valorisant la rigueur et l'excellence académiques. Elle accueille favorablement les demandes d'emploi des personnes racialisées et des minorités visibles, des femmes, des personnes autochtones, des personnes en situation de handicap, des minorités ethniques, des personnes de toute orientation sexuelle et identité de genre, ainsi que toute personne possédant les aptitudes et les connaissances nécessaires pour interagir au sein de groupes diversifiés.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly lists demographic categories that receive favourable consideration in the hiring process, indicating institutional practice of categorizing and evaluating applicants based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill dispose d'un programme d'équité en matière d'emploi et invite les membres des groupes visés à indiquer leur appartenance à ces derniers dans leur dossier de candidature.
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates that the university actively requests applicants to self-identify their membership in designated demographic groups as part of the application process, institutionalizing demographic categorization in hiring.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan advertised a dean position stating that applications from indigenous peoples and equity-deserving groups are encouraged, treating candidates differently based on demographic categories rather than individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Saskatchewan posted a job advertisement for Dean of the College of Law on April 2, 2026. The posting stated that the university 'actively seeks to create a welcoming environment where all individuals feel empowered to thrive, contribute, and grow' and that 'Applications from Indigenous peoples and equity-deserving groups are encouraged as part of our ongoing efforts to reflect the diversity of the communities we serve.' This language signals institutional preference for candidates based on demographic categories rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and merit.
