The position requires a relevant advanced degree, exemplary academic record, prior senior academic performance, demonstrated administrative and leadership experience, and superior relationship-building skills. The posting explicitly encourages applications based on whether individuals belong to particular demographic groups, framing this as part of diversity efforts.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and encouraged to apply based on demographic categories rather than being assessed solely as individuals based on their qualifications, experience, and character. The practice signals that institutions view applicants through the lens of group membership, undermining recognition of singular worth and individual merit. Candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic categories receive the message that their applications are less encouraged or valued, while those who do belong to these categories may face assumptions about whether their qualifications or their demographic status influenced hiring decisions. This approach normalizes institutional categorization of people by immutable characteristics or group identities rather than evaluating each person's unique abilities and demonstrated achievements. It undermines the principle that advancement should reflect merit and that people deserve recognition for their individual qualities, not their demographic classification.

quotes.text: The University of Saskatchewan actively seeks to create a welcoming environment where all individuals feel empowered to thrive, contribute, and grow. Applications from Indigenous peoples and equity-deserving groups are encouraged as part of our ongoing efforts to reflect the diversity of the communities we serve.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic group membership rather than individual qualifications, demonstrating institutional preference based on categorization of candidates.

incidents.short_description: The University of New Brunswick's Saint John campus advertised a tenure-track assistant professor position in mechanical engineering stating that preference will be given to candidates who meet certain demographic criteria.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of New Brunswick, Saint John campus, advertised a tenure-track assistant professor position in mechanical engineering with an application deadline of May 5, 2026. The job posting was published on April 2, 2026, seeking candidates with a Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering or closely related discipline to teach, conduct research, and supervise graduate students. The posting included a standard equity statement indicating the university is committed to employment equity and fostering diversity, and welcomes applications from qualified individuals including women, visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression. The posting stated that preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents, and also indicated preference for candidates with background in manufacturing engineering who have obtained or are eligible for professional engineering designation. The inclusion of demographic categories in the equity statement signals institutional attention to evaluating candidates through the lens of group identity rather than solely on individual merit, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities relevant to the teaching and research position.
incidents.expected_harm: The hiring process evaluates candidates in part through demographic categories rather than exclusively on their individual qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated potential for teaching and research excellence. This approach normalizes institutional consideration of immutable characteristics in employment decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed based on their unique merit and capabilities. Qualified candidates may perceive that their demographic classification influences hiring decisions alongside or instead of their scholarly achievements, teaching effectiveness, and research potential. The practice reinforces the notion that institutions should sort and evaluate people by demographic groups, rather than recognizing each applicant's singular worth as an individual scholar with distinct experiences, abilities, and contributions to offer. This framing divides the academic community along demographic lines rather than uniting faculty around shared commitments to excellence in teaching, advancing knowledge through research, and serving students as individuals.

quotes.text: The University of New Brunswick is committed to employment equity and fostering diversity within our community and developing an inclusive workplace that reflects the richness of the broader community that we serve. The University welcomes and encourages applications from all qualified individuals who will help us achieve our goals, including women, visible minorities, Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression.
quotes.rationale: This statement establishes the institutional framing that evaluates candidates through demographic categories, listing specific identity groups the university particularly welcomes, which signals that group membership is considered relevant to hiring decisions.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta advertised a faculty position stating that candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply as part of the university's priority to build a diverse faculty.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta's Faculty of Engineering posted a job advertisement on April 2, 2026, for a tenured or tenure-track faculty position in Robotics in Civil Engineering. The posting was part of a coordinated cluster hiring initiative focused on advancing next-generation infrastructure systems. Within the position description, the university explicitly stated that the positions are part of the University of Alberta's priority to build a diverse faculty and that candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply. This framing suggests the institution evaluates candidates not solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and potential contributions to research and teaching, but also considers their membership in demographic categories. The job posting otherwise outlined standard academic responsibilities including teaching, research, and service, along with technical qualifications related to robotics and civil engineering. The advertisement concluded with standard equity language stating the university is committed to creating an inclusive community and welcomes applications from all qualified persons, specifically listing women, indigenous persons, visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, and persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression.
incidents.expected_harm: Prospective faculty candidates are evaluated based on their membership in demographic categories rather than solely on their individual merit, qualifications, research potential, and teaching abilities. This approach signals that the institution values applicants differently based on immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that each person deserves recognition for their unique qualities and demonstrated capabilities. Candidates who do not belong to designated underrepresented groups may perceive that their applications receive less favourable consideration regardless of their individual strengths, while candidates from these groups may experience their selection as influenced by demographic categorization rather than full recognition of their personal achievements. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of individuals into demographic groups for hiring decisions, entrenching the notion that people should be viewed through the lens of group membership rather than as distinct individuals. This undermines the development of a faculty community united by shared commitment to excellence in teaching and research, instead fostering division based on immutable traits. The approach fails to advance a hiring process that genuinely evaluates each candidate as an individual with unique contributions to offer, and may create doubt about whether hiring decisions reflect true assessment of individual merit or compliance with demographic objectives.

quotes.text: These positions are part of the University of Alberta's priority to build a diverse faculty; thus, candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly indicates that the hiring process incorporates consideration of candidates' demographic group membership as part of institutional priorities, rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals based on their qualifications and potential.

quotes.text: The University of Alberta is committed to creating a university community where everyone feels valued, barriers to success are removed, and thriving connections are fostered. We welcome applications from all qualified persons. We encourage women, First Nations, Métis and Inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression, and all those who may contribute to the further diversification of ideas and the University to apply.
quotes.rationale: This statement identifies specific demographic categories that the university encourages to apply, demonstrating institutional emphasis on applicants' group membership based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than treating all individuals equally regardless of these characteristics.

incidents.short_description: The University of Alberta advertised faculty positions stating that candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply as part of the university's priority to build a diverse faculty.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Alberta Faculty of Engineering posted job advertisements for faculty positions in steel structures and civil engineering robotics as part of a cluster hiring initiative. The posting, advertised from April 2, 2026 until June 1, 2026, included a statement that these positions are part of the university's priority to build a diverse faculty and that candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply. This statement appeared in the position description section of the job advertisement. The advertisement also included standard Canadian employment law language stating that all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply but that Canadians and permanent residents will be given priority, and that the university is committed to creating an inclusive workplace. The job posting included an additional statement welcoming applications from all qualified persons and encouraging women, First Nations, Métis and Inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression to apply. The university framed these hiring considerations in terms of demographic group membership rather than individual qualifications, character, or demonstrated abilities relevant to the academic position.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated based on their membership in demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research potential, teaching abilities, and professional accomplishments. This approach signals that institutions consider immutable characteristics as factors in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed based on their unique merits and capabilities. Qualified candidates who do not belong to specified demographic groups may perceive that their applications receive different consideration based on characteristics unrelated to their professional competence. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of people by demographic proxies rather than recognition of each person's distinct achievements and potential contributions. The framing reinforces the idea that people should be understood primarily through group identities rather than as individuals with unique experiences, skills, and perspectives. This approach to hiring prevents the institution from fully embracing merit-based evaluation and sends a message that demographic representation goals influence personnel decisions.

quotes.text: These positions are part of the University of Alberta's priority to build a diverse faculty; thus, candidates from underrepresented groups are strongly encouraged to apply.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly identifies the hiring as part of a diversity-building priority and encourages applications based on demographic group membership (underrepresented groups), indicating that group identity is a consideration in the hiring process.

quotes.text: We encourage women, First Nations, Métis and Inuit persons, members of visible minority groups, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation or gender identity and expression, and all those who may contribute to the further diversification of ideas and the University to apply.
quotes.rationale: This statement enumerates specific demographic categories that the university is encouraging to apply, explicitly categorizing candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications.

incidents.short_description: McGill University posted a job advertisement for Head of Liaison Services that states the institution encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, ethnicity, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University posted a job advertisement on April 2, 2026, for the position of Head, Liaison Services at the Schulich Library. The posting is for a tenure-track librarian position (Assistant or Associate Librarian level) with a three-year term and possibility of renewal. The advertisement includes an equity statement declaring that McGill is committed to equity and diversity and specifically encourages applications from people categorized by demographic characteristics. The statement reads: 'We welcome and encourage applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities, as well as from all qualified candidates with the skills and knowledge to engage productively with diverse communities.' This language frames the hiring process around demographic categories, sorting prospective applicants into groups based on immutable characteristics rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications, experience, and merit. The institution explicitly names multiple demographic categories as receiving special encouragement to apply, suggesting that applicants' demographic characteristics are relevant factors in the hiring process rather than treating all applicants as distinct individuals to be evaluated solely on their abilities and demonstrated qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to view themselves and be viewed by the institution primarily through demographic categories rather than as unique individuals with distinct qualifications and experiences. This practice normalizes the institutional sorting of people based on immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that individuals should be evaluated based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated skills. Candidates who do not fit the named demographic categories receive the implicit message that their applications are less welcomed or valued, creating a tiered system of institutional encouragement based on group membership rather than individual merit. The framing teaches all applicants that institutions consider demographic characteristics as relevant factors in professional evaluation, rather than focusing exclusively on whether each individual candidate possesses the qualifications and capabilities needed for the role. This approach fails to affirm the singular worth of each applicant and instead reinforces the practice of categorizing people by demographic proxies. It prevents the institution from building a hiring culture centered on individual assessment and shared professional standards that apply equally to all candidates regardless of their background.

quotes.text: We welcome and encourage applications from racialized persons/visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of minority sexual orientations and gender identities, as well as from all qualified candidates with the skills and knowledge to engage productively with diverse communities.
quotes.rationale: This statement demonstrates the institution's practice of categorizing applicants by demographic characteristics and explicitly encouraging applications based on these categories rather than treating all candidates equally as individuals to be evaluated on merit.

incidents.short_description: The Université de Montréal posted a job for Department Head and Professor in Pediatrics that explicitly invited applications based on candidates' sex, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability status rather than solely on their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The Université de Montréal Faculty of Medicine posted a position for Department Head and Professor in Pediatrics (advertised April 2, 2026, until May 1, 2026). The posting outlined extensive academic and leadership responsibilities including teaching, research, supervision of students, departmental governance, and clinical leadership at CHU Sainte-Justine hospital. The position required a doctorate or equivalent, high-level expertise in pediatrics, substantial research experience with publications and funding, teaching excellence, ability to develop interdisciplinary collaborations, functional mastery of English, and sufficient knowledge of French. However, the job posting explicitly stated that through its employment equity access program, the university invited women, indigenous people, visible minorities, ethnic minorities, and persons with disabilities to identify themselves and submit their application. The posting indicated that recruitment selection tools could be adapted confidentially according to the needs of persons living with limitations who requested accommodations. The university described this approach as part of placing values of diversity, equity and inclusion at the heart of each of its missions, adopting a broad and inclusive definition of diversity going beyond applicable laws, and encouraging all qualified persons to apply regardless of their characteristics.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated and invited to apply based on their sex, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability status rather than solely on their individual qualifications, character, and abilities. This practice normalizes institutional categorization of people by demographic characteristics, treating these immutable traits as relevant factors in academic hiring rather than focusing exclusively on merit, expertise, and potential contribution. The explicit invitation based on demographic categories sends a message that the university views candidates through the lens of group membership rather than as distinct individuals with unique accomplishments and capabilities. This approach undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated abilities, creating a system where demographic characteristics become part of the assessment framework rather than being irrelevant to hiring decisions. The practice contradicts the stated goal of encouraging all qualified persons to apply by singling out specific demographic groups for special invitation, suggesting that membership in certain categories carries weight in the selection process beyond individual qualifications.

quotes.text: L'UdeM met les valeurs de diversité, d'équité et d'inclusion au cœur de chacune de ses missions. Par l'entremise de son programme d'accès à l'égalité en emploi (PAÉE), l'Université de Montréal invite les femmes, les Autochtones, les minorités visibles, les minorités ethniques et les personnes en situation de handicap à s'identifier et à présenter leur candidature.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly invites candidates to apply based on their sex (women), ethnicity (indigenous people, visible minorities, ethnic minorities), and disability status, demonstrating that the institution is categorizing and inviting applicants based on demographic characteristics rather than solely on individual qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Western University's Department of Psychiatry advertised faculty positions stating the university invites applications from all qualified individuals while simultaneously welcoming applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University's Department of Psychiatry posted job advertisements on April 2, 2026, for three clinical academic faculty positions in Forensic Psychiatry. The posting stated that positions are subject to budget approval and that applicants should have fluent written and oral communication skills in English. The advertisement then stated 'The University invites applications from all qualified individuals' immediately followed by 'Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.' This framing presents a contradiction: the institution simultaneously claims to invite applications from all qualified individuals while explicitly calling out and categorizing applicants based on immutable characteristics including sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. The language suggests the university evaluates candidates through the lens of demographic categories rather than solely on individual qualifications, character, and abilities relevant to the forensic psychiatry positions.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are signaled that the institution evaluates them based on demographic categories rather than purely as individuals with unique qualifications and merit. This undermines the recognition of singular worth by suggesting that immutable characteristics are relevant considerations in hiring decisions. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups, communicating that these categories matter in employment decisions rather than focusing exclusively on individual character, abilities, clinical skills, research productivity, and teaching excellence. Qualified candidates who do not fit the specifically welcomed demographic categories may perceive they are disadvantaged, while those in the named categories may experience their individual accomplishments as secondary to their demographic characteristics. The institutional message fails to affirm that every applicant possesses inherent dignity deserving recognition for their unique qualities, instead reinforcing a framework where demographic proxies are foregrounded in the hiring process.

quotes.text: The University invites applications from all qualified individuals. Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.
quotes.rationale: This statement reveals the institutional practice of categorizing applicants by demographic characteristics while simultaneously claiming to invite all qualified individuals, demonstrating a contradiction between evaluating people as individuals versus evaluating them through demographic categories.

incidents.short_description: Lakehead University posted a faculty position stating preference for candidates with lived experience from under-represented groups and experience working with communities based on skin colour and ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: Lakehead University's Department of Physics posted a job advertisement on April 2, 2026, for an NSERC Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Quantum Technologies at the Assistant or Associate Professor level. The posting included an equity statement declaring the university has an Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Action Plan with a goal to recruit a diverse workforce as measured by increasing representation of under-represented groups. The statement explicitly encouraged candidates to self-identify if from an under-represented group and stated the university prefers candidates with knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience. The posting further stated that experience working with communities defined by skin colour or ethnicity, and members of other equity-deserving groups is a strong asset, and that lived experience or worked experience of these issues is preferred. The university also stated it recognizes the value of demonstrated experience in increasing diversity in the previous institutional environment and in curriculum as an asset. Candidates were required to submit a statement describing their contributions or potential contributions to equity, diversity and inclusion along with their ability to work with a culturally diverse and international student, staff and faculty body. The posting framed candidates through demographic categories and lived experiences tied to immutable characteristics rather than evaluating them solely on their individual qualifications, research excellence, teaching ability and character relevant to the academic position.
incidents.expected_harm: The hiring process evaluates candidates based on their membership in demographic categories and lived experiences tied to immutable characteristics rather than solely on their individual merit, research excellence, teaching ability and character. Qualified researchers are encouraged to highlight demographic categorizations and experiences with communities defined by skin colour and ethnicity rather than being assessed purely on their contributions to quantum technologies research and their potential as educators and mentors. The preference for candidates with lived experience from particular demographic groups signals that the institution values individuals differently based on immutable traits rather than recognizing the singular worth of each candidate. This approach normalizes sorting people into demographic categories in hiring decisions and undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people as distinct individuals based on their abilities and demonstrated achievements. Candidates learn that academic hiring considers demographic proxies alongside or even ahead of scholarly qualifications, potentially excluding excellent researchers who do not fit preferred demographic profiles. The practice reinforces division by emphasizing differences based on immutable characteristics rather than fostering a shared academic community united by commitment to research excellence, truth-seeking and student success.

quotes.text: Lakehead University has an Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Action Plan with a goal to recruit and retain a diverse workforce as measured by increasing representation of under-represented groups among applicants, candidates and hires. We encourage candidates to self-identify if you are from an under-represented group, and prefer candidates with the knowledge, competencies and relationships derived from lived experience.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly expresses preference for candidates based on demographic group membership and lived experience tied to immutable characteristics rather than solely on individual qualifications for the research position.

quotes.text: Experience working with Indigenous or racialized communities, and/or members of other equity-deserving groups is a strong asset. A lived experience or worked experience of any of these issues is preferred.
quotes.rationale: This directly states that experience with communities categorized by skin colour and ethnicity, and lived experience of these categorizations, is preferred in hiring decisions, showing evaluation based on demographic proxies.

incidents.short_description: Western University's Department of Psychiatry posted job openings for psychiatry faculty positions stating commitment to supporting diversity, inclusion, and decolonization, and implemented an indigenous affirmation policy requiring candidates who declare indigenous citizenship to undergo verification through a relational accountability process.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University's Department of Psychiatry, within the Schulich School of Medicine & Dentistry, advertised three full-time clinical academic faculty positions on April 2, 2026. The job posting included language stating the department is committed to supporting diversity, inclusion, and decolonization as different perspectives enrich their work. The posting also described an affirmation policy requiring candidates who declare Indigenous citizenship or membership to have their declaration affirmed through a relational accountability process led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives. The policy was described as a safeguard against use of incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information in circumstances where a candidate has made a declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership for material advantage at Western, including where required or preferred for the position. Candidates invited for interview or who are short-listed and who have made such a declaration would be asked to undergo this affirmation process. The posting stated Western is committed to employment equity and diversity and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.
incidents.expected_harm: The institutional framing signals to applicants that demographic categories are central to how the university evaluates candidates, rather than focusing primarily on individual qualifications, character, and abilities. By explicitly connecting diversity and inclusion to the enrichment of work, the language suggests that individuals are valued for characteristics associated with demographic groups rather than their unique contributions as distinct persons. The Indigenous affirmation policy creates a system where individuals who identify based on ethnicity must undergo additional scrutiny and verification processes not required of other candidates, categorizing and treating candidates differently based on their ethnic background. This approach normalizes institutional sorting of individuals into demographic categories and evaluation based on immutable characteristics. Applicants learn that the institution views them through the lens of group membership rather than as individuals with singular worth. The emphasis on welcoming applications from specified demographic categories reinforces division rather than recognizing the shared humanity and common aspirations that unite all potential candidates seeking to contribute to medical education and patient care.

quotes.text: Western, like many postsecondary institutions in Canada, is moving beyond sole reliance upon Indigenous self-identification in its hiring processes. This is to safeguard against the use of incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information in circumstances in which a candidate has made a declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership. Candidates who are invited for an interview or who are short-listed, and who have made a declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership for material advantage at Western, including where required or preferred for the position, will be asked to have their declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership affirmed through a relational accountability process, led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives (OII), that is consistent with Indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing.
quotes.rationale: Documents policy requiring differential treatment and verification process for candidates based on ethnic identity, creating institutional categorization system.

quotes.text: The University invites applications from all qualified individuals. Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.
quotes.rationale: Shows institutional emphasis on demographic categories in hiring communications, framing applicants by group membership.

incidents.short_description: The University of Waterloo advertised a department chair position stating it encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all applicants equally as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Waterloo's Faculty of Science advertised a tenure-track position for Professor and Chair of the Department of Earth and Environmental Sciences on April 2, 2026. The job posting explicitly stated that the university encourages applications from candidates based on demographic categories. The institution indicated it seeks applicants based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, Indigenous ancestry, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation. The posting framed this approach as part of the university's commitment to equity and diversity, stating these are integral to academic excellence. Rather than evaluating candidates solely on their scholarly achievements, leadership abilities, research record, and teaching excellence, the university signaled that it considers demographic characteristics in its hiring process. The position requires demonstrated leadership ability, strong research and teaching records, and eligibility for tenured faculty rank at associate or full professor level, with a salary range of $150,000 to $200,000.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated by the institution with consideration given to their demographic categories rather than being assessed purely as individuals based on their qualifications, scholarly achievements, and leadership capabilities. The institutional messaging signals that demographic characteristics are relevant factors in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect merit and individual accomplishment. Candidates who do not fit the encouraged demographic categories may perceive that their applications receive different consideration, while those in encouraged categories may face assumptions about whether their qualifications or demographic status influenced hiring decisions. This approach reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than recognizing their unique qualities, experiences, and demonstrated abilities. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people by immutable traits, teaching that universities view scholars primarily through demographic lenses rather than as distinct individuals with singular worth. It undermines shared understanding that academic positions should be awarded based on scholarly excellence and leadership ability regardless of demographic characteristics.

quotes.text: The University values the diverse and intersectional identities of its students, faculty, and staff. The University regards equity and diversity as an integral part of academic excellence and is committed to accessibility for all employees. The University of Waterloo seeks applicants who embrace our values of equity, anti-racism and inclusion. As such, we encourage applications from candidates who have been historically disadvantaged and marginalized, including applicants who identify as Indigenous peoples (e.g., First Nations, Métis, Inuit/Inuk), Black, racialized, people with disabilities, women and/or 2SLGBTQ.
quotes.rationale: This passage demonstrates that the university explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, Indigenous ancestry, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than treating all applicants equally as individuals. The institution signals that demographic characteristics are relevant considerations in the hiring process.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan's Western College of Veterinary Medicine posted a job advertisement that prominently featured institutional commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion principles, framing hiring through demographic categories rather than solely individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 2, 2026, the University of Saskatchewan's Western College of Veterinary Medicine advertised a 2-year term Lecturer position in veterinary anesthesia. The job posting outlined professional qualifications including Doctor of Veterinary Medicine degree, completion of an advanced degree, Diplomate status with the American College of Veterinary Anesthesia and Analgesia, and eligibility for Saskatchewan veterinary licensure. The position description detailed clinical responsibilities, teaching duties, and mentorship expectations. The advertisement included institutional statements about equity, diversity, and inclusion that emphasized evaluating candidates through demographic categories. The university stated it "aspires to be what the world needs and embraces equity, diversity and inclusion as foundational to excellence and innovation" and that "applications from equity-deserving groups are encouraged." The posting framed hiring through the lens of group membership rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications, character, and demonstrated abilities. The advertisement also included specific protocols for candidates asserting indigenous membership, requiring verification through documentation and a Standing Committee process, treating this demographic characteristic as relevant information for employment consideration.
incidents.expected_harm: Job applicants are encouraged to identify themselves by demographic categories and group memberships rather than being evaluated solely as individuals based on their unique qualifications, character, and professional abilities. The institutional messaging signals that membership in certain demographic groups is considered relevant to hiring decisions, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed on their merits and professional credentials. Candidates may feel pressure to disclose demographic information or frame their applications around group identity rather than individual achievement. The approach normalizes institutional categorization of people into demographic groups and suggests that group membership carries weight in employment decisions, rather than treating each applicant as a distinct individual with unique qualities. The verification process for indigenous membership establishes a formal institutional mechanism for sorting candidates by demographic characteristics, entrenching the practice of evaluating people through categorical lenses rather than their singular worth as individuals.

quotes.text: We continue to grow our partnerships with Indigenous communities across the province, nationally, and internationally and value the unique perspective that Indigenous employees provide to strengthen these relationships. Verification of Indigenous Membership/Citizenship at the University of Saskatchewan is led and determined by the deybwewin | taapwaywin | tapwewin: Indigenous Truth policy and the Standing Committee in accordance with the processes developed to enact the policy. Successful candidates that assert Indigenous membership/citizenship will be asked to complete the verification process of Indigenous membership/citizenship with documentation.
quotes.rationale: This establishes a formal institutional process for verifying and documenting demographic characteristics of candidates, treating group membership as relevant employment information and creating a mechanism for categorizing individuals by demographic identity.

quotes.text: The University of Saskatchewan aspires to be what the world needs and embraces equity, diversity and inclusion as foundational to excellence and innovation. We actively seek to create a welcoming environment where all individuals feel empowered to thrive, contribute, and grow. Applications from equity-deserving groups are encouraged as part of our ongoing efforts to reflect the diversity of the communities we serve: EDI Framework for Action.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic group membership ('equity-deserving groups') rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications, suggesting that group identity is relevant to hiring considerations.

incidents.institution_name: University of British Columbia Faculty of Forestry & Environmental Stewardship
incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia restricted a faculty position to applicants based on disability status, requiring candidates to self-identify as disabled to be considered for the role.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia's Faculty of Forestry & Environmental Stewardship posted a tenure-stream faculty position for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair on April 2, 2026. The posting explicitly restricted eligibility to applicants who identify as having a disability. The restriction was implemented pursuant to Section 42 of the BC Human Rights Code and aligned with UBC's Canada Research Chair Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Action Plan. Applicants were required to complete a mandatory equity survey to confirm their eligibility, and the posting stated that disabled candidates must self-identify to be considered for the position. The definition of disability was taken from the Accessible Canada Act and included physical, mental, intellectual, cognitive, learning, communication, or sensory impairments, whether permanent, temporary, or episodic. The university made clear that only those who self-identified as disabled in the survey would be eligible for consideration. The search committee would receive only the names of eligible candidates from the Equity & Inclusion Office. While the posting welcomed applications from disabled scholars who were also members of other federally designated groups, the fundamental eligibility requirement was disability status rather than qualifications, research excellence, or individual merit alone.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated for employment based on whether they possess a particular immutable characteristic rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research capabilities, and potential contributions to the institution. This approach reduces individuals to a demographic category, requiring them to categorize themselves by disability status as a condition of eligibility rather than being assessed as distinct individuals with unique abilities and achievements. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into identity-based groups for access to opportunities, undermining the principle that individuals should be evaluated based on their character, demonstrated abilities, and merit. Qualified researchers who do not identify with the specified demographic category are excluded from consideration regardless of their individual circumstances, research excellence, or potential to advance the institution's academic mission. The requirement to self-identify and disclose personal information about disability status as a threshold eligibility criterion creates a system where demographic proxies determine access to career opportunities rather than individual qualifications. This framing teaches students and the academic community that institutions properly categorize people by immutable traits when allocating opportunities, rather than recognizing each person's inherent worth and evaluating them based on what they can contribute as individuals.

quotes.text: For this position, applicants must identify as having a disability (please see below for more details).
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly restricts the position to candidates based on an immutable characteristic, making disability status a threshold requirement rather than evaluating candidates as individuals based on their qualifications and merit.

quotes.text: In accordance with UBC's CRC Equity, Diversity, & Inclusion Action Plan, and pursuant to Section 42 of the BC Human Rights code, this search is restricted to people with disabilities.
quotes.rationale: This confirms the institutional decision to restrict hiring based on disability status, framing it as an equity initiative while implementing a demographic-based eligibility requirement.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University restricted a Canada Research Chair competition to current employees who identify as members of specific demographic groups, evaluating eligibility based on immutable characteristics rather than research merit alone.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University's Department of Mechanical and Mechatronics Engineering posted a competition for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in AI-driven Navigation for Arctic and Harsh Environments on April 1, 2026. The posting explicitly restricted the competition to current Memorial University employees who identify as members of one or more employment equity groups, including women, 2SLGBTQIA+ people, indigenous peoples, racialized persons, and persons with disabilities. This restriction was implemented pursuant to approval granted by the Human Rights Commission under Section 8 of the Human Rights Act. The position offered a five-year appointment valued at $100,000 per year plus an additional $20,000 annual research stipend for first-term Tier 2 Chairs, with the possibility of renewal for another five years. The competition required candidates to have a strong record in Mechatronics Engineering, a PhD, evidence of research training supervision, and demonstrated research capability in areas including AI for arctic operations, multi-sensor navigation in harsh environments, and GPS-denied navigation. However, regardless of research qualifications, only candidates identifying with specified demographic categories were eligible to apply. The university required applicants to complete an employment equity survey to be considered as members of equity-deserving groups, making demographic self-identification a prerequisite for candidacy.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are excluded from competing for a significant academic position based on their demographic characteristics rather than being evaluated as individuals based on their research merit, abilities, and qualifications. The restriction undermines the principle that institutions should assess people based on their character, capabilities, and demonstrated achievements rather than immutable traits. Researchers who do not identify with the specified demographic categories cannot pursue this opportunity regardless of their expertise in AI-driven navigation for arctic and harsh environments, their research record, or their potential contributions to the field. This categorization normalizes the practice of sorting individuals into demographic groups and making opportunities contingent on group membership rather than individual merit. The approach sends a message that demographic characteristics are relevant factors in determining who may contribute to advancing knowledge in mechanical and mechatronics engineering, rather than treating research excellence as a standard that transcends such categories. Current Memorial University employees who possess the required qualifications but do not identify with the specified groups are denied the opportunity to compete, preventing the institution from evaluating all qualified candidates as distinct individuals. The practice reinforces division among researchers by emphasizing demographic differences rather than fostering a shared commitment to advancing understanding through research excellence and open inquiry.

quotes.text: This competition is open only to applicants who: are current employees of Memorial University; and identify as members of one or more employment equity groups, including women; 2SLGBTQIA+ people; Indigenous peoples; racialized persons; and persons with disabilities as per approval granted by the Human Rights Commission pursuant to Section 8 of the Human Rights Act.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the explicit demographic-based restriction that limits eligibility for the position to current employees who identify with specified groups, regardless of research qualifications.

quotes.text: Please note that applicants cannot be considered as a member of an equity-deserving group(s) unless they complete Memorial's employment equity survey.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that demographic self-identification through a survey is a mandatory prerequisite for candidacy, making group membership rather than individual merit the gateway to opportunity.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University restricted eligibility for a Canada Research Chair position to current employees who identify as members of specific demographic groups, excluding individuals based on immutable characteristics rather than evaluating candidates based on their research abilities and qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University's Department of Human Biosciences, Faculty of Science, posted a competition for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Computational Biochemistry on April 1, 2026, with a closing date of June 1, 2026. The position carried significant value, offering $100,000 per year for five years plus an additional $20,000 annual research stipend for first-term Tier 2 Chairs, with possibility of renewal for another five years. The university explicitly restricted the competition to applicants who met two criteria: they must be current employees of Memorial University, and they must identify as members of one or more specified demographic groups including women, 2SLGBTQIA+ people, indigenous peoples, racialized persons, and persons with disabilities. This restriction was implemented pursuant to approval granted by the Human Rights Commission under Section 8 of the Human Rights Act. The job posting sought an exceptional emerging scholar with a strong record in Biochemistry, a PhD, demonstrated experience in biomolecular simulations and multiscale modelling, and the ability to demonstrate an independent, externally funded research program. The position required contributions to both undergraduate and graduate teaching programs. Rather than evaluating all qualified researchers based on their individual research capabilities, scholarly achievements, and potential contributions to the field of computational biochemistry, the institution made eligibility for consideration contingent on candidates' demographic characteristics. The posting acknowledged Memorial University's commitment to employment equity, diversity, inclusion and anti-racism, and referenced an Indigenous Verification Policy that came into effect on February 25, 2026, noting that additional information may be required from applicants subject to this policy.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are excluded from career advancement opportunities based on their demographic characteristics rather than being evaluated as individuals based on their research abilities, scholarly achievements, and potential contributions to computational biochemistry. This institutional practice establishes a system where access to prestigious, well-funded academic positions depends on identifying with particular demographic categories, undermining the principle that people should be assessed based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated merit. The restriction prevents the university from considering the full pool of talented researchers and forecloses opportunities for individuals to compete for positions based on their unique qualifications and research potential. Scholars learn that institutions evaluate them primarily through demographic categories rather than their individual scientific accomplishments and research vision. The practice normalizes sorting people into groups based on immutable characteristics, reinforcing the idea that demographic proxies should determine who receives institutional support and recognition rather than individual scholarly merit. This approach divides the academic community by creating separate paths to opportunity based on demographic categorization, undermining shared civic belonging and common pursuit of knowledge that could unite researchers around their dedication to advancing science regardless of their backgrounds.

quotes.text: This competition is open only to applicants who: are current employees of Memorial University; and identify as members of one or more employment equity groups, including women; 2SLGBTQIA+ people; Indigenous peoples; racialized persons; and persons with disabilities as per approval granted by the Human Rights Commission pursuant to Section 8 of the Human Rights Act.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the explicit restriction of the hiring competition based on demographic characteristics, demonstrating that the institution excluded candidates based on immutable traits rather than evaluating all qualified individuals based on their research abilities and merit.

quotes.text: Please note that Memorial University's Indigenous Verification Policy came into effect on February 25, 2026. Additional information may be required from applicants, subject to the Policy and its procedural guidance.
quotes.rationale: This quote demonstrates additional institutional mechanisms for categorizing and verifying individuals based on demographic characteristics, showing the extent to which the institution prioritizes demographic classification over individual evaluation.

quotes.text: Memorial is committed to employment equity, diversity, inclusion and anti-racism, and encourages applications from all qualified candidates, including women; people of any sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender expression; Indigenous Peoples; visible minorities, and racialized people; and people with disabilities.
quotes.rationale: This quote reveals the institutional framing that justifies demographic-based restrictions, showing how the university conceptualizes its approach as promoting equity while actually restricting opportunities based on demographic categories rather than individual merit.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University restricted applications for a Canada Research Chair position to current employees who identify as members of employment equity groups, excluding applicants based on their skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University's Faculty of Medicine posted a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair position in Musculoskeletal Health and Genomic Map of the Newfoundland and Labrador Population on April 1, 2026. The competition was explicitly restricted to applicants meeting two criteria: current employment at Memorial University and identification as members of one or more employment equity groups, including women, 2SLGBTQIA+ people, indigenous peoples, racialized persons, and persons with disabilities. This restriction was approved by the Human Rights Commission pursuant to Section 8 of the Human Rights Act. The position offered a seven-year appointment valued at $200,000 per year, with appointment at the rank of Full Professor or Associate Professor. The university required applicants to complete an employment equity survey to be considered as members of equity-deserving groups, stating that applicants could not be considered as members of such groups unless they completed the survey. The hiring restriction evaluated candidates based on demographic categories rather than their individual qualifications, research excellence, and ability to fulfill the position's requirements. While the posting listed qualifications related to research excellence and mentorship, access to the competition itself was contingent on demographic characteristics rather than merit alone.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are excluded from applying for a prestigious academic position based on their skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status rather than being evaluated as individuals based on their research excellence, qualifications, and ability to contribute to the field. The institution normalizes sorting people into demographic categories and granting or denying opportunities based on immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that individuals should be evaluated on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. Current Memorial University employees who do not identify with the specified demographic groups are denied the opportunity to compete for the position regardless of their qualifications or research accomplishments. The practice sends a message that institutions should evaluate people as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualities and capabilities. This approach to hiring entrenches division among faculty and researchers by emphasizing demographic differences rather than shared commitment to research excellence and academic merit. The restriction prevents the institution from considering all qualified candidates and selecting the individual whose research vision, capabilities, and leadership would best advance the stated research goals, regardless of demographic characteristics.

quotes.text: This competition is open only to applicants who: are current employees of Memorial University, and; identify as members of one or more employment equity groups, including women; 2SLGBTQIA+ people; Indigenous peoples; racialized persons; and persons with disabilities as per approval granted by the Human Right Commission pursuant to Section 8 of the Human Rights Act.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the explicit restriction that limits the competition based on demographic categories, demonstrating that applicants are excluded based on immutable characteristics rather than individual qualifications.

quotes.text: An applicant cannot be considered as a member of an equity-deserving group(s) unless they complete an employment equity survey.
quotes.rationale: This quote demonstrates the institutional mechanism by which applicants are categorized and evaluated based on demographic characteristics, reinforcing that access to the position depends on identifying with specified groups.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's job posting for Assistant Vice-President, Spaces and Experiences strongly encouraged applications from candidates based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other immutable characteristics rather than evaluating all applicants equally as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 1, 2026, the University of Toronto posted a job advertisement for the position of Assistant Vice-President, Spaces and Experiences, a senior leadership role overseeing a $140 million portfolio managing student housing, food services, retail, hospitality, and real estate operations with over 500 staff. The position reports to the Vice President, Operations and Real Estate Partnerships and serves as CEO of campus operations on the St. George campus. The job posting stated that the university strongly encourages applications from people categorized by skin colour and ethnicity, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities. The posting further stated that the university values applicants who have demonstrated a commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion and recognizes that diverse perspectives, experiences, and expertise are essential to strengthening its academic mission. This language signals institutional preference for candidates based on demographic categories rather than evaluating all individuals solely on their character, abilities, qualifications, and demonstrated leadership experience. The position requires senior leadership experience managing large revenue-generating operations, strong strategic and financial acumen, and the ability to lead complex, multi-stakeholder organizations, yet the posting emphasizes demographic characteristics as factors in the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are encouraged or discouraged from applying based on their immutable characteristics rather than being evaluated purely as individuals with unique qualifications and experiences. The institutional message communicates that demographic categories are relevant factors in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect merit and individual capabilities. Applicants learn that the university categorizes people by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation in employment decisions, normalizing the practice of evaluating individuals through demographic proxies rather than their distinct qualities and achievements. This approach creates uncertainty about whether hiring decisions will be based on individual merit or on meeting demographic preferences, potentially causing qualified candidates to question whether they will be fairly assessed. The emphasis on demographic diversity as essential to the academic mission suggests that people's immutable traits determine their perspectives and contributions, reducing individuals to representatives of demographic categories rather than recognizing each person's capacity for reason, understanding, and unique insight regardless of their background. The practice undermines shared civic belonging within the institution by sorting people into demographic groups rather than fostering a community united by common professional standards and individual excellence.

quotes.text: UofT strongly encourages applications from Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation, demonstrating institutional preference based on immutable characteristics rather than individual merit.

incidents.short_description: McGill University posted a faculty position in forest ecology stating that hiring priority would be given to applicants based on skin colour, sex, disability status, ethnicity, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating all qualified candidates equally.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's Department of Natural Resource Sciences advertised a tenure-track position for an Assistant or Associate Professor in Forest Ecology, posted on April 1, 2026, with an application deadline of April 30, 2026. The job posting stated that McGill is firmly committed to principles of equity and diversity while valuing academic rigour and excellence. The posting explicitly welcomed applications from people categorized by skin colour and visible minorities, women, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities. The institution framed its hiring approach around demographic categories, stating these preferences alongside a note that qualified candidates would be prioritized according to Canadian immigration requirements favouring citizens and permanent residents. The posting described the position as requiring a demonstrated commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion as part of the job requirements. McGill presented this demographic-based hiring preference as compatible with, and indeed part of, its commitment to academic excellence, rather than focusing exclusively on evaluating candidates based on their individual qualifications, research potential, teaching ability, and demonstrated merit in forest ecology.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated and potentially excluded from employment consideration based on their immutable characteristics rather than their individual merit, research excellence, teaching ability, and potential contributions to forest ecology. The institution signals that demographic categories are a factor in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual qualifications and demonstrated abilities. Candidates learn that the university considers their skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation as relevant factors in employment decisions, rather than evaluating them solely as individuals based on their unique qualifications and scholarly potential. This approach normalizes the practice of sorting candidates into demographic groups for hiring purposes, treating people as representatives of categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique capabilities. The stated requirement for "demonstrated commitment to equity, diversity, and inclusion" creates ambiguity about whether candidates must embrace institutional frameworks that categorize people by immutable traits. The hiring approach fails to build understanding that employment decisions should be based on individual character, abilities, and demonstrated excellence, missing an opportunity to unite the academic community around shared principles of individual worth and merit-based evaluation.

quotes.text: L'Université McGill s'engage fermement à respecter les principes d'équité et de diversité au sein de sa communauté, tout en valorisant la rigueur et l'excellence académiques. Elle accueille favorablement les demandes d'emploi des personnes racisées et des minorités visibles, des femmes, des personnes autochtones, des personnes en situation de handicap, des minorités ethniques, des personnes de toute orientation sexuelle et identité de genre, ainsi que toute personne possédant les aptitudes et les connaissances nécessaires pour interagir au sein de groupes diversifiés.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly identifies the demographic categories that McGill welcomes for employment applications, demonstrating the institution's practice of categorizing candidates by immutable characteristics including skin colour, sex, disability, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and gender identity in its hiring process.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto advertised a tenure-track professor position in sociology while stating it strongly encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Toronto Department of Sociology posted a job advertisement on April 1, 2026, for a full-time tenure stream position at the rank of Professor in Culture, Social Interaction, and Qualitative Methods, with an anticipated start date of January 1, 2027. The position offered a salary exceeding $200,000 CAD per annum and required a PhD in Sociology or related area with demonstrated excellence in research and teaching. The job posting included a diversity statement declaring that the university strongly encourages applications from indigenous peoples, people based on their skin colour, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities. The statement framed diverse perspectives and experiences as essential to strengthening the academic mission. The posting evaluated and categorized potential applicants based on demographic characteristics rather than focusing exclusively on their individual qualifications, character, abilities, and demonstrated research and teaching excellence. While the position required substantial academic credentials including an established international reputation, sustained high-impact publications, and excellence in teaching, the university simultaneously emphasized encouraging applications based on immutable traits rather than solely on individual merit and accomplishment.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to view themselves and be viewed by the institution through demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals evaluated on their unique qualifications, character, and abilities. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that individuals should be recognized for their singular worth. Qualified candidates who do not fall into the specified demographic categories receive a signal that the institution evaluates people partly based on group membership rather than exclusively on individual merit. The approach reinforces the notion that demographic characteristics are relevant considerations in academic hiring decisions, rather than affirming that every person deserves evaluation based solely on their demonstrated abilities, accomplishments, and potential contributions. The statement contradicts the university's own commitment to excellence by suggesting that demographic diversity is essential to the academic mission, rather than maintaining that intellectual excellence, rigorous scholarship, and effective teaching transcend demographic categories. This framing divides the academic community along demographic lines rather than fostering shared civic belonging based on common pursuit of knowledge and truth.

quotes.text: The University of Toronto embraces Diversity and is building a culture of belonging that increases our capacity to effectively address and serve the interests of our global community. We strongly encourage applications from Indigenous Peoples, Black and racialized persons, women, persons with disabilities, and people of diverse sexual and gender identities.
quotes.rationale: Core evidence showing the university categorizes and encourages applications from people based on immutable demographic characteristics rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Western University posted a faculty position requiring candidates who declare indigenous citizenship to undergo an affirmation process that evaluates their declaration based on relational accountability and indigenous ways of knowing.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University's Department of Mechanical and Materials Engineering posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position on April 1, 2026, for the area of Sustainable Materials. The job posting included an Affirmation Policy Statement that applies to candidates who declare indigenous citizenship or membership. The policy requires candidates who make such declarations and are invited for interviews or shortlisted to have their declaration affirmed through a relational accountability process led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives. The posting states this process is consistent with indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing. The policy explicitly frames this requirement as applying when a candidate has made a declaration for material advantage at Western, including where indigenous status is required or preferred for the position. The job posting also includes standard equity language welcoming applications from women, members of racialized groups, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression. The position requires a PhD in relevant engineering fields, evidence of research excellence, teaching ability, and eligibility for registration as a Professional Engineer in Ontario, with compensation ranging from $115,000 to $140,000.
incidents.expected_harm: The affirmation policy creates a system where individuals who declare indigenous citizenship face additional scrutiny and evaluation beyond standard credential verification, subjecting them to assessment based on their membership in a demographic category rather than their individual qualifications for the academic position. Candidates are required to participate in a process that evaluates their identity claims through culturally specific frameworks, introducing subjective criteria into hiring decisions that should focus on academic merit, research potential, and teaching ability. The policy presumes that indigenous status declarations provide material advantage in hiring, explicitly acknowledging that such status may be required or preferred, which means the institution evaluates candidates differently based on their membership in ethnic or racial categories. This approach reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than assessing them based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated accomplishments. The requirement for relational accountability processes based on ways of knowing creates barriers that apply only to certain candidates based on their ancestry, undermining equal treatment in the hiring process and sending a message that the institution sorts and evaluates people according to their demographic background rather than their individual merit.

quotes.text: Western, like many postsecondary institutions in Canada, is moving beyond sole reliance upon Indigenous self-identification in its hiring processes. This is to safeguard against the use of incorrect, incomplete, or misleading information in circumstances in which a candidate has made a declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the institution has implemented a policy that treats indigenous identity declarations differently from other aspects of candidate applications, requiring additional verification processes.

quotes.text: Candidates who are invited for an interview or who are short-listed, and who have made a declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership for material advantage at Western, including where required or preferred for the position, will be asked to have their declaration of Indigenous citizenship or membership affirmed through a relational accountability process, led by the Office of Indigenous Initiatives (OII), that is consistent with Indigenous ways of knowing, being, and doing.
quotes.rationale: Core evidence showing the specific requirement imposed on candidates who declare indigenous citizenship, explicitly acknowledging that such status may be required or preferred for positions, and detailing the culturally-specific evaluation process.

quotes.text: The University invites applications from all qualified individuals. Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.
quotes.rationale: Shows the institution's broader equity framework that categorizes and labels applicants by demographic characteristics, indicating institutional practice of sorting candidates into identity categories.

incidents.short_description: Western University posted a faculty position that states the university welcomes applications from people based on demographic categories including skin colour and sex, rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University's Department of Mechanical & Materials Engineering posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Thermofluids on April 1, 2026. The posting included standard qualifications related to engineering expertise, teaching ability, and research potential. However, the job posting contained language stating that the university welcomes applications from specific demographic categories. The posting declared that Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression. This language signals that the institution categorizes applicants based on immutable characteristics and demographic groupings rather than treating all candidates uniformly as individuals to be evaluated solely on their qualifications, abilities, and potential contributions to the academic mission.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are signaled that the institution views them through the lens of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualifications and potential. This framing undermines the principle that people should be evaluated based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated merit. The explicit welcoming of applications based on demographic characteristics suggests that immutable traits will be considered in hiring decisions, rather than focusing exclusively on each candidate's individual scholarly accomplishments, teaching effectiveness, and research potential. Candidates may question whether they are being assessed fairly as individuals or whether demographic categorization influences evaluation. The institutional messaging normalizes sorting people into groups based on skin colour, sex, and other immutable characteristics, rather than fostering a culture where each person's singular worth is recognized. This approach fails to build common understanding among faculty and students that academic excellence should be the sole criterion for advancement, and instead reinforces division by emphasizing demographic differences rather than shared commitment to scholarly inquiry and teaching.

quotes.text: The University invites applications from all qualified individuals. Western is committed to employment equity and diversity in the workplace and welcomes applications from women, members of racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, and persons of any gender identity or gender expression.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly welcomes applications based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, and other immutable characteristics, signaling that the institution categorizes applicants by these traits rather than treating all candidates uniformly as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières posted a job opening that explicitly encouraged applications based on immutable characteristics including sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 1, 2026, the Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières advertised a tenure-track faculty position in tourism and global health within the Department of Leisure, Culture and Tourism Studies.
