This framing fails to foster an academic environment where all qualified individuals understand they will be evaluated solely on their scholarship, teaching potential, and professional capabilities, regardless of their background. The practice entrenches the idea that universities should categorize and track faculty by demographic characteristics rather than building academic communities united by shared commitment to excellence in teaching, research, and advancing knowledge.

quotes.text: Laurentian University is an inclusive and welcoming community committed to employment equity. Applications are encouraged from members of equity-seeking communities including women, racialized and Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour/ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than treating all candidates equally as individuals to be evaluated on merit alone.

incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University posted a biology faculty position stating particular interest in applications from members of historically disadvantaged and marginalized groups based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Toronto Metropolitan University's Department of Chemistry and Biology advertised a one-year limited term Assistant Professor position in Biology, posted on March 30, 2026, with an application deadline of April 30, 2026. The position requires teaching anatomy, biology, and physiology courses and includes standard academic qualifications. The job posting explicitly states that while all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, the university is "particularly interested in applications from members of historically disadvantaged and marginalized groups" which it defines as "First Nations, Métis and Inuit peoples, Indigenous peoples of North America, Black-identified persons, other racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and those who identify as women and/or 2SLGBTQ+." The university justifies this approach by stating its belief that "diversity of knowledge, worldviews and experiences that come from membership in different groups, is fundamental to innovation, teaching and scholarship" and that students are "best served by faculty who reflect the diversity of TMU's student body and the community in which TMU is situated." The institution frames this hiring preference as necessary "to ensure representation of the excellent talent in Canada" while categorizing applicants based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them solely on their individual qualifications, teaching ability, and scholarly merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated by the institution based on their membership in demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching excellence, and scholarly merit. This approach communicates that certain applicants receive preferential consideration based on immutable characteristics or identity categories, undermining the principle that individuals should be assessed on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. Candidates who do not belong to the specified groups learn that their applications are of less interest to the institution regardless of their individual qualities or potential contributions. The requirement to submit an equity, diversity and inclusion statement creates pressure on all applicants to demonstrate alignment with the institution's categorical framework for understanding people. The practice normalizes sorting individuals into demographic groups and evaluating them through that lens, rather than recognizing each person's unique background, perspective, and capabilities. This approach reduces applicants to representatives of demographic categories rather than treating them as distinct individuals, and it signals that institutional belonging and advancement depend partly on possession of particular immutable characteristics rather than solely on merit and individual contribution.

quotes.text: To ensure representation of the excellent talent in Canada, we are particularly interested in applications from members of historically disadvantaged and marginalized groups including: First Nations, Métis and Inuit peoples, Indigenous peoples of North America, Black-identified persons, other racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and those who identify as women and/or 2SLGBTQ+.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly declares the institution's particular interest in applications from individuals based on their membership in specified demographic categories, establishing a hiring preference based on immutable characteristics and identity categories rather than solely individual merit.

incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated based on their demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, teaching abilities, and character. This approach signals that institutions consider group membership a relevant credential for academic positions, undermining the principle that people deserve recognition for their unique qualities and demonstrated capabilities. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic groups learn that their applications are of lesser institutional interest regardless of their individual merits, sending a message that their singular worth as scholars and educators is diminished by their demographic characteristics. The practice normalizes categorizing people by immutable traits in employment decisions, treating demographic proxies as indicators of perspective or capability rather than evaluating each person's actual knowledge, experience, and teaching philosophy. Students receive instruction from faculty selected partly on demographic grounds rather than purely on pedagogical excellence and subject matter expertise, potentially compromising the quality of education. The institutional approach entrenches the idea that people should be understood primarily through group identities rather than as distinct individuals, undermining shared civic belonging in academic communities and dividing potential colleagues along demographic lines before they even join the institution.

quotes.rationale: This is the core statement expressing particular interest in candidates based on demographic categories rather than evaluating all applicants equally on merit. It explicitly lists the demographic groups receiving preferential consideration in the application process.

incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University restricted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Chemistry and Biology to candidates who self-identify as Toronto Metropolitan University restricted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Chemistry and Biology to candidates who self-identify as Indigenous peoples, excluding all other qualified applicants based on ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: Toronto Metropolitan University's Department of Chemistry and Biology advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position with an application deadline of April 30, 2026. The position explicitly restricted eligibility to candidates who self-identify as First Nations, Métis, Inuit peoples, or Indigenous peoples of North America. The job posting stated that the position is restricted to candidates who self-identify in the Applicant Diversity Self-ID questionnaire and who subsequently complete an affirmation process at TMU involving providing documentation to support claims of Indigenous identity and participating in discussions with the University's Indigenous Human Resources Lead about their lived experience. The posting emphasized research methodologies and epistemologies specific to Indigenous communities, including Two-Eyed Seeing frameworks, Indigenous data sovereignty principles, and engagement with Indigenous communities. While the posting stated that all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, it explicitly prioritized applications from members of historically disadvantaged and marginalized groups. The institution made employment eligibility contingent on ethnicity rather than evaluating candidates based on their individual qualifications, research excellence, teaching ability, and commitment to the academic mission.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from employment consideration based on their ethnicity rather than being evaluated as individuals based on their character, abilities, research accomplishments, and teaching excellence. The hiring restriction treats ethnicity as a credential for employment, reducing candidates to demographic categories rather than recognizing their unique qualities and individual merit. This approach normalizes institutional practices that grant or deny opportunities based on immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that people should be evaluated based on what they can contribute as individuals. The restriction prevents the institution from considering the full pool of qualified scholars who could advance knowledge in chemistry and biology, regardless of their background. Candidates who do not fit the specified ethnic categories learn that their qualifications, dedication to research, and commitment to teaching are insufficient because institutions evaluate them first through demographic lenses. The practice reinforces division within academic communities by signaling that ethnicity determines who belongs in certain roles, rather than building common understanding around shared academic values and the pursuit of knowledge that benefits all people.

incidents.short_description: McMaster University's Faculty of Health Sciences posted a job advertisement for obstetricians/gynecologists that explicitly states preference for candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: McMaster University's Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology posted a job advertisement on March 30, 2026, seeking two full-time obstetrician/gynecologists for West Lincoln Memorial Hospital. The advertisement included an employment equity statement that explicitly welcomed applications based on demographic categories rather than solely on qualifications and merit. The statement specifically indicated that the university 'especially welcomes applications from indigenous (First Nations, Métis or Inuit) peoples, members of racialized communities, persons with disabilities, women, and persons who identify as 2SLGBTQ+.' This represents an institutional policy of evaluating candidates based on immutable characteristics and demographic categories. The job posting required applicants to submit a statement describing contributions made or planned in relation to advancing equity, diversity and inclusion, making demographic considerations a formal component of the evaluation process. The advertisement also invited applicants to complete a confidential Applicant Diversity Survey requesting voluntary self-identification in relation to demographic groups, institutionalizing the collection and use of demographic data in hiring decisions.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified physicians are evaluated for employment based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status rather than solely on their medical qualifications, clinical skills, teaching ability, and individual merit. This approach signals that the institution categorizes candidates by demographic proxies rather than assessing them as distinct individuals with unique talents and abilities. Applicants learn that their immutable characteristics are institutional considerations in hiring decisions, undermining recognition of their singular worth as professionals. The practice normalizes sorting people into demographic categories and making employment decisions based on group membership rather than individual character and demonstrated competence. Physicians who do not fall into the preferred demographic categories face institutional disadvantage in the hiring process, being evaluated differently based on characteristics unrelated to their ability to provide excellent patient care or teach medical trainees. Patients are ultimately served by a system that considers demographic composition rather than simply selecting the most qualified physicians, potentially affecting the quality of medical care. The institutional approach entrenches division among colleagues by emphasizing demographic differences rather than fostering a shared professional identity based on common commitment to patient care and medical excellence.

incidents.short_description: Laurentian University advertised a tenure-track faculty position stating that applications are encouraged from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates as individuals based on their qualifications and merit.

The job posting stated that the university is committed to employment equity and explicitly encouraged applications from specific demographic categories: women, people based on their skin colour and ethnicity, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and persons of all sexual orientations and gender identities/expressions. The posting described these groups as 'equity-seeking communities.' The university framed this approach as part of being an 'inclusive and welcoming community,' positioning the encouragement of applications based on immutable characteristics as an institutional value. While the posting included standard language about Canadian immigration priority requirements, the equity statement went beyond legal requirements to actively encourage applications based on demographic categories rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications, expertise, and merit for the academic position.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are signaled that the institution evaluates people through the lens of demographic categories rather than solely as individuals with unique qualifications and abilities. Qualified candidates who do not fit the specified demographic categories receive a message that they are less encouraged to apply, undermining the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated expertise. The framing teaches that demographic group membership is a relevant consideration in professional advancement, rather than reinforcing that each person deserves recognition for their individual qualities and accomplishments. This undermines a merit-based system where candidates are assessed on their research contributions, teaching abilities, and potential to contribute to the academic community regardless of their immutable characteristics.

incidents.short_description: A Globe and Mail business article repeatedly identified the ethnicity of business participants in a casino acquisition story without explaining the relevance of these demographic characteristics to the commercial transaction.
incidents.description_of_event: The Globe and Mail published a business article on March 30, 2026, reporting on Pure Casino Entertainment LP's bid to acquire Gamehost Inc. for $282-million. Throughout the article, the journalist repeatedly identified participants by ethnicity, describing Pure Casino as 'indigenous-owned' in the headline and opening paragraph, and later specifying that 'Five Nova Scotia-based First Nations own Pure' with detailed naming of each nation. The article framed this commercial real estate and gaming transaction through an ethnic lens without providing explanation for why the ownership structure's ethnic composition was relevant to the business deal, the financing arrangements, the regulatory environment, or any other aspect of the transaction. The story covered standard corporate acquisition details including price, premium, financing, property sales, and executive statements, but chose to foreground and emphasize the ethnicity of certain participants while treating this demographic categorization as self-evidently newsworthy without journalistic justification.
incidents.expected_harm: Readers are presented with a framework where people's ethnicity is treated as a relevant categorization for business activity without explanation of its materiality to the transaction. This approach normalizes the practice of evaluating and describing people through demographic proxies rather than their individual capabilities, business track records, or the merits of their commercial activities. The framing suggests that ethnicity is inherently newsworthy in business contexts, potentially reducing individuals to representatives of demographic categories rather than autonomous actors pursuing commercial opportunities. This journalistic practice reinforces patterns where institutions and media organize information around immutable characteristics, treating people's ethnic backgrounds as primary identifiers rather than focusing on their individual achievements, business acumen, or the substantive details of their commercial endeavours.

quotes.text: Indigenous-owned Pure Casino bids for Gamehost with backing from U.S. real estate giant
quotes.rationale: The headline immediately frames the story through an ethnic lens, making the ownership's ethnicity the lead identifying characteristic rather than the business's track record, market position, or other relevant commercial factors.

incidents.short_description: An Ontario Superior Court judge reduced a kidnapping sentence after considering the defendant's skin colour and experiences, drawing a connection between his demographic background and his degree of responsibility for the crime.
incidents.description_of_event: Justice Sandra Nishikawa of the Ontario Superior Court of Justice sentenced Samir Abdelgadir to 9.5 years in prison after crediting time served for kidnapping a 14-year-old boy in March 2020. The kidnapping involved snatching the victim as he left for school, holding him bound and blindfolded for 36 hours to leverage the return of 90 kilograms of stolen cocaine, then releasing him in a wooded area without proper clothing in cold weather. The victim suffered immeasurable harm, becoming withdrawn and reliving the trauma when trying to sleep. While the judge determined the appropriate sentencing range was 11 to 13 years for this serious offence, she applied mitigation based on an Impact of Race and Cultural Assessment commissioned by the defendant. The judge explicitly stated she drew some connection between Abdelgadir's life experience and his commission of the offence such that it mitigated somewhat his degree of responsibility. The court considered the defendant's skin colour and experiences as factors that played some role in limiting his opportunities and contributing to the circumstances leading to the criminal conduct. This approach evaluated the defendant through the lens of demographic categories and life experiences attributed to those categories, rather than solely on his individual actions, character, and choices. The defendant, who had family support including a diplomat father who helped purchase his first condo and a brother who provided financial support for a house and business ventures, denied involvement in the kidnapping and showed no acceptance of responsibility for the harm caused to the victim.
incidents.expected_harm: Individuals who commit serious crimes are evaluated and sentenced based on their demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual actions, choices, and character. This approach normalizes institutional categorization of people by immutable traits, treating demographic background as a mitigating factor in criminal responsibility. The practice undermines the principle that each person possesses individual agency and should be held accountable for their own decisions and conduct regardless of their skin colour or ethnicity. Victims of crime receive unequal justice depending on the demographic characteristics of their perpetrators, as sentences vary based on categories rather than consistent application of law to individual circumstances. The institutional framework reinforces the message that people should be understood primarily through demographic lenses rather than as distinct individuals making their own choices. This approach to sentencing fails to recognize the singular worth of both defendants and victims as individuals, instead sorting them into categories that are deemed relevant to assessing moral and legal responsibility. The practice erodes shared standards of justice and civic belonging by applying different frameworks of accountability based on demographic classifications rather than universal principles that apply equally to all individuals.

incidents.short_description: The University of Winnipeg posted a job advertisement for an Applied Computer Science instructor position that encouraged applicants to self-identify based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Winnipeg's Department of Applied Computer Science advertised a probationary instructor position on March 27, 2026. The job posting stated that the university is committed to equity, diversity and inclusion, and that it recognizes a diverse staff and faculty benefits the institution. The posting explicitly encouraged applicants from specific demographic categories to self-identify at the time of application. The categories listed were women, racialized persons, Indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, and 2SLGBTQ+ persons. The posting framed this demographic self-identification as confidential and connected it to the institution's commitment to what it described as academic and institutional excellence. The position required a Master's degree in Computer Science or related discipline, with teaching experience in areas including Databases, Software Engineering, Programming, and Data Structures and Algorithms considered an asset. The job posting represented an institutional practice of requesting applicants provide information about immutable characteristics or demographic categories as part of the application process, rather than evaluating candidates solely on their qualifications, experience, and individual merit for the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are prompted to categorize themselves by demographic labels rather than being evaluated solely as individuals based on their qualifications, experience, and merit. The practice signals that the institution considers demographic category membership relevant to hiring decisions, potentially influencing how candidates are assessed. This undermines the principle that people should be evaluated based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications rather than being reduced to demographic categories. The institutional messaging that connects demographic diversity to excellence suggests that individual worth and contribution to the academic community are linked to immutable characteristics rather than to individual qualities and capabilities. Job seekers may feel pressure to disclose personal information about immutable traits to improve their prospects, compromising their privacy and autonomy. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of individuals into demographic categories and perpetuates a system where demographic proxies substitute for individual assessment.

incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to identify themselves to the institution based on immutable characteristics and demographic categories rather than being evaluated solely as individuals based on their scientific expertise, research accomplishments, teaching abilities, and unique qualifications for the position. Candidates may feel pressure to disclose personal information about characteristics such as sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation to improve their candidacy, undermining their privacy and autonomy. The approach reinforces the idea that institutions should sort people into demographic groups and consider group membership in employment decisions, rather than focusing exclusively on individual capabilities and achievements. This creates a system where applicants understand that their demographic characteristics may influence their evaluation, rather than having confidence that they will be assessed purely on their scholarly record, research vision, and ability to advance the field of RNA biology and cancer research. incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board organized a racially segregated entrepreneurship program restricted to students based on their skin colour.
incidents.description_of_event: The Peel District School Board, in partnership with Toronto Metropolitan University's Diversity Institute, organized the ADaPT for Black Youth Entrepreneurship Badging Program for students in grades 10 to 12. The program ran during March Break 2026 and culminated in a pitch competition and award ceremony held at the Centre of Black Excellence on March 26, 2026. The program explicitly restricted participation to students based on their skin colour, as indicated by the designation for students who identify based on this demographic category. The six-day entrepreneurship program focused on building skills in business planning, networking, marketing, financial literacy, and digital innovation. Students from 14 schools developed and presented business ideas to judges, peers, families, and staff. The school board framed this racially segregated programming as empowering youth with real-world skills, confidence, and pathways to future careers and entrepreneurship, rather than offering such opportunities based on individual interest, merit, or demonstrated need.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are granted or denied access to valuable educational opportunities based on their skin colour rather than their individual interests, abilities, or aspirations in entrepreneurship. This segregation normalizes the institutional practice of sorting students into demographic categories and evaluating their eligibility for programs based on immutable characteristics rather than merit or need. Students interested in entrepreneurship who do not fit the designated demographic category are excluded from accessing skills training, mentorship, networking opportunities, and pathways to future careers, solely because of their skin colour. The practice undermines shared civic belonging within the school community by dividing students based on demographic proxies rather than bringing together all students with common interests in business and innovation. Students learn that institutions view them primarily through demographic lenses rather than as distinct individuals with unique talents and goals. The segregation forecloses opportunities for students to build understanding and connections across perceived differences while pursuing shared entrepreneurial aspirations, reinforcing division rather than fostering a common educational community where all students can develop their potential together.

quotes.text: Black-identifying students (Grades 10–12) from 14 schools pitched business ideas developed during a 6-day March Break entrepreneurship program
quotes.rationale: Explicitly states the racial restriction on program participation, limiting access to students based on their skin colour. This is the core evidence of discriminatory conduct and segregation.

incidents.short_description: Canada's procurement watchdog found that the federal government failed to enforce its own rules regarding contracts allocated based on indigenous ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Office of the Procurement Ombud released findings indicating that the Government of Canada failed to enforce its own contracting rules related to indigenous procurement. The watchdog identified enforcement failures in the government's system for allocating contracts based on ethnicity. The report documented what the ombud characterized as a cascading failure in the administration and oversight of these contracting policies. The finding represents an institutional acknowledgment that government procurement processes categorized and evaluated businesses based on the ethnicity of their ownership rather than merit-based criteria alone, and that even the government's own framework for managing this system was not properly implemented.
incidents.expected_harm: Businesses are evaluated and granted access to government contracts based on the ethnicity of their owners rather than solely on merit, capability, or demonstrated need. This approach entrenches the practice of sorting organizations by demographic categories, normalizing institutional decision-making that treats ethnicity as a relevant factor in competitive procurement rather than evaluating each business as a distinct entity with unique qualifications. The failure to enforce even the government's own rules governing this system compounds the harm by creating arbitrary and inconsistent application of demographic preferences, undermining any claim that such categorization serves a legitimate administrative purpose. Business owners face a procurement environment where their ethnicity becomes a factor in institutional evaluation rather than their individual capabilities, experience, and competitive offerings. The system sends a message that demographic characteristics are appropriate bases for government decisions about resource allocation, contradicting principles of individual worth and merit-based evaluation. Citizens see their government operating procurement systems that divide and categorize participants by ethnicity, undermining shared civic belonging and equal treatment under government programs.

quotes.text: Ottawa failed to enforce its own Indigenous contracting rules, procurement watchdog finds
quotes.rationale: This headline establishes that the federal government had rules governing contracts allocated based on indigenous ethnicity and that enforcement of these rules failed, as documented by an official watchdog.

The policy normalizes reducing people to demographic proxies rather than recognizing their singular worth as individuals, and creates divisions among potential faculty members based on immutable traits rather than fostering shared academic community and common purpose.

quotes.text: The University of New Brunswick is committed to employment equity and fostering diversity within our community and developing an inclusive workplace that reflects the richness of the broader community that we serve. The University welcomes and encourages applications from all qualified individuals who will help us achieve our goals, including women, visible minorities, Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression. Preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly states the university's policy of giving hiring preference based on demographic categories including skin colour (visible minorities), ethnicity (Aboriginal persons), sex (women), disability, and sexual orientation/gender identity, demonstrating institutional categorization of candidates by demographic proxies rather than individual merit.

incidents.short_description: Université de Montréal posted a job advertisement for a Canada Impact+ Research Chair position that encourages applications based on demographic categories including sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation rather than solely on individual qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 26, 2026, Université de Montréal advertised a Full Professor position for a Canada Impact+ Research Chair in Physics through the Faculty of Arts and Science. The position posting included a section titled 'Equity, Diversity and Inclusion' that stated the university 'encourages all qualified individuals, regardless of their characteristics, to apply' but then specifically invited applications from women, indigenous peoples, members of visible or ethnic minorities, persons with disabilities, and members of the 2SLGBTQI+ communities. The posting directed applicants from these demographic categories to complete a self-identification form, describing this process as directly supporting employment equity objectives and helping strengthen representation of members of these groups within the university community. The advertisement stated that completing the self-identification questionnaire is 'strongly encouraged' and that the information would be used to advance employment equity, recognize expertise of members of designated groups, and enhance their representation within the university. This approach evaluates and categorizes candidates based on demographic proxies rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications, research excellence, teaching ability, and demonstrated merit for the prestigious research chair position.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to be evaluated and considered based on demographic categories rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research achievements, teaching capabilities, and merit. This practice normalizes the reduction of individuals to demographic labels in academic hiring, undermining the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated excellence. Qualified candidates who do not fall into the specified demographic categories receive the message that their applications are less valued by the institution, creating a two-tiered evaluation system where advancement may reflect demographic category membership rather than pure merit. The practice also pressures individuals to disclose personal information about immutable characteristics and to self-categorize into demographic groups as a condition of full consideration for employment. This segregation of applicants into demographic categories erodes shared civic belonging within the academic community and prevents the institution from building a culture where colleagues are recognized primarily for their individual contributions to knowledge, research excellence, and teaching effectiveness rather than their demographic profiles.

quotes.text: This process directly supports employment equity objectives and helps strengthen the representation of members of these groups within our community.
quotes.rationale: This reveals the institutional objective of using demographic category membership as a factor in hiring decisions to achieve representation targets, rather than evaluating candidates purely as individuals based on merit.

quotes.text: Completing the self-identification questionnaire is strongly encouraged when submitting your online application.
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates institutional pressure on applicants to disclose and categorize themselves by demographic characteristics as part of the application process.

incidents.short_description: Ontario Tech University posted job advertisements for three tenure-track Assistant Professor positions in Industrial Engineering that explicitly encouraged applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: Ontario Tech University advertised three tenure-track Assistant Professor positions in Industrial Engineering on March 25, 2026, with an application review date of April 24, 2026. The job posting stated that while all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, the university especially encourages applications from individuals based on specific demographic categories: First Nations, Métis, Inuit peoples, Indigenous peoples of North America, racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and those who identify as women and/or 2SLGBTQ+. The posting framed this preference within the university's commitment to equity, diversity, inclusion, indigenization and decolonization. The positions required candidates to hold a PhD in Industrial Engineering or related field, demonstrate excellence in research and teaching, and be registered or eligible to register as a Professional Engineer in Ontario. Expected start dates were July 2026 for two positions and January 2027 for one position. This represented an institutional decision to evaluate and encourage applicants based on demographic proxies rather than solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and merit for the academic positions.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are encouraged to apply and may be evaluated based on demographic categories rather than being assessed purely as individuals on their unique qualifications, research potential, and teaching abilities. This creates a system where demographic characteristics become relevant factors in academic hiring rather than focusing exclusively on individual merit, character, and demonstrated capabilities. The practice signals that the institution categorizes people by immutable traits and group identities, undermining the principle that each person possesses inherent dignity as an individual deserving recognition for their unique qualities. It normalizes the idea that advancement in academic careers should reflect demographic representation rather than individual achievement and ability. This approach may discourage applicants who do not fit preferred categories from applying, and it creates uncertainty about whether hiring decisions reflect genuine assessment of individual merit or adherence to demographic preferences. The policy fosters division by emphasizing differences based on immutable characteristics rather than building shared civic belonging through recognition of common humanity and individual worth.

quotes.text: Ontario Tech University is actively committed to equity, diversity, inclusion, indigenization and decolonization (EDIID), and welcomes applications from all qualified candidates, while especially encouraging applications from First Nations, Metis, Inuit peoples, Indigenous peoples of North America, racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and those who identify as women and/or 2SLGBTQ+.
quotes.rationale: This quote directly establishes that the university is explicitly encouraging applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status, rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals.

This hiring preference categorizes candidates based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their qualifications, teaching excellence, and professional credentials. incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary's Department of Psychology posted a teaching position with a stated preference for members of designated demographic groups including based on skin colour, sex, and sexual orientation.

incidents.short_description: Queen's University posted a librarian position stating it encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation.

incidents.short_description: The University of Winnipeg posted a job opening for an Instructor position that explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals.

incidents.short_description: McMaster University's Centre for Global Peace, Justice and Health hosted a symposium on March 25, 2026, promoting "decolonial health justice" frameworks that categorize health interventions and academic research through colonial versus decolonial lenses based on demographic considerations.
incidents.description_of_event: McMaster University's Centre for Global Peace, Justice and Health organized a day-long symposium titled "Decolonial Health Justice" on March 25, 2026. The event featured research panels and a roundtable explicitly framed around decolonial theory applied to health, medicine, and humanitarian work. Panel presentations included "What's Colonial about Global Health?" and "Accessing Indigenous Health Archives: Colonialism in an Era of Reconciliation," suggesting that health research and practice are evaluated through a framework that categorizes approaches based on their relationship to colonial history and identity categories. A roundtable titled "Who Thrives in Canada?" further framed health outcomes through demographic group lenses. The symposium concluded with an "Arts-based Health Advocacy Workshop," indicating institutional support for activism organized around these frameworks.
incidents.expected_harm: The symposium's framework will encourage evaluating health research, medical practice, and humanitarian interventions not primarily on evidence-based effectiveness or individual patient outcomes, but on their alignment with decolonial ideology and demographic categorizations. This approach may discourage rigorous scientific inquiry when findings conflict with ideological expectations, potentially compromising the quality of health research and patient care. Students and faculty may feel pressured to frame their work through approved ideological lenses rather than pursuing questions based on scientific merit and individual patient needs. The framing of health outcomes by demographic group rather than individual circumstances undermines the principle that each patient deserves evaluation based on their unique medical situation. The institutional endorsement of advocacy workshops signals that the university expects researchers to adopt particular political stances rather than maintaining scholarly independence and focusing on evidence-based approaches to improving health outcomes for all individuals.

quotes.text: Vanessa Watts, Indigenous Studies and Amber Dean, English & Cultural Studies, "Accessing Indigenous Health Archives: Colonialism in an Era of Reconciliation" McMaster University
quotes.rationale: This presentation frames health archives and research through identity categories and colonial frameworks rather than focusing on individual patient care and evidence-based health outcomes.

incidents.short_description: Dalhousie University posted a job opening for a biochemistry instructor position stating that preference in hiring will be given to candidates based on their skin colour, ancestry, sex, sexual orientation, and disability status rather than evaluating all applicants equally on their individual qualifications and merit.

The job posting stated that while all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply, the university's Employment Equity Policy provides that preference will be given in hiring processes to candidates who self-identify as members of designated groups based on their skin colour, ancestry, sex, sexual orientation, or disability status. The posting specifically listed Indigenous Peoples, persons of black/African descent, other racialized groups, persons with disabilities, women, and persons identifying as 2SLGBTQIA+ community members as groups that would receive hiring preference. The position involves teaching undergraduate biochemistry courses including lectures and laboratory sections, and reports to the Head of the Department of Biochemistry & Molecular Biology.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring preference reduces applicants to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their unique qualifications, teaching abilities, and potential contributions to the department. Candidates will be advantaged or disadvantaged in the hiring process based on immutable characteristics rather than their individual merit, experience, and demonstrated capabilities. This approach undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and achievements. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the designated demographic groups will face discrimination in the hiring process regardless of their individual qualifications or circumstances. The policy creates a system where demographic characteristics become a determining factor in employment decisions, treating people as representatives of categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique skills and perspectives.

incidents.short_description: Nipissing University posted a job opening for Canada Impact+ Research Chairs that encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, treating demographic categories as qualifications rather than evaluating candidates as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Department of Immunology posted a job opening for an Assistant Professor position that explicitly encourages applications based on applicants' skin colour, ancestry, sex, and other immutable characteristics rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The University of New Brunswick Faculty of Education posted a tenure-track assistant professor position that includes a stated hiring preference for candidates based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all applicants equally as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 23, 2026, the University of New Brunswick Faculty of Education advertised a tenure-track assistant professor position in Teacher Education for the School Years program. The job posting requires experience working with diverse populations and specifically lists demographic groups by naming them. The posting concludes with an equity statement indicating that while the university welcomes applications from all qualified individuals, preference will be given to certain applicants based on their membership in designated demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation. This approach to hiring prioritizes demographic characteristics over the individual evaluation of each candidate's unique qualifications, teaching philosophy, research contributions, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring preference reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their distinct qualifications, experiences, and capabilities. Applicants who do not belong to the preferred demographic groups will face disadvantage in the hiring process regardless of their individual merit, teaching excellence, or research contributions. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should assess people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. It creates a system where demographic status becomes a factor in career advancement rather than individual accomplishment. This approach also risks fostering resentment and division within the academic community by signaling that some individuals receive preferential consideration based on immutable characteristics rather than their unique contributions to education and scholarship.

quotes.text: The University of New Brunswick is committed to employment equity and fostering diversity within our community and developing an inclusive workplace that reflects the richness of the broader community that we serve. The University welcomes and encourages applications from all qualified individuals who will help us achieve our goals, including women, visible minorities, Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, persons of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression. Preference will be given to Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly indicates that hiring preference will be given based on demographic categories including sex, skin colour, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals based on their unique qualifications.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Safety Canada
incidents.short_description: Public Safety Canada announced $4.7 million in funding for a reintegration program exclusively for indigenous women, restricting eligibility based on ethnicity and sex rather than evaluating individuals based on their circumstances and needs.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 20, 2026, Public Safety Canada announced $4,737,448 over five years to the Thunder Women Healing Lodge Society for the Maashkaawizii Kwe project through the Aboriginal Community Safety Development Contribution Program. The program provides reintegration supports including accommodation, counselling, and skills workshops exclusively for indigenous women leaving federal and provincial corrections. The funding restricts program eligibility based on participants' ethnicity and sex, rather than assessing individuals based on their demonstrated reintegration needs, personal circumstances, or risk factors. The announcement describes the program as serving "First Nations (Status and Non-Status), Inuit, and Métis 2SLGBTQIA+ women and their families," explicitly limiting access by demographic categories. Ministers justified the restriction by citing "overrepresentation of Indigenous Peoples in the criminal justice system" as rationale for category-based program design.
incidents.expected_harm: This program design reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating their unique reintegration needs, circumstances, and potential. Women who are not indigenous but face identical challenges upon release from corrections are categorically excluded regardless of their individual situation or need for support. The approach undermines the principle that institutions should assess people based on their character, demonstrated needs, and individual circumstances. By designing public safety programs around demographic membership rather than individual assessment, the government establishes a framework where access to taxpayer-funded reintegration resources depends on ethnicity and sex rather than on each person's specific challenges, risks, and capacity to benefit from support. This creates a two-tier system where identical needs receive different responses based on immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: announced $4,737,448.00 over five years in federal support to the Maashkaawizii Kwe "Woman of Inner strength" project, led by Thunder Women Healing Lodge Society (the Healing Lodge) through the Aboriginal Community Safety Development Contribution Program (ACSDCP). This funding will help the Healing Lodge provide comprehensive supports for Indigenous women reintegrating into our communities from federal and provincial corrections.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that federal funding is directed to a program specifically for indigenous women, demonstrating restriction based on ethnicity and sex.

incidents.short_description: Legal Aid Ontario published its 2026 Racialized Communities Strategy Action Plan, which includes eligibility criteria based on applicants' skin colour and ethnicity for certain programs, and commits to proportional representation targets based on demographic categories rather than individual need or qualification.
incidents.description_of_event: Legal Aid Ontario released an action plan update describing programs and policies that evaluate and categorize individuals based on their skin colour and ethnicity. The Criminal Senior Counsel Program explicitly includes an applicant's skin colour as a primary eligibility criterion for receiving legal services. The organization tracks and aims to ensure that people categorized by skin colour are proportionately represented among clients and staff, rather than assessing individuals based on their specific legal needs or qualifications. The plan describes collecting demographic data on skin colour and ethnicity to drive programming decisions, targets staff composition to match demographic proportions of low-income populations, and prioritizes hiring candidates based on experience supporting people grouped by skin colour categories. The approach frames legal service access and employment decisions through demographic classification rather than individual circumstances, legal needs, or merit-based evaluation.
incidents.expected_harm: Individuals will be evaluated for legal assistance and employment based on their demographic classification rather than their specific legal needs, qualifications, or individual circumstances. Applicants with identical legal situations and financial constraints will receive different treatment based on how the institutions  categorize their skin colour or ethnicity. The approach reduces people to demographic proxies rather than recognizing their distinct situations and individual merit. Legal professionals will be assessed and selected based on their experience working with demographic categories rather than their legal expertise and ability to serve individual clients effectively. The system creates differential access to justice based on how people are classified demographically, undermining equal treatment and the principle that legal representation should address individual legal circumstances regardless of immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: Data collection continues to be a priority at LAO. Collecting information about the race of our clients helps LAO identify and address potential barriers to accessing services that some communities may face.
quotes.rationale: Shows the organization's systematic approach to classifying and tracking clients by demographic categories rather than focusing on individual legal needs and circumstances.

incidents.short_description: Graffiti disparaging people based on their skin colour was spray painted multiple times on a fence in a Peterborough subdivision. Police are investigating the incident.
incidents.description_of_event: In Peterborough, Ontario, graffiti messages disparaging people based on their skin colour were spray painted nine times along a 300-metre fence in a residential subdivision. The incident was reported to police who opened an investigation. The graffiti represents an act of individual conduct that targeted people based on their skin colour, reducing individuals to a demographic category and promoting division along racial lines.
incidents.expected_harm: This incident will create fear and diminished sense of safety among residents who are targeted by such messages. It reinforces divisive categorization of people based on immutable characteristics rather than individual worth. The repeated nature of the graffiti across a significant distance amplifies the intimidation effect and signals hostility toward community members based on their skin colour. Such acts undermine the principle of singular worth by treating people as members of demographic groups rather than as individuals deserving of dignity and respect.

quotes.text: Police investigating after anti-Black racist graffiti sprayed on fence in Peterborough
quotes.rationale: Confirms the nature of the incident as graffiti targeting people based on skin colour and that police are investigating, establishing this as individual conduct requiring law enforcement response.

incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa posted a job opening for a replacement professor position that states the institution strongly encourages applications from people based on their skin colour, ancestry, sex, and disabilities.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Ottawa Department of Chemistry & Biomolecular Sciences advertised a replacement professor position with a stated preference for candidates based on immutable characteristics. The posting indicated the institution strongly encourages applications from indigenous peoples, people based on their skin colour, persons with disabilities, and women. The posting invited applicants who wish to be considered as members of these groups to complete a confidential self-identification questionnaire. While the posting states all qualified candidates are invited to apply, it explicitly frames certain demographic categories as preferred, reducing individuals to group membership rather than evaluating them based on their unique qualifications, teaching abilities, and individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring approach will harm individuals who are evaluated based on demographic categories rather than their individual qualities, teaching excellence, and academic contributions. Candidates will be pressured to identify themselves by immutable traits rather than being assessed solely on their demonstrated capabilities in teaching chemistry and biomolecular sciences. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. It creates a system where advancement reflects demographic classification rather than merit, and where individuals are reduced to representatives of demographic groups instead of being recognized for their unique perspectives and individual experiences.

quotes.text: We strongly encourage applications from Indigenous Peoples in Canada, visible minority members (racialized persons), persons with disabilities, women and all qualified applicants with the skills and knowledge to productively engage with equitable, diverse and inclusive communities.
quotes.rationale: This statement demonstrates the institution's explicit preference for applicants based on demographic categories including ancestry, skin colour, disabilities, and sex, rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals based on their teaching qualifications and academic merit.

incidents.short_description: Unifor issued a World Water Day statement highlighting water access disparities in Canada, specifically framing water shortages affecting First Nations communities through an ethnic lens while discussing corporate data centre water consumption.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 18, 2026, Unifor released a World Water Day statement addressing water resource challenges in Canada. The statement highlighted that 39 long-term drinking water advisories remain in effect across 37 First Nations communities, with some communities going without safe tap water for over 25 years. The organization characterized this situation as a national scandal requiring urgent government action. The statement also discussed corporate data centre water consumption by technology companies and called for regulatory measures. Throughout the communication, Unifor framed the water access issue by emphasizing the ethnic identity of affected communities rather than focusing on the universal human need for clean water or the individual circumstances of all Canadians facing water challenges.
incidents.expected_harm: By framing water access issues through an ethnic categorization lens, the statement reinforces the practice of dividing Canadians based on immutable characteristics rather than addressing water scarcity as a challenge affecting Canadian communities. This approach obscures the shared human need for clean water and reduces people to demographic categories. When institutions communicate about resource allocation and infrastructure challenges by emphasizing group identities rather than individual needs and circumstances, they undermine the principle that every person deserves recognition for their unique situation. This framing creates an environment where policy discussions prioritize demographic labels over the evaluation of actual individual needs, circumstances, and the universal human right to safe drinking water that applies equally to all Canadians regardless of background.

quotes.text: Right now, 39 long-term drinking water advisories remain in effect across 37 First Nations communities in Canada. Some of these communities have gone without safe tap water for more than 25 years.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates how the organization frames the water crisis through ethnic community categorization rather than as individuals facing water access challenges.

incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University advertised a tenure-track faculty position in social work restricted to candidates based on their ancestry, requiring applicants to be First Nations, Métis, or Inuit and complete an Indigenous identity verification process.
incidents.description_of_event: Wilfrid Laurier University's Lyle S. Hallman Faculty of Social Work posted a job advertisement for an Assistant or Associate Professor position with a July 1, 2026 start date. The position was explicitly restricted to candidates based on their ancestry: applicants were required to be First Nations, Métis, or Inuit and to complete the university's Indigenous identity verification process. The posting stated that candidates must be strongly rooted in their Indigenous identity, culture, and practice, including ongoing lived relationships with Indigenous knowledge systems, teachings, community, ceremony, and cultural responsibilities. The university indicated it would confirm Indigenous identity as part of the recruitment process through procedures developed by the Indigenous Initiatives Office, citing concerns about Indigenous identity fraud or theft. The position involved teaching in the Master of Social Work Indigenous Field of Study program, which the posting described as having an entirely Indigenous faculty. The advertisement was posted on March 18, 2026, with an application deadline of April 8, 2026.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction evaluates candidates based on their ancestry rather than their individual qualifications, lived experiences, perspectives, and demonstrated capabilities. By categorically excluding individuals who do not meet ancestry-based criteria, the restriction denies employment opportunities to qualified social work scholars who may have deep knowledge of Indigenous communities, lived experiences working with these communities, and demonstrated commitment to Indigenous wholistic practice and research methodologies. The policy reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than assessing their unique contributions, expertise, and capacity to teach and conduct research in ways that serve Indigenous communities. This approach conflicts with merit-based evaluation and prevents the institution from considering the full range of candidates who could effectively contribute to the program's educational and research mission. The requirement for identity verification creates a system where individuals must prove their ancestry to access employment, fundamentally treating people as members of groups rather than as distinct individuals with varied qualifications and experiences.

quotes.text: Successful candidates must Be Indigenous and complete Laurier's Indigenous identify verification process for consideration.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly requires candidates to be Indigenous and undergo identity verification, demonstrating the hiring restriction based on ancestry and the verification mechanism.

incidents.short_description: Brock University posted a faculty position that encouraged applications from people based on their skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, sex, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates as individuals based on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Brock University posted a faculty position on March 17, 2026, for a Canada Impact+ Emerging Leader in Sustainable Systems Engineering for Digital Agriculture. The posting stated that Indigenous peoples, members of racialized groups (including those who identify based on skin colour), people with disabilities, women, and Two-Spirit, lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and queer (2SLGBTQ+) persons are encouraged to apply and to voluntarily self-identify as members of one or more designated groups as part of their application. The posting indicated this information would be securely accessed to fulfill the hiring initiative and ensure accurate data regarding Brock's Research Chairs program. The university framed this approach as consistent with Section 14 of the Ontario Human Rights Code and its Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Action Plan. Rather than evaluating all applicants solely on their individual qualifications, research excellence, teaching ability, and potential contributions to the department, the institution organized its hiring process around demographic categories. The posting required applicants to demonstrate qualifications including a PhD in engineering, professional engineering licensure, research excellence in sustainable systems engineering, and teaching experience, yet simultaneously encouraged applications based on immutable characteristics unrelated to these professional qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are evaluated and sorted by institutions based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, sex, and sexual orientation rather than being assessed solely as individuals on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. This practice signals that demographic categories matter in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect merit and individual achievement. Applicants learn that institutions view them through the lens of group membership rather than their unique professional accomplishments, research contributions, and potential. The approach normalizes the institutional practice of categorizing people by immutable characteristics, encouraging applicants to present themselves as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals with singular worth. Candidates who do not fit into encouraged categories receive a message that their applications may be valued differently based on characteristics unrelated to their professional qualifications or research excellence. The practice entrenches the idea that demographic proxies should influence professional opportunities, rather than fostering a system where each person's advancement reflects their individual abilities, achievements, and contributions to their field. By making group membership relevant to hiring rather than irrelevant, the institution fails to build a community united by shared professional standards and common commitment to research excellence that transcends demographic divisions.

quotes.text: Consistent with Brock University's Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Action Plan, in recognition of and pursuant to Section 14 of the Ontario Human Rights Code, Indigenous peoples, members of racialized groups (including Black persons), people with disabilities, women, and Two-Spirit, lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and queer (2SLGBTQ+) persons are encouraged to apply and to voluntarily self-identify as a member of one or more designated groups as part of their application.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes that the university explicitly encourages applications based on demographic categories including skin colour, ethnicity, disability, sex, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals.

incidents.short_description: Interval House Hamilton received federal funding to deliver workplace safety training programs in skilled trades that are restricted based on sex, targeting male-dominated workplaces to make them safer specifically for individuals based on their sex.
incidents.description_of_event: The federal government through the Minister of Women and Gender Equality announced $95,674 in funding to Interval House Hamilton as part of a $2 million gender-based violence prevention initiative. This funding supports workplace training programs in skilled trades that explicitly focus on making these workplaces safer based on the sex of workers. The organization has partnerships with major unions including LIUNA, Canadian Union of Skilled Workers, and International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers to deliver training aimed at retaining individuals in skilled trades based on sex and gender identity rather than individual circumstances. The program evaluates workplace safety needs through the lens of demographic categories rather than assessing individual workers' needs, experiences, or vulnerabilities. The initiative frames workplace safety as contingent on workers' sex rather than addressing harassment, violence, and unsafe conditions as issues affecting all workers based on their individual experiences and circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will reduce workers to demographic categories rather than evaluating their individual safety needs, experiences of harassment, or workplace vulnerabilities. Workers who experience harassment or unsafe conditions but do not fall into the targeted demographic categories will find their individual circumstances overlooked in favour of categorical assumptions. The program reinforces the notion that workplace safety should be allocated based on immutable traits rather than individual need, undermining the principle that all workers deserve safe workplaces evaluated on their specific circumstances. By framing safety interventions around demographic identity, institutions will perpetuate systems that assume shared experiences based on sex rather than recognizing the distinct individual factors that make any particular worker vulnerable to harassment or unsafe conditions.

quotes.text: Interval House Hamilton will use almost $100K in new funding from the federal government to help make male-dominated skilled trades safer places for women to work.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the program explicitly targets workplaces based on the sex of workers, framing safety as a sex-based rather than individual concern.

incidents.short_description: The District of Squamish established a Land Back Task Force that will grant exclusive access to public sites based on ancestry and explore transferring public lands based on ethnic and racial heritage claims, rather than evaluating residents as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 17, 2026, the District of Squamish council unanimously approved terms of reference for a Land Back Task Force and appointed three councillors to serve alongside three Squamish Nation councillors. The task force will operate until 2028 with a mandate to develop a framework for land back initiatives. According to the terms of reference, potential initiatives include granting exclusive access to traditional cultural sites for people based on their indigenous ancestry, exploring repatriation of public lands based on ethnic heritage, co-managing parks, facilitating access to harvesting sites based on ancestry, and ensuring input into District leases based on ethnic affiliation. The task force focuses on publicly held lands and explicitly states it does not involve private property. Council members emphasized this represents reconciliation work to address historical wrongs. One councillor characterized those with concerns about the initiative as driven by fear and feelings of superiority. The District allocated $90,000 for 2026 and $15,000 annually thereafter for this work. The initiative follows a 2025 court ruling on Aboriginal title and a 2025 Memorandum of Understanding between the District and Squamish Nation.
incidents.expected_harm: This initiative will create a system where access to public lands and sites, use of public resources, and governance of public spaces is determined by a person's ancestry and ethnic heritage rather than their individual circumstances, needs, or citizenship status. Residents will be excluded from or granted preferential access to public areas based on their racial and ethnic background. This approach evaluates people as members of demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique qualities and needs. The exclusive access provisions mean some residents will be barred from public sites based on their ancestry. By embedding ancestry-based criteria into land management policy, the initiative institutionalizes the practice of sorting residents by ethnic heritage and treating them differently based on immutable traits. This framework undermines the principle that public institutions should serve all residents equally as individuals and that public lands belong to all citizens regardless of their ethnic background.

quotes.text: The Land Back Task Force exists to research, learn, and engage both District of Squamish residents and Sḵwx̱wú7mesh People on the concept of Land Back. Its primary goal is to develop a framework for Land Back in the District of Squamish, including policy considerations for future public land dispositions by the District and joint use of District lands.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the task force's mandate to create policies governing public land based on ancestry considerations rather than individual merit or need.

incidents.short_description: York University mandated faculty and staff update email signatures to include new land acknowledgements tied to a Memorandum of Understanding with the Mississaugas of the Credit First Nation, framing these acknowledgements as institutional requirements rather than individual expressions.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 17, 2026, York University's Faculty of Liberal Arts & Professional Studies instructed all faculty and staff to update their email signatures and event materials to incorporate new land acknowledgements. These acknowledgements were described as having been 'gifted' to the university through a Memorandum of Understanding with the Mississaugas of the Credit First Nation. The directive required standardized acknowledgements be used across all university communications, with templates organized by campus, faculty, and subbrand. The implementation was characterized as supporting the university's commitments to decolonization and strengthening relationships with indigenous communities. Faculty received explicit instructions to ensure the new land acknowledgement appears in email signatures, events, and meetings as part of a coordinated, university-wide transition.
incidents.expected_harm: Mandatory land acknowledgements in email signatures reduce individuals to institutional mouthpieces, compelling them to participate in political messaging regardless of their personal views or relationship to the subject matter. This requirement treats faculty and staff as demographic representatives rather than individuals, undermining their autonomy to determine how they express themselves professionally. The practice will erode institutional spaces for genuine intellectual discourse by replacing individual expression with prescribed institutional orthodoxy. By mandating uniform political statements, the policy creates an environment where conformity is prioritized over truthful engagement with complex historical and contemporary issues, preventing individuals from forming and expressing their own reasoned perspectives on reconciliation, land rights, and institutional relationships with indigenous communities.

quotes.text: We are asking all units within our Faculty to update their email signatures and to ensure that the new Land Acknowledgement is used at events and in meetings.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the mandatory nature of the requirement across all faculty communications and activities.

incidents.short_description: A Globe and Mail opinion piece advocates for systematic collection of race-based health data across Canada, arguing this is necessary to address racial disparities in health outcomes and correct systemic racism in healthcare.
incidents.description_of_event: Health columnist André Picard published an opinion piece in The Globe and Mail arguing that Canada should systematically collect race-based data in healthcare settings. The article notes that Prince Edward Island recently announced it will collect voluntary information about race and ethnicity during health card applications, joining Nova Scotia, Manitoba, Nunavut, and the Northwest Territories. Picard contends that Canada's lack of comprehensive race-based health data represents a deliberate state of ignorance that harms patients and practitioners. The piece frames the collection of such data as necessary to identify and address racial disparities in health outcomes, citing examples from the United States and limited Canadian research showing differences in wait times and outcomes. The author argues that technical barriers exist but are surmountable, pointing to COVID-19 data collection systems as proof of concept.
incidents.expected_harm: The advocacy for systematic race-based data collection in healthcare will institutionalize categorical thinking about Canadians based on demographic characteristics rather than treating them as individuals. By embedding racial and ethnic categorization into health systems, institutions will be encouraged to evaluate patients through the lens of group membership rather than individual circumstances, medical history, and unique health needs. This approach reduces people to demographic proxies and assumes that racial categories are meaningful predictors of health outcomes, when individual variation within any demographic group far exceeds differences between groups. The systematic collection and use of such data may lead healthcare providers to make assumptions about patients based on their categorized race or ethnicity rather than conducting thorough individual assessments. Furthermore, the framing of disparities primarily through a racial lens may obscure other individual factors such as specific genetic markers, lifestyle choices, economic circumstances, and personal medical history that are more directly relevant to individual patient care. The institutionalization of race-based thinking in healthcare systems will perpetuate rather than diminish the tendency to view patients as representatives of demographic categories rather than as unique individuals deserving personalized medical attention.

quotes.text: Collecting race-based data can help tease out these issues and, more importantly, spur action to correct them.
quotes.rationale: Core advocacy position that racial categorization should drive healthcare interventions and policies.

quotes.text: Race, culture, language and socio-economic status can all have a profound impact on health, individually and collectively.
quotes.rationale: Conflates demographic categories with individual factors, suggesting institutional approach will treat race as determinative of health outcomes.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Women and Gender Equality Canada
incidents.short_description: The federal government announced $2 million in funding for 18 organizations to deliver initiatives engaging men and boys in preventing gender-based violence, restricting program eligibility based on sex.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 16, 2026, Women and Gender Equality Canada announced close to $2 million in new funding for 18 organizations across Canada delivering initiatives specifically designed to engage men and boys in preventing gender-based violence. This builds on a previous investment of over $14.3 million for these projects.
