Hewitt declined to give a direct answer, instead citing a Federal Court settlement and Canadian Human Rights Commission mediation as the basis for the program's demographic targets, stating the program must "look" more like Canada by 2030.

The program has enforced strict demographic quotas, with the result that certain research chair postings at Canadian universities explicitly exclude applicants based on their sex or skin colour. For example, a physics chair posting at the University of New Brunswick had been restricted for over a year to exclude men of certain backgrounds, and a posting at Dalhousie University excluded applicants based on skin colour. As of the time of testimony, the program had already surpassed its quota for one demographic category — reaching 29 per cent against a 22 per cent target — yet the demographically restricted postings continued.

When asked directly whether the Tri-Council had undertaken any work to establish conditions under which these demographic hiring practices would be eliminated, Hewitt indicated there was no such plan, stating he wanted to reach the remaining targets first before having that conversation. The vice-president of research at the Université du Québec, Céline Poncelin de Raucourt, separately defended the restricted postings, arguing they were not exclusionary and that debating the practice would run "counter to excellence and merit."
incidents.expected_harm: Researchers are excluded from competing for prestigious, multi-year federal research grants based solely on their sex or skin colour. This means qualified individuals lose career-defining opportunities not because of their abilities, achievements, or demonstrated needs, but because of immutable personal characteristics. The program entrenches the institutional practice of evaluating people as members of demographic categories rather than as individuals. When quotas are exceeded and the exclusions continue regardless, it signals that demographic engineering — not individual merit — has become the permanent operating principle of the program. This undermines the integrity of federal research funding, erodes trust in academic institutions, and normalizes the idea that sorting people by demographic proxies is an acceptable, even laudable, basis for institutional decision-making.

quotes.text: Canadian universities, at the direction of the federal Liberal government and the research granting agencies for which it's responsible, have made race- and sex- based discrimination a matter of everyday business. And they don't seem to have a plan to pare it back.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core allegation: that demographic-based hiring restrictions in the Canada Research Chairs Program are systemic and ongoing, with no exit plan.

quotes.text: the current opening for a Canada Research Chair in physics (specifically, quantum sensing) at the University of New Brunswick, which has been vacant for one whole year, will not accept applications from white men. Similarly, white people can't apply to Dalhousie's opening for a chair in industrial engineering.
quotes.rationale: Provides concrete examples of postings that restrict applicants based on sex and skin colour, directly illustrating the discriminatory hiring restriction.

quotes.text: Those policies are contained in an addendum to the program and are set to ensure that by the year 2030, the Canada Research Chairs Program will 'look' more like Canada….
quotes.rationale: Hewitt's own words confirm the program is designed to achieve a demographic composition target rather than evaluate individuals on merit.

quotes.text: it surpassed its 2029 racial minority quota of 22 per cent (representation is now up to 29), but the racially exclusionary job ads persist.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that even after quotas are exceeded, the demographically restricted hiring practices continue — there is no mechanism to stop them.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
incidents.short_description: Treasury Board President Anita Anand stated her mandate includes dismantling systemic barriers in the federal public service and ensuring workforce composition reflects demographic categories. She described advocating for representation based on immutable characteristics at all levels of government briefings and decision-making.
incidents.description_of_event: In April 2024, Treasury Board President Anita Anand gave interviews describing her approach to federal public service management. She stated that part of her mandate is to dismantle systemic barriers and that she actively monitors the demographic composition of briefing rooms and leadership positions. Anand recounted an incident where she questioned why no employees matching a specific demographic category were present at a briefing about mental-health counselling for that same demographic group, calling the situation unacceptable. She stated her goal is to ensure visible demographic representation at the minister, deputy minister, and assistant deputy minister levels. Anand described this focus on categorizing and tracking employees by immutable characteristics as integral to her identity and approach. A panel of experts recommended mandatory training focused on demographic categories, and Anand indicated she would develop an action plan before summer 2024. The approach emphasizes evaluating workforce composition through the lens of demographic categories rather than individual capabilities and contributions.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will reduce individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their unique qualifications, experience, and individual merit. Federal employees will be assessed and valued based on immutable characteristics rather than their distinct capabilities and professional contributions. The institution's focus on demographic representation in briefing rooms and leadership positions signals that demographic category membership may influence professional opportunities and advancement. Canadians seeking public service careers will receive the message that their demographic category matters as much as or more than their individual talents and accomplishments. The mandatory training requirements centred on demographic categorization will reinforce rather than diminish the practice of sorting people by immutable traits. This institutional approach undermines the principle that each person possesses inherent dignity as an individual deserving recognition for their unique qualities, and it fails to evaluate people based on character, abilities, and demonstrated contributions.

quotes.text: Case in point: in February, Anand walked into a briefing regarding mental-health counselling for Black public-service workers. There were no Black employees in the room, she said. 'I said to the individuals briefing me: Why aren't there any Black individuals facing me?' This is not acceptable.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates Anand actively monitoring and evaluating employee presence based on demographic categories, expecting demographic representation to match the topic being discussed.

incidents.short_description: Wilfrid Laurier University restricted applications for a Canada Research Chair position to candidates who self-identify based on demographic categories, excluding all others from consideration regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Wilfrid Laurier University's Faculty of Education posted a job competition for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair (SSHRC) in Innovation and Inclusion in Education, dated April 26, 2024, with a closing date of April 29, 2024. The posting explicitly stated that the position is only open to applicants who identify as indigenous, racialized, or as having a disability. Applicants were required to confirm their eligibility as a member of at least one of these groups in their cover letter, and the successful candidate was required to confirm eligibility prior to receiving an offer. Indigenous candidates were required to pass a verification process, while candidates who are racialized or have disabilities were required to sign a declaration confirming eligibility. All other candidates, regardless of their scholarly record, research accomplishments, or teaching ability, were categorically excluded from competing for the position based solely on demographic self-identification. The position included appointment as a tenured or tenure-track faculty member at the Assistant or Associate Professor level.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified scholars are excluded from competing for a tenured academic position at a publicly funded university based on their demographic characteristics rather than their individual abilities, qualifications, or demonstrated needs. This institutional practice reduces candidates to demographic categories and denies individuals the opportunity to be evaluated on their character, scholarly record, and merit. It establishes a precedent in a publicly funded institution that demographic labels are legitimate gatekeeping criteria for prestigious academic appointments, normalizing the sorting of individuals by immutable traits. It also sends a message to students and the broader academic community that who a person is categorically matters more to the institution than what they are capable of contributing.

quotes.text: this position is only open to applicants who identify as Indigenous, racialized, or having a disability; applicants with this lived experience who also identify as women, gender minorities and other equity deserving groups are also encouraged to apply.
quotes.rationale: This is the core restriction in the posting, explicitly limiting who may apply based on demographic self-identification rather than qualifications alone.

quotes.text: identify as Indigenous, racialized, or having a disability
quotes.rationale: This appears under the formal Qualifications section, confirming that demographic self-identification is listed as a required qualification for the role.

quotes.text: As this position is open only to those who identify as Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, and racialized persons, candidates must confirm their eligibility as a member of at least one of these groups in the cover letter.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that demographic eligibility is mandatory and must be declared, and that the restriction is enforced rather than aspirational.

quotes.text: Prior to offer, the successful candidate will need to confirm their eligibility for this role. An Indigenous candidate will need to go through Laurier's Indigenous Verification Process; a candidate who is racialized and/or has disabilities will need to sign a declaration confirming eligibility.
quotes.rationale: Shows that the demographic restriction is enforced through a formal verification and declaration process, not merely encouraged.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Global Affairs Canada > Permanent Mission of Canada in Geneva, Switzerland
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Permanent Mission in Switzerland hosted a reception for the Permanent Forum on People of African Descent, with attendees identified by racial designation through the Black Diplomats Academy.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 25, 2024, the Canadian Permanent Mission in Switzerland hosted a reception for the Permanent Forum on People of African Descent. The event featured representation from the Black Diplomats Academy, an organization that categorizes diplomats based on skin colour. The reception was promoted through social media by the organization Developing Young Leaders of Tomorrow Today, which identified attendees through racial designation. The event took place in the context of Canada's diplomatic activities in Geneva and involved the participation of Minister Kamal Khera. The institutional communication promoted categorization of diplomatic representatives based on skin colour rather than individual qualifications, experience, or diplomatic expertise.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach to diplomatic representation reinforces the practice of defining individuals by skin colour rather than their individual capabilities and achievements. It establishes institutional precedent for organizing diplomatic activities around racial categories, which undermines the principle that individuals should be recognized for their unique qualifications and contributions. Canadian citizens and aspiring diplomats will encounter a system where demographic categorization takes precedence over individual merit in how diplomatic representation is structured and communicated. This creates an expectation that diplomatic identity and professional recognition should be filtered through racial designation rather than individual accomplishment and expertise.

quotes.text: We are honoured to represent the Black Diplomats Academy at the reception for the Permanent Forum on People of African Descent, hosted by the Canadian Permanent Mission in Switzerland!
quotes.rationale: This quote demonstrates that the Canadian government diplomatic mission hosted an event where attendees were identified and organized through racial categorization, specifically through an organization that designates diplomats by skin colour.

incidents.short_description: The Waterloo Region District School Board organized a racially segregated parent information night.
incidents.description_of_event: The Waterloo Region District School Board, in partnership with Rhythm and Blues Cambridge, organized a 'Black Parent Information Night' scheduled for April 30, 2024, at Galt Collegiate Institute and Vocational School in Cambridge. The event was designed to provide information about curriculum expectations, special education procedures, progressive discipline, and changes to reading instruction. The event explicitly restricted attendance to families and caregivers of students based on their skin colour, as stated in the promotional materials. The restriction was emphasized with a note that the event was only for families and caregivers of students who identify based on this demographic category. This represented an institutional decision to organize programming around demographic categories rather than individual needs or interests, and to exclude families from educational information based on their skin colour.
incidents.expected_harm: Parents and families are excluded from accessing educational information and school board engagement opportunities based on their skin colour, creating a system where institutions evaluate and grant access to people based on demographic categories rather than individual needs or interests. This segregation undermines shared civic belonging and common educational community, sending a message that families should be divided and separated based on immutable characteristics. The practice normalizes institutional categorization of individuals by demographic proxies rather than their unique circumstances, concerns, or their children's educational needs. It prevents families from building understanding across perceived differences and forecloses opportunities for parents to engage in shared discourse about common educational challenges affecting all children.

quotes.text: The Waterloo Region District School Board and Rhythm and Blues Cambridge invites families and caregivers of African, Caribbean and Black identifying (ACBi) students to Black Parent Information Night Cambridge.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the institutional organization of an event explicitly organized around demographic categories, identifying the organizing institution and the basis for attendance restriction.

quotes.text: PLEASE NOTE that this event is for families and caregivers of students who identify as Black.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly states the restriction of attendance based on demographic category, confirming the exclusionary nature of the event and the targeting basis.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Privy Council Office
incidents.short_description: The Government of Canada posted a job opportunity for Chairperson of the Canadian Museum of History stating that preference may be given to applicants from designated employment equity groups (indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women).
incidents.description_of_event: On or around April 24, 2024, the Privy Council Office posted an appointment opportunity for Chairperson of the Canadian Museum of History Board of Trustees. The posting includes standard qualifications related to education, board experience, executive leadership, and governance knowledge. In the section titled "Equity, Diversity and Inclusion," the posting states that candidates belonging to designated employment equity groups are encouraged to self-identify when applying, and that "preference may be given to applicants who are members of one or more of the following groups: Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women." This language indicates that applicants will be evaluated not solely on their individual qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated experience, but also on their membership in demographic categories. The posting frames this approach as part of building "a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves."
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions grant hiring preference based on demographic categories, they depart from evaluating candidates as individuals based on their unique qualities, character, and capabilities. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic groups will face reduced opportunities regardless of their individual merit, experience, or potential contributions. This approach reduces people to demographic proxies rather than recognizing their singular worth. It undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated abilities. The practice may also create perceptions that appointees from preferred groups were selected primarily for demographic reasons rather than their qualifications, which diminishes recognition of their individual achievements and expertise. Furthermore, by institutionalizing demographic considerations in selection processes, such policies normalize the practice of categorizing people by group membership rather than fostering genuine diversity of thought and experience that emerges from evaluating each person's unique perspectives and varied life experiences.

quotes.text: Candidates who belong to one or more of the designated employment equity groups are encouraged to self-identify when applying. Preference may be given to applicants who are members of one or more of the following groups: Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly states that hiring preference may be granted based on demographic category membership rather than solely on individual qualifications and capabilities. It directly demonstrates the policy of evaluating candidates based on group membership.

incidents.institution_name: University of British Columbia > Department of Physical Therapy
incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia restricted applications for a tenure-track Canada Research Chair position to candidates belonging to specific demographic groups, excluding all others from consideration regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia's Department of Physical Therapy posted a job advertisement for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Neuromodulation in Spinal Cord Rehabilitation, an Assistant Professor tenure-track position. The posting explicitly states that the selection is restricted to members of designated groups: people with disabilities, indigenous people, racialized people, women, and people from minoritized gender identity groups. Candidates who do not identify as belonging to one or more of these groups are ineligible to be considered for the position. Applicants are required to complete an equity survey and self-identify as a member of one of these groups; those who do not self-identify in this way are excluded from the hiring process entirely. The posting notes this restriction is made pursuant to Section 42 of the BC Human Rights Code and UBC's Canada Research Chairs Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Action Plan. Only the names of candidates who identify as belonging to the designated groups are shared with the search committee, meaning any candidate who does not meet these demographic criteria is screened out before their qualifications are evaluated. Review of applications was set to begin on April 15, 2024.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified scholars are excluded from consideration for a publicly funded academic position solely on the basis of their demographic characteristics. This institutional practice reduces individuals to their membership in demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, scholarly achievement, and potential. Candidates who fall outside the designated groups — regardless of their expertise in neuromodulation or spinal cord rehabilitation — are denied the opportunity to compete for the position. This entrenches the idea that demographic identity is a legitimate primary filter for professional opportunity, normalising the sorting of individuals by immutable traits rather than merit. The practice also sends a message to researchers in the field that their eligibility for publicly supported positions depends on who they are rather than what they can contribute.

quotes.text: pursuant to Section 42 of the BC Human Rights code, UBC is currently restricted in the recruitment, selection, and nomination of CRCs to members of these groups.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the hiring restriction is explicitly framed as a legal requirement applied to all Canada Research Chair recruitment at UBC, limiting who may be considered based on demographic group membership.

quotes.text: the selection will be restricted to members of the following designated groups: people with disabilities, Indigenous people, racialized people, women, and people from minoritized gender identity groups.
quotes.rationale: Directly states that the position is closed to anyone who does not belong to the listed demographic categories, confirming the nature and scope of the hiring restriction.

quotes.text: candidates from these groups must self-identify as belonging to one or more of the designated equity groups to be considered for the position. Because the search is limited to those self-identifying as members of designated equity groups, candidates must also provide their name to be considered.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the operational mechanism of the restriction: candidates who do not self-identify in demographic terms are excluded before their qualifications are reviewed at all.

quotes.text: only the names of those who identify as people with disabilities, Indigenous people, racialized people, women, and people from minoritized gender identity groups be shared with the search committee.
quotes.rationale: Shows that the demographic screening occurs before the search committee ever sees an application, meaning no merit-based review is possible for those outside the designated groups.

incidents.short_description: School District No. 43 (Coquitlam) promoted a teacher workshop that restricted participation based on teachers' skin colour and ethnicity, excluding educators who did not meet specific demographic criteria.
incidents.description_of_event: School District No. 43 (Coquitlam) advertised a professional development workshop titled 'Creating Capacity for BIPOC Teachers' facilitated by Melissa Illing, who chairs the Committee for Anti-racist and Social Justice. The workshop was based on the book 'Creating a Home in Schools' by Francisco Rios and A. Longoria and stated it would discuss key ideas aimed to help sustain teachers based on their skin colour and ethnicity. The workshop description explicitly limited participation to teachers categorized by their demographic characteristics rather than their professional development needs, teaching challenges, or individual circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: This practice divides educators into separate groups based on skin colour and ethnicity rather than evaluating their professional development needs as individuals. It denies teachers the opportunity to learn from colleagues with different backgrounds and perspectives, limiting the diversity of thought and experience that enriches professional growth. Teachers excluded from the workshop are denied access to professional development resources based solely on demographic categorization. Teachers included in the workshop receive messaging that their demographic category, rather than their individual professional qualities and challenges, defines their primary identity within the institution. This approach undermines the principle that institutions should recognize each person's unique contributions and circumstances, and it fragments the teaching community along demographic lines rather than building solidarity around shared professional challenges and the common goal of educating students effectively.

quotes.text:
A- Creating Capacity for BIPOC Teachers
- Melissa Illing
quotes.rationale: The workshop title explicitly identifies the demographic criteria used to determine eligibility for participation, demonstrating that access was restricted based on categorization by skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual professional needs.

incidents.short_description: The Université de Moncton explicitly restricted a Canada Research Chair position to applicants of a specific racial category, excluding all others from consideration based solely on their skin colour.
incidents.description_of_event: The Université de Moncton posted a job competition (reference UMAP24-05) on 9 April 2024 for a Canada Research Chair (Tier 2) in causal inference and data science, based at the Moncton campus within the Department of Mathematics and Statistics. The posting explicitly stated that the position was reserved for racially categorized persons ('personnes racisées'), requiring applicants to complete a self-identification form at the time of application. This restriction meant that any candidate who did not identify within this designated racial category was ineligible for consideration, regardless of their qualifications, research record, or teaching ability. The university justified this restriction by citing its equity and diversity targets under the Canada Research Chairs Programme. The position was a full-time tenure-track appointment with a five-year mandate and a closing date of 15 June 2024.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are excluded from competing for a publicly funded academic position solely on the basis of their skin colour. The university evaluates applicants not as individuals with distinct abilities, research accomplishments, and potential, but through a demographic lens. This reduces persons to a racial category and denies them the opportunity to be assessed on their own merits. The practice entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable characteristics rather than treating each candidate as a unique individual. It sends a clear message to prospective applicants that their racial background, rather than their intellectual contributions, determines their eligibility for publicly supported positions.

quotes.text: Dans le but d'atteindre les cibles en matiere d'equite et de diversité du Programme des chaires de recherche du Canada, ce poste est destiné aux personnes racisees. Un formulaire d'autoidentification doit être complété au moment du dépôt de candidature.
quotes.rationale: This is the core passage establishing the hiring restriction. It explicitly limits eligibility for the position to persons of a designated racial category and requires a self-identification form, directly excluding all other applicants on the basis of skin colour.

quotes.text: L'Université de Moncton est engagée à éliminer les barrières à l'emploi pour les personnes issues des quatre groupes désignées (femmes et personnes de minorités de genre, personnes de minorités racisées, Autochtones et personnes en situation de handicap) afin de créer un milieu d'études et de travail enrichi par la diversité.
quotes.rationale: This passage provides the university's stated rationale for the restriction, framing demographic categorisation as a path to an inclusive environment. It illustrates how the institution frames the sorting of people by racial and other demographic categories as a positive institutional goal.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board restricted eligibility for its Pathways to Veterinary Medicine Program to students based on their skin colour, denying access to students who did not meet this demographic criterion.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board's Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement, in collaboration with the Black Veterinary Association of Canada, announced the Pathways to Veterinary Medicine Program in April 2024. The program was designed to help students in grades 7-12 explore veterinary career options through virtual information and training sessions scheduled for April and May 2024. Registration materials and promotional announcements explicitly restricted program eligibility to students based on their skin colour. The program offered exposure to various veterinary career paths including veterinary technologists, animal care attendants, and animal hospital managers. Students were required to register through a form that collected their contact information and school details, with sessions running from 5:00 p.m. to 6:00 p.m. on specified dates in April and May 2024. This created a segregated educational program where access was determined by demographic categorization rather than individual interest, academic standing, or demonstrated passion for veterinary medicine.
incidents.expected_harm: Students who did not meet the skin colour criterion were denied access to educational and career exploration opportunities that will influence their future academic and professional pathways. This approach evaluated students based on an immutable demographic characteristic rather than their individual interest in veterinary medicine, academic qualifications, or demonstrated passion for animal care. The practice signals to all students that institutions will categorize and separate them by demographic labels rather than recognizing their unique qualities and aspirations. Students excluded from the program lost networking opportunities with veterinary professionals, exposure to career information, and mentorship that will shape their educational choices. The institutional message communicates that access to opportunities depends on demographic categories rather than individual merit, interest, or need, undermining the principle that each person should be evaluated based on their character and capabilities.

quotes.text: Calling all animal lovers! In collaboration with Black Veterinary Association of Canada (BVAC) we invite Black students from Grades 7 -12 who are interested in exploring veterinary career options for the Pathways to Veterinary Medicine Program.
quotes.rationale: This quote from the social media announcement demonstrates that program eligibility was explicitly restricted based on skin colour rather than individual qualifications or interest in veterinary medicine.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of Canadian Heritage
incidents.short_description: The Department of Canadian Heritage established the Changing Narratives Fund, which prioritizes funding applications from specific demographic groups while providing mentorship and training opportunities based on demographic identity rather than individual merit or need.
incidents.description_of_event: In Budget 2024, the federal government committed $10 million over three years to the Department of Canadian Heritage for the Changing Narratives Fund. This fund distributes resources through three existing programs: the Canada Media Fund, the Local Journalism Initiative, and the Collective Initiatives Component of the Canada Periodical Fund. The fund explicitly prioritizes projects and hiring decisions based on demographic categories, specifically indigenous, racialized, ethno-religious minority, people with disabilities, and 2SLGBTQI+ communities. The Local Journalism Initiative stream prioritizes hiring journalists from these designated groups, while other streams prioritize training, mentorship, and professional development opportunities for members of these communities at all career stages. The fund structures access to government resources based on demographic identity rather than individual circumstances, abilities, or demonstrated need.
incidents.expected_harm: This program reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their unique character, abilities, and individual circumstances. By structuring access to funding, hiring preferences, mentorship, and professional development opportunities around demographic classifications, the program undermines the principle that people should be assessed as individuals. It creates a system where demographic identity becomes the primary criterion for accessing government support, rather than demonstrated talent, need, or merit. This approach may disadvantage qualified individuals who do not fit the designated categories, regardless of their personal circumstances or abilities. The program also risks perpetuating the very categorizations it claims to address by institutionalizing demographic classification as the basis for opportunity allocation.

quotes.text: The Changing Narratives Fund stream of the Local Journalism Initiative prioritizes the hiring of diverse (that is, Indigenous, Black, racialized, ethno-religious minority, people with disabilities and 2SLGBTQI+ communities) journalists in diverse-owned and mainstream news organizations to produce civic journalism for underserved communities.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that hiring decisions are explicitly prioritized based on demographic categories, constituting a hiring preference system.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Service Commission of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Public Service Commission advertises student employment programs that give hiring preference to applicants based on demographic categories including sex, race, ethnicity, and disability status, rather than evaluating students as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The Government of Canada's Federal Student Work Experience Program website explicitly states that certain student jobs and programs are restricted to applicants who self-declare membership in designated demographic groups. The page indicates that some positions are 'only open to people who have self-declared as a member of an employment equity group,' specifically identifying women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities. The program creates separate specialized initiatives including the Employment Opportunity for Students with Disabilities and the Indigenous Student Employment Opportunity, which provide distinct support structures and opportunities based on demographic self-declaration rather than individual circumstances or qualifications. While the general program is open to all eligible students, the policy establishes a two-tier system where certain opportunities and enhanced support services are allocated according to demographic category membership.
incidents.expected_harm: This policy will reduce students to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique qualities, abilities, and circumstances. Students who do not belong to designated demographic groups will be excluded from certain employment opportunities regardless of their individual merit, character, or demonstrated needs. The practice of categorizing people by demographic characteristics and providing differential access to opportunities undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their individual qualities and potential. Students may feel pressure to emphasize demographic identity over personal achievement, and the policy may create resentment by signaling that some students receive opportunities based on group membership rather than individual merit. The separate support structures reinforce the notion that people should be treated differently based on demographic categories rather than assessed for their individual support needs.

quotes.text: Some student jobs and programs are only open to people who have self-declared as a member of an employment equity group.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly confirms that certain employment opportunities are restricted based on demographic category membership, excluding students who do not belong to designated groups regardless of their individual qualifications.

quotes.text: When you apply, you can indicate (self-declare) if you identify as a woman, an Indigenous person, a member of a visible minority, or a person with a disability.
quotes.rationale: This passage identifies the specific demographic categories used to determine eligibility for restricted programs, demonstrating that sex, race or ethnicity, and disability status are the bases for differential treatment.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan posted a tenure-track Canada Research Chair position requiring applicants to self-identify with designated equity groups based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, sex, Indigenous status, or disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Saskatchewan advertised a tenure-track Canada Research Chair Tier 2 position in Protein Science for Pathogen Defense. The job posting stated that recruitment would be guided by Canada Research Chairs Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Practices and explicitly required that the successful candidate self-identify with one of four designated equity groups: women and gender equity-seeking groups, individuals categorized by skin colour, Indigenous Peoples, or people with disabilities. The posting indicated the university relied on section 56 of the Saskatchewan Human Rights Code to give this preference in employment. The position was posted on February 13, 2024, with applications reviewed starting April 1, 2024.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction prevents individuals from being evaluated based on their individual qualifications, research excellence, and merit. Qualified candidates who do not self-identify with the specified demographic categories are excluded from consideration regardless of their scientific capabilities, publication record, or potential contributions to the research program. The practice reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than assessing their unique perspectives, varied life experiences, and demonstrated capabilities. It undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and individual qualities rather than immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: The University of Saskatchewan is committed to diversity, inclusion, and equity in the workplace. Recruitment will be guided by the Canada Research Chairs Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Practices. The successful candidate will self-identify with one of the four designated equity groups (women and gender equity-seeking groups, racialized individuals, Indigenous Peoples, and people with disabilities).
quotes.rationale: This passage establishes the explicit requirement that candidates must self-identify with demographic categories based on immutable characteristics as a condition for the position.

incidents.short_description: Mount Allison University maintains a dedicated campus space, mentorship program, wellness circles, and other programming restricted to students based on their skin colour, institutionally sorting students into racially defined groups rather than serving them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Mount Allison University, located in Sackville, New Brunswick, operates a suite of programs and spaces that restrict participation based on students' skin colour. These include the Black Resource and Information Centre (BRIC), described as a dedicated space that "centres the Black experience"; the Black Students Mentorship Program, explicitly designed for students of a particular skin colour and pairing them with mentors on that basis; ongoing health and wellness circles restricted by skin colour; and a Black Caucus affinity group open only to students, faculty, staff, and alumni of a particular skin colour. The university's student life office frames these initiatives as a response to what it describes as unique challenges faced by people of a particular skin colour, and states its commitment to creating "safe and affirming spaces" defined along these lines. Programming such as "Braids and Fades Fridays" and a skin-colour-specific graduation celebration are also listed. The university's EDI office provides advising described as serving "BIPOC and other equity deserving students," explicitly categorizing students by skin colour and ethnicity as a condition of access. A financial award administered through the university also gives preference to students based on their skin colour and regional origin. Across all of these initiatives, the university institutionally categories and separates students based on immutable characteristics rather than their individual needs, abilities, or circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are evaluated and sorted by the institution based on their skin colour rather than their individual character, needs, or abilities. Students whose skin colour does not qualify them for these programs are excluded from spaces, mentorship relationships, wellness support, and networking opportunities on that basis alone. This institutional practice normalizes the idea that people's skin colour defines their experiences and needs, rather than their unique individual circumstances. It entrenches division among the student body by creating separate programming streams defined by skin colour, undermining the shared sense of community and common humanity that a university campus should foster. Students learn from the outset that the institution views them primarily through a demographic lens, rather than as distinct individuals deserving support and recognition on their own terms. The creation of racially segregated physical spaces on campus further signals that students of different skin colours should gather separately, reversing the principle that a university is a place where people of all backgrounds come together in shared pursuit of knowledge and understanding.

quotes.text: The Black Resource and Information Centre (BRIC), located in WMSC 124, is a dedicated safe space designed to support the Black experience on campus and in our community. The space serves as a community gathering space, and gives access to peer support and resources.
quotes.rationale: Describes a physically dedicated space on campus defined by and restricted to students of a particular skin colour — a racially segregated gathering space.

quotes.text: The Black Students Mentorship Program is designed to help Black students thrive academically, socially, and professionally at Mount Allison. By pairing students with experienced mentors, we provide guidance, encouragement, and meaningful connections to help you succeed on campus and beyond.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly restricts the mentorship program to students of a particular skin colour, denying access to students on the basis of an immutable characteristic.

quotes.text: Black health and wellness circles — on-going wellness support groups with a focus on lived experience.
quotes.rationale: Wellness and mental health support is offered on a skin-colour-restricted basis, meaning students with other skin colours are excluded from these support groups.

quotes.text: Mount Allison's Black affinity group, the MtA Black Caucus, is aimed at empowering and supporting Black students, faculty, staff, and alumni. This is the first group of its kind on campus that includes the entire Mount Allison Black community. We invite all Black community members to join by contacting Lucy Ofori, EDI Student Advisor, at lofori@mta.ca.
quotes.rationale: A formally organized institutional group restricted entirely to members of a particular skin colour, including staff and faculty — institutionalizing racial segregation within the campus community.

quotes.text: Preference given to Black, Indigenous, and People of Colour from Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, and Newfoundland
quotes.rationale: A financial award explicitly gives preference based on skin colour and ethnicity, distributing institutional resources on the basis of immutable characteristics rather than individual need or merit alone.

quotes.text: In acknowledgement of the unique challenges that Black people face in this society and on our campus, Student Life, in cooperation with various stakeholders and community partners, has committed to the following initiatives towards substantive change.
quotes.rationale: The university's stated rationale for all these programs frames the initiatives as responses to presumed shared experiences based on skin colour, rather than addressing individual students' distinct circumstances.

incidents.short_description: The Alberta government launched two new grant programs in January 2024 that allocate public funds specifically to initiatives targeting people based on their ethnicity and skin colour, rather than addressing racism as a shared civic concern affecting all individuals.
incidents.description_of_event:
On 18 January 2024, the Government of Alberta announced the launch of two new grant programs—the Ethnocultural Grant program and the Anti-Racism Grant program—created by splitting the former Multiculturalism and Anti-Racism Grant program in December 2023, following advice from the Alberta Anti-Racism Advisory Council.

The Ethnocultural Grant program received $8 million over three years. Its Stream 1 funds projects that create opportunities for intercultural connections with ethnocultural and indigenous groups, and Stream 2 funds projects celebrating diversity.

The Anti-Racism Grant program received $1.5 million over three years. Its design explicitly targets people based on their ethnicity and skin colour: Stream 1 funds projects that promote awareness of racism and its impacts as experienced by indigenous and 'racialized groups,' and Stream 2 funds community anti-racism projects meeting the same objectives. The program's stated purpose is to increase awareness of racism and the challenges faced by these designated groups, rather than framing racism as a violation of individual dignity that can affect any person.

The government frames eligibility and program objectives around demographic categories—ethnicity, indigenous status, and skin colour—as the organizing principle for who receives public support and whose experiences of discrimination are recognized. The minister's announcement celebrated support for 'multicultural, indigenous and Métis communities' as distinct groups deserving targeted government intervention, embedding an identitarian view of citizens into the architecture of public grant funding.
incidents.expected_harm: Public funds are directed based on people's ethnicity and skin colour rather than on universal principles of individual dignity and the wrongness of discrimination against any person. This embeds an identitarian view of citizenship into government grant-making, normalizing the practice of sorting Albertans into demographic categories to determine who receives state support. Individuals whose experiences of discrimination fall outside the designated ethnic and skin-colour categories receive no comparable recognition or support, creating a two-tiered system of civic concern. The framing teaches recipients and the broader public that combating racism is the business of particular ethnic groups rather than a shared civic responsibility grounded in the equal worth of every person. Over time, this reinforces division among Albertans by treating ethnicity and skin colour as the primary lens through which government understands and responds to social problems, rather than fostering the common humanity and shared belonging that make a unified civic community possible.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board, in partnership with ECDI, ran a racially segregated EarlyON Child and Family Centre program open only to children and families based on their skin colour. Children aged birth to six and their families were admitted or excluded on this basis.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board (TDSB), in partnership with ECDI, created EarlyON Child and Family Centre programs running from January to March, hosted at Glen Ravine P.S. (11 Gadsby Dr.) and Oakridge Jr. P.S. (110 Byng Ave.) in Toronto. The programming ran Tuesday and Wednesday and Tuesday and Thursday respectively, from 3:00 PM to 6:00 PM at each site. The program was explicitly restricted to children from birth to age six and their families on the basis of the children's skin colour. The promotional poster described the program as "free programming for Black children and families" and stated that the programs aim to provide culturally responsive and supportive programming specifically for children based on their skin colour. New families were offered a grocery gift card as an incentive to join. The institutional decision to restrict access to a publicly supported early childhood program on the basis of skin colour represents a formal policy of sorting young children and their families into demographic categories rather than welcoming all children based on their individual developmental needs or family circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: Young children and their families are excluded from publicly supported early childhood programming based on the skin colour of the child. Families whose children do not meet the skin colour criterion are denied access to a free educational and support program that their tax dollars help fund. Children are introduced from birth to an institutional framework in which their skin colour is treated as a defining characteristic that determines access to services, rather than being recognized as individuals with unique developmental needs. This normalizes the practice of categorizing children by demographic characteristics and sorting families accordingly, undermining the principle that every child deserves to be evaluated and supported as an individual. The segregation of early years programming along lines of skin colour sends families the message that institutions view them primarily through a demographic lens, eroding shared civic belonging and preventing the kind of community building that comes from parents and children of different backgrounds engaging together in common programs.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Employment and Social Development Canada
incidents.short_description: The Government of Canada launched a $200 million endowment fund that restricts grant eligibility to organizations defined by the skin colour of the people they serve or are led by, explicitly excluding other Canadians from accessing the funding.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 18, 2023, Employment and Social Development Canada announced the launch of the first call for proposals under the Black-led Philanthropic Endowment Fund, administered by the Foundation for Black Communities (FFBC). The fund, established through a $200 million federal endowment committed in Budget 2021, restricts eligible applicants to organizations that are led by, focused on, or serve people of a particular skin colour. The FFBC allocated $5.88 million for an estimated 120 projects in this first call. Projects must either address discrimination against people of a particular skin colour or work to improve social and economic outcomes specifically for communities defined by skin colour. The call for proposals was open until February 1, 2024. The government's announcement framed the fund as an inclusion initiative, yet the program's eligibility criteria are explicitly structured around the skin colour of an organization's leadership and the people it serves, making skin colour a prerequisite for accessing federal grant funding. The program is designed to continue for at least a decade, with annual calls for proposals.
incidents.expected_harm: Canadians are granted or denied access to federal government funding based on the skin colour of themselves or the communities they serve. Organizations doing work to improve social and economic outcomes for individuals are excluded from this funding stream not because of the quality or relevance of their work, but because of the demographic composition of their leadership or clientele. This entrenches a system where government resources are distributed based on demographic categories rather than the individual merits, needs, or demonstrated impact of an organization. It signals to Canadians that their eligibility for public support depends on immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that government institutions should evaluate people and organizations as individuals. The program also normalizes the sorting of Canadians into groups defined by skin colour as a basis for resource allocation, reinforcing division rather than shared civic belonging.

incidents.short_description: All 12 members of CN's advisory council of indigenous community representatives resigned in December 2023, saying the railway company ignored their advice and fell short of meaningful engagement. CN responded with a statement that stopped short of an apology.
incidents.description_of_event: Canadian National Railway Company (CN) formed an Indigenous Advisory Council in 2021, made up of 12 prominent community representatives including former Assembly of First Nations national chief Phil Fontaine and former senator Murray Sinclair. The council's stated mandate was to advise CN's board and executives on matters relevant to the company's relationships with indigenous communities. The council submitted two reports containing recommendations, neither of which was acted upon by CN. In December 2023, the full 12-member council announced its resignation, effective December 31, 2023. Council co-chair Roberta Jamieson stated that it had become apparent CN had no intention of taking the proper steps the council recommended, including issuing a formal apology for the company's historical role in transporting children to residential schools and in the dispossession of land. CN declined an interview and instead issued a six-paragraph statement it described as an 'acknowledgement,' which council members said fell short of an apology. The council stated in a resignation letter that CN's 'ineffective' use of its advice showed the company had 'missed the mark on reconciliation.'
incidents.expected_harm: CN structured an advisory body along ethnic and ancestry lines, creating an institutional arrangement that evaluates and engages with people primarily based on their ethnicity rather than as individual Canadians with shared civic standing. When the body collapsed without producing meaningful outcomes, it left indigenous community members who participated feeling their concerns were tokenized and ignored. The episode illustrates how institutions can use identity-based advisory structures as a form of symbolic engagement while failing to address substantive concerns — undermining trust and reinforcing a pattern where people are sorted into demographic categories for institutional purposes without genuine accountability to them as individuals.

quotes.text: The head of Canadian National Railway Co.'s council of Indigenous advisers says the group quit after it became apparent the company had no intention of taking the proper steps to repair its relationship with Indigenous peoples.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core fact: CN's advisory council resigned after concluding the company was not acting on its recommendations.

quotes.text: The 12-member body, CN's Indigenous Advisory Council, announced its resignation on Monday, after failing to convince the Montreal-based railway to apologize and take other measures to address its roles in colonization and residential schools.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the resignation and the specific demands that were not met, providing key incident details.

incidents.short_description: The City of Ottawa set a quota requiring that a majority of Transit Advisory Working Group members identify based on sex or gender identity, embedding demographic targets into a public volunteer recruitment process.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 7, 2023, the City of Ottawa's OC Transpo sent a recruitment notice for a new Transit Advisory Working Group — a volunteer advisory body intended to provide guidance on transit operations, including Para Transpo, conventional bus service, and the O-Train. The notice stated that the city's goal was to have "at least 50 percent plus one" of Working Group members made up of individuals self-identifying as women, non-binary, transgender, and/or gender non-conforming persons. The application form made this demographic criterion operational by requiring applicants to answer whether they identify as female, non-binary, transgender, and/or gender non-conforming, as a mandatory field. Applications were to be reviewed by Transit Services' General Manager and the Chairs of the Transit Commission and Light Rail Sub-Committee, with successful candidates notified after the December 22, 2023 deadline. The recruitment process thereby introduced a formal demographic quota and required applicants to disclose their sex and gender identity as part of the selection criteria.
incidents.expected_harm: Members of the public are evaluated for a civic advisory role based on their sex and gender identity rather than solely on their interest in transit, their expertise, or their individual perspective. People who do not fall into the preferred demographic categories face a structural disadvantage in being selected, regardless of the quality of their contribution. The requirement to disclose sex and gender identity as a mandatory application question treats applicants as representatives of demographic categories rather than as individuals, reducing them to group characteristics. This approach normalizes the practice of institutions sorting people by demographic proxies when filling civic roles, undermining the principle that every person's individual qualities and capabilities should be what determines their participation in public life.

incidents.short_description: The Writers' Union of Canada runs an annual virtual writers conference that restricts participation to people of specific skin colours and ethnic backgrounds, explicitly excluding others from applying as mentees.
incidents.description_of_event: The Writers' Union of Canada (TWUC), in partnership with the League of Canadian Poets (LCP), organizes a recurring annual virtual conference called 'BIPOC Writers Connect: Facilitating Mentorship, Creating Community.' The conference offers emerging writers one-on-one manuscript feedback from established authors, a query letter writing workshop, an industry panel discussion, and networking opportunities with literary professionals. Eligibility to apply as a mentee is explicitly restricted to writers who belong to specific racial and ethnic groups. Writers outside those demographic categories are not permitted to apply, regardless of their individual circumstances, writing ability, or need for mentorship. The conference was held on October 19, 2023, and again on October 22, 2025, with applications each year closed to those who do not identify within the specified demographic categories. Mentors selected for the conference are also described in terms of their racial and ethnic backgrounds. The event's accessibility statement frames the racial eligibility restriction as a response to barriers in the literary industry, but the practical effect is a segregated professional development conference where access is determined by a person's skin colour and ethnicity rather than their individual qualities or needs. The conference is funded by Penguin Random House Canada, the Writers' Trust of Canada, Kids Can Press, the Canada Council for the Arts, the Ontario Arts Council, and the Government of Ontario.
incidents.expected_harm: Writers are evaluated and granted or denied access to professional development, mentorship, manuscript feedback, and literary industry networking based on their skin colour and ethnic background rather than their individual talent, circumstances, or demonstrated need. This sorting of people by demographic category entrenches the practice of institutions making decisions about individuals based on immutable characteristics rather than who they are as people. Writers excluded from the opportunity receive no pathway to equivalent support regardless of how much they might benefit from it. The use of public funds — from the Canada Council for the Arts, the Ontario Arts Council, and the Government of Ontario — to operate a racially segregated professional event means that taxpayers of all backgrounds are financing a programme that turns some of them away based on their skin colour. The practice also normalizes within the literary community the idea that demographic categories are an appropriate and legitimate basis for institutional decision-making about individuals, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits.

incidents.institution_name: University of British Columbia > Student Services
incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia operates a student space on its Vancouver campus that is accessible only to students who identify based on their skin colour. All other enrolled students are excluded from the facility regardless of their individual needs or circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia's Office of the Vice-President, Students and the Centre for Student Development and Leadership opened the Black Student Space at the Exchange Building (5960 Student Union Boulevard) on the Vancouver campus in fall 2023. The space offers a range of amenities including kitchen facilities, quiet study areas, a lounge and napping area, showers, lockers, a library, a printer and scanner, a television and projector, board games, and regular events and programming. Access to the space is restricted by skin colour: only students who self-identify as having a particular skin colour are permitted to use it. All other currently enrolled UBC students are excluded. Students must complete an online application form to be granted access, with requests processed weekly. The university describes the space as "identity-affirming" and frames its purpose around celebrating shared experiences within a demographic community. The space was created in response to institutional strategic planning documents and a taskforce report, and is positioned as a student services offering within UBC's broader support infrastructure.
incidents.expected_harm: Students who do not share the designated skin colour are excluded from a well-resourced campus facility paid for and operated by the university. The university, as an institution, evaluates students' eligibility to access campus space based on their skin colour rather than their individual needs, interests, or circumstances. This sends a clear institutional message that skin colour is a legitimate basis for sorting and separating students. The arrangement normalises the use of demographic categories to grant or deny access to shared campus resources, undermining the principle that all students possess equal individual worth. It entrenches division among the student body by creating spaces defined by immutable characteristics, foreclosing opportunities for students to connect across perceived differences and weakening the shared civic belonging that a university community should foster.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Health Agency of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Public Health Agency of Canada recommended prioritizing COVID-19 vaccination for members of specific groups based on their ethnicity and other immutable characteristics, treating individuals as members of demographic categories rather than evaluating individual risk factors.
incidents.description_of_event: On July 11, 2023, the National Advisory Committee on Immunization (NACI) published guidance recommending that a dose of the new COVID-19 vaccine formulation be offered in fall 2023 to individuals in authorized age groups. The guidance identified specific populations for whom immunization is "particularly important," including "individuals in or from First Nations, Métis and Inuit communities" and "members of racialized and other equity-deserving communities." These groups were listed alongside populations at increased biological risk such as adults 65 years and older, residents of long-term care homes, individuals with underlying medical conditions, pregnant individuals, and people who provide essential community services. The recommendation characterized these groups based on their ethnicity and demographic categories rather than individual health circumstances or demonstrated risk factors. The guidance did not provide evidence that membership in these ethnic or demographic groups independently increased risk of severe COVID-19 outcomes when controlling for individual factors such as age, medical conditions, or vaccination status. The document acknowledged high levels of antibodies in the Canadian population from past vaccination and infection, declining rates of severe outcomes, and that risk factors for severe disease include age, vaccination status, prior infection, and comorbidities in adults. The recommendation to prioritize based on ethnicity and demographic group membership appeared alongside, and was given equal weight to, evidence-based individual risk factors.
incidents.expected_harm: The guidance will result in individuals being categorized and prioritized for vaccination based on their ethnicity and demographic group membership rather than their individual health circumstances, needs, and risk factors. Healthcare providers and vaccination programs implementing this guidance will assess people through the lens of demographic categories rather than evaluating each person's unique medical history, comorbidities, vaccination status, and individual risk profile. This approach undermines the principle of evaluating people as individuals with distinct characteristics. The guidance may lead to healthcare resources being allocated based on assumptions about group characteristics rather than demonstrated individual need. Individuals who do not belong to the identified demographic groups but who have significant individual risk factors may receive lower priority, while individuals within the identified groups who have lower individual risk may receive higher priority, regardless of their actual medical circumstances. The recommendation establishes a framework where ethnicity becomes a formal criterion in public health decision-making, institutionalizing the practice of sorting Canadians into categories based on immutable traits rather than individual assessment.

quotes.text: Immunization is particularly important for those at increased risk of COVID-19 infection or severe disease, for example: • Adults 65 years of age or older • Residents of long-term care homes and other congregate living settings • Individuals with underlying medical conditions that place them at higher risk of severe COVID-19 • Individuals who are pregnant • Individuals in or from First Nations, Métis and Inuit communities* • Members of racialized and other equity-deserving communities • People who provide essential community services
quotes.rationale: This passage lists the specific groups identified for prioritization, including ethnic and demographic categories alongside evidence-based individual risk factors, demonstrating the policy of categorizing people by demographic group membership.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Military Journal, a publication of the Department of National Defence, published a special issue that frames military culture problems through ideological categories such as race, sex, and sexual orientation, treating demographic group membership as the primary lens for understanding and reforming the institution.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Military Journal (Vol. 23, No. 3, Summer 2023), an official publication of the Department of National Defence, published a special issue titled 'Transforming Military Cultures,' edited by three co-directors of the DND-funded Transforming Military Cultures (TMC) network. The editorial, which introduces and frames the entire issue, presents a sweeping analysis of Canadian Armed Forces (CAF) culture grounded in what the authors call a 'feminist intersectional trauma-informed approach.' The editors argue that any meaningful discussion of military culture must engage with concepts including 'patriarchy, heteronormativity, colonialism, white supremacy, ableism, and classism' as 'interrelated root causes.' Throughout the editorial, military personnel are discussed primarily as members of demographic categories — grouped by sex, sexual orientation, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability status — rather than as individuals with distinct character, abilities, and experiences. The issue includes articles focused on 'racialized military personnel,' 'whiteness and power,' 'heteronormative patriarchal assumptions,' and 'feminist identity in a military context.' The DND provided funding through its MINDS program to support the TMC network responsible for producing this content. A perspectives piece from a serving Lieutenant-General is included, reinforcing the institutional endorsement of this framing. The journal situates these ideological frameworks within a broader call for 'progressive societal change,' explicitly linking the CAF's internal reform agenda to political and social movements outside the institution.
incidents.expected_harm: When an official government military publication frames service members and institutional problems primarily through demographic categories — sorting personnel by skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and ethnicity — it signals to members of the armed forces that the institution views them first as members of groups rather than as individuals of singular worth. Personnel who do not fit neatly into the preferred categories, or who hold different views on the causes of institutional problems, are implicitly cast as obstacles to reform rather than as equal participants in a shared institution. The approach entrenches division within the military by treating demographic identity as the dominant lens for understanding conduct, culture, and belonging. It forecloses the possibility of addressing real problems of misconduct and mistreatment by reducing them to group-level grievances rather than violations of individual dignity. By embedding ideologically specific frameworks — drawn from activist and academic traditions — into publicly funded military communications, the Department of National Defence uses its institutional authority to legitimize one particular ideological view of Canadian society, undermining the open and reasoned discourse that a healthy institution requires.

incidents.short_description: A grade 10 English exam at a Halton District School Board school required students to identify and advocate for what the course called a 'racialized voice' missing from the curriculum, framing literary study around demographic categories rather than individual merit or shared human experience.
incidents.description_of_event: An ENG2D (Grade 10 English) exam at a Halton District School Board school tasked students with a 'Dragon's Den Pitch Project' in which they were required to identify a so-called 'racialized voice' absent from the course content and persuade the class and instructor to add it. Students were directed to locate a media tool — such as a documentary, TED Talk, news article, podcast, or newscast — addressing what the exam described as 'problematic, critical issues' affecting a 'racialized group of people.' Students were then instructed to research obstacles and struggles that this group faced 'historically and today' that prevented them from 'finding equity and equality in a dominant white society.' The exam framed literary and media analysis explicitly through the lens of racial and demographic categorization, requiring students to approach texts and voices primarily as representatives of racial or ethnic groups rather than as individual human beings. The assignment also prompted students to consider additional identity categories — including religion, sexual orientation, gender, and disability — as layered onto the central racial framing.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are taught, through a graded exam, to perceive and evaluate people — including authors, speakers, and public figures — primarily as members of racial and demographic groups rather than as individuals. This normalizes the practice of sorting human beings into identity categories and treating group membership as the defining lens through which to understand a person's ideas, experiences, and worth. The assignment frames society as fundamentally divided between a 'dominant white' group and racialized groups, embedding a worldview of fixed racial hierarchy into a compulsory academic exercise. Students who do not share this framework are not given room to engage with literature or media on the basis of individual human experience, reasoned argument, or shared humanity. The assignment forecloses the kind of open, evidence-based inquiry that allows students to form their own conclusions, replacing it with a predetermined ideological premise they are required to argue. Over time, this approach undermines students' ability to engage with one another across perceived differences and erodes the understanding that literature and human expression speak to common experiences rather than exclusively to the concerns of particular demographic groups.

incidents.short_description: Raft Brew Labs published a series of social media posts characterizing men of a particular skin colour, religion, and sexual orientation as holding oppressive dominance over the brewing industry, framing other brewers and beer drinkers through identity-based group categories.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 13, 2023, Raft Brew Labs posted a thread on Twitter in response to criticism it received following a May 12 statement about the Craft Brewers Conference. In the posts, the company characterized the brewing industry as dominated by a specific identity group, describing men of a particular skin colour, sexual orientation, and religion as exercising 'hegemony' over the industry. The company invoked the concept of 'toxic masculinity' and applied it broadly to the industry, citing the Brett Kavanaugh hearings and a beer company's marketing decisions as examples. The posts framed critics of the company's earlier statement, including named public commentators Jonathan Kay and Jordan Peterson, as defenders of that group's dominance. The company pledged to continue its advocacy and called on industry members to 'identify and uproot toxic masculinity' and stand in solidarity with those it described as lacking privilege. Throughout the posts, individuals in the brewing industry and its customer base are evaluated and discussed primarily through the lens of demographic categories — skin colour, sex, sexual orientation, and religion — rather than as distinct individuals with their own merits, views, and circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: When a business publicly sorts people in its industry and customer base into identity-based groups — assigning collective blame or privilege based on skin colour, sex, religion, and sexual orientation — it undermines the principle that every person deserves to be recognized as an individual. This framing tells people that their identity group, not their character or actions, defines their role and standing in the industry. It fosters division among brewers and customers by treating demographic categories as the primary lens through which belonging and exclusion are understood. People who do not share the company's ideological framework are implicitly cast as defenders of oppression, discouraging open and reasoned disagreement and chilling honest discourse within the industry.

incidents.short_description: The York Region District School Board organized a workplace workshop restricted to staff based on their skin colour, excluding all other employees from attending.
incidents.description_of_event: The York Region District School Board (YRDSB) organized an in-person workshop held on Monday, May 8, 2023, from 8:45 am to 11:00 am at The Venu Event Space, 2800 Highway 7, Woodbridge, Ontario. The workshop featured Dr. Howard Stevenson, a professor and author, presenting content described as a model for resolving racial stress and building what the poster calls 'racial literacy.' Attendance was explicitly restricted to staff who identify based on their skin colour — specifically those who identify as black — across all employee groups. The poster directed recipients to share the invitation only with those who may be interested in attending and noted that coverage (release time from regular duties) would be available upon registration. Staff who do not identify according to the specified demographic category were excluded from the event entirely, regardless of their interest in the subject matter or their professional role within the school board.
incidents.expected_harm: Staff who do not identify according to the specified skin colour category are excluded from a professional development opportunity offered during working hours, with release time from duties provided to those who qualify. This creates a two-tiered workplace where access to training, professional development, and working-time benefits is determined by an employee's immutable characteristics rather than their individual role, needs, or interests. The event normalizes the practice of sorting employees into demographic categories and treating them differently on that basis. It sends a message that understanding and addressing stress or difficult interpersonal situations in the workplace is a concern reserved for people of a particular demographic group, rather than a shared human concern relevant to all staff.
