This approach undermines the sense of shared civic and professional belonging within the school board, entrenches demographic divisions among colleagues, and models for students and the broader community that separating people by skin colour is an acceptable institutional practice.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Prosecution Service of Canada > Saskatchewan Regional Office
incidents.short_description: The Public Prosecution Service of Canada's Saskatchewan Regional Office posted a job listing for federal prosecutors that explicitly gives hiring priority to candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation. Candidates who do not fall into these demographic categories are appointed only after all others.
incidents.description_of_event: The Public Prosecution Service of Canada (PPSC) Saskatchewan Regional Office published a job posting on GC Jobs for federal prosecutor positions (LP-01 and LP-02) in Regina and Saskatoon. The posting was titled 'Looking for Diverse Legal Talent!' and closed on 1 March 2024. Under the 'Intent of the process' section, the posting states explicitly that candidates who identify as indigenous peoples, black people, members of other racialized groups, women, persons living with a disability, or persons of the 2SLGBTQIA+ community will be appointed before all others. The posting also lists under 'Organizational Needs' that qualified applicants who self-declare as members of a visible minority group, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and/or women may be selected for appointment to fulfil organizational needs. The institution thus ranks applicants not solely by their legal qualifications, knowledge, and competencies, but by their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation. Any candidate who does not belong to one of these demographic categories is placed at the back of the queue regardless of their individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified legal candidates are passed over for federal prosecutor positions solely because of their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, or sexual orientation. The institution reduces individuals to demographic categories and uses those categories — rather than character, ability, and demonstrated legal skill — as the decisive factor in who is appointed first. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It sends a clear signal to candidates that their immutable characteristics, not their qualifications, determine their place in the hiring queue, which deters individuals outside the preferred demographic groups from applying and erodes confidence in the impartiality of federal prosecutorial appointments.

incidents.short_description: This Magazine restricted fiction submissions to writers of certain skin colours only, explicitly excluding other writers from consideration. The call also expressed a preference for writers with particular sexual orientations.
incidents.description_of_event: This Magazine announced via its social media account on April 27, 2023, and on its website's contributor guidelines page, that its fiction submissions were open exclusively to writers categorised as BIPOC — a demographic label based on skin colour and ethnicity — with a stated special interest in writers identifying as queer or trans. The announcement was made by the magazine's fiction editor, H Felix Chau Bradley. Writers who did not fall into these demographic categories were not eligible to submit fiction during this open call period. The deadline for submissions was May 1, 2023. The magazine's contributor guidelines further stated that the publication believes in what it calls 'structural racism' and seeks to 'actively dismantle it,' framing its submission restrictions as a deliberate editorial policy based on demographic categorisation rather than the quality of writing or individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Writers are evaluated and granted or denied access to publication opportunities based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than the quality of their work or their individual abilities. This practice excludes writers as individuals and reduces them to demographic categories, denying them the chance to be assessed on their own merits. It normalises the institutional sorting of people by immutable characteristics and entrenches the idea that a person's skin colour determines their opportunities and standing in the literary community. The explicit preference for writers of particular sexual orientations further narrows access based on personal characteristics unrelated to the quality of the work submitted.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Association for Mathematics Education (OAME) featured a presentation titled 'What is Social Justice Math?' at its 2023 annual conference, introducing political and ideological content into mathematics teacher professional development.
incidents.description_of_event: At the OAME 2023 annual conference held on April 27–28, 2023, at the Beanfield Centre in Toronto, the Ontario Association for Mathematics Education included a session titled 'What is Social Justice Math?' in its professional development programming for Ontario mathematics educators. The conference, themed 'EmpoweringME in 2023,' also featured several other sessions and keynote speakers oriented around applying political and social frameworks to mathematics education. Keynote speaker Rochelle Gutierrez was described in the conference program as focusing on issues of 'identity and power in mathematics education,' arguing that mathematics teachers require 'political knowledge,' and advocating for a form of mathematics called 'living mathematx' where humans are 'no longer centered.' Other sessions in the program addressed redesigning math tasks 'for social justice' and empowering students to use mathematics as a 'tool for social change.' The 'What is Social Justice Math?' session represented the introduction of explicitly ideological framing into a professional development conference intended to train Ontario mathematics teachers, with the potential to shape how mathematics is taught across Ontario classrooms.
incidents.expected_harm: When a professional association responsible for mathematics education in Ontario incorporates sessions asking teachers to view and teach mathematics through a political and social justice lens, it diverts professional development away from mathematical rigour and toward ideological instruction. Teachers trained in this framework are steered to evaluate students not as individuals with distinct mathematical abilities and needs, but through the lens of group identity and political categories. This undermines the teaching of mathematics as an evidence-based, universal discipline and risks reducing students' mathematical education to a vehicle for political messaging. Students receive less focus on mathematical understanding and skill, and instead experience a classroom shaped by their teachers' politically oriented training. The normalization of this approach across Ontario's mathematics education community, through a province-wide professional association, amplifies its reach and influence across thousands of classrooms.

incidents.short_description: The Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board organized a staff celebration event restricted to employees who identify as 'racialized,' explicitly excluding other staff based on their skin colour or ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board's Equity Team sent an invitation to staff for 'A Celebration of Belonging,' an event held on Tuesday, May 30 at the Education Centre from 4:30–6:30 pm. The event was explicitly restricted to staff who identify as 'racialized,' meaning employees were sorted and admitted based on their skin colour or ethnic background. The event featured a meet-and-greet with Director Sheryl Robinson Petrazzini, dinner provided by La Luna, and planning discussions about inclusion and belonging at HWDSB. The invitation was circulated via email on behalf of the Equity Team and noted a date change from an earlier date that had conflicted with Education Week. Despite being framed as a celebration of belonging and inclusion, the event categorized staff by demographic identity and limited attendance accordingly, rather than welcoming all staff as individuals with a shared interest in a positive workplace.
incidents.expected_harm: Staff members are divided and excluded from a board-organized event based on their skin colour or ethnic background. The event treats employees not as individuals with shared professional interests and workplace concerns, but as members of distinct demographic categories deserving separate gatherings. This practice normalizes the institutional sorting of staff by immutable characteristics, directly contradicting the stated goal of belonging and inclusion. Staff not categorized as 'racialized' are excluded from conversations about workplace culture and planning, preventing the development of genuine shared civic belonging across the school community. The model sends a message to all employees that the institution views them primarily through a demographic lens rather than as colleagues with common humanity and shared goals.

quotes.text: On Tuesday, May 30, we will be holding A Celebration of Belonging for racialized staff at the Education Centre from 4:30 - 6:30 pm.
quotes.rationale: Directly confirms the event was restricted to staff categorized as 'racialized,' establishing the segregated nature of the gathering.

quotes.text: An Opportunity to connect and get to know other racialized staff
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the event explicitly structured networking and connection around a demographic category rather than shared professional roles or interests.

quotes.text: This celebration will be an opportunity to connect and get to know other Racialized identifying staff over dinner and conversation. We will collaborate on ways to support and promote inclusion and belonging at HWDSB.
quotes.rationale: Illustrates the contradiction: planning for 'inclusion and belonging' was conducted in a demographically restricted, segregated setting rather than across the whole staff community.

incidents.short_description: The Thames Valley District School Board promoted a student leadership conference exclusively open to students who identify based on their skin colour, restricting participation in a school-organized event to a single demographic group.
incidents.description_of_event: The Thames Valley District School Board (TVDSB) promoted a 'Black Excellence Student Leadership Conference' on May 9, 2023, via its official Twitter account on April 18, 2023. The conference was advertised as open only to 'Black-identifying students in grades 9-12,' explicitly restricting attendance based on how students identify in terms of skin colour. Students were invited to connect, collaborate, and celebrate at the event. To attend, students were directed to speak with their principal, vice-principal, or Black Student Association advisor. The event was organized and promoted by the school board through official institutional channels, making this a board-sanctioned, racially segregated student gathering. Students in grades 9-12 who do not identify based on this demographic category were excluded from participation regardless of their individual interest, leadership qualities, or circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are excluded from a school board-organized leadership conference based on their skin colour, normalizing the institutional practice of sorting students into demographic groups and granting or denying access to opportunities based on immutable characteristics. Students excluded from the event receive the message that their school board organizes programming that divides rather than unites the student community. Students included in the event are similarly taught that institutions properly sort and separate people based on skin colour. This approach undermines the development of shared civic belonging and common community within the school system, and entrenches the practice of evaluating individuals through demographic categories rather than their unique qualities, interests, and abilities. Leadership development opportunities — which benefit all students — are made conditional on an immutable trait, depriving excluded students of access and reinforcing division rather than common understanding.

incidents.short_description: The McGill Tribune student newspaper changed its name to The Tribune, citing the need to distance itself from McGill University's founder James McGill due to his history as an enslaver. The editorial board announced the change as part of efforts to address systemic racism and advocate for institutional changes at the university.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 12, 2023, The McGill Tribune Editorial Board announced the newspaper would change its name to The Tribune, divorcing itself from the McGill name. The decision was presented as necessary to address institutional racism and to advocate for change at McGill University. The editorial cited research by former professor Charmaine Nelson documenting James McGill's history as an enslaver who owned at least five people. The editorial board stated they could no longer bear a name that "unapologetically upholds and honours" systems of oppression. They framed the decision as part of ongoing efforts to eliminate institutionalized oppression and to center voices that journalism has historically silenced. The newspaper acknowledged its own past exclusion of students based on race and stated it had revised hiring processes and workplace policies. The editorial called on McGill University to also change its name, though the university has not done so.
incidents.expected_harm: The name change frames institutional identity through the lens of racial categorization rather than individual merit and character. By making race central to evaluating institutional legitimacy and individual worthiness to participate, this approach reduces people to demographic categories. The editorial board's statement that they revised hiring processes to remedy "institutional underrepresentation" suggests evaluation criteria that prioritize demographic representation over individual qualifications. This creates an environment where individuals may be assessed first as members of racial groups rather than as unique people with individual qualities and capabilities. The approach undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs, potentially creating barriers for individuals who do not fit preferred demographic categories.

quotes.text: Black and Indigenous faculty and students are still dramatically underrepresented in number and in the curricula of most academic programs, which fail to reflect demographic, methodological, and epistemological diversity.
quotes.rationale: Frames institutional problems in terms of racial group representation metrics.

incidents.institution_name: Toronto Metropolitan University > Community Safety and Security
incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University's Community Safety and Security department hosted a mixer event restricted to students based on their skin colour. The event excluded students who did not identify as a particular demographic group from attending a social and informational gathering on campus.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 11, 2023, Toronto Metropolitan University's Community Safety and Security department hosted an event called the 'Black Students x TMU Security Mixer' at POD 250 on the TMU campus. The event ran from 3:00 p.m. to 5:00 p.m. and featured games, food catered by Scotty Bons, music, prizes, and swag. The explicit purpose of the event was to allow students to meet members of the Community Safety and Security team and learn about the services the department offers. Attendance was restricted to students who self-identified based on their skin colour, along with general TMU community members who were presumably staff or other affiliates. TMU, a publicly funded post-secondary institution, organized and promoted this racially segregated social event through its official security and safety department, making it an institutional decision to divide students by skin colour when engaging them about campus safety services.
incidents.expected_harm: Students who do not identify based on the specified skin colour are excluded from a social event and an opportunity to connect with and learn about their university's safety and security services, based solely on their skin colour. The university sends a message that access to informal engagement with campus institutions is determined by demographic categories rather than individual interest or need. This normalizes the sorting of students by immutable characteristics, undermines shared civic belonging on campus, and treats students as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals. It prevents the kind of cross-community relationship-building between students and campus security that benefits all students and forecloses the shared understanding that comes from people of different backgrounds engaging together around common concerns.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a job for a Funding Representative at its Anjou, QC office that was explicitly restricted to applicants of first nations, Métis, or Inuit ethnicity. Candidates of other ethnicities were excluded from consideration by the job title itself.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank posted a job listing on its careers site for a Funding Representative position with its Scotia Dealer Advantage division in Anjou, Québec. The job title itself — "First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Funding Representative" — made plain that the role was restricted to applicants of a particular ethnicity. The posting described standard banking responsibilities: verifying and validating approved loan applications before releasing funds to automotive dealerships, communicating with credit analysts and dealers to resolve discrepancies, and maintaining compliance with the bank's risk and regulatory frameworks. The qualifications listed — organizational skills, attention to detail, communication skills, preferred post-secondary education, and prior customer-facing experience — were entirely ethnicity-neutral. Nothing in the role's duties or requirements bore any relationship to the applicant's ethnicity. The restriction on applicants was embedded in the job title and applied at the point of application, meaning any person who did not identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit was excluded from applying for a role they may have been fully qualified to perform.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are turned away from employment at Scotiabank solely because of their ethnicity, not because of any lack of skill, experience, or character. A person's ethnicity is irrelevant to their ability to verify loan documents, communicate with dealers, or fulfil any of the listed accountabilities. By embedding the ethnicity restriction in the job title itself, Scotiabank signals to all applicants — both those included and those excluded — that ethnicity is a legitimate basis for employment decisions. This normalises the institutional practice of sorting individuals into demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their abilities and character. Canadians excluded from consideration receive the message that their individual qualifications are subordinate to their ethnic background, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a job opening for an Assistant Manager role in the Greater Toronto Area that required applicants to self-identify as first nations, métis, or inuit. Candidates who did not identify with these ethnic categories were ineligible to apply.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a position titled 'First Nations/Metis/Inuit – Assistant Manager – GTA' on its careers website, with locations across the Greater Toronto Area including Toronto, Brampton, Mississauga, Oshawa, Richmond Hill, Scarborough, Thornhill, and Vaughan. Among the listed requirements for the role, the posting explicitly states that applicants must 'Self-Identify as First Nations, Metis, or Inuit.' All other requirements — such as post-secondary credentials, financial planning knowledge, and people management skills — relate to professional qualifications. The ethnic self-identification requirement, however, gates eligibility to the position entirely based on an applicant's ancestry and ethnic origin, rather than their individual abilities, character, or demonstrated qualifications. The posting was captured on approximately April 9, 2023, and the role has since been closed to further applicants.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are excluded from consideration for employment at Scotiabank solely because of their ethnic background. This practice reduces people to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with distinct skills, experience, and character. It entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by ancestry and ethnic identity, normalizing the idea that a person's ethnic origin is a legitimate basis for denying them access to employment opportunities. This causes direct, concrete harm to every person who meets the professional qualifications but is turned away before their abilities are even considered.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a Small Business Advisor position in the GTA that explicitly required applicants to self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit. Candidates who did not identify by these ethnic categories were excluded from consideration.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Small Business Advisor role (Requisition ID 173521) covering multiple locations in the Greater Toronto Area, including Toronto, Brampton, Mississauga, Oshawa, Richmond Hill, Scarborough, Thornhill, and Vaughan. The job posting listed "Self-Identify as First Nations, Metis, or Inuit" as a formal requirement alongside other qualifications such as post-secondary education, communication skills, and a valid driver's licence. This requirement meant that applicants were screened and excluded based on their ethnic and ancestral background rather than being evaluated solely on their professional abilities, experience, and suitability for the role. All other stated qualifications — such as business development skills, relationship-building ability, and knowledge of small business financial structures — were equally attainable by any candidate, yet the ethnic self-identification criterion served as an absolute gate to employment.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are turned away from employment at Scotiabank based on their ethnic background rather than their skills, experience, or character. This reinforces the practice of institutions sorting individuals into ethnic categories and making consequential decisions about their lives based on those categories. It signals to the broader workforce that a person's ancestry determines their access to certain jobs, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It also normalises ethnic criteria in hiring across the private sector, eroding the expectation that employment opportunities are open to all on the basis of merit.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a Senior Financial Advisor position for the Ontario region that required applicants to self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit, making ethnicity a formal job requirement. Candidates who do not identify this way are explicitly excluded from consideration.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Senior Financial Advisor role covering multiple Ontario locations, including Thunder Beach, Rama, Sault Ste. Marie, Sudbury, and Timmins. The job posting listed among its requirements that applicants must "self-identify as first nations, Metis, or Inuit." This was not a preference or an invitation for self-disclosure for equity monitoring purposes — it was listed as a formal requirement alongside professional competencies such as communication skills and the ability to build client relationships. The posting was publicly available on Scotiabank's careers website under the requisition ID 173523. The position has since closed and is no longer accepting applicants.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not identify with a particular ethnic or ancestral group are categorically excluded from applying for this position, regardless of their skills, experience, or ability to serve customers in the region. Scotiabank, a major national financial institution, uses ethnicity as a hiring gate, normalizing the practice of evaluating job applicants based on ancestry and ethnic identity rather than their character, abilities, and individual merits. This sends a message to all prospective employees that ethnic categorization is a legitimate basis for determining who may work in certain roles, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a financial advisor position in the Greater Toronto Area that restricted applicants based on their ethnicity, requiring candidates to self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit. This is a hiring restriction that evaluates candidates based on ancestry rather than their abilities and character.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Financial Advisor role for the Greater Toronto Area — covering Toronto, Brampton, Mississauga, Oshawa, Richmond Hill, Scarborough, Thornhill, and Vaughan — that listed ethnic self-identification as a formal job requirement. Among the listed requirements, alongside communication skills and the ability to build client relationships, the posting stated that applicants must self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit. This criterion is unrelated to the duties of a financial advisor and functions as an explicit ethnic restriction, disqualifying all candidates who do not belong to these designated ancestry groups regardless of their skills, experience, or qualifications. The posting was live on Scotiabank's official careers website and framed the role within the bank's broader inclusivity commitments, while simultaneously excluding the vast majority of qualified candidates on the basis of their ethnic background.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are turned away from a financial services career opportunity purely because of their ethnic background, not their character, abilities, or demonstrated potential. This institutional practice treats ancestry as a professional credential, reducing individuals to demographic categories and undermining the principle that people deserve to be evaluated on their own merits. It normalises the idea that a person's ethnic identity is a relevant criterion for employment in a mainstream banking role, entrenching demographic sorting into hiring processes. Other candidates who may have strong qualifications and a genuine desire to serve diverse communities are denied the opportunity to apply or be considered on equal terms.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a Customer Experience Associate position in the GTA that required applicants to self-identify as first nations, métis, or inuit, restricting hiring based on ethnicity. This excludes all other candidates from consideration regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Customer Experience Associate role across multiple Greater Toronto Area locations — including Toronto, Brampton, Mississauga, Oshawa, Richmond Hill, Scarborough, Thornhill, and Vaughan — under the title "First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Customer Experience Associate - GTA" (Requisition ID: 173424). The posting listed among its requirements that applicants "self-identify as First Nations, Metis, or Inuit." This requirement was placed alongside standard skills-based criteria such as communication skills, customer service experience, and willingness to train. The role itself — assisting customers with day-to-day transactions, mobile banking applications, and financial goals — carries no inherent connection to a specific ethnic background. The posting restricted access to the position entirely on the basis of ethnicity, effectively barring qualified candidates from applying because of their ancestry rather than their abilities, character, or demonstrated needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates who are fully qualified to serve customers in a banking environment are turned away from employment solely because of their ethnic background. This institutionalises the practice of evaluating people by demographic category rather than individual merit, normalising the idea that a person's ancestry is a legitimate basis for determining who may work in a given role. It reduces individuals to a demographic label at the very first stage of the employment relationship, denying them the opportunity to be assessed on their own terms. The approach entrenches division by sorting job seekers into groups defined by ethnicity and signalling that certain roles are reserved for people of particular backgrounds, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank advertised a Branch Manager position in Ontario that required applicants to self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit, restricting the role based on ethnicity. Candidates who did not identify with these groups were ineligible regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank posted a job listing for a Branch Manager role covering multiple Ontario locations, with the title explicitly designating the position as open to first nations, Métis, or Inuit applicants. The posting described standard branch management responsibilities, including team leadership, customer relationship management, risk compliance, and meeting financial objectives. Among the listed requirements, the first and most prominent criterion was that candidates must "self-identify as First Nations, Metis, or Inuit." All other requirements — communication skills, post-secondary education, mutual fund licensing, banking experience, and coaching ability — were professional and merit-based. The ethnic self-identification requirement functioned as a gatekeeping criterion: applicants who did not belong to these designated groups were excluded from consideration irrespective of their qualifications, experience, or suitability for the role. The posting covered an extensive list of Ontario communities, including Sudbury, Timmins, Sault Ste Marie, Peterborough, Niagara Falls, and dozens of others.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from a professional employment opportunity based on their ethnic background rather than their individual abilities, experience, or character. This practice reduces people to demographic categories at the point of hiring, denying individuals the right to be evaluated on their own merits. It also normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people by ethnicity, reinforcing the idea that a person's ancestry is a legitimate and primary basis on which to grant or deny professional advancement.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a job opening for a Funding Representative role at its Hamilton, Ontario office that was restricted to applicants of a specific ethnicity. The posting explicitly identified the role as available only to first nations, Métis, and Inuit candidates.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Funding Representative position with its Scotia Dealer Advantage division in Hamilton, Ontario. The job posting, captured on April 9, 2023, bore the title "First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Funding Representative - Scotia Dealer Advantage - Hamilton, ON," making the role's ethnic restriction explicit in the title itself. The position involved verifying and validating information in approved loan applications before releasing funds to automotive dealerships. The duties, required skills, and working conditions described in the posting — organizational skills, document review, customer communication, post-secondary education — were entirely standard and bore no connection to ethnicity. Despite this, the role was reserved exclusively for candidates based on their ethnicity. Scotiabank is a major Canadian bank operating nationwide, and this posting was for an in-office role at its Hamilton, Ontario location.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants who do not belong to the specified ethnic groups are excluded from consideration for this role solely on the basis of their ethnicity. A person's ethnicity is entirely irrelevant to the duties of a funding representative, and being turned away from a job opportunity for this reason is a direct harm to individual dignity. The practice normalizes the institutional habit of evaluating people by demographic category rather than by their character, skills, and abilities. It signals to all job seekers that a major Canadian financial institution assigns people to employment opportunities based on immutable traits, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated ability.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a Collections Advisor position at its Hamilton, Ontario office restricted to applicants of specific ethnicities. The job listing explicitly limited applicants to those of first nations, Métis, or Inuit descent.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank posted a Collections Advisor role for its Scotia Dealer Advantage division in Hamilton, Ontario, with the job title explicitly reading "First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Collections Advisor." The posting described a standard collections role involving overdue payment recovery, customer service, and loan management — work with no evident connection to a person's ethnicity. Despite listing qualifications based entirely on skills and experience, such as collections experience, communication ability, and computer proficiency, the position was restricted to applicants based on their ethnicity. The role was otherwise identical in duties and requirements to positions open to all applicants, with the only distinguishing feature being the ethnicity-based restriction embedded in the job title and requisition.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are excluded from consideration for this position solely on the basis of their ethnicity. This reduces employment to a matter of ancestry rather than individual skill, experience, or character — the very qualities listed in the job posting itself. The practice normalises institutional sorting of people by ethnicity, reinforcing the idea that a person's background determines what opportunities they may access. It also signals to all job seekers, regardless of ethnicity, that Scotiabank evaluates candidates through the lens of demographic categories rather than individual merit.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a job for an Income Analyst position at its Scotia Dealer Advantage office in Anjou, QC, restricting applicants to those of first nations, métis, or inuit ethnicity. This is a hiring restriction based on ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank posted a job listing for an Income Analyst role within its Scotia Dealer Advantage division at its Anjou, Quebec location. The job title itself — 'First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Income Analyst' — signals that the position was restricted to candidates of a particular ethnicity. The role involved verifying income details and documentation, analysing loan applications, and supporting the bank's income department. The job's substantive requirements — customer service experience, familiarity with Microsoft Office, and experience analysing income sources — have no apparent connection to ethnicity. Scotiabank nonetheless structured the posting to accept only applicants who identify based on their first nations, métis, or inuit ethnicity, effectively barring all other candidates from consideration regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are categorically excluded from consideration for this position based solely on their ethnicity, not their skills, experience, or character. This denies individuals the opportunity to be evaluated on their own merits and treats ethnicity as a determining factor in employment decisions. It establishes a precedent within a major financial institution that demographic classification is an acceptable basis for hiring, normalising the practice of sorting job applicants by immutable traits rather than their individual abilities and demonstrated experience.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a Financial Advisor position in Ontario that explicitly required applicants to self-identify as first nations, métis, or inuit, restricting the role based on ethnicity. Candidates who do not identify with these groups were excluded from consideration regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Financial Advisor position across numerous Ontario locations under the title "First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Financial Advisor - Ontario Region" (Requisition ID 173537). The job posting listed as a formal requirement that applicants "Self-Identify as First Nations, Metis, or Inuit." This requirement appeared alongside standard professional qualifications such as communication skills, client relationship ability, and the capacity to meet business goals. The ethnic self-identification requirement was presented as a prerequisite equal in standing to the professional competencies listed. The posting covered a wide range of Ontario communities including Sudbury, Timmins, Sault Ste. Marie, Peterborough, Niagara Falls, and many others. By making ethnic identity a stated requirement, the posting explicitly excluded anyone who does not self-identify within these categories from applying for the role, irrespective of their skills, experience, or suitability for the position.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are denied the opportunity to apply for employment based solely on their ethnicity rather than their abilities, character, or demonstrated competencies. This practice reduces individuals to demographic categories and evaluates them on the basis of ancestry rather than merit. It normalizes institutional sorting of people by ethnic identity in hiring, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be considered as an individual. The posting also sends a message that certain jobs are reserved for people of particular backgrounds, which entrenches demographic division in the workplace rather than fostering a shared civic environment where everyone competes on equal footing.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a collections advisor role at its Anjou, Quebec office restricted to applicants of first nations, Métis, or Inuit ethnicity. Limiting a job opportunity to people of a specific ethnicity excludes all other candidates on the basis of an immutable characteristic.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Collections Advisor position for its Scotia Dealer Advantage division, based in Anjou, Quebec, under the title "First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Collections Advisor - Scotia Dealer Advantage - Montreal/Anjou, QC." The job posting's title makes clear that the position was restricted to applicants of first nations, Métis, or Inuit ethnicity. The role itself — conducting payment collections, managing delinquent accounts, and providing customer service — has no apparent connection to the applicant's ethnicity. The qualifications listed focus on bilingual communication skills, experience in collections or customer service, and familiarity with standard office software. There is no functional or operational reason stated in the posting that would require a candidate to be of any particular ethnicity. Despite this, Scotiabank structured the posting as ethnicity-specific, effectively barring anyone who does not identify with those ethnic backgrounds from being considered.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from a job opportunity solely on the basis of their ethnicity, regardless of their skills, experience, or character. This practice reduces individuals to a demographic label and denies them consideration as unique persons with relevant abilities. It entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by ethnicity rather than evaluating them on individual merit, normalising the use of immutable characteristics as gatekeeping criteria in employment. It also sends a broader message that a person's ethnic background — rather than their demonstrated abilities — is a legitimate basis for institutional decisions.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a Small Business Advisor position for Ontario that required applicants to self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit, making ethnicity a formal prerequisite for employment.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Small Business Advisor role covering multiple Ontario locations, from Sudbury and Timmins to Niagara Falls and Guelph. The job posting listed, as the first and most prominent requirement, that applicants must "Self-Identify as First Nations, Metis, or Inuit." This made a person's ethnicity a mandatory condition of eligibility, ahead of any skills, qualifications, or experience. All other listed requirements — communication skills, post-secondary credentials, a valid driver's licence, relationship-building ability, and knowledge of the small business market — were secondary to this ethnic criterion. Applicants who did not belong to these ethnic groups were excluded from consideration regardless of their qualifications or capacity to serve the role.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are turned away from employment at one of Canada's largest banks solely because of their ethnicity, not because of any shortcoming in their abilities, character, or experience. The practice reduces people to a demographic label at the very first step of the hiring process, treating ethnicity as more determinative of a person's suitability than their actual skills or demonstrated record. This normalises the institutional use of ethnic criteria as a gatekeeping tool in employment, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It also signals to excluded applicants that their worth to the institution is contingent on their ancestry, which is contrary to the recognition of singular individual dignity.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a job listing for an Assistant Manager role in Ontario that required applicants to self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit. All other candidates were excluded from consideration based on their ethnicity.

The posting listed a range of professional qualifications including post-secondary education in financial planning, a mutual funds licence, and management experience. Among the listed requirements, applicants were required to self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit. This requirement was presented as a formal condition of eligibility on equal footing with professional qualifications. The position covered dozens of Ontario locations including Sudbury, Timmins, Sault Ste. Marie, Peterborough, Guelph, Niagara Falls, and others. Candidates who did not identify with these ethnic categories were ineligible for the role regardless of their skills, experience, or character.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from employment consideration solely on the basis of their ethnicity, not their abilities, character, or demonstrated performance. This practice embeds ethnic categorisation into hiring decisions, treating ethnicity as a qualification rather than evaluating people as individuals. It normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into ethnic groups and making consequential decisions about their lives on that basis. It also signals to all employees and applicants that advancement within the institution is tied to demographic identity, undermining the principle that people deserve to be assessed on their individual merits.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a job opening for an IT Accommodation Specialist that was explicitly restricted to applicants identifying as First Nations, Métis, or Inuit, excluding all other candidates on the basis of their ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a 12-month contract position for an IT Accommodation Specialist within its Scotia Digital division, based in Toronto, Ontario. The job posting, titled 'First Nations/Métis/Inuit - IT Accommodation Specialist, Scotia Digital (12 months)', restricted eligibility to individuals who identify as First Nations, Métis, or Inuit. The posting stated it was 'posted pursuant to Scotiabank's Special Program under the Employment Equity Act and Canadian Human Rights Act,' which Scotiabank used as the legal basis for limiting who could apply. The role itself involved supporting workplace accommodation policies related to assistive technology — a function with no apparent connection to an applicant's ethnicity. All other candidates, regardless of their qualifications, skills, or professional experience, were categorically excluded from consideration based solely on their ethnic background.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are barred from applying for and obtaining employment at Scotiabank based solely on their ethnic background rather than their abilities, experience, or character. This institutional practice reduces people to demographic categories and denies them individual evaluation on the merits of their skills and qualifications. It entrenches the habit of sorting job candidates by ethnicity, normalizing the idea that a person's ancestry is a relevant criterion for employment in roles where it has no bearing on job performance. It also excludes individuals who may share the same genuine commitment to the role's goals, simply because they do not belong to a designated ethnic group.

incidents.short_description: Nunavut Tunngavik Incorporated revoked the enrolment of twin sisters from the Nunavut Agreement beneficiary list after determining they were enrolled based on fraudulent adoption claims. The sisters had received benefits including scholarships and business certifications designated for indigenous people.
incidents.description_of_event: Nunavut Tunngavik Incorporated, the organization responsible for enrolling Inuit under the Nunavut Agreement, removed twin sisters Nadya and Amira Gill from its beneficiary list on April 6, 2023. The sisters were initially enrolled in 2016 after their mother claimed she adopted them from an Inuk woman. The alleged birth mother's family disputed this claim and stated they have no biological relationship to the twins. An investigation by the Qikiqtani Inuit Association found a separate 2018 enrolment application from the sisters' mother claiming she had been adopted by two Inuit from Iqaluit, which was rejected due to lack of supporting documentation. The sisters' older brother stated they share the same biological parents and were not raised with any specific culture. Between the time of their enrolment and its revocation, the sisters received scholarships, bursaries, and other benefits designated for indigenous students at Queen's University. They also launched a business certified as indigenous-owned and received recognition in that capacity. Both Nunavut Tunngavik Incorporated and the Qikiqtani Inuit Association called for RCMP investigation into alleged fraud.
incidents.expected_harm: This incident illustrates how eligibility requirements based on claims of indigenous ancestry create systems where access to benefits, educational opportunities, and business certifications depends on proving demographic category membership rather than evaluating individuals based on their character, abilities, or demonstrated needs. Such systems incentivize fraudulent claims and require institutions to investigate and adjudicate the authenticity of people's ancestral backgrounds, reducing individuals to their purported ethnic origins. The enrolment revocation process itself demonstrates how institutions must surveil and verify demographic classifications, directing resources toward policing identity categories rather than assessing individual merit or need. When benefits are allocated based on ethnic status rather than individual circumstances, the system creates perverse incentives for misrepresentation and breeds distrust within communities. Genuine indigenous people face increased scrutiny of their identities, while institutions must develop ever-more-robust verification processes that treat people as members of demographic categories first and as individuals second. This approach undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their unique qualities and demonstrated circumstances.

quotes.text: The Nunavut Agreement is the largest Indigenous land claim settlement in Canada. Registering under the agreement grants Inuit access to benefits and rights, including scholarships, health care and use of Inuit-owned lands.
quotes.rationale: Explains the benefits system that allocates resources based on ethnic category membership rather than individual need or merit

incidents.short_description: The Elementary Teachers Federation of Ontario offers a teacher qualification course that frames students based on their sexual orientation as a distinct group requiring specialized pedagogical approaches, rather than treating all students as individuals deserving equal consideration.
incidents.description_of_event: The Elementary Teachers Federation of Ontario (ETFO), through its Additional Qualification (AQ) course provider, offers a course titled 'Teaching LGBTQ Students' at a cost of $685. The course was offered in Spring 2023 (April 3 to June 16, 2023) and is available again in 2026. The course trains Ontario teachers to apply specific pedagogies and practices organized around students' sexual orientation and gender identity as defining characteristics. The course description states that candidates will develop theoretical understanding to 'apply, implement and assess programs and/or practices related to Teaching LGBTQ Students,' and that a significant element of the course involves 'ongoing critical examination of personal biases and privilege.' Rather than developing teachers' capacity to treat every student as a distinct individual with unique needs, the course structures its entire pedagogical framework around categorizing students by their sexual orientation. The course is open to any Ontario College of Teachers member in good standing with a permanent teaching certificate and basic qualifications in any division.
incidents.expected_harm: When teachers are trained to categorize students by their sexual orientation as a primary lens for pedagogy, students are evaluated and approached through a demographic label rather than as individuals with unique characters, abilities, and needs. This reduces students to a group identity and normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable characteristics. It also creates conditions where students who do not wish to be identified or treated according to a demographic category find that institutional processes have already assigned them one. The framing of a specialized course around a category of students reinforces the idea that certain students require a fundamentally different kind of teaching based on who they are rather than what they individually need, undermining the principle of singular worth and common humanity.

incidents.short_description: The City of Calgary released a community anti-racism strategy that frames city services, hiring, and governance reform around skin colour and ethnicity, treating these traits as the central lens for evaluating and redesigning how the city serves residents.
incidents.description_of_event:
In April 2023, the City of Calgary published the Community Anti-Racism Action Strategy (titled "Weaving a Shared Path Forward for an Anti-Racist Calgary"), developed to inform the city's Anti-Racism Strategic Plan 2023–2027. The strategy was co-developed with external consultants Habitus Consulting Collective and ActionDignity, an Anti-Racism Action Committee, and a circle of elders and knowledge keepers, and was built on community engagement with over 2,500 Calgarians conducted between March 2021 and August 2022.

The strategy proposes sweeping changes to how the City of Calgary hires staff, designs services, allocates resources, and structures governance — with skin colour and ethnicity as the primary organising criteria throughout. Key recommended actions include: increasing the representation of people based on skin colour and ethnicity in City leadership, front-line, and management roles; tracking and publishing employment demographic data across the City; collecting racially disaggregated data on service access and outcomes; and designing an anti-racism and equity office to embed race-based outcomes as a top corporate priority. The strategy explicitly calls for services to be "informed or led and designed by" people selected on the basis of their skin colour and ethnicity.

The strategy's engagement process itself was designed to preferentially reach and centre people based on their skin colour and ethnicity, with community brokers deployed specifically to access those populations. The document frames the City's role not merely as addressing discriminatory conduct by individuals, but as becoming an "anti-racist organization" that restructures its internal hiring, policy, and program design through a race-based lens.
incidents.expected_harm: When a municipal government commits to evaluating, hiring, and designing services through the lens of skin colour and ethnicity, it embeds demographic categorisation into the machinery of city government. Every resident then encounters institutions that sort people by immutable traits rather than individual need, ability, or circumstance. Staff are hired, retained, and promoted based in part on skin colour and ethnicity rather than on merit alone. Service design is shaped by the demographic identity of those leading it rather than by the needs of any individual who walks through the door. This normalises the idea that a person's skin colour determines what services they need, what voices should design those services, and who should lead them — the opposite of treating people as individuals with singular worth. It also displaces residents who fall outside the defined demographic categories, communicating that the city's institutions view them as less relevant to the reform agenda, and entrenches division rather than fostering the shared civic belonging that should unite all Calgarians.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of Finance Canada
incidents.short_description: The federal government's Budget 2023 Impacts Report systematically evaluates the effects of budget measures by sorting Canadians into demographic categories based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other immutable traits. This approach, embedded throughout official government policy analysis, frames Canadians as members of group identities rather than as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Government of Canada's Department of Finance Canada published the Budget 2023 Impacts Report, which provides a mandatory Gender-based Analysis Plus (GBA Plus) assessment of every new measure in the federal budget. The report, produced under the Canadian Gender Budgeting Act 2018, evaluates the impacts of each budget policy not primarily on individuals based on their circumstances and needs, but on named demographic groups defined by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation.

Across dozens of budget measures — ranging from affordable housing and dental care to clean energy investments and apprenticeship training — the government consistently identifies and names groups such as 'Black and racialized people,' 'Indigenous people,' '2SLGBTQI+ people,' and women as distinct categories of Canadians whose experiences of government policy are expected to differ based on those immutable characteristics. This categorisation is applied uniformly and systematically throughout the report, embedding demographic group identity as the primary lens through which the government interprets who benefits from — and who is disadvantaged by — federal spending decisions.

The report also directs certain programs to be designed with these demographic groups explicitly in mind. For example, the 988 Suicide Prevention Line's responsive approach states that 'tailored supports' will be developed for 'priority populations' defined by skin colour and sexual orientation. Housing strategy programs explicitly prioritise access and design based on similar demographic categories. The framework thus moves beyond simply observing who is statistically likely to benefit, and embeds demographic categorisation into the design and delivery of public services and programs.

The report is an archived document dated May 2023, covering the 2023 federal budget tabled by the Department of Finance Canada.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government systematically evaluates its citizens through the lens of skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and other immutable traits, it entrenches the idea that individuals' relationship to the state is determined by their group membership rather than their individual circumstances, character, or demonstrated needs. This normalises the practice of sorting Canadians into demographic categories in the design and delivery of public programs, causing real people to be seen first as members of a group and only secondarily — if at all — as individuals. Programs shaped by this logic distribute benefits and design services based on demographic proxies, which displaces attention from the actual needs and merits of individual Canadians. The framework also risks reinforcing social divisions by signalling that Canadians' experiences of government are fundamentally defined by immutable characteristics, undermining a sense of shared civic belonging and common humanity. Over time, institutionalising this approach across the full breadth of federal spending embeds identity-based categorisation deep into the machinery of government, making it progressively harder for individuals to be evaluated on their own terms.

quotes.text: this Impact Report provides a summary of the gender and diversity impacts for each new measure in this budget. Summaries of impacts are based on the Gender-based Analysis Plus submitted with budget proposals.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the foundational framework of the report: every budget measure is assessed through a demographic group lens, as required by government policy.

quotes.text: Supporting new affordable housing construction will benefit those experiencing housing affordability challenges, including particular groups that are prioritized under the National Housing Strategy such as persons with disabilities, Black and racialized people, Indigenous people, seniors, women and children fleeing domestic violence, and those in core housing need.
quotes.rationale: Illustrates how the government explicitly names and prioritises groups based on skin colour and ethnicity within housing policy, rather than addressing need on an individual basis.

quotes.text: Work will be undertaken to expand language availability beyond English and French and to develop community appropriate services to provide tailored supports for priority populations (e.g., Indigenous people, Black and racialized people, 2SLGBTQI+ people, and newcomers).
quotes.rationale: Shows that the government moves beyond analysis into program design, explicitly directing services to be tailored based on demographic group identity including skin colour and sexual orientation.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Safety Canada
incidents.short_description: Public Safety Canada announced two correctional programs that restrict eligibility based on ethnicity and skin colour, directing millions in federal funding exclusively to organisations serving offenders sorted by these characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: On 21 March 2023, the Minister of Public Safety, Marco Mendicino, announced two federal initiatives framed around addressing statistical overrepresentation in the criminal justice system. The first, the Indigenous Community Corrections Initiative (ICCI), directs $5.21 million in 2023/2024 and $12 million ongoing to organisations that rehabilitate or reintegrate offenders based on their ethnicity, with eligibility limited to those serving indigenous offenders. The second initiative involves the Correctional Service of Canada (CSC) seeking Expressions of Interest exclusively from community groups, experts, and organisations defined by the skin colour and ethnicity of those they serve — specifically groups serving people the government categorises as black and racialized. Both programs determine eligibility and funding allocation on the basis of offenders' ethnicity or skin colour rather than individual needs, circumstances, or the demonstrated effectiveness of the services provided. The announcement was made through an official Government of Canada news release and attributed to Public Safety Canada.
incidents.expected_harm: Funding and rehabilitation services are allocated based on the ethnicity and skin colour of offenders rather than individual need or the merit of the programming. This means that an offender whose needs may be identical to those of another receives access to different resources and community supports solely because of immutable characteristics. Organisations providing effective corrections and reintegration services are included or excluded from federal funding streams based on the demographic characteristics of the people they serve, rather than the quality or reach of their work. The framework normalises the institutional practice of sorting Canadians — including those at their most vulnerable, seeking reintegration — into categories defined by ethnicity and skin colour, reinforcing the idea that a person's background determines what support they deserve. It also forecloses funding opportunities for organisations that serve offenders as individuals regardless of ethnicity, undermining the principle that every person possesses singular worth irrespective of their demographic characteristics.

incidents.short_description: The University of Guelph hosted a sensual embodiment workshop that was explicitly restricted to attendees based on their skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation. People who did not identify within these demographic categories were excluded from the event.
incidents.description_of_event:
On March 20, 2023, the University of Guelph hosted an event titled 'Live Your Best Sensual Life: A Sensual Embodiment Practice' at room UC332 on campus, facilitated by Natasha "Natty Love" Lawrence. The event was organized by the university's Cultural Diversity, OUTline, and Sexual and Gender Diversity campus organizations. The event involved embodiment and sensuality practices, and offered free refreshments and prize giveaways to participants.

Attendance at the event was explicitly restricted based on participants' skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation. Promotional materials stated clearly that the event was exclusive to individuals who identified as belonging to specific demographic categories. University-affiliated organizations hosted the event and promoted it through the university's official campus community platform, meaning the exclusionary restrictions carried institutional weight. Members of the university community who did not identify within the specified demographic categories were barred from attending, regardless of their individual interest, circumstances, or needs.
incidents.expected_harm: University students, staff, and community members are turned away from a campus event based on their skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation — immutable or deeply personal characteristics that have nothing to do with their individual interest in the subject matter or their suitability to participate. The university, through its own affiliated organizations and its official campus platform, signals that access to programming and community is determined by demographic identity rather than individual needs or interests. This normalizes the institutional practice of sorting people into categories and granting or denying access accordingly. It undermines a sense of shared civic belonging on campus, sending a message to excluded individuals that the institution views them primarily through demographic labels rather than as full members of the university community. Students learn that demographic categorization is an acceptable basis for exclusion, entrenching division rather than building the common understanding and shared community that a university should foster.

incidents.short_description: The Alberta NDP announced a promise to create a school curriculum specifically for Somali students if elected. The promise categorizes students by ethnicity and proposes publicly funded education programming built around that demographic identity.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 20, 2023, Sarah Hoffman, a prominent Alberta NDP figure, announced via Twitter that the Alberta NDP would, if elected, create a dedicated Somali curriculum for Alberta schools. The announcement was framed as a critique of the incumbent United Conservative Party government for not having prioritized this during their term. The post stated that 'Alberta's diversity is our greatest strength and should be reflected in our curriculum.' The promise envisions a curriculum defined and organized around the ethnic identity of a specific group of students, rather than a curriculum designed around individual educational needs, universal learning goals, or shared civic content accessible to all students.
incidents.expected_harm: Designing curriculum around the ethnicity of specific student groups embeds the institutional practice of categorizing students by their background rather than their individual learning needs or shared civic belonging. Students are sorted and addressed as members of ethnic demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals. This approach signals to all students that their ethnic background is a defining feature by which the education system organizes and delivers programming, reinforcing division rather than common understanding. Students outside the targeted ethnic group are implicitly excluded from curriculum content designed for others, while those within the group risk being reduced to their ethnicity rather than recognized for their individual character, interests, and abilities. The practice normalizes ethnicity-based partitioning in public institutions and undermines the shared educational experience that builds civic solidarity.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Broadcasting Corporation
incidents.short_description: CBC linked sensitive personal information — including employees' religion and sexual orientation — from a voluntary diversity survey to individual HR files without employees' knowledge or consent. The federal privacy commissioner launched a probe into the matter.
incidents.description_of_event:
CBC and Radio-Canada conducted a voluntary internal survey called a "cultural census," asking employees to provide sensitive personal details including their religion and sexual orientation. Management communications to employees explicitly described the survey as "completely confidential" and indicated the data would be used only for anonymised, aggregate statistical analysis. Employees were not told that their responses would be linked to their individual HR profiles.

Employees later discovered their personal information — including details about their sexual orientation and religion — had been loaded into their personal profiles on a new cloud-based HR platform called Workday. Some employees reported they had not disclosed this information even to family or friends, and felt surprised and betrayed when they found it in their employer's records.

The Canadian Media Guild, which represents CBC employees, urged management to remove the personal data from the Workday portals, expressing frustration at the lack of firm action. Following media reporting by the National Post, the federal privacy commissioner's office opened a probe and sought further information from CBC. CBC's spokesperson said the corporation would cooperate if the commissioner determined further action was necessary, and CBC updated its intranet to clarify how the data is used. However, as of the time of reporting, the personal data remained in employee files.
incidents.expected_harm: Employees who provided sensitive personal information under the assurance of confidentiality have had that information recorded in institutional files without their consent. This exposes individuals to the risk of being evaluated, categorised, or treated differently by their employer on the basis of deeply personal characteristics — including their religion and sexual orientation — rather than their abilities, character, and work. Employees who had not disclosed their sexual orientation to their own families now have that information held in a corporate database, stripping them of control over their own private lives. The breach of the stated terms of the survey destroys employee trust in institutional data collection processes. The practice also exemplifies the broader harm of institutions systematically cataloguing individuals by demographic and identity categories, reducing people to sets of personal attributes rather than treating them as whole persons deserving of privacy and individual dignity.

incidents.short_description: Ottawa Public Health restricts access to its sexual health clinic's STBBI testing services based on patients' ethnicity and sexual orientation, among other criteria, meaning most Ottawa residents cannot access testing at the clinic regardless of individual need or risk.
incidents.description_of_event: Ottawa Public Health's Sexual Health Clinic, located at 179 Clarence Street in Ottawa, limits STBBI (sexually transmitted and blood borne infection) testing appointments to residents who belong to specific listed groups. Alongside criteria based on age, occupation, and drug use, the clinic explicitly lists membership in indigenous communities and African, Caribbean, and black communities as qualifying criteria for testing access. Membership in 2SLGBTQ+ communities and men who have sex with men is also listed as a qualifying criterion. The clinic's policy, documented in a screenshot dated March 16, 2023, and confirmed in the current web page, directs residents who do not meet these eligibility criteria to contact their primary care provider or visit a walk-in clinic instead. The policy is framed using the institution's own language of 'equity-denied groups,' defined on the page as people who face barriers to access due to systemic discrimination. The practical effect is that a person's ethnicity or sexual orientation determines whether they can access public health testing at this facility.
incidents.expected_harm: Residents of Ottawa who do not belong to the listed ethnic or sexual orientation categories are turned away from publicly funded STBBI testing at this clinic and directed elsewhere, regardless of their individual health circumstances or risk level. The policy embeds ethnicity and sexual orientation as gatekeeping criteria for a public health service, meaning individuals are evaluated not on their personal medical situation but on their demographic characteristics. This approach reduces people to group membership rather than recognising them as individuals with distinct health needs. It also normalises the institutional practice of sorting people into categories based on immutable traits to determine who receives public services. People who fall outside the listed groups but have genuine testing needs face additional barriers to care, potentially delaying diagnosis and treatment.

incidents.short_description: The Durham Catholic District School Board ran a march break leadership camp restricted to students based on their skin colour, excluding grade 7 and 8 students from participating on the basis of their race.
incidents.description_of_event: The Durham Catholic District School Board offered a half-day march break leadership camp for grade 7 and 8 students, held daily from March 13 to March 17, 2023, at Archbishop Denis O'Connor Catholic High School in Ajax, Ontario. The camp ran from 9:00 a.m. to 12:00 noon each day and was facilitated by Morlan Washington and Chevoy Hickey of Coach Advocates for Black Students (CABS). The programming included recreational activities aimed at building leadership skills, fitness, peer relationships, and community. Snacks were provided. Eligibility for the camp was explicitly restricted to students who identify as Black, meaning students were admitted or excluded based on their skin colour rather than their interests, abilities, or needs. This was a racially segregated school board programme, organized and offered through a publicly funded Catholic district school board.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are excluded from a school board-sponsored leadership development programme solely on the basis of their skin colour. The camp denies non-qualifying students access to leadership skills training, fitness programming, and peer relationship building during the march break. By restricting participation based on a demographic category, the school board treats students not as individuals with unique qualities and interests but as members of a racial group. This normalizes institutional sorting of children by skin colour and undermines a shared sense of school community and civic belonging. It sends a message to all students — those included and those excluded — that their skin colour determines what opportunities they are entitled to access. The practice entrenches rather than reduces division among young people at a formative stage in their development.

quotes.text: For Grade 7 & 8 Students who identify as Black
quotes.rationale: This is the explicit eligibility restriction that limits camp participation to students based on their skin colour, establishing the racially segregated nature of the programme.

quotes.text: This half day camp is being offered daily over the March break to a limited number of Grade 7 & 8 students of the Durham Catholic District School Board.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the camp is a Durham Catholic District School Board programme, establishing institutional responsibility for the racially restricted event.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Human Rights Commission issued a public statement framing racism in education as a problem defined by and belonging to one skin-colour group, rather than treating discrimination as a universal harm to individual dignity. The statement commits the OHRC to developing an action plan organised around this demographic lens.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 8, 2023, the Ontario Human Rights Commission (OHRC) published a statement on its website addressing racism in Ontario's publicly funded education system. Rather than framing discrimination as a violation of individual dignity that can affect any student, the statement categorises students, communities, and professionals by skin colour, treating the problem as one belonging to a specific demographic group. The statement announces that the OHRC will conduct community engagement, research, and consultations with the education sector over the coming months, culminating in an action plan and practical guidance for schools. Chief Commissioner Patricia DeGuire is quoted committing the organisation to hear specifically from community voices and professionals identified by their skin colour, and to develop solutions focused on students identified by that same demographic category. The statement also references the OHRC's strategic plan, which frames accountability for discrimination in education through a lens of improving outcomes for students grouped by demographic identity rather than addressing discrimination as a shared human concern.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government body responsible for human rights frames discrimination in education as a problem defined by and reserved for one demographic group, it reinforces the practice of sorting people into categories based on immutable traits rather than treating each student as an individual deserving equal dignity. Students and families not belonging to the designated group receive the institutional message that their experiences of mistreatment in school are categorically less important. The approach normalises the idea that institutions should evaluate and respond to people based on their skin colour, rather than their individual circumstances and needs. It forecloses the opportunity to build a shared civic understanding that mistreating any student based on assumptions about their background is wrong, and undermines the common purpose that could unite educators, parents, and students around universal principles of individual worth.

incidents.short_description: The Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board published a Spring 2023 mental health update that singled out students based on their skin colour, framing mental health needs and access to services through a demographic lens rather than treating all students as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: In March 2023, the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board published its 'We Help Update – Spring 2023,' a mental health communications document distributed to students, families, and caregivers. The update was timed to coincide with Social Work Week and was also explicitly tied to 'Black Mental Health Week' (March 6–12). Within the document, the school board asserted that certain systemic conditions in the mental health system create barriers specifically affecting youth based on their skin colour, and that these youth face particular difficulties accessing what the board described as 'culturally affirming and responsive mental health services.' The board promoted a dedicated set of supports and resources labelled specifically for students based on their skin colour, including a referral to the Hamilton Urban Core Substance Abuse Program for African Canadian and Caribbean Youth. The board also promoted the AFFIRM program — a Cognitive-Based Therapy group restricted to Two Spirit and LGBTQIA+ youth — as a treatment option for coping with stress and anxiety related to experiences of discrimination.
