The communications document thus directed different students toward different mental health resources based on their skin colour and sexual orientation rather than their individual circumstances, symptoms, or needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are sorted and directed toward mental health resources based on their skin colour and sexual orientation rather than their individual needs, symptoms, or circumstances. This approach embeds the practice of categorizing young people by demographic characteristics into a school board's core wellness communications, sending students the message that institutions view them primarily through the lens of their immutable traits rather than as distinct individuals. Students whose skin colour or identity does not match a designated program receive no equivalent acknowledgement of potential barriers, while those who are designated receive support framed around their group membership rather than their personal situation. This normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories as the primary means of addressing individual wellbeing, undermining the recognition of each student's singular worth and discouraging the development of a shared school community where all students are understood to face common human challenges.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board hosted a literacy professional development event framed exclusively around the skin colour of students and the authors featured, separating educational content by demographic category rather than treating it as relevant to all students.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board's Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement hosted a 'Show Me' Literacy Series session on March 7, 2023, from 4:00 to 5:30 PM. The session, promoted by a TDSB staff member on social media, was directed at Grade 7–12 English teachers and focused on using what the organizers called 'Black centred narratives' to increase representation of what was described as 'Black lived experiences.' The session featured works including the play 'Master Harold and the Boys' by Athol Fugard and the novel 'Brother' by David Chariandy, presented by Joseph Galiwango and Dayo Baiyewu. Participants were offered a complimentary copy of either 'Brother' or 'Black Writers Matter.' The event was organized and promoted specifically through the lens of sorting literary content and teaching practice by the skin colour of authors and the demographic identity of students, rather than presenting the works as literature of broad human relevance.
incidents.expected_harm: Teachers are directed to approach literacy education by categorizing literature and student experiences according to skin colour, embedding the practice of demographic sorting into classroom instruction. Students are implicitly divided into groups based on their skin colour, with certain narratives framed as belonging to or being primarily relevant for students of a particular background. This undermines the recognition of each student as an individual whose engagement with literature is shaped by their own curiosity, imagination, and experiences rather than their demographic category. Literary works of genuine human depth are reduced to instruments of identity representation rather than being offered as windows into the shared human condition. The institutional framing normalizes the idea that teachers should curate content based on the skin colour of their students, entrenching demographic categorization in everyday educational practice and foreclosing opportunities to build shared understanding across all students.

quotes.text: Hey Gr 7-12 English teachers, join us Tuesday for practical ideas on using Black centred narratives to increase authentic representation of Black lived experiences
quotes.rationale: This promotional message directly shows the event was framed around sorting literacy content by the skin colour of those whose experiences are represented, rather than presenting literature as universally relevant.

quotes.text: Using Black centred narratives to increase authentic representation of Black lived experiences
quotes.rationale: The official event title confirms the session's organizing principle is demographic categorization of literary content by skin colour.

quotes.text: English/Literacy, The Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement
quotes.rationale: Identifies the TDSB unit responsible for organizing the event, confirming institutional involvement and the demographic framing built into the centre's mandate.

incidents.institution_name: Toronto District School Board > Forest Hill Junior and Senior Public School
incidents.short_description: Forest Hill Public School's parent advisory council invited families to an online workshop on raising children to be 'anti-racist,' led by a self-described abolitionist and anti-oppression consultant, and offered a $25 gift card as an incentive for attending.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 27, 2023, the Forest Hill Public School School Advisory Council (SAC), operating under the Toronto District School Board, sent a message to all school families advertising an online workshop titled 'Raising Anti-Racist Kids,' scheduled for March 2, 2023, from 6:00 to 8:00 p.m. All parents and guardians were invited to attend. To encourage participation, the school offered those who attended a chance to win a $25 Amazon gift card through a draw. The workshop was to be led by Rania El Mugammar, described in the invitation as an artist, liberation educator, abolitionist, anti-oppression consultant, multidisciplinary performer, speaker, and published writer. The school's promotional materials highlighted that the presenter's work explores themes including identity, womanhood, skin colour, exile, migration, belonging, and gender. The workshop invitation was shared publicly by commentator Jonathan Kay, who noted that some parents viewed the gift card incentive as evidence that the school needed to resort to inducements to attract attendance to what they characterised as yet another ideologically driven session.
incidents.expected_harm: Inviting parents to a workshop framed around a specific ideological approach to discussing skin colour and identity — and offering financial incentives to attend — places school families in a position where their participation in school community life is tied to engagement with one particular viewpoint on contested social questions. The presenter's described framework, rooted in abolitionism and anti-oppression ideology, goes beyond factual education and advances a specific political perspective. Parents who hold different views are nonetheless invited into a setting designed to promote this outlook. Rather than fostering open, evidence-based dialogue, this approach uses the school's trusted position in families' lives to promote a particular ideological lens. It treats parents not as individuals with their own considered views, but as a group requiring instruction on how to raise their children through the lens of demographic categories. This undermines the kind of open, reasoned discourse and shared civic belonging that a school community should foster.

quotes.text: Rania "explores themes of identity, womanhood, Blackness, flight, exile, migration, belonging, gender, sexuality and beyond."
quotes.rationale: Shows the thematic framing of the presenter's work centres on identity and demographic categories, indicating the workshop approaches child-rearing through an identity-group lens rather than in terms of individual character and dignity.

incidents.short_description: Simon Fraser University's job posting for a tenure-track professor required all applicants to submit a written statement describing their past or potential contributions to equity, diversity, and inclusion, effectively screening candidates on ideological commitment rather than academic merit alone.
incidents.description_of_event: The School of Communication at Simon Fraser University advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Gaming, Media and Communication with a review of applications beginning March 1, 2023. As part of the required application package, every candidate was asked to submit a statement — up to one page — describing their past or potential contributions to equity, diversity, and inclusion through teaching, mentoring, research, or service. This requirement is mandatory, not optional, and sits alongside the curriculum vitae, research statement, and teaching dossier as a condition of a complete application. The posting also states that SFU will advance the interests of what it calls underrepresented members of the workforce — specifying people based on their ethnicity, disability, sex, and sexual orientation — and explicitly encourages candidates from those groups to apply. Taken together, the posting conditions hiring consideration on a candidate's demonstrated alignment with a particular ideological framework, and signals that certain demographic characteristics will advantage an applicant.
incidents.expected_harm: Requiring a written ideological statement as a formal application requirement means that candidates are evaluated not solely on their scholarly ability, teaching excellence, and research potential, but on their expressed commitment to a specific set of institutional values. Academics who do not share or choose not to perform alignment with those values face a material disadvantage in the hiring process, regardless of their qualifications. This functions as a litmus test that narrows the range of intellectual perspectives hired into the institution. By also signalling that candidates of certain demographic backgrounds are particularly encouraged to apply, the posting further reduces the likelihood that all individuals are assessed purely on their individual merits and circumstances. The cumulative effect is a hiring process that sorts applicants by demographic characteristics and ideological conformity rather than by the singular worth and distinct abilities of each person.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: The Department of National Defence published an anti-racism toolkit that categorises military and civilian personnel by skin colour and assigns them differentiated roles, responsibilities, and moral standing based on those categories.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Department of National Defence (DND) and the Canadian Armed Forces (CAF), through the Director Anti-Racism Implementation and Chief Professional Conduct and Culture, published an anti-racism toolkit on Canada.ca. The toolkit, dated February 2023, is presented as mandatory guidance for the entire Defence Team and includes guides, tip sheets, and activities intended to shape workplace culture.

The toolkit divides personnel into groups defined by skin colour and instructs them to relate to one another on that basis. It introduces the concept of 'white privilege' as a framework through which all personnel must evaluate their own standing, and asks leaders to hold teams accountable to goals framed around these groupings. The 'Essential Questions to Apply an Anti-Racism Lens' checklist directs personnel to consider 'White privilege and how historically the dominant group has benefitted from this privilege' as a standard planning step.

The terminology sheet distributed to all personnel defines 'systemic racism' as the embedding of 'whiteness and white superiority' into institutional policies, and introduces 'white fragility' — defined as an intolerance of racial stress specific to people based on their skin colour — as standard vocabulary for workplace discussions. Personnel are instructed to 'validate and accept the experiences of your racially marginalized colleagues,' a directive that pre-determines which colleagues occupy which roles in workplace conversations based on skin colour.

The 'Racism or Allyship?' resource classifies all personnel into one of three moral categories — racist, ally, or anti-racist — with the characteristics of each category partly defined by one's attitude toward group-based measures and whether one 'supports reconciliation efforts.' This framing treats agreement with the toolkit's own premises as a condition of moral standing within the institution.

The myth-busting section dismisses objections to employment equity as myths requiring rebuttal, characterising concerns about demographic-based hiring preferences as factually wrong rather than as legitimate points of view to be considered through open discourse.

The toolkit was published by a federal government department and applies across the entire Defence Team, encompassing both DND civilian employees and Canadian Armed Forces members.
incidents.expected_harm:
Personnel are sorted into categories defined by skin colour and assigned differentiated responsibilities, privileges, and moral standing on that basis. Individuals are not evaluated by their character, abilities, or conduct but instead by their demographic group membership. This institutionalises the very logic it claims to oppose — assessing people primarily through the lens of their immutable characteristics rather than as distinct individuals.

Personnel who raise good-faith questions about these approaches are pre-emptively classified as expressing 'myths' or, through the racism-allyship framework, risk being placed in the 'racist' category. This chills open and reasoned discourse within the institution and removes the space for intellectual honesty that is a foundation of a healthy workplace.

The toolkit's language assigns collective guilt to personnel based on skin colour — for example, through the concept of 'white fragility' — creating conditions for personal embarrassment, professional risk, and social pressure that flow directly from a person's demographic characteristics rather than anything they have said or done. This undermines individual dignity and autonomy.

By directing that conversations about discrimination be organised around racial groupings rather than around the shared principle that mistreating any individual is wrong, the toolkit entrenches division among colleagues and forecloses the possibility of building common understanding grounded in shared human dignity.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board's Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement launched a 'Pathways to Public Health' program for grades 10–12 students, restricting enrolment to those who self-identify based on their skin colour. Students in the same grades who do not share that identity were excluded from the program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board's Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement, in partnership with the University of Toronto's Dalla Lana School of Public Health, launched a 10-week program called 'Pathways to Public Health' for TDSB students in grades 10–12. The program, announced on February 26, 2023, was designed to explore pathways into health and health systems. Registration was open from February 28, with an information session on March 1 and the program beginning March 7, held at the Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement at 2239 Lawrence Ave. E in Scarborough. Eligibility for the program was explicitly restricted to students who self-identify based on their skin colour. Grades 10–12 students who do not share that identity were not invited to register, regardless of their interest in public health careers or individual circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: Students in grades 10–12 are sorted by a school board institution into eligible and ineligible groups based solely on their skin colour, denying some students access to a career-pathway program and the professional connections and academic opportunities it provides. Students excluded from the program receive a clear institutional message that their skin colour determines what programming is available to them, normalizing the idea that demographic categories rather than individual interest, ability, or need should govern access to educational enrichment. The practice reinforces the habit of dividing students by immutable characteristics, undermining the development of a shared civic community in secondary schools and foreclosing the cross-background collaboration that a public health program, by its very nature, would seem designed to foster.

incidents.short_description: Yale Secondary School in Abbotsford hosts a school club restricted to students based on their skin colour. The club operates on school grounds with the support of a teacher, and has been endorsed by B.C.'s minister of education.
incidents.description_of_event: A club called Black Connections HS operates at Yale Secondary School in Abbotsford, B.C., within the Abbotsford School District. The club was founded in 2021 by teacher Shayla Bird and has approximately 100 members, with around 60 students attending biweekly after-school meetings. The club is explicitly described as a space for students of a particular skin colour, offering mentorship, events, and discussions about identity. A professor at UBC runs a connected program called Black Futures, which brings high school students from the club together once a month. B.C.'s minister of education publicly endorsed the club, stating that "spaces like this can lead to a greater sense of belonging, identity, and community." The club operates as a racially segregated gathering within a publicly funded school, where access and belonging are determined by students' skin colour rather than shared interests, circumstances, or individual needs.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school organizes or hosts a club that admits students based on their skin colour, it sends a clear institutional message that skin colour is a meaningful and appropriate basis for separating young people. Students who do not share the designated skin colour are excluded from the group's mentorship, events, and support network. This normalizes the practice of sorting individuals into groups based on immutable characteristics, undermining the principle that every student deserves to be seen and evaluated as an individual. It also works against the development of a shared civic community within the school, teaching students that belonging is tied to demographic categories rather than common humanity. The endorsement of this arrangement by B.C.'s minister of education amplifies the harm by lending government authority to the practice of racially segregated student spaces in publicly funded schools.

incidents.short_description: Community pressure groups successfully lobbied the City of Beaconsfield to cancel a children's puppet show created and performed by a Black artist, citing the puppet's appearance. The cancellation suppressed an artist's work based on complaints about how a character looked, raising serious concerns about free expression and institutional capitulation to pressure campaigns.
incidents.description_of_event:
Franck Sylvestre, a Black artist of Martinican origin, created and has performed a children's puppet show — L'Incroyable Secret de Barbe Noire — for approximately 15 years. The central puppet was inspired by Sylvestre himself; he has described the character as reflecting his own physical features, with exaggeration as an intentional theatrical technique. In February 2023, during Black History Month, the show was scheduled to be performed in both Beaconsfield and Pointe-Claire in Montreal's West Island.

The West Island Black Community Association (WIBCA) and the Red Coalition held a press conference on February 24, 2023, denouncing the show and calling on both municipalities to cancel it. They characterised the puppet's appearance as evoking blackface imagery and described the show as racist and grotesque. Beaconsfield's mayor confirmed that council held a special caucus meeting and voted to cancel the performance scheduled for February 27, explicitly stating the decision was motivated by a desire to avoid controversy. Pointe-Claire initially refused to cancel and indicated it had attempted to arrange a meeting between Sylvestre and the community groups before the performance, but that no mutually available time was found.

Sylvestre defended his work, noting he had performed the show to diverse audiences over 15 years without incident, that the puppet was a self-portrait, and that cancellation set a harmful precedent for artistic freedom. He noted support from other Black artists and stated the WIBCA did not speak for the entire community. A member of Montreal's executive committee publicly supported Sylvestre on social media, stating that restricting him would be damaging to necessary democratic debate. The community groups also indicated they intended to seek cancellation of a March performance in Gatineau.
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions cancel artistic works in response to pressure campaigns organised along demographic lines, they substitute group complaint for individual artistic assessment. This suppresses free expression and artistic liberty, penalising an artist for how a self-portrait character appears rather than evaluating the work on its artistic merits. It sets a precedent that vocal community associations can veto artists' work by framing it through a particular ideological lens, regardless of the artist's own identity or intent. The episode also demonstrates that institutions, in seeking to avoid controversy, will cancel cultural programming without engaging in open, reasoned discourse — the very discourse that could build understanding across different perspectives. Artists, and particularly those from minority communities, face chilling effects on creative expression when they know their work can be pulled based on its visual appearance rather than its actual content or impact.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto > Anti-Racism and Cultural Diversity Office
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Scarborough Campus Students' Union, promoted through the university's Anti-Racism and Cultural Diversity Office, held a racially segregated study hall restricted to students of one skin colour. Students of other backgrounds were explicitly excluded from the event.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 23, 2023, the Scarborough Campus Students' Union (SCSU) organized a study hall event called the 'Black Study Hall / Inspire Study Talk' at the UTSC Hub, running from 12:00 pm to 6:00 pm. The event was promoted on the University of Toronto's Anti-Racism and Cultural Diversity Office website as part of its 'Black History 365' programming. The promotional materials explicitly stated: "This is a closed space for Black Students," restricting attendance based on the skin colour of students. The event combined a study hall with guest speakers from Inspire Hub. By hosting and promoting this event, the university's institutional infrastructure actively facilitated and gave visibility to a racially segregated gathering, excluding students from participation on the basis of their skin colour rather than any individual need, interest, or circumstance.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are excluded from a university-organized gathering based solely on their skin colour. This exclusion treats students not as distinct individuals but as members of demographic categories, determining who may or may not enter a space on that basis. The institutional endorsement of this segregated event normalizes the practice of dividing students along racial lines, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated and included based on their individual character and circumstances. It sends a message to the broader student community that access to university-affiliated spaces and programming is appropriately determined by immutable physical characteristics. This erodes shared civic belonging on campus and forecloses the opportunity for students to build understanding across perceived differences.

incidents.short_description: The Thames Valley District School Board organized a racially segregated community engagement session on February 22, 2023, open only to students and families based on their skin colour. Families were sorted by demographic category to access a school board engagement opportunity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Thames Valley District School Board (TVDSB) organized a 'Black Community Engagement Session' held on Wednesday, February 22, 2023, from 5:30 p.m. to 7:00 p.m. at Sir Wilfrid Laurier Secondary School, 450 Millbank Drive, London, Ontario. The session included a free meal and was advertised as an opportunity to build relationships and for the school board to learn about participants' hopes and dreams for their children. According to the promotional poster, attendance was restricted to students and families based on their skin colour. The consent and registration form, hosted through the TVDSB's Qualtrics survey platform, confirmed that participation was directed at this specific demographic group. The form stated that feedback gathered would inform planning in support of what the board described as equitable outcomes for students. Families wishing to attend were required to register and provide consent for feedback collection and photography. The event was explicitly organized around a demographic category rather than around shared educational interests or individual needs, and families outside that category were excluded from participation.
incidents.expected_harm: Families are excluded from a school board engagement opportunity — including relationship-building with the institution and input into educational planning — based on their skin colour. This practice signals that the school board evaluates and sorts parents and students according to demographic categories rather than treating them as distinct individuals with their own needs, concerns, and aspirations. Children learn that their school institution divides families by immutable characteristics, normalizing the idea that one's skin colour determines which community spaces and conversations one is entitled to participate in. The segregated format forecloses the opportunity for families across the community to connect with one another around shared concerns for their children's education, undermining the development of a common school community. Rather than building shared civic belonging, the school board entrenches division by organizing public engagement along demographic lines.

incidents.short_description: The Canadian Federation of Library Associations gives formal preference to applicants of indigenous ancestry when selecting members of its Indigenous Matters Committee, using ethnicity as a factor in committee membership decisions.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Federation of Library Associations (CFLA-FCAB) published the Terms of Reference for its Indigenous Matters Committee, a standing committee that advises the federation on library and archival services relating to indigenous peoples. The Terms of Reference set out how committee members are selected and explicitly state that preference is given to librarians of indigenous ancestry when filling membership positions. The preference is framed as a way to "prioritize Indigenous voices on Indigenous matters." In practice, this means that a person's ethnicity — specifically, whether they are of indigenous ancestry — is a formal factor in determining who may serve on the committee. All other candidates, regardless of their qualifications, subject matter expertise, or professional experience, are disadvantaged in the selection process based solely on their ethnic background.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified library and information professionals are passed over for committee membership based on their ethnicity. The policy institutionalises the principle that a person's ethnic background is a legitimate basis for granting or withholding opportunities, which undermines the evaluation of individuals based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated expertise. It entrenches the idea that institutional roles should be filled according to demographic categories rather than individual merit, normalising ethnic sorting within a national professional association. Those excluded based on their ethnicity receive the message that their background, not their qualifications, determines their access to participation in national professional governance.

incidents.institution_name: Thames Valley District School Board > Prince Charles Public School
incidents.short_description: Prince Charles Public School in London, Ontario shared student poetry created in response to the book Anti-Racist Baby, demonstrating that grade 6 students are being taught to categorize people by skin colour and physical characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 17, 2023, Prince Charles Public School, part of the Thames Valley District School Board, posted on its official school Twitter account celebrating a grade 6 class assignment built around the book Anti-Racist Baby. Students were directed to write couplets in response to the book, which centres on teaching young children to identify and sort people by physical characteristics including skin colour. A partial image of student work is visible and includes a student's written definition referring to a group of people distinguished by physical characteristics, consistent with the book's framing. The school publicly celebrated this work and praised students for writing poetry about what it described as anti-racist practices in the community. The exercise directed students to think about and categorize individuals based on physical traits rather than character, ability, or individual worth.
incidents.expected_harm: Young students are being taught through classroom assignments to view people primarily through the lens of physical characteristics and skin colour rather than as distinct individuals. This approach conditions children to sort and categorize their peers and community members into demographic groups based on immutable traits, normalizing a way of seeing people that runs counter to recognizing individual worth and dignity. By building curriculum around identity-based categorization, the school undermines students' capacity to engage with one another as unique individuals and instead reinforces the habit of reducing people to their physical characteristics. Shared civic belonging and understanding across differences are weakened when children are taught from a young age that demographic categories are the primary lens through which people should be understood.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Arts Council publicly promoted that it has distributed $5.1 million in grants to artists based on their skin colour since 2018–19, framing demographic-based grant allocation as a point of pride.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 17, 2023, the Ontario Arts Council posted a tweet under the hashtag #BlackHistoryMonth highlighting that artists identified by skin colour had received $5.1 million in grants from the OAC since 2018–19. The post was framed as an 'OAC Funding Facts' feature, presenting the demographic-based distribution of public arts funding as a noteworthy achievement. The communication explicitly identifies a group of artists by their skin colour and publicly celebrates the allocation of substantial public grant funding to that group as a category, rather than describing grant recipients in terms of their artistic merit, individual projects, or demonstrated needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Publicly celebrating the distribution of public funds based on the skin colour of recipients normalizes the practice of categorizing artists as demographic groups rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their artistic merit, vision, or project needs. Artists learn that a public institution tracks and promotes who receives funding along demographic lines, signalling that skin colour is a meaningful criterion in grant decisions. This undermines the principle that individuals deserve to be assessed on their own character and abilities. It also entrenches division within the arts community by presenting funding outcomes through the lens of demographic categories rather than celebrating the breadth and quality of artistic work supported.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Arts Centre
incidents.short_description: The National Arts Centre, a federally funded Crown corporation, organised a racially segregated theatre performance open only to audience members based on their skin colour. The event excluded other Canadians from attending one of nine performances of the same play.
incidents.description_of_event:
In January 2023, the National Arts Centre (NAC) in Ottawa announced a 'Black Out Night' performance of the play Is God Is, scheduled for February 17, 2023, at the Babs Asper Theatre. The event was initially advertised as being exclusively for an 'all-Black identifying audience,' with people of other backgrounds directed to attend any of the other eight performances during the February 9–18 run. Tickets were listed on Ticketmaster under the label 'Adult Tickets Black Out Night,' and event information explicitly described the performance as 'exclusively for Black audiences.' The NAC described the purpose of the evening as providing 'a dedicated space' for theatregoers of a particular skin colour to experience the performance together, framing the exclusion of others as a means of fostering community and conversation.

Following public criticism and media coverage, the NAC quietly amended the event language, removing the word 'exclusively' and revising the description to say the evening 'will welcome Black audiences' without explicitly telling others to stay away. The NAC issued a statement saying no one would be turned away at the door and no questions would be asked about identity or race. A second segregated performance of the play Heaven was also planned for May 5, 2023. The NAC indicated that such racially designated performances would become an ongoing initiative, with more planned for the 2023–2024 season. Human rights complaints were subsequently filed with both the Canadian Human Rights Commission and the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario against the NAC and a Toronto theatre company that had hosted similar events.
incidents.expected_harm: Theatre patrons are excluded from a publicly funded performance based on their skin colour, establishing the principle that a Crown corporation can designate certain public events as open only to people of a particular background. This sends a clear institutional message that people should be sorted and separated at public cultural venues according to immutable characteristics. Canadians who do not belong to the designated group — regardless of their personal connection to the work being performed, their relationships, or their individual circumstances — are turned away from a shared civic and cultural experience. The practice normalises racial sorting in public institutions and undermines the idea that cultural spaces belong to all citizens equally. It also places attendees in the uncomfortable position of self-certifying their skin colour to access a publicly funded service, reducing individuals to demographic labels rather than recognising them as people with unique interests and worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of Justice Canada
incidents.short_description: The federal Department of Justice announced the next phase of Canada's Black Justice Strategy, establishing a steering group and consultation framework that categorises Canadians by skin colour rather than addressing justice system concerns as matters of individual rights and dignity.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 15, 2023, the Department of Justice Canada issued a news release announcing the establishment of a nine-member Steering Group to lead the next phase of Canada's Black Justice Strategy. The announcement was made by the Minister of Justice and Attorney General of Canada alongside the Minister of Housing and Diversity and Inclusion and the Minister for Women and Gender Equality and Youth. The Steering Group is tasked with developing a consultation framework led by, and centred on, communities defined by skin colour. The strategy explicitly frames the criminal justice system's challenges through the lens of skin colour, organising research, consultations, data collection, and proposed reforms around this demographic category. The government's stated goal is a comprehensive national strategy that addresses what it characterises as discrimination rooted in skin colour, with the Steering Group directed to ground its work in the experiences and realities of communities defined this way. The announcement draws on international frameworks—including the UN International Decade for People of African Descent—to justify an approach that categorises Canadians by ancestry and skin colour as the primary organising principle for justice reform.
incidents.expected_harm: By organising a national justice strategy around skin colour as the primary lens, the Government of Canada institutionalises the practice of sorting Canadians into demographic categories rather than treating them as individuals with unique circumstances and needs. This approach embeds demographic labelling into federal policy, normalising the idea that a person's skin colour is the defining lens through which government institutions should evaluate their experiences and needs. It forecloses a universalist approach to justice reform—one grounded in the individual worth and equal dignity of every Canadian—in favour of a framework that divides people by immutable traits. Justice system concerns that affect many Canadians are framed as belonging exclusively to people of a particular background, which undermines the shared civic belonging that comes from addressing common challenges together. Resources, consultations, and policy attention are directed based on skin colour rather than demonstrated individual need or circumstance, setting a precedent for demographic-based governance that cuts against the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits.

incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University hosted a racially segregated campus event restricted to students based on their skin colour. The university and affiliated student groups excluded other students from attending a healing and community discussion.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 15, 2023, Toronto Metropolitan University's Consent Comes First office, along with the Centre for Women and Trans People, Tri Mentoring, and the BIPOC Students Collective, hosted a 'Table Talk' event at the Oakham Lounge, 35 Gould Street, on campus. The event, titled 'We Heal Together Table Talk: Black Community Healing,' was explicitly restricted to students based on their skin colour. Promotional materials stated the event was 'Open to Black TMU Students' only. The gathering was held to mark the launch of a colouring book and healing journal, and featured student leaders in conversation about survivorship and healing. By restricting attendance based on skin colour, the university institutionally organised a racially segregated event, excluding all other students from participating.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are excluded from a university-sponsored event based on their skin colour, establishing a precedent where the institution sorts and divides its student community into demographic groups rather than fostering shared belonging. Students who fall outside the designated skin colour category are denied access to a community space on campus. The practice normalises the university's role in categorising individuals by immutable characteristics rather than recognising each student's singular worth as an individual. It undermines the conditions for a shared campus community and common civic belonging, and signals to all students that their access to university programming is contingent on demographic identity rather than individual need or interest.

incidents.short_description: The Halton District School Board required a member of the public to alter her prepared remarks before being permitted to speak at a public board meeting, removing specific factual content the board deemed unacceptable. Parents who declined to submit their presentations for advance vetting were barred from speaking altogether.
incidents.description_of_event: At a Halton District School Board public meeting held on the evening of 15 February 2023, members of the public wishing to address the board were required to submit their presentations in advance for review and approval before being permitted to speak. Julia Malott, who had registered to address the board regarding a proposed teacher dress code policy, agreed to submit her statement in advance as required. She was then informed by the board that two specific sentences in her presentation were forbidden and could not be spoken aloud at the public meeting. The board communicated this restriction by email, which also contained the line: "We are sorry you felt this was not open and transparent process." Malott reluctantly agreed to alter her presentation and remove the prohibited passage before being allowed to speak. Other members of the public who wished to address the board but declined to submit to advance vetting were barred from speaking entirely. Margo Shuttleworth, the chair of the board of trustees, defended the vetting practice by stating it was done to ensure "all the voices of the community are heard," a justification that drew jeers from the public gallery. The board's communications manager, Heather Francey, also justified the practice as necessary to ensure delegates follow the board's rules on public delegations and to allow "the business of the board to be conducted." The meeting was part of an ongoing dispute about a teacher's conduct and dress at Oakville Trafalgar High School, which had already generated significant public concern, protests, and a series of anonymous threats against the school.
incidents.expected_harm: When a public institution requires citizens to submit their speech for approval and removes specific content before permitting them to speak, it suppresses open and reasoned discourse at the very forum designed for public accountability. Citizens are denied the ability to present their genuine, unaltered views to elected trustees. Those who decline to pre-submit their remarks are silenced entirely. The practice chills public participation: knowing that prepared remarks will be reviewed and potentially censored, community members self-censor or stay home. This undermines the transparency and accountability that public board meetings are meant to provide, and prevents the kind of honest, evidence-based dialogue that allows institutions to be held to account by the communities they serve.

incidents.institution_name: Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement
incidents.short_description: The Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement organized a racially segregated aviation day for students in grades 10 to 12, restricting participation to students based on their skin colour.
incidents.description_of_event: The Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement organized an event called 'A Day in Aviation' held on February 15, 2023, at the Hangar at Pearson Airport and the Air Canada Operations Centre. The event was designed to introduce students in grades 10 to 12 to aviation and aerospace as academic and career pathways. Participation was explicitly restricted to students who identify based on their skin colour. The program also offered mentorship exclusively from professionals selected on the basis of their skin colour. Students were excluded or included in this educational opportunity not on the basis of their interest in aviation, academic background, or individual circumstances, but solely on the basis of their skin colour.
incidents.expected_harm: Students who do not identify based on the designated skin colour are excluded from an educational and career exploration opportunity, regardless of their individual interest in aviation or their need for mentorship and guidance. The program teaches young people that institutions sort them into categories based on their skin colour, normalizing the idea that immutable characteristics determine who belongs in which spaces. It forecloses the opportunity for students from different backgrounds to build shared understanding and common purpose around a career interest, replacing it with a model that emphasizes demographic division. Students learn that access to professional networks and educational experiences is mediated by skin colour rather than individual curiosity, ability, or ambition.

incidents.institution_name: Ottawa-Carleton District School Board (OCDSB) > Vimy Ridge Public School
incidents.short_description: Students at Vimy Ridge Public School engaged in repeated physical violence and bullying, including incidents where children asked peers if they were Muslim before attacking them.
incidents.description_of_event: Multiple parents at Vimy Ridge Public School reported that their children experienced persistent physical violence and bullying throughout the 2022-2023 school year. One mother reported that her Grade 3 son told her about an incident where a child and his cousins ganged up on other children, asking them if they were Muslim. Her son came home saying he told the attackers "I'm Muslim, don't hit me!" in an attempt to avoid violence. Other children experienced punching, hitting, swarming, and threats of violence. Parents staged a demonstration in February 2023 calling for action against violence and bullying. The school board acknowledged the concerns and outlined measures to address conflict and bullying, including class time devoted to positive behaviour, conflict resolution skills training, and personalized support for students with concerning behaviour.
incidents.expected_harm: When children are targeted with violence based on their religion, they experience physical harm, psychological trauma, and fear that interferes with their ability to learn. The child who felt compelled to identify his religion to avoid being hit learned that his safety depends on revealing personal information about his beliefs rather than being treated as an individual deserving of respect regardless of background. This creates an environment where children are reduced to religious categories rather than recognized for their singular worth. Other students witnessing these incidents learn that it is acceptable to sort peers by religion and treat them differently based on that sorting. The normalization of religion-based bullying undermines the development of shared civic belonging and teaches children that demographic categories determine how they should be treated rather than their individual character and actions.

quotes.text: Her son told her about a recent incident when one child and his cousins were ganging up on other children and asking them if they were Muslim.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes that children were being sorted and targeted based on their religion, demonstrating how students were reducing peers to religious categories rather than treating them as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The Durham District School Board administered a school climate and well-being survey to students that asked them to disclose their sexual orientation and gender identity, sorting students into demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The Durham District School Board included questions about sexual orientation and gender identity in its School Climate / Well-Being Survey administered to students. The sexual orientation question asked students to select from options including asexual, bisexual, gay, lesbian, pansexual, queer, straight, two-spirit, and questioning. The gender identity question asked students to select from options including girl/woman, boy/man, gender fluid, gender nonconforming, non-binary, transgender, two-spirit, questioning, and agender. Both questions were framed with definitional explanations and offered a 'choose not to answer' option. The survey institutionally collects sensitive personal information about students' sexual orientation and gender identity, categorizing students along these dimensions as part of a school board data-gathering exercise. The submitter indicates the survey also included questions about students' racial background, though those pages were not captured in the available images.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are asked to disclose intimate personal information about their sexual orientation and gender identity to their school board, reducing them to demographic categories rather than engaging with them as distinct individuals. This creates institutional records sorting students by sensitive personal characteristics that have no bearing on their educational needs or abilities. Younger students in particular face pressure to define and label themselves along axes of sexuality and gender identity at a formative age, in a context where such disclosure is not truly voluntary given the power imbalance between students and their school board. The collection of this information normalizes the practice of institutions cataloguing individuals by personal traits rather than assessing them on their character, abilities, and individual circumstances. It also creates privacy risks, as sensitive data about students' sexual orientation and gender identity is held by an institution.

incidents.institution_name: Greater Essex County District School Board
incidents.short_description: The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario delivered a teacher professional development workshop that categorizes educators and students by skin colour, instructing teachers to adopt a framework in which every classroom interaction is assumed to carry racial meaning and in which people are defined primarily by their demographic characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario (ETFO) organized and delivered a professional development workshop titled 'Re-Think, Re-Connect, Re-Imagine: Thinking About White Privilege' for teachers, apparently hosted at the Ciociaro Club in the Windsor/Essex area (consistent with the Greater Essex County District School Board's territory) and presented by Velvet Lacasse. The workshop was developed following a 2013 ETFO Annual Meeting resolution directing the federation to provide resource materials on what the organization describes as white privilege, anti-racist theory, and culturally relevant pedagogy.

The workshop materials instruct teachers to accept as foundational that nothing in the classroom is neutral with respect to skin colour, and that every choice a teacher makes is either racist or anti-racist. Teachers are told they all possess unearned privileges or disadvantages based on skin colour and that these determine how schools are organized, what is taught, and how teachers interact with colleagues, students, and families.

The materials present a framework — described as 'equity competencies' — directed exclusively at teachers of one skin colour, setting out obligations including listening to and taking leadership and direction from people based on their skin colour, assuming that discrimination based on skin colour is always at least part of any situation, and treating individual concerns through the lens of group demographic identity. Teachers are told to manage their 'defensiveness' when confronted by others and to always assume that racism is 'at least part of the picture.'

The workshop also includes discussion prompts that frame scepticism about this framework — statements such as 'I do not see race, every child is the same' or 'children aren't ready for discussions about power and privilege' — as positions to be challenged and overcome rather than as legitimate perspectives to engage with openly.

The materials were developed into a 29-page booklet available for purchase and included a resource list directing teachers to activist websites and publications organized around skin colour identity. The workshop was presented as foundational professional practice for educators across the province.
incidents.expected_harm:
Teachers are instructed to view themselves and their students primarily through the lens of skin colour and to treat demographic categories as the defining lens for all classroom interactions. This teaches educators to reduce children to their demographic characteristics rather than engaging with them as distinct individuals with unique qualities, abilities, and needs. The framework's insistence that every classroom choice carries racial meaning removes the possibility of neutral, universal human engagement between teacher and student, embedding a divisive way of seeing people into professional practice.

By directing teachers of one skin colour to follow a separate list of competencies, defer to colleagues and students based on demographic identity, and assume discrimination is always present, the workshop embeds a race-essentialist view of people into schooling. Teachers trained in this framework sort children and colleagues into demographic categories and assign meaning, credibility, and moral weight based on those categories rather than on individual character, conduct, or demonstrated need.

The dismissal of viewpoints like 'every child is the same' or 'children aren't ready for these discussions' as positions to be overcome forecloses genuine open inquiry and intellectual honesty among teachers. It normalizes an approach where predetermined conclusions about people based on skin colour are treated as professional obligations rather than as a matter for evidence-based reflection. This undermines the conditions for shared civic belonging and common educational community in Ontario schools.

quotes.text: White privilege is about the concrete benefits of access to resources and social rewards and the power to shape the norms and values of society that whites receive, unconsciously or consciously, by virtue of their skin color in a racist society.
quotes.rationale: The workshop presents this definition as foundational truth for teachers, framing an entire group of people — defined solely by skin colour — as holders of unearned power. This reduces individuals to demographic proxies.

quotes.text:
Always assume that racism is at least part of the picture.
Look for patterns, rather than treating events as isolated occurrences.
quotes.rationale: Teachers are instructed to approach every situation with a predetermined assumption about the cause of events, bypassing individual assessment and evidence-based reasoning in favour of a demographic framework.

quotes.text: ETFO members have called on ETFO to create resource materials on white privilege, anti-racist theory and culturally relevant pedagogy. The most recent of these resources is a 29-page booklet called Re-Think, Re-Connect, Re-Imagine.
quotes.rationale: Confirms this is an institutional ETFO initiative producing and distributing materials that frame teachers and students primarily through skin colour, extending the reach of this approach across Ontario schools.

incidents.institution_name: Simcoe County District School Board > Orillia Secondary School
incidents.short_description: A teacher at Orillia Secondary School sent parents an introductory email in which she framed her role and authority in the classroom based on her own ethnic heritage, positioning her European background as a reason she is not a "knowledge holder" in the subject she teaches.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 10, 2023, a First Nations, Métis and Inuit Studies teacher at Orillia Secondary School, part of the Simcoe County District School Board, sent an introductory email to parents and guardians for Semester II. In the email, the teacher described herself as a "Canadian of European heritage" and stated that this heritage meant her role in the classroom was more about "facilitating learning" than educating. She explained that she is "not a knowledge holder" nor can she be "an expert on a culture that is not my own." The teacher framed her relationship to the course content and her authority as an educator through the lens of her own ethnic background, presenting her heritage as the defining factor in what kind of teacher she can be. This approach signals to students and families that the teacher's ethnic and ancestral identity — rather than her professional qualifications, knowledge, or pedagogical skill — determines the legitimacy and limits of her teaching. The email also outlined course content, assessment structure, and classroom routines.
incidents.expected_harm: When an educator publicly defines the limits of her own teaching authority based on her ethnic heritage, it normalises the idea that a person's value and role in an institution is determined by their demographic background rather than their individual character, qualifications, and abilities. Students learn that their teacher views herself — and implicitly others — through the lens of ancestry and heritage first. This framing reduces both teacher and student to demographic categories, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It also models for students an identity-based approach to knowledge and authority, suggesting that access to certain ideas or roles is mediated by one's ethnic background rather than one's effort, learning, and demonstrated competence.

incidents.short_description: Theatre Passe Muraille in Toronto runs a recurring 'Black Out Night' programme that restricts attendance at select performances to audiences based on skin colour, turning those performances into racially segregated events. Non-black patrons who arrive are redirected to a different night.
incidents.description_of_event:
Theatre Passe Muraille (TPM), a registered Canadian charity located in Toronto, Ontario, operates an ongoing programme called 'Black Out Nights.' Under this programme, designated performances of TPM productions are reserved exclusively for audience members based on their skin colour. The programme began on 5 March 2020 with a production called 'The Negroes Are Congregating' and has continued across subsequent seasons. The next advertised Black Out Night at the time of capture was 9 February 2023 for the production 'Okay, you can stop now' by Shakeil Rollock.

TPM's own FAQ confirms that during Black Out Night performances, the audience is comprised only of people of a designated skin colour, with hosts stationed in the lobby to screen attendees. If a person of a different skin colour attempts to enter, a staff member intervenes, explains the purpose of the evening, and redirects the person to exchange their tickets for a different performance date. TPM states it tries to ensure that this screening labour falls on staff members who are not of the designated skin colour. The theatre has also committed to holding a Black Out Night for all future productions whose playwrights or creators share that skin colour, and actively encourages other theatre companies to adopt the same practice.
incidents.expected_harm: Theatergoers are turned away from or denied entry to a public arts performance based solely on their skin colour. This is racial segregation applied to a cultural venue: people are sorted at the door by an immutable characteristic and either admitted or redirected. The practice establishes the norm that skin colour is a legitimate basis on which to grant or deny access to a public cultural event, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be treated as an individual. It signals to those excluded that they are unwelcome in a space funded in part by public arts grants, and it normalises institutional division of audiences along racial lines. By committing to repeat this practice across all future productions whose creators share a designated skin colour, TPM embeds racial segregation as a standing feature of its programming rather than an isolated occurrence.

incidents.short_description: Multiple Ottawa Catholic School Board school councils invited parents, guardians, and students to an evening session aimed at moving participants from being 'non-racist' to 'anti-racist.' The event frames a particular ideological position on racism and bias as the appropriate goal for families and students.
incidents.description_of_event: Multiple Catholic School Councils affiliated with the Ottawa Catholic School Board co-organized a parent and student information evening at St. Paul's High School in Ottawa, scheduled for February 7 from 7:00–8:30 p.m. The participating councils represented schools including St. Mother Theresa High School, St. Emily, St. Joseph High School, St. Cecilia, St. Patrick Elementary, Monsignor Paul Baxter, St. Luke Nepean, St. Pius, Immaculata, and St. Andrew. The event featured speaker Aubrey Noronha, described as a Character Education and Safe Schools Speaker. According to the promotional announcement, Noronha was to speak about conscious and unconscious bias, the evolution of the word 'racism,' and what 'systemic' means. The central framing of the event was a call for attendees to 'transform' themselves from being 'non-racist' to 'anti-racist.' Parents, guardians, and students were encouraged to attend, with child minding offered. The announcement was sent to families through school council channels.
incidents.expected_harm: Families and students are presented with a single ideological framework on race and societal structures as the approved community standard, with the implication that simply treating people as individuals — being 'non-racist' — is no longer sufficient. This framing pressures participants to adopt a specific activist worldview rather than engaging in open, reasoned discourse about how to treat others with dignity. Students and parents who hold different views on how best to address prejudice are implicitly told their perspective is inadequate. By directing families toward a prescribed ideological transformation, the school board uses its institutional position to foreclose genuine inquiry and dialogue, undermining intellectual honesty and the open exchange of ideas. The framing also encourages people to view themselves and others primarily through the lens of demographic categories, rather than as individuals of singular worth.

incidents.institution_name: University of British Columbia > Black Student Union
incidents.short_description: UBC's Black Student Union is publicly calling for a dedicated campus space restricted to students based on their skin colour, framing racially segregated student spaces as a necessary support measure.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 7, 2023, CBC News reported that UBC's Black Student Union (BSU) was calling on the university to provide a dedicated campus space exclusively for students of a particular skin colour. The BSU organizes weekly events where approximately 50 students congregate and socialize, and says its resources are being stretched thin as the group grows. The union is pushing UBC to formalize and expand this by establishing a dedicated physical space on campus restricted by skin colour. UBC's vice president of students, Ainsley Carry, confirmed that the university began working with students on identifying such a space in 2020, with conversations paused during the COVID-19 pandemic and resumed thereafter. Carry indicated that the university is leaning toward a multicultural shared space that would include a designated area for students of a particular skin colour within it. The advocacy is grounded in the stated experiences of isolation felt by some international and domestic students who are a demographic minority in Vancouver and on campus.
incidents.expected_harm: When a student union at a major public university calls for — and a university administration considers — physically segregated student spaces organized by skin colour, it institutionalizes the practice of sorting people by immutable characteristics rather than shared interests, needs, or experiences. Students who do not belong to the designated group are excluded from a publicly funded campus space on the basis of their skin colour. This reinforces the idea that people's skin colour is the most relevant fact about them and that it should determine where they are permitted to gather. Rather than fostering a sense of shared campus belonging and common humanity, segregated spaces send a message that students are fundamentally separate from one another based on their appearance. Students from all backgrounds lose the opportunity to build understanding across perceived differences, and the university normalizes an institutional culture in which demographic categories, rather than individual character or need, govern access to resources and spaces.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto Mississauga restricts a full four-year tuition scholarship and associated STEM supports to high school students who self-identify as a specific skin colour, excluding all other students regardless of their academic achievement or financial need.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Toronto Mississauga (UTM) offers the UTM STEM Scholars Program, which provides a full four-year undergraduate tuition scholarship, a fully funded eight-week residential Summer Bridge program, paid summer internships and research opportunities, tutorial assistance in first-year science courses, and dedicated academic and personal mentorship. Eligibility for this program is explicitly restricted to students who self-identify based on their skin colour, as stated in the published minimum requirements. Applicants must also meet academic criteria — achieving a minimum of 85% in at least three of four specified Grade 12 courses — and must intend to pursue a graduate degree. The program is promoted directly to secondary school teachers, guidance counsellors, and librarians, who are invited to nominate qualifying students. The application deadline for the 2023–24 intake was April 1, 2023. The program's stated goal is to increase diversity among future STEM leaders, but to achieve this it categorizes high school students by skin colour and grants access to substantial financial and academic resources on that basis alone.
incidents.expected_harm: High-achieving high school students are evaluated for a substantial scholarship and suite of academic supports not on the basis of their individual abilities, demonstrated financial need, or personal circumstances, but on the basis of their skin colour. Students who meet every academic and extracurricular requirement but whose skin colour does not match the program's eligibility criterion are denied access to a full tuition scholarship and years of mentorship and professional development. This institutional practice reduces individuals to a demographic label at a formative stage of their educational journey, normalizing the idea that skin colour is a legitimate and decisive criterion for distributing opportunity. It entrenches the habit of sorting people into groups based on immutable characteristics rather than recognizing each person's singular worth and unique qualities.

quotes.text: Self-identification as Black
quotes.rationale: This is the explicit eligibility criterion restricting the scholarship to students based on their skin colour, listed directly among the minimum requirements for entry.

quotes.text: This unique program seeks to identify high-achieving Black students and mentor them throughout the pathway from high school to graduate school.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the program's stated purpose is to identify and support students selected by skin colour, not by individual need or merit alone.

quotes.text: Full tuition scholarship for four years of undergraduate study
quotes.rationale: Establishes the magnitude of the benefit — a full four-year tuition scholarship — that is restricted based on skin colour.

quotes.text: It is open to high-achieving Black students who are entering undergraduate studies in STEM and who aspire to graduate school.
quotes.rationale: Reaffirms across multiple source documents that the restriction on skin colour is a defining eligibility requirement, not an ancillary consideration.

incidents.institution_name: Nova Scotia College of Art and Design (NSCAD)
incidents.short_description: NSCAD University restricted six faculty positions exclusively to applicants based on their skin colour and ethnicity, explicitly barring all other candidates from applying. The postings represent a cohort of racially restricted academic hiring across multiple disciplines.
incidents.description_of_event: NSCAD University in Halifax, Nova Scotia, posted six faculty positions in early 2023 as a coordinated cohort hire spanning the disciplines of Art History Theory and Philosophy, Drawing, Indigenous Material and Visual Culture, Fashion (3-year limited term), Expanded Media, and Design. Every position was explicitly restricted to applicants who identify as black, indigenous, or a person of colour — meaning applicants of any other background were categorically ineligible, regardless of their qualifications, experience, or merit. The restriction applied universally across all six postings and was framed by NSCAD as part of its "ongoing development of anti-racist practices and the dismantling of oppressive institutional structures." The position in Indigenous Material and Visual Culture went further still, restricting applicants to those of indigenous ethnicity specifically, and invited applicants to self-identify as First Nations, Métis, or Inuit. All positions had an application deadline of February 17, 2023, with an anticipated start date of July 1, 2023. The restriction was stated plainly on the university's dedicated cohort hire webpage and reproduced in each individual position call document.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are denied the opportunity to compete for six university faculty positions solely on the basis of their skin colour or ethnicity — characteristics that have no bearing on their ability to teach, research, or contribute to academic life. Individuals who do not identify as black, indigenous, or a person of colour are flatly excluded from consideration before their credentials are even reviewed. This institutionalises the principle that a person's immutable characteristics, not their individual abilities or achievements, determine their access to employment. Faculty hired through such a restricted process will know their appointment was conditioned on their background rather than solely on their merit, which undermines the integrity of the positions themselves. Students and colleagues at NSCAD are taught by example that institutions assess people through demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. The practice normalises the sorting of Canadians into racial and ethnic groups for the purpose of distributing professional opportunities, entrenching division rather than fostering a shared sense of common humanity and civic belonging.

incidents.short_description: The Ottawa-Carleton District School Board advertised guidance counsellors designated for students based on their sexual orientation and skin colour, directing students to specific counsellors according to demographic identity categories rather than individual need.
incidents.description_of_event: The Ottawa-Carleton District School Board (OCDSB) circulated promotional materials advertising identity-designated guidance counsellors for students. One advertisement promoted a counsellor named Jess Houghton, described as a 'Queer Guidance Counsellor,' specifically inviting members of the 2SLGBTQ+ community to connect with someone with 'lived experience' in that identity category. A second advertisement promoted a separate counsellor, David Seguin, designated specifically for 'Racialized Students.' Both advertisements used identity labels to sort students into separate counselling streams based on their sexual orientation or skin colour rather than on their individual circumstances, concerns, or needs. The school board distributed these materials to direct students toward specific counsellors based on demographic categories, creating a system where a student's first point of guidance contact is determined by their membership in an identity group rather than by their personal situation or the counsellor's area of professional expertise.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are sorted by a public school board into separate service streams based on immutable or identity characteristics, reinforcing the idea that their demographic category is the most relevant fact about them when seeking support. Students who do not fit neatly into a designated identity category receive no clear guidance on who to approach. This approach normalizes the institutional practice of categorizing individuals by group membership rather than treating each student as a unique person with their own circumstances. It undermines shared civic belonging within the school community by signalling that students belong in separate, identity-defined groups rather than a common school community served by counsellors available to all. It also sets a precedent that professional support services should be divided along demographic lines, entrenching division rather than fostering understanding across perceived differences.

incidents.institution_name: Centre for Addiction and Mental Health > Nicotine Dependence Service
incidents.short_description: The Centre for Addiction and Mental Health's Nicotine Dependence Service recruited participants for a smoking cessation program design project restricted to people based on their skin colour. Participation eligibility was explicitly conditioned on self-identifying as a member of a demographic category.
incidents.description_of_event: The Centre for Addiction and Mental Health (CAMH) Nicotine Dependence Service, operating under a project titled 'Equitable Health: Improving Cessation Outcomes within Black Communities,' circulated a recruitment poster and promoted it via Twitter on January 17, 2023, during National Non-Smoking Week. The poster and tweet solicited participants for a focus group intended to inform the design of a quit-smoking program. Eligibility to participate was explicitly restricted based on skin colour: the poster stated that participants must 'self-identify as Black,' in addition to being 18 years or older, currently smoking or having previously smoked, and living in the Greater Toronto Area. The project was framed around designing a program specifically for people defined by their skin colour rather than by any individual health characteristic, need, or circumstance. Compensation was offered to participants. The poster was reviewed and approved by CAMH's internal Quality and Program Evaluation unit, indicating this was an institutionally sanctioned program rather than an isolated action.
incidents.expected_harm: People are excluded from participating in the design of a publicly funded health program based solely on their skin colour. This embeds demographic sorting into health care program development, treating people as representatives of a racial category rather than as individuals with distinct health needs and experiences. Smokers in the Greater Toronto Area who do not meet the skin colour criterion are barred from contributing to a program that addresses a health issue affecting all people equally, regardless of background. The approach normalizes the institutional practice of dividing people by immutable characteristics when addressing shared human health challenges, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated and engaged with as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Ottawa Catholic School Board > All Saints High School
incidents.short_description: All Saints High School organized a school-wide 'United in Black' day, framing the experience around a single demographic group rather than treating discrimination as a concern for all individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: On January 16, 2023, All Saints High School, part of the Ottawa Catholic School Board, publicly promoted its participation in a 'United in Black' day via its official Twitter account. The school announced that students and staff wore black shirts in solidarity, framing the event as raising awareness of barriers affecting people based on their skin colour. The school's communications described opposition to racism exclusively through the lens of one demographic group, presenting it as a community-wide school activity. The institutional messaging did not frame racism as a violation of individual dignity that harms any person subjected to it, but instead organized the school's moral stance around a demographic category. The tweet was posted on the school's official account with the hashtag #ocsbUnitedInBlack, indicating this was a board-wide initiative endorsed at the institutional level.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school organizes its community around the idea that opposing racism is primarily about solidarity with a specific demographic group, it teaches students that discrimination is a group-versus-group concern rather than a fundamental violation of individual human dignity. Students from all backgrounds learn that institutions view people primarily through the lens of skin colour, reinforcing the habit of categorizing individuals by demographic characteristics. This framing divides the school community rather than uniting it around the shared principle that every person deserves to be treated as an individual. It also forecloses a richer understanding of discrimination — one rooted in the recognition that any person can be wrongly judged based on assumptions about their background — and replaces it with an approach that essentializes identity by skin colour.

incidents.short_description: The Ottawa Catholic School Board organized 'United In Black Day,' asking all students and staff to wear black as a show of solidarity with people based on their skin colour. The event frames the school community's civic participation around demographic categories rather than shared individual dignity.
incidents.description_of_event: On January 16, 2023, the Ottawa Catholic School Board (OCSB) held 'United In Black Day,' previously known as 'Black Shirt Day,' asking students and staff to wear black clothing to demonstrate solidarity with people based on their skin colour. The event was created by the OCSB Equity team and the OCSB Black Educators Network and promoted through the school board's official website and social media channels. The board framed participation in the day around concepts such as raising awareness of systemic barriers affecting a specific group defined by skin colour, promoting the culture of that group, and speaking out against what it described as deeply-rooted systemic inequities denying access to resources and opportunities to that group. The board explicitly asked the entire school community to signal commitment to a cause defined by the skin colour of a particular group.
