The name change from 'Black Shirt Day' to 'United In Black Day' was described as bringing a connection to social justice reform to the forefront.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school board organizes institution-wide participation around the skin colour of a particular group, it requires students and staff to publicly align themselves with a cause defined by demographic identity rather than shared individual dignity. Students who do not wish to participate, or who feel their own experiences of unfair treatment are not reflected in this framing, find themselves in an environment that pressures conformity to a particular ideological position. Framing civic participation around the skin colour of one group reinforces the habit of sorting people into demographic categories rather than recognizing each person as an individual. It also forecloses honest, open discourse about discrimination as a harm that can affect any individual, replacing it with a one-sided institutional message that students and staff are expected to visibly endorse.

incidents.short_description: The Burnaby School District hosted a large student event that defined and celebrated excellence through the lens of skin colour, organising an annual school day experience around a single demographic group rather than treating students as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: On January 13, 2023, the Burnaby School District (School District 41) hosted the second annual Black Excellence Day at Byrne Creek Community School, with more than 30,000 students from at least 20 school districts and five independent schools across BC participating virtually. The event was organized in partnership with the Ninandotoo Society, which founded the day, and received support from the Canadian Race Relations Foundation with funding from the Government of Canada. Speakers, performers, and presenters — including politicians, athletes, artists, and students — were featured in a program designed around the celebration of achievement as defined by skin colour. A 16-year-old student designed the official event logo and described her pride in being a young woman of a particular skin colour as central to the event's purpose. The event was also promoted as a learning opportunity timed to coincide with the last weekday before the birthday of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. The district framed the event as part of its broader work toward an "equitable, diverse and inclusive environment for all," while simultaneously organizing the day's programming and identity around a single demographic category. School administrators from multiple districts, including a District Principal of Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion and a District Vice Principal of Diversity, Equity, Inclusion and Antiracism, were named as key participants and organizers.
incidents.expected_harm: When a public school district organises a flagship student event that defines excellence and achievement by skin colour, it sends a message to tens of thousands of students that their identity — and their accomplishments — are best understood through a demographic lens. Students who do not share the event's designated demographic are implicitly positioned as observers of someone else's excellence rather than participants in a shared community of learners. Students within the designated group learn that their achievements are tied to their skin colour rather than to their individual character, effort, and abilities. This approach reinforces the institutional habit of sorting students by immutable traits, normalising the idea that skin colour is a meaningful and appropriate basis for how schools design programming and recognition. Rather than building a school community united by common values and shared civic belonging, this model entrenches division and encourages students to understand themselves and each other primarily through demographic categories.

incidents.short_description: The Halton District School Board's equity account promoted a weekly gathering explicitly restricted to children aged 11–14 based on their sexual orientation, gender identity, skin colour, and ethnicity. The school board directed students, families, and school groups to register for this segregated space.
incidents.description_of_event: On January 12, 2023, the Halton District School Board's official Human Rights, Equity & Inclusive Education Twitter account (@HDSBEquity) promoted a weekly program called 'Proud to Be Me,' running from January 23 to March 6, 2023, in Milton, Ontario. The program was advertised as a space for children aged 11 to 14 who meet specific criteria based on sexual orientation, gender identity, skin colour, and ethnicity. The promotional flyer defined the eligible group using the acronym QTBIPOC, which the flyer itself defines as 'Queer, Trans, Black, Indigenous and Person of Colour.' The program description stated its purpose was for eligible participants 'to hold space with each other' as they explore 'pressure to change our appearance based on societal values rooted in cisnormativity and colonial beauty standards.' The school board's equity department directed the promotion to students, families, and school Gay-Straight Alliance groups, encouraging them to share it widely. Registration was available through the ROCK Access Line.
incidents.expected_harm: Children aged 11 to 14 are sorted and separated by institutions based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and gender identity rather than being treated as individuals. This segregation sends the message to young people that immutable characteristics and identity categories define who belongs in a given space. Children who do not meet the specified demographic criteria are excluded from a school board-promoted gathering, normalising the idea that institutions should divide young people based on these traits. The practice entrenches a view of students as representatives of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals, undermining shared civic belonging at a formative age. By lending its official platform to this segregated programming, the school board signals to its entire community that categorising children by skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual characteristics is acceptable and even desirable.

incidents.institution_name: Ottawa People’s Commission on the Convoy Occupation
incidents.short_description: The Ottawa People's Commission on the Convoy Occupation held an online session on January 10, 2023, inviting only people who identify with a particular identity group to share their experiences of the convoy occupation.
incidents.description_of_event: The Ottawa People's Commission on the Convoy Occupation organized an online session on January 10, 2023, from 6:00 to 7:30 pm, titled 'Tell Your Story Online.' The promotional material explicitly directed the invitation to people who identify as part of the 2SLGBTQI+ community, asking whether they felt unsafe or harassed during the convoy occupation or lost wages or hours. The call to participate was addressed exclusively to this identity group rather than to all Ottawa residents who may have been affected by the convoy occupation. The commission, which appears to have been established to document public experiences of the convoy, structured at least some of its testimony-gathering sessions around identity categories rather than treating all affected residents as individuals with equally valid experiences to share.
incidents.expected_harm: Residents who were affected by the convoy occupation but do not identify with the designated identity group are excluded from this particular forum for sharing their experiences. By organizing participation around identity categories, the commission signals that some people's accounts are sought based on who they are rather than what they experienced. This approach fragments the community of affected residents into identity-defined groups rather than treating all individuals as equally deserving of a voice, and it normalizes the institutional practice of sorting people by demographic labels when gathering civic testimony.

incidents.short_description: Burnaby Public Library established two $2,500 awards for library school studies that are restricted exclusively to applicants based on their skin colour or ethnicity. Canadians who do not identify as indigenous, black, or a person of colour are ineligible regardless of financial need or merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The Burnaby Public Library Board established the Picard Trust Awards, offering two $2,500 grants for Burnaby residents or current BPL employees to attend a master's program at a library school in Canada. The awards are restricted exclusively to individuals based on their skin colour or ethnicity — specifically those who identify as indigenous, black, or a person of colour. The stated rationale is to increase the diversity of librarians at BPL and in the broader library community. The application form explicitly asks applicants to explain how they meet this skin colour and ethnicity criterion. Financial need is considered only as a secondary factor, with preference given to students in financial need — but only among those who have already passed the skin colour and ethnicity gate. Applicants who do not belong to the designated skin colour or ethnic categories are entirely excluded from consideration, regardless of their financial circumstances, academic merit, or demonstrated need.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and sorted by skin colour and ethnicity rather than as individuals with unique circumstances, abilities, and needs. Financially disadvantaged library school students who fall outside the designated skin colour and ethnic categories are denied access to this funding entirely, not because of anything about their character or circumstances, but because of an immutable trait. The institution normalises the practice of using skin colour and ethnicity as gatekeeping criteria for public benefit programmes, reinforcing the idea that a person's demographic category — rather than their individual merit and need — determines what opportunities they may access. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Ottawa-Carleton District School Board > Nepean High School
incidents.short_description: Nepean High School appears to reference a student excellence initiative restricted by skin colour. The evidence is partially legible and requires human review to confirm the nature of the program.
incidents.description_of_event: A screenshot of the Nepean High School website, captured on January 10, 2023, contains partially legible text that appears to reference a 'BLK EXCELLENCE' initiative alongside other school announcements. The submitter's notes indicate this relates to a segregated student excellence club — a club or program for students based on their skin colour. If accurate, this would represent the school organizing a student group restricted to participation based on skin colour rather than individual interests, abilities, or needs. The full details of the program, including its eligibility criteria and activities, are not clearly visible in the evidence provided.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school organizes student clubs or excellence programs that restrict participation based on skin colour, students are sorted and evaluated by their demographic characteristics rather than their individual qualities, interests, or achievements. Students excluded from participation on the basis of their skin colour are denied access to recognition and community on grounds that have nothing to do with who they are as individuals. The practice normalizes the institutional habit of categorizing students by immutable traits, undermining a sense of shared school community and common belonging among all students.

incidents.institution_name: Ottawa-Carleton District School Board > Nepean High School
incidents.short_description: Nepean High School's website features a land acknowledgement that singles out one group of people by ancestry and ethnicity, framing the school community's relationship to the land through an ethnic and identity-based lens rather than as a shared civic experience.
incidents.description_of_event: Nepean High School, part of the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board, displays a land acknowledgement on its website that addresses students, families, and visitors through the lens of ethnic and ancestral identity. The current version of the acknowledgement states: "We acknowledge that our learning is taking place on unceded Algonquin Anishinaabe homelands. We honour the people and the land of the Algonquin Anishinaabe nation." An earlier version captured in a January 2023 screenshot was more extensive, stating that the school community "humbly acknowledge[s] that our learning is taking place on unceded and unsurrendered homeland of the Algonquin peoples," recognizes "the enduring presence of the Algonquin Nation," and affirms "our commitment to Truth and Reconciliation, by continuing to learn, and unlearn, from the knowledge keepers of the Anishinaabe Algonquin Nation." In both versions, the acknowledgement identifies members of the school community and broader public by their relationship to an ethnic and ancestral category — implicitly casting some as belonging to the land and others as not — rather than addressing all students and families as equal members of a shared civic community. The institutional communication frames the school's educational environment through inherited ethnic identity rather than the individual character, abilities, or circumstances of the people it serves.
incidents.expected_harm: By framing the school community's relationship to place through ethnic ancestry, the acknowledgement reinforces the idea that people's standing and identity are determined by their heritage rather than their individual character or civic membership. Students are implicitly sorted into categories — those whose ancestors are identified as the original people of the land, and those who are not — undermining the sense of equal, shared belonging that a public school should foster. The practice normalizes institutional communication that emphasises ancestral and ethnic identity as a primary lens, rather than the common humanity and individual worth of every student. This erodes the experience of civic solidarity and common purpose that schools are uniquely positioned to build.

quotes.text: We acknowledge that our learning is taking place on unceded Algonquin Anishinaabe homelands. We honour the people and the land of the Algonquin Anishinaabe nation.
quotes.rationale: This is the current land acknowledgement displayed on the school's public website, addressing all visitors through an ethnic and ancestral identity framework rather than as individuals sharing a common civic community.

quotes.text: We humbly acknowledge that our learning is taking place on unceded and unsurrendered homeland of the Algonquin peoples.
quotes.rationale: Earlier version of the land acknowledgement from January 2023, showing the same pattern of framing the school community's identity and relationship to place through ethnic ancestry.

quotes.text: We recognize the enduring presence of the Algonquin Nation and thank them for their sharing of knowledge guidance as stewards of this land.
quotes.rationale: This passage from the 2023 acknowledgement further categorises people by ethnic and ancestral identity, implicitly distinguishing between those who belong to the land and others within the school community.

incidents.institution_name: Ottawa-Carleton District School Board > Nepean High School
incidents.short_description: Nepean High School circulated a sign-up notice for its Black History Month assembly that restricted participation in the assembly itself to students based on their skin colour, while directing all other students to supporting roles only.
incidents.description_of_event: A notice posted on the Nepean High School website invited students to help organize a Black History Month assembly. The flyer, attributed to the school's student diversity group (DSU Nepean), explicitly restricted sign-up for the assembly to students of a particular skin colour. All other students were directed to contribute only to peripheral activities such as announcements, display cases, and posters. The flyer provided separate contact email addresses depending on a student's skin colour: one address for those signing up for the assembly and another for all remaining students. The notice appeared on the Nepean High School website during January 2023, ahead of Black History Month in February. By sorting student participation roles according to skin colour, the school allowed an activity that evaluated students not on their interest, ability, or individual qualities, but solely on their physical appearance.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are sorted into different roles and opportunities based on their skin colour rather than their individual interest, enthusiasm, or ability. Students whose skin colour does not match the designated category are excluded from full participation in a school assembly and directed to lesser supporting roles. This sends the institutional message that skin colour is a legitimate basis for determining what opportunities a student may access at school. It normalizes the categorization of students by immutable physical traits, undermines a shared sense of school community, and teaches young people that demographic categories are an appropriate lens through which institutions should evaluate individuals.

quotes.text: ASSEMBLY sign up is for black students ONLY.
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement that explicitly restricts participation in the assembly sign-up to students of a particular skin colour, excluding all other students from this role.

quotes.text:
For black students signing up for the assembly: yagui1@ocdsb.ca
For students signing up for any other help: mfila1t@ocdsgb.ca
quotes.rationale: Separate contact pathways based on skin colour operationalize the segregated participation structure, making the division institutional and procedural rather than incidental.

quotes.text: NHS BLACK STUDENTS
quotes.rationale: The school website homepage displays the heading 'NHS BLACK STUDENTS,' indicating the notice was published on the official Nepean High School website and carries institutional visibility.

incidents.short_description: Queen's University established and awarded a race-specific mentorship award that explicitly categorizes and recognizes people based on their demographic characteristics rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Queen's University's Faculty of Arts & Science established the Black Excellence in Mentorship Award in 2023, which recognizes members of the community based explicitly on racial classification. The award acknowledges what the institution frames as a 'disproportionate service burden often placed on Black faculty, staff, and students.' Dr. Leroy Baker, a postdoctoral fellow, received this award in February 2026. The institutional communications surrounding the award emphasize the recipient's research focused on students categorized by race, and frame mentorship through the lens of racial identity rather than universal principles of supporting all students based on individual needs and circumstances. The award's framework suggests that service contributions should be evaluated differently based on demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: This race-specific recognition system reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as whole persons with unique qualities and contributions. It may create institutional structures where advancement and recognition depend partly on racial classification rather than solely on character, abilities, and demonstrated impact. The framework risks normalizing the practice of sorting people by demographic characteristics in academic advancement, potentially undermining systems where all individuals are evaluated based on their individual merit and the quality of their mentorship regardless of their background. It may also create pressure for faculty and staff to engage in identity-based rather than universally accessible mentorship practices.

quotes.text: Established in 2023, this award celebrates members of the Black community who make outstanding contributions to mentorship at Queen's University. Administered through the Faculty of Arts & Science, it also acknowledges the disproportionate service burden often placed on Black faculty, staff, and students.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes that the award is explicitly race-based, only available to people categorized by racial identity, and frames service contributions through demographic categories rather than universal individual merit.

incidents.short_description: The Globe and Mail published a feature article that consistently frames participation in Canada's wine industry through the lens of skin colour and ethnicity, presenting a group that organises scholarships and mentorships restricted to people of certain backgrounds.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 27, 2022, The Globe and Mail published a feature article profiling Vinequity, an industry group formed in 2020 that provides scholarships, mentorships, and community support specifically for people categorised by skin colour and ethnicity working in Canada's wine industry. The article consistently describes participants, barriers, and opportunities through demographic categories — repeatedly labelling individuals by their skin colour, ancestry, and ethnic background. The group, described as having nearly 300 participants and having distributed almost $50,000 in scholarships, explicitly restricts its programs to people on the basis of their skin colour and ethnicity. The article frames the wine industry's composition as a problem defined by insufficient demographic representation, and treats the awarding of scholarships and career support on the basis of skin colour and ethnicity as a straightforward solution. Throughout, individuals are introduced and described primarily by reference to their skin colour, ancestry, or country of origin rather than their expertise, credentials, or accomplishments. The article does not question whether organising industry support programs around demographic categories is appropriate; it presents such categorisation as both normal and desirable.
incidents.expected_harm: Readers and industry participants are encouraged to view career support, scholarships, and professional community as resources that should be allocated on the basis of skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual merit, demonstrated need, or professional circumstances. This framing normalises institutional sorting of people into demographic groups and entrenches the idea that a person's immutable characteristics determine which opportunities they should be able to access. Individuals who do not belong to the designated categories are excluded from scholarship and mentorship opportunities regardless of their individual circumstances or needs. The article's uncritical presentation of this model in a major national newspaper gives legitimacy to the practice of evaluating people by demographic category, reinforcing division rather than fostering a shared professional community built on common aspiration and individual worth.

incidents.short_description: The Globe and Mail published a feature article framing Canada's wine industry primarily through the lens of skin colour and ethnicity, presenting a demographic-based view of the industry rather than engaging with individual merit and shared challenges.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 27, 2022, The Globe and Mail published a feature article titled 'Vine equity: Bringing diversity to the Canadian wine industry.' The article profiles Vinequity, a group formed in 2020 to support wine industry workers categorised by skin colour, ethnicity, and other demographic characteristics. The piece frames the wine industry's history, economics, and professional culture primarily through a racial lens, attributing barriers to advancement in the industry to the demographic characteristics of those seeking entry. The article repeatedly categorises individuals by their skin colour and ethnic background, describing industry participants as 'racialized,' 'Black, Indigenous and people of colour,' and using similar demographic labels throughout. It attributes differences in tasting vocabulary and professional reception to these demographic characteristics rather than to individual circumstances or economic factors that would affect people of any background. Vinequity is described as an organisation that provides scholarships, mentorships, and community specifically on the basis of these demographic categories. The article presents corporate pledges made in the wake of the killing of George Floyd as the motivating force behind the group's formalisation, and frames accountability to those pledges as a central organisational purpose.
incidents.expected_harm: Readers are presented with a view of a professional industry in which individuals are assessed and understood primarily through their demographic characteristics rather than their individual talents, knowledge, or circumstances. This framing normalises sorting people into demographic groups and evaluating their professional experiences and prospects through those categories. It discourages thinking about barriers to entry in the wine industry — such as high education costs and inherited social networks — as challenges that affect individuals of all backgrounds, instead framing them as problems defined by skin colour and ethnicity. People working in the industry are reduced to demographic proxies, undermining recognition of their singular worth as individuals. Institutions and employers who absorb this framing are directed toward demographic-based hiring and support decisions rather than individual-centred evaluation.

quotes.text: Ms. Rawji was the only other racialized person she'd ever met working in the industry.
quotes.rationale: Illustrates the article's consistent use of demographic labelling to describe individuals in the industry rather than engaging with them as distinct people.

quotes.text: The two of them, along with five other racialized women they'd met in the industry, created a group called Vinequity to support other Black, Indigenous and people of colour (including LGBTQ+ and persons with disabilities) working in Canada's wine industry, which is overwhelmingly white.
quotes.rationale: Describes Vinequity's explicitly demographic basis for membership and support, showing how the organisation and the article's framing sorts individuals into demographic categories.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board's Supervisor Final Report affirms continuation of a race-based equity plan that includes a dedicated strategy for students of one skin colour, and treats lower academic outcomes for male students of that group as attributable entirely to systemic discrimination rather than addressing them as individuals with varied circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 23, 2022, Supervisor Bruce Rodrigues submitted his Final Report on the Peel District School Board (PDSB) supervision, which had been initiated in June 2020. The report confirms that the board will continue pursuing an extensive equity agenda structured primarily around the skin colour and ethnicity of students and staff. Direction 14, highlighted by the submitter, directs the board to develop and implement a four-year Equity Action Plan. As part of this work, the board produced the 'Black Student Success Strategy: We Rise Together 2.0,' a race-specific strategy premised on the idea that lower academic outcomes among male students of a particular skin colour are caused entirely by discrimination directed at them as a group. The strategy does not treat students as individuals with varied needs, circumstances, or abilities, but rather presumes that skin colour is the determinative factor in their academic performance. The report also confirms several other directions that embed demographic categorisation into institutional practice: Direction 7 requires racial diversity quotas on board committees based on trustees' self-identified skin colour; Direction 9 mandates an annual equity report card that tracks outcomes disaggregated by skin colour, faith, and ethnicity; Direction 17 creates guidance counsellor hiring opportunities explicitly reserved for staff of particular skin colours; Direction 18 restructures specialty programme enrolment to give priority access to students who self-identify as belonging to particular skin colour or ethnic groups; and Direction 19 tracks de-streamed course pass rates by students' racial identity. Across all of these directions, the institution consistently treats students, staff, and trustees as members of demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique qualities, needs, and abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are evaluated and sorted by institutions based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than their individual character, abilities, and circumstances. The presumption embedded in the Black Student Success Strategy — that lower outcomes for students of a particular skin colour are caused entirely by discrimination directed at them as a group — denies those students the recognition that their individual circumstances, efforts, and needs shape their experiences. Male students of that skin colour are treated as a monolithic group defined by a single explanatory framework, foreclosing the individualized support and assessment they deserve. Staff are hired or given opportunities based on their skin colour rather than their individual qualifications and merit. Students seeking access to specialty programmes are granted priority enrolment based on self-identified skin colour and ethnicity rather than demonstrated interest or ability. The ongoing institutional embedding of demographic categorisation across hiring, governance, programme access, and student support normalises the practice of reducing people to their immutable traits, undermining shared civic belonging and the recognition of each person's singular worth. It also sends a message to all students — those whose skin colour is the focus of targeted programmes and those who are not — that demographic identity is the primary lens through which the institution views them.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board Supervisor's Final Report affirms the continuation of a policy requiring trustees to self-identify by race so that racial composition can be used to determine the membership of board committees. This embeds demographic categorisation into governance structures.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 23, 2022, Supervisor Bruce Rodrigues submitted his Final Report on the Peel District School Board (PDSB) supervision period. Among the 27 binding directions issued by the Minister of Education, Direction 7 required the board to 'ensure racial diversity of standing and ad hoc committee membership.' The Final Report confirms that all board members completed a voluntary racial self-identification process, which the Supervisor used directly in determining the composition of board committees. The Ministry confirmed in a July 8, 2022 letter that this direction would be considered complete once the self-identification process and a requirement for a three-quarters majority vote on committee composition were incorporated into board bylaws. The Final Report affirms this approach as a completed or near-completed measure, entrenching the practice of using trustees' racial identity as a factor in assigning them to governance committees. Rather than selecting committee members based on individual qualifications, expertise, or interest, the board institutionalised a system in which a person's stated racial category determines their placement in governance structures. The report also confirms additional race-based measures, including creating hiring opportunities for staff based on their skin colour (Direction 17) and restructuring program enrolment to give preferential access based on racial self-identification (Direction 18).
incidents.expected_harm: Trustees are evaluated and assigned to governance roles based on their stated racial identity rather than their individual qualifications, experience, or areas of expertise. This reduces elected officials to demographic proxies, undermining the principle that people deserve to be recognised for their unique qualities and individual capacities. Embedding racial self-identification as a structural requirement in board bylaws normalises the institutional practice of sorting people by skin colour and ancestry in public governance. It creates a precedent in which demographic composition, rather than individual merit and collaborative capacity, becomes the governing criterion for who participates in which decisions. This entrenches division rather than fostering a shared civic identity among those who govern the school board, and signals to students, staff, and families that the institution views people primarily through the lens of racial categories.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board created exclusive professional development and hiring opportunities for guidance counsellor positions restricted to staff of certain skin colours and ethnic backgrounds, as documented in the Supervisor's Final Report submitted December 2022.
incidents.description_of_event: The Peel District School Board (PDSB), operating under provincial supervision, implemented Direction 17 of the Minister's binding directions, which called for the reform of the board's guidance counsellor system. In its response, the board explicitly created opportunities for staff identified as belonging to certain demographic groups — specifically those described as "Black and other racialized staff" — to obtain guidance counsellor qualifications and be hired into guidance positions in schools. The stated purpose was to address what the board characterised as underrepresentation of staff from those groups in guidance programmes. This approach directed professional development resources and hiring pathways toward staff on the basis of their skin colour and ethnic background rather than their individual qualifications, experience, or professional merit. The board's October 2022 final report on Direction 17 outlined the comprehensive changes made to the guidance system under this framework, with particular focus on students of certain skin colours as the intended beneficiaries. The Supervisor's Final Report, submitted December 23, 2022 by Supervisor Bruce Rodrigues, confirmed and endorsed these changes as progress toward completing the ministerial direction.
incidents.expected_harm: Staff who do not belong to the designated demographic categories are excluded from targeted professional development funding and accelerated pathways into guidance counsellor roles, regardless of their individual qualifications, experience, or merit. This creates a two-tiered employment system within a public school board where access to career advancement opportunities is determined by skin colour and ethnic background rather than individual ability or demonstrated need. Existing guidance counsellors — evaluated not as individuals but as members of a demographic group — face the prospect of being replaced by less experienced hires selected on the basis of their skin colour. The practice normalises institutional sorting of employees by demographic categories, undermining the principle that every staff member deserves to be evaluated on their own character, abilities, and professional record. Students are also affected, as the framing implies that the ethnic background of a counsellor is more relevant to their guidance needs than the counsellor's skill, experience, or professional judgement.

incidents.short_description: The Government of New Brunswick released a Commissioner's Final Report on systemic racism containing 86 recommendations, including a mandate to collect race-based data in health care. The report frames New Brunswickers primarily through racial and ethnic categories and recommends building provincial policy around those demographic distinctions.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 16, 2022, the Government of New Brunswick publicly released the Systemic Racism Commissioner's Final Report, prepared by Dr. Manju Varma following a one-year mandate. The report contains 86 recommendations directed at provincial institutions across health, education, justice, sports, local government, and media. A central recommendation — highlighted by the commissioner as among the most important — calls on the province to mandate the collection of race-based data in health care (Recommendation 18). The report consistently frames New Brunswickers through racial and ethnic categories, sorting individuals into demographic groups such as 'racialized,' 'Black,' 'White,' 'Indigenous,' and 'visible minority' as the primary lens through which provincial institutions are asked to assess, design, and deliver services and policies. The report recommends that GNB review all its policies and programs through a GBA+ analysis, mandate anti-racism perspectives into school improvement plans, require all sports organizations to have anti-racism policies, and create a permanent commissioner-level body to oversee the dismantling of what the report terms systemic racism. The methodology deliberately excluded voices critical of the systemic racism framework from consultations, with the commissioner noting that public consultations were avoided because individuals sceptical of systemic racism 'may present themselves and cause greater trauma for our participants.' The report was received by Premier Blaine Higgs, who committed to reviewing its recommendations.
incidents.expected_harm: Mandating the collection of race-based data in health care and across other provincial systems institutionalises the sorting of New Brunswickers by skin colour and ethnicity as a standard government practice. This embeds demographic categorisation into the delivery of public services, meaning individuals are evaluated and tracked not as distinct people with unique circumstances but as members of racial groups. Once governments collect and act on race-based data, resource allocation, program eligibility, and service delivery become shaped by a person's demographic category rather than their individual needs or merits. The report's framework, applied across health, education, justice, and employment, normalises a view of New Brunswick society as fundamentally divided along racial lines, undermining the shared civic identity and common humanity that institutions should be fostering. Excluding dissenting perspectives from the consultation process means the report reflects a predetermined framework rather than genuinely open and reasoned inquiry, weakening the quality of its evidence base and foreclosing legitimate debate about the appropriate role of demographic data in public policy.

quotes.text: Recommendation 18: Mandate to collect race-based data in health care.
quotes.rationale: This is the specific recommendation flagged by the submitter and is listed among the commissioner's key priorities. It calls for institutionalising the collection of data sorted by race across health care, embedding demographic categorisation into provincial health systems.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada > Refugee Protection Division
incidents.short_description: The Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada's job posting for Decision-Maker (Member) states that for positions located in Nunavut, Inuit applicants will be appointed first, giving preference based on ethnicity. The posting and an accompanying social media promotion also signal a broader intent to recruit and favour candidates based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Immigration and Refugee Board of Canada (IRB) posted a job competition for Decision-Maker (Member) positions within the Refugee Protection Division, open to locations including Calgary, Vancouver, Etobicoke, Ottawa, Toronto, and Montréal. The posting's preference section states: "Preference will be given to veterans first and then to Canadian citizens and permanent residents, with the exception of a job located in Nunavut, where Nunavut Inuit will be appointed first." This creates a formal hiring preference based on ethnicity for any position located in Nunavut, meaning applicants are ranked not solely on their qualifications, abilities, or individual merit, but on their membership in an ethnic group.

The work environment section of the same posting identifies specific demographic groups — categorized by skin colour, disability, sex, and sexual orientation — and frames the IRB's hiring goals around building a workforce composed of people from these groups. Applicants are invited to self-identify as members of these categories as part of the staffing process.

On December 13, 2022, the IRB's official Twitter account promoted the job opening using the hashtags #RacializedCommunities, #women, #IndigenousPeoples, and #disabilities, explicitly sorting and targeting potential applicants by demographic categories. The social media post encouraged these specifically labelled groups to apply, framing the recruitment drive around demographic identity rather than individual qualification.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants who do not belong to the preferred demographic categories are disadvantaged in the hiring process regardless of their individual qualifications, experience, or abilities. For positions in Nunavut, non-Inuit candidates — including other indigenous peoples, veterans, and qualified Canadians of any background — are ranked below Inuit candidates by formal policy, meaning a more qualified individual will be passed over in favour of a less qualified one based solely on ethnicity. This entrenches the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on demographic group membership rather than individual merit and demonstrated ability. The broader recruitment messaging, which labels and sorts potential applicants by skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, normalises the institutional practice of reducing individuals to demographic proxies, undermining the recognition of each person's singular worth and unique qualities.

quotes.text: Are you a member of one or more of the following groups: Indigenous Peoples, Black and other Racialized People, Persons with a disability, Women or LGBTQ2+? Are you looking for continuous opportunities for advancement and to make a difference? If you share our vision for a strong and inclusive Canada, come be a part of the IRB Team!
quotes.rationale: The IRB explicitly frames recruitment and advancement opportunities around demographic group membership, categorising potential applicants by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation rather than individual qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Brock University organised a therapy-dog stress relief event during exams that was restricted to students based on their skin colour and ethnicity. Students who did not identify with the designated demographic categories were excluded from attending.
incidents.description_of_event: During the December 2022 exam period, Brock University promoted a student wellness event titled 'BIPOC Paws — Take a Paws from Exams and Hangout with Our Furry Pals,' which offered students the opportunity to spend time with therapy dogs as a study break. The event was explicitly designated for students who identified under a particular demographic grouping based on skin colour and ethnicity, as indicated by the 'BIPOC' label in the event's promotional materials. This meant that access to the stress-relief event was determined not by individual need, academic standing, or any other relevant personal circumstance, but solely by a student's perceived racial or ethnic background. The promotional image showing the event's title and description was shared publicly and drew attention to the university's practice of organising student wellness programming along demographic lines.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are denied access to a university wellness event based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than any individual need or circumstance. This practice communicates to all students that the university views them primarily through the lens of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. Students excluded from the event receive the message that their wellbeing is less of an institutional concern based solely on immutable characteristics. The normalisation of racially and ethnically segregated gatherings on campus — even informal wellness events — entrenches a culture where people are sorted and separated by background, undermining shared civic belonging and the common bonds of campus community. It sets a precedent that demographic identity, rather than individual need, determines access to institutional resources and support.

quotes.text:
BIPOC PAWS
TAKE A "PAWS" FROM EXAMS
AND HANGOUT WITH OUR FURRY PALS
quotes.rationale: This is the promotional image for the event. The 'BIPOC' designation makes explicit that the therapy-dog wellness event was restricted to students of particular skin colours and ethnicities, excluding others from participating.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Human Rights Commission wrote to the Toronto District School Board urging that school safety decisions be shaped by the group-based experiences of students from certain demographic categories, framing safety policy through identity-group membership rather than individual circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 5, 2022, the Ontario Human Rights Commission (OHRC) sent a letter to the Chair and Director of Education of the Toronto District School Board (TDSB) regarding the Board's review of its School Resource Officer (SRO) Program. The TDSB had already decided to end the SRO Program. In its letter, the OHRC reminded the TDSB that any future decisions about seeking external help for school safety must be shaped by what it described as the lived experiences of students, parents, and educators who belong to specific demographic groups — defined by their membership in categories protected under the Ontario Human Rights Code. The OHRC cited its own consultations in which students described negative interactions with police in school settings, framing the safety discussion primarily through the lens of group identity. The letter instructed the Board that human rights principles and equity-seeking strategies must be applied to ensure students benefit from a welcoming school environment, directing school safety policy to be evaluated through demographic group membership rather than individual student safety needs or individual incidents.
incidents.expected_harm: When school safety decisions are directed by a government body to be filtered through the demographic identities of students rather than assessed on individual circumstances, the result is that institutions treat students as representatives of groups rather than as individuals with distinct needs. This approach instructs a school board to weigh the safety of all students through the lens of particular demographic categories, which risks subordinating the individual safety needs of every student — including victims of school violence — to group-based political considerations. Students who experience violence or disorder at school are harmed when the institution charged with protecting them is guided by external bodies to prioritize demographic categorization over straightforward responses to individual safety concerns. The OHRC's framing also normalizes the practice of government bodies directing institutions to evaluate people and situations primarily through demographic group membership, reinforcing division rather than a shared civic approach to common challenges like school safety.

quotes.text: the report and subsequent recommendations and implementation must be informed by the lived experiences of students, parents and educators who are members of Code-protected groups that have historically experienced systemic barriers in the education system and their dealings with the police.
quotes.rationale: This is the central directive of the OHRC letter, instructing the TDSB to shape safety policy decisions based on the group membership of students and parents rather than on individual circumstances or universal safety needs.

quotes.text: the OHRC has heard directly from racialized students who have raised concerns about their interactions with officers in school settings. For example, students told the OHRC they were inappropriately stopped, questioned and were subjected to over-scrutiny by police in schools.
quotes.rationale: The OHRC presents evidence about student experiences by categorizing students by a demographic label, reinforcing its approach of evaluating individuals through group identity rather than as individuals with distinct experiences.

incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa posted a job listing for a Vice-Provost or Associate Vice-President, Equity, Diversity and Inclusion position that explicitly stated hiring would be done preferentially in favour of candidates from six designated demographic groups, sorting applicants by immutable characteristics rather than evaluating each person on individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: In late 2022, the University of Ottawa, through executive search firm Boyden, advertised a newly created senior leadership position: Vice-Provost or Associate Vice-President, Equity, Diversity and Inclusion. The posting was open for applications until December 19, 2022. The job listing contained a section titled 'Preferential Hiring' that stated the hiring process would be conducted in accordance with the university's equity, diversity and inclusion action plan, which aims to address what it describes as the under-representation of people from six designated groups: women, people with disabilities, indigenous people, racialized people (visible minorities), LGBTQI2SA people, and all qualified individuals with the skills and knowledge required to engage with those communities. The university's own anti-racism and inclusion webpage, also captured in December 2022, confirmed the posting and outlined broader institutional priorities that include accelerating the hiring of professors sorted by skin colour and ethnicity, and providing student support and scholarships restricted by skin colour. The preferential hiring declaration in the job posting explicitly signals that candidates will be assessed and ranked not solely on their individual qualifications, character, and abilities, but partly on the basis of their membership in designated demographic categories defined by sex, disability, skin colour, and sexual orientation.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not belong to the university's six designated demographic groups face a structural disadvantage in being considered for this senior role, regardless of their individual qualifications, experience, or abilities. The institution reduces people to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them as individuals. This approach signals to the broader university community that demographic categorisation is an acceptable and even desirable basis for personnel decisions in a senior leadership role. It normalises sorting people by immutable characteristics rather than merit, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own qualities. Candidates who do belong to the designated groups are also placed in a position where their selection carries an inherent ambiguity about whether it reflects their individual merit or their demographic membership, which is itself a harm to their dignity.

quotes.text: This hiring will be done in accordance with the principles of the University of Ottawa's equity, diversity, and inclusion action plan, which aims to address the under-representation of people from six designated groups—women, people with disabilities, Indigenous people, racialized people (visible minorities), LGBTQI2SA people, and all qualified individuals with the skills and knowledge required to engage productively with equitable, diverse, and inclusive communities.
quotes.rationale: This is the core preferential hiring declaration. It explicitly states that the selection process will be guided by demographic group membership rather than evaluating each candidate solely as an individual, directly contradicting the principle of singular worth.

quotes.text: The University of Ottawa embraces diversity and inclusion in the workplace. We are passionate about our people and committed to employment equity. We foster a culture of respect, teamwork and inclusion, where collaboration, innovation, and creativity fuel our quest for research and teaching excellence. While all qualified persons are invited to apply, we welcome applications from qualified Indigenous persons, racialized persons (visible minorities members), persons with disabilities, women and LGBTQI2SA persons.
quotes.rationale: Reinforces the preferential framing by singling out specific demographic groups as especially welcome, further signalling that applicants are being assessed through the lens of demographic categories.

incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto published a news release promoting a perinatal care handbook developed exclusively for patients of a particular skin colour, treating that demographic category as the defining factor in navigating the healthcare system.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 2, 2022, the University of Toronto's news office published a story promoting a capstone research project completed by two graduates of the Temerty Faculty of Medicine's Master of Health Science in Translational Research Program. The graduates founded the Black Birthing Experience Project and produced a patient self-advocacy handbook and a caregiver guidebook, both designed specifically and exclusively for patients identified by their skin colour. The university's news release framed skin colour as the central lens through which perinatal care challenges should be understood and addressed, presenting the project as an institutional achievement worthy of promotion. The research underpinning the handbook drew on surveys of 145 people selected on the basis of skin colour and on studies from the United States and the United Kingdom. The graduates also secured a partnership with Toronto Metropolitan University to incorporate their findings into a module within that school's midwifery program, labelled by skin colour. The university's news office amplified this framing, presenting race-based categorisation of patients as a sound and progressive approach to healthcare.
incidents.expected_harm: By publishing and endorsing a healthcare resource organised entirely around patients' skin colour, the University of Toronto reinforces the practice of sorting people into demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique circumstances, health histories, and needs. Patients of all backgrounds who may face similar challenges in perinatal care — communication barriers, concerns about consent, lack of social support — are excluded from resources framed as belonging to one group. Clinicians and students trained using these materials learn to anticipate patient needs based on skin colour rather than individual assessment. This approach entrenches the habit of demographic labelling within health professional education, normalising the idea that a person's skin colour is a reliable and appropriate guide to their care. It also forecloses the development of universal patient advocacy tools that would benefit all birthing people who encounter difficulties in the healthcare system.

incidents.institution_name: Elementary Teachers’ Federation of Ontario > The Elementary Teachers of Toronto
incidents.short_description: The Elementary Teachers of Toronto held a Federation Day on December 2, 2022, that included workshops framing classroom instruction through identity-based and race-focused lenses. Teachers attended under their collective agreement, making participation a condition of the professional development day.
incidents.description_of_event: The Elementary Teachers of Toronto (ETT), the largest teachers' local in Canada with over 11,000 members, organized its 2022 Federation Day on Friday, December 2, at the Westin Harbour Castle in Toronto, with a virtual option via the Whova platform. The day was secured in the collective agreement as released time for ETT-organized professional development, meaning attendance was effectively compulsory for members. Alongside routine workshops on topics such as pension planning, insurance, and classroom technology, the afternoon session included a number of workshops organized around identity-based frameworks. These included workshops titled 'Centering Anti-Racist and Anti-Colonial Pedagogy in Early Years Literacy,' 'Race and Education: The Water We Swim In,' 'Teaching Math While White: An Abolitionist Approach to Dismantling Racism in the Math Classroom,' and 'Understanding Anti Black Racism in Education.' These workshops direct teachers to view and deliver classroom content through the lens of students' and teachers' demographic characteristics, particularly skin colour and ancestry, rather than treating each student as a distinct individual. The morning session featured keynote speakers and performers. The ETT also promoted a 'Social Justice Workshop' on social media in advance of the day.
incidents.expected_harm: Teachers are directed through professional development, delivered on paid released time under a collective agreement, to evaluate students and their own teaching practice through the lens of demographic categories such as skin colour and ancestry. Workshops that frame core subjects like mathematics through the demographic identity of the teacher instruct educators to see themselves and their students primarily as members of racial groups rather than as individuals. This approach shapes how teachers design and deliver instruction, embedding demographic categorization into everyday classroom practice. Students are harmed when their teachers are trained to view them through such a lens rather than as individuals deserving evaluation based on their own character, abilities, and needs. The normalization of sorting both teachers and students by demographic characteristics undermines a school environment built on shared dignity and common humanity.

quotes.text: Teaching Math While White: An Abolitionist Approach to Dismantling Racism in the Math Classroom (in-person)
quotes.rationale: This workshop title explicitly frames mathematics instruction through the skin colour of the teacher, directing educators to view their teaching identity and practice through a demographic lens rather than focusing on the subject matter or individual student needs.

quotes.text: Race and Education: The Water We Swim In (in-person)
quotes.rationale: This workshop frames the educational environment primarily through the lens of demographic categorization by skin colour, encouraging teachers to view schooling through this lens.

quotes.text: Understanding Anti Black Racism in Education (online)
quotes.rationale: A workshop focused on categorizing educational experiences by skin colour, reinforcing demographic-based frameworks in professional development for teachers.

quotes.text: your union has won this day in our collective agreement as a time where teachers are released to attend ETT organized professional development activities
quotes.rationale: Establishes that attendance at Federation Day, and by extension its workshops, is a collectively bargained entitlement and expectation, meaning teachers are directed to participate in these identity-framed sessions as part of their professional obligations.

incidents.short_description: The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario (ETFO) offers a bursary restricted to applicants who identify as a particular skin colour, excluding all other educators from eligibility based on a demographic characteristic.
incidents.description_of_event: The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario (ETFO) administers a 'Black Educator Bursary' for which eligibility is explicitly restricted to individuals who identify as a particular skin colour. The application form asks applicants to confirm and elaborate on this identity, with one question reading: 'As an individual who identifies as Black, why are you drawn to the teaching profession?' Applicants are also asked to describe their 'involvement and contributions to your community and or the Black community,' further embedding skin-colour categorization into the eligibility and evaluation criteria. The bursary is intended to support educators pursuing post-secondary study, but access is granted or denied based on the applicant's stated skin colour rather than on demonstrated individual financial need, academic merit, or other circumstance-based criteria. Financial need is listed as only one of several criteria considered, and it is secondary to the identity requirement that screens applicants at the outset.
incidents.expected_harm: Educators seeking financial support to continue their studies are screened in or out of eligibility based on their skin colour rather than on their individual circumstances, needs, or abilities. This practice entrenches the institutional habit of evaluating people as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. Educators whose financial need may be equally pressing are categorically excluded because they do not identify with the required skin-colour label. The approach normalizes the idea that a professional association's resources should flow through demographic sorting mechanisms, reinforcing division among educators and signalling that one's skin colour is a defining and relevant characteristic in professional life. It also sets a precedent within the k–12 education sector that demographic categorization is an acceptable and even laudable basis for distributing professional opportunities.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Broadcasting Corporation
incidents.short_description: CBC's The Nature of Things and DOC BC/YT/NT restricted a professional science and nature filmmaking workshop to applicants who self-identify as indigenous, black, or racialized, or who are deaf or disabled, excluding all other film and television workers in BC, Yukon, and the Northwest Territories.
incidents.description_of_event:
CBC's The Nature of Things, in partnership with DOC BC/YT/NT (the BC, Yukon, and Northwest Territories chapter of the Documentary Organization of Canada), announced a one-day in-person science and nature filmmaking workshop and networking event in Vancouver, promoted in December 2022 with an application deadline of December 13, 2022, and a workshop window of February 6–17, 2023. The workshop was open to up to 15 selected documentary filmmakers and production crew from BC, Yukon, or the Northwest Territories.

Eligibility explicitly required applicants to self-identify as indigenous, black, racialized, and/or as members of the deaf and disabled communities. Film and television workers in the region who did not self-identify under one of these demographic categories were ineligible to apply, regardless of their professional background, interest in science and nature filmmaking, or any other individual circumstance. The workshop offered access to CBC executives and experienced independent producers who work regularly with The Nature of Things, as well as travel stipends for participants located outside the Lower Mainland. Access to these professional development opportunities and industry connections was therefore distributed on the basis of applicants' demographic self-identification rather than on their individual qualifications or interest.
incidents.expected_harm: Film and television workers in BC, Yukon, and the Northwest Territories are denied access to a professional development opportunity and industry networking on the basis of their demographic characteristics. Individuals who do not fall into the specified identity categories are categorically excluded from the chance to connect with CBC executives and experienced producers, advancing their careers in science and nature filmmaking. This creates a two-tiered system in which professional opportunities are allocated according to demographic labels rather than individual merit, interest, or demonstrated need. The practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into identity groups and granting or withholding access based on those categories, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

quotes.text: Science & Nature Filmmaking Workshop for Indigenous, Black, racialized and/or Deaf and Disabled creators
quotes.rationale: Promotional tweet confirms the workshop was explicitly restricted to named demographic categories, establishing the eligibility restriction at the point of public promotion.

quotes.text: The Nature of Things is offering a one-day in person workshop and networking opportunity for up to 15 selected documentary filmmakers or production crew from SEARCH the Indigenous, Black, racialized and/or Deaf and Disabled Communities from BC, YT or NWT.
quotes.rationale: The official workshop page confirms the demographic restriction and the geographic scope, showing that all other filmmakers in the region were excluded.

quotes.text: Individuals who self-ID as Indigenous, Black, racialized and/or from the Deaf and Disabled* Communities.
quotes.rationale: The formal eligibility criteria explicitly require demographic self-identification as a condition of entry, confirming the exclusionary nature of the programme.

incidents.short_description: The City of Ottawa's committee and board recruitment call singled out applicants based on skin colour, indigeneity, disability, and gender, urging these groups specifically to apply. This divides residents into demographic categories rather than treating them as individual candidates.
incidents.description_of_event: The City of Ottawa published a recruitment call for public members to join city committees and boards, covering bodies such as the Ottawa Board of Health, the Ottawa Police Services Board, the Ottawa Public Library Board, and others. Within the eligibility section, the City stated it is committed to advancing equity, diversity and inclusion and specifically urged indigenous and racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and women and gender diverse persons to apply. Applicants from these groups were further invited to describe, on a voluntary basis, how their lived experience and perspectives strengthen their application in this regard. This language singles out residents by skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and gender as preferred applicant profiles, rather than inviting all Ottawa residents to apply on the basis of their individual knowledge, skills, and demonstrated capacity to serve.
incidents.expected_harm: Residents who do not belong to the identified demographic groups receive a signal that their applications are less welcome or less valued, creating a system in which civic participation is filtered through demographic categories rather than individual merit. This normalises the practice of institutions sorting people by skin colour, ethnicity, gender, and disability when making appointments to bodies that govern or advise on public services affecting all residents equally. It undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual, and erodes the sense of shared civic belonging that public institutions are meant to foster.

quotes.text: The City of Ottawa is committed to advancing equity, diversity and inclusion in every aspect of its work, including the appointment process and appointments to public member committees. As such, the City urges Indigenous and racialized persons, persons with disabilities, and women and gender diverse persons to apply, and to include, on a voluntary basis, how their lived experience and perspectives strengthen their application in this regard.
quotes.rationale: This is the core passage singling out applicants by skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and gender, explicitly urging these groups to apply and to frame their applications around demographic identity rather than individual qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University published a progress report describing a range of initiatives that restrict spaces, programming, scholarships, and hiring to people based on their skin colour, sorting students, scholars, and staff into separate streams defined by demographic categories rather than individual need or merit.
incidents.description_of_event:
In December 2022, Toronto Metropolitan University (TMU) published an institutional progress report from its Presidential Implementation Committee, detailing steps taken in response to recommendations from a campus climate review. The report describes several initiatives that categorise and treat individuals differently based on their skin colour.

First, the university opened a dedicated lounge on campus — the Black Student Lounge at Kerr Hall West 77A — accessible to students based on their skin colour. The space is described as reserved for students of a particular demographic group to study, relax, and gather, with a "dedicated area for Black students to freely occupy." Over 200 students attended its launch during Orientation Week 2022.

Second, the university secured funding for a postdoctoral fellowship program restricted to scholars of a particular skin colour. The program, expected to launch in September 2023, is explicitly designed for candidates selected on the basis of their demographic background rather than solely on research merit or demonstrated need.

Third, TMU posted a two-year position for an Interim Director of a proposed Black Scholarship Institute, a hiring process that, given its framing and the Institute's mandate, is oriented toward selecting candidates based on demographic identity.

Fourth, student programming including monthly lunch-and-learn events, mentorship sessions, and networking opportunities such as "Connect2Flourish: How to talk to profs" were organised exclusively for students of a particular skin colour, connecting them with faculty and staff selected on the same basis.

The report frames all of these measures as institutional commitments being embedded into TMU's daily operations and strategic vision going forward.
incidents.expected_harm: Students, scholars, and staff who do not belong to the designated demographic group are excluded from accessing dedicated campus spaces, fellowship funding, mentorship programming, and networking opportunities. Campus facilities and publicly funded university resources are allocated not on the basis of individual need, academic merit, or demonstrated circumstance, but on the basis of skin colour. Students are sorted and separated by demographic category for learning and community-building activities, normalising the institutional practice of evaluating people by immutable traits rather than as individuals. Scholars are streamed into or out of fellowship opportunities based on their skin colour rather than the quality of their research. The institutionalisation of these practices — explicitly described as becoming "part of the daily operations, strategic vision and ultimately, the fabric of the institution" — entrenches demographic categorisation as the operating principle of the university, undermining shared civic belonging and the recognition of each person's singular worth.

quotes.text: In 2022, the Tri-Mentoring Program (TMP) and the Office of the Vice-President, Equity and Community Inclusion collaborated to open the Black Student Lounge (BSL) located at Kerr Hall West 77A (KHW 77A). The intersectional-identity affirming space offers Black students at TMU a place to study, relax and build community. The space hosts approximately 30 students at a time and can be used for both planned events organized by students and/or staff and there is a dedicated area for Black students to freely occupy.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the university created a campus space restricted to students based on their skin colour — a racially segregated student lounge funded and operated by the institution.

quotes.text: TMU has posted a two-year position for an Interim Director to support the development of a Black Scholarship Institute. The primary role of the position will be to design and implement the Institute, with broad community consultation and collaboration.
quotes.rationale: Describes a hiring process for a role framed entirely around demographic identity, indicating the position is oriented toward candidates selected on the basis of skin colour.

quotes.text: In 2022, funding was secured for a forthcoming postdoctoral fellowship program for Black scholars, to help address underrepresentation. Given the underrepresentation of Black scholars in postdoctoral positions, this program is intended to build a more inclusive academy and support Black flourishing by increasing the research capacity of Black scholars, expanding their opportunities for SRC contribution to the institution and the community, and supporting their career development and career readiness.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that a fellowship program was created with eligibility restricted by skin colour, directing institutional funding and academic opportunity based on demographic category rather than individual merit or need.

quotes.text: Monthly lunch and learn events were convened this year to promote Black excellence through academic, social and emotional support and group mentorship. These activities were intentionally conducted in small groups to inspire open-dialogue, confidence and psychological safety among participating Black students. One example, Connect2Flourish: How to talk to profs, offered an interactive session for Black students to network with Black faculty and staff, strengthening social connectedness and support systems.
quotes.rationale: Documents student programming — including academic support, mentorship, and faculty networking — restricted to students of a particular skin colour, and matched with faculty selected on the same basis.

quotes.text: These changes mark an important shift towards the institutionalization of this work so that efforts to confront anti-Black racism and promote Black flourishing and excellence at TMU extend beyond any one project and become ingrained as part of the daily operations, strategic vision and ultimately, the fabric of the institution.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that demographic-based sorting of students, scholars, and staff is being permanently embedded into institutional operations rather than treated as a temporary measure — indicating this is a systemic policy commitment.

incidents.short_description: The Canada Media Fund and Ontario Creates launched a pilot funding program for interactive digital media development that restricts access to companies whose majority owners self-identify as members of designated demographic groups, excluding companies owned by people who do not belong to those categories.
incidents.description_of_event: On November 28, 2022, the Canada Media Fund (CMF) and Ontario Creates jointly announced the Ontario Creates-CMF Pilot IDM Futures Forward Program, a grant program offering up to $20,000 to Ontario-based companies for early-stage interactive digital media development. The program has two eligibility tracks. The first is open to any company that completed Ontario Creates' Futures Forward training in fall 2022. The second track restricts eligibility to companies where the majority or owners self-identify as members of designated demographic categories: women or non-binary; 2SLGBTQIA+; first nations, Inuit, or Métis; Black or racialized; persons with disabilities; and/or Francophone. Furthermore, the program commits two-thirds of its overall budget specifically to companies owned by members of these designated communities, structurally directing the majority of public funding toward applicants based on the demographic characteristics of their owners rather than the merits of their projects alone. The program was administered through Ontario Creates' online application portal, with applications open from December 8, 2022, through January 23, 2023.
incidents.expected_harm: Business owners who do not self-identify within any of the listed demographic categories are excluded from the second eligibility track and are structurally disadvantaged in accessing the majority of the program's budget, which is reserved based on the demographic identity of company owners rather than individual merit, demonstrated need, or the quality of the proposed project. This institutionalizes a system in which public funding decisions are shaped primarily by the demographic characteristics of a company's owners, reducing individuals to group membership labels rather than evaluating them on their abilities and the merits of their work. Business owners outside the designated categories receive access to only a fraction of the available funding pool, creating a two-tiered system of publicly funded opportunity defined by identity rather than individual worth. The practice normalizes the sorting of Canadians into approved and non-approved demographic categories as a condition of access to public resources, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

quotes.text: meet the eligibility criteria for the Ontario Creates IDM Fund, and where the majority or owners self-identify as members of an equity-deserving community including women or non-binary; 2SLGBTQIA+; First Nations, Inuit, or Métis; Black or Racialized; Persons with disabilities; and/or Francophone.
quotes.rationale: This is the core eligibility restriction — access to the second track of the program is explicitly conditioned on the demographic self-identification of the company's majority owners, listing specific identity categories that qualify.

quotes.text: This Program has a commitment to provide 1/3 of its budget towards French-language projects and 2/3 of its budget towards projects from companies owned by members of equity-deserving communities.
quotes.rationale: This confirms that two-thirds of the total program budget is structurally reserved for companies based on the demographic identity of their owners, making the demographic restriction a dominant funding allocation mechanism, not merely a tiebreaker.

quotes.text: Applications will be accepted from (1) companies that completed the Ontario Creates IDM Futures Forward Training program in 2022, (2) established and new content creation companies with majority ownership from equity-deserving communities.
