quotes.rationale: The appendix to the official guidelines confirms the two-track structure, making explicit that the second track is specifically for companies defined by the demographic identity of their majority owners.

incidents.short_description: The Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board launched a multi-year strategic action plan that organizes educational programming, staffing, and institutional governance around ethnic and ancestral identity rather than treating all students and staff as individuals with equal standing.
incidents.description_of_event:
On November 25, 2022, the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board (HWDSB) publicly launched its Indigenous Education Circle Strategic Action Plan (IECSAP) 2021-2025. The plan had been in development since 2018 through the Indigenous Education Circle (IEC). The plan is structured around five pillars — Revillagize, Reclaim, Renew, Restore and Celebrate — and covers a wide range of institutional actions including staffing, curriculum, governance, and programming.

The plan organizes significant portions of school board policy and practice around ethnic and ancestral identity. Specific actions in the plan include: recruiting and retaining staff based on indigenous identity; developing and maintaining cultural programs designated specifically for indigenous learners; allowing grant days from school for attendance at ceremony in adherence to indigenous nation protocols; co-creating a policy to hire, retain and promote indigenous staff at all levels; incorporating indigenous governance structures into board-level decision-making; and reviewing and renewing a First Nations, Métis and Inuit Policy.

The plan also calls for students to be categorized and tracked according to indigenous identity designations (e.g., status Indian, non-status, Métis, Inuit, persons of native/indigenous descent, self-identifying as indigenous) and for indigenous student voice to be elevated through a dedicated program (CC:ROSE) to shape board policies and practices. The plan frames the institution's purpose and governance through an ethnic and ancestral lens, describing non-indigenous staff and families as 'treaty partners' with a distinct and lesser role in advancing the plan.

The board's institutional communications, including a formal media release and social media posts, presented the plan as a guiding framework for indigenous education until 2025.
incidents.expected_harm: Students and staff are evaluated and treated by the institution based on ethnic and ancestral identity rather than as individuals with equal standing and unique needs. Students identified as indigenous receive distinct programming, dedicated advocacy structures, and separate institutional attention, while all other students are implicitly categorized as 'treaty partners' with a different relationship to the institution. This reinforces the practice of dividing people by ancestry and ethnicity within a public school system that serves all children. Staffing decisions that preference individuals based on ethnic identity mean that other qualified candidates are disadvantaged on the basis of their ancestry. Students who do not hold an indigenous identity are excluded from certain cultural programs developed with public resources. The plan's framing normalizes the idea that a person's ethnic background determines their relationship to the institution, their access to programs, and their voice in governance — undermining the principle that every student and staff member deserves recognition as an individual of equal worth.

quotes.text: Recruit and retain Indigenous staff.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly directs the board to make staffing decisions based on ethnic identity rather than individual merit and qualifications.

quotes.text: Co-create a policy to hire, retain and promote Indigenous staff at all levels.
quotes.rationale: Commits the institution to a formal policy of preferring individuals for employment and advancement based on ethnic identity at every level of the organisation.

quotes.text: Develop, implement, maintain and evaluate cultural programs for Indigenous learners.
quotes.rationale: Establishes programming designated for students based on their ethnic identity, creating a two-tier system of access to board-funded programmes.

quotes.text: Allow Indigenous student voice to improve educational policies and practices through Cultivating Community: Reclaiming Our Spaces in Education (CC:ROSE).
quotes.rationale: Creates a formal institutional channel for policy influence that is restricted to students based on their ethnic identity, giving some students a privileged role in shaping board policy over others.

quotes.text: Maintain respectful dialogue of social patterns regarding Indigenous identity (i.e., status Indian, non-status, Metis, Inuit, persons of native/Indigenous descent, self-identifying as Indigenous).
quotes.rationale: Shows the institution actively categorising and tracking students and families by ethnic and ancestral subcategory as a formal board practice.

quotes.text: Include Indigenous structures in development of the Board Annual Plan.
quotes.rationale: Embeds ethnicity-based governance structures into core board planning, institutionalising a two-track system of participation in public school governance based on ancestry.

quotes.text: Alongside Indigenous staff and partners, HWDSB is pleased to announce the launch of a strategic action plan to guide Indigenous Education until 2025.
quotes.rationale: Official institutional communication confirming the public launch of the plan on November 25, 2022, establishing the incident date.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto International Film Festival and CBC Films restrict eligibility for a $15,000 screenwriter award to members of so-called 'equity-deserving communities,' excluding Canadian screenwriters who do not belong to designated demographic groups. This condition evaluates applicants based on identity rather than the quality of their work or their individual circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event:
The TIFF–CBC Films Screenwriter Award is a $15,000 development grant administered jointly by the Toronto International Film Festival and CBC Films. Applications opened on November 21, 2022 for the 2023 award cycle, with subsequent annual cycles continuing through at least 2026. The award provides funding for a feature-film screenplay, along with mentorship and access to industry events.

To be eligible, Canadian screenwriters must belong to what the organizers call 'equity-deserving communities.' The 2023 eligibility criteria, as captured in a screenshot of the TIFF website from November 2022, stated explicitly that the award is 'open to experienced screenwriters who are from equity-deserving communities, women, and/or persons with a disability.' The current (2026) version of the program page retains this requirement, stating that membership in an 'equity-deserving community' is a condition of eligibility.

This means that the award categorizes Canadian screenwriters by demographic identity — including characteristics such as skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, religion, and sexual orientation — and restricts access to funding based on those categories. Screenwriters who do not belong to the designated groups are ineligible regardless of the quality of their screenplay or their level of need. CBC Films, a publicly funded broadcaster, co-administers and funds this restriction. TIFF's stated rationale is to 'amplify equity-deserving communities' and maintain 'gender parity.'
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified Canadian screenwriters are denied access to a publicly funded development award solely because of who they are demographically, not because of the quality of their writing or their individual circumstances. This practice entrenches the evaluation of artists by demographic category rather than by ability, craft, or need. It sends a message from a prominent cultural institution — co-funded by a public broadcaster — that a person's identity group determines their worth and eligibility for support. The use of vague, expansive language such as 'equity-deserving communities' gives administrators broad discretion to sort applicants by immutable traits, with no transparency about how membership in these categories is assessed or verified. This normalizes identity-based gatekeeping in Canadian arts funding and undermines the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as an individual.

quotes.text: Member of an equity-deserving community
quotes.rationale: This is the explicit eligibility restriction in the 2026 version of the program — applicants must belong to a designated demographic category to be considered.

quotes.text: The Award is open to experienced screenwriters who are from equity-deserving communities, women, and/or persons with a disability.
quotes.rationale: The 2023 award eligibility criteria explicitly name the demographic categories that determine access to the award, restricting it to people based on identity characteristics including sex and disability.

quotes.text: CBC Films prioritizes storytelling that amplifies equity-deserving communities, along with a parallel commitment to gender parity.
quotes.rationale: This statement from CBC Films articulates the institutional rationale for the eligibility restriction — demographic group membership, not individual merit, drives funding decisions.

incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University posted a tenure-track economics position restricted exclusively to candidates who self-identify as black of African descent, screening out all other applicants on the basis of skin colour.
incidents.description_of_event: In November 2022, the Department of Economics at Toronto Metropolitan University (formerly Ryerson University) posted a tenure-track position at the Assistant Professor level in Applied Microeconomics or Econometrics, with a deadline to apply of December 21, 2022. The posting explicitly restricted the position to candidates who self-identify as black of African descent, including people of African heritage from the Caribbean, the Americas, and Europe. The requirement was not merely a preference or an invitation to apply — the posting stated that the opportunity is "restricted to self-identified Black candidates" and that self-identification information would be used for screening and consideration. All applicants were required to complete an Applicant Diversity Self-ID questionnaire specifically for this purpose. Candidates who did not self-identify as black were excluded from consideration regardless of their qualifications, research record, or teaching ability. The restriction was framed by the university as a response to the underrepresentation of black faculty identified in its Anti-Black Racism Campus Climate Review Report.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified economists are barred from applying for a publicly funded academic position solely on the basis of their skin colour. The university evaluates candidates not as individuals with unique scholarly abilities and accomplishments, but as members of a demographic group, determining eligibility for a professional opportunity based on an immutable characteristic. This practice entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by skin colour rather than recognizing each person's singular worth. It sets a precedent that publicly funded institutions can lawfully exclude individuals from career opportunities on the basis of how they look or where their ancestors came from, undermining the principle that advancement should reflect a person's character, abilities, and demonstrated merit.

quotes.text: Successful candidates will self-identify as Black of African Descent (e.g, Africans and African heritage people from the Caribbean, Americas, Europe) and will demonstrate strong potential for excellence in, and willingness and ability to contribute to: teaching, scholarly research or creative activity and service, while maintaining an inclusive, equitable, and collegial work environment across all activities.
quotes.rationale: This passage establishes that self-identifying as black of African descent is a prerequisite for successful candidacy, not an optional characteristic, making skin colour a formal eligibility requirement.

quotes.text: All applicants to this opportunity must self-identify as Black in the Applicant Diversity Self-ID questionnaire via the Faculty Recruitment Portal... As this is a special opportunity restricted to self-identified Black candidates, applicant self-identification information will be used for the purposes of screening and consideration.
quotes.rationale: This passage makes explicit that the position is restricted to candidates of a particular skin colour and that self-identification is used as a screening mechanism, confirming that non-black candidates are excluded at the screening stage.

quotes.text: The University is committed to addressing the underrepresentation of Black faculty as outlined in the Anti-Black Racism Campus Climate Review Report, and invites applications from Black scholars who will enrich the work of the Department of Economics by bringing community connections and diverse perspectives, knowledges and lived experiences to teaching, scholarly research and creative activities, and service.
quotes.rationale: This passage provides the university's stated rationale for the restriction, framing the exclusion of candidates based on skin colour as a response to demographic underrepresentation.

incidents.short_description: The Thames Valley District School Board released student dress guidelines that restrict what clothing students are permitted to wear based on their ethnic or cultural background, prohibiting students from wearing clothing the school deems to belong to a culture that is not their own.
incidents.description_of_event: In November 2022, the Thames Valley District School Board released its 2022 Guidelines for Student Dress, applying to all 77,000 students across its schools. The guidelines include a rule stating that students' dress must not appropriate a culture that is not their own, explicitly covering Halloween costumes and special dress for theme days. The policy places school staff and administrators in the position of judging whether a student's clothing choices are culturally 'appropriate' to that individual student's background — a determination that necessarily requires assessing a student's ethnic or cultural identity. The guidelines direct that dialogue with students will focus on the 'impact of dress choices on other students,' and that understanding and identifying dress that constitutes cultural appropriation is 'an important area of learning for students, parents/guardians, and all TVDSB employees.' In practice, this means that two students wearing identical clothing could be treated differently by school staff depending on the staff member's assessment of each student's cultural or ethnic background. The broader guidelines also introduce an extensive framework of identity-based dress considerations, categorising students by sex, gender identity, creed, race, and other demographic characteristics when setting expectations and offering guidance to staff.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are evaluated and regulated based on their perceived ethnic or cultural background rather than as individuals making personal clothing choices. A student wearing a particular garment will be permitted or prohibited from doing so not on the basis of the garment itself but on the basis of who the school believes that student is — a form of differential treatment grounded in ethnic identity. This embeds institutional judgment about cultural ownership into everyday school life, requiring staff to make assumptions about students' backgrounds and to regulate student expression accordingly. Students from backgrounds the school cannot easily categorise face particular uncertainty. The policy also normalises the idea that individuals are bound by their demographic group membership in their personal choices, undermining individual autonomy and dignity. Rather than fostering a shared school community where students are free to explore and express themselves, the rule creates a framework in which cultural boundaries are policed by institutional authority, reinforcing division rather than common belonging.

quotes.text: Students' dress must not appropriate a culture that is not their own. This includes Halloween costumes and special dress for theme days or days of significance that may be permitted at the principal's discretion. Understanding and identifying dress that constitutes cultural appropriation is an important area of learning for students, parents/guardians, and all TVDSB employees. Dialogue with students will focus on the impact of dress choices on other students.
quotes.rationale: This is the core policy provision that restricts student clothing choices based on cultural or ethnic background, requiring staff to assess a student's identity when enforcing the rule.

quotes.text: However, students must avoid wearing clothes from a culture that is not their own, according to the guidelines.
quotes.rationale: News article corroborates that the restriction on wearing clothing from another culture is an explicit part of the new dress code, confirming the policy applies to all students.

incidents.institution_name: University Of Toronto > Anti-Racism & Cultural Diversity Office
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Anti-Racism and Cultural Diversity Office and Sexual and Gender Diversity Office co-hosted a wellness and breathwork session restricted to attendees based on their skin colour and gender identity. People outside those demographic categories were excluded from the event.
incidents.description_of_event: On November 18, 2022, the University of Toronto's Anti-Racism and Cultural Diversity Office and Sexual and Gender Diversity Office hosted a virtual wellness session titled 'Breathing Is A Radical Act,' held as part of Trans Awareness Week. The one-hour session offered breathwork and guided discussion led by a registered psychotherapist. Participation was explicitly restricted: the event was described as a space created for people of specific skin colours and for those who identify as trans, nonbinary, genderfluid, gender non-conforming, genderqueer, agender, or otherwise gender diverse. The promotional materials and registration page both made clear that access was conditioned on belonging to these demographic categories. U of T students, staff, or faculty who did not fall within those categories were excluded from attending, regardless of their individual wellness needs or interest in the subject matter.
incidents.expected_harm: Students, staff, and faculty are excluded from a university-sponsored health and wellness programme based on their skin colour and gender identity rather than on any individual need or circumstance. This institutional decision to sort people into demographic groups and restrict access accordingly undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It normalises the idea that a person's immutable or identity-based characteristics determine which university resources and spaces they may access. By organizing a health and wellness event along demographic lines, the university sends a message that some community members' wellbeing is the concern of the institution only when their skin colour or gender identity matches predetermined criteria. This practice entrenches division within the university community and forecloses the opportunity for shared human connection around experiences of stress, vulnerability, and the need for care — experiences that are common to all people regardless of background.

quotes.text: A session for Black, Indigenous and racialized Trans, Nonbinary, Two-Spirit, Gender Non-Conforming, and gender diverse members of the U of T community across all three campuses
quotes.rationale: Directly states the demographic eligibility criteria restricting who may attend the wellness session, combining skin colour and gender identity as conditions of access.

quotes.text: This space was created for healing, restoration and care; therefore, by registering for this event I agree to respect the intent of this space.
quotes.rationale: The registration form requires attendees to affirm the demographic intent of the space, reinforcing that exclusion of those outside the stated categories is deliberate and enforced.

incidents.institution_name: University of Calgary > Haskayne School of Business
incidents.short_description: The Haskayne School of Business at the University of Calgary posted a faculty position restricted exclusively to applicants of a particular skin colour. Qualified academics of any other background were barred from applying.
incidents.description_of_event: The Haskayne School of Business at the University of Calgary advertised a faculty position — at the Assistant, Associate, or Full Professor level — through its Inclusive Excellence Cluster Hiring Initiative. The posting explicitly stated that "this position is only open to qualified Black scholars (e.g. Black Pioneer, African, Caribbean)." Applicants were further required to self-identify as Black in their cover letter to be considered, and all applicants were required to complete an attestation confirming eligibility. The position covered any of the school's core business disciplines, including Accounting, Finance, Marketing, and Strategy. The job posting was active in November 2022 with a closing date of December 31, 2022, and an anticipated start date of July 1, 2023. The university cited Section 10.1 of the Alberta Human Rights Act as the legal basis for the restriction, framing the initiative as a means to address under-representation of certain demographic groups and build a "critical mass" of scholars defined by their skin colour and ethnic background. The restriction was categorical: no matter how qualified a candidate, those who could not identify as Black were ineligible to apply.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified academics are denied the opportunity to be considered for a faculty position solely on the basis of their skin colour. The hiring process evaluates individuals not by their character, abilities, research record, or teaching excellence, but by a demographic category — reducing people to an immutable trait. This institutionalises the practice of sorting people by skin colour in employment decisions, normalising the idea that a person's worth and eligibility in an academic institution is tied to their demographic identity. It also sends a message to all members of the university community — students, staff, and faculty — that the institution views people primarily through the lens of racial and ethnic categories rather than as distinct individuals.

quotes.text: This position is only open to qualified Black scholars (e.g. Black Pioneer, African, Caribbean)
quotes.rationale: This is the explicit hiring restriction that bars all applicants who cannot identify as Black, regardless of their qualifications. It is the core of the incident.

quotes.text: Applicants must self-identify as Black in their cover letter to be considered for this position.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the restriction is enforced at the application stage, requiring a skin-colour declaration as a condition of eligibility.

quotes.text: All applicants will be required to complete an attestation confirming their eligibility for this position in the application process.
quotes.rationale: Shows the university created a formal verification mechanism to enforce the skin-colour restriction, indicating it is a deliberate and structured policy.

incidents.short_description: Burnaby Public Library has adopted a hiring preference that gives priority to applicants based on their skin colour and ethnicity for exempt management positions, and restricts scholarships to library school students based on the same criteria.
incidents.description_of_event: At its November 17, 2022 board meeting, the Burnaby Public Library reported to its board of trustees on the progress of its anti-racism commitments. The report disclosed that four exempt manager positions had been posted and filled under a BC Human Rights Commission special program in which all exempt positions are posted and hired as preferred for applicants of particular skin colours and ethnicities. The library also reported that it had awarded two Picard Trust scholarships of $2,500 each, restricted to library school students who identify as belonging to specific skin colour and ethnic groups. A project roadmap presented to the board outlined further planned actions, including dedicated professional development opportunities, safe spaces, and review of hiring and promotion practices — all explicitly targeted at staff based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual need, circumstance, or demonstrated interest. The library framed these measures as implementing anti-racism commitments developed following a 2021 climate audit.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution fills management positions based on applicants' skin colour or ethnicity, qualified individuals are passed over or advantaged not because of their character, abilities, or demonstrated performance, but because of an immutable trait. This reduces people to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. Staff who do not belong to the designated groups are excluded from scholarship opportunities and certain professional development supports regardless of their individual circumstances or needs. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of employees by skin colour, which undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits. It also sends a message to all staff that their demographic category — rather than their individual contributions — determines what opportunities are available to them.

quotes.text: Four exempt managers have been hired under the BC Human Rights Commission's Special Program, where all exempt positions are posted and hired as BIPOC-preferred.
quotes.rationale: Directly establishes that hiring for all exempt management positions is conducted with a preference based on skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual merit alone.

quotes.text: The Board awarded two Picard Trust awards for library school students who identify as Indigenous, Black or a person of colour.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that scholarship eligibility is restricted based on skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual need or academic achievement.

quotes.text: 1B. Offer two annual scholarships of $2,500 to BPL staff who are Indigenous, Black or people of colour enrolled in library school.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the scholarship restriction is a planned ongoing policy, not a one-time occurrence, and that it explicitly uses skin colour and ethnicity as eligibility criteria.

quotes.text: 1D. Work with staff who are Indigenous, Black or people of colour to review and revise hiring, interview, evaluation and promotion practices.
quotes.rationale: Shows that future hiring and promotion reviews are structured around demographic categories, meaning institutional decisions about staff advancement will continue to be filtered through skin colour and ethnicity.

quotes.text: 1E. Actively support professional development opportunities for staff who are Indigenous, Black or people of colour.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that professional development support is allocated based on staff members' skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual interest or need.

quotes.text: 1C. Develop, support and create safe spaces for staff who are Indigenous, Black or people of colour to share experiences.
quotes.rationale: Illustrates the creation of segregated staff spaces based on skin colour and ethnicity, further entrenching demographic categorization within the institution.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University restricted eligibility for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair position based on demographic categories rather than evaluating all candidates as individuals based on their qualifications and research merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University of Newfoundland posted a job opening on November 16, 2022, for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Northern Governance and Public Policy within the Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences. The position was advertised as an open search for an accomplished scholar, with requirements including a Ph.D., excellent publication record, and capacity for interdisciplinary work. The posting indicated that Memorial University is committed to employment equity and diversity, and stated that the university 'encourages applications from all qualified candidates including women; people of any sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender expression; Indigenous peoples; visible minorities, and racialized people; and people with disabilities.' The posting noted that 'all qualified candidates are encouraged to apply,' but then specified that 'Canadians and permanent residents will be given priority.' The posting included a statement about Memorial University's commitment to employment equity and directed applicants to identify themselves as members of target groups through an employment equity survey, stating that 'applicants cannot be considered as a member of a target group(s) unless they complete an employment equity survey.' This approach to hiring framed candidate evaluation through demographic categories rather than focusing solely on individual qualifications, research excellence, and fit for the Chair's mandate in northern governance and public policy.
incidents.expected_harm: The institution evaluates candidates through demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals whose qualifications, research excellence, and potential contributions should be assessed on their own merits. Applicants are prompted to categorize themselves by immutable characteristics and informed that this categorization affects how they will be considered, signaling that demographic group membership influences evaluation rather than individual accomplishment alone. This approach undermines the principle that people deserve recognition for their unique qualities, character, and abilities. Qualified scholars learn that institutions may evaluate them partly based on demographic proxies rather than exclusively on their research record, teaching effectiveness, and scholarly potential. The practice normalizes sorting people into categories based on immutable traits in hiring decisions, moving away from merit-based evaluation where advancement reflects individual qualifications and demonstrated abilities. Scholars who do not fit targeted demographic categories may perceive that their applications receive different consideration, regardless of the strength of their research programs or their capacity to fulfill the Chair's mandate. The institutional framing suggests that demographic diversity, rather than diversity of thought, methodological approaches, or research perspectives, drives hiring priorities.

quotes.text: Memorial University is committed to employment equity and diversity and encourages applications from all qualified candidates including women; people of any sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender expression; Indigenous peoples; visible minorities, and racialized people; and people with disabilities.
quotes.rationale: This statement demonstrates the institution's practice of categorizing candidates by demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them solely as individuals with unique qualifications and research capabilities.

quotes.text: As part of Memorial University's commitment to employment equity, all applicants are invited to identify themselves as a member of a target group(s) as appropriate. Applicants cannot be considered as a member of a target group(s) unless they complete an employment equity survey.
quotes.rationale: This requirement explicitly asks applicants to self-categorize by demographic characteristics and indicates that such categorization affects how they are considered in the hiring process, demonstrating institutional evaluation based on group membership rather than individual merit alone.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Broadcasting Corporation
incidents.short_description: CBC News published an article framing crossword puzzles as exclusionary based on demographic categories such as skin colour, sex, and sexual orientation, promoting the view that cultural content should be evaluated and redesigned according to group identity representation.
incidents.description_of_event: On November 6, 2022, CBC News published a feature article examining crossword puzzles through an identitarian lens. The article, written by reporter Isha Bhargava, centres on the claim that crossword puzzles fail to adequately represent people sorted by skin colour, sex, and sexual orientation. The piece draws on comments from London, Ontario–based crossword constructor Will Nediger, who freelances with the New York Times, and co-writer Deborah Wainwright, associated with the documentary Across and Down, which aired on CBC's The Passionate Eye. The article frames crossword content and authorship as problems of demographic group representation, arguing that puzzles have historically reflected only the perspective of certain demographic groups and that this causes members of other groups to feel excluded. CBC also promoted the documentary, which it had aired weeks earlier, as part of this coverage. The article presents demographic group representation as the primary lens through which cultural participation and creative output should be evaluated, rather than treating individuals as distinct people with varied interests and abilities shaped by their own unique experiences.
incidents.expected_harm: By framing cultural participation in crossword puzzles through the lens of demographic group membership, CBC normalises the practice of sorting people into identity categories and evaluating cultural content by how well it serves those categories rather than individuals. Readers are encouraged to view themselves and others primarily through the prism of skin colour, sex, and sexual orientation when engaging with a common recreational activity. This reinforces institutional habits of categorising people by immutable traits rather than recognising them as individuals. It also frames creative and editorial decisions as exercises in demographic management rather than individual craft, undermining the principle that people should be evaluated on their character and abilities rather than reduced to group membership.

quotes.text: When you only have puzzles that represent a certain kind of topic or are only of interest to straight, white men, a lot of people won't see themselves reflected in the grid.
quotes.rationale: Illustrates the article's framing of crossword content as a matter of demographic group representation, reducing both creators and solvers to identity categories.

quotes.text: The issue of inclusion of race, gender, sexuality and other categories has been debated in the crossword world for decades.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the identitarian framing CBC adopts throughout the article, foregrounding demographic categories as the central lens for evaluating a cultural pastime.

incidents.short_description: McGill University's School of Computer Science posted a tenure-track faculty position that explicitly grants hiring preference to candidates based on their skin colour. This reduces applicants to a demographic category rather than evaluating them purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: McGill University's School of Computer Science advertised a tenure-track faculty position (all ranks) in computer science, posted on November 4, 2022 (job reference JR0000017566). The posting states that the position forms part of a strategic hiring initiative designed to increase the representation of faculty of a particular skin colour. The posting explicitly states that among candidates deemed to equivalently meet the requirements of the position, preference will be given to candidates based on their skin colour. The posting further states that a demonstrated relevance of the candidate's work to a particular skin-colour-based ideological framework is regarded as an important asset, and that background in critical race theory is desirable. The university also notes it appointed 28 faculty of a specific skin colour over the preceding two years as part of a broader initiative across multiple faculties. The review of applications was scheduled to begin December 15, 2022, with an expected start date of August 1, 2023.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are ranked and selected in part based on their skin colour rather than solely on their individual abilities, research record, and professional merits. This practice means that two candidates with equivalent qualifications are treated differently as a direct result of an immutable physical trait. Candidates whose skin colour does not match the preferred category face a structural disadvantage regardless of the quality of their work. The policy institutionalises the practice of sorting academic applicants into demographic groups and rewarding or penalising them accordingly, undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation based on their own character and abilities. It also signals to the broader academic community that demographic identity is a legitimate and institutionally sanctioned criterion in hiring, normalising the reduction of individuals to proxies for group membership.

quotes.text: This position forms part of a larger strategic hiring initiative, both within the Faculty of Science and McGill University at large, which seeks to increase the representation of Black faculty and to support their success, in line with the University's commitments set out in its Action Plan to Address Anti-Black Racism. Among candidates deemed to equivalently meet the requirements of this position, preference will be given to Black candidates.
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement in the job posting that explicitly grants a hiring preference based on skin colour, directly reducing candidates to a demographic category rather than evaluating them as individuals.

quotes.text: This recruitment is consistent with McGill's efforts to carry out range of academic searches and appointments across disciplines and Faculties, including the Faculty of Science, focused on increasing the representation of Black excellence at McGill. Over the last two years, McGill appointed 28 Black tenure-track or tenured faculty at various ranks.
quotes.rationale: This confirms that the skin-colour-based hiring preference is part of a deliberate, ongoing, institution-wide pattern rather than an isolated decision, demonstrating the systemic nature of the practice.

incidents.short_description: Brock University launched a racially segregated study hall open only to students who identify based on their skin colour and ethnicity. Students not belonging to these demographic categories are excluded from the resource.
incidents.description_of_event: Brock University's Learning Services launched a weekly study hall program in November 2022, operating Tuesday and Wednesday evenings from 6 to 8 p.m. in the Student Success Centre (TH 129). The program was explicitly restricted to students who identify as belonging to particular skin colour and ethnic categories. The resource offered free peer tutoring from fourth-year students, study space, and periodic visits from on-campus units providing additional supports, as well as snacks and refreshments. The program was developed in collaboration with the University's Hadiya'dag énhahs First Nations, Métis and Inuit Student Centre and a demographic-identity caucus on campus. University staff justified the restricted access by citing historical under-representation of certain groups in academic spaces. The program effectively sorted students into those eligible and ineligible for the resource based solely on how they identify in terms of skin colour and ethnic background, excluding other students regardless of their individual academic needs or circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are denied access to a free tutoring and academic support resource based solely on their skin colour and ethnic identity rather than their individual academic needs or circumstances. This institutional sorting of students into eligible and ineligible categories based on immutable characteristics undermines the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated and supported as an individual. Students excluded from the program receive the institutional message that their access to resources is determined by their demographic category. The practice normalizes the division of a university community along lines of skin colour and ethnicity, reinforcing the idea that people belong to distinct demographic groups rather than a shared academic community. It forecloses opportunities for students to build understanding across perceived differences and contradicts the university's role in fostering a common civic and intellectual community.

quotes.text: On Tuesday and Wednesday evenings, students who identify as Black, Indigenous and People of Colour (BIPOC) are invited to join Learning Services' BIPOC Study Hall in the Student Success Centre (TH 129).
quotes.rationale: Directly establishes that access to the study hall is restricted based on students' skin colour and ethnic identity, confirming the segregated nature of the resource.

quotes.text: We know BIPOC students are historically under-represented in academic spaces in general and had less opportunities in terms of getting to university. Feeling welcomed and supported academically in the study hall and throughout the University is just one of the ways we are trying to address that.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the university's rationale for restricting access based on demographic categories rather than individual need, illustrating the institutional framing behind the segregated program.

incidents.short_description: The University of Guelph hosted a restorative yoga session exclusively for students, staff, and faculty who identify as a particular skin colour, barring all others from attending. The university defended the racially segregated event as a "safe space."
incidents.description_of_event: On November 1, 2022, the University of Guelph's Cultural Diversity Programs, in partnership with the Guelph Black Student Association and the Student Experience Department, hosted a restorative yoga session on campus that was explicitly restricted to people who identified based on their skin colour. The event was promoted publicly via the university's official Student Experience Twitter account and through a promotional poster that stated the session was "exclusive to black-identifying students, staff, and faculty at UofG." The session was led by a Waterloo-based yoga instructor and was described as part of a three-part Art and Soul series. The university presented the event as a "safe space" and justified exclusion of other members of the campus community on the basis of their skin colour by framing it as necessary support for an equity-deserving group. After the event attracted public criticism, the university's Diversity and Human Rights Office issued a statement defending the practice of hosting racially restricted gatherings, describing them as integral to the university's commitment to wellbeing.
incidents.expected_harm: Members of the university community are excluded from a university-organised wellness activity based on their skin colour. The university, as an institution, treats individuals differently and grants or denies access to programming on the basis of a demographic category rather than individual need or circumstance. This normalises the sorting of people into separate groups according to immutable physical characteristics and signals to all members of the campus community that the institution views them primarily through the lens of skin colour rather than as unique individuals. It undermines the shared civic belonging of the campus community and forecloses opportunities for connection across perceived differences, entrenching division rather than fostering understanding.

quotes.text:
EXCLUSIVE TO BLACK-IDENTIFYING
STUDENTS, STAFF, AND FACULTY AT UOFG
FREE TO REGISTER!
quotes.rationale: The promotional poster explicitly states that the event is restricted to people based on their skin colour, confirming the segregated nature of the event.

quotes.text: Black-identifying students, staff, and faculty at @UofG are invited to join us on Tuesday, November 1st from 5pm-6:30 p.m. for Restorative Yoga hosted by anti-racist educator Selam Debs
quotes.rationale: The university's official Student Experience account publicly announced the racially restricted yoga session, confirming institutional involvement and the exclusionary basis of attendance.

quotes.text: As part of a three-part Art and Soul series, Cultural Diversity and the Guelph Black Student's Association invited a local educator and yoga instructor to provide a safe, healing space for Black students, faculty, and staff to share their lived experiences and learn about the supports available to them at U of G.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the institutional organisation of the segregated event and its explicit restriction to individuals based on skin colour, framed as a university-sanctioned program.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank included questions about gender identity and sexual orientation in its online job application form, asking candidates to voluntarily disclose personal information the bank said it would use to administer its diversity hiring practices. The practice raises serious concerns about privacy and the appropriateness of collecting such information during a hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event:
In early November 2022, a candidate applying online for a management position at Scotiabank discovered that the personal profile section of the application form included questions asking her to identify her gender identity, sexual orientation, and preferred pronouns. The gender identity question offered a list of options including various categories. The sexual orientation question offered a similar list of options. A pronoun selection was also included.

Scotiabank stated the responses were voluntary and that the information collected would be used for its diversity and inclusion initiatives and hiring practices, in accordance with the Employment Equity Act. The bank also indicated that the information would not be accessible to those making hiring decisions.

However, a communications manager at the Canadian Human Rights Commission noted that it is generally prohibited under the Canadian Human Rights Act — which applies to federally regulated institutions such as banks — to ask questions on job applications that invite or require candidates to disclose information related to protected grounds, including gender identity and sexual orientation, unless there is a legitimate, non-discriminatory professional requirement for doing so.

The candidate reported feeling uncomfortable and vulnerable during the application process, questioning whether providing or withholding the information would affect her chances. Even commentators broadly sympathetic to the bank's goals described the approach as clumsy, noting that many people do not disclose such personal information early in a job search because they do not yet know whether a workplace is safe and open.
incidents.expected_harm: Job applicants are placed in a position where they must decide whether to disclose deeply personal information about their identity and sexual orientation to a potential employer before any employment relationship has been established. This creates a genuine power imbalance: candidates who decline to answer cannot know whether their refusal will disadvantage them, and those who do answer cannot know how that information will actually be used. The practice reduces individuals to demographic categories at the very moment they are seeking to be evaluated on their character, skills, and abilities. It normalises the collection of highly sensitive personal information by institutions and entrenches the idea that a person's identity categories are relevant to whether they should be hired. It also creates a chilling effect, as candidates who wish to keep such information private face an implicit pressure to disclose or risk appearing uncooperative with the bank's stated values.

quotes.text: Quand on est en processus d'embauche, on est en situation de vulnérabilité. On se questionne : si je donne cette information-là, ou pas, est-ce que ça va me porter préjudice ? Je trouve que ça n'a vraiment pas sa place.
quotes.rationale: The applicant describes the vulnerability of being asked to disclose personal identity information during a hiring process and the implicit pressure this creates, directly illustrating the harm to individual dignity and autonomy.

quotes.text: Si vous acceptez de les fournir, la Banque Scotia pourrait les utiliser dans le cadre de ses initiatives et programmes spéciaux pour la diversité et l'inclusion, conformément à la Loi sur l'équité en matière d'emploi, ainsi qu'à d'autres fins, y compris pour administrer ses pratiques d'embauche liées à la diversité et à l'inclusion.
quotes.rationale: Scotiabank's own form language confirms that personal identity data collected from applicants would be used to administer diversity-based hiring practices, showing the institution explicitly links demographic identity to hiring decisions.

quotes.text: Je trouve ça maladroit, parce que beaucoup de personnes vont cacher leur orientation sexuelle au début, jusqu'à ce qu'elles sentent que le milieu est ouvert. Je comprends très bien qu'une personne LGBTQ+ à qui on demande son orientation sexuelle sur un questionnaire, alors qu'elle ne connaît pas les valeurs de l'organisation, trouve ça préoccupant.
quotes.rationale: Even a commentator sympathetic to the bank's inclusion goals acknowledges the practice is problematic and creates anxiety for applicants who have not yet determined whether the workplace is safe to be open in.

incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University restricted a tenure-track sociology position exclusively to candidates who identify as first nations, métis, inuit, or other indigenous peoples of North America. All other applicants are ineligible regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Toronto Metropolitan University posted a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Indigenous Studies within its Department of Sociology, with applications opening October 31, 2022 and a deadline of November 30, 2022. The posting explicitly restricts eligibility to candidates who self-identify as first nations, métis, inuit, or other indigenous peoples of North America. Applicants are required to confirm this identity through a verification process involving a reference from a member of an indigenous community with whom the applicant identifies. Any candidate who does not self-identify under these ethnic and ancestry categories is categorically excluded from consideration, regardless of their academic qualifications, research record, or teaching ability. The university further requests that one of the three required references come specifically from a member of an indigenous community. The position carries full tenure-stream standing and the privileges of an academic career at a major Canadian university.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified academics are barred from applying for a publicly funded university position solely on the basis of their ethnic background and ancestry. This institutionalizes a hiring system in which a person's immutable characteristics determine their eligibility for a position, rather than their demonstrated abilities, scholarly record, or capacity to teach the relevant subject matter. It sets a precedent that a public post-secondary institution may lawfully close entire career pathways to individuals based on who they are by birth rather than what they have achieved or can contribute. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual, and normalizes the sorting of people into categories that determine access to professional opportunity.

quotes.text: This position is restricted to candidates who self-identify as First Nations, Métis, Inuit peoples, or Indigenous peoples of North America. Candidates will be subject to a process of verification whereby acceptance by an Indigenous community is confirmed, in part, via referee from a member of the Indigenous community (reserve, urban, an organization, etc.) with whom the applicant identifies.
quotes.rationale: This is the direct statement of the hiring restriction, explicitly barring all candidates who do not self-identify under specified ethnic and ancestry categories from eligibility for the tenure-track position.

incidents.short_description: A curling program in the Cataraqui area restricted participation to youth based on their demographic characteristics, organizing access to the sport along racial lines rather than offering it to all young people.
incidents.description_of_event: A poster advertises a curling program called 'Youth @ Cataraqui,' organized in association with an organization named UnitedWeCan. The program is explicitly described as being for 'racialized youth,' restricting participation based on the demographic category of race or ethnicity rather than opening it to all interested young people. The program offered free introductory sessions on Sundays in late October and November, with full program sessions running from November through March on Sunday afternoons. Registration was required. Funding for the program was provided by external funders whose names are not fully legible in the source image. The program is described as free of cost, continuing from a prior year, suggesting it is an ongoing initiative. By limiting eligibility to youth classified under a particular demographic label, the program organizes access to a public recreational activity based on demographic categories rather than individual interest or need.
incidents.expected_harm: Young people are excluded from a publicly funded recreational program based on their skin colour or ethnicity. Access to sport and community programming is rationed by demographic category rather than offered to all youth on equal terms. This teaches young people that institutions sort them by immutable traits and that some opportunities are not open to them as individuals. It undermines shared civic belonging by separating youth along demographic lines rather than uniting them through a common activity, and it normalizes the practice of institutions evaluating people based on group membership rather than individual interest, ability, or need.

quotes.text: Curling Program for and racialized youth
quotes.rationale: This is the core evidence that the program restricts participation based on racial or ethnic demographic categorization rather than being open to all youth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: The Department of National Defence requires grant applicants to incorporate Gender-Based Analysis Plus (GBA+) into project design, mandating deliberate consideration of intersecting identity factors including age, gender, ethnicity, race, disability, religion, geographic region, and language when evaluating project diversity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Department of National Defence's Mobilizing Insights in Defence and Security (MINDS) program administers Targeted Engagement Grants for youth aged 15 to 34 interested in defence and security research. The application form, last modified October 26, 2022, allocates 20% of the total evaluation score to a mandatory diversity section. Applicants must describe how their projects incorporate Gender-Based Analysis Plus (GBA+), a framework that requires deliberate efforts to mitigate unintended bias by incorporating diverse viewpoints representing intersecting identity factors such as age, gender, ethnicity, race, disability, religion, geographic region, and language. The application guide strongly suggests applicants complete free GBA+ online training to incorporate this approach into their project applications. The diversity section requires applicants to explain how their projects include diverse perspectives beyond multi-disciplinarity into design and delivery, describe methods to reduce unintended bias, and explain alignment with federal GBA+ objectives. This requirement applies to all grant seekers regardless of their research topic or methodology.
incidents.expected_harm: This grant requirement will reduce individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating projects based on their intellectual merit, methodological rigor, and contribution to defence and security knowledge. Applicants will face pressure to design projects around identity considerations rather than substantive research questions, potentially distorting the natural direction of scholarly inquiry. Researchers who believe merit should be the primary criterion for evaluation will be disadvantaged in the selection process compared to those who emphasize demographic considerations in their project design. The requirement to incorporate identity factors into research design may introduce political considerations into what should be objective academic work. Students who do not wish to frame their defence and security research through the lens of identity categories will either need to compromise their approach or forgo funding opportunities. The 20% weighting of diversity considerations in project evaluation means that substantively strong research proposals may be rejected in favour of weaker proposals that better satisfy identity-based criteria.

quotes.text: B. Diversity (20% of total score) Describe how your project considers, fosters, and leverages diversity and Gender-Based Analysis Plus (GBA+) (maximum 3425 characters): Explain how your project includes diverse perspectives (beyond just multi-disciplinarity) into its design and delivery (research topic, team composition, engagement strategy, etc.); Describe what methods you will use to reduce unintended bias; and Explain how your project aligns with the federal objectives related to GBA+.
quotes.rationale: This is the core requirement that mandates consideration of identity-based diversity as a weighted evaluation criterion, making it central to the incident. It shows that 20% of the evaluation depends on incorporating diversity considerations into project design.

quotes.text: GBA+ is an analytical tool used to assess how people of all genders and diverse identities may experience or be impacted by policies, programs and initiatives (including but not limited to research projects, conferences or events). GBA+ requires that deliberate efforts be made to mitigate against unintended bias by incorporating diverse viewpoints representing intersecting identity factors (such as age, gender, ethnicity, race, disability, religion, geographic region, language, etc.).
quotes.rationale: Defines what GBA+ entails and explicitly lists the identity categories that applicants must consider, establishing the basis for targeting_basis classification and showing the requirement to incorporate demographic considerations.

incidents.short_description: Edmonton Public Schools launched a demographic survey asking students in Grades 4 to 12 to disclose their racial identity, indigenous identity, ethnicity, religion, gender identity, and sexual orientation. The survey institutionalizes the categorization of students by demographic labels rather than treating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: In the fall of 2022, Edmonton Public Schools introduced a student demographic survey for students in Grades 4 to 12, administered during class time. The survey was developed as part of the division's Anti-racism and Equity Action Plan, released in 2021, which identified collecting demographic data as a key priority. Students were invited to disclose information about their indigenous identity, racial identity, ethnicity, religion or spiritual affiliation, gender identity, and — for students in Grades 7 to 12 — sexual orientation. The Superintendent wrote to all affected families on October 24, 2022, describing the initiative. The survey was positioned as voluntary, with parents able to opt their child out by contacting the school. The stated purpose was to understand how different groups of students experience school and to inform future actions. The data collected was to be stored centrally and accessed only by a small number of division staff, with aggregate findings shared publicly without identifying individual schools or students. The survey was conducted between November 1 and December 16, 2022.
incidents.expected_harm: The survey institutionalizes the practice of sorting students into demographic categories based on immutable and personal characteristics, including skin colour, ethnicity, religion, and sexual orientation. Rather than engaging with students as individuals whose unique needs, abilities, and circumstances define their school experience, the division treats demographic group membership as the primary lens through which student outcomes are understood. Students as young as Grade 4 are asked to identify and label themselves by race and other sensitive characteristics, embedding in them — and in the institution — the idea that these categories are foundational to how they are seen and served. This normalizes institutional evaluation of children by demographic proxies rather than individual merit or circumstance. Asking students about sexual orientation and gender identity from Grade 7 onward, during class time in a school setting, places pressure on children to self-categorize in ways that are deeply personal and that many families may not feel a school division has standing to inquire about. The aggregation of such data risks reinforcing group-based thinking in how resources, programs, and supports are allocated, undermining the principle that each student deserves to be recognized for their singular worth.

quotes.text: Through the survey, students will be invited to share information about their: • Indigenous identity • racial identity • ethnicity • religion or spiritual affiliation • gender identity • sexual orientation (Grade 7–12 only)
quotes.rationale: This lists the full range of demographic categories students are asked to self-identify with, illustrating the breadth of personal and sensitive information being collected from children as young as Grade 4.

quotes.text: The data from the student demographic survey will help us to better understand the diversity of students. The data will be analyzed with other data that is already available to the Division. This information will help Edmonton Public Schools better understand how different groups of students experience school and inform actions that can be taken to support student success.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that the explicit purpose of the survey is to understand and act on group-level differences, framing students primarily as members of demographic categories rather than as individuals.

incidents.short_description: A 'white people first' sticker was placed on a Hamilton city council candidate's campaign advertisement at a bus station, prompting a hate crime investigation by Hamilton police.
incidents.description_of_event: During the October 2022 Ontario municipal election campaign, a sticker reading 'white people first' was placed over a campaign advertisement belonging to Kojo Damptey, a first-time Hamilton city council candidate, at a Hamilton bus station. The incident was one of three election-related hate crime investigations opened by Hamilton police's hate crime unit during that campaign period. Damptey, who had previously worked as executive director of the Hamilton Centre for Civic Inclusion, said he had anticipated this kind of targeting based on past experiences. His campaign had already put procedures in place to regularly check on the advertisement. Beyond the sticker incident, Damptey noted his campaign required canvassers to travel in pairs due to hostility they encountered at doors, diverting time and resources away from policy campaigning. Hamilton police investigated the sticker placement as a hate crime.

quotes.text: Hamilton police's hate crime unit is investigating after a sticker saying "white people first" was placed on one of Damptey's campaign ads, along with two other hate-related election incidents.
quotes.rationale: Directly describes the core incident and confirms police investigation.

incidents.short_description: The Edmonton Police Commission voted to write a letter to the Alberta government advocating for the addition of a person's skin colour to government-issued identification such as driver's licences. This represents an institutional push to have the government formally categorise and label all Albertans by their skin colour on official identity documents.
incidents.description_of_event: On October 20 or 21, 2022, a majority of Edmonton Police Commission commissioners voted to write a letter to the Alberta provincial government seeking the addition of skin colour as a data field on government-issued identification, including driver's licences. The motion followed a presentation to the commission by Statistics Canada about its plans to begin collecting skin-colour-based data from police services by 2024. The Edmonton Police Service had already been advocating to both provincial and federal governments for this change, arguing it would create a standardised field in their records management system. Police Chief Dale McFee framed the proposal as producing a reliable "source of truth" for data collection, and suggested it would reduce individual officer bias by making the categorisation self-reported. Commissioner Erick Ambtman voted against the letter, expressing unease with the practice. Commissioner Jodi Calahoo Stonehouse supported the measure, framing it as an equity step. The commission's vote represents a formal institutional decision to advocate for the state labelling of every Albertan by their skin colour on official identity documents.
incidents.expected_harm: Placing a person's skin colour on government-issued identification entrenches the state's role in formally categorising and labelling individuals by their physical appearance. Every interaction in which an Albertan presents their driver's licence or ID card becomes an occasion where their skin colour is officially recorded and potentially used as a basis for decisions. Rather than treating people as distinct individuals whose character, abilities, and circumstances matter, the policy reduces them to a demographic label affixed by the government. This normalises the institutional practice of sorting citizens by immutable physical traits and risks enabling discriminatory uses of that data across multiple government and law enforcement contexts. It undermines the principle that individuals deserve recognition on their own terms, not as members of a skin-colour category, and sets a precedent for the routine collection and use of such labels in ways that are difficult to constrain once embedded in official documentation systems.

quotes.text: On Thursday, a majority of commissioners voted to write a letter to the Alberta government "seeking the addition of race-based data on government-issued ID."
quotes.rationale: This is the core act: a formal commission vote to advocate for adding skin-colour categorisation to government ID documents.

quotes.text: The commission heard the Edmonton Police Service has been advocating provincial and federal governments to include race on government-issued identification as it would create a standard for their records management system.
quotes.rationale: Reveals that the push to label people by skin colour on ID predates this vote and originates within the police service itself, framed as an administrative convenience.

incidents.short_description: The NHL released an internal demographic study of its workforce categorising employees by skin colour and sex, and committed to using these demographic breakdowns to shape future hiring strategies. The league framed its workforce composition as a problem to be solved by targeting people based on their demographic characteristics rather than their individual qualities.
incidents.description_of_event: On October 18, 2022, the National Hockey League released a 24-page internal demographic study covering its central office staff and all 32 member teams. The report was based on responses from approximately 4,200 employees — about 67% of the total workforce — who voluntarily and anonymously disclosed their skin colour, ethnicity, and sex. The study found that 83.6% of the NHL workforce identified as white, that men made up nearly 62% of employees, and that more than 90% of players and nearly all coaches and officials were of the same demographic profile. The report was presented to the league's Board of Governors and distributed to multiple internal committees. Commissioner Gary Bettman stated the data would shape future policies. The NHL's executive vice president Kim Davis outlined seven courses of action aimed at changing the demographic composition of the workforce and fan base, explicitly framing recruitment and hiring decisions around skin colour and ethnicity. The league committed to producing an ongoing race and gender report card through the University of Central Florida's Institute for Diversity and Ethics in Sport, entrenching the practice of evaluating and tracking employees and players by these demographic categories. Davis stated the league could not expect to recruit people based on their skin colour without simultaneously undertaking broader cultural initiatives, framing the hiring goal in explicitly demographic terms. The report positioned the skin colour and sex composition of the workforce as institutional metrics to be managed and changed through targeted strategies.
incidents.expected_harm: By committing to track, report on, and actively shift the demographic composition of its workforce based on skin colour and sex, the NHL institutionalises the practice of evaluating people as members of demographic groups rather than as individuals with distinct abilities, character, and merit. Employees and candidates are assessed and counted based on their immutable characteristics rather than what they uniquely contribute. This entrenches a framework where advancement and hiring decisions are influenced by a person's skin colour or sex, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be recognised on their own terms. The commitment to ongoing demographic report cards normalises the reduction of individuals to demographic proxies, and sends the message to all employees — regardless of their background — that the organisation views them primarily through the lens of their skin colour and sex rather than their individual worth and contribution.

quotes.text: The report released Tuesday found that 83.6% of the NHL's workforce is white and that men make up nearly 62% of the total, based on the 4,200 people who participated in a voluntary and anonymous survey (about 67% of all employees).
quotes.rationale: Establishes the nature of the study: the NHL conducted an internal demographic survey categorising employees by skin colour and sex, producing statistics that form the basis of the league's stated commitment to change workforce composition.

quotes.text: The whole purpose behind doing a workforce study is to provide a baseline: a fact-based baseline so that you can begin to develop very intentional and specific strategies around where you need to hire, how you need to hire, how you need to improve your brand.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the NHL's stated intent to use demographic data to drive future hiring decisions, framing recruitment explicitly around changing the demographic make-up of the workforce rather than selecting individuals on merit.

quotes.text: One of the next steps is turning the data into a race and gender report card produced by the University of Central Florida's Institute for Diversity and Ethics in Sport, which has for years graded sports leagues on hiring practices.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the NHL's commitment to ongoing institutional tracking and public grading of workforce composition by skin colour and sex, entrenching demographic categorisation as a management tool.

quotes.text: Commissioner Gary Bettman said the data will shape policies 'that will produce the greatest impacts in the years to come.'
quotes.rationale: Senior leadership explicitly committed to allowing demographic data to shape future league policy, confirming that skin colour and sex composition are being treated as institutional objectives.

quotes.text: Davis said it is not as simple as recruiting people of color to work for the NHL, that it starts with improving how underrepresented communities see and feel about the sport itself.
quotes.rationale: Reveals the NHL's framing of recruitment as explicitly tied to skin colour, treating prospective employees and participants as members of demographic categories rather than individuals.

quotes.text: You can't expect to recruit (Black, Indigenous and people of color) folks to work in the league if you don't at the same time have your stadium fan code of conduct underway so people feel like the sport is really serious about growing the fan base.
quotes.rationale: Shows that the NHL's hiring strategy is explicitly framed around recruiting people based on their skin colour and ethnicity, institutionalising demographic targeting in employment.

incidents.short_description: Dalhousie University's Schulich School of Law organized a writing workshop restricted to students based on their skin colour and ethnicity. Students who did not identify within those demographic categories were explicitly excluded.
incidents.description_of_event: On or around October 5, 2022, a message was sent to second-year law students at Dalhousie University advertising a generative writing workshop scheduled for October 12 via Zoom at 6:30pm. The workshop was facilitated by Preeti Kaur Dhaliwal, who had previously led a women of colour writing circle at McGill Law. The promotional message explicitly restricted attendance to students who self-identify as "Black, Indigenous and/or a Person of Colour," barring all other law students from participating. The email was sent through the official law school mailing list, indicating institutional endorsement of the racially segregated session. The workshop was framed as offering a space to connect with other students sharing the same demographic characteristics, and the facilitator was described as a "critical race feminist." Students not belonging to the designated demographic categories were excluded from a creative writing, wellness, and community-building opportunity offered through their law school.
incidents.expected_harm: Law students are sorted and separated by their institution based on skin colour and ethnicity, with access to a school-organized program determined not by individual need, interest, or circumstance, but by demographic category. Students who do not fall within the designated categories are excluded from a writing and wellness opportunity made available through official school channels. This normalizes the institutional practice of evaluating and categorizing students by immutable characteristics rather than as individuals. It sends a message that belonging and access within a law school community are conditional on demographic identity, undermining shared civic belonging among future legal professionals. The practice entrenches division rather than building common understanding, and models for law students the acceptability of organizing society and institutions along lines of skin colour and ethnicity.

quotes.text: this week's workshop is for students who self-identify as Black, Indigenous and/or a Person of Colour
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement of exclusion, explicitly restricting the workshop to students of particular demographic backgrounds and barring all others from attending.

quotes.text: whether you're looking for a designated time to write and explore your creativity, a space to release some stress and anxiety, or an opportunity to connect with yourself and other BIPOC students, please join writer Preeti for a generative writing workshop on October 12 via Zoom at 6:30pm
quotes.rationale: Confirms the date of the event and frames participation in terms of connecting with others based on shared demographic identity rather than shared individual interests.

incidents.short_description: The British Columbia government's education ministry published a racism resource page that frames the problem and its solutions through demographic categories and identitarian concepts, directing students, parents, and schools to view people through the lens of group membership rather than individual worth.
incidents.description_of_event: The British Columbia Ministry of Education and Child Care published and maintained a racism information page as part of its erase (Expect Respect and A Safe Education) program, last updated October 3, 2022. The page frames the issues around concepts such as 'white privilege,' 'systemic racism,' 'intersectionality,' and 'racialized youth,' sorting people into demographic categories based on skin colour and ancestry. The page instructs students and parents that 'it is not enough to be not racist' and calls on individuals to 'dismantle racism in all its forms,' positioning this as a collective civic obligation. The reading recommendations directed at students and adults include ideologically oriented titles such as 'Me and White Supremacy,' 'White Fragility: Why It's So Hard For White People To Talk About Racism,' 'How to Be An Antiracist' by Ibram X. Kendi, and 'This Book is Anti-Racist: 20 Lessons on How to Wake Up, Take Action, and Do the Work.' These resources are associated with a particular ideological school of thought that understands racism primarily through group identity and collective guilt rather than through the lens of individual dignity and shared humanity. The page also describes 'white privilege' as a concept all students and parents should understand, telling people with lighter skin that the colour of their skin grants them advantages over others. A Community Roundtable on Anti-Racism was created to develop an anti-racism action plan described as intended to 'dismantle racism and discrimination' in the education system. The page was directed at students, families, and school staff across British Columbia's public school system.
incidents.expected_harm: Students and parents are directed by a government education authority to understand themselves and others primarily through the lens of skin colour and ancestry, undermining the recognition of each person as a distinct individual.
