Sorting people into categories of 'racialized' versus others, and telling students of lighter complexion that their skin colour confers privilege, assigns collective characteristics to individuals based on immutable traits rather than their character, actions, or circumstances. This approach divides the school community along demographic lines rather than fostering a sense of shared humanity and common civic belonging. Directing students toward ideologically oriented reading materials on these topics pushes a particular framework for understanding society without engaging with the full spectrum of perspectives, narrowing rather than broadening intellectual discourse. Students learn to evaluate themselves and their peers through demographic categories, which is contrary to a school environment where each person is assessed on their individual qualities and needs.

incidents.short_description: The British Columbia government's ERASE program published an official education page that frames racism using identitarian concepts — including 'white privilege' and 'systemic racism' — sorting students and families into demographic categories rather than addressing discrimination as a violation of individual dignity.
incidents.description_of_event: The British Columbia Ministry of Education and Child Care published a page on its ERASE (Expect Respect and A Safe Education) program website, last updated October 3, 2022, providing official guidance on racism in K–12 schools. The page defines racism through frameworks that sort people into demographic groups, instructing readers that society is structured to favour some groups over others based on skin colour and ancestry. The page introduces and endorses the concept of 'white privilege,' defining it as benefits received and barriers avoided specifically because of a person's skin colour. It directs students and families to understand participation in everyday life as potentially racist if they are unaware of 'systemic racism,' and calls on people to actively 'dismantle' these systems. The page categorizes students as 'racialized youth' and frames their experiences primarily through their demographic group membership. It recommends a curated reading list that includes ideologically uniform titles focused on concepts such as 'white fragility,' 'anti-racism,' and 'white supremacy,' authored by prominent identitarian advocates. The page also establishes a Community Roundtable on Anti-Racism and directs school districts to align codes of conduct with an anti-racism action plan developed through this framework. This represents the provincial government institutionalizing a particular ideological approach to discrimination — one centred on demographic group categorization — as official educational guidance for students, families, and school staff across British Columbia.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are taught by their provincial government to understand themselves and others primarily through the lens of skin colour and demographic group membership, rather than as individuals of singular worth. The framework instructs children that their skin colour determines the barriers they face and the privileges they hold, reducing individuals to demographic proxies. This undermines the development of a shared civic identity and common understanding of human dignity. Young people learn to evaluate themselves and their peers through racial categories, normalizing the institutional sorting of individuals by immutable characteristics. The one-sided reading list, composed entirely of works from a single ideological perspective, forecloses open and reasoned discourse on complex social questions, entrenching a particular worldview as official truth in the province's schools. Families who do not share this ideological framework find their children being taught a perspective on society — and on themselves — that they have no meaningful ability to contest within the school system.

incidents.short_description: The Ottawa-Carleton District School Board directly emailed high school students to recruit them into identity-based student groups and virtual sessions that were open only to students who identified with a particular sexual orientation or gender identity. Students were sorted and targeted based on these personal characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: The Ottawa-Carleton District School Board (OCDSB) sent a recruitment message directly to high school students asking whether they identified as a particular type of student based on their sexual orientation or gender identity, and inviting only those students to participate in school board-run activities. The message promoted a weekly Tuesday Google Meet session — described as an opportunity to meet other student leaders and earn volunteer hours — that was accessible only to students who identified in a specific way. A separate weekly Wednesday virtual gathering called "Gender Joy" was similarly restricted to students who identified within a defined category. Both sessions were facilitated by OCDSB staff, including a designated school board coordinator. The message was sent directly to students' email accounts, meaning the school board was proactively reaching out to sort and recruit students based on their personal identity characteristics. A parent shared the message publicly in February 2023, noting the board had been emailing students directly for recruitment purposes without parents necessarily being aware.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school board sends unsolicited emails to students asking them to self-identify based on sexual orientation or gender identity as a condition of participation, it normalizes the institutional practice of evaluating and sorting students by personal identity categories rather than their individual interests, abilities, or needs. Students who do not identify within the specified categories are excluded from volunteer hour opportunities and peer-connection sessions, creating a two-tiered system of participation based on identity. The practice also places pressure on minors to declare and label their sexual orientation or gender identity in order to access school board programming, which intrudes on students' privacy and autonomy. Volunteer hours earned through participation represent a tangible benefit that is denied to students on the basis of identity. The school board's direct outreach to students — bypassing parents — raises further concerns about transparency and the appropriateness of institutions communicating with minors about sensitive personal identity questions without parental knowledge.

quotes.text: Are you a 2SLGBTQ+ student looking to run a Gender & Sexuality Alliance (GSA) or make a change in your school?
quotes.rationale: This is the opening line of the recruitment message sent directly to students. It explicitly restricts the invitation to students who identify in a particular way, establishing the identity-based sorting at the heart of the incident.

quotes.text: Gender Joy - Virtual Gettogether for 2SLGBTQ+ Students in Grades 7-12
quotes.rationale: Names the identity-restricted virtual gathering, confirming it is a segregated event open only to students who identify in a specified way.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board designed a 2022 student census that asked students as young as grade 4 about their racial identity, sexual orientation, and gender identity — including questions about practices such as breast binding and tucking — and explicitly framed the data collection as a tool to advance particular political theories rooted in racial categorisation. The census was administered in class, raising serious concerns about the voluntary nature of participation and the privacy of sensitive personal data about minors.
incidents.description_of_event:
In October 2022, the Toronto District School Board (TDSB) prepared its fourth student census, to be completed in class by students in grades 4 through 12. The census questionnaires — separate versions for grades 4–6, 7–8, and 9–12 — asked students to identify their racial group, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation, gender identity, and disability status, as well as details about their parents' birthplace and education level. For grades 7 and above, the census also asked whether students had learned at school about practices such as breast binding, packing, tucking, or padding. The census was not anonymous.

The TDSB published a 40-page 'Guiding Research Principles' document, authored by three full-time TDSB research coordinators, stating that the census data would be analysed through frameworks including critical race theory, anti-colonial theory, anti-oppression theory, and quantitative critical race theory. The document stated the census teams would work to 'disrupt all structures of oppression,' 'dismantle all forms of colonial and settler colonial violence,' and centre specific demographic groups in the school system. The document's glossary characterised traditional Canadian commitments to colour-blind and multicultural policy as upholding white supremacy, and defined racism as something that can only exist when one group has 'power' over another. References cited in the document included academic works focused on the BDS movement and Palestinian politics.

The census was structured to be administered as an in-class activity rather than as a voluntary take-home survey. A Toronto lawyer wrote to the TDSB in October 2022 on behalf of concerned parents, raising grave concerns about the non-anonymous nature of the census, the lack of meaningful disclosure about how data would be used and shared with third parties, and whether the census complied with Ontario privacy legislation, including the Freedom of Information and Protection of Privacy Act and the Personal Health Information Protection Act. The lawyer argued that administering the census in class made participation effectively compulsory for many children, particularly younger students who would not understand they could opt out.

Following public attention and pushback, the TDSB removed the Guiding Research Principles document from its website — initially claiming the document was not compliant with accessibility requirements — and announced it was pausing the census to conduct an internal review. The TDSB stated the document had been posted without proper internal approval.
incidents.expected_harm:
Students as young as 8 or 9 are asked to disclose sensitive personal information — including racial identity, sexual orientation, and gender identity — in a classroom setting where the effective social pressure to participate is high. Many children will not understand they can refuse, and those who do opt out face the risk of being singled out in front of peers. The collection of this information in a non-anonymous database and its potential sharing with third parties creates lasting privacy risks for minors, including the risk of data breach or misuse. Families who have fled persecution based on personal characteristics are particularly vulnerable to the chilling effects of a government institution building detailed records linking children to racial, religious, and other identity categories.

The framing of the census data collection through explicitly partisan political theories means that the information gathered is not used to evaluate students as individuals with unique needs, but rather to sort and categorise them by demographic group. This approach embeds into the school system the assumption that a student's identity group — rather than their individual character, circumstances, or abilities — is the primary lens through which they should be understood and supported. Students learn that their school evaluates them as members of demographic categories, not as distinct persons. The census design reinforces division rather than shared civic belonging, treating students' racial, sexual, and gender identities as the defining features of their school experience and directing institutional resources according to group membership rather than individual need.

quotes.text: Historically, data collection and evidence have been abused, misused and exploited in ways that harmed communities, reads the official census guidelines, before adding that this time around, they'll be explicitly using census data to disrupt all structures of oppression and centre Indigeneity and Black lives in the school system.
quotes.rationale: Shows the census is explicitly designed to centre particular demographic groups rather than treat students as individuals, directly contrary to the Coalition's principle of singular worth.

quotes.text: An attached glossary says that the traditional Canadian belief in colour-blind and multicultural policy is itself an upholder of white supremacy. The glossary also says that racism is not simple prejudice, and can only exist when one group has power over another — ergo, that racism against privileged groups doesn't exist.
quotes.rationale: The TDSB's own document explicitly rejects equal treatment as an institutional value, instead encoding a framework that treats different demographic groups differently based on perceived group power.

quotes.text: A 15-page census questionnaire to be distributed to those in grades four through six asks students their race, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation and whether their parents were born in Canada or completed university.
quotes.rationale: Documents the specific sensitive personal data categories collected from students as young as grade 4, forming the basis for privacy and categorisation concerns.

incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary launched a program to hire 45 professors across all faculties in which some positions are explicitly restricted to applicants of specific skin colours, ethnicities, or sexes. The initiative institutionalises demographic categorisation as a central criterion for academic employment.
incidents.description_of_event: In October 2022, the University of Calgary launched its Inclusive Excellence Cluster Hiring Initiative, committing to recruit 45 professors over three academic years (2022–2023 to 2024–2025) across all faculties. The initiative explicitly restricts eligibility for a number of positions based on candidates' demographic characteristics. Specific postings listed on the university's website include: a Director of Indigenous Initiatives position in the Faculty of Nursing open only to candidates who are First Nations (Status or Non-Status), Métis, or Inuit, with verifiable evidence of indigenous identity required; a professorial position in the Haskayne School of Business open only to candidates who are black; and a professorial position also in the Haskayne School of Business open only to women. The university frames the initiative as an ameliorative measure under Section 10 of the Alberta Human Rights Act and Section 15(2) of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, and grounds it in federal employment equity obligations. All faculties — including Medicine, Engineering, Law, Science, Social Work, Education, Architecture, Veterinary Medicine, Nursing, and Business — are participating. The university collects disaggregated self-identification data on skin colour, ethnicity, indigenous identity, disability, and sexual orientation to identify what it calls equity gaps, and uses those gaps to determine hiring targets. The institution's stated goal is to create a professoriate that demographically mirrors the student body and broader Calgary community. The initiative was publicly announced and positions were advertised through the university's careers website.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from competing for publicly funded academic positions solely because of their skin colour, sex, or ethnicity — immutable characteristics that have no bearing on their ability to teach, conduct research, or contribute to a university community. The university, as a public institution, sorts applicants into demographic categories and grants or withholds access to employment on that basis, directly contradicting the principle that people deserve to be evaluated as individuals based on character, abilities, and demonstrated merit. Candidates who fall outside the designated demographic categories for specific positions are turned away at the door regardless of their qualifications, perpetuating a system in which demographic identity — not individual excellence — is a gateway to opportunity. The practice normalises the institutional labelling of people by skin colour, ancestry, and sex, signalling to the entire campus community that demographic categories are the appropriate lens through which to evaluate individuals. Over time this entrenches division within the academy rather than fostering a shared scholarly community united by common intellectual purpose. Students observe an institution that selects their teachers and mentors on demographic grounds, modelling the idea that group membership is a legitimate and desirable basis for consequential decisions about people.

quotes.text: Over the next three years, all UCalgary faculties will participate in our Inclusive Excellence Cluster Hiring Initiative, which will recruit 45 professors from equity-deserving groups.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the scope and nature of the hiring initiative — 45 positions restricted to specific demographic groups across the entire university.

quotes.text: This position is only open to qualified Indigenous scholars (First Nations (Status, Non-Status), Métis, or Inuit). Candidates will be required to provide verifiable evidence of their Indigenous identity.
quotes.rationale: Explicit restriction of a position to candidates of a specific ethnicity/ancestry, requiring documentary proof of identity — a direct eligibility restriction based on immutable demographic characteristics.

quotes.text: This position is only open to qualified Black scholars (e.g. Black Pioneer, African, Caribbean).
quotes.rationale: Explicit restriction of an academic position to candidates of a specific skin colour, excluding all other qualified applicants on demographic grounds.

quotes.text: This position is only open to qualified women candidates.
quotes.rationale: Explicit restriction of an academic position to candidates of one sex, closing the competition to all other qualified individuals regardless of merit.

incidents.institution_name: Queen's University > Department of Gender Studies
incidents.short_description: Queen's University's Department of Gender Studies organized a book drive for inmates at Collins Bay Institution that restricted participation to those of certain skin colours and ethnicities, excluding other inmates from access to donated books and resources.
incidents.description_of_event: The Department of Gender Studies at Queen's University in Kingston, Ontario promoted a book drive during October aimed at building an 'Ethnocultural Library' for inmates at Collins Bay Institution. The initiative explicitly restricted its support to inmates categorized under the 'BIPOC' label — a demographic designation based on skin colour and ethnicity — rather than making the donated books and resources available to all inmates regardless of background. Donation drop-off locations included Stauffer Library, the John Howard Society, Mackintosh-Corry Hall at Queen's, and Novel Idea bookstore. The areas of interest for donated books included works by authors selected based on their skin colour or ethnicity, writings on anti-oppression and anti-racist themes, new language instruction, business and finance materials, and legal resources. The initiative was organized and promoted through the department's social media presence, signalling institutional endorsement of sorting charitable resources according to inmates' demographic categories rather than their individual needs or interests.
incidents.expected_harm: Inmates at Collins Bay Institution who do not fall within the designated demographic category are excluded from receiving donated books and educational resources, not based on their individual needs, interests, or circumstances, but solely because of their skin colour or ethnicity. This treats people in a vulnerable setting as members of demographic groups rather than as individuals deserving support based on their own situations. A university department — an institution with significant influence over students' values and civic understanding — normalizes the practice of sorting people and distributing charitable resources according to immutable characteristics. Students and community members who participate in or observe this initiative are taught that categorizing individuals by skin colour and ethnicity is an appropriate basis for institutional and charitable decision-making, undermining the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity regardless of their background.

quotes.text: Support BIPOC inmates at Collins Bay Institution in building their Ethnocultural Library with relevant and intellectually engaging books!
quotes.rationale: This is the central statement of the initiative and directly demonstrates that support is restricted to inmates categorized by skin colour and ethnicity, rather than being offered to all inmates based on individual need.

quotes.text: Areas of Interest: BIPOC authors and theorists, BIPOC literatures, New language instruction, Writings on anti-oppression, anti-racist struggle, Business and finance, Law and legal resources
quotes.rationale: Shows that even the content of donated materials was curated according to demographic categories, further entrenching the sorting of charitable resources by skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual interest or need.

incidents.short_description: Memorial University posted a job advertisement for a Canada Research Chair position that explicitly stated preference would be given to candidates based on their sex, sexual orientation, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability status rather than evaluating all applicants equally on their qualifications and merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Memorial University posted a job advertisement on September 27, 2022, for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Ocean Biosciences in the Department of Ocean Sciences. The position was a tenure-track appointment at the Assistant Professor or Associate Professor level. The advertisement outlined academic qualifications including a Ph.D., post-doctoral experience, peer-reviewed publications, capacity for research, and commitment to teaching. However, the job posting explicitly stated institutional preference for candidates based on demographic categories. The advertisement declared that Memorial University encourages applications from candidates based on their sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, indigenous heritage, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability status. The posting required applicants to complete an employment equity survey to be considered as members of target groups, stating that applicants cannot be considered as members of these groups unless they complete the survey. This approach framed the hiring process around categorizing candidates by immutable characteristics rather than evaluating each applicant as an individual based on their unique qualifications, research potential, teaching abilities, and character.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated and categorized by the institution based on their sex, sexual orientation, skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and other immutable characteristics rather than being assessed solely as individuals on their academic qualifications, research potential, and teaching abilities. This practice normalizes institutional sorting of people into demographic groups and signals that these categories are relevant factors in determining who receives opportunities. Qualified candidates who do not fit preferred demographic categories face explicit institutional preference against them regardless of their individual merit, abilities, or potential contributions to the field. The requirement to complete a survey identifying oneself by demographic categories to be considered for target group status pressures applicants to label themselves according to institutional categories rather than being evaluated on their character and capabilities. This undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated abilities. The approach teaches that institutions should view people through the lens of demographic proxies rather than recognizing each person's inherent dignity as a distinct individual with unique qualities and circumstances.

quotes.text: Memorial University is committed to employment equity and diversity and encourages applications from all qualified candidates including women; people of any sexual orientation, gender identity, or gender expression; Indigenous peoples; visible minorities and racialized people; and people with disabilities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly lists demographic categories that the institution considers relevant in its hiring process, demonstrating that the university evaluates candidates through the lens of immutable characteristics rather than solely on individual qualifications and merit.

quotes.text: As part of Memorial University's commitment to employment equity, all applicants are invited to identify themselves as a member of a target group(s) as appropriate. Applicants cannot be considered as a member of a target group(s) unless they complete an employment equity survey.
quotes.rationale: This demonstrates the institutional requirement for applicants to categorize themselves by demographic characteristics, and that such categorization has consequences for how their applications are evaluated, rather than assessing candidates purely as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The Upper Grand District School Board administered a student census that required students from grade 7 upward — and parents of younger children — to disclose their child's sexual orientation and gender identity, alongside questions about skin colour, ethnicity, and religion. The census linked these personal identity disclosures to individual student academic records.
incidents.description_of_event:
In September 2022, the Upper Grand District School Board launched the UGDSB Counts Student Census for all students from Kindergarten to Grade 12. The census was administered online between September 19 and October 9, 2022, with class time provided for high school students to complete it.

The census collected a broad range of personal identity information from students and their families, including: first language, indigeneity, ethnic or cultural origins, skin colour category, religious or spiritual affiliation, gender identity, sexual orientation, disability status, citizenship status, and socio-economic status (through questions about parents' education and employment).

For students in Kindergarten to Grade 8, parents and legal guardians were asked to supply this information on behalf of their children — including answering questions about the child's gender identity and, for those in Grade 7 or above, the child's sexual orientation. For students in Grades 9 to 12, students were asked to answer directly, including disclosing their own sexual orientation and gender identity.

The census was described as confidential but explicitly not anonymous. Individual student responses were linked to existing student data records — including attendance, achievement, credit accumulation, suspensions and expulsions, special education services, and program participation — for the stated purpose of identifying patterns by demographic group. The school board stated the data collection was authorised under the Anti-Racism Act and the Education Act, and framed the exercise as identifying barriers faced by students based on their identity categories.

The survey framing included explanatory preambles presenting ideas about skin colour and race as social constructs imposed on people, and describing religion as something that can be "racialized." The board described its purpose as identifying and dismantling "systemic racism and oppression" by collecting identity-based data and linking it to student outcomes.
incidents.expected_harm:
Students as young as Grade 7 — roughly 12 years old — are asked by their school institution to formally disclose and record their sexual orientation. This is a deeply personal matter that many young people have not yet resolved, discussed with family, or chosen to share in any context. Having a school institution ask this question in a formal, named (non-anonymous) survey, linked to their academic record, puts pressure on minors to label themselves at a sensitive developmental stage. Some students will feel compelled to answer or will be distressed by the question itself.

For younger children in Kindergarten through Grade 6, parents are asked to formally assign and record their child's gender identity using institutional categories. This places children's developing sense of self into a permanent, named institutional record at an age when such questions are far from settled.

The census treats every student as a bundle of demographic categories rather than as an individual. By linking identity disclosures to academic outcomes and analysing results by group membership — skin colour, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation — the board embeds demographic labelling into the foundation of how it understands and responds to students. Students learn that their school views them primarily through the lens of these categories, rather than as distinct individuals with unique circumstances, abilities, and needs.

The fact that the census is confidential but not anonymous means that sensitive personal disclosures — including sexual orientation and religious affiliation — are permanently tied to identifiable student records. Even with data security measures in place, this creates lasting institutional records of private information disclosed by or on behalf of children.

quotes.text: The Ministry of Education requires school boards to collect identity-based student data. This is in order to "identify and monitor racial disparities in order to eliminate systemic racism and advance racial equity." The census data will be joined with information we already collect for research purposes (e.g., attendance, student achievement, credit accumulation, graduation rate, suspensions/ expulsions/ exclusions, special education services, and academic/ extracurricular program participation).
quotes.rationale: Reveals that personal identity data — including skin colour, sexual orientation, and religion — is linked to individual academic records, reducing students to demographic categories in institutional data systems.

quotes.text:
Sexual orientation refers to a person's sense of sexual attraction to people of the same or different sex.

What is your child's sexual orientation? Select all that apply.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that parents of children in Grade 7 and above are asked to formally record their child's sexual orientation in a named institutional census. This is a sensitive question directed at minors through a non-anonymous institutional survey.

quotes.text:
Parents/ legal guardians/ students will be asked this question if the student is in grade 7 or above.

Sexual orientation refers to a person's sense of sexual attraction to people of the same or different sex.

What is your sexual orientation? Select all that apply.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that high school students are asked directly to disclose their sexual orientation as part of a school-administered, non-anonymous census linked to their academic records.

quotes.text:
What is your child's gender identity? Select all that apply.

Boy/man
Gender Fluid
Gender Variant
Girl/woman
Non-Binary
Questioning
Trans boy or man
Trans girl or woman
Two-Spirit
quotes.rationale: Parents of all students from Kindergarten to Grade 8 are asked to formally assign and record their child's gender identity in institutional categories, including for very young children.

quotes.text: The census is confidential, but it is not anonymous. The collected responses will be encrypted and stored in a secure, password protected database to which only select Upper Grand District School Board staff will have access.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that sensitive identity disclosures by and about children — including sexual orientation and religion — are permanently tied to identifiable individual student records, not anonymised.

quotes.text: The student identity-based data will be joined with existing data in all of the areas below and then analyzed with the single purpose of identifying areas where UGDSB's policies and programs are creating barriers to the educational outcomes of your children based on their identity.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly states that the purpose is to evaluate students' outcomes through the lens of their identity categories, institutionalising the practice of assessing students by demographic group rather than as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The Waterloo Region District School Board posted a job opening for a 'Temporary Systems Navigator - Muslim Focus,' restricting or targeting applicants based on religious identity. Hiring for a publicly funded school board role on the basis of religion raises serious concerns about equal access to public employment.
incidents.description_of_event: On September 7, 2022, the Waterloo Region District School Board's careers account posted a job opening for a 'Temporary Systems Navigator - Muslim Focus,' with an application deadline of 4:00 PM on September 14, 2022. The posting was shared publicly on social media. The title of the role explicitly identifies a specific religion as a focus or qualification criterion, indicating that the position was designed to be filled by, or to serve, individuals of a particular religious identity. As a publicly funded school board, the WRDSB is advertising a role in which a person's religion appears to be a relevant hiring consideration, rather than evaluating applicants solely on their character, skills, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a publicly funded institution structures employment around religious identity, it signals to the public that access to certain jobs depends on one's religion rather than individual merit and qualifications. This reduces applicants to a demographic category rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. It also sets a precedent for publicly funded employers to fragment roles along religious lines, undermining the principle that public institutions serve and employ all people equally, regardless of their religious background.

quotes.text: We are hiring a Temporary Systems Navigator - Muslim Focus. Applications must be received by 4:00 PM on September 14, 2022
quotes.rationale: This is the core posting that identifies the role by religious focus, establishing that religion is a criterion built into the job title and presumably the hiring criteria.

incidents.short_description: The Waterloo Region District School Board created a dedicated System Navigator support service restricted to students and families based on their skin colour and ethnicity. School board support services should be available to all students and families based on individual need, not demographic identity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Waterloo Region District School Board established a System Navigator programme explicitly restricted to students and families who identify as African, Caribbean, or Black. Promotional materials, including a poster distributed by a school board-connected individual on social media on September 5, 2022, directed only those students to the service. The poster listed eligibility criteria beginning with the question "Are you African, Caribbean, or Black identifying?" — making ethnic and skin-colour identity a prerequisite for accessing support. The programme's stated purpose was to help families navigate their relationship with the school, understand programme placements, and make informed decisions about their children's education. A dedicated staff member, identified as a System Navigator, was assigned to handle these requests through a confidential intake form created within the school board's systems. The intake form was similarly framed as support exclusively for African, Caribbean, and Black students and families, and was promoted through the board's Equity and Inclusion Branch. Families not identified within these demographic categories have no equivalent dedicated navigator resource, meaning access to this navigational support is determined by a family's skin colour and ethnic background rather than by their individual circumstances or needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Families and students are sorted into categories based on skin colour and ethnicity to determine whether they can access a dedicated school navigation support service. Families outside these demographic categories who face difficulties navigating the school system — understanding programme placements, engaging with staff, or advocating for their children — are denied equivalent support. This institutional approach normalises the practice of evaluating individuals through demographic proxies rather than their actual, individual circumstances and needs. It sends a message to all students that the school board views them first and foremost as members of a demographic group rather than as distinct individuals. Rather than building a unified school community where all families are supported in navigating the system, this creates a two-tiered structure where access to support is contingent on identity, deepening division and undermining the shared civic belonging that schools should foster.

quotes.text: Attn all African, Caribbean and Black identifying students in the @wrdsb - know that if you're experiencing anti-Black racism in any form, there's a System Navigator who can support you.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that the System Navigator service was publicly directed only at students identified by skin colour and ethnicity, not all students in need.

quotes.text: Are you African, Caribbean, or Black identifying?
quotes.rationale: Direct eligibility criterion on the promotional poster, making ethnic and skin-colour identity an explicit prerequisite for accessing the service.

quotes.text: System Navigator - Support for African, Caribbean, and Black Students and Families
quotes.rationale: The official title of the intake form confirms the programme is formally restricted to families of a specific skin colour and ethnic background.

quotes.text: The System Navigator is dedicated to building trust between Black communities, students, families and the Waterloo Region District School Board in order to address and prevent issues of Anti-Black racism in all its forms.
quotes.rationale: The stated scope of the programme explicitly limits its purpose and intended beneficiaries to a demographically defined group rather than any student or family in need.

incidents.institution_name: Dalhousie University > Faculty of Medicine > Global Health Office
incidents.short_description: Dalhousie University's Faculty of Medicine Global Health Office ran a recurring professional development seminar series called the 'White Fragility Clinic,' which framed participants by their skin colour and aimed to build 'racial resilience' in attendees. The program categorises faculty, staff, and health professionals by skin colour as the basis for professional development.
incidents.description_of_event: The Dalhousie University Faculty of Medicine's Global Health Office organized and promoted a recurring online professional development seminar series titled the 'White Fragility Clinic,' offered every second Thursday beginning in September 2022. The series was led by Dr. Gaynor Watson Creed and Dr. Eli Manning and was open to faculty, staff in the Faculty of Medicine, and health professionals. The program was framed as an Anti-Oppression and EDI seminar series. Its stated learning objectives were to explore 'the concept of whiteness and its role in racism' and to 'build and practice racial resilience in attendees.' The program title and learning objectives explicitly categorise participants by skin colour, treating a person's perceived skin colour as the defining lens through which professional development is delivered. The series recurred across the 2022–2023 academic year, with sessions listed from September 2022 through at least November 2022, and archival references suggest the clinic ran in subsequent years as well (including 2024–2025 and 2025–2026 editions).
incidents.expected_harm: Faculty, staff, and health professionals at Dalhousie's Faculty of Medicine are subjected to professional development that evaluates and addresses them primarily through the lens of their skin colour rather than as individuals with distinct professional needs. The program normalises the institutional practice of sorting people into categories based on skin colour and assigning them different educational experiences accordingly. Health professionals trained under this framework learn to view colleagues and patients through demographic categories rather than as individuals. The use of a term like 'white fragility' as an institutional program title embeds a pejorative characterisation of people based on their skin colour into a professional development context, creating an environment where some employees are singled out and defined by race-based labels. This undermines a workplace culture of individual dignity and shared professional purpose.

quotes.text:
To explore in a safe and non judgmental space issues including the concept of whiteness and its role in racism
To build and practice racial resilience in attendees.
quotes.rationale: These are the stated learning objectives of the program. They reveal that the seminar explicitly frames participants by skin colour ('whiteness') and aims to modify their attitudes and dispositions based on that racial categorisation.

quotes.text: White Fragility Clinic 2022–2023
quotes.rationale: The program title itself categorises participants by skin colour using activist terminology, embedding a skin-colour-based label into an official institutional professional development series.

quotes.text:
2025/2026 White Fragility Clinic
2024 - 2025 White Fragility Clinic
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that the program is not a one-off event but a recurring, institutionalised series that has continued across multiple academic years, indicating ongoing institutional commitment to this approach.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Court of Appeal ruled in R v. King that a person's ethnicity must be considered when deciding how much of their criminal record can be used at trial, creating a two-tiered procedural standard based on ancestry.
incidents.description_of_event:
In late September 2022, the Ontario Court of Appeal issued a ruling in R v. King, a homicide case arising from the 2017 fatal shooting of Yosif Al-Hasnawi in Hamilton. The court held that a person's indigenous ancestry is a relevant factor when a trial judge decides whether the prosecution may cross-examine an accused on their criminal record — a procedure that directly affects how credible a jury may find that person's testimony.

At trial, the judge had suppressed part of Dale King's lengthy criminal record on the basis that exposing the full record could reinforce stereotypes connected to his ancestry, making the proceedings unfair. The appeal court upheld this approach and established that Ontario judges going forward must weigh a person's ethnic background when ruling on criminal record admissibility.

The decision extends a line of Canadian case law that ties procedural rights and judicial discretion to a person's ancestry or ethnic background, including the 1999 Supreme Court decision in R v. Gladue (sentencing), a 2019 Supreme Court ruling linking minority status to the threshold for establishing state detention, and the 2022 Supreme Court decision in R v. Lafrance (right to counsel). The effect is that the procedural rules governing a criminal trial differ depending on the ethnic category courts assign to the accused.
incidents.expected_harm: When courts adjust procedural rules based on the ethnic category of an accused person, they treat individuals not as persons with unique circumstances but as representatives of demographic groups. This creates a justice system in which the rights and protections available to a person at trial depend on their ancestry rather than on the facts of their case and their individual history. Equal treatment under the law — the foundation of a fair justice system — is replaced with a framework that sorts people by ethnic label and applies different standards accordingly. Victims and their families, regardless of their own backgrounds, receive a different quality of justice depending on the demographic classification of the person accused of harming them. The practice also removes the incentive to address the root causes of social disadvantage, substituting demographic adjustments inside the courtroom for the harder work of improving conditions outside it.

quotes.text: The Ontario Court of Appeal ruled late September that the criminal records of Indigenous persons can be excluded from their trials to preserve fairness. Trial fairness, to Ontario's top court, means creating race-based procedural rules to supposedly make up for the effects of systemic racism. In other words, these rules aren't fair at all.
quotes.rationale: Summarises the core ruling and directly identifies the mechanism: procedural rules in criminal trials are adjusted based on the ethnic category of the accused.

quotes.text: The R v. King appeal explored whether it's acceptable to consider a person's Indigenous background when deciding whether the prosecution should be able to cross-examine them on their criminal record; this cross-examination helps determine just how credible an accused person's testimony is.
quotes.rationale: Explains the specific procedural question decided by the court and its practical impact on trial outcomes.

quotes.text: On appeal the Crown objected to the fact that part of King's lengthy criminal record was suppressed at trial. The trial judge had reasoned that this was fair because of systemic discrimination against Indigenous people and experiences of family separation, transience, addiction and abuse. The full criminal record could prejudice the jury against King due to racial stereotypes, so the judge opted to keep some of it out.
quotes.rationale: Describes the trial-level reasoning that the appeal court endorsed: an individual's criminal history was filtered through the lens of their ethnic group membership rather than assessed on its own merits.

quotes.text: Judges in Ontario going forward will now need to consider race when letting someone's criminal record into trial, because racial stereotypes arising from a criminal record might lead to unfair treatment.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the prospective, binding nature of the ruling — it establishes a new standard that applies to all future Ontario trials.

quotes.text: But together, they mean that the charter protects Canadians differently depending on race, which is fundamentally unfair. Procedures should apply to everyone equally, and if certain groups are imprisoned at greater rates, that's a sign that underlying problems outside the justice system need to be fixed. Instead, the answer from our courts seems to be to use overt systemic discrimination 'to level the playing field.'
quotes.rationale: Articulates the systemic concern: Charter protections now vary by ethnic category, and demographic adjustment inside the courtroom substitutes for addressing underlying social conditions.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto > Faculty of Law
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto Faculty of Law restricted enrolment in its Julius Alexander Isaac Moot to students who are members of the Black Law Students Association, barring other law students from participating based on their skin colour.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Toronto Faculty of Law offered a credit-bearing competitive moot program — the Julius Alexander Isaac Competitive Program (LAW422Y1Y) — worth three academic credits and open to second- and third-year JD students. However, enrolment was explicitly conditioned on students being members of the Black Law Students Association (BLSA). Students who did not hold that membership were ineligible to participate, regardless of their interest, qualifications, or academic standing. The requirement was stated directly in the course listing for the 2022–2023 academic year. Because BLSA membership is defined by students' skin colour, the Faculty of Law created a racially segregated academic program in which access to a credited learning opportunity — including moot preparation, oral argumentation, and written advocacy experience — was determined by a student's demographic background rather than their individual abilities or interests.
incidents.expected_harm: Law students are excluded from a credited academic opportunity solely because of their skin colour. Students denied access to the moot lose out on valuable oral and written advocacy training, three academic credits, and the professional development that comes with competitive moot participation. The policy entrenches the practice of sorting students into groups based on immutable characteristics, normalising the idea that a person's skin colour determines which institutional opportunities are available to them. This undermines the principle that each student deserves to be evaluated and given access to programs based on their individual merit and demonstrated interest, not their demographic category. It also deepens divisions within the law school community by signalling that some educational experiences are reserved for particular groups rather than being open to all.

quotes.text: Students in second and third year must be members of the Black Law Students Association (BLSA) to qualify for this moot.
quotes.rationale: This is the core restriction: enrolment in a credit-bearing moot is conditioned on membership in an organisation defined by skin colour, directly excluding students on a demographic basis.

quotes.text: Note: 3 credits (conditional enrol)
quotes.rationale: Confirms the program carries real academic credit, meaning the demographic restriction affects students' academic records and professional development, not merely an extracurricular activity.

incidents.short_description: Queen's University's vice-chancellor issued a statement confirming the university had accepted in principle that employment decisions requiring indigenous representation would involve a new process for verifying individuals' claims to indigenous identity, while also affirming academic freedom and commitment to current employees.
incidents.description_of_event: On August 26, 2022, Queen's University Principal and Vice-Chancellor Patrick Deane issued a public statement to faculty regarding a report titled "GII-IKIDONAANIWAN - IT HAS BEEN SAID," produced by the First Peoples Group on indigenous identity. The statement confirmed that Queen's accepted in principle that a new process is needed to confirm claims to indigenous identity when future employment considerations require indigenous representation. This means the university is formally establishing a system under which employees and candidates may be evaluated and assessed based on their ancestry and ethnic identity as a criterion for certain positions. The statement indicated that the Office of Indigenous Initiatives would announce next steps for developing this verification process. At the same time, the vice-chancellor sought to reassure current faculty and staff that existing employees would not be dismissed over unresolved identity questions, and affirmed that academic expertise — not identity — remains the sole criterion for teaching roles. The university framed the report as a starting point, reserving the right to accept or reject its specific recommendations.
incidents.expected_harm: By accepting in principle that employment decisions will incorporate a process for verifying individuals' claims to a particular ethnic or ancestral identity, Queen's University normalises the institutional practice of evaluating people based on their ancestry rather than their individual character, abilities, and expertise. This creates a framework where a person's access to certain employment opportunities is tied to whether they can satisfy an institution's judgement about their ethnic or ancestral identity — reducing individuals to demographic categories. The verification process also invites institutional scrutiny of a deeply personal and complex aspect of a person's background, placing the university in the role of arbiter of ethnic belonging. This undermines the principle that people should be evaluated as individuals and that employment decisions should reflect merit and demonstrated ability rather than demographic representation targets.

quotes.text: Queen's accepted the principle that a new process for confirming claims to Indigenous identity is needed when future employment considerations require Indigenous representation.
quotes.rationale: This is the core admission that Queen's is building a system to verify employees' ethnic/ancestral identity for employment purposes — directly relevant to the concern about categorising individuals by demographic traits.

incidents.short_description: The Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board publicly advertised separate principal and vice-principal job postings restricted by skin colour, directing some candidates to a different application stream based on their demographic group.
incidents.description_of_event: On August 25, 2022, the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board (HWDSB) posted on social media announcing it was building a leadership pool of candidates for future elementary and secondary Principal and Vice-Principal positions. The announcement provided two distinct job posting links: one labelled for all candidates, and a separate posting explicitly designated for candidates based on their skin colour and perceived racial identity. This meant that a formal, publicly announced hiring stream existed in which access to a particular job posting was determined by a person's membership in a demographic category rather than by their individual qualifications, experience, or demonstrated abilities. The board framed this approach as building a diverse leadership pool.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for leadership positions are evaluated and streamed based on their skin colour and perceived racial identity rather than their individual character, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities. This institutional practice reduces people to demographic categories and signals that the school board considers a person's immutable traits to be relevant criteria in hiring processes. It sets a precedent that advancement within a public institution is tied to demographic identity, which undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as a unique individual. Students, families, and staff receive a message from their school board that demographic categorization is an appropriate basis for institutional decisions, normalizing the sorting of people by immutable traits. It forecloses the possibility of a merit-based process that respects each candidate's singular worth and prevents the building of a genuinely shared civic institution.

quotes.text: HWDSB is creating a diverse leadership pool of qualified candidates for future Elementary and Secondary Principal and Vice-Principal positions See our job postings to learn more! All candidates: [link] Black and Racialized candidates: [link]
quotes.rationale: This is the primary evidence: the board's own public announcement explicitly advertising separate job postings differentiated by skin colour and perceived racial identity.

quotes.text: Black and Racialized candidates: network.applytoeducation.com/Applicant/AttJ...
quotes.rationale: Screenshot confirms the separate posting link directed specifically at candidates based on their skin colour and racial categorisation, corroborating the Twitter post text.

incidents.short_description: Here For Now Theatre in Stratford, Ontario hosted a theatre performance exclusively for audience members who identify based on their skin colour, barring all others from attending. This is a racially segregated event, regardless of the intentions behind it.
incidents.description_of_event: On August 9, 2022, Here For Now Theatre hosted a performance of its production Every Day She Rose at the Falstaff Family Centre in Stratford, Ontario, under the name 'Black Out Night.' Attendance at this performance was restricted exclusively to audience members who identify based on their skin colour — specifically, those who self-identify as a particular group. All other members of the public were excluded from attending this performance. The event was framed by the theatre company as a way to make theatre more accessible and to create a 'safe space' for a racially defined audience. The organizers explicitly described the night as 'an exclusively Black-identifying' performance, meaning the sole criterion for entry was a demographic characteristic — a person's skin colour and identity. Other performances of the same production were open to the general public, but this specific performance was closed to anyone who did not identify under the designated skin-colour category. Tickets were offered on a pay-what-you-can basis for this segregated performance.
incidents.expected_harm: People are turned away from a public cultural event based solely on their skin colour. This practice — restricting access to a venue and performance based on a demographic characteristic — treats individuals not as unique people with their own interests and merits, but as members of a racial category. Those who do not identify under the designated skin-colour group are excluded from participating in a shared cultural experience in their own community. This normalises the idea that institutions should sort and separate people based on immutable traits, and undermines the shared civic belonging and common humanity that cultural institutions are well placed to foster. It reinforces the habit of viewing people primarily through the lens of skin colour rather than as individuals with equal standing in a shared community.

incidents.short_description: Vancouver Coastal Health published a multi-part social media thread identifying a named employee by her sexual orientation and relationship structure as part of a staff profiling campaign, then deleted the thread. The post disclosed intimate personal details about the employee in a way that reduces her to demographic categories rather than recognising her as an individual professional.
incidents.description_of_event: In August 2022, Vancouver Coastal Health (VCH), a publicly funded health authority in British Columbia, published a thread on its official Twitter account profiling Alix Bacon, a registered midwife, clinical instructor at UBC, and president of the Canadian Association of Midwives. The thread was part of a campaign in which VCH stated it would share stories from medical staff based on their membership in a particular identity group throughout August. Rather than focusing on Bacon's professional accomplishments, the thread prominently disclosed that she is queer and polyamorous, framing these personal details as central to her story and her experience working in health care. The thread quoted Bacon describing the experience of choosing whether to disclose her relationship structure to others and discussing how she navigates assumptions about her personal life. VCH used the profile to advocate for administrative changes to health care paperwork related to gender and family structure. The thread was subsequently deleted, which Jonathan Kay noted publicly on 10 August 2022.
incidents.expected_harm: By profiling an employee primarily through the lens of her sexual orientation and relationship structure, the institution reduces a accomplished professional to a set of demographic and personal identity labels rather than recognising her as an individual whose worth is defined by her character, skills, and contributions. The public disclosure of intimate personal details — including details about her relationship structure — without clear evidence this was meaningfully informed or freely separated from workplace dynamics, sets a troubling precedent for how employers treat staff. The campaign also reinforces the institutional habit of sorting and presenting people according to identity group membership rather than as distinct individuals, normalising the idea that a person's demographic characteristics are their most newsworthy attribute. This undermines the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated and recognised as an individual rather than as a representative of a group.

quotes.text: Throughout August, VCH will be sharing stories and lived experiences from medical staff who belong to the 2SLGBTQIA+ community. #Pride
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the campaign explicitly selects and profiles employees on the basis of their sexual orientation and related identity, rather than on professional merit or accomplishment.

quotes.text: Like many others in the 2SLGBTQIA+ community, Alix faces a constant choice about coming out — over and over — or to lean into the privilege of passing as heteromonogomous.
quotes.rationale: The institution frames the employee's professional and personal life through the lens of her sexual orientation and relationship structure, reinforcing the pattern of categorising individuals by demographic group.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
incidents.short_description: The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat posted administrative job openings exclusively available to applicants who self-declare membership in designated demographic groups, excluding all other Canadians from consideration. People are evaluated for employment based on their demographic identity rather than their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat (TBS) advertised AS-01 and AS-02 administrative positions through GC Jobs, with a closing date of 13 January 2023. The posting explicitly restricted eligibility to individuals who self-declare as belonging to one or more of the following groups: aboriginal peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities. The posting stated clearly that applicants must self-declare membership in one of these groups at the time of application in order to be considered. TBS also promoted the posting on Twitter, reinforcing that the administrative jobs were available only to members of these designated groups. The intent of the process was stated to be building an inventory of candidates drawn exclusively from these demographic categories for use by hiring managers. Applicants who do not identify as belonging to any of the designated groups are categorically excluded from consideration, regardless of their qualifications, experience, or individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified Canadians are barred from applying for government positions based solely on their demographic identity rather than their individual character, skills, or demonstrated abilities. The hiring process evaluates people as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals, directly contradicting the principle that each person deserves recognition based on their unique qualities. This practice entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by group membership and signals to all Canadians that government employment is distributed on the basis of identity rather than merit. It normalises the idea that demographic characteristics are a legitimate and primary basis for access to public service opportunities, undermining shared civic belonging and the equal standing of all citizens before public institutions.

quotes.text:
Self-declaring as belonging to one or more of the following Employment Equity groups: Aboriginal peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities.
*In order for you to be considered for this process, you must self-declare at the time of application as a member of one or more of the following Employment Equity groups: Aboriginal peoples, members of visible minorities and persons with disabilities.*
quotes.rationale: This is the core eligibility restriction: applicants who do not belong to the listed demographic groups are categorically excluded from consideration, making demographic identity a prerequisite for employment.

quotes.text: Selection will be limited to members of the following Employment Equity groups: Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, visible minorities.
quotes.rationale: Explicit statement that selection — not merely preference — is limited to designated demographic groups, confirming the exclusionary nature of the hiring process.

quotes.text: The intent of this process is to create an inventory as an employment equity source of talent for hiring managers which consists of candidates who self-declare as a member of one or more of the following employment equity groups: Aboriginal peoples, person with disabilities and members of visible minorities.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the institutional design of the process: the talent pool for future government administrative hires is being built exclusively on the basis of demographic group membership.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Housing, Infrastructure and Communities Canada
incidents.short_description: Infrastructure Canada required all staff to attend training sessions, including a documentary screening and content from Egale Canada covering identity classification frameworks and sexual orientation labelling systems.
incidents.description_of_event: On August 3, 2022, Infrastructure Canada's Anti-Racism Equity and Inclusion team held a required Microsoft Teams event for all INFC users, which included a screening of the documentary 'Sex, Sin & 69.' The session was bilingual and marked as required for all Infrastructure Canada staff. Additional training materials visible in the evidence, shared under the same programme, were sourced from Egale Canada and included slides presenting frameworks for categorising people by gender, sexual attraction, assigned sex, and gender expression as 'four components of identity,' as well as a slide exploring various labelling acronyms used to classify people based on their sexual orientation and gender. Jonathan Kay, editor of Quillette, posted images of these training materials on Twitter on August 5, 2022, identifying them as DEI training sessions at Infrastructure Canada. The sessions represent an institutional programme requiring federal public servants to engage with materials that sort and categorise individuals by demographic characteristics related to sex and sexual orientation.
incidents.expected_harm: Federal employees are required to engage with training that frames individuals primarily through demographic and identity categories rather than as whole persons with individual worth. The materials encourage staff to adopt classification systems that sort people by sexual orientation, gender, and related attributes, normalising the institutional practice of categorising individuals by immutable or personal characteristics. Requiring attendance means employees have no choice but to participate in a framework that reduces personhood to demographic labels, which cuts against the principle that each person deserves to be recognised for their unique qualities rather than their membership in demographic categories.

quotes.text: Sex, Sin & 69 Documentary Screening (INFC) (Bilingual)
quotes.rationale: Identifies the specific content of the required session — a documentary screening — held on August 3, 2022 via Microsoft Teams.

quotes.text:
Four Components of Identity
Gender
Attraction
Assigned Sex
Gender Expression
quotes.rationale: Egale Canada training slide classifying individuals into four identity components related to sex and sexual orientation, illustrating the demographic categorisation approach of the training.

quotes.text:
Which Acronym is Right?
LGBTTQQIA_ LGBTQIA
Many variations of the acronym exist.
Organizations must take time to consider which version of the acronym they wish to adopt as standard for all pieces and marketing.
quotes.rationale: Training slide focused on selecting and standardising acronyms used to label people by sexual orientation and gender, reinforcing institutional emphasis on demographic classification.

incidents.institution_name: Univerisity of Guelph > Department of Physics
incidents.short_description: The University of Guelph's Department of Physics restricted applications for a Canada Research Chair position to candidates belonging to specific demographic groups, excluding all others from consideration regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Guelph's Department of Physics posted a job advertisement on July 27, 2022 (reference AD #22-61) for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair in Experimental Physics. The position is associated with a $1.4 million funding package over seven years. The posting explicitly restricted who could apply: only candidates belonging to one or more of the following designated groups were permitted to submit applications — women, persons with disabilities, indigenous peoples, and racialized groups. Candidates outside these categories were categorically excluded from consideration regardless of their academic accomplishments, research record, or qualifications. The restriction was stated plainly in the posting: 'the University accepts applications from qualified applicants from these four groups only.' The posting framed this restriction as part of the university's Employment Equity Program and its obligations under the federal Canada Research Chairs Program, citing underrepresentation of designated groups within the university's CRC allocation. Applicants were also required to complete a self-identification form and submit an Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion statement describing their experience fostering a diverse research environment.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified physicists who do not belong to the designated demographic groups are entirely barred from applying for a prestigious, publicly funded research position, regardless of the quality of their research, their accomplishments, or their potential contribution to the field. This means that individual merit, character, and ability are set aside in favour of demographic categorization as the primary criterion for access to an opportunity. The practice reduces candidates to their membership in demographic groups rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. It sends a message that some people's qualifications are irrelevant to certain opportunities based solely on immutable characteristics. It also undermines public trust in the fairness of publicly funded research chair appointments and normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people into categories rather than recognizing their singular worth.

quotes.text: Candidates must be from one or more of the following equity-seeking groups to apply: women, persons with disabilities, Indigenous peoples, and racialized groups.
quotes.rationale: This is the core restriction in the job posting, explicitly stating that only candidates from designated demographic groups may apply, directly excluding all others from consideration.

quotes.text: For this position, the University accepts applications from qualified applicants from these four groups only.
quotes.rationale: Confirms unambiguously that the hiring restriction is absolute — no applicant outside the four designated groups may apply, regardless of qualifications.

quotes.text: The University of Guelph is committed to an Employment Equity Program that includes special measures to address the underrepresentation of individuals from four designated groups (women, persons with disabilities, Indigenous peoples, and members of visible minorities/racialized groups) within its allocation of Canada Research Chairs.
quotes.rationale: The university frames the restriction as part of a formal equity program, revealing the institutional rationale behind excluding candidates based on demographic category membership.

incidents.short_description: The University of Saskatchewan introduced a policy requiring applicants to provide documentation proving community membership in order to access jobs, scholarships, and placements reserved for people of a specific ethnic background. It is the first such policy at a Canadian university.
incidents.description_of_event:
On July 25, 2022, the University of Saskatchewan announced a new policy requiring students and faculty to provide external documentation proving membership in an indigenous community when applying for any role, scholarship, or placement reserved for people of that background. The policy was described as the first of its kind at a Canadian university.

The policy was developed by a 29-member task force struck in January 2022, which included academics, administrative staff, students, Elders and community leaders. The task force decided the university would not attempt to determine whether someone is indigenous, but would instead verify that an indigenous community claims the applicant as a member. Verification is to be conducted by external indigenous communities and governments, not by the university internally.

University president Peter Stoicheff said the previous approach of trusting self-identification was "no longer sufficient or respectful." The policy was said to take effect immediately, with a phase-in period of two to five years. Most existing staff and students already serving in such positions would not be required to provide proof, though the policy could be applied retroactively where there is reason to question an existing claim.

The policy was developed in response to a series of incidents at Canadian universities in which high-profile academics were found to have made claims of indigenous ancestry they could not substantiate. A prominent case at the U of S involved a health researcher whose claims of Anishinaabe, Métis and Tlingit ancestry were questioned following a CBC investigation published in October 2021.

The university acknowledged that the process would not be simple. Many first nations people do not hold Indian status under federal law, and others with genuine indigenous ancestry may lack documentation to prove community membership.
incidents.expected_harm:
The policy formally institutionalises the sorting of people into categories based on ethnicity as a condition of accessing academic roles, scholarships, and placements. By building an identity-verification infrastructure around ethnicity, the university entrenches the practice of evaluating individuals not on their character, abilities, or demonstrated needs, but on their membership in an ethnic category as certified by an external body. This undermines the principle that each person deserves recognition as an individual.

Persons with genuine ancestral connections who cannot produce formal documentation — including those whose families were disconnected from community records through historical displacement — are excluded from opportunities not because of any failing of their own, but because they cannot satisfy a bureaucratic ethnic membership test. The policy therefore causes direct, concrete harm to individuals who are denied access based on an inability to prove an identity that is genuinely theirs.

More broadly, the policy normalises the idea that institutions should assign opportunities based on ethnic group membership rather than individual merit or need, reinforcing a framework that divides people by ancestry and entrenches demographic categories as the basis for institutional decision-making.

quotes.text: The school is the first university in Canada to require students and faculty to provide documentation proving membership to an Indigenous community when applying for any job or opportunity that is reserved for Indigenous peoples.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core nature of the policy: access to opportunities is formally gated by documented ethnic community membership, making ethnicity the primary criterion for eligibility.

quotes.text: University president Peter Stoicheff said the school believes its previous policy of trusting people to be honest was "no longer sufficient or respectful."
quotes.rationale: Frames the policy as a deliberate institutional shift away from self-identification toward externally verified ethnic credentials as a condition of participation.

quotes.text: Verification will not be determined by us, by the university internally. Instead it's going to be determined with the guidance and support of external Indigenous communities and governments.
quotes.rationale: Describes how the ethnic verification mechanism works — outsourced to external bodies — illustrating the institutional infrastructure built around ethnic categorisation.

quotes.text: The policy says, though, that it may apply retroactively to faculty and students when "there is reason to question the authenticity of that claim" or when "the absence of verification could be otherwise contrary to the principles recognized in this policy."
quotes.rationale: Shows that the policy can be applied retroactively to individuals already in positions, extending the ethnic membership verification requirement backward and creating uncertainty for existing staff and students.

incidents.short_description: The University of Calgary ran a summer youth leadership program restricted to young people based on their skin colour, while also giving hiring preference for staff positions to applicants of the same background. The program organizes youth into a racially segregated setting and frames their identity in essentialist terms.
incidents.description_of_event:
The University of Calgary's Faculty of Social Work, through its Anti-Black Racism Task Force, launched a summer program called 'Transformative Racial and Spatial Justice: Celebrating Black People's Knowledges and Contributions' beginning July 4, 2022. The program ran out of a pop-up space at the Calgary Design Clinic on Stephen Avenue in downtown Calgary and was led by Faculty of Social Work professor Dr. Patrina Duhaney.

The youth leadership component accepted eight to twelve participants aged 15 to 18. All programming, workshops, and activities were designed exclusively for youth selected based on their skin colour. The program described its purpose as celebrating a specific culture and excellence tied to that demographic group, and framed participants' identities in terms of shared racial characteristics and collective histories. Youth were directed to develop awareness of issues framed as particular to their group and to design an action plan around those group-defined concerns.
