quotes.rationale: Explicit commitment to prioritise hiring based on skin colour, demonstrating that the institution evaluates job candidates using a demographic category rather than individual qualifications.

quotes.text: Support the creation of a Black Employee Network that provides Black teachers and staff with Black-only safe spaces to gather, share, and heal, and an opportunity to give feedback on the implementation of this strategy.
quotes.rationale: Institutionally supported racially segregated spaces for employees, restricted by skin colour. This is a direct example of the board creating separate, unequal spaces based on immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: Create programs that encourage and empower Black students to aim for high academic achievement, and that provide identity-affirming spaces that allow them to maintain a strong and positive sense of self rooted in their racial identity.
quotes.rationale: Directs the board to build student programming around racial identity as the foundation of selfhood — a race essentialist framing that reduces students to their skin colour rather than recognising them as individuals.

quotes.text: The measures of progress for Black students and staff will be outcomes, not activities. Success will be measured not by the completion of actions, but by the change made to the well-being and academic achievement of Black students and the representation and well-being of Black staff.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the board's entire accountability framework is organised around measuring outcomes by skin colour, institutionalising demographic categorisation as the primary lens for institutional self-evaluation.

quotes.text: Black children experience an educational system that limits their ability to learn. The gap in achievement for Black students does not reflect their innate ability, but rather the systemic anti-Black racism within the education system.
quotes.rationale: Encodes a race essentialist premise that all children sharing a skin colour have a shared, uniform experience of educational harm — denying the individual variation in student circumstances and treating children as defined by their skin colour group.

quotes.text: Partner and advocate with the University of Windsor for the admission of more Black students, especially to the Faculty of Education, to graduate more Black teachers.
quotes.rationale: The board commits to advocating for admission preferences at a post-secondary institution based on skin colour, extending demographic-based selection beyond its own hiring into university admissions.

quotes.text: Provide Black students with culturally appropriate supports, including for academic achievement and well-being, such as the Black Graduation Coach Program, and ongoing opportunities to gather and heal in a safe space.
quotes.rationale: Dedicated support programs and gathering spaces are organised by skin colour, meaning access to certain institutional resources is determined by a student's demographic category rather than their individual needs.

incidents.short_description: Birds Canada published a statement on racism, diversity, and inclusion that singles out people based on their skin colour and other demographic categories, treating these as the defining lens through which participation and belonging in birding should be understood.
incidents.description_of_event: Birds Canada, a national non-profit bird conservation organization, published a 'Statement on Racism, Diversity, and Inclusion in Birding' on its website (archived in a screenshot dated May 22, 2022). The statement frames participation in birding and the natural outdoors as a problem to be understood primarily through the lens of demographic categories — skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and gender identity. The statement identifies certain groups of people by their demographic characteristics as the subjects of organizational commitment, pledging to recruit specifically to improve demographic representation among volunteers, staff, committees, and board members, to prioritize listening to voices from "minorities," and to grow events led by members of "minority or marginalized groups." Rather than treating all individuals as equally deserving of welcome based on their interest in birds, the statement institutionalizes a hierarchy of belonging organized around demographic categories. It commits the organization to evaluating and advancing people based on which demographic group they belong to, rather than their individual character, abilities, or contributions to bird conservation. The current version of the webpage (retrieved April 2026) appears to have replaced this specific statement with updated language, though the organization's IDEA (Inclusivity, Diversity, Equity, and Accessibility) framework continues to be foregrounded as a core organizational value.
incidents.expected_harm: By organizing its commitments around demographic categories rather than individual worth, Birds Canada normalizes the practice of sorting people into groups based on immutable traits and then treating them differently based on group membership. Volunteers, staff, and members are evaluated and recruited not purely on their passion for birds or their abilities, but on which demographic category they belong to — meaning some individuals receive preference and others face disadvantage based solely on their skin colour, sexual orientation, or other identity characteristics. This entrenches division among people who share a common love of birds and nature, undermining the shared civic belonging the organization claims to promote. It signals to Canadians from all backgrounds that their welcome in the organization is conditional on demographic representation calculus rather than their individual merit and contribution.

quotes.text: Unacceptable prejudice, bigotry, and discrimination affect Black people and other racialized groups around the world and in Canada.
quotes.rationale: The statement singles out people by skin colour as the primary subjects of concern, rather than framing discrimination as something that can affect any individual. This reflects the race-essentialist approach the submitter flagged.

quotes.text: Include and listen carefully to voices from minorities to better address conservation and education objectives of greatest concern to them.
quotes.rationale: Framing participation and organizational decision-making around demographic minority status institutionalizes a system where individuals are heard and prioritized based on their group membership rather than the merit of their ideas or their individual circumstances.

quotes.text: Continue to grow the number and quality of public events we host that encourage participation from, and are led by, members of minority or marginalized groups.
quotes.rationale: Committing to events organized around demographic group leadership and participation reflects an institutional approach that prioritizes demographic categories over individual worth and shared participation.

incidents.short_description: The Simcoe County District School Board announced the formation of demographically segregated staff affinity groups, restricting participation in each group to staff who share a particular identity such as skin colour, sexual orientation, or religion.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 20, 2022, the Simcoe County District School Board's Equity, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) Department announced the formation of Staff Affinity Groups via its official social media account. The announcement invited all staff to register for groups described as designated safe spaces where everyone in the group shares a particular identity. The accompanying promotional material listed examples of the groups as being organized around skin colour, sexual orientation, and religion — for example groups for people identified as black, South Asian, Jewish, or LGBTQ. The stated rationale was that people of 'under-represented identities' need time and space away from what the materials called 'dominant systems in society.' Staff wishing to participate were directed to contact the Principal of EDI. The board framed the initiative as part of its commitment to equity and affirming the lived experiences of staff, particularly those it described as traditionally marginalized. In practice, the program organized staff into separate gatherings based on demographic categories including skin colour, sexual orientation, and religion, rather than on shared professional interests or individual needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Staff are sorted and separated from one another based on immutable characteristics such as skin colour, sexual orientation, and religion rather than being engaged as distinct individuals. This institutionalizes segregated spaces within a public school board, normalizing the idea that people's demographic categories — not their individual character, abilities, or circumstances — determine which groups they belong to and which spaces they are entitled to occupy. Staff who do not fit neatly into the designated identity categories are implicitly excluded from the support structures being built. The practice reinforces division among colleagues rather than fostering shared civic belonging and common understanding, and it sends a message to the broader school community, including students, that sorting people by immutable traits is appropriate and beneficial.

quotes.text: Our affinity groups are designated safe spaces, where everyone in that group shares a particular identity, for example, LGBTQ, Black, South Asian, Jewish or any others.
quotes.rationale: This passage directly establishes that the groups are restricted to people sharing a particular demographic identity, including skin colour, sexual orientation, and religion — the core concern of the incident.

quotes.text: The purpose of the space is to first honour and acknowledge that people of under-represented identities need time and space away from the dominant systems in society to be together, share and reflect on stories and build relationships.
quotes.rationale: This passage reveals the ideological rationale behind the segregated groups — that certain staff need separation from colleagues based on their identity — which reflects an institutional decision to evaluate and divide staff by demographic categories.

quotes.text: The SCDSB is committed to fostering structures that value and affirm the lived experiences of our staff, especially those that have been traditionally marginalized. As part of the commitment to ensuring equity at the SCDSB, the EDI Department is reaching out to all staff to determine their interest in participating in specific affinity groups.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that the initiative is formally institutional, driven by the EDI Department of the school board, and framed around demographic categories of staff.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Association of Social Workers offered a professional development webinar framing poverty and barriers to income support as experiences defined by a person's skin colour and ethnicity, and required registrants to disclose their skin colour and ethnic identity as part of the sign-up process.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Ontario Association of Social Workers (OASW) Learning Centre hosted a webinar titled 'The Racialization of Poverty: Understanding Opportunities and Barriers in Income Support for Racialized Clients' on May 19, 2022. The webinar was offered free of charge to Registered Social Workers, Registered Social Service Workers, and social work students in Ontario, and was funded in part by the Government of Canada and the Government of Ontario through an Employment Ontario project.

The webinar framed poverty and access to income supports as experiences that are fundamentally shaped by a person's skin colour and ethnic background. The event description states that mental and physical health 'can look quite different for racialized communities' and that there are 'many unique barriers' specific to these communities. The session promised to explore 'the history of South Asian communities in Canada and Ontario' and 'the realities of poverty in these and other racialized communities,' instructing social workers to understand clients primarily through the lens of demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique circumstances.

As part of the registration process, attendees were asked whether they self-identify as a person of colour, whether they identify as black and from which region of origin, and whether they identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuk/Inuit. These questions were described as optional but framed as necessary to help OASW ensure its work is 'reflective of the diverse membership.' The professional development content directed social workers to view client poverty and barriers to support through an essentialist lens organized around skin colour and ethnicity.
incidents.expected_harm: Social workers trained through this framework learn to assess clients primarily through the lens of skin colour and ethnicity rather than their individual circumstances, needs, abilities, and lived experiences. This approach embeds demographic categorization into professional practice, meaning clients are evaluated and understood based on group membership rather than who they are as individuals. The registration process itself normalises the routine collection of sensitive identity data about professionals seeking education, treating skin colour and ethnicity as professionally relevant information. The framing that poverty and health outcomes 'look different' based on skin colour, rather than on individual factors such as income, history, language, geography, or personal circumstance, forecloses the kind of individual assessment that respects each person's singular worth and reduces the social worker's ability to respond to a client's actual situation.

quotes.text: Across Ontario, racialized communities are facing poverty and income insecurity at disproportionately higher rates. While income supports are available through provincial and federal programs, we know that mental and physical health can look quite different for racialized communities, and that there are many unique barriers to accessing support.
quotes.rationale: This is the core framing of the webinar: it presents poverty, health outcomes, and barriers to support as experiences defined by skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual circumstances. Social workers are being trained to view clients through this demographic lens.

quotes.text: Join jasleen kaur johal of the South Asian Legal Clinic of Ontario to explore the history of South Asian communities in Canada and Ontario, and the realities on poverty in these and other racialized communities. Gain a deeper understanding of the distinct barriers these communities face, learn more about available income supports and how clients can access them, and understand how our legal system can be leveraged to secure needed income support for racialized populations facing mental and physical health challenges.
quotes.rationale: The learning objectives explicitly direct social workers to understand clients as members of demographic groups defined by skin colour and ethnicity, rather than as individuals with unique needs and circumstances.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board offered preferential registration for its summer Coding Camp to students based on their skin colour and ethnicity. Students identifying as a specified demographic group were granted priority access over all other students.
incidents.description_of_event: The Peel District School Board promoted a two-week summer Coding Camp for students in grades 6 to 10, running in July 2022. The camp was open to all Peel District School Board students enrolled in grades 6 to 10 and included both in-person and online options. However, the board announced that preferential registration would be given to students who self-identified based on their skin colour and ethnic background. Students who did not fall into the preferred categories were only eligible to fill remaining spaces on a first-come, first-served basis. The same preferential registration policy applied to at least two other summer programs offered by the board: the Agents 4 Social Change Africentric Summer Program and the Leading 4 Me Camp. Students were asked to self-identify as part of the registration process in order to qualify for priority access. The board announced the program and its preferential registration policy via its official Twitter account on 17 May 2022 and on its website.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are sorted and given unequal access to a publicly funded educational program based on their skin colour and ethnic background rather than their interest, ability, or individual need. Students who do not belong to the preferred demographic categories are placed at a systematic disadvantage when registering for a limited-space program, meaning some are turned away not due to anything about them as individuals but purely because of their skin colour. This practice normalizes the idea that a public school board evaluates and ranks students based on immutable demographic characteristics. It sends students the message that their skin colour and ethnicity determine the opportunities available to them, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be recognized as a distinct individual. It also entrenches institutional habits of sorting children into demographic groups rather than treating every student's interest in coding as equally worthy of support.

quotes.text: Preferential registration for students who identify as Black, African, Afro-Caribbean and/or Indigenous
quotes.rationale: This quote, taken directly from the promotional image posted by the school board, states explicitly that registration priority is granted based on students' self-identified skin colour and ethnic background, establishing the core of the incident.

quotes.text: Please note: Preferential registration will be given to students who identify as Black, African or Afro-Caribbean and/or Indigenous. Students will be asked to self-identify as part of the registration process.
quotes.rationale: This is the official website language confirming the preferential registration policy for the Coding Camp specifically, requiring students to self-identify by demographic category to receive priority access.

incidents.institution_name: Toronto District School Board > Harbord Collegiate Institute
incidents.short_description: Harbord Collegiate Institute held a student conference centred on the concept of 'Black Excellence,' framing student achievement and identity through the lens of skin colour. The TDSB Director of Education publicly praised the event.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 17, 2022, Harbord Collegiate Institute, a school within the Toronto District School Board, held its second annual student conference organized by the school's Whole of Africa and Caribbean Club (WACC). The event featured workshops, dancing, painting, music production, and a Q&A session with a guest speaker. The conference was themed around 'Black Excellence,' explicitly framing student achievement, creativity, and identity through the category of skin colour. Students produced artwork titled 'Melanin,' further centring racial identity as the organising principle of the event. TDSB Director of Education Colleen Russell-Rawlins publicly praised the event on social media, commending the students and staff who organized it. The school library also promoted the conference on social media, describing it as a celebration of student excellence defined by skin colour. The institutional promotion of the event — from both the school and the board's director — signals endorsement of an approach to student achievement that groups and celebrates students based on their immutable characteristics rather than their individual merits and contributions.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school organizes and an institution's most senior education official publicly endorses a student achievement conference built around a racial category, it sends students a clear message that their accomplishments are understood primarily through the lens of their skin colour rather than their individual character, effort, and talent. This approach reduces students to demographic categories and reinforces the habit of sorting people by immutable traits. Students outside the targeted group receive the implicit message that this celebration of excellence is not for them, fragmenting the school community along racial lines rather than uniting it around shared aspirations. The institutional normalization of race-essentialist framing in educational programming teaches students that skin colour is a meaningful and appropriate basis on which schools divide, celebrate, and organize people — a lesson directly at odds with recognizing every student's singular worth as an individual.

quotes.text: Thx to all of our Harbord students for getting involved, and getting to know each other — like family. It was a FUNOMENAL day, filled with joy, laughter, dancing, painting, beats creating, & learning about Black Excellence!
quotes.rationale: The school's own social media describes the conference as being about 'Black Excellence,' confirming that student achievement and the event's purpose were framed around skin colour rather than individual merit.

quotes.text: 2/2 Black Excellence @HarbordCI delegates were thrilled to screen @revengebbf and participate in an amazing Q&A with the incomparable @amanda_parris in our library! we're beyond proud to bring home their artwork entitled 'Melanin'
quotes.rationale: The school library's promotion explicitly links student artwork and intellectual engagement to racial identity ('Melanin'), reinforcing the race-essentialist framing of the event's programming.

incidents.short_description: The Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board distributed a student survey that asked students to sort themselves into racial categories as part of data collection for its Mental Health Strategy. The survey treated students' racial group membership as a meaningful lens for analysing their school experiences and mental health needs.
incidents.description_of_event:
In May 2022, the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board (HWDSB) launched a Student Voice Survey for all grade 7 to 12 students. The survey was developed under the leadership of Student Trustees and the Student Senate, with support from HWDSB's Research and Analytics Department. Responses were to inform HWDSB's Mental Health Strategy for the following three years and shape Student Senate priorities.

Among the survey's final section — framed as questions about student identity — students were asked to select which racial group best described them from a detailed list of categories. The stated rationale was that it is "important that we recognize patterns based on a student's identity to address inequity when working to improve mental health support." Students were also asked about their gender identity, whether they identified as part of the LGBTQIA+ community, and whether they lived with various health or disability conditions.

By embedding racial group classification as a data-collection instrument within a student mental health survey, the school board institutionalised the practice of sorting students by skin colour and ethnicity, framing these demographic categories as necessary variables for understanding and responding to student wellbeing.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are asked by their school board to identify themselves according to racial group categories, normalising the institutional practice of sorting individuals by skin colour and ethnicity from a young age. This conditions students to understand themselves and their peers primarily through demographic labels rather than as distinct individuals whose needs and experiences arise from their personal circumstances. When a school board ties mental health policy to racial group data, it embeds the assumption that a student's skin colour or ethnic background is a meaningful and necessary variable in assessing their wellbeing — an assumption that reduces individuals to demographic proxies. The practice entrenches division among students along racial lines and undermines the development of a shared civic identity within the school community.

quotes.text:
What racial group(s) best describe you?
Black (e.g., African descent, Afro-Caribbean, African-Canadian descent)
East Asian (e.g., Chinese, Korean, Japanese, Taiwanese descent)
Indigenous (e.g., First Nations, Metis, Inuit descent)
Latino / Latina/ Latinx (e.g., Latin American, Hispanic descent)
Middle Eastern (e.g., Arab, Persian, West Asian descent)
South Asian (e.g., East Indian, Pakistani, Bangladeshi, Sri Lankan, Inda-Caribbean, etc.)
Southeast Asian (e.g., Filipino, Vietnamese, Cambodian, Thai, Indonesian, Pacific-Islander, etc.)
White (e.g., European, Slavic descent)
Not sure
Prefer not to answer
Another race category not listed here
quotes.rationale: This is the core question that asks students to classify themselves by racial group. It demonstrates that the survey institutionalises racial categorisation of children as part of a school board data-collection exercise.

quotes.text: It's important that we recognize patterns based on a student's identity to address inequity when working to improve mental health support.
quotes.rationale: This is the stated justification for collecting demographic identity data, including racial group classification. It reveals the institutional logic: that a student's identity category — including their racial group — is treated as a necessary lens for mental health policy.

quotes.text: This survey will serve as the foundation for HWDSB's Mental Health Strategy for the next three years and determine the priorities of the Student Senate.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that the data collected — including racial group classifications — will directly shape the school board's mental health policy and institutional priorities for three years, amplifying the significance of the categorisation practice.

incidents.short_description: The Halton District School Board delivered 'Critical Consciousness' teacher training that reportedly sorted teachers and students by skin colour and espoused race-essentialist ideas. Screenshots of the training materials were shared publicly in May 2022, prompting a response from the local teachers' union.
incidents.description_of_event: In May 2022, screenshots from a 'Critical Consciousness' training programme delivered to staff at the Halton District School Board were shared publicly on social media. The training was described by the board and its teachers' union (ETFO Halton) as part of 'Culturally Relevant and Responsive Pedagogy' (CRRP) work. Based on commentary accompanying the screenshots, the materials appeared to frame teachers and students primarily through the lens of their skin colour and to incorporate race-essentialist concepts — treating immutable demographic characteristics as defining features of individuals and their experiences. The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario's Halton local issued a public statement on May 18, 2022, defending the training and characterizing criticism of it as racism. The actual slide content is not fully available in the captured evidence, but the training was specifically oriented around categorizing people by demographic identity rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.expected_harm: When teacher training instructs educators to view students and colleagues primarily through the lens of skin colour and ethnicity, it normalizes the institutional habit of reducing individuals to demographic categories. Teachers trained this way are less likely to evaluate students on their individual character, abilities, and circumstances. Students are consequently seen and treated as representatives of a group rather than as unique individuals. This undermines the development of a shared school community and reinforces the idea that immutable traits are the most important thing to know about a person. The framing also suppresses open inquiry and honest dialogue among staff, as the union's response demonstrated: criticism of the training's content was publicly labelled as racism, discouraging teachers from raising legitimate questions about whether sorting people by skin colour is educationally sound or consistent with treating every person as an individual of singular worth.

incidents.short_description: The Halton District School Board promoted a racially and sex-segregated summer program open only to self-identified black male youth in grades 6–8, excluding all other students on the basis of skin colour and sex.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 11, 2022, the Halton District School Board sent a message to families promoting the Strong Academy Summer Program, offered in partnership with the 100 Strong Foundation. The four-week program, running July 4–29, 2022, was described as designed exclusively for self-identified black male youth between the ages of 11 and 14, currently enrolled in grades 6, 7, and 8. The program offered full-day academic instruction delivered by a registered Ontario teacher, physical and recreational activities, and practical life skills mentorship. Registration was capped at 25 students on a first-come, first-served basis. The board actively promoted this opportunity through its official school communication system, SchoolMessenger, to all affiliated families — while the program itself was explicitly restricted by skin colour and sex. An in-person information session was held on May 19 at the University of Toronto Mississauga Campus, with a virtual session on May 26.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are excluded from a publicly promoted educational enrichment program on the basis of their skin colour and sex. The school board, by endorsing and distributing this opportunity through official channels, signals to its entire student population that access to certain programmes is determined by immutable characteristics rather than individual interest, ability, or need. Students who do not meet the demographic criteria receive the institutional message that they are less deserving of targeted support and opportunity. This normalises the sorting of children into separate groups based on skin colour and sex, undermining the principle that each student possesses equal worth as an individual. The segregation of a summer learning programme along these lines reinforces division within the school community rather than fostering a sense of shared belonging and common purpose among students of all backgrounds.

quotes.text: Strong Academy is a four-week summer program designed to inspire self-identified Black male youth ages 11-14 years old (currently in Grade 6, 7 and 8).
quotes.rationale: Directly establishes that the programme is restricted by skin colour and sex, admitting only self-identified black male youth.

quotes.text: Please note, registration is capped at 25 students. Applications should be submitted to [...] Participants will be admitted to the program based on the submission of a completed application on a first-come first-served basis.
quotes.rationale: Shows the programme had limited capacity and that the board formally administered enrolment, reinforcing its institutional involvement in a programme restricted by demographics.

incidents.short_description: Canadian Stage, in collaboration with Obsidian Theatre and Necessary Angel, hosted a racially segregated theatre night on May 10, 2022, restricting attendance to people of a particular skin colour. Members of the public who did not identify with that group were excluded from the performance.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 10, 2022, Canadian Stage hosted a performance of 'Is God Is' by Aleshea Harris — a co-production with Obsidian Theatre Company and Necessary Angel Theatre Company — designated as a 'Black Out Night.' The event was explicitly restricted to people based on their skin colour, described in promotional materials as 'exclusive to the Afro/Black community.' Discounted tickets at $15 were offered using a promotional code, and the event included a post-show panel discussion. The performance was held at Canadian Stage's venue at 26 Berkeley Street in Toronto, Ontario. Promotional materials openly stated the intent to exclude the general public based on skin colour, with a note that tickets would only become available to the general public after this designated night. The institutions involved framed the segregated gathering as providing a 'safer space' for people of a particular skin colour to experience theatre, explicitly organising access to a public cultural event around demographic categories rather than individual interest or need.
incidents.expected_harm: Theatre-goers are denied access to a publicly presented cultural event based solely on their skin colour. This practice normalises the sorting of people into racial categories to determine who may attend shared civic and cultural spaces. It sends the message that institutions consider skin colour a legitimate basis for granting or restricting access to cultural life, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. By designating an entire performance night as accessible only to people of a specific skin colour, the institution reinforces the idea that people's immutable characteristics, rather than their individual character or interests, define their place in public life. This forecloses opportunities for shared cultural experience and understanding across perceived differences, and entrenches the habit of dividing Canadians by demographic category rather than uniting them through common human experience.

incidents.institution_name: Garth Webb Secondary School / Halton District School Board
incidents.short_description: Garth Webb Secondary School in Oakville organized a celebration event explicitly targeting students based on their skin colour, excluding other students and families from the focus of the event. The school framed the gathering around demographic identity rather than individual achievement or shared community.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 10, 2022, Garth Webb Secondary School, part of the Halton District School Board in Burlington, Ontario, sent a school-wide communication announcing a 'Celebration of Garth Webb's Black Students: Current, Graduating, and Alumnus,' scheduled for May 19, 2022 at 7:00 pm in the school library. The event was organized to celebrate the achievements of students identified by their skin colour — current students, graduating students, and alumni — along with their parents and guardians. Attendees were also to hear from former alumni about post-secondary experiences. The event invitation referenced a June 2020 meeting held in the aftermath of George Floyd's death and framed the event as a continuation of efforts to support students based on their demographic identity. The school described students of a particular skin colour as being 'in dire need of support' and committed to creating 'a safe and brave place' for them specifically. The event communication was sent through the Halton District School Board's official notification system to the broader school community. Although the invitation included a line stating the event was open to the 'whole school community,' the event's stated purpose, framing, and celebratory focus were organized exclusively around students of a particular skin colour.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school organizes events and communications that single out and celebrate students based on their skin colour, it reduces those students to a demographic category rather than recognizing them as individuals with unique achievements, characters, and abilities. Other students and families receive the message that the school evaluates and groups people by immutable traits rather than individual merit. This institutional practice of organizing school life around skin colour normalizes the sorting of people into demographic categories and undermines shared civic belonging within the school community. Students learn that their identity, as defined by a demographic label, is what institutions see first — not their individual qualities. The event's framing, describing students of a particular skin colour as uniquely vulnerable and in need of distinct institutional support, reinforces stereotyping of individuals based on their skin colour rather than their personal circumstances or demonstrated needs.

quotes.text: Celebration of Garth Webb's Black Students: Current, Graduating, and Alumnus
quotes.rationale: The event title explicitly organizes a school celebration around students identified by skin colour, framing recognition and celebration through a demographic category rather than individual achievement.

quotes.text: Please join us for an engaging evening on Thursday, May 19, 2022 at 7:00 pm in the Garth Webb Library to celebrate the achievements of Garth Webb's students who identify as Black: Current, Graduating, and Alumnus and their parents/guardians.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the event was organized to celebrate students based on their skin colour identity, formally sorting students and families into demographic groups for institutional recognition.

quotes.text: Garth Webb parents continue to be committed to working with the school to create a safe and brave place for our Black and Biracial students because as we know the impact racism has and continues to have on our student's personal identity and their need to feel a sense of belonging.
quotes.rationale: The school explicitly targets institutional support and programming at students based on skin colour, framing the school community through demographic divisions rather than shared student needs or common humanity.

incidents.short_description: The Ottawa Police Service publicly advertised that it was specifically seeking candidates based on sex and skin colour, making demographic characteristics a stated factor in its recruitment effort.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 10, 2022, the Ottawa Police Service posted a message on its official X (Twitter) account announcing a recruitment drive for sworn new recruits. Rather than inviting all qualified candidates to apply, the post explicitly stated the service was seeking candidates based on their sex and skin colour. The tweet read: "There has never been a better time to apply to the Ottawa Police Service. We're looking for female or racialized candidates to help us better reflect the communities we serve." A French-language reply to the same thread repeated the message, confirming this was an intentional and bilingual institutional communication. The recruitment post directed candidates to a job application page on the City of Ottawa's employment portal. By framing the hiring drive around demographic categories, the Ottawa Police Service signalled that candidates' sex and skin colour were relevant factors in their consideration for employment.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates who do not belong to the preferred demographic categories are discouraged from applying and treated as less desirable regardless of their individual qualifications, character, or abilities. This reduces policing to a demographic exercise rather than a search for the best individuals for public safety roles. It sends a message that a person's worth as a candidate is tied to their sex and skin colour rather than their merit, undermining the principle that individuals deserve to be evaluated based on their unique qualities. It also normalizes the practice of institutions sorting people into demographic groups when making decisions that affect individual livelihoods and opportunities.

quotes.text: There has never been a better time to apply to the Ottawa Police Service. We're looking for female or racialized candidates to help us better reflect the communities we serve.
quotes.rationale: This is the primary statement from the Ottawa Police Service's official account explicitly naming demographic categories as the basis for their recruitment preference, directly evidencing a hiring preference based on sex and skin colour.

quotes.text: Jamais il ne fut de moment plus opportun pour postuler auprès du Service de police d'Ottawa. Nous recherchons des candidates ou candidats racisés afin de nous aider a mieux refléter les collectivités que nous servons.
quotes.rationale: The French-language follow-up tweet from the Ottawa Police Service repeats the same demographic preference in French, confirming this was a deliberate bilingual institutional communication rather than an isolated post.

incidents.institution_name: Toronto District School Board > Swansea Junior and Senior Public School
incidents.short_description: Swansea Junior and Senior Public School, a Toronto District School Board school, circulated a survey that frames classroom quality through a group-identity lens, treating students primarily as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Swansea Junior and Senior Public School, operating within the Toronto District School Board, distributed an 'Equity Reflections' survey created through TDSB's Google Forms infrastructure. The survey asks respondents to rate the school and classroom environment across a range of criteria. Several questions in the classroom climate section frame educational quality through a group-identity lens. One criterion asks whether students see their lives and others' represented in classroom materials by explicitly referencing specific ethnic and demographic categories — first nations, métis and inuit, and African-Canadian — and contrasts this favourably against what it describes as a 'white middle class' curriculum. Another criterion asks whether social justice concepts — named as anti-racism, anti-classism, anti-sexism, anti-ableism, and anti-homophobia — are 'central to the classroom curriculum.' A further criterion links classroom curriculum directly to 'local issues of equity and social justice.' The survey also asks whether the school incorporates leaders who are described as 'racialized and/or historically marginalized' into curriculum lessons and units of study. Across multiple pages, the survey treats demographic group membership — by ethnicity, gender, and other identity categories — as the primary lens through which classroom quality and student experience should be evaluated, embedding a group-identity framework into the standard by which teachers and schools are assessed.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school survey frames classroom quality primarily through demographic group membership, it trains teachers, parents, and students to see individuals first as representatives of identity categories rather than as distinct people with unique characters, abilities, and needs. This normalises the institutional habit of sorting students into groups based on immutable traits and evaluating their education through those groupings. Students learn that institutions view them through demographic labels, which undermines their recognition as individuals deserving assessment based on their own merits and circumstances. The framing discourages the kind of open, evidence-based discourse that allows all students to engage with ideas on their own terms, replacing it with a curriculum that treats certain viewpoints — framed as social justice — as the established standard against which schools are measured. This forecloses genuine intellectual inquiry and pressures teachers to orient their classrooms around a particular ideological framework rather than the individual needs and capacities of their students.

quotes.text: Students see their lives and others' represented in the materials, books, pictures, teachers, administrators, etc., within the classroom and school (e.g. First Nations, Metis & Inuit, African-Canadian, etc.). The curriculum speaks to the lives of the students in the classroom and does not mandate a "one size fits all curriculum" based on a white middle class view.
quotes.rationale: This criterion explicitly frames curriculum quality by contrasting against a 'white middle class view,' reducing students and curriculum to demographic categories defined by skin colour, ethnicity, and class rather than individual needs or interests.

incidents.short_description: The Deeply Rooted Farmers Market in Toronto explicitly restricted vendor participation to people based on their skin colour and ethnicity, barring all others from selling at the market. Vendor applications required applicants to confirm their demographic identity as a condition of participation.
incidents.description_of_event: Deeply Rooted Farmers Market, located at 455 Cosburn Avenue in East York, Toronto, operated a weekly Sunday market from May 8 to September 25, 2022. The market explicitly restricted vendor participation based on skin colour and ethnicity. The vendor application form — for both the Farmer/Produce Form and the Non-Farmer Form — included a mandatory question asking whether the applicant identified as belonging to particular demographic categories. The form stated in plain terms: "You MUST be either Black or Indigenous to participate in this market." Applicants who selected "Neither" were ineligible to participate as vendors. The market's founder described the restriction as intentional, framing it as a response to the perceived demographic makeup of existing farmers markets and a desire to circulate money within particular communities. The market received coverage in multiple outlets as Toronto's first market of this kind, and the segregated vendor policy was presented positively as a form of community-building. The restriction applied regardless of the quality of goods, business experience, or individual circumstances of any prospective vendor.
incidents.expected_harm: Vendors are excluded from a public-facing commercial market based solely on their skin colour and ethnic identity, rather than on their abilities, the quality of their products, or any individual characteristic. This institutional policy reduces people to demographic categories and denies them economic opportunity on that basis. People who do not identify as belonging to the designated groups are turned away regardless of merit, perpetuating the practice of sorting individuals by immutable traits. The policy normalizes the idea that economic spaces should be organized along demographic lines, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It also sends a broader civic message that segregated commercial spaces — where access is granted or denied based on skin colour — are acceptable or even praiseworthy, setting a precedent that cuts against shared civic belonging.

quotes.text: You MUST be either Black or Indigenous to participate in this market.
quotes.rationale: This is the explicit eligibility restriction stated on the vendor application form, directly establishing that participation is conditioned on skin colour and ethnic identity.

quotes.text: You MUST be either black or Indigenous to participate in this market.
quotes.rationale: The same restriction appears in the Non-Farmer Form section of the vendor application, confirming the policy applied to all vendor categories.

quotes.text: The market officially launched on May 8.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the launch date of the market and confirms the policy was in effect from the outset.

quotes.text: It was just always a very, very, very white space ... If you're not white, it's not the most comfortable setting. I want to be able to go to a farmers' market and get culturally relevant food ... I wanted a space that was representative of Black people as well.
quotes.rationale: The founder's stated rationale for the exclusionary policy — discomfort in existing spaces — illustrates how a subjective experience is used to justify restricting participation based on demographic categories.

quotes.text: Our market will also generate and circulate money within Black and Indigenous communities to combat the injustices within the food
quotes.rationale: Reveals the explicit intent to restrict economic participation to particular demographic groups, framing the commercial exclusion as a community benefit.

quotes.text: The Black & Indigenous Farmers Market — Now Accepting Vendors for the 2022 Market Season
quotes.rationale: The market's own website header confirms the demographic restriction is central to its public identity and vendor recruitment.

incidents.short_description: Rowing Canada Aviron's member registration form asks all applicants to identify their race and sexual orientation. The form categorises people by skin colour and ethnicity as a standard step in joining the national sport organisation.
incidents.description_of_event: Rowing Canada Aviron's online member registration form requires all new members to answer questions about their racial category, sexual orientation, and indigenous identity as part of the standard account creation process. The form asks members to select their 'population group' from a list that includes categories defined by skin colour and ethnic background. A separate question asks whether the member identifies as 2SLGBTQI+. A further question asks whether the member identifies as first nations, Métis, and/or Inuk/Inuit. These questions are presented alongside practical fields such as name, date of birth, and language preference, framing the collection of demographic identity data as a routine part of membership registration. An archived screenshot from May 2022 shows the same form labelled the field 'What is your race,' confirming this data collection practice has been in place for several years. The current version of the form retains the same structure under the label 'population group.' Members are given a 'prefer not to disclose' option, but the questions are embedded directly in the registration flow.
incidents.expected_harm: Members are asked to classify themselves by skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation before they can join a national sport organisation. This normalises the institutional practice of categorising individuals by demographic labels rather than treating them as distinct persons defined by their abilities, character, and individual circumstances. Athletes and coaches seeking to participate in the sport are immediately sorted into demographic buckets, signalling that the organisation views them through the lens of group identity. Over time this entrenches a culture in which membership, programming, or advancement decisions risk being tied to these categories. It also exposes members' sensitive personal data — race, sexual orientation, indigenous identity — as a condition of participation, raising concerns about privacy and the appropriate use of that information.

quotes.text:
What is your population group (Select what is applicable)
White
South Asian (e.g., East Indian, Pakistani, Sri Lankan)
Chinese
Black
Filipino
Arab
Latin American
Southeast Asian (e.g., Vietnamese, Cambodian, Laotian, Thai)
West Asian (e.g., Iranian, Afghan)
Korean
Japanese
Other
Prefer not to disclose
quotes.rationale: The current registration form asks all members to select a racial or ethnic population group from a detailed list, categorising individuals by skin colour and ethnic background as a standard step in joining the organisation.

quotes.text: Do you identify as 2SLGBTQI+? - Select One - Yes No Prefer not to disclose
quotes.rationale: The registration form also collects sexual orientation data from all members, embedding demographic categorisation by sexual orientation into the standard sign-up process.

quotes.text:
Do you identify as First Nations, Métis and/or Inuk/Inuit? (Select what is applicable)
No - Does not Identify
Yes - First Nations
Yes - Inuk/Inuit
Yes - Métis
Prefer not to disclose
quotes.rationale: A further question requires members to identify whether they belong to specific indigenous peoples, again categorising individuals by ethnicity and ancestry as part of routine registration.

quotes.text: What is your race: (select what is applicable)
quotes.rationale: The 2022 screenshot shows the field was previously labelled explicitly as 'What is your race,' confirming the practice of collecting racial data from members has been ongoing for several years and was not framed subtly.

incidents.short_description: Loblaw Companies Limited's 2021 ESG Report commits to numerical representation targets for management, executive, and board positions based on sex and skin colour, and mandates an inclusion training program for its approximately 200,000 employees by 2024. These policies institutionalise demographic categorisation as a basis for personnel decisions at one of Canada's largest private sector employers.
incidents.description_of_event: In May 2022, Loblaw Companies Limited published its 2021 Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Report, which sets out binding workforce targets based on demographic characteristics. The report commits to achieving 40% representation of women on the board of directors, 40% in executive positions, and 43% in management positions by 2024. It separately commits to achieving 25% representation of people categorised as visible minorities on the board, 25% in executive positions, and 30% in management positions by 2024. The report also commits to deploying a mandatory inclusion training program to the company's entire workforce of approximately 200,000 employees by the end of 2024. The report states that in 2021, the company already exceeded its women-in-management representation goal ahead of schedule, and that inclusion training was initiated across the full workforce. These commitments are presented as central to what the company calls 'advancing social equity' and improving 'leadership representation.'
incidents.expected_harm: By setting numerical targets for management and executive positions based on sex and skin colour, Loblaw institutionalises the practice of evaluating and advancing employees based on demographic categories rather than individual character, ability, or merit. Employees who do not belong to the designated groups face a workplace where their advancement is structurally deprioritised because of immutable traits they did not choose. Employees in targeted groups are placed in a position where their achievements are shadowed by the question of whether they advanced on the merits or to fulfil a quota. The mandatory inclusion training program, rolled out to all 200,000 employees, imposes ideological content on staff regardless of individual belief or perspective, treating employees as members of demographic groups who require attitudinal correction rather than as individuals. Taken together, these policies normalise the institutional sorting of people by skin colour and sex, undermine individual dignity and privacy, and entrench demographic categorisation as a standard business practice across one of Canada's largest workforces.

quotes.text: Achieve 25% representation of members of visible minorities on our board of directors, 25% representation of members of visible minorities in executive positions, and 30% representation of members of visible minorities in management positions, in 2024.
quotes.rationale: Directly states numerical representation targets for management and leadership positions based on skin colour categorisation, the core concern of this incident.

quotes.text: Maintain 40% representation of women on our board of directors, and achieve 40% representation of women in executive positions and 43% representation of women in management positions, in 2024.
quotes.rationale: States numerical representation targets for leadership positions based on sex, the second targeting basis of this incident.

quotes.text: Achieve representation goals for management, executives and our Board of Directors by the end of 2024.
quotes.rationale: Summary-level commitment to demographic representation targets across all leadership tiers, reinforcing the policy statement nature of this incident.

quotes.text: In 2021, we exceeded representation goals for women in management roles years early, and initiated training across our entire workforce to embed the importance of inclusion and being an ally.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that demographic-based management targets and workforce-wide inclusion training were already being implemented in 2021, not merely planned for the future.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Association for Mathematics Education featured a conference session titled "Teaching Math While White: An Abolitionist Approach to Dismantling Racism in the Math Classroom," which categorized educators based on their skin colour and promoted the notion that mathematics education requires race-based approaches to teaching.
incidents.description_of_event: In March 2022, the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board circulated a memorandum to all elementary and secondary principals and vice-principals promoting the upcoming Ontario Association for Mathematics Education (OAME) 2022 virtual conference, scheduled for May 4, 5, 9, and 10, 2022. The conference program included a session titled "Teaching Math While White: An Abolitionist Approach to Dismantling Racism in the Math Classroom" presented by Michelle Munk. This session was among multiple presentations focused on what organizers described as "destreaming" mathematics education and addressing systemic barriers. The memorandum specifically highlighted this session alongside others addressing topics such as "Dismantling Oppression in Mathematics Education" and sessions focused on serving students categorized by immutable characteristics. The conference documentation and promotional materials presented mathematics education through a framework that emphasized the skin colour of educators and students, suggesting that teaching approaches should differ based on these characteristics. The school board actively encouraged participation by offering fully subsidized registration through their Program and Learning department, with priority given to educators in designated schools and specific program streams.
incidents.expected_harm: When educational institutions promote frameworks that categorize educators and students by skin colour, they undermine the principle that individuals should be evaluated based on their capabilities and character. This approach will lead students to view themselves and their teachers primarily through the lens of immutable traits rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualities and perspectives. Educators who are sorted into categories based on their skin colour may face pressure to adopt teaching methods aligned with these demographic labels rather than approaches grounded in their professional judgment and students' individual needs. Students will learn that their mathematical abilities and learning needs are connected to their demographic categories, potentially diminishing their confidence in their own capacity to master mathematical concepts through reason and effort. The framing of mathematics education as requiring race-based interventions obscures the universal nature of mathematical reasoning and risks creating artificial barriers between students and this fundamental domain of human knowledge. By emphasizing demographic divisions rather than shared human capacity for logical thinking, such programming will foster separation rather than the recognition of our common intellectual inheritance and potential.

quotes.text: Teaching Math While White: An Abolitionist Approach to Dismantling Racism in the Math Classroom - Michelle Munk
quotes.rationale: This session title explicitly categorizes educators by skin colour and suggests that teaching approaches must be differentiated based on this immutable characteristic. The framing reduces educators to demographic labels rather than recognizing them as individuals with distinct professional capabilities.

incidents.short_description: Hockey Canada's minor hockey registration portal requires families to declare their child's indigeneity, ethnicity, and gender identity as mandatory fields. The collection of this demographic information from children enrolling in a sport normalizes institutional categorization of individuals by race and identity.
incidents.description_of_event: Hockey Canada introduced a new registration portal for minor hockey that requires parents and guardians to complete mandatory fields disclosing whether their child identifies as indigenous, their child's ethnicity, and their child's gender identity. A screenshot of the registration form shows these as required fields marked with an asterisk, meaning families cannot complete registration without providing this information. The portal was flagged publicly in May 2022. The form treats demographic categories — including race and indigeneity — as necessary data points for enrolling a child in sport, requiring families to categorize their children in these terms before they can participate.
incidents.expected_harm: Children are categorized and labelled by their race, indigeneity, and gender identity before they can even participate in sport. Families are compelled to disclose and assign demographic identities to their children as a condition of registration, normalizing the institutional practice of sorting individuals by immutable characteristics rather than registering them simply as participants. This conditions children and families to think of themselves primarily through demographic categories. It also raises privacy concerns, as sensitive identity data about minors is collected by a national sports organization without a clear and publicly stated purpose for that data. Families who are uncomfortable assigning racial or identity labels to their children face a barrier to participation.

quotes.text: when you register for minor hockey under Hockey Canada's new portal, it now asks if your child identifies as Indigenous, their "gender Identity," and their racial identity
quotes.rationale: Describes the core incident: mandatory collection of indigeneity, gender identity, and racial identity during minor hockey registration.

quotes.text:
Identify as Indigenous *
Select
quotes.rationale: Screenshot of the registration form confirms that identifying as indigenous is a required field (asterisk denotes mandatory).

quotes.text:
Ethnicity *
earch...
quotes.rationale: Confirms ethnicity is a mandatory required field in the registration portal.

quotes.text:
Gender Identity *
Select
quotes.rationale: Confirms gender identity is a mandatory required field in the registration portal.

incidents.short_description: The British Columbia government introduced the Anti-Racism Data Act, which requires the collection of demographic data — including ethnic origin, ancestry, faith, and gender identity — categorizing residents by these characteristics to inform government programs and services.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 2, 2022, the British Columbia government, through the Office of the Premier, introduced the Anti-Racism Data Act. The legislation mandates the systematic collection of disaggregated demographic data from British Columbians, including information on ethnic origin, ancestry, faith, ability, and gender identity. The stated purpose is to identify gaps in government programs and services and to direct resources based on these demographic categories. Government ministries are required to follow guidelines for sharing statistical data publicly and to release statistics annually. A new data advisory committee will be established to oversee implementation. The act was framed as addressing systemic barriers but institutionalizes the practice of categorizing residents according to their immutable characteristics and other personal attributes as a standard function of government. The legislation was co-developed with indigenous leadership and builds on prior reports recommending disaggregated data collection as a tool for policy-making.
incidents.expected_harm: By enshrining in law the routine collection and reporting of demographic data organized around characteristics such as ethnic origin, ancestry, and faith, the government embeds the practice of categorizing individual British Columbians according to their immutable traits into the core operations of government. This approach treats people primarily as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals deserving evaluation on their own merits and circumstances. Government programs and services will be designed and assessed through the lens of group identity, normalizing the idea that a person's background and heritage are the relevant lens through which the state should understand them. The institutionalization of this data collection shapes policy incentives so that future decisions are driven by demographic tallies rather than individual needs, entrenching division rather than fostering a shared civic identity where all residents are evaluated equally as individuals.

quotes.text: As a step to dismantle systemic racism and discrimination faced by Indigenous, Black and people of colour, the Province is introducing the anti-racism data act.
quotes.rationale: Opening statement of the legislation's purpose — explicitly frames the act around categorizing residents by skin colour and ethnicity as a basis for government action.

quotes.text: Data collected under the act will help identify gaps in programs and services, and allow government to better meet the needs of Indigenous, Black and racialized British Columbians.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that government programs and services will be directed based on demographic categorizations of residents rather than individual needs.

incidents.short_description: The City of Toronto's public survey on street and place name changes asked respondents to identify themselves using racial categories, sorting participants by skin colour as part of the consultation process.
incidents.description_of_event: As part of its Recognition Review project, the City of Toronto commissioned a public survey in May 2022 to gather feedback on new guidelines for naming and renaming streets, lanes, parks, and civic buildings. The survey was developed by The Strategic Counsel on behalf of the City. In addition to questions about naming principles and preferences, the survey included a socio-demographic section that asked respondents to categorize themselves by race. The race question listed categories such as

quotes.text: People often describe themselves by their race or racial background. For example, some people consider themselves "Black", "White" or "East Asian". Which race category best describes you?
quotes.rationale: This is the survey question that explicitly asks respondents to categorize themselves by race or skin colour as part of a government consultation on street naming. It demonstrates the City's practice of sorting participants into demographic categories.

quotes.text: Co-Creation: Choosing names significant to Indigenous Peoples, Black communities, and other underrepresented groups should be done in meaningful partnership with members of these groups.
quotes.rationale: This draft guiding principle frames the naming process explicitly around racial and ethnic identity categories, treating community input as valid or relevant based on demographic group membership rather than individual perspectives.

quotes.text: This survey includes socio-demographic questions to help us understand who is responding. These questions are voluntary and you can select 'Prefer not to answer' for any question.
quotes.rationale: The City frames racial categorization of respondents as a standard data-collection tool in a civic consultation, normalizing the sorting of citizens by demographic categories.

quotes.text: Help shape the City's new commemorative framework by filling out a survey. Survey closes May 29, 2022.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the survey was live and publicly promoted in May 2022, establishing the incident date.

incidents.short_description: The Halton District School Board prepared a staff presentation on elementary de-streaming that framed the pedagogical policy primarily through a 'cycle of oppression' framework, categorising students and society by demographic group rather than addressing individual student needs and learning outcomes.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Halton District School Board developed a multi-session training presentation titled 'Elementary De-streaming Series — Session 1: Elementary Connections,' apparently being finalised for use in September 2022. The presentation was intended to build educator support for removing differentiated learning streams in kindergarten through grade 8 classrooms across the board, which serves approximately 65,000 students in Burlington, Halton Hills, Milton, and Oakville.

Rather than grounding the case for de-streaming in evidence about student learning outcomes or individual student needs, the presentation relied heavily on ideological framing. A substantial portion of the slides depicted a 'cycle of oppression' model that sorted society into systemic, institutional, interpersonal, and internalised layers, and connected classroom practices to broad societal structures including housing, banking, criminal justice, and public health. The presentation posed the question 'In what ways does our action in the classroom impact the trajectory of students' lives?' alongside this oppression framework, implying educators bear responsibility for broad societal outcomes categorised by group identity.

Approximately 10 percent of the slide deck consisted of land acknowledgements presented bilingually in English and French. The presentation also directed educators to read the Truth and Reconciliation Commission Calls to Action and the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of indigenous Peoples as foundational preparation for understanding de-streaming, without explaining the connection to classroom streaming decisions.

The document contained an attributed student quotation — 'They give me the easy math, but I can do the hard one' — as a framing device, but the body of the presentation did not provide research evidence explaining how streaming affects individual student achievement. Educators were instead asked to commit to opposing streaming based on the equity and inclusion framework presented. The presentation was shared with journalist Jonathan Kay by a board insider in May 2022 and was described as being in draft form.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are not served by an education policy discussion grounded in group-based ideological frameworks rather than evidence about individual learning outcomes. When a school board frames a significant pedagogical decision — one that affects how every student is taught from kindergarten through grade 8 — primarily through a lens that categorises children and families by demographic group, it substitutes political commitment for educational analysis. Educators asked to 'commit' to a policy position rather than evaluate evidence are less equipped to advocate effectively for each student's individual learning needs. The presentation's approach normalises the idea that students' classroom experiences and life trajectories are determined by group membership rather than their individual character, effort, and abilities. This undermines the recognition of each child as a distinct individual deserving instruction calibrated to their particular capabilities and circumstances.

quotes.text: How will you continue to learn about Indigenous Rights and utilize these documents as a way to ground and frame both your learning and that of students? Take a moment to read through the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) Calls to Action and United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)
quotes.rationale: Directs educators to read political and legal documents related to indigenous rights as preparatory grounding for a presentation on de-streaming, without explaining the connection — illustrating that the policy is justified through group identity frameworks rather than pedagogical evidence.

incidents.short_description: Upper Canada College administered a demographic census to students from Grade 4 upward, asking children as young as nine to categorize themselves by skin colour and to disclose their sexual orientation and gender identity. Parents were not adequately informed in advance.
incidents.description_of_event:
In spring 2022, Upper Canada College (UCC), a private school in Toronto, Ontario, administered a student experience survey and census to students from Years 4 to 12 (approximately ages 9 to 17). The survey was developed with guidance from an outside consultant and was framed as a tool to deepen the school's understanding of its community and improve the student experience.

The survey asked students to identify themselves by racial category, selecting from a list of groups defined by skin colour and ethnic descent. It also asked students to identify their gender identity from a list that included categories such as non-binary, transgender, two-spirit, and questioning, and asked older students to disclose their sexual orientation, with options including lesbian, gay, bisexual, queer, pansexual, and others. Students in Grades 4 to 6 were asked about their gender identity, including whether they were transgender. Older students were additionally asked about sexual orientation.

The survey was sent to students without adequate prior notice to parents. Following significant community backlash, UCC's principal and board chair issued a letter acknowledging that the school could have done a better job engaging parents in advance, and apologized. The school offered families the option to retroactively withdraw their child's responses. A survey advisory board was announced to provide parent input into future surveys of this nature.

The school described the initiative as part of its "pluralism work" and stated it was conducted following guidelines published by the Canadian Association of Independent Schools, with the consultant having been engaged based on her background in equity and diversity research.
incidents.expected_harm:
Children as young as nine are asked to sort themselves into racial categories based on skin colour and ethnic descent, normalizing the practice of viewing oneself primarily through demographic labels rather than as a distinct individual. Asking young students to categorize themselves this way embeds the habit of identity-based thinking at an early and formative age.

Asking students in Grade 4 to publicly declare whether they are transgender, and asking older students about their sexual orientation, introduces intimate and personal questions into a school institutional setting without parental knowledge or consent. This bypasses the family's role in guiding their children through complex questions about identity and development, and places children in the position of disclosing sensitive personal information to an institution.

The collection of this data — even when anonymized — creates institutional records in which students are sorted by skin colour and sexual characteristics rather than evaluated as individuals based on their character, abilities, and needs. This reinforces a model of schooling in which demographic categories, rather than individual circumstances, drive institutional decision-making. The lack of advance parental notice compounds the harm, as families were denied the opportunity to prepare their children for these questions or to opt out proactively.

quotes.text: In our society, people are often described by their race or racial background. Which racial group(s) best describes you? (Choose one answer only.)
quotes.rationale: This is the framing used to introduce the race categorization question to students as young as Grade 4. It normalizes sorting people by skin colour and ethnicity as a standard institutional practice.

quotes.text:
10. What is your gender identity? (You may choose more than one answer.) If your gender identity is not listed here, please choose "other" and specify it there.
Check all that apply.
Boy
Girl
Non-Binary
Questioning (not sure about my gender identity)
Transgender boy
Transgender girl
Two-Spirit
quotes.rationale: This question was posed to students in Years 4 to 6, meaning children as young as approximately nine years old were asked to declare whether they were transgender or non-binary, without parental notification.

quotes.text:
Q9. What is your sexual orientation? Select all that apply.
Straight / heterosexual
Lesbian
Gay
Bisexual
Two-Spirit
Queer
Questioning
Asexual
Pansexual
quotes.rationale: Older students were asked to disclose their sexual orientation in detail. This is a deeply personal matter being collected institutionally, bypassing family guidance.

quotes.text: Please note, K-8 parents do not answer Q9 on sexual orientation. You will skip to Q10.
quotes.rationale: This note confirms the survey was age-stratified: younger students (K-8) were shielded from the sexual orientation question, but older students were not. It also indicates a parent version of the survey existed, confirming the institution collected this demographic data from families as well.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board ran a racially segregated after-school basketball program for grades 1–3 that was restricted to students based on their skin colour and perceived social category, excluding other children from participating.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board organized an after-school basketball skills program for grades 1–3 students. Rather than opening the program to all interested students, the TDSB explicitly restricted participation to students who identify as belonging to particular demographic categories defined by skin colour and community background. The program was designed and promoted on the basis of these demographic labels, with a TDSB coach of more than ten years' experience leading the sessions. The program was not open to all students regardless of their individual interests or abilities; instead, access was gated by whether a child identified with the specified demographic categories. This represents a racially segregated after-school activity organized and operated by a public school board.
incidents.expected_harm: Children who do not identify with the designated demographic categories are excluded from a publicly run after-school sports program, denied the opportunity to develop basketball skills and benefit from experienced coaching, solely on the basis of their skin colour or perceived background. This normalizes the practice of sorting children into separate programs based on immutable characteristics, teaching students from an early age that institutions evaluate and divide people by demographic labels rather than by individual interest, ability, or need. Segregated programming of this kind undermines the development of shared civic belonging among students and erodes the common experience of growing up alongside one another as individuals.
