It sends a message to all children — those included and those excluded — that their demographic category, rather than their character or enthusiasm, determines what opportunities are available to them.

quotes.text:
This program has been designed to provide an opportunity for grades 1-3 students who identify as Black, racialized and/or from marginalized communities
not  to begin developing their basketball skills under the supervision of an experienced TDSB coach.
quotes.rationale: This is the core evidence showing the program explicitly restricts participation to students based on their skin colour and demographic identity, rather than opening access to all students.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Association of Social Workers promoted monthly gatherings premised on the idea that white social workers are inherently part of the problem of racism. The invitation sorts members by skin colour rather than addressing discrimination as a concern that applies to all individuals equally.
incidents.description_of_event: The Ontario Association of Social Workers (OASW), through its Local Engagement Networks, sent a bulletin to members promoting a series of monthly gatherings titled 'Addressing Racism.' The promotional email, distributed via the OASW/ATTSO bulletin, described the gatherings as an opportunity for social workers to reflect on the premise that white social workers are part of the problem in sustaining white supremacy, and to deconstruct what the bulletin called blind spots resulting from an education rooted in white privilege, colonialism, and racism. The framing of the invitation explicitly sorted members by skin colour, directing the conversation toward the idea that a person's role in racism is determined by their skin colour rather than their individual conduct, beliefs, or actions. The gatherings were scheduled monthly beginning May 5, from 7:00 pm to 8:30 pm, and registration was open to social workers across Ontario.
incidents.expected_harm: Social workers receive institutional messaging from their professional association that assigns moral culpability based on skin colour, reinforcing the idea that individuals are defined by their demographic characteristics rather than their own character and conduct. Members are evaluated and categorized by the association based on an immutable trait, which undermines the principle that each person deserves to be recognized for their unique qualities. This framing discourages open, evidence-based inquiry into how discrimination actually occurs and instead promotes a fixed ideological framework that treats skin colour as determinative of a person's worldview and complicity. It normalizes the practice of dividing professionals into groups based on immutable characteristics, rather than fostering a shared commitment to treating every person with dignity.

quotes.text: They are social workers who want to deconstruct their blind spots resulting from their education that is rooted in White privilege, colonialism, and racism.
quotes.rationale: Characterizes the professional education of members as inherently tainted by their skin colour, reinforcing an essentialist framing that attributes characteristics and fault to individuals based on demographic category.

quotes.text: Social workers across Ontario from different localities have come together to facilitate and reflect on the important topic of White social workers recognizing that they are part of the problem in sustaining White supremacy and what that means for ongoing reconstruction of our ways of being.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly frames the gatherings around the premise that social workers bear collective responsibility for racism based on their skin colour, sorting members by a demographic characteristic rather than individual conduct.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board proposed changes to entrance requirements for specialty programs that would give priority access to applicants based on their demographic identity rather than individual merit or need.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board considered policy changes to the entrance requirements for centralized and specialty programs. Under the proposed approach, students who meet the base application requirements would be entered into a random selection process when demand exceeds available spaces. However, priority access would be given to students who self-identify as belonging to what the board characterizes as 'currently and historically underserved communities.' The board also proposed an annual review of demographic data within centralized programs and schools to ensure student enrolment reflects the demographic composition of the broader TDSB student population. Based on these annual reviews, the board indicated it would adjust priority groups and the number of priority seats reserved each year. The policy was discussed at a Governance and Policy Committee meeting on or around April 27, 2022. These changes represent an institutional decision to allocate seats in sought-after educational programs based on students' demographic self-identification rather than their individual abilities, character, or demonstrated needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are evaluated for access to specialty educational programs based on demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique abilities and circumstances. A student who meets the entry requirements can be displaced from a program not because of any individual failing, but solely because of their demographic background. This entrenches the practice of sorting students into demographic groups and using those categories to determine access to opportunity. Students learn from the earliest stages of their education that institutions view them primarily through the lens of group membership rather than individual worth. The annual demographic review process institutionalizes ongoing demographic engineering of school populations, meaning enrolment decisions are perpetually shaped by group identity rather than individual merit. This undermines the principle that every student deserves to be evaluated on their own character and abilities, and erodes shared civic belonging by teaching students that educational institutions distribute opportunity based on which demographic group one belongs to.

quotes.text: Students meeting the base requirements of application will be considered for a random selection process if demand exceeds available spaces. Priority access will be given to students self-identifying as representing currently and historically underserved communities and students who are residing in the City of Toronto.
quotes.rationale: This passage directly establishes that access to specialty programs is allocated in part based on demographic self-identification, giving priority to certain students over others on that basis rather than individual merit or need.

quotes.text: Every year, the TDSB will examine students' demographic data within centralized programs and schools to create learning environments that are made up of student demographics that are representative of the wider TDSB student population. Based on this review, there may be adjustments made to the priority groups and the number of priority seats set aside each year.
quotes.rationale: This passage shows the board intends to continuously adjust who receives priority access to programs based on demographic engineering goals, institutionalizing demographic categorization as the primary mechanism for managing school enrolment.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
incidents.short_description: The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat ran a federal public service leadership development program that restricted eligibility based on demographic categories including skin colour, sex, disability status, and sexual orientation. Public servants who do not belong to designated groups are excluded from access to this career advancement pathway.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat operated the Mosaic Leadership Development Program, a federally funded leadership development initiative designed to prepare public servants at the EX-minus-1 level for appointment to executive (EX-01) positions. To be eligible, candidates were required to identify as a member of one of four designated employment equity groups — women, indigenous peoples, members of visible minorities, and persons with a disability — or as a member of LGBTQ2+ communities. Public servants who do not identify under any of these categories are ineligible to apply, regardless of their qualifications, demonstrated leadership potential, or career needs.

The program provides substantial career benefits to participants: sponsorship, stretch assignments and secondments, a customized learning curriculum, and a formal assessment against EX qualification standards that feeds directly into appointment processes for executive positions. Departments are also instructed to identify specific EX-01 positions in succession planning for Mosaic participants. Selection among eligible candidates is further filtered by factors including the "intersectionality of identities," meaning that a candidate's membership in multiple demographic categories is treated as a relevant qualification for access to the program.

The program was administered by the Centre on Diversity and Inclusion within the Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat, with deputy heads across federal departments responsible for implementation. Departments were guided to prioritize certain demographic groups over others based on their internal representation gaps and diversity plans.
incidents.expected_harm: Federal public servants who do not belong to the designated demographic categories are denied access to a structured career development pathway that leads directly to executive appointments. Career advancement in the federal public service becomes contingent not only on ability and merit but on an individual's demographic identity. This creates a two-tier system within the public service where opportunities for leadership development and executive appointment are distributed based on immutable or personal characteristics rather than individual ability, demonstrated need, or merit. Employees excluded from the program receive no sponsorship, no stretch assignments structured around their development, and no formal assessment pathway to executive positions through this channel — disadvantages that compound over time. The program also institutionalises the practice of categorising employees by skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation as the basis for professional advancement decisions, normalising the reduction of individuals to demographic proxies. By embedding "intersectionality of identities" as a selection criterion, the program further entrenches the view that a person's demographic profile — rather than their character, skills, and individual circumstances — is the relevant lens through which their career potential should be assessed.

quotes.text: identify as a member of a designated employment equity group and/or LGBTQ2+ communities
quotes.rationale: This is the eligibility gate: participation in the program requires self-identification within demographic categories. Public servants who do not identify this way are excluded from the program entirely.

quotes.text: Equity-seeking groups, for the purposes of Mosaic, means the four Employment Equity groups - women, Indigenous Peoples, members of visible minorities, and persons with a disability - subgroups within these four, and LGBTQ2+ communities.
quotes.rationale: Defines the specific demographic categories used to restrict program access, confirming that eligibility is structured around sex, skin colour/ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation.

quotes.text: intersectionality of identities.
quotes.rationale: Identifies 'intersectionality of identities' as a selection criterion among eligible candidates, meaning a person's membership in multiple demographic categories improves their chances of selection — further entrenching demographic categorisation as the basis for career opportunity.

quotes.text: The Mosaic Leadership Development program is available throughout the public service to equip equity-seeking employees to enter the EX group and provide flexibility for departments in addressing their unique gaps in employment equity representation.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the program's explicit purpose is to provide a pathway to executive positions specifically for employees in designated demographic groups, to the exclusion of others.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada School of Public Service
incidents.short_description: The Canada School of Public Service published a training course for federal public servants that explicitly states treating people as individuals rather than by skin colour is no longer acceptable. The course frames public servants' work through the lens of demographic categories rather than individual merit and dignity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canada School of Public Service, operating under the Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat, published an online training course titled 'Taking Action to Build an Anti-Racist Canadian Public Service,' accessible to federal public servants through the learn-apprendre.ca platform. The course instructs public servants that a 'colourblindness strategy' — that is, evaluating people as individuals regardless of their skin colour — is 'no longer acceptable.' The course calls on public servants to reject this approach and instead adopt what it terms an 'anti-racist' framework, which organizes thinking and action around people's skin colour and ethnicity. The course content includes sections on skin-colour-specific observances and resources, framing the public service's role through demographic categories. It draws on a quote from the Clerk of the Privy Council and a statement from the Prime Minister to lend institutional authority to this approach. The course directs federal employees toward further resources that reinforce categorizing people by skin colour and ethnicity as the correct mode of professional conduct.
incidents.expected_harm: Federal public servants are instructed by their employer that evaluating colleagues and members of the public as individuals — without regard to skin colour — is an unacceptable professional posture. This institutional directive normalizes the practice of sorting people into demographic categories and treating those categories as the primary lens through which public servants do their work. Citizens seeking government services, and public servants themselves, are evaluated and engaged with based on their skin colour rather than their individual circumstances, needs, abilities, or character. The course undermines the principle that every person deserves recognition as a distinct individual, and instead embeds into federal institutional culture the expectation that skin colour should shape how public servants perceive and interact with people. Over time this shapes hiring decisions, policy development, service delivery, and workplace culture across the entire federal public service.

quotes.text: colorblindness strategy is no longer acceptable and we need to take action to enact change.
quotes.rationale: This is the central statement of concern: the course explicitly tells federal public servants that treating people without regard to skin colour is unacceptable, directly contradicting the Coalition's principle of evaluating people as individuals.

quotes.text: The past several months have precipitated deep reflection on the unjust treatment of Black people, other racialized groups, and Indigenous peoples in our society. As public servants come forward and courageously share their lived experiences, the urgency of removing systemic racism from our institutions and from our culture becomes more evident.
quotes.rationale: Quote from the Clerk of the Privy Council lends senior institutional authority to the framework, reinforcing that this demographic-category approach is endorsed at the highest levels of the federal public service.

incidents.short_description: Mount Royal University Faculty Association retreat materials included a reporting mechanism allowing members to report colleagues for statements that made them feel "invalidated" during discussions, treating disagreement as potential harm requiring intervention.
incidents.description_of_event: The Mount Royal University Faculty Association held a spring retreat in April 2022 with retreat materials that framed disagreement as potentially harmful and established formal reporting mechanisms for members who felt affected by colleague statements. The materials assumed conversations would "activate members" and be "challenging in various personal, interpersonal, and emotional ways." Members encountering "concerning content, behaviours, and/or discussion" could complete a Member Support Form monitored by an on-site support person who would respond promptly. The form allowed members to report sessions where they experienced content they found concerning, with options for online, phone, or in-person support. The materials treated spaces as "brave, though not entirely safe" and emphasized that members could disengage from conversations and "remove yourself from the space" if they found it harmful, with assurances that "many of your colleagues in the space with you are allies and will support your decision to leave a space that is harmful to you." Community agreements required members to "not invalidate others' experiences" and recognize that "our intention does not mitigate impact," creating standards where the subjective reception of statements could warrant formal reporting regardless of speaker intent.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will discourage open dialogue and intellectual exchange among faculty members. When disagreement itself is treated as potentially harmful and subject to formal reporting, faculty will self-censor rather than engage in substantive debate on complex issues. The reporting mechanism creates an environment where colleagues monitor each other's speech and where expressing views that make others uncomfortable becomes a reportable offence. This undermines the pursuit of truth through reasoned discourse and evidence-based thinking. Faculty members will avoid engaging with the full spectrum of perspectives when doing so could result in being reported to union staff. The emphasis on subjective feelings of being "invalidated" over the content of arguments privileges emotional comfort over intellectual honesty and the free exchange of ideas necessary for academic inquiry.

quotes.text: Recognize that our intention does not mitigate impact.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that speaker intent is irrelevant to whether their speech is problematic, privileging subjective reception

quotes.text: This form has been created by the MRFA's PD Committee to serve as a resource for members who encounter concerning content, behaviours, and/or discussion during the sessions held at the 2022 retreat.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the form is specifically for reporting concerning discussion content, not just harassment

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Broadcasting Corporation
incidents.short_description: CBC Arts promoted a comedian primarily through a series of demographic identity labels rather than focusing on their artistic work and individual character. The article frames the comedian's significance almost entirely through group membership categories.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 21, 2022, CBC Arts published a profile of comedian Rush Kazi to mark the April 29 release of their TV special and comedy album. The article, written by CBC Arts producer Peter Knegt for his LGBTQ-culture column 'Queeries,' introduces Kazi to readers by leading with a string of demographic identity labels — describing them as 'the first non-binary, pansexual, South Asian, Muslim comedian' to release a special and album simultaneously in Canada. The headline and opening framing anchor Kazi's newsworthiness firmly in these demographic categories. While the article does include quotes from Kazi noting that they are not solely defined by these identities — 'I'm me, I'm clumsy, I'm a procrastinator, I can be a conman' — the institutional framing provided by CBC continues to centre demographic labelling as the primary lens through which the comedian's achievement is presented. The piece appears in a column explicitly dedicated to LGBTQ culture, further situating the coverage within an identity-category framework rather than a general arts context.
incidents.expected_harm: When a publicly funded national broadcaster consistently frames artists and their achievements primarily through demographic identity labels, it reinforces the idea that a person's group memberships are their most newsworthy attribute, rather than their individual talents, effort, or creative work. This normalises the institutional habit of sorting and evaluating people by demographic categories, which undermines recognition of each person's singular worth. Audiences — including young people — receive the message that identity labels are the defining feature of a person's public significance. This fragments rather than unifies, directing attention toward demographic difference rather than toward the shared human qualities and individual achievements that connect people.

quotes.text: Kazi is ready to be your new favourite non-binary, pansexual, South Asian, Muslim comedian
quotes.rationale: This is the article's promotional tagline, appearing as a subheadline, and it defines Kazi exclusively through a list of demographic identity labels rather than through their comedic voice, style, or achievements.

quotes.text: This also almost certainly makes them the first non-binary, pansexual, South Asian, Muslim comedian to achieve either feat in Canada, let alone both at the same time.
quotes.rationale: CBC frames the comedian's milestone achievement primarily in terms of demographic identity categories, treating group membership as the most significant aspect of the accomplishment rather than the creative and professional achievement itself.

incidents.short_description: The Youth Media Alliance, in partnership with Radio-Canada, Téléquébec, TFO, and APTN, restricted eligibility for a paid production internship scholarship to applicants based on their racial or ethnic background. People who do not identify as a visible minority or as indigenous are excluded from applying.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Youth Media Alliance (YMA), in partnership with Radio-Canada, Téléquébec, TFO, and APTN, launched the French-Language Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Youth Production Scholarship beginning April 19, 2022. The scholarship offers four internship placements per year over three years, each covering up to 10 days of paid work at $250 per day (totalling $2,500 per placement) within a French-language youth television production. Applications opened April 19, 2022, and closed May 31, 2022, with winners selected by a committee of YMA members.

Eligibility for the scholarship is explicitly restricted by racial and ethnic background. Applicants must identify as a member of a visible minority or an indigenous community to qualify. All other applicants — regardless of their interest in youth media, their French-language ability, or their financial need — are categorically excluded on the basis of their background. The eligibility criterion appears prominently in both the French and English versions of the scholarship description and is stated as a hard requirement alongside citizenship status and age.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are assessed not on their individual abilities, interests, financial circumstances, or career stage, but on their racial or ethnic background. People who do not belong to the designated demographic categories are excluded from a paid professional opportunity, regardless of their individual merits or needs. This institutionalises the practice of sorting people by demographic category when allocating professional development resources, reinforcing the idea that a person's background is a legitimate basis for granting or denying opportunity. It normalises demographic gatekeeping within the media industry and sends a signal that advancement in the sector is tied to group membership rather than individual talent and effort.

quotes.text: 2- Identify as a member of a visible minority or an Indigenous community
quotes.rationale: This is the explicit eligibility criterion that restricts the scholarship to applicants based on racial or ethnic background, directly excluding all others regardless of individual merit or need.

quotes.text: OBJECTIVE: TO OFFER INTERNSHIPS IN YOUTH PRODUCTION TO FRENCH-SPEAKING PEOPLE FROM DIVERSE OR INDIGENOUS COMMUNITIES
quotes.rationale: The stated objective of the scholarship makes explicit that the program is designed from the outset to target participants based on demographic categories rather than individual circumstances.

incidents.short_description: The Jessie Richardson Award Society announced it is suspending its theatre awards program, citing a belief that the social structure of Canadian society causes people of certain backgrounds to have less free time available for volunteer jury service. The board's statement frames the inability to recruit diverse jurors as an insurmountable structural problem rooted in race and other demographic characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 19, 2022, the Board of Directors of the Jessie Richardson Award Society issued a public statement announcing the Jessie Awards would go on hiatus effective May 1, 2022, with all jurying suspended. The board explained that its central difficulty was recruiting a sufficiently diverse pool of volunteer jurors. Rather than addressing this as a practical resourcing or outreach challenge, the board framed it as an insoluble consequence of society being "structured by white supremacy, capitalism, colonialism, ableism, and patriarchy," which it stated causes free time to be "more frequently available to people who hold privilege in these systems." In other words, the board concluded that people of certain skin colours and demographic backgrounds structurally lack the time to volunteer as jurors — a conclusion drawn from demographic categories rather than from the individual circumstances of any actual or potential volunteer. The statement also described changes made to the awards program since 2020, including mandatory ideological training for all board members, jury members, and affiliated individuals, and revision of performance categories along demographic lines. The board cited its failure to achieve what it called "industry-wide buy-in" for these changes as a further reason for the hiatus, and called on community members to come forward and reshape the organization.
incidents.expected_harm: The award program is suspended for the entire Vancouver theatre community, depriving artists of recognition and the career benefits that come from it. The stated rationale treats people's demographic characteristics — particularly their skin colour — as determinative of their capacity and availability to participate in civic and cultural life. This framing reduces individuals to proxies for group identities rather than recognizing them as people with unique circumstances and the capacity to make their own choices. Artists who volunteer, have volunteered, or would have volunteered as jurors are implicitly told their participation reflects demographic privilege rather than individual commitment or generosity. The suspension of a community institution based on ideological conclusions about group-level privilege causes concrete harm to the theatre community and normalizes the practice of viewing people's actions and capabilities through the lens of their immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: It's also true that in our society, structured by white supremacy, capitalism, colonialism, ableism, and patriarchy, the ability to offer that time is more frequently available to people who hold privilege in these systems.
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement underpinning the board's decision: it attributes the volunteer availability of potential jurors to their demographic characteristics rather than to individual circumstances, treating skin colour and other group identities as determinative of people's capacity to participate.

incidents.short_description: The Equity Chair of the Simcoe County Elementary Teachers' Federation publicly stated she was waiting for the day when Critical Race Theory becomes mandatory in Ontario education and those who oppose it lose their teaching licenses.
incidents.description_of_event: Sara Savoia, who held the position of Equity Chair for the Simcoe County Elementary Teachers' Federation, posted on social media that she was waiting for the day when Critical Race Theory becomes mandatory in Ontario education and all who oppose it lose their teaching license. The post was made on a public platform and subsequently drew attention from journalists and commentators who raised concerns about a union official advocating for colleagues to lose their professional credentials based on their views about educational approaches. The statement suggested support for mandatory adoption of a particular ideological framework and professional consequences for those who disagree with it.
incidents.expected_harm: This incident will create an environment where teachers feel unable to express concerns or alternative views about pedagogical approaches without fear of professional repercussions. Educators may self-censor rather than engage in honest discourse about teaching methods and curriculum content. The statement by a union official advocating for license revocation based on disagreement with a particular theoretical framework undermines the capacity for reasoned debate about educational practice and may discourage teachers from evaluating policies and approaches based on their individual assessment of evidence and effectiveness. This chills open dialogue and threatens the evaluation of educators based on their individual capabilities and professional judgment rather than adherence to specific ideological positions.

quotes.text: Waiting for the day CRT is mandatory in #OntEd, and all who oppose (rightfully) lose their license.
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement that constitutes the incident. It explicitly calls for mandatory adoption of Critical Race Theory and for professional consequences (license revocation) for those who oppose it. This demonstrates advocacy for employment restrictions based on views rather than individual professional competence.

incidents.institution_name: University of Waterloo > Faculty of Mathematics
incidents.short_description: The University of Waterloo Faculty of Mathematics advertised multiple faculty and postdoctoral positions restricted to applicants based on their skin colour or ethnicity, excluding individuals who did not possess these immutable characteristics from eligibility.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Waterloo Faculty of Mathematics posted employment opportunities that restricted eligibility based on applicants' immutable characteristics. The Provost's Program for Black and Indigenous Postdoctoral Scholars explicitly limited applications to individuals who self-identify as black or indigenous. Similarly, the Indigenous and black excellence faculty hiring initiative restricted tenure-track and tenured positions to applicants who self-identify as Indigenous or self-identify as black. A Tier 1 Canada Research Chair position was advertised exclusively for applicants who self-identify as Indigenous, with self-identification information used for screening purposes. These positions span assistant professor through full professor ranks across all mathematics disciplines, including mathematics, actuarial science, statistics, and computer science. The university framed these restrictions as addressing systemic underrepresentation and ensuring equity, diversity, and inclusivity.
incidents.expected_harm: These hiring restrictions will deny opportunities to qualified candidates based solely on their skin colour or Indigenous identity rather than their individual qualifications, achievements, and potential contributions. Applicants who do not possess the specified immutable characteristics will be excluded from consideration regardless of their merit, research excellence, teaching abilities, or alignment with institutional needs. This practice reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as unique persons with distinct capabilities and perspectives. The restrictions undermine fair competition and signal that certain groups are unwelcome in these academic positions, contradicting the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. By institutionalizing demographic-based exclusions in faculty hiring, the university establishes a precedent that legitimizes evaluating people through the lens of immutable traits rather than individual merit.

quotes.text: Provost's Program for Black and Indigenous Postdoctoral Scholars The aim of this program is to support Black and Indigenous scholars, as aligned with the University's strategic plan to strengthen sustainable and diverse communities, with particular attention to promoting Indigenous initiatives and ensuring a culture of equity, diversity, and inclusivity for all.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the postdoctoral program restricts eligibility to applicants based on their skin colour or Indigenous identity, framing this restriction as promoting equity and diversity.

quotes.text: All applicants to this open Tier 1 CRC position must self-identify as Indigenous using the self-identification applicant survey. Because this is a special opportunity for self-identified Indigenous applicants, applicant self-identification information will be used for the purposes of screening and consideration.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that self-identification based on Indigenous identity is explicitly used to screen applicants, meaning candidates without this characteristic are excluded from consideration regardless of qualifications.

incidents.institution_name: University of Waterloo > Faculty of Environment
incidents.short_description: The University of Waterloo Faculty of Environment restricted a Canada Research Chair position to applicants who self-identify as women, transgender, non-binary, or two-spirit, excluding other candidates from consideration based on sex and gender identity.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Waterloo Faculty of Environment advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position combined with a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair in Climate Change, Water or Future Cities. The job posting explicitly stated that the opportunity was open only to individuals who self-identify as women, transgender, non-binary, or two-spirit. The posting indicated this restriction followed provisions for a special program under the Ontario Human Rights Commission to address underrepresentation among Canada Research Chairs. All applicants were required to complete a self-identification survey, and this information would be used for screening and consideration purposes. The posting stated that while all qualified candidates were encouraged to apply, Canadians and permanent residents would be given priority, but the primary eligibility restriction was based on sex and gender identity. The position had a salary range of $90,000 to $110,000 for Assistant Professor rank, with a closing date of April 22, 2022. A separate posting for Assistant Professors in Sustainability and Financial Management included diversity impact statement requirements and encouraged applications from historically disadvantaged groups but did not restrict eligibility.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction will exclude qualified candidates from consideration for an academic position based on their sex and gender identity rather than their individual qualifications, research excellence, and teaching capabilities. Candidates who do not identify within the specified categories cannot be evaluated on their merit, accomplishments, or potential contributions to the field regardless of their expertise in climate change, water science, or future cities research. The practice reduces individuals to demographic categories and denies them the opportunity to be assessed as individuals with unique qualities and capabilities. This approach undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements rather than immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: This call is open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women, transgender, non-binary, or two-spirit.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly restricts eligibility for the position based on sex and gender identity, excluding individuals who do not fit these categories from consideration regardless of qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Professional Engineers Ontario adopted a policy code that formally commits the organization to sorting and prioritising licence holders, applicants, and staff based on their demographic group membership, including skin colour, ethnicity, and gender identity.
incidents.description_of_event:
At its April 8, 2022 meeting, the Council of Professional Engineers Ontario (PEO) approved its Anti-Racism and Equity (ARE) Code, a formal policy document that governs PEO's operations as a regulator, employer, and service provider for over 91,500 licence and certificate holders in Ontario.

The code explicitly adopts an approach that prioritises people based on their demographic categories rather than their individual circumstances. The foundational section states that policy development will "initially prioritize the most marginalized communities, including Black, Indigenous other racialized populations," framing group membership—particularly skin colour and ethnicity—as the primary lens through which PEO will direct resources, strategy, and attention.

Principle 8 of the code commits PEO to "equitable hiring and representation of persons from all equity-seeking backgrounds, with first focus on Indigenous, Black, and racialized groups, and high focus towards gender equity," explicitly ranking which demographic groups will receive preferential focus in hiring and advancement decisions.

The code commits PEO to collecting and publicly reporting disaggregated race-based data on licence applicants, staff, and others it governs, broken down by skin colour, ethnicity, and other identity categories. Under Principle 1, this data is to be used to direct resources and interventions toward specific demographic groups identified as underrepresented.

Principle 3 extends the code's reach beyond PEO itself: it commits PEO to reforming its regulatory oversight processes to "reinforce the professional obligations of all licence holders to uphold human rights laws" and to tracking professional conduct with a DEI lens—effectively extending the code's demographic categorisation framework to all engineers PEO governs.

The code was drafted primarily, in its own words, "through a racial equity lens," treating skin colour and ethnicity as the organizing framework for institutional decision-making across licensing, complaints, discipline, hiring, and leadership.

PEO's president, Christian Bellini, described the code as "a key strategic direction" and stated that "the ARE Code will help guide us in this respect by promoting equity and inclusion, and advancing our commitment to fairness and human rights."
incidents.expected_harm:
By formally embedding demographic categories—particularly skin colour, ethnicity, and gender identity—as the primary lens for institutional decision-making, PEO treats the engineers and applicants it governs as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualifications, circumstances, and abilities. This approach creates a system where access to opportunities, resources, and regulatory attention is distributed based on group membership rather than individual merit or demonstrated need.

Licence applicants are assessed not only on their engineering qualifications but through a demographic lens, with the code directing PEO to track and act on whether applicants of certain skin colours or ethnicities succeed at different rates. Principle 8's explicit commitment to demographic-based hiring preferences for PEO staff means individuals competing for positions within PEO face a process where their group membership is a factor in outcomes.

Engineers governed by PEO—a mandatory regulatory body they cannot opt out of—are subject to an institution that has formally categorised them by skin colour and ethnicity and committed to directing its regulatory attention based on those categories. This normalises the institutional practice of sorting people by immutable traits.

The code's reach into professional conduct oversight means engineers may face regulatory scrutiny tied to DEI compliance expectations, extending demographic-based institutional thinking into the practice of the profession itself. Rather than evaluating all engineers as individuals deserving consistent, merit-based regulation, PEO has created a tiered system where demographic identity shapes how the regulator sees and treats those it governs.

quotes.text: The new Anti-Racism and Equity (ARE) Code promotes policy development approaches that initially will prioritize the most marginalized communities, including Black, Indigenous other racialized populations, and provide a high focus on developing strategies to counter any systemic discrimination impacting persons based on gender identity—including female, two-spirit, intersex, transgender and gender variant persons.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that PEO's policy development explicitly prioritises specific demographic groups, directing institutional resources and attention based on skin colour, ethnicity, and gender identity rather than individual circumstances.

quotes.text: Principle 8: Equitable organization. As the primary steward of Ontario's engineering profession, PEO commits to leading by example and ensuring equitable hiring and representation of persons from all equity-seeking backgrounds, with first focus on Indigenous, Black, and racialized groups, and high focus towards gender equity. PEO commits to achieving such representation at all levels in the organization, and fostering retention, inclusion, advancement, belonging and equity regarding equity-seeking persons and everyone, at all levels in the organization.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly commits PEO to demographic-based hiring preferences, naming specific groups by skin colour and ethnicity as priority recipients. This formalises group-based preferential treatment in employment decisions.

incidents.short_description: The Canada Media Fund issued a statement categorizing creators by skin colour and committing to funding mechanisms that support storytellers based on their skin colour rather than individual merit or creative vision.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 8, 2022, the Canada Media Fund posted a statement on social media responding to Barry Avrich's acceptance speech at the Canadian Screen Awards. The statement divided creators into categories based on skin colour and committed to implementing funding mechanisms that support storytellers based on their skin colour. The organization announced it would add inclusion measures to its funding programs and continue a pilot program for what it termed racialized communities. The statement framed these initiatives as necessary to achieve what it called systemic change and authentic representation on Canadian screens.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will reduce creators to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique artistic visions, life experiences, and creative capabilities. Funding decisions based on skin colour rather than individual merit, creative quality, or demonstrated storytelling ability undermine the principle that each person should be recognized for their character and abilities. This practice establishes institutional barriers where access to resources depends on demographic classification rather than individual achievement or creative potential. It signals that institutions will categorize and treat Canadians differently based on immutable characteristics, fragmenting the creative community along demographic lines rather than fostering shared civic belonging and evaluating each creator's distinct contributions to Canadian storytelling.

quotes.text: BLACK CREATORS NEED TO BE GIVEN THE TOOLS TO CREATE CULTURALLY RELEVANT CONTENT AND SHARE STORIES ABOUT THEIR HISTORY, COMMUNITIES AND LIVED EXPERIENCES.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the organization's commitment to categorizing creators by skin colour and providing resources based on that categorization rather than individual creative merit or demonstrated need.

incidents.institution_name: The Academy of Canadian Cinema & Television
incidents.short_description: Multiple film organizations sent an open letter to the Academy of Canadian Cinema & Television and Canadian funders asserting that only individuals of certain skin colours should direct documentaries about people of those same skin colours. The letter demanded a public denunciation of a filmmaker who stated that the teller of stories does not matter.
incidents.description_of_event:
On April 7, 2022, a coalition of film organizations including BIPOC TV & Film, Black Screen Office, Reelworld Screen Institute, and others sent an open letter to the Academy of Canadian Cinema & Television and major Canadian film funders. The letter responded to director Barry Avrich's acceptance speech at the Canadian Screen Awards on April 7, 2022, where he stated after winning best direction for his documentary Oscar Peterson: Black + White that "there are so many Black stories in Canada that need to be told. It doesn't matter who tells them, we just need to tell them."

The organizations disagreed with Avrich's position, arguing that only individuals with specific lived experiences based on their skin colour should direct documentaries about people of the same skin colour. They asserted that "authenticity and lived experiences provide context for authorship" and that directors with different skin colours "will simply not have the same first person lens." The letter claimed Avrich's statement represented "a deliberate assertion" that he could "appropriate our stories and be rewarded for it."

The letter demanded the Academy issue a public statement denouncing Avrich's comments. The organizations framed their position as essential to "dismantling systemic racism" and stated there should be "No Stories About Us, Without Us" for all marginalized storytellers. They argued that funding decisions should be contingent on the demographic characteristics of filmmakers rather than their individual capabilities and artistic merit.

The Academy responded via Twitter without naming Avrich, stating "It is clear, however, that there is still much work to be done in dismantling the system that has stood in the way of diverse voices being rightfully heard. It does matter." Avrich subsequently apologized, stating he "misspoke" and affirming "Of course it matters who tells stories; intentionality, lived experience, an anti-oppression stance matter."
incidents.expected_harm: This incident will restrict opportunities for individual filmmakers based on their skin colour rather than their abilities, experience, or artistic vision. Documentary filmmakers will face barriers to telling stories about subjects unless they share specific demographic characteristics with those subjects, reducing the pool of artists who can explore particular topics and limiting artistic freedom. The position articulated in the letter establishes that individuals must be evaluated and sorted by demographic categories rather than assessed on their individual qualities, research capabilities, and demonstrated filmmaking skills. This framework will lead to the exclusion of capable artists from projects based solely on immutable traits, denying them professional opportunities they would otherwise merit. Audiences will receive a narrower range of perspectives and artistic approaches to documentary subjects as the diversity of thought and individual viewpoints becomes constrained by demographic gatekeeping. The principle that storytellers must match the demographic profile of their subjects will spread beyond documentary filmmaking to other areas of cultural production, progressively limiting who can explore which topics based on categorical sorting rather than individual capability. Funders and institutions will face pressure to make decisions based on demographic criteria rather than artistic merit, creating a system where individual achievement and capability become secondary to group identity in determining access to resources and opportunities.

quotes.text: During the tail end of his speech (full speech starts at 39:40), Mr. Avrich ended with the words: "That this is a testament that there are so many Black stories in Canada that need to be told." We are vehemently opposed to Mr. Avrich's next comment that "it doesn't matter who tells them, we just need to tell'em".
quotes.rationale: Establishes the statement that triggered the organizational response and their opposition to the position that storyteller identity is irrelevant

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of Justice Canada
incidents.short_description: The Department of Justice posted a job advertisement that restricted screening to indigenous peoples, members of racialized groups, persons with disabilities, and equity-seeking groups, excluding other qualified applicants from consideration based on demographic categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The Department of Justice Canada posted a legal counsel position advertisement (reference JUS21J-010588-000616) that limited screening of applicants based on demographic categories. The posting stated that the department would increase workforce representation by restricting screening to indigenous peoples, members of racialized groups, persons with disabilities, and equity-seeking groups. The advertisement appeared on the Government of Canada's official jobs website and was open to positions at multiple locations across Canada at the LP-01 and LP-02 levels. The posting required applicants to self-declare membership in designated groups through the application process, and explicitly stated this information would be factored into screening, assessment, and selection decisions. The position remained open until April 1, 2023.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction denies qualified legal professionals the opportunity to be considered for government positions based on their demographic characteristics rather than their individual qualifications, abilities, and demonstrated merit. It reduces applicants to membership in demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique skills and experiences. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should assess people based on their character, capabilities, and achievements. It creates a system where career advancement depends on group identity rather than individual merit, potentially excluding highly qualified candidates who could serve Canadians effectively. This approach to hiring fails to recognize the singular worth of each applicant and may diminish public confidence in the fairness and competence of government legal services.

quotes.text: Justice Canada will be looking to increase representation in our workforce and will limit screening of applicants to Indigenous Peoples, members of a racialized group, persons with a disability and equity seeking groups.
quotes.rationale: This quote directly establishes that the Department of Justice restricted applicant screening based on demographic categories, excluding individuals who do not belong to specified groups from consideration.

incidents.short_description: Brant Family and Children's Services published a webpage recommending children's resources focused on race consciousness, including materials that encourage discussing and categorizing children by skin colour and promoting activist frameworks for understanding social issues.
incidents.description_of_event: Brant Family and Children's Services, a child welfare agency serving Brantford, Brant, and New Credit in Ontario, maintained a webpage titled "Children's Resources" under its Equity & Inclusion section. The page stated that children can internalize bias between ages 2 and 4, and emphasized the importance of conversations about equity, anti-racism, and social justice. The agency recommended numerous resources for parents and caregivers, including books, videos, and activities organized by age group. These materials consistently focused on discussing and highlighting children's immutable traits, particularly skin colour and ethnicity. The recommended materials included "10 Tips for Teaching and Talking to Kids about Race" which advised parents to let children know it's okay to notice and talk about skin colour, and "The ABCs of Diversity: Helping Kids (and Ourselves!) Embrace Our Differences." The page also promoted books such as "A is for Activist" for young children, described as teaching "the alphabet of humane values" and covering activism, environmental justice, civil rights, and LGBTQ rights, and "Stamped: Racism, Antiracism, and You," which frames the construct of race as always being used to gain and keep power. The agency's approach centered on encouraging adults to actively discuss and emphasize children's demographic categories from an early age as part of building what they termed racial literacy.
incidents.expected_harm: Children served by this child welfare agency will be taught to view themselves and others primarily through the lens of demographic categories rather than as unique individuals. By encouraging adults to emphasize skin colour and other immutable traits from preschool age, these materials risk socializing children to reduce themselves and their peers to group identities. The promotion of activist frameworks for understanding social issues may limit children's capacity to engage with multiple perspectives and develop independent critical thinking. Children may internalize the message that their demographic characteristics are their most salient features, potentially undermining their sense of individual agency and singular worth. The emphasis on group-based categories from such a young age may also entrench rather than reduce bias, as children learn to categorize and make assumptions about others based on immutable traits rather than character and individual qualities.

incidents.short_description: Toronto District School Board distributed a resource document titled 'Talking About Race Resources for Families' that organizes children and educational content primarily around categories of skin colour and ancestry, promoting frameworks that categorize people based on immutable characteristics rather than individual qualities.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board created and distributed a resource document for parents and guardians titled 'Talking About Race Resources for Families.' The document presents books, websites, and materials organized around discussions of skin colour and ancestry categories. The resource list includes materials such as 'Antiracist Baby' by Ibram X. Kendi, 'Not My Idea: A Book About Whiteness,' and 'Let It Shine: Stories of Black Women Freedom Fighters.' The document explicitly states its purpose is to 'talk about race and racism as it affects a wide range of identities' and provides guidance for families to engage children from early years through secondary school in conversations that center on categorizing people by skin colour and ancestry. The materials recommend teaching children to view themselves and others through demographic categories, with books specifically designed to address children based on their skin colour rather than their individual characteristics, experiences, or capabilities. The resource includes sections specifically targeted at families based on demographic assumptions, such as 'Not My Idea: A Book About Whiteness' described as 'inviting white children and parents to become curious about racism.' The approach embeds a framework where institutions guide families to understand their children primarily through demographic lenses rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualities, abilities, and perspectives.
incidents.expected_harm: This educational approach will shape how children understand themselves and their peers, teaching them to organize their social world around categories of skin colour and ancestry rather than individual character, abilities, and demonstrated qualities. Students will learn to view demographic categories as primary markers of identity, potentially limiting their capacity to recognize the inherent dignity and unique qualities of each person they encounter. Children taught to interpret experiences and interactions through demographic frameworks may develop reduced capacity to evaluate themselves and others as individuals, instead defaulting to categorical thinking that obscures the singular worth of each person. The institutional endorsement of materials that address children differently based on demographic categories communicates that such categorization is appropriate and necessary, potentially normalizing practices where people are evaluated through demographic proxies rather than individual merit, character, and capabilities. Students may internalize that their demographic categories carry predetermined significance for their experiences, perspectives, and relationships, limiting their autonomy to define themselves beyond such categorization. The framework discourages genuine diversity of thought by suggesting that demographic categories reliably correlate with particular perspectives or experiences, rather than recognizing that meaningful diversity emerges from each person's unique combination of experiences, capabilities, and viewpoints. This approach to education may impair students' capacity to engage in reasoned discourse about complex social issues by establishing demographic identity as a primary analytical framework rather than cultivating habits of evidence-based thinking, intellectual honesty, and engagement with multiple viewpoints on their individual merits.

quotes.text: This document is an introductory and sample list of resources intended to support parents/guardians to begin critical conversations around race and racism. The document presents a range of websites, novels and picture books for early years learners to secondary students. It is important that we talk about race and racism as it affects a wide range of identities and allow students to begin to understand the ways race and racism impacts all of our lives.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the explicit institutional purpose of organizing educational content around demographic categories and identities, demonstrating the school board's intention to guide families toward frameworks that center on categorizing people rather than evaluating individuals based on their unique qualities.

quotes.text: Kiera is a Black female honour-roll student, math tutor and game-developer of SLAY. The game is home to thousands of Black gamers. She finds herself stuck between protecting her online identity and being unapologetically Black after SLAY becomes the target of anti-Black racism following a murder resulting from a dispute on SLAY.
quotes.rationale: Shows the type of content being recommended that presents characters and narratives organized around demographic categories as central to identity and experience, reinforcing frameworks where people are understood primarily through such categories rather than their individual qualities, achievements, and character.

incidents.short_description: Peel District School Board provided anti-racism resources that included a book framing people based on their skin colour rather than as individuals with inherent dignity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Peel District School Board published a webpage offering resources to help families understand and discuss racism. Among the recommended materials was "Not My Idea: A Book About Whiteness" by Anastasia Higginbotham, described as a picture book about racism and racial justice. The board provided links to two versions of the book. The resource page categorized and labeled people by skin colour throughout its recommendations, presenting an approach that divides rather than unites Canadians based on observable individual qualities and shared experiences.
incidents.expected_harm: Students and families will be encouraged to view themselves and others primarily through the lens of demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique qualities and shared human experiences. This approach undermines the recognition of singular worth by reducing people to assumed characteristics based on skin colour. Children exposed to these materials will learn to categorize people by immutable traits rather than evaluate others based on character, abilities, and individual circumstances. The framing reinforces divisions within the school community rather than fostering the understanding that all people share common aspirations, vulnerabilities, and capacity for connection that transcends surface differences.

quotes.text: We humbly offer these resources and supports as a potential starting point to help support mental health and well-being, and to provide a deeper understanding of anti-Black racism
quotes.rationale: Establishes the institutional framing and purpose of the resource page, showing the board's approach to addressing racism through categorical thinking about groups rather than individuals

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Broadcasting Corporation
incidents.short_description: CBC required news producers to complete mandatory two-page race-ethnicity-gender-orientation profiles for every guest appearing on news and current affairs programs, categorizing individuals by demographic characteristics and directing producers to book more people of some races and fewer of others.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Broadcasting Corporation implemented a Guest Sourcing Survey system requiring producers to compile mandatory two-page racial and demographic profiles for every person appearing on CBC news and current affairs programs, including experts, panelists, commentators, and passersby in street interviews. Producers were required to categorize guests by race, ethnicity, gender, and sexual orientation. When this information was not obvious, producers were directed to search Google and other public sources rather than asking guests directly. The surveys required producers to note whether interviewees appeared biracial or multiracial, and for individuals identified as BIPOC, the corporation requested additional details such as whether they were African, Arab, Asian, First Nations, Inuit, or Métis. Former CBC producer Tara Henley reported in January 2022 that producers were directed to actively book more people of some races and fewer of others. According to an access to information request reported by Blacklock's Reporter, these surveys were mandatory for all appearances on the CBC main network and its all-news channel, requiring producers to complete scores of profiles weekly.
incidents.expected_harm: This system reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their expertise, experience, or individual perspectives. The practice of categorizing and selecting guests primarily based on immutable characteristics undermines the principle of recognizing each person's inherent worth as an individual. By directing producers to favor some races over others in booking decisions, the policy creates a framework where people are valued or devalued based on demographic characteristics rather than their unique qualities and contributions. The policy fragments discourse by prioritizing superficial demographic diversity over genuine diversity of thought and experience, potentially excluding individuals with valuable perspectives who do not fit preferred demographic profiles. The practice of researching guests' racial and ethnic backgrounds without their knowledge or consent violates individual privacy and autonomy.

quotes.text: If a particular guest's race, ethnicity or sexual orientation is not obvious, producers are not to ask the guest such personal information directly. Instead, they are directed to search Google or other public sources to find out what they can about a guest's gender and ethnicity.
quotes.rationale: Describes how producers investigate personal demographic information without consent, demonstrating privacy violations

incidents.short_description: The Archives Association of British Columbia offers a bursary for distance education courses that is restricted to applicants based on their skin colour and ethnicity. People who do not identify under these categories are ineligible to apply.
incidents.description_of_event: The Archives Association of British Columbia (AABC) created and administers an Anti-Racism Bursary that covers registration and textbook costs for one applicant per distance education course. The bursary is explicitly restricted to individuals who self-identify as indigenous, black and/or a person of colour. All other applicants are categorically excluded from eligibility regardless of their individual financial circumstances or professional development needs. The bursary was established following a June 2020 statement by the AABC Executive Committee and was active at least from the 2022–2023 course year through the 2026–2027 fiscal year. Applicants are required to confirm their racial or ethnic identity as the first eligibility question on the application form. The AABC frames this restriction as a response to financial barriers in the archival profession, but the financial support itself is allocated based on demographic identity rather than demonstrated individual need.
incidents.expected_harm: Individuals who do not identify under the designated racial and ethnic categories are excluded from financial assistance for professional education solely because of their skin colour or ethnicity, not because of their individual financial circumstances. This means that a person in genuine financial need who does not fit the approved demographic categories will be denied support that would otherwise be available to them. The programme institutionalises the practice of evaluating people based on demographic categories rather than their individual character, circumstances, or abilities. It normalises the idea that a professional association should distribute resources and opportunities based on immutable traits, and sends a message that some members of the archival community have worth and needs that are more deserving of recognition than others. This approach undermines shared civic belonging within the profession and entrenches division among aspiring archivists.

quotes.text: All candidates who identify as Indigenous, Black and/or a Person of Colour (IBPOC) are eligible to apply.
quotes.rationale: Directly states the eligibility restriction: only individuals who identify under designated racial and ethnic categories may apply for the bursary.

quotes.text: The purpose of the bursary is to eliminate the financial barrier for IBPOC individuals who want to join the archival profession.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that financial support is allocated on the basis of racial and ethnic identity rather than individual demonstrated financial need.

quotes.text: 1) Do you identify as Indigenous, Black and/or a Person of Colour (IBPOC)?
quotes.rationale: The application form's first eligibility question requires applicants to declare their racial or ethnic identity as a condition of consideration, confirming that demographic labelling is the gateway criterion.

quotes.text: The bursary covers registration and textbook costs for one Indigenous, Black and/or Person of Colour (IBPOC) per distance education course.
quotes.rationale: Screenshot evidence corroborates the restriction and confirms it has been in place since at least 2022.

quotes.text: In June 2020, the AABC Executive Committee presented a statement in response to the call for an end to racism, injustice, and violence faced by Indigenous, Black and/or People of Colour (IBPOC) communities in Canada and abroad.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the origin and framing of the bursary programme, showing it was designed around demographic categorisation from the outset.

incidents.short_description: The Simcoe County District School Board administered a school climate survey to grade 7 and 8 students that asked children to disclose their sexual orientation, gender identity, race, and ethnicity. The survey treats demographic labelling of minors as routine educational data collection.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Simcoe County District School Board administered its 2021–2022 School Climate Survey to grade 7 and 8 students. The survey, hosted on SurveyMonkey and accessible via a board email address (schoolclimate@scdsb.on.ca), asked students a series of questions that went well beyond assessing school safety and belonging.

In an 'About Me' section, students were asked to select their gender identity from a list that included numerous categories, and separately to select their sexual orientation from a list of options. Students in grades 7 and 8 are typically 12 to 14 years old. The survey also required students to identify their race from a prescribed list, their ethnic or cultural origin, and whether they identified as first nations, métis, or inuit.

Additional sections asked students to report whether their school had discussed specific identity-based topics, including named community groupings defined by sexual orientation, and to report awareness of a range of named social concepts. The survey also asked students whether their school had a Gender Student Alliance or GSA club.

While the survey included a note that students could skip uncomfortable questions and provided the Kids Help Phone number, there is no indication in the evidence of parental notification or consent prior to children being asked to disclose sexual orientation, gender identity, and detailed racial and ethnic background.
incidents.expected_harm: Children as young as 12 are required to categorize and label themselves by sexual orientation, gender identity, race, and ethnicity as a condition of participating in a school-sanctioned survey. This practice normalizes the institutional classification of children by demographic categories rather than treating them as individuals. Asking children to select a sexual orientation from a prescribed list introduces adult identity frameworks into the lives of young adolescents who are still developing their sense of self, and does so in a school context that carries implicit authority. Children who do not fit neatly into any listed category, or who have not yet thought about these questions, are placed in an uncomfortable position by a trusted institution. The collection of detailed racial and ethnic data from minors, without clear evidence of parental consent, raises serious privacy concerns. The overall approach conditions students to understand themselves and each other primarily through demographic labels, undermining the recognition of each child as a distinct individual whose worth is not defined by these categories.

quotes.text:
Please indicate the sexual orientation(s) you identify with:
Select all that apply.
Asexual Pansexual
Bisexual Queer
Fluid Questioning
Gay Two-Spirit
Heterosexual/Straight Skip this question.
Lesbian
quotes.rationale: Directly demonstrates that the survey asks grade 7 and 8 students, who are typically 12–14 years old, to formally select and disclose their sexual orientation from a prescribed institutional list.

quotes.text:
Please indicate your gender identity.
Select all that apply.
Boy/Male Transgender female
Girl/Female Transgender male
Fluid Two-spirit
Non-binary Skip this question.
Transgender
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that the survey asks young adolescents to select a gender identity from a list of institutionally defined categories, treating demographic labelling as routine.

quotes.text:
Which race category best describes you?
Select all that apply.
quotes.rationale: Shows the survey sorting children into racial categories, reinforcing the practice of classifying individuals by demographic proxies rather than treating them as individuals.

quotes.text: Do you identify as Afro-Indigenous, First Nations, Métis, and/or Inuit?
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the survey's collection of indigenous identity data from minors as part of routine demographic categorisation.

incidents.short_description: The Hamilton-Wentworth Catholic District School Board restricted eligibility for its Anti-Racism Permanent Committee to parents and students who identify as belonging to specific demographic groups, excluding others based on their background. This denies equal participation in a school board governance process on the basis of immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: The Hamilton-Wentworth Catholic District School Board (HWCDSB) posted an invitation for parents, guardians, and students to join its newly formed Anti-Racism Permanent Committee, established to oversee implementation of recommendations from the Anti-Racism Education Committee and to report to the Board of Trustees. The committee was intended to cover a one-year term from May 2022 to June 2023. However, eligibility to apply was explicitly restricted: only parents and guardians whose children "identify as Black, Indigenous, and/or racialized" and only students in grades 7–12 who similarly identify under those demographic categories were invited to participate. Three parents and three students were to be selected by lottery from this restricted pool. The board framed the initiative as part of its commitment to celebrating diversity and opposing racism, but the eligibility criteria meant that families and students outside those demographic categories were barred from participating in a formal school board governance committee, regardless of their individual interest, experience, or commitment to the committee's goals.
incidents.expected_harm: Families and students are evaluated for participation in a school board committee not on the basis of their individual character, knowledge, or commitment, but solely on the basis of their demographic background. This excludes a large portion of the school community from meaningful civic participation in a formal governance process. The practice normalizes the idea that school board decision-making on issues affecting all students should be reserved for participants sorted by demographic categories. It sends a message to excluded families and students that their perspectives and experiences are irrelevant to the committee's work, undermining shared civic belonging and the principle that every person deserves to be recognized as an individual. It also entrenches the institutional habit of categorizing students and families by immutable traits rather than treating them as distinct individuals with unique insights to contribute.

quotes.text: Parents/Guardians with children in Kindergarten to Grade 12 in the HWCDSB, and HWCDSB students in Grades 7-12 who identify as Black, Indigenous, and/or racialized are invited to indicate their interest in participating on the HWCDSB Anti-Racism Permanent Committee for the one-year term, May 2022 to June 2023.
quotes.rationale: This is the core eligibility restriction: participation in the committee is explicitly limited to parents and students who identify within specific demographic categories, excluding all others on the basis of their background.

incidents.short_description: Vancouver Status of Women launched a Community Action Grant program restricted to applicants based on their skin colour and ethnicity, excluding women and gender-diverse people who do not identify as indigenous, black, or racialized. This distributes public-facing grant funding on the basis of demographic categories rather than individual need or merit.
incidents.description_of_event: In April 2022, Vancouver Status of Women (VSW) launched its Community Action Grants program, offering funding of up to $5,000 for grassroots projects, small businesses, and community organizations in the Vancouver Lower Mainland. Applications opened April 1, 2022, with a deadline of June 30, 2022, and approximately $20,000 in total annual funding was made available. The grant guidelines state explicitly that only applications from self-identified indigenous, black, and racialized women, trans, or non-binary people would be considered. The promotional materials similarly described the grant as open only to indigenous, black, and racialized women and gender-diverse people. While the grant materials frame this restriction as targeting those who are underserved by mainstream grants and wealth distribution, the eligibility criteria as written restrict access based on applicants' skin colour and ethnicity rather than on assessed individual need, financial circumstances, or the merits of the proposed project. Other individuals — regardless of their economic situation, grassroots involvement, or the community value of their work — are categorically excluded from applying based on their skin colour and ethnic background.
incidents.expected_harm: Individuals are denied access to grant funding based on their skin colour and ethnic background rather than on their individual circumstances, financial need, or the quality of their proposed work. This practice entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories and deciding who deserves support based on immutable traits. It sends the message that a person's skin colour and ethnicity determine their eligibility for resources, normalizing the reduction of individuals to demographic proxies. Community members who do not identify within the specified categories are excluded from participating in grassroots funding opportunities regardless of their actual need or the value of their proposed initiatives. The approach undermines shared civic belonging by treating skin colour and ethnicity as the defining criteria for access to institutional support.

quotes.text: Only applications from self-identified Indigenous, Black, and racialized woman, trans, or non-binary will be considered.
quotes.rationale: This is the core eligibility restriction, explicitly limiting grant access based on skin colour and ethnicity rather than individual need or merit.
