incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for library positions are evaluated and prioritised based on their skin colour and ethnic background rather than their individual qualifications, abilities, and character. This approach institutionalises demographic categorisation in hiring decisions, reducing individuals to proxies for group identity and undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation on their own merits. Candidates whose background does not match the preferred demographic profile face disadvantage regardless of their skills or experience. The toolkit normalises the practice of sorting job applicants by immutable characteristics, entrenching division within the profession rather than fostering a workplace culture built on individual worth and shared civic belonging.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board published a five-year strategy that organizes school programs, hiring, parent engagement, and staff supports along racial lines, singling out students and staff based on their skin colour for distinct treatment across dozens of institutional actions.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Peel District School Board (PDSB) released its Black Student Success Strategy 2.0, a formal five-year plan (2022–2027) covering six focus areas and over thirty specific actions. Throughout the strategy, the board commits to organizing programs, services, resources, hiring, and community engagement explicitly according to the skin colour of students, parents, and staff.

The strategy establishes racially separate parent advisory groups (Action 4.2), direct communications channels targeted at parents on the basis of their skin colour (Action 4.4), and parent resources and engagement events restricted to families on the same basis (Actions 4.5 and 4.6). Student input is solicited only from students identified by skin colour (Action 4.7).

For students, the board commits to an annual leadership conference for students selected by skin colour (Action 5.2), to providing culturally specific mental health supports and dedicated gathering spaces for students on this basis (Action 5.5), and to developing programs exclusively for these students (Actions 5.6, 5.8, 5.9, 5.10).

In hiring and staffing, the board commits to prioritizing the hiring of teachers and staff based on their skin colour and supporting their advancement into leadership roles (Action 6.1). The board also commits to advocating with faculties of education to admit more students specifically on the basis of skin colour in order to produce more teachers of that background (Action 6.6). A racially segregated staff gathering space is established through Action 6.8, described as a safe space for teachers and staff of a particular skin colour to meet, share experiences, and heal.

The accountability framework tracks all measures of progress broken down by students' skin colour, institutionalizing the practice of evaluating individuals through a demographic lens across every area of school life.
incidents.expected_harm: Students, parents, and staff are evaluated, grouped, and served by their school board on the basis of their skin colour rather than their individual circumstances, abilities, or needs. This reduces people to a demographic category and denies them recognition as distinct individuals. Children learn from an early age that institutions divide people based on skin colour and that access to programs, conferences, support services, and engagement opportunities is determined by this characteristic. Parents are sorted into separate advisory and communication channels based on skin colour rather than being engaged as individuals with common interests in their children's education. Staff are hired and advanced based on skin colour, meaning individuals are judged on an immutable characteristic rather than their character and abilities. The segregated gathering space for staff of a particular skin colour normalizes racial separation within a public institution. Taken together, these actions entrench demographic categorization as the operating logic of the school board, undermine the shared civic community of the school, and foreclose opportunities for students, families, and staff to engage with one another as individuals with common humanity.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board adopted a five-year strategy that organizes students, staff, parents, and institutional resources primarily around skin colour, prioritizing hiring, programming, and engagement on the basis of this demographic category rather than individual need or merit.
incidents.description_of_event: In early 2022, the Peel District School Board (PDSB) published the 'We Rise Together 2.0: Black Student Success Strategy' (2022–2027), a comprehensive institutional policy document governing how the board approaches curriculum, hiring, community engagement, student support, and staff performance evaluation. The strategy organizes virtually every dimension of school life around the skin colour of students and staff. Its six focus areas commit the board to: integrating curriculum content based on students' skin colour; prioritizing the hiring of teachers and other staff based on skin colour; forming parent advisory groups organized by skin colour; running student leadership conferences, mentorship programs, and summer camps for students selected on the basis of skin colour; ensuring staff performance appraisals include goals tied to outcomes for students of a particular skin colour; and measuring institutional progress through data sets that track students and staff by skin colour across achievement, graduation, mental health, discipline, and hiring. The strategy explicitly commits the board to 'prioritizing the hiring of Black teachers and other staff' (action 6.1) and to advocating with faculties of education to admit more students based on skin colour (action 6.7). All superintendents are required to establish annual performance goals tied to the strategy, and these goals are embedded in individual position profiles and teacher appraisal processes. The board frames the entire education system as structurally biased and frames this strategy as the appropriate institutional response, treating demographic skin-colour categories as the primary lens through which students, families, and staff are seen and served.
incidents.expected_harm: Students and staff are evaluated, sorted, and served primarily as members of a skin-colour demographic group rather than as distinct individuals with unique abilities, needs, and circumstances. Students who do not belong to the designated skin-colour group are excluded from specific programs, leadership conferences, mentorship opportunities, and targeted academic supports, receiving a message from their school board that their demographic category determines what institutional resources and attention they deserve. Staff are hired and evaluated not solely on their abilities and demonstrated performance but also on their skin colour, undermining a system of advancement based on individual merit. Parents are organized into community engagement structures based on skin colour rather than shared concerns as parents, reinforcing division rather than common civic belonging. The institutional entrenchment of skin-colour categories in performance appraisals, hiring targets, curriculum mandates, and accountability frameworks normalizes the practice of sorting people by immutable traits, making it the default way the institution sees and responds to individuals. This forecloses the development of a school community united by shared educational goals and common human dignity.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Services and Procurement Canada
incidents.short_description: Public Services and Procurement Canada launched a Supplier Diversity Action Plan that directs federal contracts to businesses based on the demographic identities of their owners rather than on price, quality, and merit. The policy also mandated that five per cent of all federal contracting budgets be spent with businesses selected on the basis of their owners' background.
incidents.description_of_event: Public Services and Procurement Canada, which manages roughly $22 billion per year in federal contracts, developed a system that awards government contracts to businesses based on the demographic identity of their owners. In May 2021, the department introduced a "social procurement" policy directing that contracts be used to advance socio-economic objectives, including creating advantages for groups designated as underrepresented. In August 2021, the procurement minister mandated that all federal departments spend five per cent of their contracting budgets — approximately $1.1 billion annually — with businesses selected on the basis of their owners' backgrounds. In January 2022, the department launched a Supplier Diversity Action Plan to operationalise this approach. Under the plan, certain contracts are set aside exclusively for "diversity suppliers," defined as businesses where 51 per cent or more of ownership self-identifies as belonging to one of several groups the federal government designates as disadvantaged, including categories based on sex, skin colour, and sexual orientation. The government also changed the formal definition of "best value" in the Treasury Board Directive on the Management of Procurement, removing the previous standard — a combination of price, technical merit, and quality — and replacing it with a broader concept that incorporates socio-economic outcomes. The department indicated it would follow the action plan with a full Program on Social Procurement, and it stated plans to collect demographic data on contractors.
incidents.expected_harm: Businesses are evaluated and awarded government contracts based on the demographic identities of their owners rather than on the quality, price, or merit of their bids. This means that businesses owned by people who do not belong to designated identity categories are disadvantaged in competing for public contracts, regardless of the value they offer taxpayers. The policy redirects public spending — potentially over a billion dollars annually — away from the criterion of best value for Canadians and toward demographic sorting. It institutionalises the practice of categorising business owners by immutable traits and making those categories determinative of access to public resources. This entrenches a two-tiered system in which a person's identity, rather than their abilities and the quality of their work, determines their access to government opportunity.

quotes.text: The federal government's procurement department, which spends about $22 billion per year on contracts with private businesses, is spinning up a secondary system that awards contracts to companies based on the identities of its owners.
quotes.rationale: Core description of the policy: contracts awarded on the basis of owner identity rather than merit or value.

quotes.text: In August 2021, the procurement minister mandated that all federal departments and agencies spend five per cent of their contracting budgets on Indigenous businesses — that's about $1.1 billion, if total spending is $22 billion.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the scale of the identity-based set-aside and that ethnicity is one of the targeting bases.

quotes.text: Among other things, the new policy reveals that the government intends to collect contractor demographics. It also involves setting aside certain contracts for diversity suppliers, setting up mentorship programs for certain groups, and more consultation. The goal is to contract more with "social-purpose organizations" that have social, cultural or environmental missions, as well as businesses owned by women and minorities (sexual, gender, and racial). The goal of government contracting, in this way, is no longer to get the best value for Canadians.
quotes.rationale: Details the categories used to sort contractors and explicitly states that best value for Canadians is no longer the procurement objective.

quotes.text: How does one qualify to be a diversity supplier? You, or 51 per cent of your business's ownership, self-identify as one of the many groups the feds consider disadvantaged.
quotes.rationale: Explains the mechanism by which identity determines access to set-aside contracts.

incidents.short_description: George Brown College adopted an Anti-Racism Action Plan that organizes institutional practices, hiring, procurement, and student supports around demographic categories defined by skin colour and ethnicity. The plan embeds race-based classification throughout all college operations rather than addressing discrimination as a violation of individual dignity.
incidents.description_of_event: George Brown College published its Anti-Racism Action Plan covering the period 2022–2024. The plan commits the college to a sweeping transformation of its institutional practices, with nearly every department assigned specific actions organized around the demographic categorization of employees and students by skin colour and ethnicity. Key actions include: creating designated physical spaces exclusively for students and employees based on their skin colour or ethnicity (action 2.1); reviewing campus safety protocols specifically to protect people grouped by skin colour (action 2.12); establishing a subcommittee composed in part based on skin colour (action 1.8); requiring the college's security staff to undergo training focused on a doctrine of skin colour-based power and privilege (action 2.6); auditing textbooks to ensure representation of authors categorized by skin colour (action 4.9); building research mentorship programs limited to faculty based on their skin colour (action 4.10); revising the procurement policy so that vendor contracts favour businesses categorized by skin colour (action 5.17); reviewing admissions processes with the goal of adjusting enrolment by skin colour (action 5.14); and developing financial aid and awards targeted at students based on skin colour (action 5.16). All employees are required to complete mandatory anti-racism training, and individual anti-racism priorities must be incorporated into every employee's annual performance goals. Job postings for all faculty and staff positions are to include language about integrating anti-racism and decolonizing principles. The plan frames individuals throughout primarily in terms of their skin colour and ethnic identity, treating these demographic categories as the central lens for institutional decision-making across hiring, curriculum, research, student services, procurement, and governance.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution systematically sorts people into demographic categories based on skin colour and ethnicity, and then directs resources, opportunities, hiring, procurement, and physical spaces accordingly, it reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and individual circumstances. Students and employees learn that the institution views them first and foremost through the lens of their skin colour. Mandatory ideological training for all employees removes the freedom of individual conscience and frames the world through a lens of group-based power and racial identity rather than shared individual worth. Race-based preferences in hiring and procurement exclude individuals from opportunities based on immutable traits rather than merit. Segregated physical spaces signal that people should gather and belong in groups defined by skin colour, undermining the shared civic community of the college. Targeting admissions review and financial awards by skin colour means individuals are advantaged or disadvantaged based on demographics rather than their own demonstrated needs or abilities. Taken together, the plan normalizes institutional classification of every person by their skin colour as a routine administrative practice, entrenching division rather than building common understanding across a diverse community.

quotes.text: Explore opportunities to create designated spaces and opportunities at George Brown for Black, Indigenous and other racialized students and employees to gather, host events, celebrate and share traditions and achievements to foster belonging.
quotes.rationale: Documents the creation of segregated spaces on campus organized by skin colour and ethnicity, directing people into separate gatherings based on demographic categories rather than shared community.

quotes.text: Reassess existing campus security and safety infrastructure and protocols with a view to protecting the safety of Black, Indigenous, and racialized employees, students, and community members on campus.
quotes.rationale: Frames campus security as a service differentiated by skin colour and ethnicity, treating safety as a resource to be allocated based on demographic identity rather than the individual needs of every person on campus.

quotes.text: Review and revise the procurement policy to ensure that GBC's vendor contracts are equitably distributed, with criteria to promote and sustain purchasing/contracting processes that support the advancement of Black, Indigenous, and racialized businesses.
quotes.rationale: Establishes procurement criteria based on the skin colour and ethnicity of business owners, directing public institutional spending based on demographic classification rather than merit or individual circumstances.

quotes.text: Review fundraising activities and develop a plan to increase need-based financial aid and awards targeted at students from Black, Indigenous, and racialized communities.
quotes.rationale: Directs financial aid toward students based on skin colour and ethnicity, contradicting the stated 'need-based' framing by adding demographic restrictions. Awards are allocated through demographic category rather than individual need or merit.

quotes.text: Identify and implement specific supports and development opportunities/programs for Black, Indigenous and racialized part-time faculty and support staff.
quotes.rationale: Creates career development opportunities restricted by skin colour and ethnicity, meaning some employees are excluded from opportunities based on their demographic classification.

quotes.text: Audit textbooks and other educational materials to ensure diverse representation of authors and content that reflect historical facts of Black, Indigenous and other racialized peoples and highlight material of Black, Indigenous, and racialized authors to build greater awareness.
quotes.rationale: Directs curriculum selection based on the skin colour and ethnicity of authors rather than the quality or relevance of educational materials, embedding demographic classification into the academic programme.

quotes.text: Identify opportunities to build concrete research support practices (including mentorship and sponsorship) to encourage Black, Indigenous and racialized faculty to become involved in research projects.
quotes.rationale: Restricts research mentorship and sponsorship to faculty based on their skin colour and ethnicity, excluding others from the same institutional support based on immutable traits.

incidents.short_description: Ontario Superior Court Justice Faisal Mirza has systematically applied considerations based on individuals' skin colour and ethnicity in criminal sentencing and evidentiary rulings, reducing sentences for violent offenders and excluding evidence in firearms cases based on demographic categorization rather than individual circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: Justice Faisal Mirza, appointed to the Ontario Superior Court of Justice in 2022, has built a career advocating for and implementing considerations based on individuals' skin colour and ethnicity throughout the criminal justice system. Beginning in 2001 as a law student, Mirza argued that mandatory minimum sentences would have disparate impacts based on demographic categories. As a lawyer, he successfully intervened in multiple cases to establish frameworks where courts consider individuals' demographic characteristics in sentencing and evidentiary decisions. In the 2018 case R. v. Morris, Mirza filed reports categorizing the accused and broader populations, leading to a 50% sentence reduction. This approach became Ontario's standard in 2021. Mirza co-founded a non-profit in 2020 that produced pre-sentence reports emphasizing demographic characteristics, receiving $780,000 in federal funding. As a judge, Mirza has applied these principles in multiple cases: reducing a child sexual abuser's sentence from 4.5-5.5 years to 3 years after considering demographic factors; reducing a human trafficker's sentence by one year based on the offender's demographic background; and excluding all evidence including loaded firearms in cases where he determined police actions were motivated by the accused's skin colour rather than suspicious circumstances. In one case, he excluded evidence of a loaded gun found after police detained occupants of a gang-flagged vehicle at 3 a.m. In another, he excluded evidence of a loaded handgun that fell from a robbery suspect's waistband, reasoning that the officer's actions were based on the suspect's skin colour rather than matching the victim's description and circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will undermine public safety by releasing violent offenders with reduced sentences and excluding critical evidence in serious criminal cases based on demographic categorization rather than individual circumstances. The framework treats individuals as representatives of demographic groups rather than as distinct persons whose actions and character should be evaluated individually. It institutionalizes a system where criminal accountability varies according to immutable characteristics, violating the principle that justice should be blind to such factors. The exclusion of evidence in firearms cases based on assumptions about police motivation rather than actual circumstances will allow dangerous individuals to evade accountability. Victims of crime, including the Indigenous woman trafficked by the offender who received a reduced sentence, are denied justice when their harm is deemed less significant due to the offender's demographic characteristics. The approach also insults the dignity of individuals from the relevant demographic groups by suggesting they bear diminished responsibility for their choices and actions.

quotes.text: In 2018, Mirza laid the foundation for Ontario's racial sentencing regime. He was the defence lawyer of Kevin Morris, a Black man who was convicted of various firearms offences...Mirza filed two racial context reports about Morris and Black people as evidence, and the judge emphatically agreed to consider them. He settled on a 15-month sentence to account for the racial factors, even though three years was considered the starting point.
quotes.rationale: Documents how Mirza successfully advocated for sentencing reductions based on demographic categorization rather than individual circumstances, achieving a 50% reduction from the standard sentence.

incidents.short_description: Peel Regional Police announced a pilot project to collect race-based data during traffic stops, with officers recording their perception of individuals' race during interactions. The program will expand force-wide after initial testing.
incidents.description_of_event: In January 2022, Peel Regional Police launched a pilot project requiring officers to collect race-based data during traffic stops, with plans to expand the program across the entire police force. Officers record their perception of each person's race during interactions, along with age, gender, reason for stop, and outcome. The initiative was developed in collaboration with York University researchers and the Ontario Human Rights Commission as part of a multi-year systemic racism project. The traffic unit pilot was designed to run for several months before scaling up to all police units. Officers have discretion to explain the project to stopped individuals. Deputy Chief Anthony Odoardi stated that an officer's perception of race, though acknowledged as imperfect, serves as a data point that can be enhanced through roadside discussion with the person stopped.
incidents.expected_harm: This policy reduces individuals to demographic categories during police interactions, treating race as a salient characteristic to be officially recorded and analyzed rather than evaluating each person based on their individual circumstances and behaviour. The practice institutionalizes racial categorization in law enforcement operations, with officers making subjective determinations about people's racial identity that become part of official police records. The approach assumes that demographic patterns, rather than individual actions and character, should drive police training and policy development. By focusing institutional attention on perceived racial identity during routine encounters, the policy reinforces rather than transcends the reduction of individuals to group membership, contradicting the principle that people should be assessed based on their unique qualities and demonstrated conduct.

quotes.text: Ayear after committing to clean up systemic racism and discrimination across the service, Peel Regional Police are preparing to launch a pilot project that will see officers collect race-based data during traffic stops.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core policy and its connection to addressing systemic racism, showing this is an institutional initiative.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
incidents.short_description: The federal government established a mental health fund exclusively for public servants of a particular skin colour, restricting access to support based on a demographic characteristic rather than individual need.
incidents.description_of_event: In a mandate letter dated December 16, 2021, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau directed Treasury Board President Mona Fortier to establish a mental health fund restricted to federal public servants based on their skin colour. The government budgeted $3.7 million over four years for the program. The initiative arose partly in response to a proposed class-action lawsuit filed in 2020 alleging that tens of thousands of public servants had experienced discrimination, and partly from a motion filed within that lawsuit calling for a $100-million mental health fund for current and former employees of a specific demographic group. A working group was established to develop a mental health action plan, but the process became contentious. The Federal Black Employee Caucus subsequently sent a letter to Treasury Board's chief human resources officer accusing government representatives of bad-faith negotiating and discriminatory conduct during the working group process, and the caucus indicated it was considering withdrawing from the process entirely. The Treasury Board stated it remained committed to establishing the program. At its core, the program was designed from the outset to deliver mental health supports based on the skin colour of public servants, not on their individual circumstances or demonstrated needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Public servants who do not belong to the designated demographic group are excluded from accessing a government-funded mental health resource based solely on their skin colour, not their individual circumstances or needs. This creates a two-tiered system in which the government treats employees differently based on an immutable characteristic rather than evaluating each person's unique situation. The practice normalises the sorting of public servants into demographic categories, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their individual merits and needs. It sends a message that access to government-provided wellbeing supports is contingent on one's skin colour, eroding the shared civic identity that should unite all public servants regardless of background.

incidents.short_description: York University posted a tenure-track faculty position requiring applicants to have 'experiential knowledges and demonstrable engagements' with specific communities defined by ancestry, limiting the candidate pool based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating individuals on merit.
incidents.description_of_event: York University's School of Gender, Sexuality and Women's Studies advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Black Feminist & Queer Studies with a start date of July 1, 2022. The job posting, which opened December 15, 2021 and closed February 15, 2022, stated that applicants must have 'experiential knowledges and demonstrable engagements in their teaching and research with Black Canadian or African Canadian or African communities.' This requirement appears to condition employment eligibility on belonging to or having specific connections with communities defined by ancestry or demographic category, rather than evaluating candidates as individuals based on their scholarly work, research capabilities, teaching excellence, and demonstrated contributions to the field. While the position seeks expertise in Black Feminist and Queer Studies—a legitimate academic specialization—the mandatory 'experiential knowledges' requirement suggests that scholarly expertise alone is insufficient and that candidates must possess particular lived experiences tied to demographic identity.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring requirement will exclude qualified scholars who have developed expertise in Black Feminist and Queer Studies through rigorous academic study, research, and intellectual engagement but who do not possess the specified demographic connections. It reduces individuals to assumptions about their background and lived experience rather than evaluating them on their individual scholarly merit, research contributions, teaching effectiveness, and capacity to advance knowledge in the field. The requirement assumes that certain forms of knowledge can only be accessed through particular demographic identities rather than through intellectual inquiry, study, and engagement with scholarship. This approach undermines the principle that academic positions should be awarded based on demonstrated individual capability and scholarly achievement. It establishes a precedent where employment in certain academic fields becomes conditional on demographic characteristics, effectively creating separate standards for evaluation that contradict the principle of singular worth. Furthermore, it may discourage talented scholars from pursuing research in particular areas if they believe their demographic background will disqualify them regardless of their scholarly contributions.

quotes.text: York University is an Affirmative Action (AA) employer and strongly values diversity, including gender and sexual diversity, within its community. The AA Program, which applies to women, members of visible minorities (racialized groups), Aboriginal (Indigenous) people and persons with disabilities
quotes.rationale: Provides context about the university's stated commitment to affirmative action policies that categorize people by demographic characteristics, which may relate to the approach taken in this posting.

incidents.short_description: The Winnipeg School Division unanimously voted to establish Manitoba's first education equity office to oversee anti-racism initiatives and collect diversity demographic data across its 78 schools.
incidents.description_of_event: The Winnipeg School Division trustees unanimously voted to create the province's first education equity office. This new branch will oversee anti-racism initiatives and collect data on diversity demographics across the division's 78 schools, which serve nearly 170 student ethnicities and almost 7,000 immigrant and refugee learners. The office represents a new institutional structure focused on categorizing students by demographic characteristics rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique needs and capabilities.
incidents.expected_harm: The collection and tracking of diversity demographics will categorize students based on demographic groups rather than their individual qualities, needs, and achievements. Anti-racism initiatives implemented through this lens will treat students as representatives of demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique experiences and perspectives. This approach will reduce students to demographic proxies, undermining evaluation based on individual character, abilities, and circumstances. The institutional focus on demographic data collection will embed systems that assess students through the lens of group membership rather than their demonstrated individual potential and contributions.

quotes.text: Winnipeg School Division trustees unanimously voted to establish a first-of-its-kind branch that will oversee anti-racism initiatives and collect data on diversity demographics in its 78 schools.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core action taken by the institution: creation of an equity office with specific functions related to anti-racism and demographic data collection.

incidents.short_description: Ontario Bill 67 proposes mandatory anti-racism training, competency assessments, and racial equity plans across education institutions, requiring evaluation of teachers and education professionals based on racial equity awareness rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Bill 67, titled the Racial Equity in the Education System Act, 2021, was introduced as a Private Member's Bill in Ontario's Legislative Assembly on December 2, 2021. The bill amends multiple education-related statutes to mandate anti-racism policies, training, and accountability mechanisms throughout Ontario's education system from K-12 through post-secondary institutions. Key provisions include: requiring performance appraisals of teachers to assess anti-racism awareness and efforts to promote racial equity; mandating that boards establish racial equity plans; requiring new teachers to complete anti-racism examinations and training as a condition of certification; establishing fines for racist language or activities that disrupt schools; directing the Minister to establish policies on promoting racial equity in schools; requiring colleges and universities to develop anti-racism policies and collect data on racism incidents; and mandating that education governance boards include members with racial equity expertise or requiring existing members to undergo anti-racism training. The bill defines racism as the use of socially constructed ideas of race to justify superiority of one race over another, and defines anti-racism as opposing various forms of racism including anti-Indigenous, anti-Black, anti-Asian racism, antisemitism, and Islamophobia. The legislation creates comprehensive reporting and accountability structures requiring institutions to track, measure, and respond to incidents of racism, and to provide regular reports to the Minister and public.
incidents.expected_harm: This legislation will result in educators and education professionals being evaluated and advanced based on their adherence to prescribed ideological positions on race rather than their individual competencies, character, and teaching abilities. Teachers will face mandatory assessment of their racial equity awareness as part of performance appraisals, creating a system where career progression depends on demonstrating commitment to specific racial categorizations and frameworks. The requirement that all education governance board members either demonstrate expertise in racial equity or undergo mandatory anti-racism training imposes ideological prerequisites for participation in educational governance, excluding individuals based on their perspectives rather than their qualifications. By mandating that institutions establish protocols for tracking and responding to racism as defined through socially constructed racial categories, the bill entrenches systems that evaluate students, teachers, and staff through demographic lenses rather than as individuals. The requirement for new teachers to pass anti-racism examinations as a condition of certification creates an ideological barrier to entry into the teaching profession. Students will be subjected to educational environments where racial categorization is institutionalized through mandatory equity plans, potentially experiencing differential treatment based on perceived racial identity rather than individual needs and circumstances. The legislation's emphasis on collecting demographic data and tracking incidents by racial category reinforces rather than transcends racial divisions. Faculty and staff who question these frameworks face professional consequences through performance evaluation systems and mandatory training requirements. The bill's approach to addressing inequity through demographic targeting and mandatory ideological training undermines the recognition of individual dignity and merit-based evaluation that should characterize educational institutions.

quotes.text: At least one member of the Council shall be a person who has expertise in racial equity in the post-secondary education sector.
quotes.rationale: Creates representation requirements based on ideological expertise rather than allowing boards to select members based on individual qualifications and diverse perspectives.

incidents.short_description: The Edmonton Public School Board passed a motion to embed anti-racism into the kindergarten-to-grade 6 curriculum, adopting an approach that acknowledges privilege, challenges beliefs, and promotes institutional action against racism.
incidents.description_of_event: On November 30, 2021, the Edmonton Public School Board of Trustees passed a motion put forward by Trustee Julie Kusiek to embed anti-racism within the K-6 curriculum. The motion was documented in the December 14, 2021 consolidated board meeting package. According to the board's definition, anti-racism means direct action to acknowledge where privilege exists, raise awareness, advocate for change, and challenge beliefs such as prejudice, bias and stereotypes at the personal and institutional level. The board's policy states that racism can be perpetuated at individual, institutional and systemic levels and is rooted in historical oppression, and that proactive action is required to create anti-racist environments. The motion was part of a broader advocacy position that also included calls for environmental and climate change literacy to be embedded in the K-6 curriculum and delays to implementing Alberta's proposed curriculum changes.
incidents.expected_harm: This policy will expose young children to ideological concepts that reduce individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their character and abilities. The anti-racism framework adopted by the board is predicated on the assertion that societies are systemically racist and that certain groups possess inherent privileges based on demographic characteristics. By introducing such concepts to children as young as kindergarten, the policy may undermine the development of genuine understanding across differences and instead foster division by emphasizing group identity over individual worth. Students will be taught to view themselves and others through the lens of demographic categories, potentially limiting their capacity to engage with each other as unique individuals with singular worth. The framework's emphasis on collective guilt and privilege based on ancestry contradicts the principle that each person deserves recognition for their unique qualities rather than assumed characteristics derived from demographic categories.

incidents.institution_name: The Association fo Canadian Mountain Guides
incidents.short_description: The Association of Canadian Mountain Guides adopted a 102-page accountability and learning plan that frames mountain culture through demographic categories and seeks to address 'European ethno-centrism, masculinity & heteronormativity' in mountain guiding.
incidents.description_of_event: The Association of Canadian Mountain Guides developed and implemented a comprehensive accountability framework titled 'Accountability, Learning & Support for a Diverse and Inclusive Professional Mountain Culture.' The 102-page document establishes a Safe Reporting Process for code of conduct complaints based on restorative justice principles and a trauma-informed approach. The framework explicitly identifies 'European ethno-centrism, masculinity & heteronormativity' as problems to be addressed in mountain culture. The process includes detailed investigative procedures with flowcharts for handling complaints, reparative interviews, and restorative justice facilitation. The document was developed by Lotus Mountain Consulting Inc. and represents a shift in how the professional association addresses psychological and emotional safety alongside physical safety in mountain guiding.
incidents.expected_harm: By framing mountain culture through demographic lenses and identifying characteristics associated with demographic groups as problematic, this framework will reduce guides to their demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals. The explicit targeting of 'European ethno-centrism, masculinity & heteronormativity' categorizes people based on ancestry, sex, and sexual orientation, creating an environment where guides may face scrutiny based on these characteristics rather than their professional conduct, skills, or character. This approach will undermine the principle of singular worth by treating demographic attributes as indicators of problematic behaviour, potentially subjecting guides to complaint processes based on perceived group membership rather than individual actions. The framework's emphasis on demographic categories in defining professional culture will discourage open dialogue and reason-based discourse about guiding practices, as discussions become filtered through identity frameworks rather than merit, safety standards, and individual capabilities.

incidents.short_description: Professor Patanjali Kambhampati was denied two federal research grants after stating in his applications that he would hire research assistants based on merit and qualifications rather than demographic criteria. Both NSERC and the National Frontiers in Research Fund rejected his applications on grounds of insufficient equity, diversity, and inclusion considerations.
incidents.description_of_event: Patanjali Kambhampati, a chemistry professor at McGill University conducting research on ultrafast laser science, applied for federal research grants from the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) and the National Frontiers in Research Fund. In the equity, diversity, and inclusion sections of his applications, he wrote that he would hire the most qualified people based on their skills and mutual interests, treating all candidates as individuals regardless of demographic characteristics. NSERC rejected his $450,000 grant application in November 2021, stating that the equity, diversity and inclusion considerations were deemed insufficient and that the training plan did not adequately describe specific, concrete practices to ensure EDI was intentionally and proactively supported. His earlier application to the National Frontiers in Research Fund was rejected on similar grounds. Both applications were rejected at the administrative level before reaching scientific peer review. Professor Kambhampati publicly stated that he believes in merit-based hiring and treating people as individuals, and that his approach reflects his commitment to fairness and equality. He reported having mentored a diverse group of students throughout his career based on their individual abilities and interests rather than demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: The grant rejection criteria establish a precedent where researchers must commit to demographic-based hiring practices rather than individual merit-based evaluation to receive federal funding. This creates a system where scientists are compelled to adopt hiring criteria that evaluate candidates based on group membership rather than individual qualifications, skills, and demonstrated abilities. Researchers who believe in treating each person as an individual and evaluating them on their unique merits face exclusion from government funding opportunities. This will discourage merit-based evaluation in academic hiring, reduce intellectual diversity as researchers self-censor to conform to required statements, and undermine scientific innovation by prioritizing demographic considerations over research excellence and individual capability. The requirement effectively mandates that researchers adopt specific ideological positions about hiring practices as a condition of receiving public research funding, regardless of their demonstrated track record of mentoring diverse individuals based on their unique abilities.

quotes.text: The Equity, Diversity and Inclusion considerations in the application were deemed insufficient. The training plan did not adequately describe specific, concrete practices that will be put in place to ensure that EDI is intentionally and proactively supported in the training plan. Vague or generic statements are not sufficient and therefore the application is being rejected.
quotes.rationale: This is the official rejection notice from NSERC explaining why the application was denied. It demonstrates that the grant was rejected specifically because the professor would not commit to demographic-based practices in his hiring approach.

incidents.short_description: Dalhousie University restricted applications for an Assistant Professor position in Experimental Chemistry to candidates who self-identify as indigenous persons, persons with a disability, racially visible persons, women, and persons of a minority sexual orientation and/or gender identity, with Canadians and permanent residents given priority.
incidents.description_of_event: Dalhousie University advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor position in Experimental Chemistry with a posting date of November 22, 2021, and an anticipated start date of July 2022. The position was explicitly restricted to candidates who self-identify in one or more designated equity-seeking groups: indigenous persons, persons with a disability, racially visible persons, women, and persons of a minority sexual orientation and/or gender identity. The university stated this restriction was implemented in keeping with employment equity principles, though Canadians and permanent residents would be given priority. The job posting directed candidates to the university's diversity and inclusiveness policy and required applications through PeopleAdmin including cover letter, curriculum vitae, detailed research proposal, teaching statement, and references.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction will prevent qualified candidates from being considered for employment based solely on demographic characteristics rather than individual merit, abilities, and qualifications. Candidates who do not self-identify within the specified demographic categories will be categorically excluded from consideration regardless of their unique perspectives, research capabilities, teaching excellence, or potential contributions to intellectual diversity. The policy reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating each person's character, demonstrated achievements, varied life experiences, and capacity to contribute to the institution's academic mission. This approach undermines the principle that advancement should reflect merit and that institutions should assess people based on their individual qualities rather than assumed characteristics derived from demographic categories.

quotes.text: In keeping with the principles of employment equity, this position is restricted to candidates who self-identify in one or more of the following groups: Indigenous persons, persons with a disability, racially visible persons, women, and persons of a minority sexual orientation and/or gender identity.
quotes.rationale: This is the core policy statement that explicitly restricts the position based on demographic categories rather than individual merit or qualifications. It directly demonstrates how the institution is reducing candidates to group membership rather than evaluating them as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The City of Toronto posted a job opening for Director Equity & Human Rights stating that while all qualified applicants will be considered, lived experience as an Indigenous person or as a person from specific demographic categories is a strong asset. This hiring preference reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their unique qualifications and capabilities.
incidents.description_of_event: On November 22, 2021, the City of Toronto posted a job opening for Director Equity & Human Rights, a senior leadership position responsible for developing equity frameworks and guiding workforce initiatives. The posting stated that while all qualified applicants would be considered, lived experience as an Indigenous person or as a person who is from specific demographic categories is a strong asset for the position. This language establishes a hiring preference based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating candidates solely on their individual qualifications, professional experience, leadership abilities, and demonstrated commitment to the role's objectives. The position involves leading equity and human rights programs, developing frameworks for equitable outcomes, and guiding teams across the organization. By explicitly privileging certain demographic backgrounds as assets, the posting suggests that membership in particular groups provides inherent advantages for performing the role's duties, rather than assessing each candidate's unique perspective, experience, and capacity to lead organizational change.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring preference will result in qualified candidates being disadvantaged based on their demographic characteristics rather than their individual merit and capabilities. Candidates who do not belong to the specified demographic categories may be passed over despite possessing superior qualifications, relevant professional experience, and demonstrated commitment to equity and human rights principles. The approach reduces complex individuals to demographic proxies, failing to recognize that people's capacity to advance equity, foster inclusion, and lead organizational change stems from their individual character, values, experiences, and demonstrated abilities rather than from assumed characteristics associated with demographic categories. This practice undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their unique qualities and proven capabilities. It also reinforces divisive categorization by suggesting that demographic background rather than individual perspective and competence determines suitability for leadership roles focused on advancing equity and inclusion.

quotes.text: While all qualified applicants will be considered, lived experience as an Indigenous person or as a Black, racialised, LGBTQ2S person or as a person with a disability or a newcomer is a strong asset.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly establishes a hiring preference based on demographic categories, treating membership in specific groups as a strong asset for the position rather than evaluating candidates based on their individual qualifications and demonstrated capabilities.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board's Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement organized a webinar on COVID-19 vaccines framed exclusively for families based on their skin colour, dividing access to public health information along demographic lines.
incidents.description_of_event: On November 16, 2021, the Toronto District School Board's Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement, in collaboration with the Black Physicians' Association of Ontario, the Black Scientists' Taskforce on Vaccine Equity, and the Black Health Vaccine Initiative, hosted a webinar titled 'Understanding COVID-19 Vaccine: Making Evidence-Based Choices for Black Children and Youth.' The event was promoted on social media and through an Eventbrite listing as a forum to share information about the COVID-19 infection and vaccine specifically with families based on their skin colour. The promotional materials, including a tweet posted on November 8, 2021, explicitly framed the event as being for families of children identified by skin colour. The webinar featured four health professionals discussing vaccines and children's health. While the Eventbrite listing noted that 'parents, caregivers, students, community members and staff are cordially invited,' the entire framing, branding, and promotion of the event organized and targeted participation based on demographic categories rather than presenting COVID-19 vaccine information as a shared public health concern relevant to all families.
incidents.expected_harm: Organizing and promoting public health information sessions along demographic lines normalizes the institutional practice of sorting families and children into categories based on their skin colour, rather than treating them as individuals with equal access to important health information. This approach sends a message to all families that institutions divide people by demographic characteristics when addressing shared health challenges, undermining the principle that all children and families face common concerns and deserve to be addressed as individuals. It forecloses opportunities to build shared understanding around universal health challenges and reinforces division at the institutional level, with the school board lending its name and resources to an initiative that frames health information as belonging to particular groups rather than to the whole community.

quotes.text: On Nov. 16, join us for a panel discussion on Black children's health & education and the COVID-19 infection. A collaboration with Black Physicians' Association of Ontario, Black Scientists' Task Force & @tdsb.
quotes.rationale: The tweet from the TDSB-affiliated account promotes a public health panel discussion framed exclusively around children's skin colour, demonstrating how the institution organized and communicated about a health event using demographic categories.

quotes.text: The Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement in collaboration with Black Physicians' Association of Ontario, the Black Scientists' Taskforce on Vaccine Equity and the Black Health Vaccine Initiative is hosting a webinar to share information with Black families, caregivers and staff about the COVID-19 infection and vaccine.
quotes.rationale: The event description explicitly frames COVID-19 vaccine information as being directed at families identified by skin colour, rather than as universal public health information available to all families.

quotes.text: UNDERSTANDING COVID-19 VACCINE: Making Evidence-Based Choices for Black Children and Youth
quotes.rationale: The event title itself signals that the health information and decision-making support is being organized around the skin colour of children, rather than being presented as relevant to all children and families.

incidents.institution_name: University of Waterloo > School Of Computer Science
incidents.short_description: University of Waterloo's David R. Cheriton School of Computer Science advertised two Canada Research Chair positions restricted exclusively to applicants who self-identify as women, transgender, non-binary, or two-spirit, excluding other candidates based on sex and gender identity.
incidents.description_of_event: The David R. Cheriton School of Computer Science at the University of Waterloo posted a job advertisement on November 12, 2021, for two tenured faculty positions at the rank of Full Professor or Associate Professor, each eligible for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair. The posting explicitly stated that the call was "open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women, transgender, non-binary, or two-spirit." The advertisement required all applicants to complete a self-identification survey, and indicated that self-identification information would be used for screening and consideration purposes. The positions offered salaries ranging from $170,000 to $250,000, with an expected start date of July 1, 2022. The posting justified this restriction by citing the underrepresentation of individuals from equity-deserving groups among Canada Research Chairs and invoked provisions for a special program under the Ontario Human Rights Code. While the posting encouraged applications from various historically disadvantaged groups in general terms, it restricted this particular selection process to only those who self-identified within specific sex and gender identity categories.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction will deny employment opportunities to qualified scholars based solely on their sex and gender identity rather than their individual merit, research accomplishments, and potential contributions. Candidates who do not self-identify as women, transgender, non-binary, or two-spirit will be categorically excluded from consideration regardless of their qualifications, research excellence, or demonstrated abilities. This approach reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their unique qualities, experiences, and achievements. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should assess people as individuals, and it creates a system where advancement depends on group membership rather than individual merit. By making sex and gender identity the primary screening criterion, the institution signals that demographic characteristics matter more than individual capabilities, potentially discouraging talented researchers who are excluded from applying to the institution in the future. This will diminish the genuine diversity of thought and experience that emerges when individuals are evaluated on their varied perspectives, problem-solving approaches, and unique contributions to their fields.

quotes.text: This call is open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women, transgender, non-binary, or two-spirit.
quotes.rationale: This statement establishes the explicit hiring restriction based on sex and gender identity, making demographic category the primary criterion for eligibility rather than individual qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Université Laval posted a Canada Research Chair position in Canadian-Quebec history that explicitly excluded applicants based on their skin colour and sex. A historian filed human rights complaints after being disqualified from applying solely on those grounds.
incidents.description_of_event:
Université Laval's Department of Historical Sciences advertised a Canada Research Chair (Level 2) position in Canadian-Quebec history, with a deadline of November 8, 2021. The posting stated that only candidates who had self-identified as a member of at least one of four designated groups — women, indigenous people, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities — would be selected. The university stated it could not consider other applicants until its representation targets were met, as required by the federal Canada Research Chairs program. Candidates were also required to complete a self-identification form disclosing their gender, sexual orientation, indigenous identity, visible minority status, and disability status; failure to submit this form disqualified a candidate from consideration entirely.

Frédéric Bastien, a historian and professor at Dawson College in Montreal with a doctorate in history and multiple publications, saw the posting and determined he was ineligible to apply because he did not belong to any of the designated groups. On October 31, 2022, he filed complaints against Université Laval and the Canada Research Chairs program with both the Quebec and Canadian human rights commissions. He also indicated he was pursuing a separate complaint regarding the mandatory self-identification questionnaire as a violation of personal privacy. The practice of excluding applicants on these grounds was not unique to Laval; several other Canadian universities had posted similar restrictions for federally funded research chair positions. The federal diversity targets were set following a 2019 Federal Court ruling and are linked to national population representation goals.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are categorically excluded from competing for prestigious, federally funded academic positions based on their skin colour, sex, and other demographic characteristics rather than their individual abilities, qualifications, or research merit. This denies individuals the opportunity to even be considered for roles they are otherwise competent to fill, reducing them to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct persons. The mandatory self-identification questionnaire requires applicants to disclose intimate personal information — including sexual orientation — as a condition of employment consideration, violating the privacy of every person who applies. Normalising hiring restrictions of this kind in academic institutions signals that demographic sorting is an acceptable basis for evaluating people, undermining the principle that individuals deserve to be judged on their character, abilities, and demonstrated contributions. It also entrenches division within academic communities by institutionalising the idea that certain demographic groups are inherently more deserving of opportunity than others, regardless of individual merit.

quotes.text: seules les personnes candidates possédant les compétences requises ET s'étant auto-identifiées comme membre d'au moins un de ces quatre groupes sous-représentés (femmes, Autochtones, personnes en situation de handicap et personne appartenant aux minorités visibles) seront sélectionnées au terme du présent concours. L'Université Laval ne peut déposer d'autres types de profils de candidatures tant que ses cibles de représentation ne sont pas atteintes.
quotes.rationale: The actual job posting text establishing that only candidates self-identifying in one of four designated demographic groups would be selected, and that all other applicants were excluded until representation targets were met. This is the core restriction at issue.

quotes.text: Si vous n'êtes pas soumettant le questionnaire avec votre résumé, ils ne considéreront pas votre candidature. Je travaille maintenant avec des avocats pour déposer une autre plainte sur la question de violation de la vie privée.
quotes.rationale: Documents that submission of a demographic self-identification questionnaire was a mandatory condition for any candidate to be considered, raising privacy concerns about compelled disclosure of personal characteristics.

incidents.institution_name: Queen’s University > Department of Civil Engineering
incidents.short_description: Queen's University's Department of Civil Engineering restricted a tenure-track faculty position and Canada Research Chair nomination to candidates who self-identify as women, excluding men from consideration regardless of their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Queen's University posted a tenure-track faculty position at the rank of Associate Professor or Professor in Civil Engineering for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair. The posting explicitly stated that the appointment was "open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women." This restriction applied to a seven-year renewable term position with significant research funding and institutional prestige. The university justified this policy by citing its Canada Research Chairs Program equity targets and its employment equity program. While the posting indicated that preference would be given to members of designated groups until equity targets were met, the restriction to women only was absolute for this particular position. Men, regardless of their research excellence, qualifications, or individual circumstances, were categorically excluded from applying for or being considered for this position.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction reduces candidates to a demographic category rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their unique qualifications, research accomplishments, and potential contributions. Qualified researchers are excluded from opportunities based solely on their sex, denying them the chance to compete on merit. The policy creates a system where institutional advancement depends on demographic characteristics rather than individual achievement, undermining the principle that people should be assessed based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated capabilities. This approach reinforces categorical thinking about people and normalizes institutional practices that divide rather than unite candidates through recognition of their common humanity and individual potential.

quotes.text: This appointment is open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women. We welcome and support applications from such candidates who also identify as members of other equity-deserving groups, i.e. Indigenous/Aboriginal peoples, persons with disabilities, racialized/visible minorities, and members of 2SLGBTQ+ communities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly restricts the position to women only, categorically excluding men from consideration regardless of their individual qualifications or merit.

incidents.short_description: Burnaby Public Library implemented a formal hiring program that prioritised job applicants based on their skin colour and ancestry for all exempt management positions, with résumés from white candidates reviewed only as a last resort. A mid-term progress report presented to the Library Board in June 2024 confirmed the practice had been in place since late 2021.
incidents.description_of_event:
In November 2021, the BC Human Rights Commissioner approved an application from Burnaby Public Library (BPL) for a five-year 'Special Program' that gave preferential treatment in hiring to candidates who self-identified by their skin colour or ancestry for all exempt positions, with one notable exception: the Chief Librarian role held by the person who implemented the program.

Under the program, hiring managers were directed to review résumés from candidates sorted by demographic category first. Résumés from white candidates were only considered if hiring managers determined there was not a sufficient pool of qualified candidates from the preferred demographic group. In all six exempt-level competitions conducted under the program up to the mid-term evaluation, hiring managers found sufficient candidates from the preferred demographic group and did not review résumés from white candidates at all. All six positions were filled exclusively by candidates from the preferred demographic categories.

A mid-term report on the program was presented to the Burnaby Public Library Board at its regular meeting on 20 June 2024. The report described the program's data and its impacts, including testimonials from staff hired under the program. BPL stated it intended to continue the program and described the race-based hiring preference as part of a broader institutional commitment framed as an 'anti-racism journey.'
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified job applicants are excluded from consideration for public institution positions based solely on the colour of their skin or ancestry. Their résumés are not reviewed if hiring managers determine enough candidates from preferred demographic groups have applied, meaning individual merit, experience, and ability play no role in whether a person's application is even read. This reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities. The practice entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable physical characteristics, normalising the idea that skin colour is a legitimate basis for distributing employment opportunities. It sends a message to white job-seekers that a publicly funded institution has formally decided their applications are of lower value. It also undermines trust in public institutions by signalling that advancement within those institutions reflects demographic identity rather than individual merit.

quotes.text: In November 2021, the BC Human Rights Commissioner granted an application from Burnaby Public Library (BPL) for a Special Program for a five-year term to allow preferential hiring of candidates who self-identify as Indigenous, Black or a person of colour (IBPOC) for all exempt positions except that of Chief Librarian.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the formal basis and scope of the race-based hiring preference, including the carve-out for the Chief Librarian position.

quotes.text: From a process perspective, hiring managers look first at résumés from racialized candidates, and only look at résumés from white candidates if there isn't a sufficient pool of qualified racialized candidates. In all competitions to the mid-year evaluation point, there has been a sufficient pool of qualified racialized candidates.
quotes.rationale: Describes the operational mechanics of the hiring preference: white candidates' résumés were not reviewed in any of the six competitions conducted under the program.

quotes.text: BPL has had six (6) vacancies at the exempt level. All vacancies have been filled by applicants who are IBPOC.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the outcome of the program: every exempt-level hire over the period was made exclusively from the preferred demographic group.

incidents.short_description: Chinook High School's Instagram account posted Halloween costume guidelines that categorized police alongside Hitler, Columbus, and Ted Bundy as "perpetrators of violence or oppression." The school and Lethbridge School Division apologized and removed the post after public backlash.
incidents.description_of_event: On October 27, 2021, the Chinook Coyotes Instagram account, operated by Chinook High School in Lethbridge, Alberta, posted Halloween costume guidelines advising students to avoid certain costumes. The post included a bullet point stating students should avoid "Costumes depicting perpetrators of violence or oppression (police, Hitler, Columbus, Ted Bundy)." This guidance placed law enforcement officers in the same category as a genocidal dictator and serial killer. The image was originally created by an unaffiliated account called On Canada Project but was shared by school administrators. Local MLAs Nathan Neudorf and Joseph Schow publicly criticized the post on their Facebook pages. The school removed the post within hours and issued a written apology on Instagram, stating that linking police officers to figures like Hitler was "deplorable and something that never should have been shared." The Lethbridge School Division released a formal statement taking full responsibility, calling it an "error in judgement." School administrators met with students to discuss the ramifications of such posts, sent an apology letter to parents, and posted a video apology. The Division emphasized their appreciation for local police partnerships in maintaining safe learning environments.
incidents.expected_harm: This institutional communication reduces police officers to a category of people associated with historical perpetrators of mass violence and oppression. By categorizing all law enforcement as perpetrators rather than evaluating officers as individuals with varied motivations, capabilities, and conduct, the guidance promotes tribal thinking that undermines reasoned discourse about policing. Students exposed to this framing learn to view an entire profession through a lens of collective guilt rather than individual assessment. The comparison will harm relationships between schools and law enforcement, potentially compromising safety partnerships that protect students. Officers who work to build trust with youth and serve their communities with integrity are told their profession is morally equivalent to genocide and serial murder. This categorical condemnation silences constructive dialogue about legitimate concerns regarding policing practices while failing to recognize the many officers who demonstrate dedication to public service and community protection.

incidents.short_description: A member of the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board's Indigenous Education Advisory Council expressed the opinion during a meeting that the practice of singing O Canada in schools is distasteful and should be replaced with something more healthy and positive.
incidents.description_of_event: During the October 21, 2021 meeting of the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board's Indigenous Education Advisory Council, advisory council member and educational assistant Lili Miller expressed the opinion that the practice of standing and singing O Canada during opening exercises in school is distasteful and should be replaced with something more healthy and positive. Chair Manatch suggested that indigenous students be permitted to sit and not participate in the singing of O Canada. Elder Dumont expressed the opinion that the government of Canada needs to hold people accountable for the deaths of students at residential schools. The Board's Superintendent of Instruction noted that schools are required by law to sing the national anthem under the Education Act, section 304 and Ontario Regulation 435/00, though students can be exempted with parental permission or by adult students requesting exemption. The Indigenous Education Team committed to providing awareness training to inform educators about indigenous students' right to remain seated during the singing of O Canada without being singled out for not participating.
incidents.expected_harm: The characterization of singing the national anthem as distasteful and the suggestion it should be replaced undermines shared civic belonging and the recognition of common citizenship that binds all Canadians together. While respecting individual conscience through exemption rights is appropriate, framing the national anthem itself as something negative rather than a symbol of our collective journey erodes the foundations of common identity. This approach will foster division by encouraging students to view a unifying national symbol through the lens of grievance rather than as an expression of the aspirations and values we share as Canadians. The emphasis on separating students by background during a daily practice meant to acknowledge our common membership in the Canadian community works against the goal of building understanding and solidarity across diverse experiences.

incidents.short_description: The Walrus published an article framing access to mental health care as a problem defined primarily by the skin colour of both patients and therapists, treating therapy as a service best delivered within demographic categories rather than to individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The Walrus, a Canadian media outlet, published an article in October 2021 titled 'All Booked Up: The Frustrations of Finding a Black Therapist.' The article frames the mental health care system's shortcomings through the lens of skin colour, arguing that people seeking therapy should be matched with practitioners who share their demographic background. It presents the low number of therapists of a particular skin colour as a systemic crisis, and frames the therapeutic relationship itself as most effective when practitioner and client share the same skin colour and cultural background. The article quotes practitioners and students who describe their own experiences in terms of skin colour, and advocates for restructuring mental health practitioner pipelines along demographic lines. The piece presents therapy as an experience that is fundamentally shaped by the skin colour of the provider, and suggests that care delivered across demographic lines is likely to be inadequate. The article also argues that psychology education is structured around a 'Eurocentric perspective,' and calls for the system to be rebuilt with demographic representation as a central organizing principle.
incidents.expected_harm: By framing therapeutic relationships and mental health care primarily through the lens of skin colour, this kind of media coverage normalizes the idea that people's needs and experiences are best understood through their demographic category rather than their individual circumstances. It reinforces the view that people should seek out or be directed toward service providers based on shared immutable characteristics, rather than on the quality of care and individual fit. This approach treats skin colour as a defining feature of a person's inner life and therapeutic needs, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated and served as a unique individual. It also discourages people from seeking care from practitioners who do not share their demographic background, which narrows access rather than expanding it. The framing encourages institutions to organize services and practitioner training around demographic categories, embedding skin-colour-based sorting into the structure of care delivery.

incidents.short_description: The Hamilton-Wentworth Catholic District School Board's Anti-Racism Education Committee released a report recommending policies that sort students, staff, and parents into demographic categories based on skin colour and ethnicity, and direct hiring practices, committee membership, and school programming accordingly.
incidents.description_of_event:
In October 2021, the Hamilton-Wentworth Catholic District School Board (HWCDSB) Anti-Racism Education Committee (AREC) published a formal recommendations report intended to guide board-wide policy. The report was the product of a committee established following a special board meeting in June 2020, and was mandated to include only staff, students, and parents drawn from what the board designated as specific demographic groups — described in the report as Black, indigenous, and other racialized community members.

The report's recommendations direct the board to restructure several core institutional functions around demographic categorization. On hiring and staffing, the report recommends enhancing hiring, retention, promotion, and succession planning practices to make the workforce "more representative of the diversity of the HWCDSB student population," explicitly framing this around the demographic backgrounds of employees rather than their individual qualifications or merit. The proposed Permanent Anti-Racism Committee is to include parent and student representatives specifically drawn from people identified as Black, indigenous, or Persons of Colour, structurally excluding others from these designated seats based on their background.
