On data collection, the report recommends administering identity-based demographic surveys of both students and staff every five years, with self-identification of race embedded in registration forms, hiring applications, promotion processes, and program applications — creating a system in which the board routinely categorizes individuals by skin colour and ethnicity at multiple touchpoints in their educational and employment journey.

On student programming, the report recommends that classroom environments, school clubs, activities, mentorship opportunities, and scholarship access be curated so that students of particular demographic backgrounds "see themselves authentically reflected." It further recommends a "decolonized learning experience" that highlights the cultures and contributions of specifically named demographic groups, directing curriculum content around group identity.

Mandatory annual professional development in anti-racism is recommended for all staff, with a dedicated lens applied to all board professional development — institutionalizing a particular ideological framework as compulsory training.

The report frames all of these measures through the language of Catholic social teaching on human dignity, though the practical effect of the recommendations is to embed demographic categorization of individuals throughout the board's institutional structures.
incidents.expected_harm: Students, staff, and parents are evaluated, sorted, and granted access to roles, resources, and representation based on their skin colour and ethnic background rather than as individuals with unique circumstances, needs, and abilities. Staff members are subject to hiring and promotion processes in which their demographic characteristics are treated as institutionally relevant qualifications, undermining the principle that individuals should advance on the basis of their character and demonstrated abilities. Students encounter an institution that routinely categorizes them by skin colour from the point of registration onward, normalizing the idea that their identity is defined by group membership rather than their individual qualities. Families and community members seeking input into school governance find that formal advisory and committee representation is allocated by demographic background, excluding some parents from designated seats on the basis of their ethnicity. The mandatory professional development requirements embed a particular ideological framework as compulsory for all staff, foreclosing open inquiry and reasoned discourse on complex social questions. Taken together, these measures entrench the institutional habit of viewing people primarily through the lens of demographic categories, undermining shared civic belonging and the recognition of each person's singular worth.

quotes.text: That a committee of Black, Indigenous, and other racialized Staff, Students and Parents develop a report to present to the Board of Trustees with regard to strategies to promote and strengthen Anti-Racism Education.
quotes.rationale: The committee itself was constituted by explicitly selecting members based on their skin colour and ethnicity, establishing demographic categorization as the foundational principle of the advisory process.

quotes.text: The Hamilton-Wentworth Catholic District School Board (HWCDSB) staff will enhance the HWCDSB hiring, retention, promotion, and succession planning practices, for all HWCDSB employee groups, to ensure the HWCDSB staff is more representative of the diversity of the HWCDSB student population.
quotes.rationale: This recommendation directs hiring and promotion decisions to be shaped by the demographic composition of the workforce relative to the student population, making skin colour and ethnicity institutionally relevant factors in employment decisions.

quotes.text: The Permanent Committee will be comprised of two HWCDSB Trustees, a representative of the Diocese of Hamilton, and shall include student and parent/guardian representatives from Black, Indigenous, and Persons of Colour background, as well as appropriate HWCDSB staff.
quotes.rationale: Formal governance committee membership is explicitly allocated by skin colour and ethnicity, structurally excluding people of other backgrounds from designated representative seats.

incidents.institution_name: Queen’s University > Department of Mechanical and Materials Engineering
incidents.short_description: Queen's University posted a tenure-track faculty position in nuclear waste storage corrosion that was open only to applicants who self-identify as women, restricting candidacy based on sex.
incidents.description_of_event: Queen's University's Department of Mechanical and Materials Engineering advertised a tenure-track faculty position at the Assistant or Associate Professor level, specializing in nuclear waste storage corrosion, with a preferred start date of July 1, 2022. The successful candidate would submit an external nomination for a Tier 2 Canada Research Chair. The posting explicitly stated that the appointment was open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women. The university justified this restriction by citing its employment equity program goals under the Canada Research Chairs program and noting an under-representation of women among Tier 2 Chairholders. While the posting welcomed applications from women who also identify as members of other groups (Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, members of visible minorities, and 2SLGBTQ+ communities), the fundamental eligibility criterion excluded all applicants based solely on sex. The posting indicated this initiative followed provisions for a special program as described by the Ontario Human Rights Commission, with targets for CRC representation to be met between 2020 and 2029.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction will exclude qualified candidates from consideration based solely on their sex rather than their individual qualifications, research excellence, or potential contributions to the field of nuclear materials research. Candidates who do not self-identify as women, regardless of their merit, experience, or ability to advance the department's research objectives, will be denied the opportunity to apply for this publicly funded academic position. The practice reinforces categorical thinking about people based on demographic characteristics rather than evaluating individuals on their unique capabilities and achievements. It contradicts principles of fair access to employment opportunities and sets a precedent where institutions use sex-based restrictions in faculty hiring. The restriction also undermines genuine intellectual diversity by prioritizing demographic characteristics over diversity of thought, research approaches, and individual perspectives that scholars bring to academic discourse.

quotes.text: This appointment is open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly restricts eligibility for the position based on sex, excluding all candidates who do not identify as women regardless of their qualifications or merit.

incidents.institution_name: Queen's University > Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering
incidents.short_description: Queen's University restricted a tenure-track faculty position to applicants who self-identify as women, excluding qualified candidates based on sex in pursuit of Canada Research Chairs Program equity targets.
incidents.description_of_event: Queen's University Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering posted a tenure-track faculty position for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair in Applied Artificial Intelligence with an explicit sex-based restriction. The posting stated the appointment was open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women. The university justified this restriction by citing under-representation of women among Tier 1 Chairholders and the need to meet Canada Research Chairs Program equity targets with staggered deadlines between 2020 and 2029. The position sought candidates at Associate Professor or Professor rank with expertise in applied AI for intelligent systems. While the posting welcomed applications from women who also identify as members of other equity-deserving groups, it categorically excluded all applicants who do not self-identify as women, regardless of their qualifications, research excellence, or ability to contribute to the department's academic mission. The university required all applicants to self-identify, with this information held by the Human Rights and Equity Office and one specially trained selection committee member.
incidents.expected_harm: This restriction will exclude qualified researchers from employment consideration based solely on sex rather than individual merit, capabilities, or research potential. Candidates who do not self-identify as women will be denied the opportunity to compete for this position regardless of their qualifications, expertise in applied artificial intelligence, research accomplishments, or potential to advance knowledge in the field. The practice reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their unique research contributions, teaching abilities, and demonstrated capacity to lead innovative programs. This approach to addressing historical under-representation prevents the university from identifying and selecting the most qualified individual researcher for the position, potentially diminishing research excellence and innovation. It also signals to all faculty and students that demographic categorization can supersede individual evaluation in institutional decision-making, undermining the principle that people should be assessed based on their character, abilities, and achievements.

quotes.text: This appointment is open only to qualified individuals who self-identify as women1. We welcome and support applications from such candidates who also identify as members of other equity-deserving groups, i.e. Indigenous/Aboriginal peoples, persons with disabilities, racialized/visible minorities, and members of 2SLGBTQ+ communities.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the explicit sex-based restriction that categorically excludes applicants who do not self-identify as women, demonstrating the policy reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals based on qualifications and merit.

incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa posted a job advertisement for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair in Green Chemistry that strongly encouraged applications from candidates based on demographic categories rather than individual qualifications alone.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Ottawa advertised a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair position in Green Chemistry with an application deadline of November 14, 2021. The posting stated the university's commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion and strongly encouraged applications from members of four designated groups: indigenous peoples, visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women, as defined in the Employment Equity Act. Candidates were encouraged to complete an Employment Equity self-identification section during the application process. The posting also required applicants to submit a statement on equity, diversity, and inclusion philosophy and practice, and noted that a demonstrated track record of incorporating and improving EDI in research and training was among the required qualifications. This approach directed the hiring process to consider applicants based on demographic categories alongside their individual qualifications and research achievements.
incidents.expected_harm: By encouraging applications based on demographic categories and requiring statements on equity, diversity and inclusion philosophy as a qualification, this hiring process signals that demographic characteristics influence evaluation alongside individual merit. This reduces candidates to group identities rather than evaluating them solely as individuals based on their unique research achievements, teaching abilities, and potential contributions. Qualified candidates who do not identify with designated groups may perceive diminished opportunity or feel their applications receive different consideration based on demographic factors rather than their individual capabilities. The requirement to demonstrate a track record in EDI may also impose ideological conformity as a condition of employment, limiting intellectual diversity and discouraging candidates who believe evaluation should focus exclusively on research excellence and individual qualifications.

quotes.text: The University of Ottawa is committed to ensuring equity, diversity and inclusion in the scholarly and leadership environments of our students, staff, and faculty. Accordingly, we strongly encourage applications from members of the four designated groups: Indigenous peoples, visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women, as defined in the Employment Equity Act, as well as from all candidates with the skills and knowledge to productively engage with equitable, diverse and inclusive communities.
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly encourages applications based on demographic group membership, directing hiring focus toward categorical identities rather than individual qualifications alone.

quotes.text: Self-Identification Form: We encourage all applicants to fill the Employment Equity section in their online profile during the application process online.
quotes.rationale: This repeats the encouragement for demographic self-identification, demonstrating the institution's systematic collection of group identity information during hiring.

incidents.short_description: Woodroffe High School established a Graduation Coach Program that provides academic support and mentorship exclusively to students based on their racial or ethnic background, specifically those identified as African, black and Caribbean.
incidents.description_of_event: The Ottawa-Carleton District School Board implemented a Graduation Coach Program at Woodroffe High School that restricts eligibility to students based on their racial or ethnic background. The program hired Taylor Benjamin as "Graduation Coach for black Students" to provide intensive support, mentorship, academic guidance, and access to resources exclusively to students categorized as African, black and Caribbean. The coach's role includes assisting with credit accumulation, pathway navigation, community service hours, transition support, field trips, well-being services, and mentorship. The program identifies students based on racial categories and provides them with individualized academic and well-being goal development, connections to community resources, and advocacy services. The school board justified this approach by citing research about outcome disparities and attributing these to systemic inequities.
incidents.expected_harm: This program reduces students to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique needs, circumstances, and capabilities. Students requiring academic support, mentorship, or guidance to improve attendance and graduation outcomes are either granted or denied access to intensive coaching resources based on their assigned racial or ethnic classification rather than their demonstrated individual needs or circumstances. This creates a system where institutional support depends on demographic categorization rather than individual assessment. Students who do not fit the specified racial categories but who face similar academic challenges or barriers to engagement will be excluded from this dedicated support structure. The program reinforces the practice of sorting students by immutable traits and treating race as a proxy for individual needs, undermining recognition of each student's singular worth and unique circumstances.

quotes.text: Advancing the academic success of African, Black and Caribbean students in the OCDSB.
quotes.rationale: Confirms the program explicitly targets students based on racial and ethnic categories, establishing the basis for program eligibility.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto > Rotman School of Management
incidents.short_description: The Rotman School of Management restricted applications for a Canada Research Chair position to individuals who self-identified as members of designated demographic groups, excluding candidates based on their immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: On September 8, 2021, the University of Toronto's Rotman School of Management announced a special call for nominations to a Canada Research Chair, Tier 2 position in all areas of management. The position was restricted to full-time tenure-stream Assistant or Associate Professors holding budgetary appointments at the school. The institution explicitly limited eligibility, stating the special call was open only to individuals who self-identified as members of at least one of four designated groups: women, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and indigenous peoples. The restriction was implemented to address what the institution characterized as underrepresentation of these groups in the Canada Research Chairs program. Applications were accepted until October 8, 2021. While the posting outlined rigorous research criteria including excellence as emerging world-class researchers and potential for international recognition, access to the opportunity was conditioned on demographic self-identification rather than being open to all qualified candidates based on their individual merit, research capabilities, and potential contributions.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction creates a multi-tiered system where career advancement opportunities are allocated based on demographic categories rather than individual qualifications and merit. Qualified scholars who do not belong to the designated groups are categorically excluded from consideration regardless of their research excellence, creativity, or potential contributions to the field. This approach reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them as unique persons with distinct capabilities and perspectives. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should assess people based on their individual character, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. By making demographic group membership a prerequisite for eligibility, the restriction treats demographic categories as determinative of a person's value or contribution, contradicting the recognition of singular worth. This creates an environment where professional opportunities are distributed according to immutable characteristics rather than individual merit, potentially discouraging talented researchers and signaling that demographic identity supersedes research excellence in institutional decision-making.

quotes.text: In order to address the underrepresentation of the members of the four designed groups (women, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and Indigenous peoples) in the CRC program (as identified by the Tri-agency Institutional Programs Secretariat), this special call is open only to individuals who self-identify as members of at least one of these four designated groups.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the core eligibility restriction that limits the position to individuals who self-identify with specific demographic groups, excluding others regardless of qualifications.

incidents.short_description: Ontario's Grade 9 mathematics curriculum, implemented in September 2021, emphasizes categorizing students by demographic identities and attributes systemic barriers in mathematics achievement to these identities rather than individual circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: In September 2021, Ontario implemented a revised Grade 9 mathematics curriculum that replaced previous academic and applied streams with a single course. The curriculum document repeatedly emphasizes grouping students by demographic categories, stating that certain groups of students (specifically naming indigenous students, students based on skin colour, students experiencing homelessness, students living in poverty, students with LGBTQ+ identities, and students with special education needs and disabilities) continue to experience systemic barriers to learning mathematics. The curriculum positions these demographic categories as central to understanding student achievement and directs educators to centre pedagogical practices on students' identities, lived experiences, and cultural backgrounds. The document instructs teachers to reflect on their own identities and learn about students' identities, identifications, and affiliations, and to develop understanding of how students think about mathematical concepts according to their cultural backgrounds. It frames mathematics education through the lens of culturally responsive and relevant pedagogy, directing teachers to make connections with cultural ways of knowing and to valorize students' cultural backgrounds and linguistic competences. The curriculum states that educators must work actively and with urgency to address and remove barriers, and emphasizes creating identity-affirming learning environments. Rather than evaluating students as individuals with unique capabilities and circumstances, the curriculum institutionalizes a framework that categorizes students by demographic group membership and attributes differences in educational outcomes to these group identities.
incidents.expected_harm: Students will be evaluated and understood primarily through demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique capabilities, interests, and circumstances. This approach will reduce students to membership in demographic groups, undermining their individual dignity and autonomy. Teachers will be directed to make assumptions about how students think and learn based on their cultural backgrounds and demographic characteristics rather than discovering each student's individual strengths, needs, and learning approaches. Students who do not conform to assumed characteristics of their demographic groups will find their individual experiences invalidated. The curriculum's emphasis on systemic barriers attributed to demographic identity will cultivate a mindset that achievement differences are primarily explained by group membership rather than individual effort, circumstance, and capability. This framework will entrench divisions among students based on immutable characteristics, undermining the development of common citizenship and shared educational community. The focus on identity-based pedagogy will divert attention and resources from evidence-based instructional practices that support all students in mastering mathematical concepts regardless of their backgrounds. Students will be taught to view themselves and others primarily through the lens of demographic categories, inhibiting the development of relationships based on shared humanity and individual character.

quotes.text: Research indicates that there are groups of students (for example, Indigenous students, Black students, students experiencing homelessness, students living in poverty, students with LGBTQ+ identities, and students with special education needs and disabilities) who continue to experience systemic barriers to accessing high-level instruction in and support with learning mathematics.
quotes.rationale: This passage demonstrates the curriculum's approach of categorizing students by demographic identities and attributing educational barriers to these group memberships rather than individual circumstances.

incidents.short_description: Applewood Heights Secondary School announced a Contemporary Black Studies course that categorizes and examines students through demographic lenses rather than as individuals, using Critical Race Theory as its framework.
incidents.description_of_event: In 2021, Applewood Heights Secondary School, part of the Peel District School Board, introduced a Contemporary Black Studies course for the 2021/2022 school year. The course explicitly uses Critical Race Theory as its analytical framework and organizes curriculum around demographic categories rather than individual experiences. The course description states it explores content "from the viewpoint of Black Canadians" and focuses on "Black cultural identity," "Black experiences," and "the Diversity of Blackness." Students are directed to examine topics including "the Social Construction of Race" and develop understanding of "Black identity and culture" to "showcase Black Excellence." The curriculum structures learning around categorical demographic identity rather than evaluating students as individuals with unique perspectives and experiences. The course materials promote examining Canadian society and history through the lens of demographic group membership, organizing students' educational experience around immutable characteristics rather than their individual qualities, reasoning abilities, or diverse life experiences.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will reduce students to demographic categories rather than recognizing them as individuals with unique perspectives and experiences. By organizing curriculum around group identity and teaching students to view themselves and others primarily through demographic lenses, the course undermines the development of reasoned discourse and individual evaluation. Students will be encouraged to interpret their experiences and those of others through categorical frameworks rather than engaging with the full complexity of individual circumstances, character, and merit. The explicit focus on demographic group identity as the primary analytical lens fragments students into separate categories, working against recognition of common humanity and shared civic belonging. By embedding Critical Race Theory as the course framework, students will be directed toward interpreting social phenomena through predetermined categorical analyses rather than developing their own capacity for evidence-based reasoning and open inquiry. This structured demographic categorization in educational practice establishes patterns where institutions evaluate and organize people based on immutable characteristics rather than individual qualities.

quotes.text: Contemporary Black Studies explores contemporary Black culture in Canada, through the lens of Critical Race Theory with a focus on studying the cultures and histories of the Caribbean, Africa and Canada from the viewpoint of Black Canadians.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that the course explicitly uses Critical Race Theory as its analytical framework and organizes content around demographic viewpoint rather than individual perspectives.

incidents.short_description: The Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board implemented Learn.Disrupt.Rebuild lesson plans for students from kindergarten through grade 8, explicitly grounded in in a framework that teaches young children that racism is the normal way society operates and that it serves the material interests of people in power.
incidents.description_of_event: In September 2020, the Hamilton-Wentworth District School Board developed a series of lesson plans called Learn.Disrupt.Rebuild for students from kindergarten through grade 8. The program explicitly states it is grounded in a theory that promotes race essentialism. The program was created in response to COVID-19 and what officials describe as two pandemics: the virus and social inequities like anti-racism. The curriculum materials require primary teachers to bring race into conversations with children as young as kindergarten age, presenting this as necessary for disrupting racist beliefs. The lesson plans define racism using CRT principles, including teaching that racism is the normal way society does business and serves the material interests of people in power. For kindergarten through grade 3 students, teachers are instructed to help children identify racism and understand that race is a heavy burden many students carry at a very early age. Intermediate students in grades 4-6 receive lessons on interpersonal, institutional, and ideological racism, including instruction about the Black Lives Matter movement. One lesson titled Check Your Privilege asks students to complete questionnaires examining their unearned privileges based on demographic categories. The curriculum materials state that educators are legally obligated to teach this content under human rights legislation, though educators may edit or revise the modules. When a board source questioned the program content, the school board director instructed staff to stop responding to questions about it.
incidents.expected_harm: These lessons reduce children to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique qualities and experiences. By teaching kindergarten-age children that racism is the normal way society operates and that certain groups inherently benefit from systemic advantages while others carry heavy burdens based on their demographic characteristics, the curriculum creates divisions along categorical lines from an early age. Young children who are still developing their understanding of themselves and others will be taught to view their peers through the lens of group membership and assumed privilege or oppression rather than as individuals. The privilege questionnaire explicitly asks students to assess themselves based on demographic categories, potentially causing children to feel shame or resentment based on immutable characteristics rather than their individual actions or character. Students may internalize the message that their demographic category determines their worth, opportunities, and moral standing in society. This approach undermines the development of genuine relationships based on shared humanity and individual character. The program's explicit reliance on an ideological framework that presents racism as ordinary and normal may cause children to interpret innocent interactions through a lens of suspicion and assumed prejudice. By centering differences rather than common humanity, the curriculum will impede children's ability to form authentic connections across demographic lines and may foster the very divisions and resentments it purports to address.

quotes.text: In the Learn.Disrupt.Rebuild program, primary teachers are asked to 'bring race into the conversation' – a 'necessary and important' way of disrupting the development of racist beliefs.
quotes.rationale: Shows teachers are instructed to introduce race-based categorization to young children as part of the curriculum

incidents.short_description: The City of Ottawa restricted eligibility for its Anti-Racism Advisory Table to residents from indigenous and racialized communities, excluding all other Ottawa residents from participation based on their background. This is a formal government body where membership is determined by demographic category rather than individual qualifications alone.
incidents.description_of_event:
On July 29, 2021, the City of Ottawa announced it was accepting applications for a new Anti-Racism Advisory Table, a formal advisory group intended to help shape the city's first Anti-Racism Strategy. The advisory table was described as being made up of leaders from racialized communities, with the mandate to raise awareness about racism, encourage action, and provide input into the strategy across six priority areas: employment equity, housing, governance, economic development, health outcomes, and youth development.

The City explicitly restricted eligibility to apply for the advisory table to Ottawa residents from indigenous and racialized communities who are 18 years of age and older. All other Ottawa residents — regardless of their expertise, professional background, lived experience with discrimination, or commitment to addressing racism — were categorically excluded from eligibility based on their background. The selection process was further described as being "mindful of the importance of intersectional representation," reinforcing that demographic categorization was a central criterion for membership. The deadline to apply was August 10, 2021, with appointments to be finalized by August 31, 2021.
incidents.expected_harm: Ottawa residents are excluded from participating in a formal city advisory process based on their background rather than their individual qualifications, expertise, or commitment to the issues at hand. This means that many people with relevant knowledge, professional experience, or personal encounters with discrimination are denied the opportunity to contribute to civic policy based solely on their demographic identity. The approach sends a message that government advisory structures are not open to all citizens equally, undermining the principle of shared civic belonging. By organizing a formal government body around demographic categories, the City normalizes the practice of evaluating people's eligibility for civic participation through the lens of immutable traits rather than individual merit, knowledge, or demonstrated need. It also risks producing a strategy that reflects only a narrow slice of community perspectives, weakening the quality and legitimacy of the resulting policy for all Ottawa residents.

quotes.text: The advisory table will be made up of anti-racism leaders from racialized communities who will provide subject matter expertise to the City's Anti-Racism Secretariat.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that membership of this formal advisory body is framed around a particular demographic category from the outset, rather than qualifications or expertise available to all residents.

quotes.text: All Ottawa residents from Indigenous and racialized communities that are 18 years of age and older are eligible to apply.
quotes.rationale: This is the clearest statement of the eligibility restriction. It explicitly limits who may apply based on background, excluding all other Ottawa residents regardless of their individual qualifications or interest in the issues.

quotes.text: A selection committee of community leaders and City staff will choose members for the table, being mindful of the importance of intersectional representation.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that demographic categorization is embedded in the selection process itself, not merely in the eligibility criteria.

incidents.short_description: The City of Ottawa organized anti-racism action planning sessions in July and August 2021 that restricted participation based on demographic categories including race, sex, sexual orientation, disability, age, and language, segregating residents rather than engaging them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The City of Ottawa conducted a series of public consultation sessions as part of anti-racism and ethnocultural relations initiatives. Registration forms show that sessions were restricted to specific demographic groups: sessions limited to racialized women and gender diverse persons, racialized youth, racialized community members with disabilities, racialized Francophones, and racialized 2SLGBTQQIA+ community members. These sessions occurred between July 27 and August 11, 2021. While general sessions were available for all residents and community stakeholders, the city created parallel consultation processes that categorized and separated residents based on race combined with other demographic characteristics. The registration system required individuals to self-select into demographic categories to participate in specific sessions, effectively restricting access to civic engagement opportunities based on group identity rather than individual interest, expertise, or stake in the outcomes.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will undermine civic belonging by signaling that residents are primarily members of demographic categories rather than individual citizens with unique perspectives and experiences. By organizing consultation based on assumed shared perspectives within demographic groups, the city communicates that people's race, sex, sexual orientation, disability status, age, and language determine their viewpoints and needs. This reduces individuals to demographic proxies and suggests their individual circumstances, reasoning, and contributions matter less than their group membership. The segregated structure prevents diverse perspectives from emerging through open dialogue where individuals with varied life experiences can engage directly with one another. It reinforces divisions by institutionalizing the notion that meaningful civic participation requires demographic sorting rather than individual engagement. Residents who do not fit neatly into prescribed categories, or who reject being defined primarily by demographic characteristics, face barriers to participating in consultation processes that shape policies affecting their community. This approach will discourage individuals from engaging based on their unique knowledge, values, and reasoning, instead conditioning civic participation on willingness to be categorized by immutable characteristics.

quotes.text: Racialized Women and Gender Diverse Persons Action Planning Session - English - Wednesday August 4, 2021 - 5:30-7:30pm
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that the City of Ottawa restricted a consultation session to individuals based on race and sex/gender identity, preventing participation based on individual interest or expertise.

quotes.text: Racialized Community with Disabilities Action Planning Session - English - Monday August 9, 2021 - 5:30-7:30pm
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates restriction based on race and disability status, further illustrating how the city segregated participation by demographic categories.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto > Ontario Institute for Studies in Education
incidents.short_description: The Ontario Institute for Studies in Education restricted a Canada Research Chair position to applicants who self-identify as members of designated demographic groups, excluding others from consideration based on immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: On July 14, 2021, the Ontario Institute for Studies in Education at the University of Toronto issued a special call for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair position. The position was explicitly restricted to faculty members who self-identify as members of at least one of four designated demographic groups: women, indigenous people, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities. The posting stated that the special call was open only to OISE faculty members who self-identify as members of these groups. This restriction was framed as addressing under-representation within the Canada Research Chair Program and supporting the institution's commitment to social justice. The position sought world-class researchers acknowledged as leaders in their fields, with applications due August 19, 2021. The selection committee was to consist of at least four OISE faculty members, and the posting included detailed requirements for letters of interest and curriculum vitae submissions.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction will prevent qualified individuals from being considered for the position based solely on demographic characteristics rather than their individual merit, qualifications, or research accomplishments. Faculty members who do not self-identify as members of the designated groups will be categorically excluded from applying, regardless of their scholarly achievements or potential contributions to the institution's research mission. The practice reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their unique capabilities, research programs, and demonstrated excellence. This approach undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and that people should be recognized for their character and abilities rather than assigned group characteristics.

quotes.text: In order to address the under-representation of women, Indigenous people, persons with disabilities and members of visible minorities within the CRC Program as a whole (as identified by the Tri-Agency Institution Programs Secretariat), this special call is open only to OISE faculty members who self-identify as members of at least one of these four designated groups
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the explicit hiring restriction limiting eligibility to members of designated demographic groups, which is the core of the incident

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto > Faculty of Dentistry
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto Faculty of Dentistry restricted a prestigious Canada Research Chair position to applicants who self-identified as members of designated demographic groups, excluding qualified candidates based on their sex, skin colour, or disability status.
incidents.description_of_event: On July 9, 2021, the University of Toronto Faculty of Dentistry announced a Canada Research Chair Tier 1 position in oral health sciences. The position was restricted to full-time tenured professors who self-identified as members of at least one of four designated groups: women, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and indigenous peoples. The posting stated this special call was open only to individuals from these groups to address their underrepresentation in the Canada Research Chairs program. This meant that qualified tenured professors who did not belong to these demographic categories were ineligible to apply, regardless of their scholarly achievements or research excellence. The application deadline was August 10, 2021. The posting justified this restriction by citing the university's equity, diversity, and inclusion commitments and the Canada Research Chairs Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Action Plan.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction prevents the university from evaluating all qualified scholars as individuals based on their academic merit, research accomplishments, and potential contributions to oral health sciences. Candidates are assessed first through demographic categories rather than their individual character, capabilities, and demonstrated excellence. This approach denies opportunity to accomplished researchers solely because they do not belong to specified demographic groups, treating people as representatives of categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualifications. The practice undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and creates a system where demographic characteristics become prerequisites for eligibility, reducing scholars to their immutable traits rather than recognizing their singular worth and individual achievements.

quotes.text: In order to address the underrepresentation of members of the four designated groups (women, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and Indigenous peoples) in the CRC program (as identified by the Tri-agency Institutional Programs Secretariat), this special call is open only to individuals who self-identify as members of at least one of these four designated groups.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the hiring restriction that excludes applicants based on demographic characteristics, directly demonstrating discriminatory conduct that evaluates people through demographic categories rather than individual merit.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto > Faculty of Medicine
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Temerty Faculty of Medicine restricted applications for a Canada Research Chair Tier 1 position to individuals who self-identified as members of designated groups based on sex, skin colour, disability, or ancestry.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 28, 2021, the Temerty Faculty of Medicine at the University of Toronto issued a call for applications for nomination to a Canada Research Chair, Tier 1 position. The call explicitly restricted eligibility: it was "open only to individuals who self-identify as members of at least one" of four designated groups defined by sex (women), skin colour (members of visible minorities), disability status (persons with disabilities), or ancestry (indigenous peoples). The restriction applied to applicants who were already full-time tenured Full or Associate Professors at the Faculty. The university stated this restriction was intended to address underrepresentation of these groups in the Canada Research Chairs Program. The call required all applicants to complete a self-identification form collecting demographic data, with this information to be held separately and accessed only by designated university officials. The application deadline was July 29, 2021.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction will exclude qualified scholars from consideration based solely on their demographic characteristics rather than their individual merit, research accomplishments, or potential contributions. Scholars who do not belong to the designated groups will be denied the opportunity to apply regardless of their qualifications, research excellence, or ability to advance knowledge in their field. The practice reduces individuals to demographic categories, treating group membership as a proxy for qualification rather than evaluating each person's unique capabilities, achievements, and research program. This approach undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated ability. The restriction establishes a system where demographic classification determines eligibility, creating institutional barriers based on immutable characteristics. Scholars excluded from applying will lose access to substantial research funding, infrastructure, and career advancement opportunities associated with a Canada Research Chair position.

quotes.text: In order to address the underrepresentation of members of the four designated groups (women, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and Indigenous peoples) in the CRC Program (as identified by the Tri-Agency Institutional Programs Secretariat), this special call is open only to individuals who self-identify as members of at least one of these four designated groups.
quotes.rationale: This passage establishes the explicit hiring restriction based on demographic group membership, showing that the opportunity was not open to all qualified scholars but only to those who self-identified with specific demographic categories.

incidents.short_description: Edmonton Public Schools adopted a board policy defining diversity categories and committing to actions that acknowledge privilege based on demographic groups, framing institutional approaches through categories rather than individual assessment.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 22, 2021, Edmonton Public Schools' Board of Trustees issued policy HAAB.BP on Anti-racism and Equity. The policy defines concepts including a definition of white supremacy as "the systematic marginalization of People of Colour based on a socially constructed racial hierarchy that privileges people who identify as white." The policy commits the board to "acknowledge where privilege exists" and to "eradicate racism, discrimination and privilege from our learning and working environments." It establishes frameworks for "equity" that involve "providing each learner with the type and amount of support needed to be successful, including culturally relevant and responsive environments and resources," and commits to "using evidence and data to better understand issues of racism and equity." The policy requires professional development for staff focused on understanding "diversity within their school" and mandates engagement with "members of the community who have experienced systemic and institutional racism and marginalization" to inform division efforts. The policy calls for establishing "an anti-racist environment that reflects the cultural diversity of the school" and supporting environments "where students' diverse histories, identities and lived experiences are valued and recognized as part of their success, engagement and belonging in schools."
incidents.expected_harm: The policy's framework categorizes students and staff according to demographic attributes and posits systemic privilege based on these categories. By embedding concepts that attribute characteristics to individuals based on group membership rather than individual circumstances, the policy risks encouraging educators to view students through categorical lenses rather than as distinct individuals with unique needs and capabilities. The commitment to provide differentiated support based on "culturally relevant and responsive environments" and to value "diverse histories, identities and lived experiences" as components of success creates pressure to make assumptions about students' needs based on their perceived group membership. Professional development requirements focused on understanding privilege and addressing institutional racism based on demographic categories will shape how educators interact with students, potentially leading them to approach individuals as representatives of demographic groups rather than as unique persons. The policy's call to collect and use "evidence and data to better understand issues of racism and equity" creates infrastructure for tracking and analyzing students by demographic categories, institutionalizing categorical thinking in decision-making processes. Students evaluated through this categorical framework may find their individual qualities, abilities, and demonstrated needs subordinated to assumed characteristics derived from their perceived group membership, undermining recognition of their singular worth and creating barriers to evaluation based on character, abilities, and individual circumstances.

quotes.text: White Supremacy: in this context, is the systematic marginalization of People of Colour based on a socially constructed racial hierarchy that privileges people who identify as white. It does not refer to extremist ideologies which believe that white people are genetically or culturally superior to non-whites.
quotes.rationale: Defines a framework that categorizes individuals based on demographic characteristics and attributes systemic privilege to one category, establishing categorical thinking as institutional practice.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario government allocated $1.43 million in funding restricted to programs serving students based on their skin colour, establishing grants available only to organizations providing race-specific educational services and supports.
incidents.description_of_event: In June 2021, the Ontario Ministry of Education announced $1.43 million in funding for programs explicitly restricted to serving students based on their skin colour. The funding included grants to multiple community organizations with eligibility criteria tied to demographic categories rather than individual need or demonstrated disadvantage. For example, $500,000 was allocated to the Lifelong Leadership Institute specifically for programs serving students based on their skin colour in the Greater Toronto Area, $280,000 to ANCHOR for programs restricted to students based on demographic classification, and $100,000 to Jaku Konbit to provide support exclusively to students in Ottawa based on their skin colour. The government also announced the Graduation Coach Program, allocating $2.94 million for 2021-22 to provide intensive support exclusively to students based on demographic categories rather than individual academic need. These funding decisions created a system where program eligibility and resource allocation were determined by categorizing students according to immutable characteristics rather than assessing individual circumstances, abilities, or demonstrated needs. The announcement was framed as addressing pandemic impacts, yet the eligibility restrictions applied demographic proxies rather than evaluating which individual students experienced the greatest disruption or required the most support.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are reduced to demographic categories rather than recognized as individuals with unique circumstances, abilities, and needs. Those experiencing academic difficulty or pandemic-related disruption will be assessed for program eligibility based on their categorization into demographic groups rather than their individual situation. Students categorized into groups deemed ineligible for these programs—regardless of their personal need or circumstances—will be denied access to resources and supports available to others. This approach teaches students that institutions should evaluate them according to demographic classification rather than individual merit, character, or demonstrated need. It undermines the principle that support should address individual circumstances and that opportunity should reflect individual capabilities. The system creates an environment where students learn to view themselves and others through the lens of demographic categorization rather than recognizing the singular worth of each person. Students may internalize the message that their value and deservingness of institutional support derives from group membership rather than their individual qualities and needs. This will foster division among students who might otherwise find common ground in shared experiences of academic challenge, pandemic disruption, or aspirations for success. The approach also fails students whose individual circumstances warrant support but whose demographic classification renders them ineligible, leaving them without assistance while others with potentially less severe needs receive resources based solely on categorical membership.

quotes.text: To improve education outcomes for Black students and break down systemic barriers, the Ontario government is introducing a new tool for school boards and making available $1.43 million in programs that help address policies and practices that have had adverse impacts on Ontario's Black students
quotes.rationale: Establishes the total funding amount and confirms that programs are restricted to students based on demographic categorization rather than individual assessment of need or circumstance.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council > Canada Research Chairs Program
incidents.short_description: The Canada Research Chairs Program requires participating institutions to meet demographic quotas for research chair appointments, with consequences including restrictions on new nominations and allocation reductions for institutions that fail to meet targets based on census demographics rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canada Research Chairs Program implemented mandatory equity targets requiring all participating institutions to achieve specific demographic percentages in their research chair appointments by 2029: 22% racialized individuals, 4.9% Indigenous Peoples, 7.5% persons with disabilities, and 50.9% women and gender equity-seeking groups. These targets are based on 2016 Census population data rather than individual qualifications or merit. Institutions must submit equity target plans and are actively monitored for progress. The program justifies this approach by claiming that systemic barriers exist in academia and that demographic diversity leads to increased excellence, thereby conflating demographic representation with research excellence. Institutions failing to meet interim deadlines (December 2022, 2025, 2027) face immediate consequences: they can only submit new nominations for individuals from designated groups until gaps are filled. Institutions missing the 2029 deadline will have their total chair allocations reduced. While the program states that all researchers must meet excellence requirements assessed through peer review, the mandatory demographic targets and consequences create a system where institutions must prioritize demographic characteristics in their hiring decisions to avoid penalties, rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals based on their research capabilities and potential contributions.
incidents.expected_harm: This policy will harm academic excellence and individual dignity by requiring institutions to evaluate researchers primarily through demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique qualifications and perspectives. Researchers from designated groups will face the stigma of preferential treatment, with their appointments questioned as quota-filling rather than merit-based recognition of their capabilities. Researchers outside designated groups will be denied equal consideration for positions regardless of their individual excellence, research potential, or unique perspectives. The policy undermines genuine diversity of thought by assuming that demographic categories determine intellectual perspectives and contributions, rather than recognizing that diverse viewpoints emerge from individual experiences, reasoning, and scholarly approaches. Academic institutions will be pressured to make hiring decisions based on demographic targets rather than assembling research teams based on complementary expertise and intellectual contributions. The consequences mechanism creates perverse incentives where institutions may lower standards or overlook more qualified candidates to avoid penalties, ultimately diminishing research quality. By reducing individuals to demographic proxies and mandating outcomes based on population statistics rather than the actual pool of qualified researchers in specific fields, the policy violates the principle of singular worth and contradicts the program's own stated foundation on excellence. The program contributes to a regressive racialist and ethnocentric view of the citizenry rather than seeing them valuing Canadians for their unique qualities.

quotes.text: The equity target-setting methodology implements incremental equity targets for the program, based mainly on Canada's population (2016 Census), for each of the four designated groups, as follows: racialized individuals 22%, Indigenous Peoples 4.9%, persons with disabilities 7.5%, and women and gender equity-seeking groups 50.9%, over 2021 to 2029.
quotes.rationale: Specifies the exact demographic quotas required and confirms these are based on general population statistics rather than the pool of qualified researchers or individual merit, demonstrating the demographic targeting basis of the policy.

incidents.short_description: Hamilton Public Health Services prioritized COVID-19 vaccine access for black and other racialized populations ages 18+ in specific postal codes, restricting appointment availability based on racial demographic categories rather than individual health risk factors.
incidents.description_of_event: On April 23, 2021, Hamilton Public Health Services announced a COVID-19 vaccination program that prioritized black and other racialized populations/people of colour ages 18 and older living in postal codes L9C, L8W, L8L, L8N, and L9K. The program established dedicated clinics, including a mobile pop-up clinic at Restoration House and appointments at FirstOntario Centre, exclusively for individuals meeting these racial and geographic criteria. Appointments were available only through a dedicated hotline (905-974-9848, option 7), and proof of address was required. The policy explicitly restricted access to vaccine appointments based on racial demographic categories, preventing residents of the same postal codes who did not identify as racialized from accessing these priority appointments. While the stated rationale cited that racialized people represented 47% of COVID-19 cases but only 19% of Hamilton's population, the program did not evaluate individuals based on their actual health vulnerabilities, occupation, living conditions, or other individual risk factors that might make them more susceptible to severe COVID-19 outcomes.
incidents.expected_harm: This policy will cause multiple forms of harm. Individuals who face high COVID-19 risk due to health conditions, caregiving responsibilities, essential work, or crowded housing will be denied priority vaccine access if they do not belong to the preferred racial categories, potentially resulting in preventable illness and death. The policy teaches community members that institutions may allocate life-saving medical resources based on demographic categories rather than individual need, undermining trust in public health systems and the principle that healthcare should be delivered based on medical necessity. Residents who need vaccines but are excluded from priority access based on their racial identity will experience the message that their health and lives matter less than others, inflicting dignity harm. The policy establishes a precedent for race-based allocation of government services and medical care, normalizing the practice of sorting people into categories and treating them differently based on immutable characteristics rather than individual circumstances. Healthcare workers and public servants will be required to implement racial screening, forcing them to serve as gatekeepers who turn away individuals based on demographic characteristics rather than medical judgment. The community will experience division and resentment as neighbours discover they have unequal access to protection from a deadly disease based on racial categories, fracturing social cohesion at a time when collective action is most needed.

quotes.text: To increase vaccine accessibility to those who are disproportionately impacted by COVID-19, Hamilton Public Health Services is prioritizing Black and other racialized populations/people of colour beginning with serving those ages 18+ and live in postal codes L9C, L8W, L8L, L8N and L9K for COVID-19 vaccination.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the explicit policy of restricting vaccine priority based on racial demographic categories combined with geographic location, confirming that access was conditioned on racial identity rather than individual health risk assessment.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Global Affairs Canada
incidents.short_description: Global Affairs Canada implemented mandatory anti-racism training for diplomats teaching that only people with white skin can be racist and that principles like objectivity and individualism are markers of white supremacy.
incidents.description_of_event:
Global Affairs Canada introduced mandatory anti-racism training materials for Canadian diplomats, obtained through a freedom of information request by the Toronto Sun and reported in April 2021. The training was part of a broader federal government initiative begun in 2019 to address what officials characterized as systemic racism within the bureaucracy, intensifying after 2020 following worldwide protests.

The training explicitly cites critical race theory as its foundation and differs markedly from earlier anti-racism training that emphasized tolerance and accommodation. The materials define racism as exclusively the domain of people with white skin, stating in a "Myths and Facts" section that people without white skin cannot be racist—they can only express "racial prejudice" because racism requires a "systemic relationship to power." According to the materials, "In Canada, white people hold this cultural power due to Eurocentric modes of thinking, rooted in colonialism, that continue to reproduce and privilege whiteness."

The training characterizes numerous concepts as markers of "white supremacy culture," including objectivity, individualism, perfectionism, worship of the written word, and the idea of personal advancement through merit ("pulling oneself up by one's bootstraps"). The materials describe these as examples of "covert white supremacy," defined as white supremacy that is "socially acceptable." The training also labels statements like "we're all just one human family" and claiming to be "colourblind" as additional markers of covert white supremacy.

The materials teach that the absence of explicitly "antiracist" policies will itself precipitate a racist system, and that ignoring categorization based on skin colour is an act of white supremacy designed to maintain inequity. The training adopts the position that "if you aren't an antiracist, you are complicit." The course draws heavily from sources including the workbook "Dismantling Racism" and recommends Ibram X. Kendi's "How to be an Antiracist" for further reading.

This training initiative was part of broader federal programming expansion, with the 2021 federal budget allocating over $300 million toward "equity" programs, including race-focused data collection, funding for "equity deserving groups," and prioritized government procurement from businesses categorized by the skin colour of their owners.
incidents.expected_harm:
The training reduces individuals to their skin colour rather than evaluating them as unique persons with individual character, abilities, and perspectives. By teaching that people with white skin hold inherent power and can be racist while people without white skin cannot be racist regardless of their prejudices or actions, the training divides Canadians into categories based on immutable physical characteristics and assigns different moral capacities to different groups.

The characterization of objectivity, individualism, reasoned thinking, and merit-based advancement as markers of white supremacy undermines the foundations of evidence-based policy, rational discourse, and fair evaluation of individuals. This approach discourages diplomats from assessing situations and people based on observable facts, logical analysis, and individual circumstances—core competencies for effective diplomatic work.

By mandating that federal employees adopt a framework viewing Canadian society as immutably tainted by white supremacy requiring constant "antiracist" intervention, the training compels acceptance of a particular ideological perspective rather than fostering genuine understanding across diverse viewpoints. The assertion that being "colourblind" or recognizing common humanity constitutes white supremacy directly contradicts principles of equal treatment and shared civic belonging.

For diplomats specifically, this training creates practical harms: the materials acknowledge that managers tell staff they won't be posted to certain countries based on their ancestry or skin colour—yet the training's framework provides no coherent basis for addressing such discrimination, since it redefines racism as something only people with white skin can perpetrate. The training thus fails to protect all employees from prejudice and discrimination based on their individual characteristics.

The approach fosters division rather than solidarity, teaching employees to view colleagues through the lens of skin colour categories and assumed power dynamics rather than as individuals navigating shared professional challenges. This will undermine trust, honest communication, and collaborative problem-solving within Global Affairs Canada and in Canada's diplomatic relationships abroad.

quotes.text: In a "Myths and Facts" section opening the document, it is called a myth that non-white people are capable of racism, which is defined as exclusively being the domain of white people. Non-white people can express "racial prejudice," to be sure (the example provided is "white people can't dance") but this doesn't qualify as racism because of the "systemic relationship to power." "In Canada, white people hold this cultural power due to Eurocentric modes of thinking, rooted in colonialism, that continue to reproduce and privilege whiteness," it reads.
quotes.rationale: This directly supports the core claim that the training teaches only people with white skin can be racist, a fundamental element distinguishing this incident from traditional anti-discrimination approaches.

incidents.short_description: The Simcoe County District School Board distributed a 30-step teaching guide that instructs teachers to sort students and themselves into racial and demographic categories and adopt activist ideological frameworks as standard classroom practice.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Simcoe County District School Board (SCDSB) Equity Team created and distributed a document titled 'Embedding Anti-Racism and Equity Into Your Teaching: A 30 Step Self-Directed Journey,' authored by Theresa Lau and others. The resource was shared with all SCDSB staff and promoted publicly by the SCDSB Equity Team on 6 April 2021. The document directs teachers through 30 steps organized in three stages: gaining knowledge, making connections, and applications and actions.

The guide instructs teachers to classify themselves and their students according to racial, gender, and other demographic categories, distinguishing between 'dominant' and 'non-dominant' groups — explicitly placing people of different skin colours, genders, and sexual orientations into power hierarchies. Teachers are directed to assess their own racial 'privilege,' examine what their racial identity means for how they teach, and ask themselves what their 'honest opinion of Black people' is.

The guide instructs teachers to evaluate their classroom libraries, lesson materials, and physical environments through a racial lens, and to apply an 'anti-racist lens' to literary texts. It promotes the 'Pyramid of White Supremacy' as a classroom framework and includes a step called 'Decentering Whiteness in the Curriculum.' Teachers are directed not to remain silent on 'racially charged' events and are told that 'silence is a privilege.'

The document recommends works by ideological advocates including Ibram X. Kendi's 'How to Be an Anti-Racist,' and instructs teachers to follow specific social media accounts and hashtags associated with activist movements including #BlackLivesMatter and #CheckYourPrivilege. The resource was created for and distributed to all staff of the school board.
incidents.expected_harm: Teachers are trained to view and evaluate students primarily through the lens of racial and demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. Students are sorted into group hierarchies based on their skin colour, and classroom content is selected or rejected based on whether it conforms to a particular ideological framework. This reduces students to demographic proxies and denies them recognition as individuals with unique character, abilities, and worth. Students whose background or identity places them in the 'dominant' category are singled out as bearers of unearned power or bias, while others are framed through a lens of victimhood — both of which undermine individual dignity and honest, open inquiry. The insistence that teachers take political positions on contested current events and condemn silence as a form of privilege forecloses genuine intellectual openness and pressures educators to deliver ideological instruction rather than reasoned, evidence-based teaching. The overall effect is a classroom environment where students are categorized, divided, and evaluated based on their skin colour and other immutable traits rather than their individual character and abilities.

quotes.text:
What aspects of my identity fall into the 'dominant' group? What aspects fall into the 'non-dominant' group?
Identity Dominant Group: holds the most power / Non-Dominant Group: holds less power in comparison to the dominant group
Race: white / people of colour
Gender: men / women, transgender people
Sexual Orientation: heterosexual people / LGBTQ+
quotes.rationale: Illustrates the document's explicit instruction to sort individuals — including teachers and students — into hierarchical groups based on skin colour, gender, and sexual orientation, directly reducing people to demographic categories rather than recognizing them as individuals.

quotes.text:
Step 13: Disrupt Your Dinner
What is your honest opinion of Black people? Name 3 sources of influence that have shaped that perspective (parents, music, news).
quotes.rationale: Directs teachers to interrogate their attitudes toward people of a particular skin colour as a professional development exercise, framing teaching practice through a racial essentialism lens.

quotes.text:
Step 20: Decentering Whiteness in the Curriculum
Where does whiteness get exemplified in the classroom?
Pyramid of White Supremacy
quotes.rationale: Instructs teachers to apply an ideological framework that evaluates curriculum content based on the racial identity associated with its origins, embedding racial categorization as a standard teaching tool.

incidents.short_description: The Waterloo Region District School Board ran a teacher training workshop that promoted essentialist ideas about people based on their skin colour, framing individuals as fundamentally different based on their ancestry and physical characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 10, 2021, the Waterloo Region District School Board hosted a workshop titled "Creating the Conditions for Antiracist Teaching and Learning," attended by approximately 600 WRDSB educators. The workshop's content, later shared publicly via social media, revealed several recurring themes that raise concerns about race essentialism. Presenters described people in terms of their physical bodies defined by skin colour — using phrases such as "white bodies" and "spaces" that cause harm — framing racism as something experienced through and by racially categorized bodies rather than as a violation of individual dignity. One segment reportedly advanced the idea that people of certain ancestry possess a "blood memory" of historical wrongs that gives them a particular kind of wisdom, suggesting that inherited ancestry determines moral or epistemic standing. Another segment reportedly framed public education as operating on an explicit agenda rooted in racial hierarchy, and another educator reportedly advised school administrators to have a scripted response ready for parents who raise concerns about such content, and urged principals to support teachers delivering this material on a no-questions-asked basis. A senior school board administrator appeared in the closing segment. The workshop content was presented to educators as professional training, normalizing the framing of students and staff as representatives of racial categories rather than as individuals.
incidents.expected_harm: When teachers are trained to view students through the lens of inherited racial categories and ancestry, they evaluate children not as individuals with unique character, abilities, and needs, but as embodiments of group identities. Students learn that their skin colour and ancestry determine what they know, what they feel, and what burdens they carry — a fundamentally essentialist view that reduces a person's worth and nature to immutable characteristics. The idea that some people carry ancestral wisdom in their blood while others carry ancestral guilt entrenches division and undermines the recognition of each person's singular worth. Advising administrators to dismiss parental concerns with scripted responses and to support content delivery without question removes a critical check on the quality and integrity of what children are taught. This forecloses the open, reasoned discourse that allows communities to evaluate educational materials honestly. The overall effect is a school culture in which sorting people by demographic category is normalized and celebrated as progressive practice, undermining shared civic belonging and common humanity.

quotes.text: here we get another speaker hitting on the theme that white and non-white bodies are different in some kind of moral (or even cosmic) way—including the idea that non-whites possess a "blood memory" of past white-supremacist historical sins, which gives them wisdom...
quotes.rationale: Describes an explicitly essentialist claim that ancestry and skin colour determine a person's moral insight or epistemic standing — a direct violation of the principle that individuals should be evaluated on their character and abilities, not inherited traits.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board operates a Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement, establishing a program structure that determines eligibility and access based on students' racial category rather than individual needs or circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board has operated a Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement since approximately February 2021. The centre is described as 'the first of its kind in North America' and is celebrated by the institution as an achievement during Black History Month 2026. The centre's name and framing indicate it provides programs, resources, or services designated specifically for students based on their racial classification rather than evaluating students as individuals with unique circumstances, abilities, and demonstrated needs.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach may undermine the principle of singular worth by categorizing students according to demographic group rather than recognizing each student's individual qualities, challenges, and potential. When institutions structure programs around racial categories, they risk reducing students to group membership rather than supporting them based on their unique character, abilities, and circumstances. Such categorical frameworks may also foster division rather than common humanity, emphasizing demographic differences over the shared experiences and aspirations that unite all students in their educational journey.

quotes.text: This Black History Month, we are proud to celebrate 5 years of the @tdsb Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement. The first of its kind in North America.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the existence of the Centre, its operational timeframe of five years, its association with the Toronto District School Board, and its explicit focus on students defined by racial category. The celebration during Black History Month and claim of being 'first of its kind' indicates institutional pride in this categorical approach.

incidents.short_description: Peel District School Board implemented a mandatory professional learning plan for senior staff grounded in an approach that reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on individual merit and capabilities.
incidents.description_of_event: In February 2021, Peel District School Board presented a Senior Team Professional Learning Plan to address findings from a Ministry of Education review. The plan mandates senior leadership to learn concepts including white supremacy, settler colonialism, anti-black racism, and intersectionality, drawing from critical race theory scholarship. The curriculum includes training on institutional accountability, identity and social location, and application of equity principles across board decisions including streaming, guidance, discipline, and promotion processes.
