The plan explicitly states that learning will be rooted in critical race theory and that all decision-making must be connected to a critical consciousness framework. Senior staff are required to apply these concepts when reviewing data and making decisions about students and staff, with an accountability framework to ensure compliance.
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions mandate that all decision-making be filtered through frameworks emphasizing demographic group membership and systemic oppression narratives, there is substantial risk that individuals will be evaluated based on perceived group characteristics rather than their unique qualities, achievements, and potential. Students and staff may be sorted and treated according to demographic categories rather than being recognized for their individual circumstances, abilities, and character. This approach contradicts the principle of singular worth by potentially reducing people to representatives of identity groups. The mandatory nature of the training and the requirement that all decisions reflect this ideological framework may suppress intellectual diversity and reasoned discourse, as staff who question these premises may face professional consequences. The emphasis on collective group identities over shared humanity risks deepening divisions rather than building common understanding and civic belonging across the school community.

quotes.text: The Senior Team will be mandated to learn core concepts related to anti-racism work, including white supremacy, settler colonialism, anti-Black racism, and intersectionality. They will explore these concepts by drawing from critical race theory scholarship and human rights legislation.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the mandatory nature of critical race theory-based training for senior leadership, indicating that this framework will inform institutional decision-making.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto > Faculty of Applied Science & Engineering
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering restricted applications for two Canada Research Chair Tier 1 positions to individuals who self-identified as women, persons with disabilities, or indigenous peoples, excluding applicants based on these demographic categories.
incidents.description_of_event: On February 10, 2021, the Faculty of Applied Science and Engineering at the University of Toronto announced a special call for nominations to Canada Research Chair Tier 1 positions. The call explicitly restricted eligibility, stating it was "open only to individuals who self-identify as women, Indigenous peoples, or persons with disabilities." Applicants had to be full-time tenured Full or Associate Professors holding budgetary appointments in the Faculty. The Faculty expected to select up to two nominees through this restricted process. The restriction was justified as addressing under-representation among current Chairholders and fulfilling equity commitments. The application deadline was March 12, 2021. This hiring practice evaluated candidates based on demographic self-identification rather than solely on their individual qualifications, research excellence, and demonstrated capabilities as scholars.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction will exclude qualified scholars from consideration based on demographic categories rather than individual merit. Applicants who do not self-identify within the specified groups are denied the opportunity to compete for these prestigious research positions regardless of their qualifications, accomplishments, or potential contributions. The practice reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them as unique scholars with distinct research programs and capabilities. It undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated excellence. The restriction creates a system where demographic self-identification becomes a prerequisite for opportunity, establishing institutional precedent for evaluating people through categorical rather than individual assessment.

quotes.text: this special call is open only to individuals who self-identify as women, Indigenous peoples, or persons with disabilities
quotes.rationale: This quote directly establishes the hiring restriction at the core of the incident, demonstrating that eligibility was limited based on demographic self-identification rather than individual qualifications alone.

incidents.institution_name: Centre for Addiction and Mental Health
incidents.short_description: The Centre for Addiction and Mental Health published a formal strategy organizing its clinical care, staffing, research, and advocacy work around patients' and staff members' skin colour, treating people as members of demographic categories rather than as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Centre for Addiction and Mental Health (CAMH) published a formal institutional strategy titled 'Dismantling Anti-Black Racism: A Strategy of Fair & Just CAMH,' prepared by its Office of Health Equity and an advisory committee. The strategy, released in February 2021, outlines 22 actions to be achieved by 2022, organized across three areas: care for patients and families, working conditions for staff, and broader health system advocacy.

Throughout the strategy, CAMH commits to organizing clinical services, hiring and career advancement, training requirements, research protocols, and external advocacy specifically around patients' and employees' skin colour. In practice, this means CAMH structures access to programs such as the Substance Abuse Program for African Canadian and Caribbean Youth (SAPACCY) and culturally adapted CBT based on patients' perceived demographic background. The strategy directs CAMH to develop 'pathways of progression' for staff members specifically on the basis of their skin colour, to collect socio-demographic data on staff to monitor the racial composition of its workforce, and to recruit researchers specifically based on their skin colour.

CAMH commits to embedding mandatory diversity, equity and inclusion measures in performance evaluations for all staff and leadership, and to training all staff in ideological frameworks tied to the strategy's assumptions. The strategy also directs CAMH to use its position as a health system leader to advocate to the Ontario Ministry of Health for funding models and program access structured around patients' skin colour.

The strategy treats skin colour as the primary lens through which patients, staff, and the health system should be understood and organized, rather than evaluating and supporting individuals based on their specific clinical needs, demonstrated abilities, or personal circumstances.
incidents.expected_harm:
Patients are assessed and channelled into programs and services based on their skin colour rather than their individual clinical needs, symptoms, or preferences. This means some patients receive a different standard of attention or access to resources depending on which demographic category the institution assigns them to, undermining the principle that every person deserves care based on their own circumstances.

Staff members are evaluated for hiring, retention, advancement, and performance in part on the basis of their skin colour, meaning individuals are not assessed solely on their professional abilities and character. Staff who do not belong to designated demographic groups find advancement pathways structured around the attributes of colleagues rather than individual merit.

All staff are required to complete training grounded in a particular ideological framework as a condition of employment, foreclosing open inquiry and reasoned professional judgment about complex clinical questions.

The institution's public advocacy role is directed toward entrenching skin-colour-based funding and program structures across the broader Ontario health system, extending the impact of these approaches well beyond CAMH itself. The cumulative effect is a health institution that formally sorts, evaluates, and treats the people in its care as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals.

quotes.text: CAMH will develop, implement and monitor a plan to improve the recruitment, retention and progression of Black physicians, staff, students and trainees.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that CAMH formally commits to structuring hiring, retention and career progression specifically around staff members' skin colour rather than individual merit and ability.

quotes.text: CAMH will collect socio-demographic data on staff and will use this to measure progress on the diversity of its workforce and equality and inclusivity of its recruitment, retention and talent development practices.
quotes.rationale: Shows that the institution will collect and use demographic data on staff to drive personnel decisions, treating skin colour as a measure of institutional success.

quotes.text: CAMH will prioritize this research and recruit Black researchers working in these areas to decrease research disparities. Similar to the guidelines on data collection, research involving Black populations will follow ethical protocols developed by Black communities.
quotes.rationale: Shows that researcher recruitment and research governance are organized around skin colour, meaning individuals are evaluated and granted authority based on their demographic category rather than their individual expertise.

quotes.text: CAMH will work with the Ontario Ministry of Health to develop mental health funding models that better meet the needs of Black populations in Ontario.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that CAMH uses its institutional influence to advocate for health funding structured around skin colour at the provincial level, extending demographic-based resource allocation across the broader health system.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Service Commission of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Government of Canada's Federal Student Work Experience Program includes a stream that restricts access to student employment opportunities based on ancestry, limiting who can apply based on their ethnic background rather than skills or demonstrated need.
incidents.description_of_event: The Public Service Commission of Canada operates the Federal Student Work Experience Program (FSWEP), a national student employment initiative. Within this program, two targeted streams exist: the Employment Opportunity for Students with Disabilities (EOSD) and the Indigenous Student Employment Opportunity (ISEO). The ISEO stream is exclusively open to students who self-declare as aboriginal persons under the Employment Equity self-declaration section. Students hired through the ISEO stream are further required to sign an Affirmation of Aboriginal Affiliation Form confirming their ancestry. This means access to a subset of government student job opportunities is determined not by a student's skills, academic background, or demonstrated need, but solely by their ethnic or ancestral identity. The program posting, published on the GC Jobs platform and dated January 26, 2021, explicitly states that the ISEO is "only open to students who have answered 'YES' to the question 'Are you an Aboriginal person?'" The EOSD stream similarly restricts access based on disability status, limiting those opportunities to students who self-declare as persons with a disability.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are sorted into different employment opportunity streams based on their ancestry and ethnicity rather than being evaluated as individuals based on their abilities, qualifications, or circumstances. Students whose ancestry does not match the prescribed categories are excluded from certain government job opportunities regardless of their merit or need. This institutional practice reduces individuals to demographic categories, signalling that the government views students through the lens of their ethnic background rather than their unique qualities and potential. The requirement to sign an Affirmation of Aboriginal Affiliation Form further embeds ethnic labelling into the hiring process, normalizing the idea that ancestral background is a legitimate basis for determining who receives access to public employment. This approach undermines the principle that government institutions should evaluate people as individuals, and entrenches a system where immutable characteristics determine access to opportunity.

quotes.text: Indigenous Students Employment Opportunity (ISEO) is only open to students who have answered "YES" to the question "Are you an Aboriginal* person?" under the "Employment Equity self-declaration" section.
quotes.rationale: Directly establishes that access to the ISEO hiring stream is restricted based on ancestry and ethnic self-identification, excluding students who do not meet this demographic criterion.

quotes.text: For ISEO — Hired students will be required to sign the Affirmation of Aboriginal Affiliation Form.
quotes.rationale: Shows that the ethnic categorisation is enforced through a formal documentation requirement, further embedding ancestry-based identity classification into the government hiring process.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat
incidents.short_description: The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat announced a set of federal public service hiring and promotion priorities explicitly structured around employees' skin colour and ethnicity. The announcement commits the government to increasing representation at senior levels by targeting specific demographic groups rather than evaluating individuals on their merits.
incidents.description_of_event: On January 26, 2021, the Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat issued a news release announcing the federal government's priorities for diversity and inclusion in the public service. The announcement identified a set of initiatives explicitly organised around employees' skin colour and ethnic background. Key among these were: the release of disaggregated workforce data broken down by demographic sub-groups defined by skin colour and ethnicity; a commitment to increasing representation of specified demographic groups at senior leadership levels through promotion and recruitment; the introduction of a Mentorship Plus Program offering mentoring and sponsorship opportunities directed at employees identified by their demographic characteristics; proposed amendments to the Public Service Employment Act and Employment Equity Act to entrench demographic benchmarks in hiring; and a $12 million Centre on Diversity and Inclusion tasked with co-developing initiatives based on employees' demographic identities. The announcement also referenced a Call to Action issued by the Clerk of the Privy Council on January 22, 2021, directing public service leaders to take practical actions within this demographic framework. Throughout, the policy framed advancement and support in the public service as matters to be allocated based on employees' skin colour, ethnicity, and other demographic group memberships, rather than on individual character, abilities, or demonstrated needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Public servants are evaluated, categorised, and granted advancement opportunities — including mentorship, sponsorship, and promotion — based on their skin colour and ethnic background rather than their individual abilities and character. This entrenches a system where demographic identity determines institutional treatment, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be recognised and evaluated as an individual. Employees who do not belong to the designated demographic groups are passed over for targeted support and advancement opportunities regardless of their individual merit or circumstances. The normalisation of demographic group membership as the basis for institutional decisions reinforces divisions among public servants rather than fostering a shared civic culture. Collecting and publishing disaggregated workforce data organised by skin colour and ethnicity further embeds the practice of sorting people into racial and ethnic categories as a routine government function, reducing individuals to demographic proxies.

quotes.text: The public service has long made diversity and inclusion a core value and continuously reflects on the treatment of Black Canadians, Indigenous Peoples, and other individuals who face racial discrimination and other barriers in the workplace, and who are often underrepresented at the most senior levels of the public service.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the framing of the announcement: the institution explicitly identifies and categorises employees by skin colour and ethnic group membership as the basis for its workforce priorities.

quotes.text: Already, the government has released disaggregated datasets, providing first‑ever views into the composition of public service employees who self‑identify in Employment Equity sub-groups, such as Black or Métis for example.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the government's practice of collecting and publishing workforce data sorted by skin colour and ethnic identity, institutionalising demographic categorisation of employees.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Privy Council Office
incidents.short_description: The Clerk of the Privy Council issued a call to action directing federal public service leaders to appoint, sponsor, and recruit employees specifically on the basis of their skin colour and ethnicity. The document frames advancement decisions around demographic categories rather than individual merit and circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: On January 22, 2021, the Clerk of the Privy Council and Secretary to the Cabinet, Ian Shugart, issued a formal call to action directed at all federal public service leaders. The document calls on leaders to appoint employees to the Executive Group, sponsor employees for leadership roles, and recruit candidates specifically on the basis of skin colour and ethnicity. Leaders are directed to target individuals from particular communities defined by their skin colour and ethnicity for career development opportunities, leadership development programs, and recruitment. The document frames the federal public service's approach to advancement and hiring around demographic categories — particularly skin colour and ethnicity — rather than around individual character, abilities, and circumstances. The call to action instructs leaders to disaggregate data by demographic category and to make promotion and mobility decisions informed by that categorization. It also directs leaders to amplify certain voices specifically because of these demographic characteristics, and to bring grassroots identity-based networks into senior executive discussions as a matter of institutional policy. The document was published on the Government of Canada website through the Privy Council Office and applies across the entire federal public service.
incidents.expected_harm: When the federal government directs leaders to make appointment, sponsorship, and recruitment decisions based on skin colour and ethnicity, it reduces employees and candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their abilities and demonstrated merits. Employees who are not members of the designated demographic groups are passed over for advancement opportunities not because of their individual qualities or performance, but because of the group they belong to. This entrenches the practice of sorting people by immutable characteristics throughout the largest employer in Canada, normalizing the idea that a person's skin colour and ethnicity are relevant criteria for determining who receives career opportunities and institutional support. The policy sends the message to all federal public servants that their demographic identity, not their individual character and abilities, is a defining factor in how the institution sees and treats them. It undermines shared civic belonging within the public service by institutionally dividing employees along demographic lines rather than uniting them around common purpose and individual worth.

quotes.text: Appoint Indigenous employees and Black and other racialized employees to and within the Executive Group through career development and talent management
quotes.rationale: Directs leaders to make appointment decisions based on employees' skin colour and ethnicity rather than on individual merit alone.

quotes.text: Sponsor high-potential Indigenous employees and Black and other racialized employees to prepare them for leadership roles
quotes.rationale: Directs that sponsorship for leadership development be allocated on the basis of skin colour and ethnicity.

quotes.text: Recruit highly qualified candidates from Indigenous communities and Black and other racialized communities from across all regions of Canada
quotes.rationale: Instructs leaders to direct recruitment efforts specifically toward communities defined by skin colour and ethnicity, framing hiring as a demographic exercise.

quotes.text: The past several months have precipitated deep reflection on the unjust treatment of Black people, other racialized groups, and Indigenous peoples in our society.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the framing of the entire policy: the problem and the proposed solutions are defined by demographic categories of skin colour and ethnicity.

incidents.short_description: The Trent Central Student Association established the Freedom Lounge, a student lounge inside Trent University's Student Centre that is designated exclusively for students based on their skin colour and ethnicity. The space explicitly excludes students who do not identify within those categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The Trent Central Student Association (TCSA), the student association of Trent University in Peterborough, Ontario, established the Freedom Lounge in 2021 on the second floor of the Trent Student Centre. The lounge was created following a 2020 internal TCSA review of its policies and practices. The space is designated exclusively for students who identify as indigenous, black, brown, racialized, or as students of colour. Signage displayed in the lounge states explicitly: "This space is for, and celebrates, the lives of students who identify as Indigenous, Black, Brown, Racialized, and Students of Colour." The TCSA's own description of the lounge on its website describes the space as being for "BIPOC and Racialized students" to gather, build community, access resources, and participate in events and workshops. Non-members of these demographic categories are not the intended users of the space and are effectively excluded based on their skin colour or ethnic background. The lounge is located within a shared university student centre, a facility funded by and accessible to the full student population. In October 2022, members of the Society for Academic Freedom and Scholarship wrote an open letter to Trent University's Board of Governors challenging the lounge as a form of racial segregation, arguing that the university's institutional backing of the initiative undermined the principle of civic individualism.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are sorted and separated within a shared university facility based on their skin colour and ethnic background. Students whose appearance or background does not match the designated categories are excluded from a portion of their own student centre. This institutional arrangement teaches students that their skin colour and ethnicity determine which spaces they belong in, normalizing the practice of categorizing people by immutable characteristics rather than recognizing them as distinct individuals. Students excluded from the lounge receive a message from their student association — an institution funded by all students — that certain physical spaces within their university are not for them. This division undermines the development of a shared campus community and forecloses the kind of cross-cultural understanding that comes from students simply sharing common spaces. The arrangement also reinforces the logic that people should be grouped and evaluated based on demographic categories, which is the very logic that underlies discrimination of all kinds.

quotes.text: This space is for, and celebrates, the lives of students who identify as Indigenous, Black, Brown, Racialized, and Students of Colour.
quotes.rationale: This is the primary sign posted in the Freedom Lounge explicitly designating the space based on skin colour and ethnic identity, establishing the segregated nature of the space.

quotes.text: This space is a safe, comfortable, and accountable space for BIPOC and Racialized students to create community, feel empowered, celebrate their lived experiences, and honour their histories.
quotes.rationale: The TCSA's own official description of the lounge confirms the space is formally designated for students based on their demographic categorisation, not open to the full student body.

quotes.text: In August 2020, the TCSA conducted a review of our policies, training, advocacy, and community engagement practices to recognize the areas in which we can better support anti-racism initiatives as well as our BIPOC and Racialized membership.
quotes.rationale: Establishes institutional origin of the lounge as a deliberate policy decision by the TCSA following a formal review, indicating this is not an informal arrangement but an institutional one.

quotes.text: We understand that a group of Trent students have established a BIPOC only space from which whites are excluded--the so called 'freedom lounge'.
quotes.rationale: An external academic letter to Trent's Board of Governors characterises the space as one from which students are excluded based on their skin colour, corroborating the segregated nature of the arrangement.

incidents.short_description: A researcher publicly accused a university professor of fraud and pretending to be Indigenous, leading to a defamation lawsuit. The court found the accusations defamatory, ruling the professor had reasonable grounds for her identity claims based on organizational membership decisions at the time.
incidents.description_of_event: Between 2021 and 2022, Darryl Leroux, a researcher who studies false claims to Indigenous identity, made multiple public statements about Michelle Coupal, a University of Regina professor holding a Canada Research Chair. Leroux stated on social media and in presentations that Coupal was pretending to be Indigenous, had committed fraud, relied on forged documents, and had made millions of dollars from false identity claims. Coupal had begun identifying based on family information and received membership in the Bonnechere Algonquin First Nation in 2014 based on genealogical assessment by that organization. Following Leroux's statements, Coupal sued for defamation. In March 2026, a Saskatchewan judge ruled that Leroux's statements were defamatory, finding that Coupal had reasonable grounds for her identity claims based on the organizational decisions at the time, was not dishonest in her belief, and did not pretend to be Indigenous. The court awarded Coupal $70,000 in damages.
incidents.expected_harm: The public accusations of fraud and dishonesty damage an individual's professional reputation and personal standing. When individuals are publicly labeled as fraudulent based on contested interpretations of ancestry and organizational membership decisions, it subjects them to professional consequences and public stigma. The court decision itself may create uncertainty about how identity questions can be publicly discussed and researched, potentially constraining academic inquiry into membership criteria and genealogical claims. The case illustrates how disputes over ancestry documentation and organizational membership decisions can lead to public accusations that courts later find defamatory, creating ongoing uncertainty for individuals whose identity claims rest on institutional determinations that may change over time.

quotes.text: According to the court document, she was told she was eligible for membership on the basis that her grandfather's father's ancestral line could be traced to Thomas Lagarde, a man born in the early 1800s near Montreal, who was believed to be Algonquin.
quotes.rationale: Explains the basis on which Coupal received organizational membership, showing she relied on institutional determinations rather than self-declaration.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board launched a mandatory anti-racism training initiative during Bullying Awareness and Prevention Week (December 7-11, 2020), requiring students, families, and educators to participate in activities that categorize and address people based on their skin colour and ethnic background rather than as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: During Bullying Awareness and Prevention Week in December 2020, the Peel District School Board implemented a comprehensive anti-racism initiative titled "#ChooseACTION to Be Anti-Racist." The board provided resources and educational materials that required participation from students, educators, and families. The initiative focused explicitly on categorizing individuals according to their skin colour and ethnic backgrounds, with specific attention to what the board termed "anti-Black racism, anti-Indigenous racism, anti-Muslim hate and Islamophobia and antisemitism." The program materials instructed families to discuss racism with children by focusing on their skin colour and ethnic identity, provided tip sheets on "how to talk to your child about racism" that emphasized these categories, and encouraged families to create pledges addressing their complicity in racism based on these group identities. The educational materials stated that "hate incidents informed by anti-Black racism and misrepresented as bullying will not be condoned," suggesting different treatment protocols based on the skin colour or ethnic background of those involved. Resources were distributed to all schools with expectations that educators would use these materials throughout the year. The board framed this approach as necessary transformational change, stating that commitment to anti-racism requires "acknowledging racism, validating difference, and interrupting differential treatment based on race" while simultaneously organizing educational activities around these very categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Students will be conditioned to view themselves and their peers primarily through the lens of skin colour and ethnic categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualities. This approach will undermine the development of relationships based on shared interests, character, and common humanity. Children will internalize the message that their skin colour or ethnic background determines their experiences, perspectives, and even their moral standing in society. The program creates a framework where incidents involving students are assessed differently depending on the skin colour of those involved, potentially resulting in unequal treatment and justice outcomes. Students from all backgrounds will learn that demographic categories should be the primary lens through which they understand social interactions, which will hinder the development of critical thinking skills and individual assessment. The emphasis on collective guilt and complicity based on group membership will foster resentment rather than understanding. By teaching children to view every interaction through a framework of group-based power dynamics rather than individual circumstances, the program will impede the development of genuine empathy and the ability to evaluate situations based on specific facts and context. The initiative will create divisions among students who might otherwise form friendships across demographic lines, as they are taught to see fundamental differences rather than common humanity.

quotes.text: Resources provided to schools support how anti-Black racism and other forms of systemic discrimination outlined in the Ministry Review can be addressed and eliminated. Specific learning related to anti-Black racism, anti-Indigenous racism, anti-Muslim hate and Islamophobia and antisemitism are featured as we #ChooseACTION to Be Anti-Racist.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the board's approach of organizing educational initiatives around categorizing students based on skin colour and ethnic/religious backgrounds rather than addressing bullying through individual assessment.

incidents.short_description: Peel District School Board issued a statement rejecting race neutrality and framing bullying incidents through a lens of anti-Black racism, directing students, staff, and families to view interactions through racial categories.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 7, 2020, during Bullying Awareness and Prevention Week, the Peel District School Board issued a communication signed by Colleen Russell-Rawlins and the senior team. The statement declared that the board would reject colour-blindness and race neutrality as approaches to addressing bullying and discrimination. The communication specifically reframed certain bullying incidents as manifestations of anti-Black racism, stating that "hate incidents informed by anti-Black racism and misrepresented as bullying will not be condoned." The board directed students, staff, and families to engage with materials promoting this framework and committed to eliminating what it characterized as systemic discrimination through an anti-racist lens that explicitly rejects treating students without regard to racial categories.
incidents.expected_harm: By directing educators, students, and families to reject colour-blindness and race neutrality, the board encourages viewing and categorizing individuals through racial demographics rather than as distinct persons. This approach trains students and staff to interpret interpersonal conflicts and bullying incidents primarily through a racial lens, potentially obscuring the individual circumstances, motivations, and character of those involved. Students may learn to view themselves and their peers as representatives of racial groups rather than as individuals with unique qualities and experiences. The framework risks reducing complex interpersonal dynamics to racial categories, undermining the development of genuine understanding between individuals and discouraging evaluation based on character, behaviour, and individual circumstances. This institutional directive to view students through demographic categories contradicts principles of singular worth and may foster division rather than common humanity in the school environment.

quotes.text: In order to be anti-racist, we must challenge ideas of color-blindness or race neutrality.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly rejects evaluating students without regard to racial categories, directly contradicting principles of singular worth and individual evaluation.

incidents.short_description: McMaster University launched a cohort hiring initiative restricting up to 12 tenure-track faculty positions to applicants who self-identify as persons of a specific ancestry, evaluating candidates based on demographic characteristics rather than individual merit alone.
incidents.description_of_event: On November 23, 2020, McMaster University announced a cohort hiring initiative seeking up to 12 tenure-track and tenured faculty appointments across all six faculties. The initiative was described as advancing what the university termed academic excellence while requiring that successful applicants self-identify as persons of a specific ancestry and lived experience. The job postings, advertised in Canada and internationally, explicitly stated that the university seeks qualified individuals who self-identify based on ancestry and lived experience. The initiative was positioned as the first under the university's Strategic Equity and Excellence Recruitment and Retention program and aligned with the institution's strategy toward what it described as inclusive excellence. Applicants were required to submit a statement describing contributions made or planned in relation to advancing institutional priorities around group-based categories in teaching, research, or service, including lived experiences and understandings related to particular cultural groups. The positions spanned multiple disciplines including business, engineering, health sciences, humanities, science, and social sciences, with the Faculty of Health Sciences advertising at least one position in March 2022. The university framed the initiative as addressing what it characterized as underrepresentation in the professoriate, attributing this to what administrators described as cumulative effects of biases and inequities rather than differences in individual qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring practice will result in qualified academics being excluded from consideration for faculty positions based solely on their ancestry and lived experience rather than their individual qualifications, research excellence, teaching ability, and scholarly contributions. The restriction evaluates candidates through the lens of demographic categories rather than assessing each person's unique capabilities, achievements, and potential to contribute to academic discourse. Individuals who do not self-identify with the specified ancestry will be denied equal opportunity to compete for these positions regardless of their individual merit, expertise in relevant research areas, or capacity to advance knowledge in their fields. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated accomplishments rather than reducing them to demographic proxies. By explicitly restricting eligibility based on ancestry, the initiative creates a system where access to academic positions depends on group membership rather than individual excellence, contradicting the university's stated commitment to recognizing the singular worth of each person. This approach also compromises intellectual diversity by prioritizing demographic characteristics over the diversity of thought, varied life experiences, and demonstrated capabilities that emerge from evaluating individuals based on their unique perspectives and scholarly contributions.

quotes.text: McMaster University is pleased to announce a cohort hiring initiative of up to twelve appointments of emerging and established academics and scholars who will contribute to the advancement of Black academic excellence across all six McMaster Faculties.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core nature of the hiring initiative: positions restricted to a specific demographic group across the entire university.

incidents.short_description: The Limestone District School Board adopted an Equity Action Plan that commits to recruitment practices designed to increase the number of applicants selected for interviews based on skin colour, with the goal of achieving staff demographics that mirror student and community populations.
incidents.description_of_event: In November 2020, the Limestone District School Board adopted a three-year Equity Action Plan (2020-2023) that establishes human resource practices explicitly focused on demographic representation. The plan commits the board to "increase the number of Black, Indigenous & People of Colour applicants selected for interviews" and states that the organization should work toward "fostering representative staff and student populations." The plan frames demographic diversity as beneficial to organizational effectiveness and states that "it is important that recruitment efforts reach a broad, diversified applicant pool in order that our employee demographics mirror our student and community populations to the extent possible." The board also committed to adding voluntary self-identification options to application systems and exploring ways to attract applicants through "diverse job boards and other means to attract a diversified applicant pool." The plan establishes racialized advisory groups for students, staff, and community members, and mandates anti-racism training across all departments. Schools were required to establish equity goals in their learning plans addressing anti-racism.
incidents.expected_harm: The plan creates a system where applicants' skin colour and ancestry become factors in determining who receives interview opportunities, treating individuals as representatives of demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their individual qualifications, experience, and capabilities. By committing to increase the number of applicants selected for interviews based on demographic characteristics, the board establishes a preference system that will advantage some candidates and disadvantage others based on immutable traits rather than merit. This approach reduces individuals to demographic proxies and creates unequal treatment in the hiring process, where similarly qualified candidates will receive different consideration based on their ancestry or skin colour. The policy also signals to current staff that their demographic characteristics are viewed as relevant to their professional role, potentially creating an environment where individuals are valued for their demographic category rather than their unique contributions. The requirement for demographic data collection and the establishment of separate advisory groups organized by skin colour reinforces the categorization of people by immutable traits rather than fostering genuine community based on shared purpose and individual merit.

quotes.text: Explore ways to increase the number of racially diverse applicants selected for interviews through diverse job boards and other means to attract a diversified applicant pool
quotes.rationale: This quote directly establishes that the board commits to increasing the number of applicants selected for interviews based on demographic characteristics, creating a preference system in hiring.

quotes.text: Increase the number of Black, Indigenous & People of Colour applicants selected for interviews
quotes.rationale: This action item explicitly commits to increasing interview selection rates based on skin colour and ancestry, demonstrating demographic preferences in the hiring process.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board implemented a policy allowing students who identify as belonging to certain demographic groups to bypass the lottery system for admission to elite academic programs, while other students who meet admission criteria must enter a random selection process.
incidents.description_of_event: In November 2020, the Peel District School Board announced a procedural change to admissions for its Regional Learning Choices Programs, which include International Baccalaureate and advanced courses in science, technology, arts, and music. The board determined that students who identify as belonging to specific demographic categories and meet admission criteria would bypass the regular selection lottery and gain direct entry to specialty programs of their choice. This change applied to applications for fall 2021 enrollment. The board characterized this as a necessary step to eliminate what it called systemic inequities, noting that while certain students comprised 10 per cent of secondary enrollment, they represented less than 2 per cent of RLCP enrollment. The policy creates a two-tier admissions process where some qualified applicants gain guaranteed admission based on demographic self-identification, while other qualified applicants must submit to a lottery with uncertain outcomes. The board's interim director stated this was the first step, with plans to review the entire admissions process.
incidents.expected_harm: This policy reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their unique qualities, abilities, and demonstrated achievements. Students who meet academic criteria but do not belong to preferred demographic groups face uncertainty through a lottery system, while others with identical qualifications receive guaranteed admission based on how they identify. This approach undermines the principle that advancement should reflect individual merit and creates a system where demographic category membership, rather than individual character and capability, determines access to educational opportunities. The policy may foster resentment among students denied guaranteed admission despite meeting the same academic standards, potentially damaging the shared civic belonging that comes from institutions treating all individuals fairly based on their demonstrated abilities. Students admitted through demographic preferences may face questions about whether they earned their places through individual achievement, while students excluded from guaranteed admission may perceive the system as fundamentally unfair, eroding trust in institutional fairness.

quotes.text: Starting now, on applications for fall 2021, students who identify as Black, African, Métis, First Nations or Inuit and meet admission criteria, will bypass the regular selection lottery to enrol in the specialty program of their choice, according to a report released late Tuesday by the Peel District School Board.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core policy change creating a two-tier admissions system based on demographic self-identification rather than individual merit alone.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Space Agency
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Space Agency posted a Communications Officer job that explicitly stated selection could be limited to applicants based on their demographic characteristics, including skin colour, disability, and sex. Applicants were also encouraged to self-identify their demographic group membership as part of the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Space Agency (CSA) posted a Communications Officer position (IS-03, $71,599–$77,368) based in Saint-Hubert, Québec, with a closing date of December 1, 2020. The posting stated that selection could be limited to members of designated employment equity groups: indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, people categorized as visible minorities based on skin colour, and women. A linked government page defined these groups in detail, categorizing people according to their skin colour and race, disability status, and sex. Applicants were explicitly encouraged to indicate whether they belong to one of these designated groups when applying. The employment equity information page directed candidates to self-declare their membership in demographic categories as part of the application process. This means that a candidate's skin colour, ancestry, disability status, or sex could determine whether their application is even considered, with the pool of eligible candidates potentially narrowed to those who self-identify within these categories rather than all qualified individuals being evaluated on their abilities and experience alone.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are assessed not only on their skills, experience, and demonstrated abilities, but on their demographic characteristics — including skin colour, ancestry, disability status, and sex. When selection is restricted to designated groups, individuals who do not belong to those categories are excluded from consideration regardless of their qualifications. This reduces people to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique abilities and character. The practice also pressures applicants to label and self-categorize based on immutable traits in order to compete for public service positions. It normalizes the institutional sorting of people by demographic characteristics and undermines a hiring culture grounded in individual merit, sending the message that who you are born as matters more than what you can do.

quotes.text: Selection may be limited to members of the following Employment Equity groups: Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, women
quotes.rationale: This is the core statement establishing that selection can be restricted based on demographic characteristics including skin colour, ancestry, disability, and sex rather than merit alone.

quotes.text: The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the Canadians we serve. We promote employment equity and encourage you to indicate if you belong to one of the designated groups when you apply.
quotes.rationale: This passage shows applicants are actively encouraged to self-identify by demographic category as part of the application process, normalizing the labelling of individuals by immutable traits.

quotes.text: A person in a visible minority group is someone (other than an Indigenous person as defined above) who is non-white in colour/race, regardless of place of birth.
quotes.rationale: This definition shows that the government categorizes people explicitly by skin colour as a basis for employment preference, directly illustrating how immutable traits are used to sort job applicants.

quotes.text: More information on employment equity and how to self-declare as a member of a designated group
quotes.rationale: This confirms that applicants are directed to formally self-declare their demographic group membership as part of the hiring process, making skin colour, ancestry, disability, and sex relevant inputs to their application.

quotes.text: An Indigenous person is a North American Indian or a member of a First Nation, Métis or Inuit.
quotes.rationale: Illustrates how the government defines and categorizes people by ancestry and ethnicity as a basis for employment equity designation.

incidents.short_description: The Halton Catholic District School Board published an anti-racism page that frames racism as a problem defined by the skin colour of those affected, directing students and families toward resources organised by demographic identity rather than treating discrimination as a shared human concern.
incidents.description_of_event: The Halton Catholic District School Board published an Anti-Racism page on its website presenting its approach to addressing racism in schools and at the system level. The board's framing centres on the concept of being 'anti-racist' and becoming 'accomplices to dismantle systemic inequity,' language drawn from a particular ideological tradition that categorises individuals and institutions primarily by demographic identity. At the school level, the board singles out specific groups by skin colour and ethnicity — naming particular communities and forms of discrimination tied to those categories — rather than addressing discrimination as a violation of individual dignity that can harm any person. The board also references a professional development session held at the start of the 2020–2021 school year focused specifically on addressing concerns about one group defined by skin colour, framing the problem and its solution in identity-based terms. The 'Supports for Students and Families' section directs families to resources organised by demographic identity, including a helpline specifically for youth of one skin colour. The overall approach treats discrimination as a phenomenon defined by the demographic categories of those involved, rather than as a wrong that violates the inherent dignity of every individual regardless of background.
incidents.expected_harm: Students and families are sorted and addressed as members of demographic groups rather than as individuals with unique dignity and circumstances. By framing anti-racism work around specific skin colour categories, the board teaches students that institutions understand and respond to people primarily through the lens of their demographic identity. This undermines the principle that every person deserves recognition as an individual and normalises the idea that one's skin colour determines which resources, supports, and institutional attention one receives. The identity-based approach divides the school community rather than uniting it around the shared principle that mistreating any person based on assumptions about their background is wrong. Students from groups not named in the board's framing receive the message that their experiences of mistreatment are of lesser institutional concern, while students from named groups are defined in relation to their skin colour rather than their individual character and worth. The approach forecloses opportunities to build genuine understanding across the school community by framing discrimination as a group-versus-group issue rather than a universal human concern.

quotes.text: At the school level, we will keep challenging all staff and students to critically examine and address systemic injustice and inequity in society, and we will continue encouraging students to speak up on issues around anti-racism including anti-Black and anti-Indigenous racism, anti-Islamophobia, anti-Semitism, xenophobia and many other forms of oppression.
quotes.rationale: The board frames discrimination through named demographic categories, treating racism as a problem defined by the identity of specific groups rather than as a violation of individual dignity applicable to all.

quotes.text: At the beginning of the 2020-2021 school year, we held a professional development session for HCDSB staff on Addressing Anti-Black Racism in Schools.
quotes.rationale: Confirms that staff training was organised around a single demographic group defined by skin colour, rather than addressing discrimination as a universal concern affecting all students.

quotes.text: Dealing with traumatic events that include acts of hate such as anti-Black racism can be very emotionally taxing, most especially on youth. Please do not hesitate to contact your School Principal if your son or daughter is struggling and requires support from our school social worker.
quotes.rationale: Student support is framed around a specific skin colour group, implying that the board's concern and support structures are oriented by demographic identity rather than individual need.

quotes.text: Black Youth Helpline: 1-833-294-8650
quotes.rationale: The board directs families to a helpline defined by the skin colour of its intended users, organising mental health support access by demographic category rather than individual need.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada
incidents.short_description: The Government of Canada launched the Black Entrepreneurship Program in September 2020, making loans and business support available only to entrepreneurs based on their skin colour.
incidents.description_of_event: In September 2020, the federal government announced the Black Entrepreneurship Program, committing $265 million over four years in partnership with financial institutions and organizations. The program restricts eligibility for loans (between $25,000 and $250,000) and business support services to individuals who self-identify as having a particular skin colour or to businesses with majority ownership by people of that skin colour. The program is administered by the Federation of African Canadian Economics (FACE) in partnership with the Business Development Bank of Canada and other financial institutions. By July 2022, more than 16,000 entrepreneurs had applied, but only 173 loans totalling $16 million had been approved, representing an approval rate below 15 percent. The program also includes $92 million for organizations providing coaching and mentorship services restricted to entrepreneurs based on skin colour. Applicants who were denied loans reported receiving no explanation for rejection and found no functional appeals process despite contractual requirements for transparent, independent review mechanisms.
incidents.expected_harm: The program divides Canadians by categorizing them according to their skin colour rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their business capabilities, financial needs, and entrepreneurial merit. By restricting access to government-funded loans and business support based on immutable characteristics, the program denies opportunity to qualified entrepreneurs who do not possess the required skin colour. This approach reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than assessing their unique circumstances, business plans, or demonstrated needs. The lack of transparency in loan decisions and absence of a functioning appeals process further harms applicants by denying them fair evaluation and recourse. The program establishes a precedent for government institutions to allocate economic resources based on grouping people by physical characteristics, undermining the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on individual character, abilities, and circumstances.

quotes.text: The Black Entrepreneurship Program (BEP) is a partnership between the Government of Canada, Black-led business organizations, and financial institutions. With an investment of up to $265 million over four years, it will help Black Canadian business owners and entrepreneurs grow their businesses and succeed now and into the future.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the program's existence, government partnership, funding amount, and that eligibility is restricted based on skin colour.

incidents.short_description: The Globe and Mail published a feature article selecting and amplifying booksellers specifically chosen based on their skin colour and ethnicity, framing book recommendations through a racial and identitarian lens rather than treating readers and authors as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: On August 21, 2020, The Globe and Mail published a feature article titled 'Five Black and Indigenous booksellers on reading race in Canada,' written by Josie Kao. The article was prompted by a surge in demand for anti-racism literature following the death of George Floyd in May 2020. The Globe and Mail explicitly selected the five booksellers featured in the article based on their skin colour and ethnicity, framing their expertise and recommendations through those characteristics. The article presents book recommendations from booksellers in Winnipeg, Vancouver (two), Montreal, and Ottawa, and the recommendations are similarly framed through the skin colour and ethnicity of authors rather than the literary or intellectual merits of the books themselves. The institutional framing treats the booksellers' skin colour and ethnicity as the defining qualification for their inclusion and the basis for the value of their perspectives. Notably, one of the featured booksellers — Hilary Atleo of Iron Dog Books in Vancouver — directly identifies and critiques this tendency, observing that recommending books because they are written by an indigenous author rather than because they are great art becomes 'a kind of moral rectitude type of thing,' and that books risk being treated as though they 'exist for educational purposes' based solely on the author's identity. Despite this internal critique, the article's overall structure and premise rest on selecting voices and framing recommendations according to demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Readers are directed toward books primarily on the basis of the skin colour and ethnicity of their authors, rather than on the quality, relevance, or insight of the works themselves. This approach treats authors as representatives of demographic categories rather than as individual writers with distinct voices, ideas, and craft. It signals to readers that the appropriate reason to engage with a book is the author's identity, embedding the habit of sorting cultural and intellectual life by race and ethnicity. The Globe and Mail, as a nationally prominent media institution, normalises and amplifies this identitarian framing, reinforcing the idea that Canadians should sort their reading — and by extension their intellectual and civic lives — along demographic lines rather than engaging with ideas and art on their own terms.

incidents.institution_name: Canadian Media Guild > Joint Committee on Equity, Diversity and Inclusion
incidents.short_description: Canadian Media Guild members delivered an open letter to CBC senior management calling for hiring and advancement decisions to be made based on employees' demographic characteristics, including skin colour, indigeneity, and other immutable traits.
incidents.description_of_event: On July 7, 2020, members of the Canadian Media Guild (CMG) sitting on the Joint Committee on Equity, Diversity and Inclusion delivered an open letter to CBC senior management, signed by 494 current and former CBC employees. The letter urged the corporation to take a series of actions framed around dismantling what it called 'structural racism' at the CBC. Among the demands, the letter called on CBC to ensure that employees belonging to specific demographic categories have decision-making authority at all levels of the corporation, from editorial to management. The letter also called for CBC's Journalistic Standards and Practices to be reformed, including provisions that would permit employees to publicly advocate for particular social and political movements associated with their demographic identity — such as attending political rallies or publicly endorsing specific campaigns — without facing discipline. The letter characterised CBC's editorial standards as reflecting a 'white, middle-class worldview,' framing neutrality and objectivity in journalism as inherently aligned with a particular demographic group. The letter was written collaboratively by a group of employees described as 'BIPOC and allied employees' and was posted publicly on the CMG website.
incidents.expected_harm: When institutions commit to ensuring that people from specific demographic groups hold decision-making positions, they shift the basis for advancement and authority from individual character, ability, and merit to demographic identity. This reduces individuals to proxies for their group memberships rather than evaluating them as distinct people with unique qualities. It also entrenches a view of the workplace as a collection of demographic factions rather than a community of individuals united by shared professional purpose. The demand that employees be allowed to publicly advocate for particular political and social movements associated with their demographic identity, without professional consequence, further ties individual professional standing to group membership rather than to conduct, judgment, and individual responsibility. For a public broadcaster, embedding demographic-based decision-making into editorial structures undermines the foundation of trustworthy, independent journalism that serves all Canadians as individuals.

quotes.text: Ensure BIPOC employees have decision-making authority at all levels of the corporation, from editorial to management
quotes.rationale: This demand explicitly calls for allocating decision-making authority based on demographic categories rather than individual qualifications or merit, which directly conflicts with the principle of evaluating people as individuals.

quotes.text: Do not discipline employees for publicly defending their humanity or that of others. Just as an LGBTQ employee should never be told they can't attend Pride, a Black employee should never be told they can't publicly support Black Lives Matter, and an Indigenous employee should never be told they can't attend a MMIWG rally.
quotes.rationale: This demand links permissible employee conduct to demographic identity, suggesting that professional standards should vary depending on a person's group membership rather than applying equally to all individuals.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board published a comprehensive strategy document that organises programs, resources, staffing, and institutional structures around students' skin colour rather than treating all students as individuals with distinct needs. The board created dedicated programs, coaching positions, advisory committees, and a new Centre of Excellence restricted to students of one skin colour.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Toronto District School Board (TDSB) published a document in July 2020 outlining its strategies, actions, policies, and research related to student achievement and racism. The document reveals a broad institutional framework that consistently categorises students, families, and staff by skin colour, directing programs, resources, and institutional structures based on that demographic characteristic.

The board established a dedicated Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement, described as the first of its kind in Canadian public education, staffed with 20.5 positions including social workers, counsellors, and graduation coaches — all specifically allocated based on students' skin colour. The board also created five graduation coaches designated for students of one skin colour, organised parent workshops restricted to parents and guardians of one skin colour, and established Summer Programs for student well-being restricted by skin colour.

The board's Multi-Year Strategic Plan includes action plans with goals structured explicitly around students' skin colour across multiple domains: early years programming, academic pathways and transitions, special education, and suspensions and expulsions. The plan includes an Africentric Pre-Kindergarten program and directs educators to develop curriculum and resources specifically for students of one skin colour.

A Black Student Achievement Advisory Committee, established in 2016, was given direct influence over board policies, programs, and services on behalf of one skin colour community. The board also collects and reports race-based demographic data to drive these targeted institutional interventions.

Across all of these elements, students are evaluated, grouped, and served primarily through the lens of their skin colour rather than as individuals with unique circumstances, abilities, and needs.
incidents.expected_harm: Students who do not belong to the designated skin colour group are excluded from specific programs, resources, graduation coaching, and institutional supports that are distributed based on demographic category rather than individual need or demonstrated circumstance. Students in the designated group are institutionally defined and approached primarily through their skin colour rather than as individuals, reinforcing the idea that their identity and trajectory are determined by their demographic category. Parents and caregivers are sorted into skin-colour-based engagement channels, preventing the formation of a unified school community where families connect around shared goals for their children. Teachers and administrators are trained and evaluated through a framework that emphasises demographic categorisation, normalising the practice of assessing students and families through racial lenses rather than individual ones. The overall institutional structure entrenches the habit of sorting people by skin colour as the primary means of delivering education and support, undermining the recognition of each student's singular worth and foreclosing the shared civic belonging that a common school system is meant to build.

quotes.text: As part of this commitment, we are focused in particular on supporting Black student achievement. The TDSB is equally committed to combating anti-Black racism.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the board's institutional commitment is explicitly structured around students' skin colour as the primary organising principle.

quotes.text: TDSB will create and fund a new TDSB Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement – the first of its kind in public education in Canada.
quotes.rationale: Documents the creation of a major institutional structure — staffed and funded — that is explicitly restricted to students of one skin colour.

quotes.text: The new Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement will be supported by 20.5 staff positions, including a social worker, child and youth counsellor, and additional graduation coaches, all focused on supports for Black students.
quotes.rationale: Confirms significant institutional resources — staffing, counselling, coaching — are allocated exclusively based on students' skin colour rather than individual need.

quotes.text: Five Graduation Coaches for Black Students
quotes.rationale: Dedicated graduation coaching positions are restricted by skin colour, illustrating how individual student support roles are distributed on a demographic basis.

quotes.text: Parent Workshops – Talking about Race and Navigating the School System for Black Parents/Guardians
quotes.rationale: Parent engagement programming is organised and restricted based on skin colour, creating demographically segregated family engagement channels.

quotes.text: Summer Programs for Black Students' Well-being
quotes.rationale: Extracurricular and well-being programming is restricted to students of one skin colour rather than offered to all students based on individual need.

incidents.short_description: The Waterloo Region Record published a curated list of children's books promoting frameworks that categorize children by immutable demographic characteristics rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 26, 2020, The Waterloo Region Record published an article by a Toronto District School Board teacher-librarian recommending eight children's books for summer reading. The article presented books explicitly designed to teach children to view themselves and others through categorical frameworks based on demographic characteristics. Several recommended titles focus on teaching children to identify people by immutable traits and to interpret experiences through those categorical lenses. The recommendations were framed as helping readers 'learn a little more about each other' and 'disrupt stereotypes,' but the approach centres demographic categorization rather than individual character assessment. The teacher-librarian described her role as curating books 'responsive and reflective' of communities through these categorical frameworks.
incidents.expected_harm: Children exposed to these materials will be trained to perceive themselves and others primarily through demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique qualities. This categorical thinking undermines the development of genuine understanding between people by substituting demographic assumptions for actual knowledge of individual character, capabilities, and circumstances. Young readers learning to interpret all social phenomena through these frameworks will be less equipped to evaluate people based on their demonstrated qualities and more inclined to reduce individuals to demographic proxies. The institutional endorsement by a major regional newspaper lends authority to approaches that prioritize categorical identity over individual assessment, potentially influencing curriculum choices and reading selections across educational institutions in the region.

incidents.short_description: Holland Bloorview Kids Rehabilitation Hospital published a family resource tipsheet that promoted a perspective treating people as representatives of demographic groups rather than as individuals, organizing resource recommendations around categorizing people based on immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: In June 2020, Holland Bloorview Kids Rehabilitation Hospital, a pediatric rehabilitation hospital in Toronto, created and published a family tipsheet titled "How to Talk to Your Child About Racism." The tipsheet was later updated in February 2022 and remained available on the hospital's website. The document provided definitions, resources, and guidance for families discussing race relations with children. The tipsheet's framework centered on categorizing people by demographic labels, repeatedly using terms that sorted individuals into groups based on skin colour and ancestry. The hospital organized its recommendations around the premise that understanding challenges requires viewing people through these demographic categories. The resource list included materials specifically organized by these same demographic classifications, and the hospital presented this categorical approach as the foundation for families to discuss mutual respect and acceptance. Rather than emphasizing individual character and unique qualities, the institutional communication reinforced a framework where people's immutable characteristics served as primary identifiers in healthcare and family education contexts.
incidents.expected_harm: Families seeking guidance from a trusted healthcare institution will be directed toward a framework that reduces individuals to demographic categories. Children learning about respect and human dignity through these hospital-endorsed materials will be taught to view themselves and others primarily through immutable characteristics rather than individual qualities. Parents will receive institutional endorsement for organizing their children's understanding of other people around categorization systems based on ancestry and appearance. The hospital's authority as a healthcare provider lends weight to this approach, normalizing the practice of sorting people into groups and treating group membership as a primary lens for understanding human interactions. Families will integrate this categorical thinking into how they discuss fairness, respect, and human worth with their children during formative years. The emphasis on demographic classification over individual evaluation will shape children's developing understanding of how to assess and relate to other people, potentially limiting their capacity to recognize the unique worth of each person they encounter.

quotes.text: Parenting when BIPOC (Black, Indigenous, People of Color)
quotes.rationale: Illustrates how the hospital organized its resources around demographic classifications, treating parenting challenges as fundamentally defined by group membership rather than individual family circumstances.

quotes.text: We believe it is important for everyone to understand the challenges faced by parents who identify as being Black, Indigenous, People of Color (BIPOC) and for that reason have included those perspectives.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the hospital's framework of organizing understanding around demographic categories, treating group identification as the primary lens for comprehending challenges rather than individual circumstances.

incidents.short_description: Earl Haig Public School published a statement and resource list promoting race-conscious parenting approaches that encourage adults to emphasize children's skin colour and racial categories in conversations, contradicting the school's stated goal of dismantling systemic racism by reinforcing racial classification systems.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 14, 2020, Earl Haig Public School's school council published a 'Black Lives Matter Statement + Resources' on their website. The statement declared 'At Earl Haig, Black Lives Matter' and provided extensive resources for families. The recommended materials included books, videos, and websites that instruct parents to proactively label children by skin colour and racial categories from toddler age onward. The resources specifically advocate against 'colorblind' approaches and instead promote 'race-conscious' parenting that emphasizes noticing and naming racial differences. One linked resource provides '100 race-conscious things you can say to your child' with sample scripts like describing people as having 'White skin, like us' or 'brown skin' and teaching children as young as two to classify others by these categories. Another resource argues that teaching children to ignore skin colour differences 'will not stop the spread of racism' and that silence about race 'removes the opportunity for children to learn about diversity from their parents.' The materials train adults to preemptively introduce racial labeling into everyday activities like reading books, going to the grocery store, or meeting neighbors, turning routine interactions into opportunities to emphasize immutable traits.
incidents.expected_harm: These resources train families to view children primarily through the lens of immutable characteristics rather than as distinct individuals. By instructing adults to repeatedly label and categorize young children by skin colour in everyday situations, the approach embeds racial thinking into children's cognitive development from their earliest years. Children learn to automatically sort people into demographic boxes rather than recognizing each person's unique qualities, experiences, and character. The constant emphasis on noticing and naming racial differences contradicts the goal of reducing prejudice, as it trains young minds to see superficial categories as fundamental aspects of identity. Parents following these scripts will teach their children that skin colour is among the most important things to observe and discuss about others, rather than fostering genuine curiosity about individuals' ideas, interests, kindness, or capabilities. The approach also places children in uncomfortable positions where they are expected to perform racial analysis rather than simply playing and learning alongside their peers. By making race a central organizing principle of how children understand themselves and others, these educational practices undermine the development of authentic human connection based on shared experiences and individual character. The institutional endorsement signals to families that the school evaluates students through demographic categories rather than as unique learners with individual needs and potential.

quotes.text: Here are some resources to share with your personal communities and families, especially the children and young people in your lives. While there are many resources out there, we have prioritized those written and developed by Black authors and activists.
quotes.rationale: Shows the school is actively curating and recommending specific resources to families based on authors' skin colour rather than content quality or alignment with treating children as individuals.

incidents.short_description: Kingston Frontenac Public Library published a list of anti-racism resources for children that included books categorizing and labeling people based on their skin colour and ethnic background.
incidents.description_of_event: Kingston Frontenac Public Library created a curated list titled "Anti-Racism Resources for Kids" that recommended books for children. The list was presented as providing resources to "encourage inclusiveness, spark conversations, and help answer difficult questions about current events." Multiple books on the list describe and categorize people based on their skin colour, with titles such as "The Colors of Us" described as "a positive and affirming look at skin color" where a seven-year-old discovers "brown comes in many different shades."
