Another book, "Not My Idea: A Book About Whiteness," is described as addressing a statement "In our family, we don't see color" by having the child protagonist note "he sees the colors plain enough." The list includes books that frame children's identities and experiences primarily through the lens of immutable characteristics like skin colour and ethnic heritage, rather than encouraging institutions and individuals to evaluate people based on their individual character and capabilities.
incidents.expected_harm: Children exposed to these materials will be taught to view themselves and others primarily through the lens of skin colour and ethnic categories rather than as distinct individuals. This approach reinforces the practice of sorting people into demographic groups and attributing shared characteristics based on immutable traits. Young readers will learn to foreground differences based on physical appearance rather than developing the capacity to recognize the common humanity that connects all people. The materials normalize institutional practices that categorize people based on demographic characteristics, potentially shaping how children understand belonging and evaluation in society. By presenting identity primarily through immutable traits rather than individual character, abilities, and choices, these resources work against the development of systems where people are recognized for their unique qualities and contributions.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board established a dedicated Centre of Excellence that restricts programs, resources, and engagement opportunities to students based on their skin colour. The centre organises scholarships, academic programs, and family events on a racially segregated basis.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board approved the creation of the Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement in June 2020. The centre was officially launched in June 2021 and opened its physical location at Winston Churchill Collegiate Institute in Scarborough in June 2022. The centre is explicitly designed to serve students based on their skin colour, and its programs, scholarships, and family engagement activities are directed at and restricted to individuals identified by that demographic category. Examples include the 'Building Black Futures in Architecture Scholarship,' an 'Africentric early years family event,' and a numeracy webinar described as 'designed for Black families and caregivers.' The centre also runs a 'Black Student Summer Leadership Program.' Resources and community engagement activities are similarly framed around skin colour as the basis for participation. The institution has made the categorisation of students and families by skin colour a central and formalised part of its programming structure, establishing a permanent, publicly funded body to operationalise this approach.
incidents.expected_harm: Students and families are sorted and served by a publicly funded institution on the basis of their skin colour rather than their individual needs, abilities, or circumstances. Scholarships and leadership programs that restrict eligibility by skin colour deny equal access to students who do not fit the designated demographic category, regardless of their individual merit or need. Family events and engagement sessions structured around skin colour exclude parents and caregivers from accessing educational support based solely on their demographic identity. The institutionalisation of this approach within a permanent, named body normalises the practice of categorising students by immutable characteristics, reinforcing the idea that skin colour is a defining feature of a student's educational identity. This undermines the recognition of each student as a distinct individual and works against the shared civic belonging that comes from treating all students and families as equally valued members of a common school community.

quotes.text: The Centre of Excellence for Black Student Achievement is the first of its kind in public education in Canada. The Centre of Excellence is dedicated to improving the experiences and outcomes for Black students and aims to be responsive to the voices of Black communities who continue to advocate for systemic change within educational institutions and for dismantling anti-Black racism at the TDSB.
quotes.rationale: Establishes that the centre's mandate is explicitly organised around serving a demographic group defined by skin colour, framing its entire purpose around racial categorisation.

quotes.text: Webinar Wednesday: 123s of Numeracy – Making Math Make Sense: Practical Strategies for Black Parents and Caregivers
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that academic support programming, including basic numeracy guidance, is explicitly restricted to families based on their skin colour.

quotes.text: We are excited to invite you to our virtual session designed for Black families and caregivers of students in Grades...
quotes.rationale: Confirms that access to the webinar is framed as being designed for and directed at a specific skin-colour demographic, excluding other families.

incidents.institution_name: Greater Essex County District School Board (GECDSB)
incidents.short_description: The Greater Essex County District School Board sent a letter to all families responding to the death of George Floyd that singled out students of certain backgrounds as the ones most likely experiencing emotional distress, implying that reactions to the event were defined by skin colour rather than individual circumstance.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 1, 2020, the Greater Essex County District School Board sent a letter to all GECDSB families addressing the death of George Floyd and the protests that followed. The letter expressed concern for students and families experiencing difficult emotions. However, the letter specifically identified students who identify as African, Black and Caribbean and their families as those for whom emotions would be most forefront, effectively sorting students and families by skin colour and heritage when characterizing who would be most affected by the event. The board framed emotional responses to the killing as particularly belonging to people of certain backgrounds, rather than treating the event as a human tragedy to which any person might respond with grief, fear, or outrage. The letter was sent to all families but made a demographic distinction in describing whose feelings were most understandable or most at the fore, embedding a race-essentialist assumption into an official institutional communication.
incidents.expected_harm: When a school board communicates to all families that students of certain skin colours and backgrounds are the ones most likely experiencing powerful emotions in response to a violent death, it tells every child and parent that the institution sorts people by skin colour when assessing their inner lives and experiences. Students not identified in that demographic grouping receive the implicit message that their feelings matter less or are less expected. Students who are identified receive the message that their emotional reactions are defined by their ancestry rather than their individual humanity. This framing normalizes the idea that a person's skin colour or ethnic background predicts how they feel, undermining recognition of each student as a distinct individual. It also deepens division among families by drawing a demographic line within a school community that the board otherwise claims to be united.

quotes.text: In times like these, it is understandable that emotions are forefront especially with those students who identify as African, Black and Caribbean and their families.
quotes.rationale: This is the central problematic passage. The board singles out students based on their skin colour and ethnic background as those most likely experiencing powerful emotions, essentializing emotional responses along demographic lines rather than treating all students as individuals who may be affected.

incidents.short_description: TVO published an opinion piece describing experiences of living in Kingston, Ontario while identifying as a person based on skin colour. The article frames interactions and social dynamics through a lens focused on categorizing people by their immutable traits.
incidents.description_of_event: On June 1, 2020, TVO published an opinion piece titled "What it's like to be Black in Kingston" by Tianna Edwards on its website. The piece describes the author's personal experiences growing up and living in Kingston, Ontario, with particular focus on interactions and social dynamics interpreted through the framework of skin colour categories. The article discusses the author's high school experience, her decision to return to Kingston, and her perspective on raising a mixed-race daughter. The piece advocates for continued awareness and conversation about categorizing people based on immutable characteristics, referencing broader social movements and events in the United States.
incidents.expected_harm: Publishing this type of opinion piece reinforces institutional patterns of categorizing and discussing Canadians based on their skin colour rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. When a publicly funded broadcaster presents narratives that centre on dividing people into demographic categories, it normalizes the practice of reducing individuals to group identities. This approach undermines efforts to build a society where people are recognized for their unique qualities, character, and individual circumstances. The piece encourages readers to view social interactions primarily through the lens of immutable traits, which will perpetuate rather than diminish the significance institutions and individuals place on such categories. By framing personal experiences exclusively through demographic classification, the publication contributes to an environment where people's individual stories and distinct perspectives are subordinated to group-based narratives.

quotes.text: What it's like to be Black in Kingston
quotes.rationale: The title itself frames the entire article around categorizing people by skin colour, presenting experiences as determined by demographic classification rather than individual circumstances.

incidents.short_description: Theatre artist Yolanda Bonnell and her company Manidoons Collective restricted press reviews of their show 'bug' at Theatre Passe Muraille to critics of certain ethnic and racial backgrounds only, explicitly excluding others based on their skin colour and ancestry.
incidents.description_of_event: In February 2020, Yolanda Bonnell and her company Manidoons Collective opened a theatrical production called 'bug' at Theatre Passe Muraille in Toronto. Bonnell and her company announced that only critics who identified as indigenous, black, or people of colour would be invited to review the show. Critics who did not belong to those designated categories — including established theatre critics from the Toronto Star — were explicitly excluded from receiving press tickets and reviewing the production. The decision was made on the basis that the show functions as an artistic ceremony, and that critics without certain demographic backgrounds were considered unsuitable to evaluate it. Theatre Passe Muraille, as the co-presenting venue, supported and facilitated this arrangement. The restriction was not based on a critic's knowledge, skill, professional record, or any individual quality — it was based solely on skin colour and ethnic ancestry. Two outlets agreed to send only critics fitting the approved demographic profile.
incidents.expected_harm: Critics are excluded from professional access to a publicly staged theatre production based solely on their skin colour and ethnic ancestry rather than their individual qualifications, knowledge, or demonstrated conduct. This sets a precedent in which institutions sort people into those permitted or denied access to cultural spaces based on immutable demographic characteristics. It normalizes the idea that a person's skin colour or ancestry determines their capacity to engage meaningfully with art, undermining the principle that individuals deserve to be evaluated on their own merits and abilities. The arrangement also harms the broader public by restricting the flow of critical coverage through demographic filtering, meaning audiences receive only perspectives pre-selected by ancestry. Journalists who are excluded on this basis are subjected to a form of discrimination that, were it applied on any other basis, would be widely condemned. The practice entrenches the idea that people's skin colour and ancestry are the defining lens through which their contributions to public discourse should be judged.

quotes.text: Bonnell and her company, Manidoons Collective, have invited only folks who are Indigenous, Black or people of colour (abbreviated as IBPOC) to write about 'bug.'
quotes.rationale: Directly states the demographic restriction placed on which critics may review the production.

quotes.text: Critics who aren't Indigenous, Black or people of colour aren't invited to 'bug.' Yolanda Bonnell explains why
quotes.rationale: The headline of the article confirms the explicit exclusion of critics based on skin colour and ancestry.

quotes.text: So far, two outlets have agreed to send IBPOC reviewers to 'bug.'
quotes.rationale: Confirms that the demographic restriction was operationalised — media outlets selected reviewers based on skin colour and ancestry to comply with the artist's conditions.

incidents.short_description: A francophone Catholic school board in southwestern Ontario removed and destroyed over 4,700 books from 30 school libraries, burning some in a 'flame purification' ceremony as a gesture of reconciliation with indigenous peoples.
incidents.description_of_event: Between 2019 and 2021, the Conseil scolaire catholique Providence conducted a systematic removal of books from its 30 school libraries across southwestern Ontario. The board formed a committee that included individuals presented as indigenous knowledge keepers to evaluate children's books about indigenous peoples. Over 4,700 books were removed from circulation and either destroyed or recycled. In 2019, the board held a 'flame purification' ceremony where approximately 30 books were burned for educational purposes, with the ashes used as fertilizer to plant a tree. A video prepared for students explained they were burying 'the ashes of racism, discrimination and stereotypes.' The removed materials included classic literature such as Tintin and Asterix comics, biographies of historical explorers like Jacques Cartier and Étienne Brûlé, books by both non-indigenous and indigenous authors, and various educational materials. Books were flagged for reasons including use of terms now considered outdated, depictions of indigenous people deemed stereotypical, perceived cultural appropriation, representations of indigenous people without shirts, and authorship by non-indigenous writers. Authors were not notified of the removals. The initiative was influenced by Suzy Kies, described as an indigenous knowledge keeper and co-chair of the Liberal Party of Canada's Indigenous Peoples Commission, who advised that books perpetuating negative stereotypes of indigenous peoples were harmful to children.
incidents.expected_harm: This practice categorizes and evaluates books based on the perceived identity characteristics of both authors and depicted subjects rather than examining content on its individual merits. The removal and destruction of thousands of books limits students' access to diverse materials and historical texts that could serve as educational tools when properly contextualized. By evaluating literature primarily through demographic lenses, the school board teaches students to assess creative works and historical materials based on identity categories rather than encouraging critical thinking about complex historical periods and literary representations. The practice of burning books, regardless of stated intent, establishes a concerning precedent for handling controversial or challenging materials in educational settings. Authors from various backgrounds, including indigenous writers, had their work removed without consultation, suggesting the policy operated according to categorical rules rather than individual assessment of each work's educational value. Students are deprived of opportunities to engage with imperfect historical materials under proper guidance, limiting their ability to understand how representations have evolved and to develop skills in critical analysis of sources.

quotes.text: More than 4,700 books were removed from library shelves at 30 schools, and they have since been destroyed or are in the process of being recycled
quotes.rationale: Establishes the scale of book removal across the school board's institutions

incidents.short_description: EdCan Network published an article promoting classroom book selection based on authors' demographic categories rather than literary merit, advocating for students to be categorized and treated according to their immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: EdCan Network published an article titled 'The 3 R's of Diversifying Your Classroom Booklist' advocating that educators select classroom literature based on authors' demographic characteristics. The author describes rejecting traditional literary works because the authors were 'Western, white, male, and long dead,' and instead choosing books to 'privilege the voices of women and contemporary writers of colour.' The article promotes selecting books based on whether authors belong to particular demographic categories including categorizations based on sexual orientation, and recommends books organized by students' demographic groupings. The approach treats students as representatives of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals, and frames literary value through the lens of authors' immutable characteristics rather than the merit of the works themselves.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will reduce students to demographic categories rather than recognizing them as individuals with unique perspectives and interests. Students will receive implicit messages that their demographic characteristics define what literature is relevant to them and that authors should be evaluated based on immutable traits rather than the quality of their work. The practice will undermine the development of shared cultural knowledge and common understanding across student populations. Students will be directed toward literature based on assumed alignment between their demographic categories and those of authors, limiting their exposure to the full range of human experience and literary achievement. The emphasis on categorizing both students and authors by demographic characteristics will fragment classroom communities and reinforce the notion that people are primarily defined by group membership rather than individual character and capability.

quotes.text: I couldn't help but notice that the writers populating the shelves presented a very narrow demographic: they were mostly Western, white, male, and long dead.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the author's framework for evaluating literary works based on authors' demographic characteristics rather than literary merit or educational value.

quotes.text: To find diverse writers for my classroom, I made a pledge to research and read books by female writers, LGBTQ+ artists, Indigenous writers, and authors of colour.
quotes.rationale: Explicit statement of selecting authors based on demographic categories rather than evaluating works on their individual merits.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Tri-agency Institutional Programs Secretariat
incidents.short_description: The Canada Research Chairs Program implemented mandatory quota requirements based on immutable characteristics including skin colour, sex, disability status, and Indigenous identity. These requirements restrict hiring at Canadian universities to candidates from specified groups and impose financial penalties on institutions that fail to meet demographic targets.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canada Research Chairs Program (CRCP), administered by the Tri-agency Institutional Programs Secretariat and funded by the federal government, implemented a system of mandatory demographic quotas following a 2019 addendum to a 2006 settlement agreement. The program requires universities to meet specific numerical targets for hiring research chairs based on group membership: 50.9% women and gender minorities, 22% individuals labelled by skin colour, 7.5% persons with disabilities, and 4.9% Indigenous peoples by December 2029. Universities must set incremental targets leading to these deadlines. Institutions failing to meet targets face restrictions on nominating candidates and potential reduction of chair allocations. The program requires extensive documentation of demographic characteristics, mandatory training on unconscious bias and equity concepts, and institutional action plans focused on group representation. Some universities have posted positions explicitly restricting applications to individuals from specified groups, excluding candidates based on characteristics such as being male, not having a disability, or skin colour. The program collects detailed self-identification data including sexual orientation and gender identity, and requires institutions to implement recruitment processes that prioritize demographic outcomes. Institutions must provide attestations confirming compliance with equity requirements for each nomination, and the program conducts monitoring to enforce these requirements. Universities risk losing federal research funding if they do not comply with the demographic targeting system.
incidents.expected_harm: The quota system evaluates and restricts individuals based on immutable characteristics rather than individual merit, qualifications, or research excellence. Qualified researchers are excluded from opportunities because of their sex, skin colour, or other characteristics. The system pressures institutions and individuals to classify people by demographic categories and make decisions based on those categories. Academic freedom is constrained when researchers must demonstrate adherence to specific ideological frameworks to qualify for positions or funding. The emphasis on group representation over individual assessment undermines the principle that each person should be evaluated based on their unique qualities and contributions. Researchers who question or decline to participate in the categorization system face professional consequences. The administrative burden of compliance diverts resources from research activities. The focus on demographic targets rather than removing barriers to individual participation entrenches rather than eliminates the practice of judging people by group membership.

quotes.text: If an institution is found not to have met its equity target(s) at any of the given equity target deadlines leading up to 2029, it will be limited to submitting only new nominations where individuals self-identify as belonging to one or more of the four designated groups and where the institution has target(s) to be met for that group leading up to the 2029 period, until their equity target gap(s) for that deadline is met.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the mandatory nature of quotas and the enforcement mechanism that restricts hiring based on demographic group membership.

incidents.short_description: The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario developed and distributes lesson plans for elementary school students premised on the idea that people receive advantages or disadvantages based solely on the colour of their skin. The materials instruct teachers to sort students and their experiences through the lens of skin colour rather than individual character and merit.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario (ETFO) developed a suite of classroom resources — including a booklet titled Re-Think, Re-Connect, Re-Imagine and an accompanying set of lesson plans called Rethinking White Privilege — for use in Ontario elementary schools at the primary, junior, and intermediate grade levels. The materials were published in 2015 and 2019 respectively and remain available to ETFO members on the organization's website.

The lesson plans and booklet direct teachers to instruct children that people with lighter skin colour receive unearned advantages in Canadian society by virtue of that skin colour alone, framing this as a fixed, structural reality that students should internalize and act upon. The materials explicitly state: "White privilege describes the benefits, resources and rewards that White people receive by virtue of their skin colour" and define this as "the concrete benefits of access to resources and social rewards and the power to shape the norms and values of society that Whites receive, unconsciously or consciously, by virtue of their skin color in a racist society."

The booklet and lesson plans guide teachers to lead classroom discussions and reflection activities organized entirely around the skin colour of students, teachers, and community members. Teachers are instructed to lead students through exercises examining how their skin colour either confers privilege or subjects them to oppression, and to apply this framework explicitly to classroom dynamics, school structures, and community life. One section states: "Nothing in the classroom or school is race-neutral. Everything we do either disrupts or perpetuates racism."

The materials also address the scenario of an entirely light-skinned classroom, telling teachers that such work is still necessary and important and that students must understand "racialized power and marginalization."

The lesson plans were organized into four sections — Myself, My Classroom, My School, My Community — and designed to be integrated into multiple subject areas across the curriculum. They include success criteria and assessment tools so that students' engagement with these concepts can be reflected on report cards. The materials were developed by ETFO staff and member teachers and piloted in Ontario schools. They are distributed free to ETFO members upon request.
incidents.expected_harm:
Children are taught from an early age to understand themselves and others primarily through the lens of skin colour, rather than as individuals of equal worth whose character, abilities, and actions define them. Students with lighter skin are told they carry unearned advantages that make them complicit in a system of oppression, while students with darker skin are framed as inherently disadvantaged and oppressed. This sorting of children by skin colour undermines each child's sense of individual dignity and worth, replacing it with a group-based identity defined by a trait they were born with and cannot change.

Teachers are directed to embed this framework throughout the curriculum, meaning children encounter these ideas repeatedly and across subjects. The approach forecloses the possibility that students will be evaluated and engaged with as individuals, and instead trains both teachers and students to view every classroom interaction through a racial lens. Rather than fostering a shared sense of common humanity and civic belonging, the materials entrench the idea that Canadians are divided into groups with fundamentally different and opposing experiences based on skin colour. This teaches children that what unites them is less important — and less real — than what divides them.

quotes.text: Nothing in the classroom or school is race-neutral. Everything we do either disrupts or perpetuates racism.
quotes.rationale: This instruction tells teachers that every classroom interaction must be interpreted through a racial lens, institutionalizing a framework that reduces all educational relationships to skin colour rather than individual merit or shared humanity.

quotes.text: White Privilege is about the concrete benefits of access to resources and social rewards and the power to shape the norms and values of society that Whites receive, unconsciously or consciously, by virtue of their skin colour in a racist society.
quotes.rationale: This glossary definition, intended as a teaching tool for Ontario elementary classrooms, labels an entire group of people as holding power and privilege solely based on their skin colour, framing children's identities through a fixed demographic lens.

quotes.text: When we avoid or deny the complicated history of racism, we fail to acknowledge how our White students benefit every day from their skin privilege. The impact of colonization and slavery in the past helped to create a system that continues to privilege White people in the present.
quotes.rationale: This passage explicitly tells teachers to instruct lighter-skinned children that they personally benefit from historical atrocities based solely on the colour of their skin — a message that reduces individual children to demographic proxies for historical groups.

incidents.short_description: The Canadian Gender Budgeting Act requires the federal government to analyse and publicly report on the impacts of budget decisions, tax expenditures, and spending programs by sorting Canadians into demographic identity groups. The law institutionalises the practice of viewing individuals primarily through the lens of group categories rather than as distinct persons with unique circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Gender Budgeting Act (S.C. 2018, c. 27, s. 314), which received royal assent on December 13, 2018, enshrines in federal law the requirement that the Government of Canada systematically assess and report on all major fiscal decisions — including new budget measures, tax expenditures, and ongoing spending programs — by sorting their impacts according to demographic identity categories, specifically sex and what the Act calls "diversity." The policy statement in section 2 declares it government policy to consider these categories in all taxation and resource allocation decisions, including transfers to individuals. Sections 3, 4, and 5 impose legal obligations on the Minister of Finance and the President of the Treasury Board to produce and publish annual analyses of program and tax impacts broken down by these identity groupings. The preamble frames this as a means of improving evidence-based decision-making and reducing inequality. The Act institutionalises the routine categorisation of Canadians by demographic proxies as the primary lens through which fiscal policy is designed and evaluated, embedding this approach across federal departments through capacity-building requirements in section 2(d).
incidents.expected_harm: By requiring that every budget measure, tax policy, and spending program be assessed and reported through the lens of demographic identity categories, the Act embeds into law the practice of viewing Canadians as members of identity groups rather than as distinct individuals with unique needs and circumstances. Government decisions are systematically framed around demographic categories, which normalises the treatment of people as representatives of groups rather than as individuals whose character, abilities, and personal situations should determine how policy affects them. This approach entrenches division by institutionalising the sorting of citizens by identity markers at the highest level of government decision-making. It shapes how departments develop all policies in a budgetary context, meaning the demographic-categorisation lens permeates the design of programs, transfers, and tax measures that affect every Canadian. The framework forecloses approaches that would assess individual impacts or universal human needs, instead directing resources and analytical capacity toward measuring outcomes by group membership.

incidents.short_description: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced Canada's official recognition of the UN International Decade for People of African Descent, committing the federal government to policies, data collection, and programming organised around people's ancestry and skin colour. The government framed its social policies and commitments through a racial classification framework rather than addressing Canadians as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: On January 30, 2018, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announced that the Government of Canada would officially recognize the United Nations International Decade for People of African Descent (2015–2024). In doing so, the federal government committed to a framework that organises recognition, justice, and development programming around a specific ancestry-based demographic category. The announcement stated that the government would improve research and data collection aimed specifically at this group, and would pursue policies and initiatives directed at Canadians sorted by their ancestry and skin colour. The government framed mental health, corrections overrepresentation, and other social challenges as issues to be addressed through a racial categorisation lens rather than as individual challenges shared across the population. The Prime Minister's quote affirmed the government's intention to shape public policy and spending around this demographic classification.
incidents.expected_harm: By anchoring federal policy commitments to ancestry and skin colour classifications, the government normalises the practice of sorting Canadians into demographic categories rather than addressing them as individuals with unique circumstances. Programs, data collection, and resource allocation shaped by this framework evaluate people through the lens of their ancestry rather than their individual needs, character, or demonstrated circumstances. This approach entrenches racial categorisation as a legitimate instrument of government, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. It also signals to all Canadians that the government views some people's challenges as belonging to a racial group rather than to individual human beings, weakening the foundation of shared civic belonging and common humanity that a cohesive society requires.

quotes.text: The Prime Minister, Justin Trudeau, today announced the Government of Canada will officially recognize the International Decade for People of African Descent. This Decade, which spans from 2015 to 2024, is an opportunity to highlight and celebrate the important contributions people of African descent have made to Canadian society. It also provides a framework for recognition, justice, and development to fight racism, discrimination, and the ongoing inequalities that Canadians of African descent face.
quotes.rationale: Core announcement establishing that the government is formally adopting an international framework organised around ancestry and skin colour as the basis for recognition, justice, and development programming.

quotes.text: In recognizing the International Decade, the Government of Canada commits to a better future for Black Canadians. This means learning more about the issues that affect Black Canadians, including improving research and data collection, so we can better understand the particular challenges they face.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the government's commitment to directing research, data collection, and policy specifically toward Canadians sorted by skin colour and ancestry, rather than addressing social challenges through an individual or universal lens.

quotes.text: Today is an important day for Canada. Our commitment to the International Decade will help us better address the very real and unique challenges that Black Canadians face, and bring us closer to a more just and inclusive country.
quotes.rationale: Prime Minister's own words affirming the government's commitment to framing policy around a demographic category defined by ancestry.

incidents.institution_name: University of Winnipeg > University of Winnipeg Students’ Association
incidents.short_description: The University of Winnipeg Students' Association operates a dedicated lounge that restricts access based on skin colour and ethnicity. The space explicitly excludes students who do not fall into designated demographic categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Winnipeg Students' Association (UWSA) operates the BIPoC Lounge, a physical space on the Concourse Level of the Bulman Student Centre. According to the UWSA's own description, the lounge is a dedicated space for students of certain skin colours and ethnicities to gather, learn, and socialize. It functions as a library, study space, and hang-out area. Access to the lounge requires contacting a coordinator or visiting the UWSA General Office, and is restricted based on demographic identity. The lounge was established in 2018 through the UWSA and the Students of Colour Collective. The space is not open to all students; it is explicitly designated for individuals who identify within specific demographic categories based on skin colour and indigeneity. The UWSA promotes events and programming through the lounge, including tabling and cultural celebration events.
incidents.expected_harm: Students who do not belong to the designated demographic categories are excluded from a shared campus space funded and operated by the students' association. This creates a racially segregated environment within a publicly accessible post-secondary institution, where access to a common student space is determined by a person's skin colour or ethnicity rather than by their individual needs or interests. Students learn that their institution endorses the sorting of people into separate spaces based on immutable characteristics. This undermines shared civic belonging on campus, normalizes demographic categorization as a basis for access, and contradicts the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual rather than reduced to a demographic group. It forecloses opportunities for students to build understanding across perceived differences and entrenches division rather than common community.

incidents.short_description: TVO published an article featuring personal essays that describe individuals primarily through racial categorization and frame experience through the lens of immutable characteristics rather than individual identity.
incidents.description_of_event: On July 14, 2016, TVO published an article titled 'What does it mean to be brown-skinned in Canada?' featuring four writers discussing their experiences. The article frames personal identity and social experience primarily through skin colour categories, with contributors describing themselves and others through racial labels. The piece presents identity as fundamentally defined by demographic categories rather than individual character, experiences, or achievements. Contributors discuss phenomena like 'shadeism' (discrimination based on lightness of skin within communities) and describe monitoring representation by counting individuals based on appearance. One essay describes dividing society into categories and characterizes historical events through a racial framework that attributes collective guilt and privilege based on demographic identity. The article structures discourse around the assumption that individuals' skin colour is their primary defining characteristic and determinant of experience.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach to identity will reinforce the practice of categorizing and evaluating individuals based on immutable characteristics rather than their unique qualities, experiences, and character. It will encourage readers to view themselves and others primarily through demographic labels, undermining recognition of individual dignity and singular worth. The framing normalizes the reduction of complex human experience to group membership and may discourage institutions from evaluating people as distinct individuals. By presenting identity as fundamentally determined by appearance and demographic category, the article will discourage the development of shared civic belonging that transcends surface differences and will promote division based on immutable traits rather than common humanity.

quotes.text: We asked four TVO.org contributors with some ideas and definitions of their own to answer this question: What does it mean to you to be brown— and what does it mean to have others see you as such?
quotes.rationale: Shows TVO's editorial decision to frame contributors' identities and experiences primarily through skin colour categorization.

incidents.short_description: The City of Ottawa published an official staff handbook that organizes residents and employees into demographic categories — including skin colour, ancestry, and sexual orientation — as the primary lens through which city services, hiring, and programs are designed and evaluated.
incidents.description_of_event: The City of Ottawa, in partnership with the City for All Women Initiative and with funding from Status of Women Canada, produced and distributed the Equity and Inclusion Lens Handbook (second edition, 2015) for use by city councillors, staff, management, and community partners delivering services on the city's behalf. The handbook functions as an operational guide instructing staff to view residents and colleagues primarily through a set of demographic categories — including skin colour, ancestry, age, sex, sexual orientation, religion, language, and income — when making decisions about communications, hiring, program planning, service delivery, and policy development. A central component of the handbook is a worksheet that directs staff to sort residents and potential service recipients into named demographic groups — including categorizations based on skin colour and ancestry — and to identify which of those groups may be excluded from city programs. Staff are asked to apply this group-based lens across all areas of their work, from recruiting and hiring through to strategic planning and community engagement. The handbook defines "racialized" as a process by which people come to be seen as different and subjected to unequal treatment, and explicitly states that this term is preferred over legislated terminology. It introduces concepts such as privilege and intersectionality as analytical frameworks, and instructs staff to assess their own advantages and disadvantages based on their demographic characteristics before engaging with the public or colleagues. Training sessions, community events, and internal tools such as the Diverse-City Calendar are embedded in the toolkit to reinforce the demographic categorization approach on an ongoing basis.
incidents.expected_harm: City staff are trained to evaluate residents and colleagues through demographic group membership rather than as distinct individuals with their own circumstances, abilities, and needs. This embeds a group-based framework into hiring, program planning, communications, and service delivery across the entire municipal government, meaning that access to city services and employment opportunities is shaped by which demographic categories a person is placed into. Residents are reduced to a checklist of immutable characteristics rather than being engaged as individuals. The institutionalization of this approach normalizes demographic sorting as standard practice across a major Canadian city government, making it harder for staff to evaluate people on their individual merits and demonstrated needs. It also entrenches division among residents by continuously emphasizing differences based on skin colour, ancestry, and other immutable traits, rather than fostering a shared civic identity and common belonging.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's job posting for a Senior Supervisor, Enforcement role strongly encourages candidates to self-identify based on ethnicity, disability, and sex under the federal employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Bank of Canada posted a job opening for a Senior Supervisor, Enforcement position based in Ottawa or Toronto. The posting includes an Equity, Diversity & Inclusion section that commits the Bank to a workforce reflective of demographic categories including race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, and gender identity. More specifically, the posting invites all candidates to apply and strongly encourages them to self-identify if they belong to what the Bank calls an "Employment Equity designated group" — listing indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, and racialized or visible minorities. The posting frames self-identification by these demographic categories as part of the Bank's recruitment process, meaning candidates are evaluated not only as individuals but also in relation to which demographic groups they belong to.
incidents.expected_harm: By asking candidates to self-identify according to demographic categories — ethnicity, disability, and sex — the Bank of Canada reduces applicants to labels tied to immutable traits rather than evaluating them purely as individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and qualifications. Each person who applies is nudged to place themselves into a demographic box, and that categorisation becomes part of how the Bank sees and sorts its applicant pool. This directly contradicts the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual and deserves to be assessed on their unique qualities. When demographic membership influences how candidates are perceived or advanced in a hiring process, the message sent is that a person's identity group matters as much as — or more than — who they actually are.

quotes.text: We invite you to join an organization where differences are seen as strengths and are recognized, valued and respected. We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities).
quotes.rationale: This is the core self-declaration/self-identification trigger, explicitly inviting candidates to identify themselves as members of designated employment equity groups enumerated as indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, and racialized/visible minorities.

quotes.text: The Bank is committed to achieving a workforce that reflects the diversity of our country and is representative and inclusive of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, socio-economic background or lived experience. By living our values and prioritizing our actions to enhance equity, diversity and inclusion, the Bank ensures broader discussions, better decisions, and a more engaging workplace.
quotes.rationale: This is the standard equity, diversity and inclusion policy statement enumerating demographic categories — race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, and gender identity — as the basis for the Bank's workforce composition goals.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Defence Construction Canada
incidents.short_description: Defence Construction Canada's posting for a Business Intelligence Analyst encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, framing hiring through demographic categories rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: Defence Construction Canada (DCC) posted a job opening for a Business Intelligence Analyst, Operations, based in Ottawa, closing June 14, 2026. The posting includes a section headed "Be part of a diverse and inclusive workplace!" that explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. The relevant passage reads: "We actively encourage applications from all, including but not limited to, women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities and members of the LGBTQ2+ community." This language singles out candidates by demographic characteristics — sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation — as a basis for encouraging their applications. The posting also invites accommodation requests through a dedicated email address, which is a separate, non-categorizing measure. The core incident is the enumeration of demographic groups as a rationale for encouraging applications, a near-universal feature of federal government and Crown corporation postings that systematically frames hiring in terms of demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal Crown corporation explicitly lists demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — as reasons to encourage someone to apply, it reduces prospective candidates to those traits rather than evaluating them as individuals with distinct abilities, experience, and character. Candidates learn that an institution views them first through the lens of their demographic profile. This normalises the idea that a person's sex, skin colour, ancestry, disability status, or sexual orientation is a relevant factor in whether they belong in a workplace, rather than treating each applicant as a singular individual whose merits speak for themselves. Over time, the ubiquitous repetition of this framing across thousands of federal postings entrenches demographic categorisation as a default mode of institutional thinking, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own qualities and circumstances.

quotes.text:
Be part of a diverse and inclusive workplace!

At DCC, we are committed to ensuring everyone has a voice and can be their authentic selves in our workplace. We actively encourage applications from all, including but not limited to, women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities and members of the LGBTQ2+ community.
quotes.rationale: Standard employment equity encouragement boilerplate enumerating designated groups: women (sex), Indigenous Peoples (race/ethnicity), persons with disabilities (disability), visible minorities (race/ethnicity), and members of the LGBTQ2+ community (sexual orientation).

incidents.short_description: Ingenium posted a job for an Advisor, Governance and Compliance, encouraging applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, and asking candidates to self-identify as members of federal "employment equity" groups.
incidents.description_of_event: Ingenium — the federal crown corporation that operates Canada's national science and technology, aviation and space, and agriculture and food museums — posted a full-time, six-month term position for an Advisor, Governance and Compliance (Policy Renewal Project) based in Ottawa. The posting includes a section titled "Our Commitment to Employment Equity" that encourages applications based on disability, ethnicity (including indigenous persons, black and other racialized persons), sex, sexual orientation, and gender identity. The posting also asks candidates to self-identify as members of one or more federal "employment equity" groups through a pre-screening question during the application process. The specific language reads: "We encourage everyone interested in working with us to apply, including people with disabilities, Indigenous, Black and racialized individuals, as well as people from all ethnicities, cultures, sexual orientations, gender identities and gender expressions. Candidates are encouraged to self-identify as members of one or more designated employment equity groups. This is asked in a pre-screening question during the application process."
incidents.expected_harm: By encouraging applications specifically on the basis of disability, ethnicity, sex, sexual orientation, and gender identity, and by asking candidates to self-identify under the federal government's "employment equity" categories during screening, Ingenium reduces applicants to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their character, abilities, and qualifications. Every person who applies for this role is prompted, at the pre-screening stage, to define themselves by immutable traits. This practice embeds identity-based sorting into the hiring process itself, signalling to candidates that their demographic characteristics are relevant to their prospects, and undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. The normalisation of this categorisation across the federal public service — replicated in posting after posting — entrenches a hiring culture in which people are seen first as members of groups rather than as distinct individuals.

quotes.text: Ingenium champions the values of diversity and inclusion. We encourage everyone interested in working with us to apply, including people with disabilities, Indigenous, Black and racialized individuals, as well as people from all ethnicities, cultures, sexual orientations, gender identities and gender expressions. Candidates are encouraged to self-identify as members of one or more designated employment equity groups. This is asked in a pre-screening question during the application process.
quotes.rationale: This is the core qualifying passage. It combines the standard EE encouragement boilerplate (enumerating disability, indigenous ethnicity, black and racialized persons, sexual orientation, gender identity/expression, and sex) with an explicit self-declaration request tied to designated employment equity groups at the pre-screening stage. It triggers patterns 1 and 2.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Gallery of Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Gallery of Canada posted a Front End Web Developer position and explicitly encourages applications based on ethnicity, skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Gallery of Canada posted a competition (26-OC-81) on June 1, 2026, for a Front End Web Developer position in Ottawa. In the posting's Values section, the Gallery states it encourages applications from individuals based on their ethnicity (indigenous and racialized communities), skin colour (black communities), sex (women), disability, and sexual orientation (2SLGBTQI+ communities). Rather than inviting all candidates to apply on the basis of their individual abilities and qualifications, the posting singles out candidates by immutable demographic traits as preferred categories. This language — which the federal government and its institutions routinely attach to postings under their 'employment equity' program — is the same standard encouragement boilerplate that appears across the federal public service and its affiliated institutions.
incidents.expected_harm: When a public institution explicitly encourages applications based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, it signals to candidates that their demographic identity is a relevant factor in hiring rather than their individual character, skills, and demonstrated abilities. Candidates who belong to the named categories are reduced to representatives of those categories. Candidates who do not belong to those categories receive the implicit message that they are less preferred, regardless of their individual merit. This practice normalises the sorting of people by immutable traits at the moment they first engage with a public institution, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as a distinct individual.

quotes.text: The National Gallery of Canada is dedicated to fostering an inclusive environment that values and celebrates the diverse life experiences, voices, and histories of all individuals. We encourage applications from individuals belonging to Indigenous, Black, and racialized communities, women, persons with disabilities, and members of 2SLGBTQI+ communities.
quotes.rationale: Standard employment equity encouragement boilerplate enumerating preferred applicant groups by ethnicity (indigenous and racialized communities), skin colour (Black communities), sex (women), disability (persons with disabilities), and sexual orientation/gender identity (2SLGBTQI+ communities).

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Museum of Nature
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Museum of Nature posted a job for Head, Individual Giving that invites applications from candidates based on sex, ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation, and religion, treating demographic categories as relevant factors in the hiring process.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Museum of Nature posted a permanent, full-time position for Head, Individual Giving (selection process number 2090-CMN-26-OC-018), located in Gatineau, QC, closing June 28, 2026. The posting includes a section titled "Your place at the Museum" that explicitly welcomes applications from candidates on the basis of their membership in identity categories. The museum states: "We welcome applications from persons who may identify as a member of one or more other equity seeking groups (for e.g. women, visible minorities, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, 2SLGBTQIA+, religious or cultural minority)." This language enumerates candidates by sex, skin colour and ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation, and religion as relevant considerations in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: By singling out applicants based on sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation, and religion, the museum reduces candidates to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals defined by their character, abilities, and experience. Every person who applies for this position will be processed through an institutional framework that sorts them by immutable traits. This treats demographic category membership as a meaningful hiring consideration, which undermines the principle that each person possesses inherent worth as an individual. Candidates who do not fit any named category receive no such welcome, implicitly signalling that their demographic profile matters to the institution. The practice normalises the idea that who a person is — defined by group membership — is as relevant as what they can do, eroding the standard of evaluating people purely on individual merit and demonstrated ability.

quotes.text: We value diversity and believe we benefit from working in a place where everyone feels included and where the unique qualities and strengths inherent in a diverse workforce are combined. We welcome applications from persons who may identify as a member of one or more other equity seeking groups (for e.g. women, visible minorities, Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, 2SLGBTQIA+, religious or cultural minority).
quotes.rationale: Standard employment equity encouragement boilerplate enumerating women (sex), visible minorities and Indigenous peoples (race/ethnicity), persons with disabilities (disability), 2SLGBTQIA+ (sexual orientation), and religious or cultural minority (religion) as groups whose applications are specifically welcomed.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Royal Canadian Mint
incidents.short_description: The Royal Canadian Mint's posting for a Senior Analyst, Cyber Security invites applicants to voluntarily self-identify based on sex, ethnicity, and disability in accordance with the federal employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Royal Canadian Mint posted a permanent position for a Senior Analyst, Cyber Security based in Winnipeg. The posting states that, in accordance with the Employment Equity Act, candidates are invited to voluntarily self-identify as belonging to one or more of four groups — women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities — by sending their application to a dedicated email address accessible only by Human Resources. This self-identification request categorises candidates by sex, ethnicity, and disability as part of the Mint's staffing process.
incidents.expected_harm: Asking candidates to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability reduces individuals to demographic categories at the very moment they are being considered for employment. Rather than evaluating each applicant solely on their skills, experience, and character, the Mint's process introduces demographic sorting as a formal step in recruitment. Candidates who identify into these categories are assessed at least in part through the lens of group membership rather than individual merit. This approach undermines each applicant's singular worth as a person and normalises the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable traits.

quotes.text: In accordance with the Employment Equity Act, you are invited to voluntarily self-identify with one or more of the four employment equity groups (women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities and members of visible minorities) by sending your application to the confidential email, EE@mint.ca that is accessed only by the Human Resources section.
quotes.rationale: This is the self-declaration / self-identification trigger: candidates are explicitly invited to self-identify as members of the federal employment equity groups — women (sex), indigenous peoples (race_or_ethnicity), persons with disabilities (disability), and members of visible minorities (race_or_ethnicity) — via a dedicated HR email address.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's job posting for a Project Manager, Leadership Development Program, strongly encourages candidates to self-identify based on ethnicity, disability, and sex under the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Bank of Canada posted a Project Manager, Leadership Development Program position (term, 18 months) based in Ottawa. The posting includes a dedicated 'Equity, Diversity & Inclusion' section that frames hiring around demographic categories rather than individual merit alone. The posting explicitly invites all candidates to self-identify if they belong to an 'Employment Equity designated group,' listing Indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, women, and racialized/visible minorities. This self-identification request is presented as an encouragement tied directly to the Bank's hiring process. The posting also states a commitment to developing 'inclusive, barrier-free recruitment and selection processes' and declares that the Bank's workforce should 'reflect the diversity of our country.' Taken together, these elements constitute a hiring preference based on ethnicity, disability, and sex, which are among the categories the federal government groups under its 'employment equity' program.
incidents.expected_harm: By asking candidates to self-identify based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability status, the Bank of Canada reduces applicants to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. A candidate's skin colour, ethnicity, sex, or disability status is irrelevant to their ability to manage a leadership development program; yet the posting invites the Bank to record and weight these traits in the hiring process. This labelling treats each person's immutable characteristics as meaningful data points in decisions about their career, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories are implicitly disadvantaged in a process that signals preference for demographic profiles over individual qualifications. The self-identification request also pressures candidates to disclose private information about their identity as part of a job application, eroding personal privacy and autonomy.

quotes.text: We invite you to join an organization where differences are seen as strengths and are recognized, valued and respected. We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities).
quotes.rationale: This is the core self-identification / EE encouragement trigger. It explicitly names the four designated groups under the federal 'employment equity' program — indigenous peoples (ethnicity), persons with disabilities (disability), women (sex), and racialized/visible minorities (race_or_ethnicity) — and strongly encourages candidates to self-identify as members of these groups.

quotes.text: The Bank is committed to achieving a workforce that reflects the diversity of our country and is representative and inclusive of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, socio-economic background or lived experience. By living our values and prioritizing our actions to enhance equity, diversity and inclusion, the Bank ensures broader discussions, better decisions, and a more engaging workplace.
quotes.rationale: This is a policy statement referencing the Bank's equity, diversity and inclusion commitment, framing the workforce goal in terms of demographic representation. It enumerates multiple identity categories including race, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, situating the self-identification request within a broader institutional policy of demographic categorisation.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's posting for a Clerk, Surveys position in Toronto encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation, and religion, sorting candidates into demographic categories rather than evaluating them purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Clerk, Surveys position based in Toronto, Ontario. The posting includes an employment equity encouragement statement under the heading "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion," which reads: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This language explicitly sorts prospective applicants into demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation — and signals that these categories are a factor in CMHC's hiring considerations. The posting is for a casual, two-month data entry role paying $23.89 per hour, yet it nevertheless carries this standard identity-categorization language.
incidents.expected_harm: By publicly signalling that applicants' sex, ethnicity, disability status, religion, and sexual orientation are relevant to their candidacy, CMHC reduces individuals to demographic labels at the moment they are first evaluated. Each applicant — regardless of their data entry skills, organizational ability, or demonstrated aptitude — is invited to present themselves partly through immutable characteristics. This contradicts the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their character, abilities, and individual circumstances alone. When institutions routinely attach this language to postings, they normalise the practice of categorising people by immutable traits, training both applicants and hiring managers to view candidates through demographic lenses rather than as distinct individuals. The cumulative effect across thousands of federal and quasi-federal postings is a hiring culture that systematically subordinates individual worth to group membership.

quotes.text: We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply.
quotes.rationale: Standard employment equity encouragement boilerplate enumerating women (sex), Indigenous Peoples (race_or_ethnicity), persons with disabilities (disability), all races and ethnicities (race_or_ethnicity), religions (religion), sexual orientations (sexual_orientation), and gender identities and expressions (sexual_orientation/sex). This is the primary qualifying language for the incident.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Bank of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Bank of Canada's posting for a Senior Platform Analyst position strongly encourages candidates to self-identify based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and other designated group membership under the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: A Bank of Canada job posting for a Senior Platform Analyst – Database Services (Cloud/DBaaS) position includes an invitation for candidates to self-identify if they belong to one of the groups enumerated under the federal government's employment equity program. The posting states: "We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities)." The posting also includes a broader equity, diversity and inclusion statement committing the Bank to a workforce representative of Canada based on race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, and socio-economic background. Taken together, these passages enumerate candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability, and other demographic traits, and ask them to declare their membership in those categories as part of the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: When the Bank of Canada asks candidates to label themselves by ethnicity, sex, disability, or other demographic traits, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and qualifications. A candidate who identifies with one of the listed groups will know their demographic status has been flagged and may factor into how their application is assessed. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be seen and evaluated as a distinct individual. By institutionalising demographic self-declaration in the hiring process, the Bank normalises the idea that a person's immutable characteristics are relevant to their professional worth, rather than their skills, experience, and demonstrated merit.

quotes.text: We welcome all candidates to apply and strongly encourage candidates to self-identify if they identify with an Employment Equity designated group (Indigenous Peoples, Persons with Disabilities, Women or Racialized/Visible Minorities).
quotes.rationale: This is the core self-declaration/self-identification trigger: candidates are invited to declare membership in employment equity designated groups, enumerating ethnicity (Indigenous Peoples, Racialized/Visible Minorities), disability, and sex (Women).

quotes.text: The Bank is committed to achieving a workforce that reflects the diversity of our country and is representative and inclusive of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, socio-economic background or lived experience. By living our values and prioritizing our actions to enhance equity, diversity and inclusion, the Bank ensures broader discussions, better decisions, and a more engaging workplace.
quotes.rationale: Broad equity, diversity and inclusion policy statement enumerating race, ethnicity, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, and gender identity as factors in workforce composition goals.

incidents.short_description: The Academy of Canadian Cinema & Television's 2023 Canadian Screen Awards rules require productions telling stories from an indigenous perspective to meet ethnicity-based staffing thresholds to qualify for awards, and require indigenous creators to formally document their ethnic identity and connection to indigenous communities. Non-indigenous productions touching on indigenous content must complete sensitivity training and follow prescribed on-set protocols as a condition of eligibility.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Academy of Canadian Cinema & Television's 2023 Canadian Screen Awards rules, published with an entry deadline of October 2022, include a section titled 'Narrative Sovereignty' that conditions award eligibility on the ethnicity of a production's key creative personnel. When a submitted work tells a story from an indigenous perspective, the production must affirm either that at least two-thirds of its above-the-line talent identify as indigenous, or that the production company is majority indigenous-owned. Productions that fall outside these ethnic thresholds are ineligible for the award in that category.

As a condition of demonstrating eligibility, indigenous creators are specifically required to complete a form expressing their relationship and connection to their ethnic identity — including family ties, nationality, and displacement history. No comparable documentation requirement applies to creators of any other ethnic background seeking awards eligibility.

When a production is made from a non-indigenous perspective but includes indigenous content, entrants must declare they have read the Indigenous Screen Office's On-Screen Protocols & Pathways Media Production Guide, and provide written documentation demonstrating how the production followed community protocols, engaged indigenous leadership, and maintained a respectful work environment for indigenous crew. This declaration is assessed by an 'Equity Committee' which determines eligibility based on those actions.

The sensitivity training requirement referenced in the eligibility rules draws directly from the Indigenous Screen Office's on-set protocols, which specify that 'cultural sensitivity training is a must' and describe requirements for daily smudges, community health workers on set, and ceremonial practices at the start and end of production.
incidents.expected_harm:
Award eligibility for Canadian film and television productions is determined in part by the ethnic composition of a production's creative team, treating a creator's ethnicity as a qualification rather than evaluating the work on its own merits. This subordinates individual creative achievement to demographic group membership and sets a precedent for ethnicity-based gatekeeping in a major national arts institution.

Indigenous creators face a requirement no other group faces: they must formally document and justify their ethnic identity in writing to a committee in order to unlock eligibility for their productions. This treats a person's ethnicity as something that must be proven and verified by an institution, reducing individuals to their demographic background and subjecting them to scrutiny of their family history and cultural connections that other Canadians are never asked to provide.

The rules create an incentive structure — documented in the Twitter evidence — where productions nominally hire people based on their ethnicity solely to satisfy award eligibility thresholds, rather than for their skills or creative contribution. This harms both the individuals used in this way (valued for their ethnicity rather than their abilities) and those who lose professional opportunities because their ethnicity does not meet the required threshold.

Productions that include indigenous content but are not made from an indigenous perspective face mandatory sensitivity training, prescribed on-set ceremonial practices, and oversight by an Equity Committee. This burdens creative work with ethnicity-based compliance requirements and subjects artistic decisions to institutional review on ethnic grounds, undermining open creative expression and the evaluation of individuals based on their abilities.

incidents.short_description: The Abbotsford District School Board fired a veteran teacher of four decades after he told students that deaths at residential schools were largely caused by tuberculosis — a claim supported by the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's own reports. The board treated factually grounded historical information as incompatible with its preferred institutional narrative.
incidents.description_of_event: In 2021, Jim McMurtry, a Grade 12 teacher with four decades of experience and a doctorate in the philosophy of education with a specialty in indigenous history, was teaching a class in Abbotsford, B.C., when students raised the topic of unmarked graves at the Kamloops Indian Residential School. After a student claimed that priests had murdered and tortured children and left them to die in the snow, McMurtry stated that most children at residential schools died from disease, primarily tuberculosis. This is consistent with the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's own findings, which document that as many as half of the children who attended the early years of residential schools died of tuberculosis due to overcrowding, poor sanitation, and lack of medical care. McMurtry was removed from the school within the hour following a single complaint. He was subsequently suspended. When, over a year later, he publicly criticized the suspension as unfair, the board initiated a formal discipline hearing. The board's report characterised his historical statements as contradicting "the truth and reconciliation work that is currently underway in the District" and accused him of "flagrant disregard" for his employer. The hearing, held on 21 February 2023, resulted in a recommendation that McMurtry be terminated for just and reasonable cause. The board's conclusion stated explicitly that his dismissal followed from his refusal to be silenced and his insistence on his right to speak freely.
incidents.expected_harm: A teacher is dismissed not for incompetence or misconduct toward students, but for stating a historically accurate fact and then refusing to be silenced about the injustice of his suspension. This signals to all teachers in the district — and more broadly — that institutional loyalty to a particular ideological narrative takes precedence over factual accuracy and open inquiry. Teachers learn that raising evidence-based perspectives that diverge from approved institutional positions will end their careers, and so self-censor. Students are deprived of honest, evidence-grounded historical instruction and of exposure to the kind of open, reasoned discourse that helps them develop their own capacity for critical thinking.
