The suppression of factually supported historical information in favour of an institutional orthodoxy undermines truthful discourse in the classroom and sets a precedent that positions school boards as arbiters of permissible historical truth rather than as bodies supporting the pursuit of understanding.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a department administrator contract role restricted to applicants based on their ethnicity, requiring candidates to self-identify as indigenous. People of other ethnicities were barred from applying.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a 12-month remote contract position for a department administrator supporting five Vice Presidents in its Retail & Small Business Risk Canada team. The posting explicitly required applicants to be indigenous — defined as status and non-status first nations, Métis, and Inuit people from Canada — as a condition of submitting a résumé. The role itself involved standard administrative duties: scheduling meetings, coordinating travel, preparing expense reports, onboarding new employees, and supporting departmental projects. None of these responsibilities have any connection to a person's ethnicity. The posting was described as being made pursuant to Scotiabank's "Special Program" under the federal employment equity and Canadian Human Rights Acts. Applicants who do not identify as indigenous based on the posting's definition were categorically excluded from consideration, regardless of their skills, experience, or individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are turned away from a job opportunity solely because of their ethnicity. The restriction treats ethnicity as a criterion for employment in a role where it is entirely irrelevant, reducing individuals to a demographic category rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. This practice normalises the institutional sorting of people by ethnicity, signals to non-indigenous Canadians that they are categorically unwelcome for certain roles, and reinforces a hiring culture built around demographic proxies rather than individual worth.

incidents.short_description: The City of Ottawa listed a job posting that treats an applicant's skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and other immutable traits as hiring assets. This institutionalises the evaluation of individuals based on demographic categories rather than their character and abilities.
incidents.description_of_event: The City of Ottawa posted a full-time permanent position for a Specialist in Diversity and Inclusion Programs and Projects, with a salary range of $87,069–$105,951 per year. The posting closed on January 26, 2023. Beyond the standard qualifications of a four-year university degree and five years of relevant experience, the City explicitly listed as hiring assets an applicant's "lived experience and identity" as a member of indigenous, black, and other groups identified by skin colour, as well as membership in groups identified by disability, sex, and other demographic traits. The posting effectively assigned hiring value to an applicant's demographic identity — their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability status, sexual orientation, and religion — treating these immutable characteristics as professional qualifications. The City framed this approach as part of a commitment to an "equitable, strategic and inclusive hiring process," and separately encouraged applications based on skin colour, sex, disability, ethnicity, religion, and sexual orientation. The position sat within the City's Workplace Equity, Inclusion and Belonging Branch.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are evaluated not solely on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications, but on immutable traits such as skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability. This disadvantages individuals who do not belong to the preferred demographic groups, regardless of their professional merit. It embeds the practice of sorting people into demographic categories into the City's hiring decisions, normalising the idea that a person's identity group — rather than their individual qualities — is a legitimate hiring criterion. Over time, this reinforces institutional thinking that reduces individuals to demographic proxies, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits and unique circumstances.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a Customer Experience Associate position in Ontario that required applicants to self-identify as first nations, Métis, or Inuit. The role was closed to all other applicants based solely on their ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank posted a job listing for a Customer Experience Associate covering multiple locations across Ontario, with the title explicitly naming the ethnicity requirement: 'First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Customer Experience Associate - Ontario Region.' Among the listed requirements, the posting placed self-identifying as first nations, Métis, or Inuit as the first criterion — above communication skills, customer service experience, or any other qualification. The position was open across dozens of Ontario communities including Sudbury, Timmins, Sault Ste. Marie, Niagara Falls, Peterborough, and others. All other qualifications listed — communication skills, willingness to train, customer service aptitude — are common to any such role. The ethnicity requirement operated as a hard restriction: the bank was not accepting applicants who did not belong to these ethnic groups, regardless of their skills, character, or demonstrated ability. The posting was closed to further applicants at the time of capture, indicating the role had already been filled under these ethnicity-based criteria.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are turned away from employment at Scotiabank based solely on their ethnicity. A person with strong customer service skills, communication abilities, and genuine enthusiasm for the role is categorically excluded because they do not belong to a specified ethnic group. This reduces individuals to a demographic label rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, or circumstances. The practice normalises the institutional use of ethnicity as a hiring gate in the private sector, signalling to job seekers that their ethnic background — an immutable characteristic — determines their access to employment opportunities. It also reinforces the idea that people are best understood and sorted as members of ethnic categories rather than as distinct individuals with their own merits.

incidents.short_description: The Writers Guild of Canada published its 2022 Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Report, tracking and reporting on its members' skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and disability status. The report frames these demographic categories as the lens through which representation in the Canadian television industry should be measured and addressed.
incidents.description_of_event:
On April 3, 2023, the Writers Guild of Canada (WGC) released its 2022 Equity, Diversity and Inclusion Report, covering data through December 31, 2021. The report was prepared by the WGC and its Inclusion, Diversity, Equity, and Accessibility (IDEA) Committee. It categorizes WGC members and working screenwriters by skin colour and ethnicity (including indigenous people, people described as black, people of colour broken down by ethnic subgroup, and white people), sexual orientation (LGBTQ2S), and disability status. The report tracks these demographic categories across membership composition, writers' room participation, writing credits, and staffing roles in Canadian television production.

The WGC uses these categories to assess whether participation rates align with Canadian population figures, framing gaps as evidence of underrepresentation that the Guild intends to address. The report notes that the WGC offers its diversity incentive — waiving initiation fees and reducing membership requirements — for writers who self-identify as belonging to one of the designated demographic categories. The IDEA Committee also maintains a directory search function to identify writers by these demographic labels, and has created networking and programming initiatives organized around the same categories.

The report presents writers not primarily as individual creative professionals but as representatives of demographic groups, with their career outcomes — credits earned, staffing levels, writing jobs — analysed through the lens of group identity. Conclusions drawn in the report call for ongoing tracking and industry-wide policy changes based on these demographic measurements.
incidents.expected_harm: Writers are evaluated and publicly categorized by their skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and disability status rather than as distinct individuals with unique creative talents and professional records. The Guild's institutional framing reduces screenwriters to demographic representatives, normalizing the idea that a person's group membership is a relevant and meaningful measure of their professional standing. Writers who do not fit the designated demographic categories are treated as a residual group — defined by what they are not — rather than as individuals in their own right. The practice of waiving fees and reducing membership requirements based on demographic self-identification creates a formal system in which writers' access to Guild membership is influenced by their skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, or disability, rather than solely by their work and abilities. Over time, this institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories and measuring outcomes by group identity undermines the principle that each person deserves recognition as an individual, and entrenches division among creative professionals rather than fostering a shared professional community.

incidents.short_description: The Ontario Modern Language Teachers' Association organized a racially segregated affinity group, explicitly barring people based on their skin colour from participating.
incidents.description_of_event: The Ontario Modern Language Teachers' Association (OMLTA) organized a 'Racialized Peoples Affinity Group' and circulated a sign-up form for a session scheduled in April 2024. The form described the group as a space for individuals who identify as Arab, Asian, Black, Hispanic, indigenous, Latine, Multiracial, or 'Person of the Global Majority.' Participation was explicitly restricted based on skin colour and ethnicity: the form required prospective attendees to confirm they identified as a 'racialized person.' Those who identified as white were directed to 'continue my journey as an ally in another space' and were excluded from joining. The form also required agreement to confidentiality terms, noting that sessions would not be recorded. Contact emails listed on the form belong to individuals associated with the Toronto District School Board (TDSB) and the Simcoe County School Board, suggesting the event had ties to multiple educational institutions. The group was framed as being for 'language teachers,' meaning the segregation applied to professional development or collegial programming for educators.
incidents.expected_harm: Teachers are excluded from a professional group based on their skin colour, reducing access to collegial support and professional community on the basis of an immutable characteristic. The practice institutionalizes the categorization of professional educators by demographic labels rather than treating them as individuals with shared professional interests and challenges. By directing people of a particular skin colour away from the group, the OMLTA normalizes racially segregated professional spaces and entrenches the idea that educators' skin colour defines which communities they belong to. This undermines the possibility of building genuine understanding across perceived differences and forecloses spaces where teachers of all backgrounds could engage with their shared experiences as language educators. The explicit exclusion of people based on skin colour, dressed in the language of 'safety' and 'solidarity,' reproduces the logic of racial segregation under a professional and institutional banner.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board offered virtual counselling groups restricted to students based on their skin colour and ethnicity, excluding all other students from the mental health programme.
incidents.description_of_event: The Peel District School Board promoted a programme called 'RESILIENCE' through its official Twitter account, advertising virtual counselling groups for students in grades 6–8 and 9–12. The groups were explicitly restricted to students who identified as Black, African, Afro-Canadian, Afro-Caribbean, Afro-Indigenous, or within the African diaspora. Sessions ran every Wednesday from February 1 to March 8, 2023, at 4 p.m. The school board announced the programme publicly on January 25, 2023. Students who did not identify within these skin colour and ethnicity categories were excluded from participating, regardless of their individual mental health needs or circumstances. The board presented this as a mental health and wellness initiative, but structured it as a racially segregated service.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are sorted into mental health services based on their skin colour and ethnicity rather than their individual needs or circumstances. Students who fall outside the specified categories are denied access to the programme regardless of what they are going through. This normalises the idea that institutions should assess and serve individuals based on demographic categories, undermining the recognition of each student as a person of singular worth. The racially segregated structure reinforces divisions among students rather than fostering a shared sense of community and belonging. It sends a message that one's skin colour and ethnicity determine what institutional support a person deserves, which is contrary to the principle that every individual's wellbeing matters equally.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a job opening for a Manager, Collections Strategy Data Analytics role that was restricted exclusively to applicants of a specific ethnicity. Candidates who are not indigenous are barred from applying, regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank posted a job listing titled 'First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Manager Collections Strategy Data Analytics, Remote Career Opportunity' (Requisition ID: 176220). The posting explicitly requires applicants to be indigenous persons from Canada in order to submit a résumé, stating: 'While you must be an Indigenous person in order to submit your resume, participation is completely voluntary.' The posting defines eligibility as 'status, non-status First Nation, Métis and Inuit people' who come from Canada. Scotiabank states the posting is made 'pursuant to Scotiabank's Special Program under the Employment Equity Act and Canadian Human Rights Act,' invoking the federal government's 'employment equity' program as justification. The role itself — a managerial position within Global Risk Management focused on collections strategy and data analytics — has no apparent connection to an applicant's ethnicity. All candidates are evaluated not simply on their character, skills, and demonstrated abilities, but first and foremost on their ethnicity. Anyone who is not indigenous is categorically excluded from applying, regardless of their qualifications, experience, or individual merit. The position was listed with locations across multiple Canadian provinces including Manitoba, Alberta, British Columbia, and Ontario.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are turned away from a professional opportunity solely because of their ethnicity. Every person who does not meet Scotiabank's ethnic criterion — no matter how skilled, experienced, or well-suited — is denied the chance to be evaluated as an individual. This practice entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by demographic categories rather than assessing them on their individual merits and abilities. It signals to all job seekers that their ethnicity is a primary credential, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be recognised for their unique qualities and capabilities. The normalisation of ethnicity-based hiring restrictions in the private sector reinforces divisive categorical thinking and erodes the shared civic belonging that comes from institutions treating every person as a singular individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada > Corporations Canada
incidents.short_description: Corporations Canada requires federal distributing corporations to report on the demographic composition of their boards of directors and senior management, tracking individuals by sex, ethnicity, disability, and skin colour. The policy compels corporations to treat leadership roles as demographic targets rather than evaluating individuals on their own merits.
incidents.description_of_event: Under the Canada Business Corporations Act (CBCA), Corporations Canada — part of Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada — requires all federal distributing corporations, including venture issuers, to disclose to their shareholders and to the federal government detailed information about the demographic make-up of their boards of directors and senior management teams. The requirement, with guidelines last updated in July 2022, mandates that corporations track and report on four categories the federal government groups under its 'employment equity' program: women, indigenous peoples (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities. Corporations must disclose whether they have written policies for nominating directors based on these categories, whether they set numerical or percentage targets for representation of each group on their boards and in senior management, the progress made toward those targets, and the actual number and percentage of executives and directors from each group. Corporations that do not have such policies or targets must explain why not. The guidelines encourage corporations to consider a candidate's sex, ethnicity, skin colour, and disability status when identifying, nominating, and appointing directors and senior managers. Information filed with Corporations Canada is made available to the public.
incidents.expected_harm: By compelling corporations to track, report, and set targets for the demographic composition of their leadership, the policy institutionalises the practice of evaluating individuals in part on the basis of their sex, ethnicity, skin colour, and disability status rather than solely on their character, abilities, and experience. Directors and senior managers are counted and categorised by immutable traits, reducing them to demographic representatives rather than recognising them as distinct individuals. Corporations face regulatory pressure to factor these traits into hiring and nomination decisions, which displaces merit-based evaluation and treats demographic group membership as relevant to a person's fitness for leadership. The requirement to publicly disclose numerical targets and progress toward them reinforces a framework in which institutional leadership is viewed primarily through the lens of demographic composition, normalising the idea that a person's sex, skin colour, ethnicity, or disability status is a legitimate and important criterion for corporate appointment.

incidents.institution_name: University of Toronto > Department of Social Justice Education > Ontario Institute for Studies in Education
incidents.short_description: The University of Toronto's Department of Social Justice Education posted a faculty position that gives hiring preference to candidates based on their ethnicity and sexual orientation. The posting explicitly states that preference will be given to candidates who self-identify as indigenous.
incidents.description_of_event: On December 19, 2022, the Department of Social Justice Education at the Ontario Institute for Studies in Education, University of Toronto, posted a job listing for a full-time, tenure-stream Assistant Professor position specialising in indigenous Queer / Two Spirit Studies in Education. The posting states that preference will be given to candidates who self-identify as indigenous, framing this as part of the university's response to Canada's Truth and Reconciliation Commission. Beyond the explicit preference based on ethnicity, the posting also requires applicants to demonstrate ongoing connections to specific indigenous communities as part of their academic work, and demands that application letters address the applicant's connection to one or more specific indigenous communities — effectively making ethnicity a gatekeeping criterion at the application stage. The university's diversity statement further signals a preference for applications based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation and gender identity. The position thus evaluates candidates not purely on their research excellence, teaching ability, or scholarly merit, but on whether they belong to particular demographic categories.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates who do not share the preferred ethnicity or sexual orientation are disadvantaged in the hiring process regardless of their scholarly merit, teaching ability, or demonstrated expertise in the relevant field. The university treats a person's ethnicity and sexual identity as relevant factors in a professional hiring decision, reducing individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, qualifications, and abilities. This sends a clear institutional message that certain demographic backgrounds are worth more than others in academic hiring, normalising the practice of sorting people by immutable traits. It also sets a precedent within a prestigious institution for treating ethnicity and sexual orientation as legitimate professional credentials, undermining the principle that individuals deserve evaluation based on what they can do and know, not who they are.

incidents.institution_name: McMaster University > Equity and Inclusion Office
incidents.short_description: McMaster University's Equity and Inclusion Office advertised a Black History Month staff social event restricted to employees belonging to the 'Black Indigenous Racialized Staff Employee Resource Group' demographic, excluding other staff based on their skin colour and ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: On Thursday, February 9, 2023, McMaster University's Equity and Inclusion Office organized a Black History Month staff social event titled the 'Black Indigenous Racialized Staff Employee Resource Group BHM Social Event,' scheduled from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m. in CIBC Hall. The event advertisement, promoted through the official Black History Month at McMaster University Twitter account, explicitly restricted attendance to staff community members who are part of what the organizers called the 'BIRS demographic.' The promotional materials stated: 'The event will be opened to all staff community members who are part of the BIRS demographic.' This language confined participation to staff based on their skin colour and ethnicity, excluding colleagues who did not fall within that demographic category. The event was organized by the Black Student Success Centre and was part of a broader Black History Month programming series co-promoted by the university's Equity and Inclusion Office.
incidents.expected_harm: Staff at McMaster University are sorted and separated by their employer based on skin colour and ethnicity, with some employees excluded from a workplace social event on that basis. This is racial segregation in a professional setting, normalizing the institutional practice of dividing employees into demographic categories rather than treating them as individuals of equal worth. Employees outside the designated demographic are excluded from a social gathering in their own workplace, signalling that the university views them primarily through the lens of their skin colour or ethnicity. This undermines workplace belonging and civic cohesion, and entrenches the idea that institutions should organize people — and grant or deny access — according to immutable traits rather than individual character or need.

incidents.short_description: A technical error in the Toronto District School Board's lottery system for alternative elementary schools excluded students who had been sorted into a demographic pool based on skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation, leaving them off wait lists for roughly 75 per cent of available seats. The failure exposed both the administrative breakdown and the underlying practice of allocating school spots by demographic category.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board (TDSB) redesigned its admission process for its 17 elementary alternative schools for the 2023–24 school year, receiving more than 1,200 applications for 458 available seats. Under the new system, the board first prioritised students based on ethnicity (indigenous students) and sibling relationships. A computer system then separated students whom the board had categorised by skin colour (described in TDSB communications as Black and Middle Eastern children) and sexual orientation (students identifying as LGBTQ) into a distinct pool, from which they would be randomly awarded 25 per cent of seats. However, a third-party vendor's software then failed to carry the remaining students from that demographic pool forward into the general lottery for the remaining 75 per cent of seats. As a result, these students were excluded from the general lottery entirely and placed on wait lists, with no explanation or apology issued to affected families. The TDSB acknowledged the error in an email to families on April 3, 2023, attributing it to the outside vendor. As of April 5, 2023, the board said it was reviewing the situation and looking to add spaces, but had not identified the vendor or resolved the situation for affected families, including at least one 11-year-old whose parents described the process as neither procedurally fair nor equitable.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are denied access to sought-after elementary alternative schools because a system built around demographic sorting — allocating seats by skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation — malfunctioned and left them off the general lottery entirely. Children are reduced to demographic categories rather than evaluated as individuals, and the administrative failure compounds the harm by denying them even the places they were nominally entitled to compete for. Families lose trust in the institution's ability to administer fair admissions. The underlying system, regardless of the technical glitch, normalises the practice of sorting children by immutable characteristics as a condition of accessing public education, undermining the principle that every student deserves to be treated as an individual. Parents who raised concerns received no apology and were left uncertain whether or when their children would receive a fair chance at a seat.

incidents.short_description: Toronto Metropolitan University's School of Journalism promoted a guide that sorts aspiring journalists by skin colour and ethnicity, advising them to navigate newsrooms as members of a racial group rather than as distinct individuals.
incidents.description_of_event:
In May 2023, Toronto Metropolitan University's School of Journalism promoted the 'How-to Guide for Navigating Canadian Newsrooms as a BIPOC Journalist,' created by journalist Anita Li and facilitator Erin Kang, and funded by the RBC Foundation and the Meta Journalism Project. The guide was produced under the Journalists for Human Rights' Enhanced Access for BIPOC Youth in Canadian Media initiative. The School of Journalism publicised the guide through its website and social media accounts, lending its institutional authority to the resource.

The guide explicitly sorts its intended audience by skin colour and ethnicity, framing the journalistic workplace primarily as a terrain defined by racial group membership. It instructs readers to 'protect yourself against tokenization,' advises them that Canadian newsrooms are 'disproportionately staffed with white journalists,' warns that they 'may be one of the only members of your racial group represented,' and coaches them on how to respond when asked whether they can remain unbiased when reporting on their 'racial group.' It tells readers they 'may be repeatedly asked for connections to your racial group or to pitch stories about that group.' The guide also recommends seeking out communities specifically for journalists grouped by skin colour, such as Canadian Journalists of Colour and the Canadian Association of Black Journalists.

Rather than preparing young journalists to be evaluated on their skills, curiosity, and integrity as individuals, the guide consistently frames their professional identity through the lens of racial group membership, treating skin colour and ethnicity as central to how they should understand and navigate their careers.
incidents.expected_harm: Young journalists entering the industry are encouraged to understand themselves and their careers primarily through the lens of their skin colour and ethnicity, rather than as individuals with distinct abilities, perspectives, and professional qualities. This framing embeds the expectation that racial group membership is a defining characteristic of professional life, which undermines the development of a shared civic and professional culture in newsrooms. Journalists who internalise this framing are directed toward race-specific professional communities rather than toward broad collegial relationships built on common craft and shared journalistic standards. The institutional promotion of the guide by a university journalism school gives these ideas the weight of professional preparation, normalising the reduction of individuals to demographic categories at the very start of their careers.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a Validation Officer position in Hamilton, Ontario restricted to first nations, Métis, and Inuit applicants. The job listing uses a person's ethnicity as a condition of eligibility for the role.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank advertised a Validation Officer role within its Dealer Finance Centre in Hamilton, Ontario, under the title "First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Validation Officer - Automotive Finance - Hamilton, ON" (Requisition ID: 162722). The role itself involves standard financial verification and validation work — reviewing indirect loan and lease agreements, verifying documentation, and liaising with automotive dealerships. The posting makes no suggestion that the duties of the role require any particular cultural knowledge or community connection. Despite this, the position is designated exclusively for applicants of first nations, Métis, or Inuit ethnicity. Candidates of any other background are excluded from consideration regardless of their qualifications, skills, or experience. The posting was captured on 9 April 2023 and Scotiabank has since closed applications.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified individuals are denied the opportunity to be considered for employment based solely on their ethnicity. The bank evaluates people not as individuals with skills and abilities but as members of ethnic categories, determining who may or may not apply for work accordingly. This practice normalises the institutional sorting of Canadians by ethnicity for employment purposes, sending a clear signal that a person's ethnic background — an immutable characteristic — is a valid basis for inclusion or exclusion from the workforce. Workers excluded by this criterion receive no consideration for their individual merits, no matter how capable they are, because they were born into the wrong ethnic group.

incidents.short_description: The Toronto District School Board operates the Africentric Alternative School, which organizes programming and attendance around students' skin colour and ethnic background rather than treating all students as individuals. The source URL was unreachable at time of capture, so full details could not be confirmed.
incidents.description_of_event: The Toronto District School Board operates the Africentric Alternative School in Ontario. Based on the submitter's notes, this school is structured around demographic categories — specifically, students' skin colour and cultural background — rather than serving students as distinct individuals with unique needs and abilities. The source provided for this incident returned a connection timeout error and no substantive content was retrievable. As a result, the specific practices, policies, or communications that raise concern could not be documented from the available evidence.
incidents.expected_harm: When a publicly funded school organizes its programming and identity around students' skin colour and ethnic background, it sorts children into demographic categories rather than recognizing them as individuals. Students are taught that their immutable characteristics define where they belong and how institutions should treat them. This undermines shared civic belonging and common educational community, and normalizes the idea that public institutions should divide people based on ancestry and heritage rather than serving all students according to their individual character, needs, and abilities.

incidents.institution_name: Centre for Addiction and Mental Health > Nicotine Dependence Service
incidents.short_description: The Centre for Addiction and Mental Health offered a specialty tobacco cessation training course framed entirely around the ethnicity of clients, treating first nations, inuit, and métis people as a population requiring a distinct clinical approach based on their ethnicity rather than their individual circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: The Centre for Addiction and Mental Health's TEACH Project offered a 13.5-hour online specialty course titled 'Tobacco Interventions for First Nations, Inuit, and Métis Populations,' running from February 15 to March 22, 2023, at a cost of $400. The course was designed to train health practitioners to screen, assess, and treat commercial tobacco use — but the curriculum was organized entirely around the ethnicity of the client rather than the clinical presentation or individual needs of the person. Learning objectives asked trainees to describe the history and impact of tobacco use among first nations, inuit, and métis people specifically, and to adapt screening, assessment, psychosocial, and pharmacological interventions specifically for clients of these ethnicities. The framing of the course positions a person's ethnicity as a primary lens through which their health needs should be understood and addressed, embedding the assumption that practitioners need separate clinical knowledge and adapted tools based on who a client is ethnically, rather than what their individual clinical circumstances are.
incidents.expected_harm: Health practitioners trained through this course learn to approach clients through the lens of ethnicity first, which conditions them to treat patients as representatives of a demographic group rather than as individuals with distinct clinical needs, histories, and circumstances. This reinforces institutional habits of categorizing people by immutable traits, undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation based on their individual situation. Patients from first nations, inuit, and métis backgrounds receive care filtered through assumptions about their ethnicity rather than grounded in their personal clinical presentation, which risks distorting clinical judgement and reducing the quality of individualized care. The course also normalizes the broader institutional practice of building separate service frameworks around ethnicity, entrenching divisions rather than building a health care system that recognizes all patients' common humanity and individual worth.

incidents.short_description: The Waterloo Region District School Board issued a public open letter responding to parent concerns by framing students in identitarian categories — grouping them by skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and gender identity — rather than addressing them as individuals with shared educational needs.
incidents.description_of_event:
On January 20, 2023, the Waterloo Region District School Board (WRDSB) published an open letter addressed to parents, caregivers, families, and communities in the Waterloo Region. The letter was issued in response to questions and concerns raised by a parent at a Committee of the Whole Meeting, and was made public in the stated interest of transparency.

Throughout the letter, the WRDSB consistently frames students not as individuals but through demographic categories. The board describes its student data collection practices as necessary to understand "the cultural, social and demographic makeup of WRDSB students," explicitly referencing identity-based categories including students who identify as 2SLGBTQIA+, and groups described as "Indigenous, Black, racialized students." The letter defends identity-based data collection as mandated under Ontario's Anti-Racism Act, and frames this categorisation as essential to serving students well.

The letter dismisses the concerns raised by the parent, characterising them as "inflammatory language and misinformation" and as a "tried and tested method to attack public education." It accuses those raising questions of making veiled attempts to target specific groups of children. Rather than engaging with the substance of parental concerns — about identity surveys, library books, and school policy around gender — the board responds by sorting both students and critics into demographic and ideological camps.

The letter reaffirms the board's commitment to policies that classify students by skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and gender identity as the foundation for resource allocation, programming, and support — treating these categories as the primary lens through which the institution sees the children it serves.
incidents.expected_harm:
When a school board communicates to its community by consistently sorting students into demographic categories — by skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation — it normalises the institutional habit of seeing children first as members of groups rather than as distinct individuals. Parents and students receive the message that the institution's fundamental approach to education is built on this categorisation, not on individual character, ability, or need. Children learn that the school system defines their identity and allocates resources according to group membership rather than personal circumstance.

Parents who raise questions about these practices are publicly characterised as spreading misinformation or acting in bad faith, discouraging legitimate parental engagement and undermining the open discourse that a healthy public education system requires. This chilling effect on parental voice removes a meaningful check on institutional conduct.

The letter also forecloses common ground: rather than framing the wellbeing of all students as a shared concern, it positions families on opposite sides of demographic lines, deepening division within the school community. Students from all backgrounds lose the opportunity to be seen and supported as individuals navigating a shared educational journey.

incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia hosted a storytelling and dialogue event restricted to students based on their skin colour and ethnicity. The event excluded other students from participating in shared conversations about storytelling and community.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of British Columbia hosted the first session of its 'Decolonial Dialogues' series on October 20, 2022, at the First Nations Longhouse on the Vancouver campus. The event, titled 'Narrating Experiences In Storytelling,' featured writers and panel members Junie Désil, Jasmin Kaur, and Dallas Hunt, and included a panel discussion and Q&A with students. The series, running from October 2022 to February 2023, was explicitly designed as a segregated space for students based on their skin colour and ethnicity — restricted to those the university categorised as Indigenous, black, or people of colour. The event was organised by the First Nations House of Learning, the UBC Equity & Inclusion Office, and the Office of the Vice-President, Students. Promotional materials described the series as providing 'a safe space for IBPOC students,' deliberately excluding students who did not fall into the university's designated demographic categories from attending and connecting with speakers and peers.
incidents.expected_harm: Students are excluded from a university-organised event and community-building experience based on their skin colour and ethnicity. Rather than fostering understanding and shared civic belonging across the student body, the university institutionalises segregation by restricting access to programming based on immutable characteristics. Students outside the designated demographic categories are barred from conversations about storytelling and shared human experience — topics that are not inherently limited to any particular group. The practice normalises the labelling and sorting of students into demographic categories, signalling that the university evaluates people by their skin colour and ethnicity rather than as distinct individuals. This entrenches division within the student community and undermines the possibility of building genuine solidarity and understanding across perceived differences.

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board's Supervisor Final Report, submitted December 2022, affirms the continuation of race-based equity targets and accountability measures across the board, including an Annual Equity Report Card that tracks student outcomes by skin colour and ethnicity. The report endorses policies that sort students and staff into demographic categories as a core organisational framework.
incidents.description_of_event:
Submitted on December 23, 2022, the Supervisor's Final Report for the Peel District School Board documents the board's progress on 27 binding directions issued by Ontario's Minister of Education. The report affirms that the board will continue operating under a comprehensive framework that categorises students, staff, and families by skin colour, ethnicity, and religion as a matter of policy.

Direction 9, highlighted by the reviewer as the key concern, mandates an Annual Equity Report Card that measures student outcomes along demographic lines — including skin colour — across areas such as student achievement, special education, discipline, and wellbeing. The board released its first such report card in September 2021 and a second in October 2022, with the stated purpose of providing
incidents.expected_harm: insights into the disproportionate experiences

incidents.short_description: The Waterloo Region District School Board hosted a job information fair restricted to people based on their skin colour and ethnicity. Job seekers of other backgrounds were excluded from the event.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 29, 2023, the Waterloo Region District School Board hosted an in-person job information fair at its Education Centre in Kitchener, Ontario. The event was explicitly restricted to people the board categorised as indigenous, black, or racialized. Applicants from other backgrounds were not invited to attend. The fair offered breakout sessions with hiring managers and administrators covering a wide range of roles — from educational assistants and early childhood educators to teachers, custodial staff, and professional student services personnel. Registration closed March 27, 2023. The board justified the restriction by citing gaps between the demographic composition of its student body and its workforce, and by invoking Ontario Ministry of Education direction to encourage demographic diversity in the teaching workforce. The board's promotional materials stated that students perform better when they see themselves reflected in the education system, and framed the event as a response to those perceived gaps.
incidents.expected_harm: Job seekers are excluded from a publicly advertised employment information event based on their skin colour and ethnicity. The school board, a public institution, has organised what is in effect a racially and ethnically segregated job fair — admitting prospective employees to hiring information based solely on their background rather than their individual qualifications, skills, or interest in the roles. This practice teaches all students and community members that institutions properly divide and sort people by skin colour and ethnicity, normalising the idea that immutable characteristics are legitimate criteria for access. Rather than recognising every job seeker as an individual deserving equal access to public employment information, the board reduces people to demographic categories. The approach entrenches a view of civic and professional life organised around group membership rather than individual worth, and signals to excluded job seekers that their interest in serving the community's children is unwelcome.

incidents.institution_name: University of Ottawa > Faculty of Social Science
incidents.short_description: The University of Ottawa restricted applications for a tenure-track position in political studies to candidates of certain skin colours and ethnicities, excluding all other qualified applicants from consideration.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Ottawa's School of Political Studies posted a tenure-track Assistant or Associate Professor position in public management with an explicit restriction limiting consideration to applicants of certain skin colours and ethnicities. The job posting, with an application deadline of April 3, 2023, stated that only qualified applications from people the university categorised as "racialized" or indigenous would be considered and evaluated. All other applicants — regardless of their qualifications, research record, or teaching ability — were excluded from consideration entirely based on their skin colour or ethnicity. The restriction was framed as part of initiatives announced by the university president in January 2021, which the university described as combating systemic racial discrimination and addressing underrepresentation in faculty ranks. Applicants wishing to be considered were required to self-identify by completing a confidential questionnaire at the time of application.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are turned away from a public academic position solely because of their skin colour or ethnicity, with no consideration of their individual merit, scholarly contributions, or teaching ability. The institution reduces applicants to demographic categories, directly contradicting the principle that people deserve to be evaluated on their character and abilities. This sets a precedent within a publicly funded university that skin colour and ethnicity are legitimate grounds for excluding individuals from employment opportunities, normalising the institutional labelling and sorting of people by immutable traits. It sends a message to faculty, students, and the broader academic community that the university views people primarily through demographic categories rather than as individuals.

incidents.institution_name: British Columbia Association of Clinical Counsellors
incidents.short_description: The British Columbia Association of Clinical Counsellors released draft standards of practice that compel counsellors to adopt specific ideological frameworks — including anti-racism, anti-oppression, and intersectionality — as enforceable professional requirements. Counsellors who do not conform to these frameworks risk disciplinary consequences.
incidents.description_of_event:
The British Columbia Association of Clinical Counsellors (BCACC) released draft Standards of Clinical Practice containing two standards — Standard 4 ("Diversity, Equity, Inclusion and Anti-Racism") and Standard 5 ("Indigenous Cultural Safety, Cultural Humility, and Anti-Racism") — that go beyond requiring non-discriminatory conduct and instead mandate that counsellors embrace and actively apply a specific ideological framework in their professional work.

Standard 4 declares that its requirements are "foundational to all other standards" and must be incorporated into "all aspects" of a counsellor's work and relationships. Counsellors are required to engage in "self-reflective, anti-oppressive and anti-racist practice," understand "intersectionality," and "recognize and understand that identity and self-definition are fluid." These are not simply requirements to treat clients fairly as individuals; they are demands that counsellors adopt and internalise contested ideological positions.

Standard 5 goes further, framing the counsellor's responsibilities as including actively "dismantling systemic racism and systems of colonialism." Counsellors are required to categorise clients based on ethnicity, treat clients of one ethnic background as a distinct group with special care protocols, and undergo ongoing education in particular ideological interpretations of history and society. The standard requires counsellors to learn about the "disproportionate impact" of racism on particular sub-groups defined by a combination of ethnicity, gender identity, and sexual orientation.

The BCACC is the de facto regulatory body for the counselling profession in British Columbia, holding the Registered Clinical Counsellor (RCC) designation relied upon by more than 70% of all practising counsellors. Compliance with these standards is therefore a practical prerequisite for professional practice. Violations can result in complaints to the BCACC and the BC Human Rights Tribunal, meaning non-conformity carries enforceable professional consequences.
incidents.expected_harm:
Counsellors who do not personally subscribe to the ideological frameworks embedded in these standards — including specific views on systemic racism, colonialism, intersectionality, and the fluidity of identity — face professional discipline and the loss of their ability to practise. This compels ideological conformity as a condition of professional licensing, violating counsellors' intellectual autonomy and the principle that individuals should be evaluated based on their abilities and conduct rather than their adherence to particular political views.

The standards also direct counsellors to approach clients through the lens of their ethnicity and other group characteristics rather than as individuals with unique circumstances and needs. Clients who are indigenous are singled out for treatment under a separate, ethnicity-specific standard, meaning their care is shaped by assumptions about their group membership rather than by an individualized assessment of who they are. This reduces clients to demographic categories rather than recognising their singular worth.

The practical consequence is that the profession is reshaped around a single ideological orientation. Counsellors who engage in good-faith, evidence-based clinical practice without adopting the prescribed political framework are exposed to professional sanction. The requirement to actively "dismantle" social systems and engage in "anti-oppressive" practice transforms clinical counselling from a therapeutic relationship into a vehicle for ideological advocacy, potentially at the expense of individual clients' genuine needs and the open, reasoned professional judgment that good clinical practice requires.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Manitoba > Women and Gender Equity
incidents.short_description: The federal government and Manitoba announced a missing persons alert system restricted to women and girls of a specific ethnicity, creating a two-tier public safety notification system sorted by ethnicity and sex.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 3, 2024, the federal government and the Province of Manitoba jointly announced the creation of the Red Dress Alert, a missing persons alert system modelled on Amber Alerts but restricted exclusively to women and girls based on their ethnicity. The announcement was made in Winnipeg by Crown-Indigenous Relations Minister Gary Anandasangaree and Manitoba's Minister for Women and Gender Equity, Nahanni Fontaine. The Manitoba pilot is designed to be led by people of a specific ethnicity and is intended to inform an eventual national alert system. The program is funded through both federal and provincial budgets. The policy explicitly ties access to public safety alert infrastructure to the ethnicity and sex of the missing person, meaning that a missing person of a different ethnicity or sex would not be eligible for this alert system. The rationale given by proponents is that women and girls of this ethnicity are reported to go missing or be murdered at higher rates than others. The alert system was developed in response to calls from a 2019 national inquiry and subsequent parliamentary motions.
incidents.expected_harm: By tying access to a public safety alert system to ethnicity and sex, governments establish the principle that the urgency of a missing persons notification depends on the demographic characteristics of the missing individual rather than the circumstances of their disappearance or the danger they face. A missing person who does not belong to the designated ethnic group or is not female will not receive the same level of alert-based public mobilisation, regardless of the danger they are in. This creates a formal, institutionalised hierarchy of whose disappearance is treated as worthy of a dedicated public alert. It normalises the practice of sorting individuals into demographic categories when allocating public safety resources, entrenching the idea that ethnicity and sex are legitimate bases for differential government services. Rather than building a universal missing persons alert system that treats every individual as equally worthy of urgent public response, governments have chosen to divide Canadians by immutable traits — undermining shared civic belonging and the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual.

incidents.short_description: The Canadian Political Science Association organized a racially segregated pre-conference workshop restricted to scholars of certain ethnicities and skin colours. Attendees who did not belong to these groups were turned away from the first day of the event.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 28, 2023, the Canadian Political Science Association (CPSA) held a full-day pre-conference workshop titled
incidents.expected_harm: Scholars are excluded from a full day of a professional association's conference programming based on their skin colour and ethnicity, not their individual qualifications, interests, or needs. This segregation signals that the CPSA views its members primarily through the lens of demographic categories rather than as individual scholars with equal standing in the discipline. Scholars excluded from Day 1 lose access to speakers, networking, and community-building opportunities available to their colleagues. The practice normalizes the sorting of professionals by immutable characteristics and entrenches divisions within a field that shapes public understanding of politics and society. It sends a message that some scholars belong to a distinct community defined by skin colour and ethnicity, and that others do not, undermining the shared civic and professional belonging that a learned association should foster.

incidents.short_description: Scotiabank posted a job for an Income Analyst position at its Scotia Dealer Advantage office in Hamilton, Ontario, restricted to applicants of a specific ethnicity. The posting signals that Scotiabank evaluates candidates based on ethnicity rather than their individual qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Scotiabank posted a job listing for an Income Analyst role at Scotia Dealer Advantage in Hamilton, Ontario, with the title explicitly reading "First Nations/Metis/Inuit - Income Analyst - Scotia Dealer Advantage - Hamilton, ON." The job title and posting structure indicate that the position was restricted to applicants of a particular ethnicity — specifically those who are first nations, métis, or inuit. The role itself involves standard financial analysis work: verifying income documentation, assessing customer affordability, and managing loan-related tasks. Nothing in the job's accountabilities or requirements has any connection to an applicant's ethnicity. The restriction appears to be an ethnicity-based hiring criterion, where Scotiabank used a person's ethnicity as a qualifying condition for consideration for the role. The posting was no longer accepting applicants at the time of retrieval, but the title and structure of the original posting confirm the ethnicity-based restriction.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified candidates are excluded from applying for this position based solely on their ethnicity rather than their abilities, experience, or character. This practice reduces individuals to a demographic category at the very first step of the employment process, denying them the opportunity to be evaluated as the distinct individuals they are. It entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by ethnicity and signals to the broader workforce that Scotiabank views ethnicity as a relevant qualification for standard analytical work. Over time, such practices normalise the idea that ethnicity is a legitimate basis for institutional decision-making, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Employment and Social Development Canada
incidents.short_description: The federal government's Employment Equity Act Review Task Force published a report recommending that federally regulated employers sort workers into demographic categories — including by skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — and set numerical hiring goals based on those categories. The report proposes expanding the groups covered and strengthening enforcement mechanisms.
incidents.description_of_event:
In April 2023, Professor Adelle Blackett, chair of the Employment Equity Act Review Task Force, submitted a final report to the federal Minister of Labour recommending a sweeping overhaul of Canada's federal employment equity framework. The task force was established in July 2021 by Employment and Social Development Canada and conducted consultations from February through October 2022.

The report recommends that federally regulated employers continue and expand the practice of categorising workers by skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation, and then set numerical representation goals by those categories. A central recommendation is to create a new, separate employment equity category specifically for workers based on their skin colour (those identified as Black), in addition to maintaining and renaming the existing 'visible minority' category as 'racialized workers.' The report also recommends adding workers based on sexual orientation as a new employment equity group.

The task force recommends establishing an Employment Equity Commissioner with enforcement powers, creating Joint Employment Equity Committees in workplaces, and expanding the reach of the Federal Contractors Program to cover more employers. Employers would be required to identify and remove 'barriers,' report regularly on representation by demographic category, and demonstrate 'reasonable progress' toward numerical goals.

The report frames these measures as implementing 'substantive equality,' but in practice instructs institutions to evaluate people based on their skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than their character, abilities, or individual circumstances. The task force also proposes replacing the workforce availability benchmark with a population-level benchmark, effectively setting an aspiration that workplace representation in every occupation mirror Census population distributions by demographic group.
incidents.expected_harm: Federally regulated employers — covering roughly 7.6% of the Canadian labour force, including the federal public service, banks, airlines, and telecommunications companies — will be directed to evaluate, sort, and make employment decisions about individuals based on their skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. Individual workers will be reduced to demographic proxies rather than assessed on their character, abilities, and demonstrated merit. Workers who do not belong to a designated category face a structural disadvantage in hiring and promotion regardless of their individual qualifications. The expansion of the framework — adding new categories and strengthening enforcement — deepens institutional reliance on demographic labelling and normalises the practice across a broader swath of the economy. The proposal to mirror Census population distributions in every occupation by demographic group sets a standard that can only be achieved through sustained preference decisions based on immutable traits, further entrenching the division of Canadians into competing demographic groups rather than fostering a workplace culture that recognises each person's singular worth.

quotes.text: Our task force recommends creating a separate employment equity group representing Black workers. We also recommend adding 2SLGBTQI+ workers as an employment equity group.
quotes.rationale: This recommendation directly proposes dividing workers into separate demographic groups based on skin colour and sexual orientation, institutionalising the sorting of individuals by immutable traits for employment purposes.

incidents.institution_name: University of British Columbia > Faculty of Dentistry
incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia's Faculty of Dentistry posted a job for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair in Oral Cancer Research that restricted applicants to specific demographic categories. Qualified researchers who do not belong to those categories are excluded from consideration regardless of their academic merit.
incidents.description_of_event: On May 21, 2024, the University of British Columbia's Faculty of Dentistry posted a job listing for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair in Oral Cancer Research. The position requires a PhD, an existing full-time faculty appointment at the Professor rank, a record of research excellence, and strong teaching and funding experience. However, the posting explicitly restricts the selection pool to candidates who belong to specific demographic categories: people with disabilities, women, people whose ethnicity is indigenous, people based on their skin colour, and people from what the posting calls "minoritized gender identity groups." Candidates who do not identify as belonging to one or more of these categories are ineligible for consideration, regardless of how well they meet the academic and professional criteria. The posting instructs candidates to complete an equity survey as part of the application, and states that those who do not self-identify as belonging to a designated group will not be considered. Additionally, people who self-identify as having a disability are singled out further: their names are to be provided separately and confidentially to the search committee to enable what the posting calls "preferential hiring strategies." The restriction is framed as being made pursuant to UBC's CRC Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion Action Plan and Section 42 of the BC Human Rights Code.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified researchers are excluded from a prestigious, publicly funded academic position solely on the basis of their skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability status, or gender identity — demographic characteristics that are irrelevant to their ability to conduct oral cancer research. A researcher who is internationally recognized and meets every stated academic criterion is turned away at the door if they do not belong to a designated demographic group. This treats people as members of categories rather than as individuals with distinct abilities, achievements, and circumstances. The practice normalizes institutional sorting of people by immutable traits and signals to the broader academic community that identity characteristics, rather than research excellence, determine access to senior positions. It also requires applicants to label and disclose personal information about themselves — including disability status — as a precondition for being considered, undermining individual privacy and dignity.

incidents.institution_name: University of Victoria > Faculty of Science
incidents.short_description: The University of Victoria's Faculty of Science restricted three tenure-track assistant professor positions exclusively to applicants of indigenous ethnicity, barring all other qualified scientists from consideration. Candidates of any other ethnic background are ineligible regardless of their scientific qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: On 23 January 2024, the University of Victoria's Faculty of Science posted three tenure-track assistant professor positions open across six departments — Biochemistry & Microbiology, Biology, Chemistry, Earth & Ocean Sciences, Mathematics & Statistics, and Physics & Astronomy. The posting explicitly restricted eligibility to applicants who identify as indigenous, citing the university's Equity Plan and section 42 of the BC Human Rights Code. Applicants were required to self-identify as indigenous in their cover letter to be considered. The university framed this restriction as part of its
incidents.expected_harm: Indigenization and Decolonization

incidents.short_description: The Peel District School Board launched a 2023 Student Census that collected identity-based data from students and families, linking demographic information to academic records to inform group-level policy decisions. The census institutionalises the practice of sorting students by demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event:
In spring 2023, the Peel District School Board (PDSB) administered its second Student Census to students from kindergarten through Grade 12 across its schools in Mississauga, Ontario. Running from April 11 to May 5, 2023, the census collected demographic data and student perceptions. Students in Grades 4 to 12 completed the survey during class time, while families of younger students were invited to complete it at home.

The census was explicitly designed to collect identity-based data, including questions about students' identity, school experiences, and well-being. The board explained this collection as mandatory under the provincial Anti-Racism Act, which requires school boards to conduct a student census at least every five years. PDSB indicated the previous census, conducted in 2018, yielded identity-based data that shaped its policies, procedures, and programs.

Although the census was described as voluntary, responses were not anonymous. A unique code attached to each submission linked it to other school records, including program participation, academic achievement, and suspension history. The stated purpose of this linkage was to identify patterns at the group level — specifically, to determine how particular groups of students, identified by demographic characteristics, were being underserved. The board identified certain groups — including indigenous and African, Black and Afro-Caribbean students — as populations it expected the data to reveal as experiencing barriers.

A total of 110,659 questionnaires were completed, representing a 73% response rate. The board stated that findings would inform board and school decision-making, including the allocation of resources and revision of programs, teaching practices, and policies.
incidents.expected_harm:
By collecting identity-based data and linking it to individual student records, the school board treats students primarily as representatives of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals. The data is used to make group-level inferences and drive group-targeted policies, which entrenches the institutional habit of sorting and responding to students based on immutable characteristics such as skin colour, ethnicity, religion, sex, and sexual orientation rather than their individual circumstances, needs, or abilities.

Although individual results are reported only in aggregate, each student's identity is permanently attached to their academic record. Students are asked to disclose sensitive personal information — including aspects of their identity — during school time with staff present, creating conditions where the line between voluntary participation and implicit institutional expectation is blurred.

The census framework reinforces a worldview in which students are understood through the lens of group membership. Policies and resources shaped by this data will be directed at demographic groups rather than individual students, meaning students whose needs fall outside expected group patterns will be overlooked. It also sends a message to students that the institution sees them first through their demographic profile rather than as individuals with unique qualities, aspirations, and challenges.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada
incidents.short_description: Canada's three federal research granting agencies restrict CIHR and SSHRC undergraduate student research awards exclusively to students based on their skin colour. Students who do not identify as black are categorically ineligible for these awards.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) and the Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council (SSHRC), two of Canada's three federal granting agencies, operate their Undergraduate Student Research Awards (USRA) programs exclusively for students who self-identify as black. The awards, worth $6,000 plus an additional host institution contribution, are available to support 14–16 weeks of full-time undergraduate research. While the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council (NSERC) awards are open to all eligible students, the CIHR and SSHRC streams are entirely closed to any student who does not self-identify as black. Applicants must check a box on the application form to self-identify by skin colour, and this self-identification is shared with the institution and, if the student receives the award, made public. A March 2023 internal email from Queen's University's Vice-Principal Research Portfolio confirms that institutions were directed to widely promote these skin-colour-restricted funding streams and to extend deadlines specifically to attract applications from students of a particular skin colour. The email also notes that NSERC provides supplementary allocations — with no cap — for students who self-identify as black, and that institutions were asked to consider extending their own deadlines to allow further applications from those students beyond the standard quota.
incidents.expected_harm: Undergraduate students who do not identify as black are denied access to CIHR and SSHRC research awards solely because of their skin colour. This is a direct restriction on participation in federally funded research opportunities based on an immutable trait. Students are evaluated and sorted not by their academic excellence, research potential, or individual circumstances, but by the colour of their skin. This practice signals to all students that skin colour is a legitimate basis on which institutions divide and rank people, normalising demographic categorisation as a tool of public policy. For students who are excluded, the financial support and career-forming research experience that these awards provide are simply unavailable to them, regardless of their merit or need. The requirement to publicly disclose one's skin colour as a condition of receiving an award further reduces individuals to a demographic label in an institutional and public record.

incidents.short_description: TD Bank commissioned and published a racial equity audit that documents the bank's practice of setting numerical hiring and promotion targets broken down by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and other demographic traits, and of tying senior executive compensation to meeting those demographic representation goals.
incidents.description_of_event:
In December 2023, TD Bank released a racial equity audit conducted by law firms Covington & Burling LLP and WeirFoulds LLP over a ten-month period. The audit documents a comprehensive institutional framework at TD built around sorting employees and candidates into demographic categories — primarily based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation — and setting numerical targets for their representation at various levels of the bank.

TD has publicly committed to specific demographic representation goals, including doubling the number of employees of a particular skin colour at the vice-president level and above by 2022 (a goal the bank says it met), and reaching 25% representation of people categorized as Black, Indigenous, or Minority at the VP-plus level across North America by 2025. TD also set a target of 45% women at the VP-plus level in Canada by 2025. These commitments are tracked quarterly and reported to senior leadership and the board of directors.

Since fiscal year 2021, progress toward these demographic representation goals has been incorporated into the formula used to determine annual compensation for TD's most senior executives, carrying an aggregate weighting of 20% of business metrics. The bank requires that employees self-identify their demographic characteristics through surveys, with a completion rate above 98.6%, and this data is used to track hiring, promotion, and departure rates broken down by demographic category.

The audit also describes a wide range of programs structured around demographic categories: mentorship, sponsorship, and leadership development programs targeted at employees based on their skin colour or ethnicity; employee resource groups organized by demographic identity; and diversity councils at every level of the bank with mandates tied to specific demographic groups. Senior leaders in at least one business segment are assessed against objectives that include ensuring people from specific demographic groups are included in sponsorship programs and candidate slates.

The audit's recommendations encourage TD to expand this framework further — collecting more granular demographic data on job applicants, using exit survey data filtered by demographic categories, and evaluating more senior leaders on their progress toward demographic representation objectives.
incidents.expected_harm:
When a major employer ties executive pay to demographic representation targets and structures its hiring, promotion, and development processes around skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and sexual orientation, it reduces individual employees and candidates to proxies for the demographic categories they belong to. A person's advancement or recognition within the institution becomes partly a function of their immutable characteristics rather than solely their abilities, conduct, and demonstrated needs.

Employees who do not belong to the institution's designated demographic categories face a systemic disadvantage: the institution explicitly seeks to increase the proportion of other groups at senior levels, meaning that demographic identity — not just individual merit — shapes who is promoted and who is sponsored. This creates a workplace where people are not evaluated purely as individuals.

The practice also normalises the idea that large institutions should track and manage their employees as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct people. When this model is celebrated as a benchmark and other major Canadian banks are publicly urged to replicate it, the effect spreads across the private sector. Over time, more Canadians will find themselves in workplaces where their skin colour, ethnicity, or sex influences institutional decisions about their careers — contrary to the principle that every person deserves recognition based on their unique character and abilities.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Security Intelligence Service
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service posted an administrative assistant position that encourages applications based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability, and explicitly notes that gaps have been identified among certain groups for this position.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) posted a job advertisement for an Administrative Assistant position, open to multiple locations across Canada, with a closing date of 2026-09-30. Within the posting, CSIS states that it is committed to increasing representation of what it calls "employment equity groups" — specifically persons with disabilities, visible minorities, indigenous peoples, and women — at all levels of the organization. The posting goes further than standard encouragement language: it explicitly names that gaps have been identified among indigenous peoples and visible minorities for this specific position, signalling that applicants from those groups will receive preferential consideration. CSIS also strongly encourages individuals belonging to one or more of these groups to self-identify when applying. In this way, the posting invites candidates to be assessed not only on their skills, experience, and character, but also on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability status — traits that are entirely irrelevant to the administrative duties described.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer explicitly identifies demographic gaps and instructs candidates to self-identify based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability, it signals that these immutable traits will influence the hiring outcome. Candidates are reduced to demographic representatives rather than being evaluated as individuals with distinct abilities, experience, and character. Those whose skin colour or ethnicity happens to match an identified gap are implicitly advantaged over equally or more capable individuals who do not share those traits. This process denies each applicant the recognition of their singular worth and replaces individual evaluation with demographic accounting.
