The practice also normalises the institutional habit of labelling and sorting people by immutable characteristics, reinforcing the idea that a person's background defines their value to an organisation rather than what they can actually do.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada posted an Industrial Technology Advisor position in Gatineau stating that priority will be given to candidates based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and asking all applicants to self-declare their membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada advertised a continuing Industrial Technology Advisor role (RCO-4 level) within its Industrial Research Assistance Program in Gatineau. At the top of the posting, the NRC stated that "Priority will be given to the following designated employment equity groups: women, Indigenous Peoples* (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities and racialized persons*," with a footnote clarifying that the Employment Equity Act uses the terminology "Aboriginal peoples and visible minorities." The posting further instructed that "Candidates are asked to self-declare when applying to this hiring process." This means all applicants are asked to categorize themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status, and those who fall into the specified categories receive hiring priority over other candidates regardless of their individual qualifications relative to those of other applicants.
incidents.expected_harm: When an employer grants hiring priority based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, it reduces every candidate to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as a distinct individual with unique skills, experience, and character. Applicants who do not belong to the preferred categories are disadvantaged not because of anything related to their abilities or conduct, but because of immutable traits they were born with. Equally, applicants who do belong to the preferred categories are advanced partly on the same basis — their worth as professionals is filtered through demographic proxies rather than assessed on its own terms. The practice also compels every applicant to self-declare their demographic identity as a condition of fair consideration, treating personal details about sex, ethnicity, and disability as relevant hiring data rather than private matters. This institutionalises the habit of sorting people into demographic groups and undermines the principle that each person deserves to be seen and judged as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Security Intelligence Service
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service posted a Human Intelligence Officer job listing that encourages applicants to self-identify based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and notes gaps among women, people in certain ethnic categories, and persons with disabilities for the position.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) posted a job listing for the Human Intelligence Officer role, open across multiple regional offices in Canada. The posting includes two substantive employment equity passages. The first (block 59) states that CSIS is committed to increasing representation of its federal 'employment equity groups' — specifically persons with disabilities, racialized groups, indigenous peoples, and women — and identifies gaps among these groups for this position, strongly encouraging those who belong to one or more of these groups to self-declare in their cover letter. The second (block 138) repeats this commitment in nearly identical terms, referencing gaps specifically among indigenous peoples and visible minorities, and again strongly encouraging self-identification when applying. In both instances, the posting asks candidates to voluntarily label themselves by sex, ethnicity, or disability status as part of the application process for a national security intelligence role.
incidents.expected_harm: When CSIS asks candidates to self-identify by sex, ethnicity, or disability status, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their abilities, experience, and character. A candidate for a demanding national security position is asked to lead with a label about their body or ancestry rather than with the skills and judgement that define their fitness for the role. This practice signals that demographic traits are relevant hiring considerations, which undermines the principle that each person deserves to be assessed as a distinct individual. It also creates an incentive structure where belonging to a particular demographic group becomes a factor in advancing through a competitive process, meaning two equally qualified candidates are not treated equally. Over time, normalising this kind of self-labelling across the federal public service entrenches the idea that people are primarily representatives of demographic groups rather than individuals with singular worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Transportation Safety Board of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Transportation Safety Board of Canada posted a Director, Investigations – Rail/Pipeline position with a notice that selection may be limited to candidates based on their ethnicity, disability, or sex under the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Transportation Safety Board of Canada advertised an EX-02 Director, Investigations – Rail/Pipeline position (reference TSB26J-011170-000428). The posting includes two distinct pieces of qualifying language. First, it invites candidates to self-declare membership in one of the federal government's designated 'employment equity' groups: 'We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply.' Second, and more significantly, it explicitly states that selection may be restricted by ethnicity, disability, and sex: 'Selection may be limited to members of the following employment equity groups: Indigenous (Aboriginal) peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women.' This means candidates who do not fall into these categories based on their ethnicity, disability status, or sex face the possibility of being screened out entirely, regardless of their qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer reserves the right to limit selection to candidates based on their ethnicity, disability, or sex, it reduces every applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as individuals on their character, abilities, and experience. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the favoured categories are passed over not because of anything they have done or failed to do, but because of immutable traits they were born with. This practice institutionalises the sorting of people by demographic proxies, normalising the view that a person's background, ancestry, or biology are relevant factors in whether they deserve a senior public service role. It undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual and that advancement should reflect merit rather than membership in a demographic group.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada posted an AI Engineer position stating that priority may be given to applicants based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and asking candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada advertised a continuing AI Engineer position (CS-2) based in Ottawa under requisition number 25478, closing 29 June 2026. The posting states: "Priority may be given to the following designated employment equity groups: women, Indigenous Peoples* (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities and racialized persons*." A footnote clarifies that the federal Employment Equity Act uses the terms "Aboriginal peoples" and "visible minorities" for some of these categories. Candidates are also told: "Candidates are asked to self-declare when applying to this hiring process." A separate passage advises that indigenous candidates may be eligible for exceptions to the hybrid work arrangement on the basis of their ethnicity. Together these elements mean that candidates are sorted and potentially prioritised based on sex, ethnicity, and disability — demographic categories — rather than evaluated solely on their technical qualifications, experience, and abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution invites candidates to self-declare demographic characteristics and then signals that those characteristics will influence hiring priority, it reduces individuals to the categories they belong to rather than evaluating them on their unique abilities, character, and qualifications. An engineer assessed partly on sex, ethnicity, or disability status is not being seen as a whole person but as a representative of a group. This undermines the principle that each person possesses singular worth and deserves to be judged on what they can do and who they are. It also tells candidates from groups not listed that they will be deprioritised regardless of their skills, and tells candidates who are listed that their group membership, not their individual merit, contributed to their advancement — neither outcome honours a person's dignity as an individual. The practice normalises institutional categorisation of Canadians by immutable traits, eroding the shared civic foundation in which all people are evaluated on the same basis.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Natural Resources Canada
incidents.short_description: Natural Resources Canada posted a Research Engineer position at CanmetENERGY Varennes that invites candidates to self-declare their demographic category and states that qualified candidates who do so will be given priority consideration based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sex.
incidents.description_of_event: Natural Resources Canada advertised a Research Engineer position (EN-ENG-02) at CanmetENERGY in Varennes, Québec, closing June 25, 2026. The posting contains two distinct passages that categorize applicants by demographic traits. Under asset qualifications, an 'ORGANIZATIONAL NEEDS' section states that Natural Resources Canada is committed to maintaining a representative workforce and that qualified candidates who self-declare as members of a visible minority group, Aboriginal peoples, persons with disabilities, or women in non-traditional occupations "could be considered in priority." Separately, the Public Service of Canada's standard statement encourages candidates to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when applying. Together, these passages instruct candidates to identify themselves by skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sex, and tie that self-identification directly to priority consideration in the appointment decision.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are evaluated not solely on their engineering qualifications, knowledge, and abilities, but also on whether they can claim membership in a demographic category. A qualified engineer who does not belong to any listed group is passed over in favour of a candidate who does, regardless of relative merit. This reduces individuals to proxies for demographic representation rather than recognising them as distinct people with unique skills and demonstrated experience. The practice teaches candidates and hiring managers alike that demographic category membership is a legitimate and relevant factor in professional assessment, normalising the institutional habit of labelling and sorting individuals by immutable traits. It undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own character, abilities, and circumstances, and that advancement should reflect individual merit rather than group membership.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada posted an Instrument and Tool Maker position in St. John's stating that priority will be given to candidates based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and asking candidates to self-declare membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada advertised a continuing Instrument and Tool Maker role (TO-3) in its Design and Fabrication Services branch in St. John's, Newfoundland. The posting states that "priority may be given to" candidates based on sex (women), ethnicity (indigenous peoples including First Nations, Inuit, and Métis; and racialized persons), and disability — categories the federal government groups under its "employment equity" program. Candidates are explicitly asked to self-declare membership in one or more of these categories as part of the application process. A footnote acknowledges that the Employment Equity Act, which is under review, uses the terminology "Aboriginal peoples" and "visible minorities" for some of these categories.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government employer states that hiring priority will be given based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as an individual on the basis of their character, skills, and demonstrated abilities. Every candidate who applies for this position is asked to sort themselves into demographic categories before their qualifications are even considered. Those who fall outside the preferred categories receive lower priority regardless of their individual merits, while those who fall within the preferred categories are advanced on the basis of immutable traits rather than what they alone bring to the role. This practice treats demographic characteristics as proxies for individual worth, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be seen and assessed as a distinct individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Veterans Affairs Canada
incidents.short_description: Veterans Affairs Canada posted a Veterans Service Agent position that limits consideration, under certain appointment decisions, to candidates who self-declare based on skin colour, ethnicity, sex, or disability, and also encourages all applicants to self-declare membership in federally designated groups.
incidents.description_of_event: Veterans Affairs Canada posted a Veterans Service Agent position (reference DVA26J-069897-000292) closing June 15, 2026, for multiple locations across Ontario. Under the asset qualifications section, the posting states that Veterans Affairs Canada is committed to a representative workforce and that this organisational need may be identified as part of an appointment decision — in which case only qualified candidates who self-declare as members of a visible minority group, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, or women in non-traditional occupations would be considered. This is a hiring restriction: it explicitly narrows the eligible pool, for certain appointments, to candidates defined by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability. Separately, the posting's equity, diversity and inclusion section encourages all applicants to self-declare if they belong to one of the groups the federal government designates under its employment equity program — namely women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of a visible minority community — and states that the current Employment Equity Act does not fully encompass all such group members. The posting also invites candidates to self-identify across a broad set of characteristics including race, ethnicity, religion, age, disability, and sexual orientation through a "Gender-based Analysis Plus" lens.
incidents.expected_harm: When an appointment decision activates the organisational-need clause, candidates who do not self-declare a designated group membership are excluded from consideration entirely, regardless of their qualifications. This reduces individuals to demographic labels — skin colour, ethnicity, sex, and disability — rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and what they individually bring to the role. Even candidates who do advance are ranked partly on the basis of immutable traits rather than their unique merits. The blanket self-declaration request, applied to every applicant, institutionalises the practice of cataloguing people by demographic categories at the very first step of the hiring process. This treats each person not as a distinct individual but as a representative of a group, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own terms. The cumulative effect normalises the idea that demographic identity is a legitimate and relevant factor in public-service hiring, which erodes civic belonging and equal standing for all Canadians regardless of background.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada posted a job for an Intelligence Case Analyst and included standard federal language encouraging candidates to self-declare membership in designated groups based on sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada (FINTRAC) posted a competition for an Intelligence Case Analyst position (reference CFC26J-094095-000007) based in Ottawa. The posting includes the standard federal government statement under an 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' heading: 'The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply.' This language asks candidates to identify themselves according to the federal government's 'employment equity' program groupings, which categorise applicants based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, among other traits, rather than evaluating each person solely on their individual qualifications, experience, and abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer asks candidates to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, or disability status as part of the hiring process, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct people with unique skills, character, and experience. Candidates are sorted by immutable traits before their qualifications are even assessed. This practice signals that a person's group membership is a relevant factor in their candidacy, undermining the principle that each individual deserves to be considered on their own merits. Over time, the ubiquity of this language across federal postings normalises the idea that demographic categories are a legitimate and routine basis for employment decisions, eroding the foundation of a hiring system that treats every candidate as a singular individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: The Department of National Defence posted a job for Senior Engineering Technologist positions and included language encouraging candidates to self-declare membership in federal employment equity categories, with selection potentially based on those declared categories.
incidents.description_of_event: A Government of Canada job posting from the Department of National Defence (DND) advertised multiple Senior Engineering Technologist positions (EG-05) in Toronto, Ontario. The posting included a dedicated 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' section that invites applicants to self-declare membership in the federal government's designated employment equity groups. The posting states that selection may be based on candidates self-declaring as belonging to one or more of those groups — specifically listing ethnicity (indigenous peoples and visible minorities), disability, and sex (women). It also includes the standard Public Service of Canada boilerplate encouraging candidates to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated groups. Candidates are thus explicitly told that their demographic characteristics — their ethnicity, disability status, and sex — are factors that can determine whether they are selected over other qualified candidates.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution states that hiring selection may be based on a candidate's ethnicity, disability status, or sex, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as a distinct individual. A qualified engineer whose technical knowledge, work ethic, and professional record are assessed partly through the lens of what demographic boxes they tick is not being evaluated on their individual merit. This practice sends a clear message to every applicant: who you are demographically affects your chances of being hired, regardless of your individual abilities and character. Applicants who do not belong to the listed categories learn they will be passed over in favour of others whose qualifications may be equivalent or lesser but whose demographic profile is preferred. This undermines the principle that each person's singular worth deserves recognition on its own terms, and entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories as a routine part of decision-making.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada posted a Project Manager position in Ottawa stating that priority may be given to applicants based on their sex, ethnicity, or disability status, and asking candidates to self-declare their demographic group membership.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada (NRC) advertised a Project Manager role in its Digital Technologies unit in Ottawa. The posting states that "priority may be given to the following designated employment equity groups: women, Indigenous Peoples* (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities and racialized persons." A footnote clarifies that the federal Employment Equity Act uses the terminology "Aboriginal peoples and visible minorities." In addition to the hiring preference, the posting asks all candidates to self-declare their membership in one or more of these demographic categories during the application process, stating: "Candidates are asked to self-declare when applying to this hiring process." The posting thus both signals a preference for applicants sorted by sex, ethnicity, and disability, and invites the collection of demographic identity data from every candidate.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer signals that hiring priority turns on a person's sex, ethnicity, or disability status, it reduces candidates to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them as individuals on the strength of their character, abilities, and experience. A qualified candidate who does not belong to a preferred category is placed at a disadvantage not because of anything they have done or any ability they lack, but solely because of who they are. Equally, asking every applicant to self-declare their demographic group membership embeds the practice of labelling and categorising people by immutable traits at the very first step of the hiring process. This normalises the idea that institutions should sort people by demographic categories, eroding the principle that each person deserves to be seen and assessed as a unique individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Natural Resources Canada
incidents.short_description: Natural Resources Canada posted a PhD research affiliate position and invited candidates to self-declare membership in federal employment equity groups, categorising applicants by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Natural Resources Canada's Geological Survey of Canada posted a PhD research opportunity through the Research Affiliate Program (RAP), focused on carbon stocks in the fjords of eastern Canada and based at ISMER-UQAR in Rimouski, Québec. Under the heading "Equity, diversity and inclusion," the posting states: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This language invites candidates to categorise themselves by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — the four groups enumerated under the federal government's "employment equity" program — as part of the application process for a scientific research role. The invitation to self-declare based on these characteristics applies universally to federal postings and is the documented phenomenon here.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government employer asks candidates to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, disability, or sexual orientation, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their scientific qualifications, skills, and demonstrated abilities. A PhD applicant with expertise in marine geology and organic geochemistry is being asked, as a condition of the hiring process, to define themselves by immutable traits. This practice signals that demographic membership is a relevant factor in who advances — and it will shape hiring outcomes accordingly. The practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into identity-based groups, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be seen and assessed as a distinct individual. It also fragments the public service workforce along demographic lines rather than building a common professional community united by shared competence and shared purpose.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Fisheries and Oceans Canada > Canadian Coast Guard
incidents.short_description: The Department of National Defence and Canadian Coast Guard posted a Chief Engineer position that encourages candidates to self-identify based on ethnicity, disability, and sex under the federal government's employment equity program, and states that selection may be based on candidates belonging to these designated groups.
incidents.description_of_event: A posting for Chief Engineer (Atlantic Region) roles with the Department of National Defence / Canadian Coast Guard invites candidates to self-declare membership in what the federal government calls "designated employment equity groups" — specifically ethnicity (indigenous peoples and visible minorities), disability, and sex (women). The posting states that "selection may be based on qualified candidates self-declaring as belonging to one or more of the designated groups: Indigenous Peoples, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women," and encourages candidates to self-identify when applying. The posting also includes the Public Service of Canada's standard encouragement to self-declare membership in these same groups. This means that a candidate's skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, or sex can directly influence whether they are selected over another equally qualified candidate.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer states that hiring selection may be based on a candidate's skin colour, ethnicity, disability, or sex, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and what they have actually demonstrated. An engineer with the right certifications, experience, and competencies can be passed over — or advanced — not on the strength of their qualifications but because of immutable traits they were born with. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as a distinct individual. It also normalises the institutional practice of sorting Canadians into demographic groups, signalling that belonging to the right category is a legitimate factor in determining professional advancement. That signal, repeated across thousands of federal postings, entrenches demographic labelling as an ordinary feature of public life rather than an affront to individual dignity.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Communications Security Establishment
incidents.short_description: The Communications Security Establishment posted a Foreign Language Intelligence Analyst (Chinese) position and stated that priority in hiring will be given to candidates who self-declare membership in groups defined by sex, ethnicity, and disability under the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Communications Security Establishment (CSE) posted a job opening for a Foreign Language Intelligence Analyst (Chinese), classified at the UNI-07 level with a salary range of $93,510–$110,009. Within the posting, CSE states that it will give hiring priority to candidates who self-declare as belonging to one or more groups under the federal government's 'employment equity' program: women; indigenous peoples (including First Nations, Inuit, and Métis); persons with disabilities (including those with mental health conditions and neurodivergent people); and persons from racial or ethnic groups. The posting notes this is done to address CSE's 'current representation gaps.' The posting also references the Employment Equity Act and its terminology. Candidates are asked to self-declare their membership in these categories, meaning their sex, ethnicity, or disability status becomes a factor in determining hiring priority.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer awards hiring priority based on a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability status, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories are placed at a disadvantage regardless of their individual merits. Those who do belong face the indignity of being advanced not because of what they can do, but because of immutable traits they were born with or conditions they live with. This practice signals to all applicants that the institution views people first as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals. It undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual and that advancement in public institutions should reflect demonstrated ability and merit.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Communications Security Establishment
incidents.short_description: The Communications Security Establishment posted a job for a Clinical/Forensic Psychologist and stated that priority in hiring will be given to candidates who declare membership in federal employment equity categories based on sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Communications Security Establishment (CSE) advertised a Clinical/Forensic Psychologist position (UNI-10, closing 2026-09-04) on its careers website. Under the "Important information" section, the posting states that "priority may be given to persons who self-declare as belonging to one, or more, designated employment equity groups: women; Indigenous peoples, including First Nations who are status, or registered and non-status, or non-registered, Inuit and Métis; persons with disabilities, including people with a mental health condition and neurodivergent people; and persons from racial or ethnic groups." The posting further notes that CSE is "committed to fostering a culture of equity, diversity and inclusion" and references the federal Employment Equity Act. Candidates are thus invited to self-declare their sex, ethnicity, or disability status so that this information can be used as a factor in deciding who receives hiring priority.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer awards hiring priority based on a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability status, it reduces applicants to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Candidates who do not belong to the preferred categories are disadvantaged regardless of their individual merit, while candidates who do belong are advantaged regardless of individual circumstance. This practice signals to all applicants that their demographic identity is a material factor in institutional decisions, undermining the principle that every person possesses singular worth as an individual. Over time, systematically preferring candidates on the basis of immutable traits erodes trust in merit-based hiring, harms the dignity of those hired under a preference (whose competence may be questioned), and entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by demographic labels rather than recognising their unique qualities.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Communications Security Establishment
incidents.short_description: The Communications Security Establishment posted a Cryptanalyst / Crypto-Scientist job stating that priority in hiring will be given to candidates who self-declare membership in designated groups based on sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Communications Security Establishment (CSE) posted a job competition for Cryptanalyst / Crypto-Scientist positions at the UNMA-07 and UNMA-08 levels, closing on 2026-07-13. Within the posting's 'Important information' section, CSE states that "priority may be given to persons who self-declare as belonging to one, or more, designated employment equity groups: women; Indigenous peoples, including First Nations who are status, or registered and non-status, or non-registered, Inuit and Métis; persons with disabilities, including people with a mental health condition and neurodivergent people; and persons from racial or ethnic groups." The posting also references the Employment Equity Act by name and invites candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories. Additionally, the posting's 'About CSE' section states that "CSE is committed to fostering a culture of equity, diversity and inclusion" and directs readers to CSE's diversity and inclusion initiatives. The hiring preference is explicitly tied to sex, ethnicity, and disability — the demographic categories enumerated in the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for these positions are evaluated partly on the basis of their sex, ethnicity, and disability status rather than solely on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. When an institution attaches priority in hiring to demographic categories, it reduces individual applicants to proxies for group membership. A person's sex, ethnic background, or disability status tells an employer nothing about their capacity to perform cryptanalysis or design cryptographic algorithms — yet the posting makes these traits relevant to whether a candidate advances. Qualified individuals who do not belong to the listed categories are placed at a disadvantage not because of anything they have done or failed to demonstrate, but because of who they are. At the same time, individuals who do belong to the listed categories risk having their accomplishments attributed to their demographic characteristics rather than their skills. This approach treats people as representatives of groups rather than as distinct individuals, which undermines each person's claim to be seen and assessed on their own merits.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Royal Canadian Mounted Police
incidents.short_description: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police posted a Curriculum Designer job in Regina that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in the federal government's designated employment equity groups based on sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police advertised four indeterminate Curriculum Designer positions based in Regina, Saskatchewan (reference RCM26J-016113-000222). Under the 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' section, the posting states: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This language invites applicants to voluntarily identify themselves by sex, ethnicity, or disability status — the traits that constitute the federal government's designated groups under its employment equity program — as part of the application process. The posting was published on 9 June 2026 with a closing date of 30 June 2026.
incidents.expected_harm: By inviting applicants to label themselves according to demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, and disability — as part of a competitive hiring process, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police treats a person's immutable traits as relevant information in assessing their candidacy. This reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Candidates who choose not to self-declare, or whose traits do not match the preferred categories, are assessed on a subtly unequal footing. The practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable characteristics, and signals to all applicants that who they are demographically matters to the outcome, not just what they can do.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted a job for a Bilingual Senior Manager, Compliance and Governance while encouraging applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation under the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) advertised a permanent, full-time Bilingual Senior Manager, Compliance and Governance position open to candidates across multiple Canadian cities. The posting includes a section titled 'Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion' which states: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This language encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation — categories grouped under the federal government's 'employment equity' program. This same standardised encouragement boilerplate appears across a very large share of federal and Crown corporation postings; its near-universal presence is itself the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: When a Crown corporation signals to applicants that their sex, ethnicity, disability status, religion, or sexual orientation is a relevant factor in their candidacy, it reduces each person to a collection of demographic labels rather than recognising them as a distinct individual with unique skills, experience, and character. Candidates are prompted to understand that who they are in a categorical sense matters to the institution alongside what they can do. This framing normalises the practice of sorting applicants by immutable traits, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their individual abilities and demonstrated merit alone. Over time, widespread institutional repetition of this language embeds the expectation that demographic identity is a legitimate hiring consideration, eroding the standard that advancement should reflect individual capability.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's job posting for an Enumerator position in Toronto encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, sorting candidates into demographic categories rather than evaluating them purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator position based in Toronto, Ontario, collecting housing market survey data at $23.89 per hour. The posting includes a section titled 'Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion' which states: 'We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply.' This language explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation (which the federal government groups under its 'employment equity' program). Rather than simply inviting anyone with the relevant skills and qualifications to apply, CMHC's posting singles out candidates by their demographic characteristics, reducing them to group membership as part of the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a hiring institution encourages candidates to apply based on their sex, ethnicity, disability status, religion, or sexual orientation, it signals that these immutable traits are relevant factors in selecting who gets the job. Applicants are evaluated not solely on their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs, but at least partly on which demographic categories they belong to. This reduces individuals to proxies for their group memberships, undermining their recognition as unique persons deserving assessment on individual merit. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories receive an implicit message that the institution values their demographic background less, while those who do belong are seen first through the lens of a label rather than as distinct individuals. Over time, normalising this practice across nearly every federal posting embeds demographic categorisation as a routine feature of public hiring, chipping away at the principle that each person's worth and suitability must be judged on their own qualities alone.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted a casual Enumerator position in Regina and encouraged applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation under its federal employment equity commitment.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator role based in Regina, Saskatchewan, running from September 14 to November 6, 2026. The posting includes a section titled "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion" that explicitly encourages applications from candidates sorted by a range of immutable traits. The relevant passage reads: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This language — standard across CMHC postings — invites candidates to present themselves through demographic categories including sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation rather than purely on their individual qualifications and abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal Crown corporation openly encourages applications on the basis of sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, it signals to every applicant that demographic membership is a relevant factor in hiring. Rather than evaluating each person on their character, capabilities, and demonstrated experience, the institution sorts candidates into identity-based groups. Applicants who belong to the listed categories are implicitly invited to present their demographic traits as credentials, while those outside these categories receive the message that the institution views them as less preferred. This practice reduces people to labels defined by immutable characteristics, undermining the recognition of each person as a singular individual whose worth is not determined by the group to which they belong. Over thousands of federal postings, this approach normalises the idea that demographic classification is a legitimate basis for institutional decision-making, eroding the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own terms.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted an Enumerator position in Red Deer, Alberta, encouraging applications based on sex, ethnicity, religion, disability, and sexual orientation under its employment equity commitment.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator position based in Red Deer, Alberta, as part of its housing surveys team. The posting includes an employment equity statement that explicitly encourages applications from candidates sorted by sex, ethnicity, religion, disability, and sexual orientation. The verbatim statement reads: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This language, which the federal government groups under its "employment equity" program, is attached to the posting as a standard institutional commitment, categorizing candidates by immutable personal traits as part of the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: By explicitly sorting applicants into demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, religion, disability, and sexual orientation — CMHC reduces candidates to their immutable traits rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. A person applying for this enumerator role will encounter an institution that signals it views them first through the lens of which demographic groups they belong to. This undermines the principle that every person possesses singular worth and deserves to be assessed as an individual. Normalizing this kind of categorical labelling across nearly every federal posting reinforces the idea that demographic identity is a relevant qualification for public service employment, eroding the standard that hiring decisions should rest on individual merit and specific demonstrated competence.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted a casual Enumerator position in Lethbridge encouraging applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation as part of its stated employment equity commitment.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator role based in Lethbridge, Alberta, paying $23.89 per hour for a two-month term beginning September 14, 2026. The posting includes a section titled "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion" which states the organization is "committed to employment equity" and explicitly encourages applications from women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans, and persons based on race, ethnicity, religion, sexual orientation, and gender identity and expression. This standard encouragement language — attached to a routine survey data-collection role — categorizes candidates by a broad list of immutable personal traits rather than evaluating them purely on the skills and qualifications the role requires.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer explicitly lists demographic categories and encourages applications on that basis, it reduces candidates to those categories rather than recognising them as distinct individuals. A person applying for this Enumerator role will be viewed, at least in part, through the lens of their sex, ethnicity, disability status, religion, or sexual orientation — traits that have no bearing on their ability to collect housing survey data accurately and on time. This practice sends a message that demographic identity is a relevant criterion in hiring, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs alone. Repeated across virtually every CMHC and federal posting, the cumulative effect normalises institutional labelling of Canadians by immutable traits and erodes the expectation that hiring decisions reflect individual merit.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's job posting for an Enumerator role in Halifax encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, sorting candidates into demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator position based in Halifax, Nova Scotia. The posting includes a section titled "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion" which states: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This language explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation — sorting prospective candidates into demographic categories as part of the hiring process. The same section also states that CMHC is "an inclusive workplace where diversity of thought – and of people – are recognized, valued, and considered essential to achieving our mission," further anchoring the posting in a framework that categorizes applicants by immutable traits rather than evaluating each person on their individual character and abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer encourages applications by enumerating demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation — it reduces candidates to those traits rather than evaluating them as individuals on their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. Applicants learn that the institution views them first through the lens of demographic membership. This undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual, and it normalizes the expectation that one's immutable characteristics are relevant qualifications for employment. The practice entrenches the habit of sorting people into groups, eroding the shared civic identity that comes from being evaluated as a distinct person rather than a representative of a category.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted an Enumerator position in Edmonton while encouraging applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation under its federal 'employment equity' commitment.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator position based in Edmonton, Alberta, for a two-month term beginning September 14, 2026. The posting includes a section titled 'Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion' that states CMHC is 'committed to employment equity' and explicitly encourages applications from candidates categorized by sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation. The exact language reads: 'We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply.' This is the standard encouragement formula CMHC attaches to its job postings, sorting potential applicants into demographic categories as a feature of its hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: By singling out applicants based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, CMHC's posting reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. A person applying for this role is first invited to understand themselves as a representative of a demographic group rather than as an individual with a unique combination of skills and experience. This practice normalises the institutional habit of labelling people by immutable traits, eroding the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual. Repeated across nearly every federal posting, this pattern entrenches demographic categorisation as a routine feature of public sector hiring, undermining the ideal that institutions should evaluate people on who they are rather than what group they belong to.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted an Enumerator position in Calgary that explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation, religion, and other identity categories rather than evaluating candidates solely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator position based in Calgary, Alberta (Job Requisition ID: 12177), paying $23.89 per hour for a two-month term beginning September 14, 2026. The posting includes a standard statement under the heading "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion" that reads: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This language explicitly invites applications on the basis of sex, ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation, religion, and other identity categories. The posting categorises prospective candidates by a wide range of immutable and demographic traits and signals that membership in these categories is a relevant consideration in the hiring process, rather than evaluating each applicant purely on their individual character, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: By signalling that a candidate's sex, ethnicity, disability status, religion, sexual orientation, and gender identity are relevant factors in the hiring process, CMHC reduces individual applicants to demographic categories. Each person who applies is assessed, at least in part, through the lens of the groups they belong to rather than their unique character, skills, and demonstrated abilities. This approach sends a message to all applicants — those in listed categories and those outside them alike — that their demographic profile carries weight in hiring decisions. It undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual, and it normalises institutional sorting of people by immutable traits. Over time, this practice erodes the idea that advancement in public institutions should reflect individual merit and demonstrated need, replacing it with a framework where demographic identity becomes a credential.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's posting for a Clerk, Surveys position encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, sorting candidates into demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Clerk, Surveys position open to candidates in Calgary or Vancouver. The posting includes a standard hiring preference statement under the heading "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion," which reads: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This language explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation (which the federal government groups under its "employment equity" program). Rather than inviting all candidates on the basis of their individual qualifications and character, the posting singles out applicants by demographic categories as part of a stated institutional commitment to employment equity.
incidents.expected_harm: By encouraging applications based on demographic traits — sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation — CMHC treats candidates as representatives of categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique abilities and character. This approach reduces a person's identity to immutable characteristics, signalling that demographic membership is a relevant factor in the hiring process. Rather than evaluating each applicant solely on their skills, experience, and demonstrated abilities, the institution layers demographic considerations into its recruitment framing. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits, and normalises the idea that who a person is matters less than which demographic group they belong to.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted an Enumerator position in Vancouver that encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation under its employment equity commitment.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator position based in Vancouver, BC, collecting housing market survey data at $23.89 per hour. The posting includes a section titled 'Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion' which states the organization is 'committed to employment equity' and explicitly encourages applications based on sex (women), ethnicity (indigenous peoples and persons of all races and ethnicities), disability, religion, sexual orientation, and gender identity. The posting invites candidates to apply on the basis of these demographic traits, framing this encouragement as central to CMHC's hiring approach. This language appears in a standardized block attached to CMHC job postings, and its routine inclusion across postings is itself the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: By explicitly sorting applicants into demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation — and encouraging applications on those bases, CMHC treats candidates as representatives of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals with unique skills, character, and abilities. This reduces each applicant's identity to a checklist of immutable traits, which runs counter to the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on who they are as an individual. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories receive an implicit signal that their application is less welcome, while those who do belong to listed categories are invited to see their demographic traits as relevant qualifications. Over time, this institutionalises the habit of sorting people by demographic proxies rather than individual merit, undermining the shared civic belonging that comes from institutions treating everyone as equally worthy of fair, individual assessment.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted a casual Enumerator position in Montreal that encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation as part of its stated commitment to its 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a casual Enumerator position based in Montreal (Requisition ID 12193), closing June 26, 2026. The posting includes a section titled 'Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion' that explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation. The verbatim language reads: 'We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply.' This standard encouragement language — applied uniformly across CMHC postings — categorizes prospective candidates by immutable demographic traits as part of the institution's own framing of what makes a desirable applicant pool.
incidents.expected_harm: By sorting and encouraging applicants based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, CMHC reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. A person who applies will be assessed, at least in part, through the lens of which demographic groups they belong to, rather than solely on their character, skills, and abilities. This normalises the institutional habit of labelling people by immutable traits and signals that group membership is a relevant qualification for public employment. Over time, such framing erodes the principle that each person possesses singular worth as an individual, and it undermines the idea that hiring decisions should reflect what a person can actually do.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Energy Regulator
incidents.short_description: The Canada Energy Regulator posted a Policy Advisor position in which selection may be limited to applicants based on their ethnicity, skin colour, disability, or sex under the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canada Energy Regulator advertised a Policy Advisor position (reference ERG26J-170903-000034) based in Calgary, closing June 24, 2026. Under the asset qualifications section, the posting states that "in order to support employment equity, diversity, and inclusion goals and to achieve operational objectives, selection may be limited to a member of one of the following groups seeking equity: Indigenous peoples*, visible minorities, persons with disabilities, or women." This means that candidates could be screened or selected based on their ethnicity, skin colour, disability status, or sex — rather than solely on their individual abilities and demonstrated qualifications. The posting also invites candidates to self-declare membership in a federal "designated employment equity group," stating: "We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply."
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer restricts or limits selection to applicants based on their ethnicity, skin colour, disability, or sex, it evaluates people as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals with unique abilities and qualifications. Candidates who do not belong to a listed category face a materially reduced chance of selection regardless of how well-suited they are to the role — their individual merit is subordinated to their demographic identity. At the same time, candidates who do belong to a listed category are advanced in part because of an immutable trait rather than their character or capabilities alone. Both outcomes fail the principle that every person deserves to be assessed for their individual qualities. The practice also requires applicants to label themselves by these categories, compelling people to reduce their own identity to a demographic box in order to be considered fairly. This normalises institutional sorting of Canadians by trait categories and undermines the shared civic belonging that comes from recognising each applicant as a person first.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Border Services Agency
incidents.short_description: The Canada Border Services Agency posted a job for an Informal Conflict Management System Practitioner/Senior Practitioner and included standard federal language encouraging applications based on skin colour, sex, ethnicity, and disability, as well as a statement that candidates who self-declare as belonging to certain designated groups will be considered first.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) posted a competition for Informal Conflict Management System (ICMS) Practitioner and Senior Practitioner roles (PE-04/PE-05) across multiple Canadian locations. The posting contains several layers of identity-based hiring language. First, under the asset qualifications section, the posting states that for selection decisions, CBSA "may first consider candidates who meet the qualifications of the position and self-declare themselves as belonging to the Indigenous peoples and Persons with Disabilities designated groups," giving priority based on ethnicity and disability. The same section invites all candidates belonging to what the posting calls "equity-deserving" groups — specifically women, visible minorities, indigenous peoples, and persons with disabilities — to self-declare in their application. Second, the posting's Equity, Diversity and Inclusion section carries the standard Public Service of Canada boilerplate encouraging self-declaration for membership in what it calls "designated employment equity groups." Third, a further CBSA-specific paragraph encourages applications from visible minorities, indigenous peoples, women, persons with disabilities, and persons of the 2SLGBTQIA+ community, categorizing candidates by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation. Together these passages reduce candidates to demographic categories as part of the formal selection process.
incidents.expected_harm: When an employer signals that candidates will receive first consideration based on their ethnicity or disability, and invites all applicants to categorize themselves by skin colour, sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, the institution reduces individuals to demographic labels rather than evaluating them on character, qualifications, and demonstrated abilities. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories are evaluated on a lower-priority basis regardless of their individual merits. Candidates who do belong to the listed categories are assessed partly through the lens of immutable traits rather than as whole individuals. This practice normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people by demographic proxies, undermining the principle that every person deserves recognition for their unique qualities. Over time, repeated exposure to identity-first hiring signals erodes the understanding that advancement should reflect what a person can do and who they are as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada advertises a Technical Officer, Quality Assurance position in Ottawa while stating that priority in hiring will be given to candidates based on their sex, ethnicity, and disability status, and requiring applicants to self-declare their membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: A job posting from the National Research Council Canada for a Technical Officer, Quality Assurance role (classification TO-3, Ottawa) states: "Priority may be given to the following designated employment equity groups: women, Indigenous Peoples* (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities and racialized persons*." The posting also notes that "The Employment Equity Act, which is under review, uses the terminology Aboriginal peoples and visible minorities," and explicitly requires that "Candidates are asked to self-declare when applying to this hiring process." These statements mean that hiring decisions for this role are influenced by a candidate's sex, ethnicity, and disability status, and that applicants must categorise themselves by these traits as a formal step in the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: By giving hiring priority based on a person's sex, ethnicity, or disability status, the National Research Council sorts applicants into demographic categories rather than evaluating them purely on their character, abilities, and qualifications. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories are disadvantaged regardless of their individual merits, while those who do belong receive an advantage unrelated to their competence. The mandatory self-declaration requirement compounds this by compelling every applicant to label themselves by immutable traits as a condition of being considered. This reduces people to demographic proxies and undermines the principle that each person possesses singular worth as an individual. The practice normalises institutional categorisation of people by group membership and signals that the government values demographic identity over individual achievement, eroding the ideal of a hiring process that treats each applicant as a unique person.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's posting for a Bilingual Senior Specialist, Compliance and Quality Assurance encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, sorting candidates into demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a Bilingual Senior Specialist, Compliance and Quality Assurance position (Job Requisition ID: 12139), a temporary full-time hybrid role available across multiple Canadian cities. The posting includes a statement under the heading "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion" that reads: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This statement explicitly encourages applications on the basis of sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, categorizing prospective applicants by these demographic traits as part of CMHC's federal "employment equity" obligations.
incidents.expected_harm: By explicitly encouraging applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, CMHC reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and qualifications. Each person who applies for this role brings a unique combination of skills, experience, and demonstrated capacity — yet the posting signals that demographic characteristics are a relevant factor in the hiring process. This undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories receive an implicit message that their applications are less valued, while those who do belong receive a message that their demographic traits, rather than their individual merit, are part of what makes them desirable. This normalises institutional sorting of people by immutable characteristics and works against a hiring culture grounded in individual worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada
incidents.short_description: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada posted an IT Technical Analyst position that explicitly states preference will be given to candidates based on their ethnicity, disability, and sex, and invites applicants to self-declare membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) posted a competition for IT Technical Analyst positions (Salesforce Developer and Integration Developer streams) closing June 16, 2026. The posting contains multiple layers of identity-based hiring language under its federal "employment equity" program. Under the "Organizational Needs" section, the posting states that preference may be given to candidates who are indigenous people, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, or women. Under the "Equity, Diversity and Inclusion" section, the posting states that selection may be limited to members of the following employment equity groups: indigenous (Aboriginal) peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women. It also invites candidates who identify by these same categories to self-declare in their application. A separate link to IRCC's "commitment to anti-racism" is included in additional links, connecting the posting to a broader departmental action plan. Together, these elements mean candidates are sorted, tracked, and potentially preferred or exclusively selected based on their ethnicity, disability status, and sex rather than solely on their individual abilities and qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer explicitly states that hiring selection may be limited to people of certain ethnicities, disability statuses, and sexes, it reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals with unique abilities, experience, and character. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories face a real possibility of being excluded from competition for a position their qualifications fully merit. Candidates who do belong to the listed categories are placed in a position where their ethnicity, disability, or sex — not their abilities alone — becomes a factor in whether they advance. This system instructs individuals to label themselves by immutable traits as a condition of fair consideration, eroding the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own merits. Repeated across thousands of federal postings, this practice normalises institutional sorting of Canadians by demographic group, undermining the shared civic belonging and recognition of individual worth that a healthy public service depends on.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Natural Resources Canada > Geological Survey of Canada
incidents.short_description: Natural Resources Canada's Geological Survey of Canada posted a PhD research affiliate position that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in federal employment equity categories, and states that qualified candidates based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sex could be given priority consideration.
incidents.description_of_event: Natural Resources Canada – Geological Survey of Canada (Atlantic) posted a Research Affiliate Program position for a PhD student in Oceanography with a Marine Geology specialization, based in Dartmouth, Nova Scotia. The posting includes two distinct pieces of identity-categorization language. First, the Public Service of Canada's standard employment equity statement invites applicants to self-declare if they belong to one of the federal government's designated employment equity groups. Second, a Natural Resources Canada-specific statement goes further, advising that qualified candidates who self-declare as members of a visible minority group, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, or women in non-traditional occupations could be considered in priority as part of the appointment decision. These statements categorize candidates by skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sex, and use those categories as factors in hiring decisions.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates for this research position are sorted and prioritized based on their skin colour, ethnicity, disability status, and sex rather than being evaluated solely on their scientific qualifications, experience, and individual merit. A hiring process that assigns priority based on immutable or demographic traits reduces candidates to demographic categories, denying each applicant recognition as a distinct individual with unique abilities and character. Applicants who do not belong to the listed categories are placed at a disadvantage not because of anything they have done or failed to demonstrate, but because of who they are. This practice undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity deserving recognition on their own terms, and signals to candidates that their demographic profile shapes their chances in a federal institution.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: The Department of National Defence's posting for a Mason/Tile setter inventory states that selection may be limited to candidates based on their ethnicity, disability status, or sex under the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: The Department of National Defence posted an inventory for Mason/Tile setter positions (GL-PCF-07) located in Esquimalt, BC; Kingston, ON; and Petawawa, ON, closing August 8, 2026. Under the 'Asset qualifications' section, the posting states: "Selection may be limited to members of the following Employment Equity groups: Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, women." It further states that "selection may be based on qualified candidates self-declaring as belonging to one or more of the designated groups: Indigenous Peoples, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women," and encourages candidates to indicate if they belong to any of these groups. The posting also carries the standard Public Service of Canada statement encouraging self-declaration as a member of a federal 'employment equity' designated group. Together, these passages mean that a candidate's ethnicity, disability status, or sex can determine whether they are selected — not only their qualifications as a mason or tile setter.
incidents.expected_harm: When selection for a skilled trades position is made on the basis of a candidate's ethnicity, disability status, or sex, the institution reduces people to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as individuals on their qualifications, character, and abilities.
