A qualified mason or tile setter is passed over or advanced not because of their craftsmanship or professional record, but because of immutable characteristics they were born with. This directly undermines each applicant's singular worth, treating demographic membership as a proxy for merit and denying individuals the recognition they deserve as distinct people. It also normalises the institutional practice of sorting Canadians into categories based on traits they cannot change, eroding the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on what they actually bring to the role.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: The Department of National Defence posted a Concrete Finisher inventory in Victoria, BC, stating that selection may be limited to members of certain employment equity groups based on sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Department of National Defence posted a Concrete Finisher (GL-PCF-08) inventory position based in Victoria, British Columbia, closing August 8, 2026. Under the asset qualifications section, the posting states that "selection may be limited to members of the following Employment Equity groups: Aboriginal persons, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, women." It further states that "selection may be based on qualified candidates self-declaring as belonging to one or more of the designated groups: Indigenous Peoples, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women." Applicants are encouraged to self-identify as belonging to one of these groups when applying. The Public Service of Canada's standard section also encourages self-declaration as a member of a "designated employment equity group." The posting thus conditions selection on a candidate's ethnicity, disability status, and sex — traits unrelated to the ability to finish concrete.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government employer conditions or limits selection on whether a candidate belongs to a category defined by sex, ethnicity, or disability, it reduces individuals to demographic labels rather than evaluating them on their character, skills, and demonstrated abilities. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories face a structural disadvantage regardless of their qualifications, and candidates who do belong to those categories are assessed partly through an identity lens rather than purely as individuals. This practice treats immutable traits as relevant hiring criteria, undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation on their own merits. It also pressures candidates to self-identify by demographic category as a condition of competitive consideration, compelling them to present themselves as group members rather than as individuals with unique skills and experience. Over time, normalising this kind of categorisation in the public service erodes the standard that people should advance on the basis of what they can do, not on what demographic box they can check.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Air Transport Security Authority
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Air Transport Security Authority (CATSA) posted a General Manager, Program Deployment position that encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and asks candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories as part of the online application.
incidents.description_of_event: CATSA posted a General Manager, Program Deployment role at its Ottawa headquarters, open through June 26, 2026. Under a section titled 'Our commitment to Diversity, Equity and Inclusion,' the posting states that CATSA — as 'an employer committed to fostering a diverse and inclusive workforce that reflects the public we serve' — encourages candidates in what it calls 'designated groups' — specifically women, visible minorities, aboriginal persons, and persons with disabilities — to apply and to self-declare by answering questions on the online application form. This is the standard language attached to federal employment equity postings, and its near-universal use across the federal public service and Crown corporations is the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: By sorting applicants into demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, and disability — and inviting them to self-declare those traits as part of their application, CATSA reduces candidates to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals based on their character, abilities, and qualifications. This institutionalises the practice of assigning weight to immutable traits in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories receive an implicit signal that they are less preferred, while those who do belong are framed primarily through the lens of group membership rather than individual achievement. The routine attachment of this language to virtually every federal posting normalises demographic categorisation as a standard feature of Canadian public service hiring.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada
incidents.short_description: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada is hiring a Farm Operations Supervisor and explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, while warning that selection may be limited to candidates from those groups.
incidents.description_of_event: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada posted a Farm Operations Supervisor position (GL-MAN-09) based at experimental farm sites in Quebec. The posting contains two layers of identity-based categorization. First, a standard Public Service of Canada statement invites candidates to self-declare membership in the federal government's "designated employment equity groups." Second, Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada adds its own statement encouraging women (specifically in non-traditional occupations), indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minority groups to apply and declare themselves, and warns that "selection may be limited to candidates belonging to one or more of the groups." This warning converts what might otherwise read as mere encouragement into a potential hiring restriction, signalling that candidates who do not belong to those categories based on sex, ethnicity, or disability may be screened out.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer warns that selection may be limited to candidates belonging to specific groups defined by sex, ethnicity, and disability, it reduces applicants to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as individuals on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Candidates who fall outside those categories receive a clear signal that their application may be set aside regardless of their skills or experience. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as a unique individual, and it normalizes the practice of institutions sorting people by immutable traits as a condition of access to employment and economic opportunity.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Prairies Economic Development Canada
incidents.short_description: Prairies Economic Development Canada posted positions for Business Officer and Senior Business Officer while encouraging applicants to self-identify as members of designated groups under the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: Prairies Economic Development Canada advertised Business Officer (CO-01) and Senior Business Officer (CO-02) positions based in Winnipeg, Manitoba, closing June 22, 2026. The posting includes standard federal employment equity language stating: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the Canadians we serve. We promote employment equity and encourage you to indicate if you belong to one of the designated groups when you apply." The posting repeats a substantially identical statement in a dedicated "Equity, diversity and inclusion" section, this time explicitly inviting candidates to self-declare membership in one of the federal government's designated employment equity groups. The posting thus both encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability (which the federal government groups under its "employment equity" program) and solicits self-identification along those same lines as part of the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer asks every applicant to categorize themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status, it reduces candidates to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals with unique skills, knowledge, and experience. A person who declines to self-identify along these lines is treated differently in a process that has been structured to weigh group membership alongside individual merit. This practice signals that who a person is — in demographic terms — matters to the institution in a way that has nothing to do with their ability to perform the work. It normalizes the idea that government employment decisions turn in part on immutable traits, which undermines the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own character and abilities alone.

incidents.short_description: Parks Canada posted a Maintenance Coordinator position at the Mingan Archipelago National Park Reserve that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in the federal government's designated employment equity groups.
incidents.description_of_event: Parks Canada posted a Maintenance Coordinator position (GL-MAN-07) based in Havre-Saint-Pierre, Québec, within the Mingan Archipelago National Park Reserve. The posting includes a standard Employment Equity section stating that the Public Service of Canada is committed to building a "skilled and diverse workforce" and encouraging applicants to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when applying. This self-declaration invitation categorises candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation as part of the hiring process, under the federal government's own 'employment equity' program.
incidents.expected_harm: By inviting candidates to self-declare membership in designated demographic groups, Parks Canada treats applicants as representatives of categories based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating them purely as individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. This sorting of people into demographic groups at the point of application reduces each candidate's singular worth to their membership in one or more identity categories, undermining the principle that people deserve to be assessed for who they are as individuals. The practice normalises institutional labelling of job-seekers by immutable traits and signals that one's demographic category is a relevant factor in public hiring decisions.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Air Transport Security Authority
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Air Transport Security Authority (CATSA) posted a Senior Analyst, Advanced Analytics position encouraging applications based on sex, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability, and asks candidates in those categories to self-declare on the application form.
incidents.description_of_event: CATSA posted a job opening for a Senior Analyst, Advanced Analytics at its Ottawa headquarters, with a closing date of June 19, 2026. The posting includes a section titled 'Our commitment to Diversity, Equity and Inclusion' that encourages applications based on sex, skin colour, ethnicity, and disability (which the federal government groups under its 'employment equity' program). The posting explicitly names women, visible minorities, aboriginal persons, and persons with disabilities as the targeted categories, and asks members of those groups to self-declare by answering questions on the online application form. Candidates are thereby categorized and tracked by these demographic characteristics as part of the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When CATSA asks applicants to declare their sex, skin colour, ethnicity, or disability status, it sorts candidates into demographic categories before any assessment of their individual qualifications, skills, or character. Candidates are evaluated not solely on what they have done or what they can do, but in part on immutable traits they were born with. This reduces a person to a demographic label at the very first step of the hiring process, undermining the principle that each individual deserves to be assessed on their own merits. The practice also signals to every applicant that demographic identity is a relevant criterion for employment, normalizing the idea that institutions may appropriately weigh who a person is by birth rather than what that person brings to the role.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Air Transport Security Authority
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Air Transport Security Authority posted a Procedural Analyst position and encouraged applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, while also inviting candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories on the application form.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Air Transport Security Authority (CATSA) posted a fixed-term Procedural Analyst vacancy (posting date May 6, 2026, closing June 19, 2026) at its Ottawa head office. The posting contains a section titled 'Our commitment to Diversity, Equity and Inclusion' that describes CATSA as 'an employer committed to fostering a diverse and inclusive workforce that reflects the public we serve.' It then encourages candidates in what it calls 'designated groups' — specifically women, visible minorities, Aboriginal persons, and persons with disabilities — to apply and to self-declare by answering questions on the online application form. In practice, this means CATSA is inviting applicants to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability before being assessed, and signalling that membership in these demographic categories is a factor in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: By asking candidates to label themselves according to sex, ethnicity, and disability before assessment, CATSA reduces individual applicants to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and qualifications. Each person who applies is invited to define themselves by immutable traits — traits that have no bearing on whether they can develop operational procedures or edit bilingual documents to a high standard. The practice normalises the idea that who a person is, in demographic terms, is relevant to whether they deserve consideration for a government role. This directly undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual and should be advanced on the basis of merit and demonstrated ability. It also creates an unequal hiring environment where some candidates are signalled as preferred on the basis of characteristics they were born with, rather than anything they have achieved or can contribute.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada
incidents.short_description: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada posted a graduate research position that encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and states that selection may be limited to candidates belonging to one or more of those categories.
incidents.description_of_event: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada posted a Research Affiliate Program MSc position based in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan (reference AGR26J-092396-000007). The posting contains two layers of identity-based hiring language. First, the standard Public Service of Canada statement invites candidates to self-declare membership in a "designated employment equity" group. Second, AAFC's own departmental statement goes further: it encourages applications from women (especially in non-traditional occupations), people of indigenous ethnicity, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minority groups, and explicitly warns that "selection may be limited to candidates belonging to one or more of the groups." Candidates are asked to declare themselves as part of one or more of these categories when they apply.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal department sorts candidates by sex, ethnicity, and disability — and signals that selection may be limited to those categories — it reduces individuals to demographic labels rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. A candidate who meets every academic and scientific requirement for this role will be disadvantaged, or potentially excluded entirely, because of traits that have no bearing on their capacity to conduct plant science research. Equally, candidates who belong to a listed category are advanced not because of what they have done or who they are as individuals, but because of the group they are assigned to. This process teaches both applicants and the institution itself that demographic categories are a legitimate basis for judging people, normalizing a framework that is at odds with the principle that every person deserves to be seen and evaluated as a singular individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's posting for a Grounds Maintenance position at Granville Island explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation under a federal "employment equity" commitment.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a permanent, full-time Grounds Maintenance position at Granville Island in Vancouver. The posting includes a section titled "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion" which states: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This language explicitly categorizes prospective candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability status, religion, and sexual orientation as part of the application encouragement. The posting links to further information about CMHC's "commitment to diversity and inclusion," indicating this reflects an ongoing institutional policy rather than a one-off statement.
incidents.expected_harm: By sorting candidates into demographic categories and directing encouragement toward people based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, CMHC signals that these immutable traits are relevant to who should apply for the role. This reduces individual applicants to demographic labels rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. The practice embeds the assumption that a person's sex, skin colour, ethnicity, religion, or sexual orientation is a meaningful factor in employment decisions, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as a distinct individual. When an employer publicly signals preference along demographic lines, candidates outside those categories are implicitly treated as less welcome, and candidates within them are implicitly told their group membership — not their individual merit — is part of what qualifies them.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Natural Resources Canada
incidents.short_description: Natural Resources Canada posted a PhD research affiliate position and included standard federal language encouraging candidates to self-declare membership in groups covered by the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: Natural Resources Canada posted a Research Affiliate Program position for submarine permafrost mapping on the western Arctic shelf, based at ISMER-UQAR in Rimouski, Québec. The posting's 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' section states that the Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce and encourages applicants to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated groups under the federal government's 'employment equity' program. This standard language appears across virtually all federal postings, encouraging candidates to categorize themselves by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation as part of the hiring process. The posting also contains a geographic preference clause for positions in Nunavut and Yukon that prioritizes applicants based on their ethnicity.
incidents.expected_harm: When applicants are asked to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, disability, or sexual orientation, the hiring process sorts people into demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and actual qualifications. Each person who responds to this self-declaration request is reduced to a demographic proxy. This undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual, and it entrenches the institutional habit of making hiring decisions — even partially — on the basis of immutable traits rather than what a person can do and who they are.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Security Intelligence Service
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service posted a Security Technologist position that invites applicants to self-declare their sex, disability status, and skin colour or ethnicity, stating these characteristics will be considered in the selection process.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) posted a Security Technologist position (Reference Number 25-968-08-131, closing 2026-07-03) based in Ottawa, Ontario. The posting contains multiple passages encouraging candidates to categorize themselves by sex, disability, and skin colour or ethnicity under the federal government's 'employment equity' program. In the Notes section, the posting states that gaps have been identified among persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women, and explicitly invites candidates to self-declare membership in those groups in their cover letter, noting that this information will be considered in the selection process. A separate passage near the top and bottom of the posting repeats CSIS's commitment to increasing representation of persons with disabilities, visible minorities, indigenous peoples, and women at all levels, and strongly encourages individuals belonging to those groups to self-identify when applying. The posting thus asks candidates to label themselves by demographic traits and signals that those labels will factor into hiring decisions.
incidents.expected_harm: When an employer invites candidates to identify themselves by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, or disability and treats those labels as factors in a selection process, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals with unique skills, experience, and character. Candidates who belong to the named groups are sorted and tracked by immutable traits that bear no relation to their ability to perform technical security work. Candidates who do not belong to those groups are disadvantaged relative to equally or better qualified individuals. The practice signals to all applicants that demographic membership is a relevant credential, normalising the institutional habit of labelling people by surface characteristics and undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits. Over time, this erodes trust in the fairness of public-sector hiring and weakens the civic ideal that Canadians share a common standing before their institutions regardless of their background.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Security Intelligence Service
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service posted a Psychologist position that encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and asks candidates who belong to these categories to self-identify in their application materials.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) posted a Psychologist position (reference number 26-968-09-085, closing 2026-06-29) based in Ottawa, Ontario. The posting contains two substantially identical passages invoking the federal government's "employment equity" program. The first states that "gaps have been identified among (persons with disabilities, racialized groups, Indigenous peoples, and women) for this position" and that CSIS "strongly encourage[s] those individuals who belong to one or more of the employment equity groups to self-declare in their cover letter when applying." The second passage names "persons with disabilities, visible minorities, Indigenous peoples, and women" as the groups for whom gaps have been identified, specifically calling out "Indigenous peoples and visible minorities" for this position, and similarly asks candidates to "self-identify when applying." In both cases, CSIS is asking candidates to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status as part of the application process, and signals that belonging to these categories is a factor in hiring consideration.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer asks candidates to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status and signals that these characteristics inform hiring decisions, it reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Every candidate who self-identifies is assessed in part based on an immutable trait, not solely on what they bring as an individual. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories are disadvantaged relative to equally or less qualified candidates who do. This practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic groups and treating group membership as a professional credential, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be seen and evaluated as a singular individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Security Intelligence Service
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Security Intelligence Service posted a job for Chief, Psychological Health and Safety that explicitly invites candidates to self-identify based on their ethnicity, sex, or disability status, citing gaps identified among specific groups in its workforce.
incidents.description_of_event: A posting by the Canadian Security Intelligence Service (CSIS) for the role of Chief, Psychological Health and Safety contains two separate statements encouraging candidates to self-declare their membership in groups defined under the federal government's employment equity program. The posting states that "gaps have been identified among Persons with Disabilities, Indigenous Peoples and Visible Minorities Groups for this position" and invites candidates to "voluntary self-declare if you belong to any of these groups in your cover letter as it will be may be considered in the selection process." A second instance of the same language near the end of the posting repeats that gaps have been identified among indigenous peoples and visible minorities, and states: "we strongly encourage those individuals who belong to one or more of the employment equity groups to self-identify when applying." The groups named — persons with disabilities, people identified by their ethnicity (described in the posting as indigenous peoples and visible minorities), and women — are the four standard categories of the federal government's employment equity program. CSIS explicitly states that self-identification based on these characteristics will be considered in the selection process for this role.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution signals that a candidate's ethnicity, sex, or disability status will be factored into a hiring decision, it reduces individuals to demographic labels rather than evaluating them on their skills, experience, and character. Qualified candidates who do not belong to a targeted group are placed at a disadvantage not because of anything they have done or can offer, but because of who they are. Candidates who do belong to those groups are assessed, at least in part, through a demographic lens rather than as individuals with unique abilities. This practice normalises the idea that a person's immutable traits are relevant considerations for professional advancement, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be judged on their own merits. In a role responsible for the psychological health and safety of an intelligence service's workforce, hiring decisions shaped by demographic categorisation rather than individual capability carry particular consequences for the quality and integrity of that function.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada
incidents.short_description: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's posting for a Research Assistant – Apiculture in Beaverlodge, Alberta encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and warns that selection may be limited to candidates belonging to those categories under the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada posted a Research Assistant – Apiculture position (EG-04) at its Beaverlodge, Alberta location. The posting includes the standard Public Service of Canada employment equity statement inviting candidates to self-declare membership in a designated group. More specifically, the department's own statement goes further: it encourages women (particularly in non-traditional occupations), indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minority groups to apply and explicitly states that "selection may be limited to candidates belonging to one or more of the groups." This language signals that the hiring process can be restricted to candidates on the basis of sex, ethnicity, and disability, rather than being open to all individuals evaluated on their qualifications and demonstrated abilities alone.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer warns that selection may be limited to candidates based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than assessing them on their individual character, knowledge, and skills. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the favoured categories face the prospect of being screened out not because of any deficiency in their abilities but solely because of immutable traits. This undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual and deserves to be evaluated on what they can actually do. The practice also sends a message to all applicants — favoured or not — that demographic labels are what matter most in public service hiring, which erodes trust in merit-based evaluation and weakens the shared civic belonging that comes from knowing that institutions treat everyone as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Energy Regulator
incidents.short_description: The Canada Energy Regulator's posting for a Market Analyst role invites applicants to self-declare membership in the federal government's designated employment equity groups, categorising candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating them purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canada Energy Regulator (CER) posted a Market Analyst position (reference ERG26J-022601-000260) closing 21 June 2026. Under the heading
incidents.expected_harm: By inviting candidates to self-declare their sex, ethnicity, disability, or sexual orientation under the federal government's 'employment equity' program, the Canada Energy Regulator reduces individual applicants to demographic categories at the very outset of the hiring process. Each person who applies is asked to label themselves by immutable traits before their qualifications, abilities, or character are ever assessed. This signals to applicants that their demographic profile is a relevant hiring factor, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on what they can do and who they are as an individual. It also signals to those who do not belong to a listed category that they are comparatively less valued as candidates, fragmenting the workforce into groups rather than recognising a shared pool of talented individuals. The CER's separate declaration that it is "proud to be an employer of choice for Indigenous Peoples" compounds this by singling out candidates based on ethnicity for special institutional attention, further entrenching the practice of categorising people by immutable traits rather than their unique merits.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Transportation Safety Board of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Transportation Safety Board of Canada posted a Senior Human Factors Investigator role that may limit selection to candidates based on their ethnicity, disability, or sex under the federal 'employment equity' program, and invites applicants to self-identify using designated group categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The Transportation Safety Board of Canada advertised a Senior Human Factors Investigator position (EC-06, closing June 9, 2026). Under the 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' section of the posting, it states that selection may be limited to members of the following categories under the federal 'employment equity' program: indigenous (Aboriginal) peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women. The same section also encourages applicants to self-declare if they belong to one of these designated groups when applying. This means a candidate's ethnicity, disability status, or sex — rather than solely their qualifications, abilities, and character — can determine whether they are even considered for the role.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution limits selection based on a candidate's ethnicity, disability, or sex, it reduces individuals to demographic labels rather than evaluating them on their abilities, experience, and character. Qualified candidates who do not belong to the designated categories will be passed over not because of anything they have done or failed to demonstrate, but because of immutable traits. This directly undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual and deserves to be assessed on their own merits. Asking applicants to self-declare their demographic identity as part of the hiring process further normalises the institutional practice of sorting people into categories, treating those categories as relevant to employment decisions, and eroding the foundation of a hiring system grounded in individual assessment.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Fisheries and Oceans Canada > Canadian Coast Guard
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Coast Guard's posting for Maritime Search and Rescue Coordinator states that selection may be limited to members of designated groups under the federal employment equity program, categorising candidates by ethnicity, disability, and sex rather than individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: A Canadian Coast Guard inventory posting for the role of Maritime Search and Rescue Coordinator (GT-06), reference DND26J-182764-000023, includes two identity-categorisation provisions. First, the posting invites candidates to self-declare membership in federal employment equity groups: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." Second, and more directly restrictive, the posting states: "Selection may be limited to members of the following employment equity groups: Indigenous (Aboriginal) peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women." This means the hiring process explicitly contemplates excluding candidates from consideration based on their ethnicity, disability status, and sex, rather than evaluating each applicant solely on qualifications, experience, and demonstrated ability for a search and rescue coordination role.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal hiring process explicitly reserves the possibility of limiting selection to candidates based on their ethnicity, disability status, and sex, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as people with unique qualifications, skills, and character. A candidate who lacks the preferred demographic profile is set aside not because of anything they have done or failed to demonstrate, but because of who they are. This directly undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as an individual. It also sends a signal to all applicants — those in the named groups and those outside them — that demographic identity is a relevant criterion in public service hiring, which normalises the labelling and sorting of citizens by immutable traits. Over time, this practice corrodes the shared civic ideal of a public service that serves all Canadians impartially and is built on individual merit.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's posting for a Senior Manager, IT Portfolio and Performance Management position encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a permanent, full-time Senior Manager, IT Portfolio and Performance Management position based in Ottawa or Montreal. The posting includes a section titled 'Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion' which states: 'We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply.' This language explicitly encourages candidates to apply on the basis of sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating each person solely as an individual. The posting also includes a job responsibility directing the successful candidate to 'create opportunities that encourage bilingualism and diversity while implementing practices to advance an inclusive, healthy organization, respectful of the diversity of people and their skills and free from harassment and discrimination,' embedding identity-based categorisation into the role itself.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution frames a job posting around encouraging applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, it reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and experience. Every person who applies for this role is implicitly sorted by these immutable traits before their individual qualifications are considered. This undermines the principle that each person possesses inherent dignity as an individual and deserves to be assessed on their own unique qualities. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories receive a signal that they are less valued as applicants, while those who do belong receive a signal that their group membership is relevant to their professional standing. Both outcomes compromise the integrity of a hiring process that should focus entirely on individual merit and demonstrated capability.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada is hiring an AI Application Developer and states that priority will be given to applicants based on their ethnicity and disability status, and asks candidates to self-declare their membership in federal 'employment equity' groups.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada posted a job opening for an AI Application Developer (CS-1, continuing tenure) within its Chief Information Officer Branch. The posting states that "priority may be given to the following designated employment equity groups: Indigenous Peoples* (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities and racialized persons*" and notes that "the Employment Equity Act, which is under review, uses the terminology Aboriginal peoples and visible minorities." The posting further requires that "candidates are asked to self-declare when applying to this hiring process." This means applicants will be evaluated and potentially ranked not solely on their technical qualifications but also on their ethnicity and disability status, as categorized under the federal government's 'employment equity' program. A separate note extends a further accommodation specifically tied to ethnicity, stating that "indigenous candidates may have important connections to their communities" and may be eligible for exceptions to the work arrangement.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants for this technical role are sorted and ranked by their ethnicity and disability status rather than evaluated purely as individuals on their abilities, experience, and character. A qualified candidate who does not belong to a favoured category is placed at a disadvantage relative to a candidate with the same or lesser qualifications who does. The self-declaration requirement asks applicants to identify themselves by demographic category as a condition of full participation in the hiring process, reducing individuals to group labels at the outset. This practice normalizes the idea that a person's ethnicity or disability status is a relevant criterion in assessing their fitness for an AI development role, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be seen and evaluated as a distinct individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted a job for a Bilingual Senior Specialist, Multi-Unit Claims and included an encouragement statement inviting applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation, and religion.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) advertised a permanent, full-time Bilingual Senior Specialist, Multi-Unit Claims position open to candidates across multiple Canadian cities. The posting includes a section titled "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion" containing the statement: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This statement explicitly encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation (which the federal government groups under its 'employment equity' program). CMHC attaches this standard encouragement language broadly across its postings, and its consistent presence constitutes the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: By inviting applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, CMHC signals that candidates' demographic characteristics are relevant to their desirability as applicants. This reduces individuals to the categories they belong to rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and qualifications. Each applicant is a distinct person with a unique combination of skills and experience; sorting them by immutable traits treats those traits as meaningful measures of worth. The practice normalises institutional labelling of people by demographic proxies, undermining the principle that every person deserves to be assessed as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada (FINTRAC) posted openings for Senior Analyst roles and included the federal government's standard employment equity statement encouraging candidates to self-declare membership in designated groups based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: FINTRAC posted positions for Senior Analyst, Operations and Senior Analyst, Strategic Direction on June 9, 2026, with a closing date of June 19, 2026. The posting includes a section titled "Equity, diversity and inclusion" that states: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This is the standard federal government statement that sorts candidates by demographic categories — including sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — and invites them to self-identify accordingly when submitting their applications. The near-universal use of this language across federal postings is the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates applying to FINTRAC are asked to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation as part of the hiring process. This reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications for the role. The practice signals to applicants that their immutable traits are a relevant factor in how the institution views them, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be recognised and assessed as a unique individual. Over time, normalising this kind of demographic sorting in federal hiring trains both applicants and hiring managers to think of people in terms of group membership rather than individual merit, eroding the standard that advancement should reflect a person's own capabilities and conduct.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada posted a Health, Safety and Environment Advisor position that gives priority to candidates based on their ethnicity and disability status, and asks all applicants to self-declare their membership in the federal government's 'employment equity' groups.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada advertised a continuing Health, Safety and Environment Advisor position (AS-4) based in Ottawa. The posting states that priority may be given to candidates based on their ethnicity (identified as indigenous peoples — first nations, inuit and métis — and racialized persons) and disability status (persons with disabilities), which the NRC groups under the federal government's 'employment equity' program. All candidates are asked to self-declare their membership in these categories as part of the hiring process. A footnote acknowledges that the Employment Equity Act itself uses different terminology ('Aboriginal peoples' and 'visible minorities'), signalling that the categories are rooted in the Act's framework. The role is otherwise evaluated on occupational health and safety qualifications, experience, and bilingual competency.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution gives hiring priority based on a candidate's ethnicity or disability status, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than assessing them as an individual with distinct abilities, experience, and character. Candidates who share the same qualifications are treated differently based on immutable traits they were born with or conditions they live with. Those who do not belong to the preferred categories face a structurally disadvantaged hiring process despite possessing equivalent or superior skills. The self-declaration requirement compounds this by asking every applicant to categorise themselves by demographic identity as a formal step in seeking public employment, normalising the idea that a person's worth in a government hiring process is partly determined by the group they can claim membership in. This practice undermines the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their individual merits, and erodes the shared civic belonging that comes from institutions treating all citizens as equals.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Royal Canadian Mounted Police
incidents.short_description: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police posted a job for a Detachment Services Assistant that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in federal employment equity groups and signals preference for candidates on the basis of sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: A Royal Canadian Mounted Police posting for a Detachment Services Assistant (CR-04) based in Turner Valley, Alberta, published on June 9, 2026, contains two qualifying passages. First, under the heading "Equity, diversity and inclusion," the posting states: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This invites candidates to identify themselves by sex, ethnicity, disability, and other characteristics grouped under the federal government's employment equity program. Second, under "Preference," the posting notes that for positions in Nunavut, qualified Inuit enrolled under the Nunavut Agreement will be appointed first, and for positions in Yukon, qualified Yukon First Nations will be appointed first, followed by qualified indigenous persons — establishing appointment preference based on ethnicity for those locations. Together, these passages ask candidates to categorize themselves by demographic traits and signal that group membership influences appointment decisions.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer asks candidates to label themselves by sex, ethnicity, or disability — and signals that those labels influence hiring — it evaluates people as members of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals with their own abilities, character, and record. A candidate is reduced to a checkbox rather than assessed on what they uniquely bring to the role. This practice erodes the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual and that advancement should reflect individual merit and demonstrated capacity. It also normalizes the idea that immutable traits are professionally relevant, teaching both applicants and hiring managers to sort people by group membership rather than engaging with them as singular human beings.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada
incidents.short_description: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's posting for a Research Assistant position encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and warns that selection may be limited to candidates belonging to these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada posted a Research Assistant (Doubled Haploid Production) position based in Lethbridge, Alberta. The posting contains two layers of identity-based language. First, a general diversity statement declares the department is "dedicated to building and promoting a diverse, equitable and inclusive workforce, representative of all Canadians regardless of race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression." Second, and more significantly, a department-specific employment equity statement explicitly encourages women (especially in non-traditional occupations), indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minority groups to apply and self-declare, adding that "selection may be limited to candidates belonging to one or more of the groups" — meaning the hiring process can be restricted to candidates on the basis of sex, ethnicity, and disability. The Public Service Commission's standard statement also invites candidates to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated groups under the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government employer signals that selection will favour or be limited to candidates based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as an individual with unique abilities, experience, and character. A candidate who does not belong to the preferred categories faces a structurally lower chance of being hired regardless of their qualifications, and a candidate who does belong to those categories is evaluated in part on an immutable trait rather than solely on demonstrated merit. This institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories normalises the idea that a person's skin colour, sex, or disability status is a relevant factor in whether they deserve a job, undermining the principle that every person possesses singular worth as an individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Indigenous Services Canada
incidents.short_description: Indigenous Services Canada's posting for a Nurse Practice Advisor states that selection may be limited to candidates who self-identify based on sex, ethnicity, or disability under the federal government's 'employment equity' program.
incidents.description_of_event: Indigenous Services Canada posted a competition for a Nurse Practice Advisor (Home & Community Care) in Saskatchewan, closing June 18, 2026. Under the "Organizational Need" heading, the posting states that selection may be limited to candidates who self-identify as belonging to one of the federal government's 'employment equity' groups: women, indigenous persons, visible minorities, and persons with disabilities. The standard Public Service of Canada equity statement also appears, encouraging candidates to self-declare membership in those same designated groups. Together, these provisions mean that a candidate's sex, ethnicity, and disability status are treated as factors in determining whether their application receives further consideration — ahead of, or alongside, their individual qualifications as a nurse.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer states that selection may be limited based on a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability status, it reduces qualified individuals to demographic labels rather than evaluating them on their abilities, experience, and character. A registered nurse who does not fit the designated categories faces the prospect of being screened out entirely, regardless of their professional competence. This practice signals to all applicants that the institution sorts people into groups based on immutable traits, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed as a distinct individual. It also normalises the institutional habit of categorising Canadians by demographic characteristics as a routine part of professional life, eroding the shared civic foundation that treats people as individuals rather than representatives of a group.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Film Board of Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Film Board of Canada's job posting for a Senior Production Coordinator encourages applicants to voluntarily identify themselves based on sex, ethnicity, and disability as part of the NFB's employment equity commitments.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Film Board of Canada posted a competition for a Senior Production Coordinator based in Halifax (position number 00021502, posted June 3, 2026). In the posting, the NFB states it is committed to building a "skilled, diverse workforce" and promotes its employment equity program. The posting invites candidates to voluntarily indicate in their cover letter whether they are a woman, a member of a First Nations group, Inuk, Métis, a person with a disability, or a member of a visible-minority group. This is a direct request that candidates categorize and label themselves according to ethnicity, sex, and disability status as part of the application process. The NFB also references its commitment to "inclusive, barrier-free selection processes" and offers accommodation, further embedding demographic categorization into the hiring framework.
incidents.expected_harm: By asking candidates to label themselves according to sex, ethnicity, and disability, the NFB reduces applicants to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and qualifications. A candidate who is a woman, or who belongs to a particular ethnic group, or who has a disability, is prompted to present that trait as a relevant fact about themselves to a prospective employer. This practice signals to every applicant that the institution views demographic characteristics as meaningful inputs into hiring decisions, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be assessed on their own individual merits. Applicants who do not belong to the listed categories receive an implicit message that their demographic profile is less valued, while those who do belong are reduced to representatives of a group rather than seen as distinct individuals. Over time, this normalises the idea that immutable traits are legitimate criteria in professional evaluation, eroding the standard that advancement should reflect a person's abilities and demonstrated contributions.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Communications Security Establishment
incidents.short_description: The Communications Security Establishment's software developer posting states that priority may be given to candidates who self-declare as belonging to designated groups under the federal government's 'employment equity' program, categorising applicants by sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Communications Security Establishment (CSE) posted a Software Developer (Various Levels) competition closing 2026-07-05. Within the posting's important information section, CSE states: "To address CSE's current representation gaps, priority may be given to persons who self-declare as belonging to one, or more, designated employment equity groups: women; Indigenous peoples, including First Nations who are status, or registered and non-status, or non-registered, Inuit and Métis; persons with disabilities, including people with a mental health condition and neurodivergent people; and persons from racial or ethnic groups." The posting additionally notes that the Employment Equity Act uses the terminology "Aboriginal peoples" and "visible minorities." CSE also states in its introductory section that it "is committed to fostering a culture of equity, diversity and inclusion" and invites candidates to learn about its diversity and inclusion initiatives. Together, these passages direct candidates to self-declare their membership in categories defined by sex, ethnicity, and disability so that hiring priority decisions can be made on those bases.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal agency assigns hiring priority based on a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability status, it reduces that person to a demographic label rather than evaluating them as an individual on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Every applicant is sorted into categories defined by immutable traits before their technical skills are fully weighed; those who do not belong to a preferred category face a structurally lower chance of selection regardless of merit. This practice signals to all candidates — both those who benefit and those who do not — that demographic identity is a relevant factor in whether the government wants to hire them. It undermines the principle that each person possesses singular worth as an individual, and it normalises the institutional habit of measuring people by the groups they belong to rather than by who they are.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Energy Regulator
incidents.short_description: The Canada Energy Regulator's posting for a Professional Leader, Engineering role encourages applications based on ethnicity, sex, disability, and visible minority status, and states that first consideration for appointment may go to candidates who self-identify under these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canada Energy Regulator posted a competition for a Professional Leader, Engineering position (reference ERG26J-022610-000346) based in Calgary, Alberta. Under the "Equity, diversity and inclusion" section of the posting, the CER states that candidates who self-declare as belonging to one of four categories — people of indigenous ethnicity, visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women — may receive first consideration for appointment. The posting instructs candidates to indicate membership in these groups in the Employment Equity section of their application, and notes that if this criterion is used, only those who have indicated they belong to a specified group will be considered. The posting also includes the Public Service Commission's standard statement encouraging self-declaration as a member of a "designated employment equity group." In the asset qualifications section, the CER further states a preference may be given to qualified candidates who self-identify from these same four groups. Additionally, a link titled "Self-declare as a person with a disability" is included among the additional links.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal regulator explicitly tells candidates that first consideration for appointment will go to those who self-identify by ethnicity, sex, or disability, it reduces individuals to demographic labels at the most consequential stage of the hiring process. A highly qualified engineer is evaluated not solely on professional expertise, leadership, and demonstrated competence, but partly on whether they belong to a designated category. This directly undermines the principle that every person deserves recognition for their unique qualities and that advancement should reflect character, abilities, and individual merit. Candidates who do not fit any designated category are placed at a structural disadvantage regardless of how strong their qualifications are. The practice also signals to all applicants that the institution views them first through the lens of demographic membership rather than as distinct individuals, eroding the expectation that a regulator charged with public safety and sound technical governance will select its senior engineering authority purely on professional grounds.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Royal Canadian Mounted Police
incidents.short_description: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police posted a job for an Administrative and Operational Support position and encouraged applicants to self-declare membership in federal employment equity categories based on sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police advertised a CR-04 Administrative and Operational Support position based in Bible Hill, Nova Scotia, closing June 24, 2026. Under the heading 'Equity, diversity and inclusion,' the posting states: 'The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply.' This language asks candidates to identify themselves according to categories set by the federal government's employment equity program — categories that sort applicants by sex, ethnicity, and disability status. The posting thus invites candidates to label themselves by immutable traits as part of the application process, rather than being evaluated purely on their individual qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: When the RCMP asks candidates to self-declare membership in demographic categories — sorted by sex, ethnicity, and disability — it treats people as representatives of groups rather than as individuals to be assessed on their own character, abilities, and circumstances. Candidates are prompted to define themselves by immutable traits at the very point of applying for a position. This framing reduces a person's identity to a set of demographic labels that the institution uses to manage the composition of its workforce, bypassing the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their unique qualities. Over time, normalising such self-declaration embeds the expectation that demographic categories are a relevant and legitimate input into hiring decisions, eroding the standard that advancement should reflect individual merit.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Correctional Service Canada
incidents.short_description: Correctional Service Canada's posting for an Electronic Security Systems Specialist encourages applicants to self-identify as members of federal employment equity categories — women, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and indigenous peoples — and states the selection committee may initially evaluate only those from these groups.
incidents.description_of_event: Correctional Service Canada posted a competition (reference PEN26J-023690-000109) for an Electronic Security Systems Specialist (Project Lead) at the EL-07 level based in Laval, Québec. The posting includes two distinct passages that categorize candidates by sex, disability, ethnicity, and skin colour. First, under 'Important messages,' the posting tells applicants: 'We encourage you to self-identify during the application process. The committee could decide to initially evaluate applicants who are part of one of the four employment equity groups: Women, People with disabilities, Members of visible minorities and Indigenous.' Second, a standard Government of Canada passage under 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' states: 'The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply.' Together, these passages invite candidates to label themselves by demographic category and expressly contemplate that initial evaluation priority will be given based on those categories rather than on qualifications alone.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government employer tells applicants that the selection committee will initially evaluate only those who belong to certain demographic categories, it reduces candidates to labels — sex, skin colour, ethnicity, disability — rather than assessing them as individuals on character, ability, and demonstrated merit. A qualified candidate who does not fall into a preferred category faces the prospect of being set aside at the first screening stage, not because of anything about their work or skills, but because of who they are. This directly contradicts the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their own terms. The practice also pressures applicants to declare personal information about their bodies and backgrounds to gain a procedural advantage, which erodes individual privacy and autonomy. Over time, normalising this kind of categorical sorting in federal hiring embeds the message that demographic identity is a legitimate basis for distributing opportunity in public institutions — undermining the civic compact that government serves all Canadians as individuals.

incidents.short_description: The Parliamentary Protective Service posted a job for an Employee Relations, Health, Safety and Wellness Officer and included standard federal language encouraging candidates to self-declare membership in the federal government's designated 'employment equity' groups.
incidents.description_of_event: The Parliamentary Protective Service advertised a position (AS-04/PSA-06 level) for an Employee Relations, Health, Safety and Wellness Officer based in Ottawa. The posting includes the standard federal 'employment equity' statement: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This language invites applicants to identify themselves by sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation — the traits that fall under the federal government's 'employment equity' program — as part of the application process. The same near-identical boilerplate appears across a vast number of federal postings, and its ubiquity is itself the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: When a hiring institution encourages applicants to self-declare membership in demographic categories, it signals that a person's sex, ethnicity, disability status, or sexual orientation is relevant information in the hiring process. This reduces applicants to labels derived from immutable traits rather than evaluating them as individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Each person who applies is, from the outset, invited to present themselves through a demographic lens the institution has pre-determined to be meaningful. This practice undermines the principle that every individual possesses singular worth independent of the groups into which institutions sort them, and it normalises the idea that demographic identity is a legitimate factor in who gets hired for a public role.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Services and Procurement Canada
incidents.short_description: Public Services and Procurement Canada's posting for a Head of Technical Services (GT-06) encourages applicants to self-declare based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and states that consideration will be given to candidates who belong to the federal government's designated employment equity groups.
incidents.description_of_event: Public Services and Procurement Canada posted a competition for a Head of Technical Services position (GT-06) in Ottawa, closing June 16, 2026. The posting includes two distinct identity-categorization elements. First, a general statement commits the Public Service of Canada to building a "skilled and diverse workforce" and encourages candidates to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when applying. Second, a more specific passage states that "consideration may be given to candidates" who self-declare as belonging to one of the following groups: women, indigenous (Aboriginal) peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities. Candidates are explicitly invited to select the appropriate box under the Employment Equity section of the online application. The posting thereby invites candidates to identify themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status as a factor in their assessment for a senior federal technical management role.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer signals that a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability status will be a factor in whether they receive consideration, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as an individual on the basis of their character, skills, and demonstrated experience. Every person who applies for this role will be prompted to sort themselves into identity categories, and those who fall outside the favoured categories will have their applications considered on different terms. This practice directly undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual and deserves to be assessed on their own unique qualities and abilities. It also normalises the institutional habit of viewing people primarily through demographic proxies, eroding the shared civic belonging that comes from treating all candidates by the same standard regardless of immutable personal characteristics.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of Justice Canada
incidents.short_description: The Department of Justice Canada posted a Counsel position that invites applicants to self-declare their sex, ethnicity, disability, or sexual orientation, and states that selection may be limited to members of federal employment equity groups.
incidents.description_of_event: The Department of Justice Canada advertised a Counsel (LP-02) position in Ottawa, with a closing date of June 15, 2026. Under the "Asset Qualifications" and "Organizational Needs" section, the posting invites applicants who are women, indigenous persons, persons with disabilities, members of a racialized group, or members of the 2SLGBTQIA+ / SOGIE communities to self-declare, stating that this self-declaration information "can be factored into the screening, assessment, or selection decision." Separately, the posting's "Equity, diversity and inclusion" section states that "selection may be limited to members of the following employment equity groups: Indigenous (Aboriginal) peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and women" — language drawn from the federal government's standard employment equity framework. The posting also broadly encourages self-declaration for anyone belonging to a designated employment equity group.
incidents.expected_harm: When a hiring process factors a candidate's sex, ethnicity, disability, or sexual orientation into screening, assessment, or selection decisions, it reduces that person to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as an individual on the basis of their character, abilities, and qualifications. Candidates who belong to the listed categories receive differential treatment based on immutable traits, while those outside those categories face a comparatively disadvantaged process — all because the institution has sorted people into demographic groups. This practice undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual deserving evaluation on their own merits, and normalises the idea that demographic category membership is a legitimate and relevant factor in deciding who gets a government job.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Royal Canadian Mounted Police
incidents.short_description: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police posted a Detachment Services Supervisor position that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in federal "employment equity" groups and implies hiring preferences based on skin colour, sex, disability, and ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police advertised a Detachment Services Supervisor role (AS-02) based at the Piikani Nation RCMP Detachment in Brocket, Alberta. The posting includes standard federal language stating: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This invites candidates to identify themselves by skin colour, sex, disability, and ethnicity (the traits grouped under the federal government's "employment equity" program) as part of the application process. This language appears in virtually every federal posting and its ubiquity across the public service is itself the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer asks candidates to label themselves by skin colour, sex, disability, or ethnicity, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic categories rather than evaluating them as an individual on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. Candidates learn that their demographic traits are relevant to the hiring process, which undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual. The systematic, near-universal application of this practice across the federal public service normalises the sorting of people by immutable characteristics, entrenching a hiring culture that treats demographic membership as a credential rather than evaluating each person on their own merits.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence > Canadian Forces Health Services
incidents.short_description: National Defence – Canadian Forces Health Services is recruiting a clinical psychologist and explicitly invites candidates to self-declare based on ethnicity, disability, and sex, and states that selection may be based on those same demographic categories.
incidents.description_of_event: A job posting for a clinical psychologist with National Defence – Canadian Forces Health Services (reference DND26J-016876-000177) contains several passages that sort candidates by demographic category rather than evaluating them purely as individuals. The posting instructs applicants to self-declare which federal "employment equity" group they belong to, stating: "Creating an environment where diverse perspectives are valued begins with self-declaration. Count yourself in and self-declare by identifying to which employment equity group(s) you belong to." A second passage, attributed to the Public Service of Canada, frames this as a blanket commitment: "We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." Most directly, the department's own equity, diversity and inclusion section states that "selection may be based on qualified candidates self-declaring as belonging to one or more of the designated groups: Indigenous Peoples, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women," explicitly making a candidate's ethnicity, disability status, or sex a potential basis for the appointment decision. Applicants are encouraged to indicate membership in these categories when applying. The posting covers multiple locations across Canada and is open to persons residing in Canada and Canadian citizens and permanent residents abroad.
incidents.expected_harm: When a hiring authority states that selection among qualified candidates may turn on whether an applicant has declared a particular ethnicity, disability, or sex, it reduces individuals to demographic labels rather than recognising them for their character, abilities, and individual qualifications.
