A clinical psychologist who happens to be a woman, or whose background is from a particular ethnic group, is appointed in part because of that trait — not solely because of what they know and can do. Equally, a psychologist who does not carry any of those labels faces a structural disadvantage that has nothing to do with their competence or fitness for the role. The practice normalises the idea that people's immutable characteristics are legitimate inputs into consequential institutional decisions, which conflicts directly with the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as a unique individual. Over time, pervasive use of this framework across the federal public service entrenches a hiring culture in which demographic labelling is routine, eroding the expectation that merit — defined by individual skill, experience, and demonstrated ability — is the sole proper basis for appointment to public service positions.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation's posting for a Senior Scrum Master prioritises candidates of a specific ethnicity and encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a permanent full-time Senior Scrum Master position open to candidates in Montréal, Ottawa, and Toronto. The posting contains two distinct identity-categorisation provisions. First, it explicitly states that "members of the following employment equity deserving groups will be prioritized for this job: Indigenous Peoples," giving hiring preference to candidates based on their ethnicity. Second, the posting includes a broad encouragement statement: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." The posting also notes that "alternative work arrangements may be considered for Indigenous candidates," extending a workplace benefit specifically based on ethnicity. Together these provisions mean that candidates are sorted and evaluated based on demographic characteristics — ethnicity, sex, disability, religion, and sexual orientation — rather than solely on their qualifications and abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution openly prioritises candidates for hire based on their ethnicity, it reduces individuals to a demographic category rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. A candidate who belongs to the prioritised ethnic group advances not purely because of their skills as a Scrum Master, but because of an immutable trait they were born with. Conversely, equally or more qualified candidates who do not share that ethnicity are disadvantaged in precisely the same way — judged by a trait that has no bearing on their competence. The broader encouragement statement further signals to every applicant that their demographic profile is a relevant factor in the hiring process, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be seen and evaluated as a unique individual. Extending a workplace benefit such as flexible work arrangements on the basis of ethnicity compounds the harm, creating a two-tiered employment relationship rooted in demographic categorisation rather than individual circumstance or need.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Defence Construction Canada
incidents.short_description: Defence Construction Canada's posting for a Coordinator, Construction Services role in Cold Lake, Alberta encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: A posting by Defence Construction Canada for a Coordinator, Construction Services position (Requisition 7811) based in Cold Lake, Alberta includes standard encouragement language that sorts applicants into demographic categories. The posting states: "We actively encourage applications from all, including but not limited to, women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities and members of the LGBTQ2+ community." This language, which the federal government groups under its employment equity program, explicitly names categories defined by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation as groups whose applications are actively encouraged. The posting also references a commitment to a "diverse and inclusive workplace" as part of this framing.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government employer singles out demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, disability, sexual orientation — as the basis for actively encouraging applications, it reduces each candidate to a collection of immutable traits rather than evaluating them as a distinct individual. A person who applies is implicitly invited to present their demographic characteristics as a credential. This displaces the principle that hiring decisions should reflect individual character, demonstrated abilities, and relevant qualifications. The practice normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into predefined groups, treating membership in a demographic category as relevant to whether someone is a suitable candidate. Over time this sends a signal to all applicants — those in the listed categories and those not — that their demographic profile shapes how the institution sees them, undermining the recognition of each person's singular worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Defence Construction Canada
incidents.short_description: Defence Construction Canada posted a Coordinator, Construction Services role in Cold Lake, Alberta, and explicitly encouraged applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Defence Construction Canada (DCC) posted a job opening for a Coordinator, Construction Services position located in Cold Lake, Alberta (requisition 7667), closing June 25, 2026. The posting includes a dedicated section encouraging applications based on demographic characteristics. Specifically, the posting states: "We actively encourage applications from all, including but not limited to, women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities and members of the LGBTQ2+ community." This language sorts candidates into demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — as part of the hiring process, framing group membership as a relevant consideration for prospective applicants. The posting also invites candidates who are not Canadian citizens but are eligible to work in Canada, and separately states DCC is "committed to ensuring everyone has a voice and can be their authentic selves in our workplace." The qualifying incident is the enumeration of identity-based groups as a basis for actively encouraging applications, which is the standard federal employment equity encouragement pattern applied across Government of Canada and crown corporation postings.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer singles out applicants for encouragement based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, it reduces each person to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as a distinct individual with their own skills, experience, and character. Applicants who belong to the named groups are implicitly told their demographic profile is part of why they are wanted, rather than their individual qualifications alone. Applicants who do not belong to the named groups receive the message that their demographic profile makes them less actively sought, regardless of their individual merit. Over time, this practice normalises the idea that institutions should treat people differently based on immutable traits, eroding the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own abilities and demonstrated needs. It entrenches a hiring culture built around demographic accounting rather than individual worth, and it undermines the shared civic belonging that comes from knowing institutions evaluate everyone by the same standard.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada is hiring a Technical Officer in Mechanical Engineering and states that priority may be given to applicants based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, asking candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada posted a job opening for a Technical Officer in Mechanical Engineering (TO-4, term until March 31, 2028) based in Boucherville, QC. The posting states: "Priority may be given to the following designated employment equity groups: women, Indigenous Peoples* (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities and racialized persons*." A footnote clarifies that the federal Employment Equity Act uses the terms "Aboriginal peoples" and "visible minorities" for these categories. Candidates are explicitly asked to self-declare their membership in these categories when applying. The posting therefore categorizes applicants by sex, ethnicity, and disability as criteria that can affect hiring priority, operating under the federal government's "employment equity" program.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution signals that hiring priority will turn on a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability status, it reduces individuals to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their abilities, character, and the merits of their application. An engineer's capacity to design and optimize complex laboratory instruments has nothing to do with these traits. Candidates who belong to the listed categories are implicitly told their demographic membership is relevant to their prospects; candidates who do not belong are told the same. Both messages teach applicants that institutions sort people by immutable characteristics rather than by what they can do and who they are as individuals. The requirement to self-declare demographic information further entrenches this practice, compelling candidates to label themselves in categories the institution has chosen as relevant. This undermines the recognition of each person's singular worth and works against a civic environment where people are evaluated as distinct individuals.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada's posting for a Research Associate in Edmonton states that priority may be given to applicants based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and asks candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada posted a job for a Research Associate (In-situ / operando methods) based in Edmonton, Alberta, within its Quantum and Nanotechnologies research centre. The posting states: "Priority may be given to the following designated employment equity groups: women, Indigenous Peoples* (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities and racialized persons*." It further notes that "The Employment Equity Act, which is under review, uses the terminology Aboriginal peoples and visible minorities." Candidates are also told: "Candidates are asked to self-declare when applying to this hiring process." These statements mean that applicants are sorted and potentially advantaged in hiring based on their sex, ethnicity, and disability status — categories that fall under the federal government's "employment equity" program — rather than being evaluated purely on their qualifications, research experience, and demonstrated abilities.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal institution signals that priority will be given to candidates based on sex, ethnicity, or disability, it reduces people to demographic categories rather than assessing them as individuals. Qualified researchers are placed at a disadvantage not because of anything they have done or failed to demonstrate, but because of immutable traits. Candidates who belong to the listed categories are labelled and sorted by those traits, which strips away recognition of their individual character and accomplishments. The self-declaration request reinforces this by asking applicants to define themselves to the institution through demographic proxies. This process undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their abilities and demonstrated merit alone, and normalises a system of institutional labelling that fragments rather than unites.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Gallery of Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Gallery of Canada posted a Graphic Designer position that encourages applications based on ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, sorting candidates into demographic categories rather than evaluating them purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Gallery of Canada posted a competition for a permanent full-time Graphic Designer (Competition #26-OC-80) in its Marketing and Sponsorship department, posted June 1, 2026, with a closing date of June 21, 2026. The posting includes a Values section that states: "We encourage applications from individuals belonging to Indigenous, Black, and racialized communities, women, persons with disabilities, and members of 2SLGBTQI+ communities." This language explicitly encourages applications based on ethnicity (including indigenous status and skin colour), sex, disability, and sexual orientation. Rather than inviting all candidates to apply and be assessed on their design skills, experience, and portfolio, the Gallery singles out people for encouragement based on immutable demographic traits.
incidents.expected_harm: By explicitly encouraging applications based on ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, the National Gallery of Canada reduces prospective candidates to demographic categories rather than seeing them as individuals whose merit, experience, and creative abilities should speak for themselves. Applicants who belong to the listed groups are signalled that their demographic characteristics are a relevant factor in the hiring process, not just their skills or portfolio. Applicants who do not belong to those groups receive the implicit message that their application is less welcome. Both outcomes undermine the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated on their own character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. This practice normalizes the institutional habit of sorting people by immutable traits at the very first stage of a hiring process, entrenching demographic labelling rather than fostering an environment where individual worth is the sole basis for evaluation.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada's posting for a Microgrid Systems Technician in Vancouver states that priority may be given to applicants based on their sex, ethnicity, and disability status, and asks all candidates to self-declare membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada posted a term position for a Microgrid Systems Technician (TO-3) based in Vancouver, within its Clean Energy Innovation unit. The posting states: "Priority may be given to the following designated employment equity groups: women, Indigenous Peoples* (First Nations, Inuit and Métis), persons with disabilities and racialized persons*." A footnote clarifies that the federal Employment Equity Act uses the terms "Aboriginal peoples and visible minorities" for some of these categories. The posting further states: "Candidates are asked to self-declare when applying to this hiring process." Separately, the posting includes a note that indigenous candidates may be eligible for an exception to the full-time on-site work arrangement, granting a special consideration based on ethnicity that is not available to other candidates. The posting asks every applicant to declare whether they belong to any of these categories, making identity categorisation a formal step in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer assigns hiring priority based on a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability status, it evaluates that person as a representative of a demographic category rather than as an individual with distinct character, abilities, and experience. A qualified candidate who does not fall into one of the listed categories is disadvantaged not because of any deficiency in their skills or conduct, but purely because of who they are. Equally, a candidate who does belong to one of the listed categories is advanced in part on the same basis — their identity rather than their demonstrated merit. Asking every applicant to formally declare their demographic characteristics normalises the institutional practice of sorting people by immutable traits, reinforcing the idea that these traits are relevant to professional evaluation. The additional work-arrangement exception offered based on ethnicity compounds this by treating people's ethnicity as a relevant operational factor in how employment terms are structured, further entrenching the habit of reducing individuals to group membership rather than recognising their singular worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada posted a Solution Specialist position that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in federal 'employment equity' groups, and states that preference may be given to those who do so.
incidents.description_of_event: A job posting for a Solution Specialist role at the Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada (FINTRAC) includes standard federal language committing the Public Service to building a 'skilled and diverse workforce' and encouraging applicants to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated groups under the federal government's 'employment equity' program. The posting further states that preference may be given to candidates who are members of one or more of these designated groups and who have voluntarily completed the self-declaration form. The groups referenced include women, indigenous peoples (categorised by ethnicity), persons with disabilities, and members of visible minorities — categories defined by sex, ethnicity, and disability. This language appears across nearly all federal job postings and its ubiquity across the public service is the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: By inviting candidates to declare their sex, ethnicity, or disability status, and then stating that preference may be given to those who do, the hiring process reduces applicants to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. A candidate's worth is assessed not only on what they can do or who they are as a person, but on whether their immutable traits place them in a favoured group. This undermines the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated on their individual merits. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories are placed at a disadvantage regardless of their abilities, while those who do belong are defined primarily by demographic labels rather than their unique qualities. Repeated across thousands of federal postings, this practice normalises institutional sorting of people by demographic proxies and entrenches the assumption that immutable traits are relevant to professional evaluation.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Services and Procurement Canada
incidents.short_description: Public Services and Procurement Canada's posting for a Senior Technical Specialist (Mechanical) invites applicants to self-declare membership in federal employment equity categories, with consideration given to those who do so.
incidents.description_of_event: Public Services and Procurement Canada posted a competition for a Senior Technical Specialist (Maintenance Management Specialist), Mechanical, at the EG-06 level, based in Toronto, Ontario. The posting states that "consideration may be given to candidates, who, at the time of application, self-declare as belonging to one of the following designated Employment Equity groups: Women, Indigenous (Aboriginal) Peoples, Persons with Disabilities and Members of Visible Minorities." Candidates are explicitly encouraged to self-declare during the online application process by selecting the appropriate box under the Employment Equity section. The posting also includes a general statement that the Public Service of Canada "promote[s] employment equity and encourage[s] you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This means hiring decisions for this position are influenced by whether a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability status falls within categories defined by the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal hiring process invites candidates to declare their sex, ethnicity, or disability status, and then factors those declarations into hiring decisions, each applicant is evaluated in part as a representative of a demographic category rather than as an individual whose abilities, character, and qualifications stand on their own. A qualified candidate who does not belong to any of the listed categories will receive less favourable consideration than an equally qualified candidate who does. The posting treats immutable personal traits as relevant hiring criteria, reducing people to demographic labels at the very moment they are seeking to be recognised for their skills and experience. This undermines each candidate's singular worth and replaces individual evaluation with group-based sorting.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada posted a Senior Solution Specialist job that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in federal "employment equity" groups and states that preference may be given to those who do so.
incidents.description_of_event: A job posting for a Senior Solution Specialist at the Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada (FINTRAC) includes two pieces of identity-categorization language. First, under the "Equity, diversity and inclusion" section, the posting states that the Public Service of Canada encourages applicants to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated groups under the federal government's "employment equity" program. Second, the posting states that preference may be given to candidates who are members of one or more of these groups and who have voluntarily completed the self-declaration form. The groups covered by the federal "employment equity" program — women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and visible minorities — map to candidates being assessed partly on the basis of sex, ethnicity, disability, and other demographic characteristics rather than solely on their individual qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer signals that preference will be given to candidates based on sex, ethnicity, disability, or other demographic traits, it reduces applicants to the demographic categories they belong to rather than evaluating them as individuals on the basis of their abilities, character, and experience. Qualified candidates who do not belong to a preferred category are disadvantaged not because of anything they have or have not done, but because of immutable traits. Simultaneously, candidates who do belong to a preferred category carry the weight of that label through the hiring process, with their membership in a demographic group treated as a relevant credential. This practice normalises institutional sorting of people by demographic proxies and undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada
incidents.short_description: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada is hiring a Refrigeration Technician and encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and states that selection may be limited to candidates belonging to those categories.
incidents.description_of_event: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada posted a job for a Refrigeration Technician (GL-MAM-08) in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, closing June 16, 2026. The posting contains two distinct passages that categorize candidates by demographic traits. First, a departmental statement describes the organization as one of "Canada's Best Diversity Employers" and declares a commitment to building a workforce representative of all Canadians, enumerating race, ethnicity, colour, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, and gender identity as the dimensions of that commitment. Second, the posting explicitly encourages women (especially in non-traditional occupations), indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minority groups to apply and self-declare as members of one or more federal "Employment Equity" groups, and warns that "selection may be limited to candidates belonging to one or more of the groups." The Public Service Commission's standard language also appears, inviting candidates to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated employment equity groups. Taken together, the posting sorts applicants by skin colour, sex, ethnicity, and disability, and signals that those traits can determine who is considered for the role.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government employer signals that selection may be limited to candidates based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, it reduces individual applicants to demographic proxies rather than evaluating them on their qualifications, skills, and character. A qualified candidate who does not fall into one of the listed categories is told, in effect, that their traits may disqualify them regardless of their individual abilities. Equally, candidates who do fall into listed categories are assessed partly through the lens of their demographic characteristics rather than solely as individuals with relevant trade qualifications and experience. This practice treats a person's sex, skin colour, or disability as a meaningful criterion in a skilled trade hiring decision, which it is not. It normalises the institutional habit of labelling and sorting people by immutable characteristics, eroding the principle that every person deserves to be evaluated as a distinct individual.

incidents.short_description: The Parliamentary Protective Service posted a job for a Program Advisor, Procedural Development that includes the standard federal encouragement to self-declare membership in designated employment equity groups.
incidents.description_of_event: The Parliamentary Protective Service posted a job competition for a Program Advisor, Procedural Development position (EC-05 to EC-06) based in Ottawa. The posting includes the standard federal statement committing to a "skilled and diverse workforce" and encouraging candidates to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated groups under the federal government's employment equity program. The specific language reads: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This language, present in the vast majority of federal job postings, asks candidates to categorize themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status as part of the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: By asking candidates to self-declare membership in designated groups, the posting reduces individuals to demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, and disability status — rather than evaluating them solely on their abilities, experience, and character. A person's ethnicity, sex, or disability is irrelevant to their capacity to develop procedures and advise senior management, yet the federal hiring process explicitly solicits this information and uses it as a factor in building its workforce. This practice teaches applicants that their demographic characteristics are relevant to whether they will be hired by their government, undermining the principle that each person deserves to be evaluated as a distinct individual. The ubiquity of this language across federal postings compounds the harm: the federal government systematically signals to Canadians that their demographic identities are hiring criteria, normalizing the institutional categorization of individuals by immutable traits rather than their singular worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation posted a Specialist, Software Engineering (SDET) role that encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation, and also offers special alternative work arrangements based on ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) posted a permanent, full-time Specialist, Software Engineering (SDET) position based in Ottawa or Montreal. The posting contains two identity-categorization elements. First, the benefits section singles out candidates based on their ethnicity, stating: "alternative work arrangements may be considered for Indigenous candidates" — a distinct employment benefit offered on the basis of ethnicity. Second, under a heading titled "Our commitment to diversity, equity, and inclusion," the posting states: "We're committed to employment equity and encourage women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, veterans and persons of all races, ethnicities, religions, abilities, sexual orientations, and gender identities and expressions to apply." This is the standard federal employment equity encouragement boilerplate, enumerating candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability, religion, and sexual orientation. Rather than evaluating applicants purely on their qualifications and abilities — of which the posting lists many specific technical and professional requirements — CMHC additionally categorizes candidates by a range of demographic characteristics.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution signals that a person's sex, ethnicity, disability status, religion, or sexual orientation are relevant factors in a hiring process, it reduces applicants to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique skills, experience, and character. Candidates are sorted into categories defined by immutable traits, and the institution's interest in those traits is made explicit. The added offer of a distinct work arrangement benefit based on ethnicity compounds this, treating a person's ancestry as a job-relevant characteristic. This practice undermines the principle that each person deserves to be considered on their own merits, and it sends the message to all applicants — those in the named categories and those not — that demographic identity shapes how the institution sees them. Over time, normalising this categorisation across hundreds of federal postings entrenches the idea that institutions should weigh immutable characteristics when making decisions about individuals, eroding the recognition of singular worth that a fair hiring system requires.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Defence Construction Canada
incidents.short_description: Defence Construction Canada's posting for a Technical Specialist, Energy Management actively encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, rather than evaluating each candidate solely as an individual.
incidents.description_of_event: Defence Construction Canada posted a Technical Specialist, Energy Management position based in Trenton, Ontario. The posting includes a statement actively encouraging applications from candidates based on specific demographic traits. The statement reads: "We actively encourage applications from all, including but not limited to, women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities and members of the LGBTQ2+ community." This language sorts prospective candidates into demographic categories — sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — and signals that membership in one or more of those categories is a relevant factor in the hiring process. The posting also frames this as part of a commitment to a "diverse and inclusive workplace," embedding demographic categorisation into the organisation's stated hiring philosophy.
incidents.expected_harm: When a government employer signals that demographic traits such as sex, ethnicity, disability, or sexual orientation are relevant factors in hiring, it reduces candidates to the demographic groups they belong to rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories receive the message that they are less actively sought, while those who do belong to them receive the message that their demographic profile — not their character, abilities, or demonstrated experience — is part of what makes their application welcome. This framing undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent worth as an individual and that advancement should reflect merit and individual circumstance. Repeating this language across nearly every federal and federal-adjacent job posting normalises the practice of labelling and sorting Canadians by immutable traits as a routine feature of public-sector employment.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Defence Construction Canada
incidents.short_description: Defence Construction Canada encourages applications for a Coordinator, Contract Services role based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating all candidates equally as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Defence Construction Canada posted a job opening for a Coordinator, Contract Services (Procurement Officer) based in Comox, British Columbia, closing 25 June 2026. The posting includes standard encouragement language directing specific categories of candidates to apply: "We actively encourage applications from all, including but not limited to, women, Indigenous Peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities and members of the LGBTQ2+ community." This language sorts candidates into demographic groups — by sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation — and signals that membership in those groups is a relevant consideration in the hiring process. The posting also states a commitment to being "a diverse and inclusive workplace," framing the hiring process around demographic representation rather than individual merit.
incidents.expected_harm: By signalling that applications are actively encouraged based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation, Defence Construction Canada reduces candidates to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as distinct individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and experience. Candidates who fall outside the listed categories receive a weaker institutional signal that their application is equally welcome, creating a two-tiered dynamic based on immutable traits. Candidates within the listed categories are defined first by their demographic membership rather than their unique qualifications and professional achievements. This practice normalises the idea that demographic proxies are legitimate and relevant factors in hiring decisions, undermining the principle that every person deserves evaluation as an individual of singular worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: The Department of National Defence's job posting for an Occupational Health and Safety Analyst states that selection for the role will be based on candidates' skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sex, and asks applicants to self-identify accordingly.
incidents.description_of_event: A Government of Canada job posting for an Occupational Health and Safety Analyst (AS-04) at the Department of National Defence, closing June 17, 2026, includes language stating that selection among qualified candidates will be based on skin colour, ethnicity, disability, and sex — categories the federal government groups under its 'employment equity' program. Under the heading 'Organizational Needs,' the posting states: "selection may be based on qualified candidates self-declaring as belonging to one or more of the designated groups: Indigenous Peoples, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women." The posting also asks candidates to self-identify as belonging to those groups when applying, stating: "Creating an environment where diverse perspectives are valued begins with self-declaration. Count yourself in and self-declare by identifying to which employment equity group(s) you belong." A separate standard statement in the 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' section further encourages candidates to self-declare membership in a federal 'employment equity' designated group. Three permanent positions are being filled in Calgary, Edmonton, and Suffield, Alberta.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal department states that selection among qualified candidates will be based on their skin colour, ethnicity, disability, or sex, it reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and demonstrated needs. A qualified person who does not belong to one of the listed categories faces a lower likelihood of being selected despite possessing equal or superior abilities, solely because of traits they were born with or cannot change. This directly undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual deserving recognition for their unique qualities. It also normalises the practice of labelling job applicants by immutable characteristics, signalling to the public that government hiring turns on demographic identity rather than individual merit. Over time, this erodes the shared civic trust that comes from knowing institutions treat everyone as equal individuals on a common journey.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Defence Construction Canada
incidents.short_description: Defence Construction Canada's posting for a Technical Specialist in Project Management (Electrical) at Valcartier actively encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating candidates purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: Defence Construction Canada posted a job opening for a Technical Specialist, Project Management (Electrical) based at the Valcartier Research Centre in Courcelette, QC, with a closing date of June 23, 2026. The posting includes an encouragement statement that explicitly invites applications from candidates based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation. The specific groups named are women, indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, visible minorities, and members of the LGBTQ2+ community. The posting frames this as part of a commitment to a "diverse and inclusive workplace" and states that applications from these categorically defined groups are "actively encouraged." This standard language, attached to federal and quasi-federal job postings, categorizes candidates by immutable demographic traits as part of the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer singles out candidates for encouragement based on sex, ethnicity, disability, or sexual orientation, it reduces people to demographic labels rather than assessing them as distinct individuals with unique abilities and character. Candidates are effectively evaluated through the lens of group membership, which undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual. This practice signals to all applicants — those in the named categories and those not — that demographic identity is a relevant factor in hiring decisions, compromising the integrity of a merit-based process. Over time, repeated institutional labelling of people by immutable traits normalises the sorting of individuals into demographic categories, eroding the shared civic belonging that comes from institutions treating every person as an individual whose worth stands independent of their background.

incidents.short_description: Parks Canada posted a snow plow operator inventory listing that includes standard federal employment equity language encouraging candidates to self-declare membership in designated groups based on sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: Parks Canada's Highway Operations Unit posted an inventory for Class 1 and Class 3 Snow Plow Operator positions across the Mountain National Parks (Alberta and British Columbia). Under the 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' section of the posting, it states: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This language asks candidates to identify themselves by demographic category — sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation — as part of the application process for a physically demanding, operationally focused highway maintenance role. The self-declaration request is the standard near-universal federal employment equity boilerplate attached to Government of Canada postings.
incidents.expected_harm: Asking candidates to label themselves by demographic category — sex, ethnicity, disability status, and sexual orientation — reduces applicants to group membership rather than evaluating them as individuals on the basis of their abilities, qualifications, and character. For a role assessed on concrete occupational criteria such as a valid driver's licence, driving record, and capacity to operate heavy machinery, there is no legitimate reason for an employer to collect this information at the point of application. Candidates who do not belong to a designated group, or who decline to self-declare, are placed at a relative disadvantage in a system that rewards demographic characteristics rather than individual merit. The systematic collection of this data across nearly every federal posting normalises the institutional habit of sorting people into demographic categories, training both applicants and hiring managers to see individuals first through the lens of group membership rather than as distinct people with unique skills and histories.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada
incidents.short_description: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's posting for a Cropping Operations Labourer encourages applications based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and notes that selection may be limited to candidates from those same categories.
incidents.description_of_event: Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada posted a competition for Cropping Operations Labourer (GL-MAN-06) at the Lethbridge Research and Development Centre in Alberta. The posting contains two separate passages that categorize applicants by identity traits. First, a Public Service of Canada-wide statement in the "Equity, diversity and inclusion" section invites candidates to self-declare membership in what the federal government calls designated employment equity groups. Second, Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada adds its own department-specific statement explicitly encouraging applications from women (especially in non-traditional occupations), indigenous peoples, persons with disabilities, and members of visible minority groups, and then warns that "selection may be limited to candidates belonging to one or more of the groups." That final clause means the department reserves the right to restrict the competition to candidates selected on the basis of sex, ethnicity, and disability rather than evaluating all applicants purely on their qualifications for the role.
incidents.expected_harm: When a hiring process sorts candidates by sex, ethnicity, and disability status — and reserves the right to limit selection to those categories — it reduces every applicant to demographic labels rather than assessing them as individuals with unique abilities, experience, and character. Candidates who do not fall into the preferred categories face the possibility of being screened out regardless of their qualifications. Those who do fall into a preferred category are evaluated partly on an immutable trait rather than solely on what they can contribute. Both outcomes treat people as representatives of a group rather than as distinct individuals. The practice normalises the institutional habit of attaching demographic conditions to employment decisions, eroding the principle that each person deserves to be judged on their own merits and circumstances.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: National Defence posted a Training Officer position in Borden, Ontario, inviting applicants to self-declare membership in identity categories and stating that selection may be based on those categories, including ethnicity, disability, and sex.
incidents.description_of_event: National Defence posted a competition for an AS-02 Training Officer role at CFB Borden, Ontario. The posting contains several passages that categorise candidates by demographic traits rather than evaluating them purely as individuals. First, under 'Important messages,' the posting states: 'Creating an environment where diverse perspectives are valued begins with self-declaration. Count yourself in and self-declare by identifying to which employment equity group(s) you belong.' Second, under the standard Public Service of Canada banner, it states: 'We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply.' Third, under 'Organizational Needs,' the posting states that 'selection may be based on qualified candidates self-declaring as belonging to one or more of the designated groups: Indigenous Peoples, members of visible minorities, persons with disabilities, and women,' and goes on to encourage candidates to indicate if they belong to any of those groups. Together these passages mean that a candidate's ethnicity, skin colour, disability status, and sex are invited as factors in determining who is selected for the role.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal department states that selection decisions for a public service position may be based on a candidate's ethnicity, skin colour, disability, or sex, it reduces individuals to demographic labels at the very moment they should be evaluated on their abilities, experience, and character. Candidates who do not belong to the named categories are passed over not because of any shortcoming on their part, but because of traits they were born with and cannot change. Candidates who do belong to those categories are advanced not necessarily because of their individual merit, but because of those same immutable traits. Both outcomes treat people as members of demographic groups rather than as distinct individuals. This directly undermines the principle that every person deserves to be recognised and assessed for their unique qualities, and it signals to all applicants that the institution views them first through the lens of demographic categories.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Services and Procurement Canada
incidents.short_description: Public Services and Procurement Canada posted a Junior Property and Facilities Officer role that invites applicants to self-declare membership in federal employment equity groups and states that consideration will be given to candidates who do so.
incidents.description_of_event: Public Services and Procurement Canada advertised a Junior Property and Facilities Officer position (AS-01, Ottawa and Gatineau) closing June 2, 2026. The posting includes an "Equity, diversity and inclusion" section stating: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." It goes on to state that "consideration may be given to candidates, who, at the time of application, self-declare* as belonging to one of the following designated Employment Equity groups: Women, Indigenous (Aboriginal) Peoples, Persons with Disabilities and Members of Visible Minorities." Applicants are directed to self-declare during the online application process by selecting the appropriate box under the Employment Equity section. This means candidates are asked to categorise themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability status, and are told that doing so will factor into hiring consideration.
incidents.expected_harm: By inviting applicants to self-declare membership in demographic categories and stating that doing so may improve their chances of receiving consideration, the posting reduces candidates to labels based on sex, ethnicity, and disability rather than evaluating them as individuals on the basis of their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. A candidate who ticks one of these boxes is treated as a representative of a group rather than as a distinct person. This practice embeds demographic sorting into the hiring process, signalling to all applicants that who they are categorised as matters to the outcome, not simply what they can do. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories receive a signal that they are less valued, while those who do belong are reduced to their membership in a group. This undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual and that institutions should evaluate people on their unique qualities rather than on immutable traits.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Food Inspection Agency
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Food Inspection Agency's posting for a Veterinarian – Animal Health position states that first consideration for appointment will be given to candidates who self-declare membership in specified groups under the federal government's 'employment equity' program, categorizing applicants by ethnicity, sex, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: A posting by the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) for a Veterinarian – Animal Health position (VM-01) based in Québec City invites all persons residing in Canada to apply, but includes an organizational needs statement that gives first consideration for appointment to qualified candidates who self-declare as belonging to one of the following groups under the federal government's 'employment equity' program: indigenous peoples, women, members of visible minorities, and persons with disabilities. The posting also reproduces the standard Public Service of Canada statement encouraging candidates to self-declare if they belong to a designated employment equity group. Candidates are directed to complete a Self-Declaration Form as part of their application. The posting thus structures the hiring process around demographic categories — ethnicity, sex, and disability — by formally prioritizing candidates who label themselves accordingly.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal agency gives first consideration for appointment based on a candidate's ethnicity, sex, or disability status, it reduces each applicant to a set of demographic labels rather than evaluating them as an individual on their character, abilities, and demonstrated qualifications. A candidate who does not belong to the specified categories, or who declines to self-declare, is placed at a formal disadvantage regardless of their merit or suitability for the role. This practice treats demographic membership as a qualification in itself, which directly undermines the principle that every person possesses singular worth as an individual. It also pressures candidates to identify themselves by immutable traits in order to compete fairly, compromising their privacy and autonomy. Over thousands of postings, this pattern embeds the routine categorization of Canadians by demographic proxies into the fabric of public employment, normalizing the idea that institutions should evaluate people through the lens of group membership rather than individual worth.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Food Inspection Agency
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Food Inspection Agency posted a veterinarian position in Quebec that gives first consideration to applicants who self-declare membership in federal 'employment equity' groups categorised by ethnicity, sex, disability, and skin colour.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) posted a Veterinarian position (VM-01) for locations in Rivière-du-Loup and Saint-Alexandre-de-Kamouraska, Quebec. Under the heading 'Equity, diversity and inclusion,' the posting states that the Public Service of Canada encourages applicants to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated employment equity groups. More specifically, under 'Organizational Needs,' the CFIA states that first consideration for appointment may be given to qualified candidates who indicate they belong to one of the following groups: indigenous peoples, women, members of visible minorities, and persons with disabilities. Candidates are asked to complete a Self-Declaration Form as part of the application. The posting directs applicants to self-identify by these categories — ethnicity, skin colour, sex, and disability — as a factor influencing their ranking in the selection process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer grants first consideration based on a candidate's ethnicity, skin colour, sex, or disability, it ranks applicants by demographic category rather than by their individual character, abilities, or demonstrated needs. A candidate who declines to be labelled by these traits is placed at a disadvantage relative to one with identical qualifications who agrees to be categorised. This practice reduces people to demographic proxies, treating group membership as a credential. It sends the message that the government evaluates individuals not as singular persons but as representatives of categories — normalising the idea that immutable characteristics are legitimate grounds for institutional preference. Every appointment made through this process carries the implication that the selected person's demographic profile, rather than their individual merit alone, influenced the outcome, which undermines the trust, dignity, and sense of common belonging that a shared public service depends upon.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Public Prosecution Service of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Public Prosecution Service of Canada's posting for a Litigation Legal Assistant position in Winnipeg and Kenora states it will give first consideration to candidates based on their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: A job posting for a Litigation Legal Assistant (AS-01) with the Public Prosecution Service of Canada lists an "Organizational Needs" section that explicitly states screening, assessment, and selection will first consider candidates who self-declare as members of specific groups. The posting names those groups as: black people, indigenous persons, members of the 2SLGBTQIA+ community, persons with disabilities, racialized persons, and women. The posting also includes a separate "Equity, diversity and inclusion" statement welcoming applications "especially" from those same groups, describing them as "equity-deserving." Together, these passages mean the department sorts applicants by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation before evaluating their individual qualifications for the role.
incidents.expected_harm: Applicants are sorted and ranked first by their skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, and sexual orientation before their individual character, abilities, or qualifications are weighed. This reduces each candidate to a set of demographic labels and treats group membership as a hiring credential. A qualified applicant who does not belong to the favoured categories faces a structural disadvantage unrelated to their ability to do the work. The practice tells every applicant that the institution sees their demographic profile before it sees them as a person, undermining the principle that every individual deserves to be evaluated on their own merits.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Department of National Defence
incidents.short_description: The Department of National Defence posted a Team Leader, Avionics Maintenance position that limits selection to candidates based on their ethnicity, disability, and sex, and requests applicants self-declare membership in federal 'employment equity' groups.
incidents.description_of_event: The Department of National Defence advertised a Team Leader, Avionics Maintenance position (EL-07, Ottawa, Ontario) through the Government of Canada's GC Jobs portal. The posting includes two distinct forms of identity-based categorisation. First, it invites applicants to self-declare their membership in the federal government's 'employment equity' groups, framing this as a step that creates an 'inclusive' environment. Second, and more significantly, it states that selection may be limited to members of four specified groups under the federal 'employment equity' program: people of indigenous ethnicity (described in the posting as 'Indigenous (Aboriginal) peoples'), persons with disabilities, visible minorities (people categorised by skin colour or ethnic background), and women. This means the hiring process reserves the right to exclude qualified candidates from consideration based solely on their sex, ethnicity, or disability status.
incidents.expected_harm: When an institution reserves the right to limit hiring to candidates based on sex, ethnicity, or disability, it treats people as representatives of demographic categories rather than as distinct individuals to be evaluated on their skills, experience, and character. A qualified avionics maintenance technician whose sex or ethnicity does not match the listed categories will find their application deprioritised or excluded, regardless of their individual ability or merit. This directly contradicts the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own qualities. The practice also requires applicants to label themselves by demographic category as a condition of full participation in the hiring process, reducing individuals to group membership at the very first point of contact with the institution.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada states that priority will be given to applicants based on sex, ethnicity, and disability when filling a Research Officer role in Mississauga, and asks candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: A National Research Council Canada job posting for a Research Officer – Computational Materials and Process Simulation position at its Mississauga Clean Energy Innovation centre states that priority will be given to applicants based on sex, ethnicity, and disability — the groups the federal government clusters under its 'employment equity' program. The posting names women, indigenous peoples (First Nations, Inuit, and Métis), persons with disabilities, and racialized persons as the preferred categories, and explicitly asks all candidates to self-declare membership in these groups when applying. A footnote acknowledges that the Employment Equity Act, currently under review, uses different terminology for some of these categories. The posting also notes that indigenous candidates may be eligible for exceptions to the full-time on-site work requirement, a consideration not extended to other applicants, meaning the candidate's ethnicity bears on the terms of employment being offered.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates are sorted and prioritised based on their sex, ethnicity, and disability status rather than being evaluated purely as individuals on the strength of their qualifications, research record, and demonstrated abilities. A researcher who does not belong to any of the preferred categories faces a lower probability of selection regardless of their individual merit. At the same time, candidates who do belong to a preferred category are advanced partly because of an immutable trait, not solely because of their unique capabilities. The self-declaration requirement asks every applicant to categorise themselves by demographic label before their skills are considered, reducing individuals to group proxies at the first moment of contact with the institution. The separate work-arrangement provision for candidates of a particular ethnicity compounds this by tying a material benefit to ethnic background rather than individual circumstance. Taken together, these practices treat people as representatives of demographic groups rather than as persons of singular worth, and they signal to all candidates that the institution views them primarily through the lens of identity categories.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Royal Canadian Mounted Police
incidents.short_description: The Royal Canadian Mounted Police posted a job for a Detachment Services Assistant in Yellowknife that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in federal employment equity groups based on sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: A posting by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police for a Detachment Services Assistant position (CR-05) based in Yellowknife, Northwest Territories, includes the standard federal employment equity statement: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This language invites applicants to categorise themselves by sex, ethnicity, and disability — the traits grouped under the federal government's 'employment equity' program — as part of the application process. The posting also references a dedicated Indigenous Career Navigators Program providing support to applicants based on their ethnicity, further embedding ethnicity as a relevant criterion in the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer asks job seekers to identify themselves by sex, ethnicity, or disability as part of the application process, it reduces those individuals to demographic labels rather than evaluating them as distinct people with unique skills, experience, and character. Candidates are sorted into categories based on immutable traits before any assessment of their actual qualifications begins. This practice signals that a person's demographic profile is a relevant factor in hiring, undermining the principle that every applicant deserves to be considered on their individual merits alone. Over time, normalising this kind of self-categorisation entrenches the expectation that belonging to a particular group is a credential, which erodes the foundation of fair and impartial assessment.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > National Research Council Canada
incidents.short_description: The National Research Council Canada posted a Research Officer (LCA Data Specialist) position in Ottawa that gives hiring priority based on sex, ethnicity, and disability, and asks candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories.
incidents.description_of_event: The National Research Council Canada posted a job for a Research Officer, LCA Data Specialist, in its Clean Energy Innovation unit in Ottawa. The posting states that priority may be given to candidates based on sex (women), ethnicity (indigenous peoples — First Nations, Inuit and Métis — and racialized persons), and disability (persons with disabilities), which the NRC labels as "designated employment equity groups" under the federal government's employment equity program. The posting also explicitly asks candidates to self-declare their membership in these categories as part of the application process. The posting includes a footnote acknowledging that the Employment Equity Act uses the terminology "Aboriginal peoples" and "visible minorities," and notes that the Act is currently under review. Additionally, a separate note states that indigenous candidates may be eligible for an exception to the position's full-time on-site work arrangement, creating a further condition of employment that differs based on a candidate's ethnicity.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer announces that hiring priority will be given based on a candidate's sex, ethnicity, or disability, it reduces each applicant to a demographic label rather than evaluating them as an individual on the basis of their character, qualifications, and abilities. Candidates who do not belong to the preferred categories are disadvantaged regardless of their individual merit. Candidates who do belong to those categories are advanced, at least in part, because of an immutable trait rather than their demonstrated expertise. Asking applicants to formally self-declare which demographic categories they belong to reinforces the institutional habit of sorting people into groups, normalising the idea that a person's ethnicity, sex, or disability status is a relevant and legitimate factor in evaluating their professional worth. The separate work-arrangement exception offered to candidates based on their ethnicity compounds this by attaching different terms of employment to a particular demographic group. Taken together, these practices undermine the principle that every person deserves to be seen and assessed as a distinct individual.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Office of the Auditor General of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Office of the Auditor General of Canada posted two Director-level audit positions that encourage applicants to self-declare membership in federal 'employment equity' designated groups, categorising candidates by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation rather than evaluating them purely as individuals.
incidents.description_of_event: The Office of the Auditor General of Canada posted two Director positions (Stream 1: Director, Performance Audit; Stream 2: Director, Financial Audit) at the EX-01 level based in Ottawa, closing 19 June 2026. Under the heading 'Equity, diversity and inclusion,' the posting states: 'The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply.' This is the near-universal federal 'employment equity' boilerplate, and its appearance in this posting is the documented phenomenon. The statement invites applicants to categorise themselves by sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — the four groups the federal government gathers under its 'employment equity' program — as part of a competitive hiring process for senior audit leadership roles.
incidents.expected_harm: Candidates applying for these Director-level roles are invited to label themselves by immutable traits — sex, ethnicity, disability, and sexual orientation — rather than being evaluated solely on the qualifications, experience, and character they bring as individuals. When institutions incorporate demographic self-declaration into hiring, they signal that a person's group membership is relevant to their candidacy. This reduces individuals to proxies for categories, which is contrary to the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity and deserves to be assessed on their own abilities and demonstrated qualities. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories are treated as less relevant to the institution's stated workforce goals, and candidates who do belong to them are assessed, at least in part, as representatives of a demographic rather than as distinct individuals. Over time, the routine attachment of this language to virtually every federal posting normalises the practice of sorting people by immutable characteristics and embeds demographic categorisation as a standard feature of public-service hiring.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Office of the Auditor General of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Office of the Auditor General of Canada posted a Principal – Audit Operations role that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in federal employment equity categories based on sex, ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Office of the Auditor General of Canada published a job posting for two Principal – Audit Operations positions (PX-VFM-00 and PX-FIN-00) at an EX-03 equivalent level, based in Ottawa. The posting includes a standard federal statement under an "Equity, diversity and inclusion" heading that reads: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This language invites applicants to identify themselves by demographic category — specifically through the federal government's "employment equity" program, which groups people based on sex, ethnicity, and disability — as part of the application process.
incidents.expected_harm: Asking candidates to label themselves by demographic traits such as sex, ethnicity, or disability reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them on their character, abilities, and qualifications. When an institution signals that self-declaring a demographic characteristic is a relevant part of an application, it treats immutable traits as meaningful factors in hiring decisions. This undermines each applicant's singular worth as an individual and introduces the expectation that one's group membership — rather than one's demonstrated skills and merit — carries weight in the selection process. The practice normalises sorting people into demographic categories at the very first step of their professional relationship with a public institution, which works against a hiring environment grounded in individual assessment.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Office of the Auditor General of Canada
incidents.short_description: The Office of the Auditor General of Canada posted a Director, IT Audit position that encourages candidates to self-declare membership in federal 'employment equity' groups based on sex, skin colour and ethnicity, and disability.
incidents.description_of_event: The Office of the Auditor General of Canada posted a job competition for a Director, Information Technology (IT) Audit position (reference AUD26J-170510-000010), closing June 19, 2026. Under the heading 'Equity, diversity and inclusion,' the posting states: "The Public Service of Canada is committed to building a skilled and diverse workforce that reflects the population it serves. We promote employment equity and encourage you to self-declare if you belong to one of the designated employment equity groups when you apply." This standard federal government language invites candidates to identify themselves by sex, skin colour or ethnicity, and disability status — traits grouped under the federal government's 'employment equity' program — as part of the hiring process. This same boilerplate appears across the vast majority of federal job postings, and its pervasive use is the documented phenomenon.
incidents.expected_harm: When a hiring institution invites candidates to label themselves by sex, skin colour, ethnicity, or disability, it signals that these immutable traits influence how the institution evaluates people. Rather than assessing each applicant as a distinct individual on the basis of their skills, experience, and character, the process sorts them into demographic categories. Applicants learn that the institution sees them at least partly through the lens of group membership rather than individual merit. This undermines the principle that every person possesses inherent dignity as an individual and deserves to be evaluated on their own qualities. Repeated across virtually every federal posting, this practice normalises the reduction of individuals to demographic proxies on a national scale.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Global Affairs Canada
incidents.short_description: Global Affairs Canada is hiring for various IT positions and explicitly encourages applications based on skin colour, sex, disability, and sexual orientation, while also requesting that candidates self-identify under the federal government's employment equity program.
incidents.description_of_event: Global Affairs Canada posted a competition for various IT positions (IT-02 and IT-03 levels) in Ottawa and Gatineau. The posting includes a 'Work environment' section that encourages applications from people categorised by skin colour ("Black, other racialized"), ethnicity ("Indigenous peoples"), sex ("women"), disability ("persons with disabilities"), and sexual orientation ("persons of the 2SLGBTQI+ community"). The posting further invites candidates to complete the Employment Equity (EE) self-declaration section of their application, framing this as helping to drive organisational change. A separate section headed 'Equity, diversity and inclusion' repeats the Public Service of Canada's standard boilerplate committing to a "skilled and diverse workforce" and encourages candidates to self-declare if they belong to one of the designated employment equity groups. Together, these passages identify candidates by demographic traits and direct them to label themselves accordingly as part of the hiring process.
incidents.expected_harm: When a federal employer sorts candidates into demographic categories and encourages them to self-identify by skin colour, ethnicity, sex, disability, or sexual orientation, it reduces each person to a set of immutable traits rather than evaluating them as a distinct individual with unique abilities, experience, and character. Candidates who do not belong to the listed categories receive an implicit signal that their application is less valued. Those who do belong to a listed category are seen first through a demographic lens rather than as individuals. This practice entrenches the habit of sorting people by inherited characteristics, undermines the principle that every person deserves to be assessed on their own merits, and erodes the sense of shared civic belonging that comes from institutions treating all applicants as equals.

incidents.short_description: The Canada Media Fund introduced the PERSONA-ID system, which collects detailed demographic information — including skin colour, ethnicity, and sexual orientation — and uses it to assess eligibility for certain funding programs. This institutionalises the practice of evaluating applicants based on their demographic identity rather than their individual merit or creative work.
incidents.description_of_event:
The Canada Media Fund (CMF) launched the PERSONA-ID self-identification system, which requires individuals involved in funding applications to register and disclose a range of demographic information. The questionnaire collects data on pronouns, gender identity, sexual orientation, indigenous belonging, racial and ethnocultural identity, language, age, and disability status. The system assigns each registrant a unique PERSONA-ID number that is then attached to funding applications.

Critically, the questionnaire explicitly states that the information provided 'may be used to assess the eligibility of applications for certain programs and incentives at the CMF.' This means that a person's skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, or other demographic traits directly influence whether they or a project they are involved in qualifies for public funding.

The questionnaire introduces a defined category it calls 'Racialized Communities,' which it designates with a star marker and which includes people it identifies as non-white — specifically Black, Latin American, Middle Eastern, South Asian, Southeast Asian, East Asian, and others. The CMF states that being 'racialized' is determined not by ancestry alone but by 'the collective acknowledgement that individuals from these communities are seen as non-white and currently experience specific barriers.' This framing classifies and sorts applicants by skin colour as a condition of eligibility assessment.

The system was made available for information in questionnaire form as of April 2022, with PERSONA-ID numbers shared across applicants and locked to applications during processing.
incidents.expected_harm: Public media funding decisions are made based on applicants' skin colour, ethnicity, sexual orientation, and other demographic characteristics rather than the quality of their creative work or individual circumstances. This institutionalises a system in which a person's immutable traits determine their access to government-supported resources, directly violating the principle that people deserve to be evaluated as individuals. Creators who do not belong to the designated demographic categories face reduced access to funding on the basis of characteristics they cannot change. The system normalises the sorting of Canadians into demographic hierarchies by a federal public institution, entrenching the idea that skin colour and ethnicity are relevant and legitimate criteria for distributing public support. Applicants are encouraged to disclose highly personal information — including their sexual orientation and racial identity — under the implicit pressure that withholding this information may disadvantage their funding prospects.

quotes.text: the information provided may be used to assess the eligibility of applications for certain programs and incentives at the CMF
quotes.rationale: This is the central statement establishing that demographic self-identification is directly tied to funding eligibility, not merely used for statistics.
