quotes.text: The standardized identity categories below marked with a star (*) are considered "Racialized Communities" by the CMF. The CMF recognizes that being racialized is a social construct that cannot solely be determined by a DNA test or ancestry, but rather by the collective acknowledgement that individuals from these communities are seen as non-white and currently experience specific barriers, prejudice and discrimination in Canadian society.
quotes.rationale: This passage defines a formal institutional category based on skin colour and uses it to classify applicants, demonstrating that the system sorts people by perceived race as a basis for differential treatment.

quotes.text: I openly identify as a 2SLGBTQ+ person
quotes.rationale: Sexual orientation is collected as part of the eligibility-linked questionnaire, confirming that this characteristic is among those used to determine program access.

quotes.text: PERSONA-ID is a self-identification system that allows individuals to disclose their demographic information directly and securely to the Canada Media Fund (CMF). For more information, please visit the CMF's PERSONA-ID page.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the nature and purpose of the system — a formal institutional mechanism for collecting demographic data tied to funding applications.

incidents.institution_name: Halton Catholic District School Board (HCDSB) > Holy Cross Catholic Elementary School
incidents.short_description: Staff at Holy Cross Catholic Elementary School took part in a professional development session centred on an identitarian approach to anti-racism. The original tweet documenting the event was subsequently deleted.
incidents.description_of_event: Staff at Holy Cross Catholic Elementary School, part of the Halton Catholic District School Board, attended a professional development session that, according to the submitter, promoted an identitarian framework for addressing racism. The school shared details of the training on its official X (Twitter) account, but the tweet was deleted before it could be fully captured. One image from the tweet was saved prior to deletion, though the text within it is not legible in the available capture. Because the primary source evidence has been removed, the specific content, facilitators, and framing of the training cannot be confirmed from the surviving materials. Based on the submitter's account, the session encouraged staff to view students and their experiences through the lens of demographic categories — particularly skin colour — rather than treating each student as a distinct individual.
incidents.expected_harm: When school staff are trained to categorize students primarily by skin colour or ethnicity, they are less likely to evaluate each child as a unique individual with distinct circumstances, abilities, and needs. This approach embeds demographic labelling into everyday school interactions and decision-making, normalising the idea that a person's immutable characteristics are the most important thing to know about them. Students, in turn, receive the message that institutions see them first as members of a group rather than as individuals worthy of recognition on their own terms. Over time, this undermines the shared civic belonging that schools are well placed to build, replacing it with a framework that divides students and families along lines of perceived group identity.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canadian Institutes of Health Research
incidents.short_description: The Canadian Institutes of Health Research published a call for applicants to its Standing Committee on Institutes, stating that committee composition would preference people based on demographic identity categories including sex, indigenous status, language, disability, and sexual orientation.
incidents.description_of_event: The Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR) published a call for interest for external applicants to join its newly formed Standing Committee on Institutes, with a deadline of May 27, 2022. The committee is an advisory body providing guidance to CIHR's Governing Council on matters related to its research institutes. While the call lists legitimate professional qualifications — such as experience in health research leadership, research strategy, and international collaboration — it also states that committee composition will be structured to ensure "gender equity and representation of Indigenous Peoples and Francophones," and that the committee will, "when possible, include representation of marginalized populations including, but not limited to, applicants who identify as a racialized minority, a person with a disability, and/or a member of the LGBTQ2S+ community." Applicants are required to complete a self-identification questionnaire as part of their application. These criteria direct that demographic identity categories be used as positive selection factors in determining who joins the committee, alongside — and in some cases above — merit-based qualifications.
incidents.expected_harm: Qualified applicants are assessed not solely on their expertise, leadership experience, and ability to contribute to the committee's work, but also on their demographic characteristics. This means that individuals are evaluated partly as members of identity groups rather than as individuals with distinct skills and experiences. The practice entrenches the institutional habit of sorting people by demographic proxies, signalling that a person's immutable characteristics influence their standing in federal advisory processes. It undermines the principle that public institutions should evaluate people based on their character, abilities, and demonstrated expertise, and sets a precedent in federal health research governance that demographic categorization is an appropriate and desirable basis for selecting advisors.

quotes.text: CIHR is committed to the values and principles of equity, diversity and inclusion (EDI) across all aspects of its business. The composition of the committee will reflect CIHR's commitment to ensure gender equity and representation of Indigenous Peoples and Francophones. When possible, the committee will include representation of marginalized populations including, but not limited to, applicants who identify as a racialized minority, a person with a disability, and/or a member of the LGBTQ2S+ community.
quotes.rationale: This is the core passage establishing that committee membership will be partly determined by demographic identity categories, including sex, indigenous status, language, disability, and sexual orientation/gender identity. It is the primary basis for classifying this as a hiring preference incident.

quotes.text: a completed self-identification questionnaire. (All applicants will be asked to complete the self-identification questionnaire upon submission of their application.)
quotes.rationale: Requiring all applicants to complete a self-identification questionnaire as a compulsory part of the application process confirms that demographic identity information is formally collected and used in the selection process.

incidents.short_description: The Government of Ontario commissioned and released a report that institutionalized the practice of categorizing and dividing Canadians based on skin colour and ethnicity, framing systemic problems through demographic accounting rather than addressing discrimination as violations of individual dignity.
incidents.description_of_event: In June 1992, following riots in Toronto, Premier Bob Rae appointed Stephen Lewis as Advisor on Race Relations to conduct consultations and provide recommendations. Lewis conducted over seventy meetings across Ontario with various groups and individuals over one month. The resulting report, released June 9, 1992, framed systemic problems in Ontario institutions through demographic categories and race-based accounting rather than treating discrimination as violations of individual worth. The report explicitly stated that "what we are dealing with, at root, and fundamentally, is anti-Black racism" and proceeded to document disparities by categorizing people based on skin colour and ethnicity across multiple sectors. In education, the report documented streaming, discipline rates, and curriculum representation through racial categories. In employment, it measured representation of "visible minorities" in senior management positions and advocated for employment equity legislation based on demographic targets. In criminal justice, it described correctional facilities as having "disproportionate numbers of minority youth" and framed police relations through racial group dynamics rather than individual rights. The report's recommendations institutionalized race-based thinking by calling for enhanced race relations training, demographic monitoring and audits of institutions, race-specific complaint processes, and the creation of government structures focused on managing relations between racial groups. Rather than advocating for institutions to evaluate people as individuals based on character and demonstrated needs, the report's framework entrenched the practice of sorting Canadians into demographic categories and measuring institutional performance through representation statistics. The report recommends significant policy changes including employment equity legislation, enhanced anti-racism bureaucracy, and systematic collection of race-based data across Ontario institutions.
incidents.expected_harm: Individuals are evaluated and categorized by government and public institutions based on skin colour and ethnicity rather than their unique qualities, abilities, and circumstances. The report's framework normalizes the practice of demographic accounting, teaching institutions to view people primarily through the lens of group identity rather than individual worth. Canadians learn that government sees them as representatives of racial categories rather than as distinct persons deserving recognition for their character and merit. The emphasis on group-based disparities and representation targets undermines the principle that discrimination is wrong because it violates individual dignity, instead framing injustice as a matter of statistical imbalances between demographic groups. By institutionalizing race-based thinking in policy, training, and institutional monitoring, the report entrenches division among Canadians rather than building understanding around shared human dignity and common citizenship. Individuals raising legitimate concerns about unfair treatment are themselves categorized by demographic characteristics, and their grievances are processed through the lens of group identity rather than heard as complaints from unique persons. The framework prevents institutions from developing approaches that recognize singular worth and evaluate each person based on individual circumstances, instead mandating systems that sort people into categories and measure success through demographic representation. This approach forecloses the possibility of building a society where institutions recognize common humanity and where advancement reflects individual merit rather than group membership.

quotes.text: In any event, as I understand it, there are roughly 2,000 such positions in the OPS, and visible minority persons are largely excluded. In fact, in a very recent internal survey of employment equity in the OPS, the following categorical quotes appear. All Designated Groups are under-represented in Senior Management ... Racial Minorities experience a glass ceiling at Senior Management 1 ... Racial minority men and women experience occupational segregation which limits their upward mobility
quotes.rationale: Illustrates the report's approach of measuring institutional performance through demographic representation rather than evaluating whether individuals are assessed based on their abilities and qualifications

quotes.text: The Employment Equity legislation should be introduced for first reading before the end of June, and if the session is for some reason prolonged, second reading should proceed. Whatever the time-table for early readings and committee consideration, the Bill should be passed by December 31,1992, to take effect as early as possible in 1993.
quotes.rationale: Shows the report's recommendation to institutionalize demographic-based policies rather than merit-based evaluation systems

quotes.text: By November 1,1992, legislation be introduced to convert the Ontario Anti-Racism Secretariat into an enhanced Ontario Anti-Racism Directorate reporting directly to the Minister of Citizenship. The months between now and then should be used in the elaboration of policy, in the hiring of staff to fill current vacancies, and in the early collaboration with community groups for whom the Secretariat will be a working-partner.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates the institutionalization of race-based bureaucracy that would manage and categorize people by demographic characteristics rather than treat them as individuals

incidents.short_description: Public Library InterLINK offered a racially segregated professional coaching sessions  to library staff.
incidents.description_of_event: Public Library InterLINK, a collaborative organization supporting BC public libraries, created a professional coaching program that restricted eligibility based on participants' race or ethnicity. The organization announced it was seeking 10 library staff members who identify as indigenous, black, or people of colour to receive 8 free, one-hour confidential coaching sessions. Applications were accepted through October 13, 2023. This program operated alongside the organization's regular coaching courses, which were available to all BC public library staff. The restricted program explicitly limited participation based on demographic categories rather than evaluating individuals based on their professional development needs, career circumstances, or demonstrated interest in coaching.
incidents.expected_harm: This eligibility restriction treats library staff as members of demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique professional development needs. Staff members are excluded from or granted access to professional development opportunities based on their race or ethnicity rather than their individual circumstances, career goals, or professional challenges. This approach undermines the principle of evaluating people based on their individual qualities and needs, instead reducing them to demographic proxies. The restriction creates a system where professional advancement opportunities are allocated based on immutable characteristics rather than individual merit, need, or potential. It establishes separate and unequal access to institutional resources, dividing colleagues along racial and ethnic lines rather than fostering a workplace culture where all individuals are supported based on their unique professional development requirements.

quotes.text: In addition to it's usual coaching corses, Public Library InterLINK is seeking 10 IBPOC library staff from member libraries who are interested in receiving 8 free, 1-hour, confidential coaching sessions.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the core discriminatory practice: the organization created a separate coaching program with explicit racial and ethnic eligibility restrictions, limiting access to professional development based on demographic categories rather than individual needs or merit.

quotes.text: InterLINK invites library workers who are Indigenous, Black or People of Colour (IBPOC) to participate in a professional coaching program.
quotes.rationale: This quote from the application form confirms the explicit racial and ethnic restrictions on program eligibility, demonstrating how the institution categorized and separated individuals based on immutable characteristics rather than individual professional development needs.

incidents.short_description: The University of British Columbia's Department of Sociology required job applicants for an Assistant Professor of Teaching position to submit a mandatory statement on contributions to equity, diversity, and inclusion. The posting encouraged applications from people based on demographic categories enumerated under the B.C. Human Rights Code.
incidents.description_of_event: On March 25, 2026, the University of British Columbia's Department of Sociology advertised a tenure-track Assistant Professor of Teaching position. The job posting required all applicants to submit a separate one-page statement identifying their potential contributions to equity, diversity, and inclusion in the university context, along with their ability to work with a culturally diverse and international student body. This statement was mandatory as part of the application dossier. The posting explicitly stated that the university encourages applications from members of groups that have been marginalized on grounds enumerated under the B.C. Human Rights Code, including sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, skin colour, disability, political belief, religion, marital or family status, age, and status as First Nation, Métis, Inuit, or indigenous person. While the posting indicated that all qualified persons are encouraged to apply, it gave explicit priority to Canadian citizens and permanent residents of Canada.
incidents.expected_harm: The mandatory equity, diversity, and inclusion statement requirement compels applicants to demonstrate alignment with particular institutional frameworks rather than allowing evaluation based on individual teaching capabilities and demonstrated ability to treat students fairly as individuals. This requirement reduces candidates to their stated positions on institutional diversity concepts rather than assessing their unique qualifications, pedagogical approaches, and capacity to create genuinely inclusive learning environments through individualized engagement with students. Qualified candidates who hold different but legitimate views on how to achieve fair treatment of students as individuals will be disadvantaged or excluded from consideration, narrowing the range of perspectives within the faculty. The explicit encouragement of applications based on demographic categories listed under human rights legislation signals that group membership will influence hiring decisions, potentially advantaging or disadvantaging candidates based on immutable characteristics rather than individual merit, teaching excellence, and scholarly contributions.

quotes.text: We also require a separate one-page statement identifying the applicant's potential contributions to equity, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) in the university context along with their ability to work with a culturally diverse and international student body.
quotes.rationale: This quote establishes the mandatory EDI statement requirement that compels applicants to demonstrate alignment with institutional frameworks rather than being evaluated solely on individual teaching merit and ability to treat students as individuals.

quotes.text: We encourage applications from members of groups that have been marginalized on any grounds enumerated under the B.C. Human Rights Code, including sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, racialization, disability, political belief, religion, marital or family status, age, and/or status as a First Nation, Métis, Inuit, or Indigenous person.
quotes.rationale: This quote demonstrates institutional encouragement of applications based on demographic categories and immutable characteristics, signalling that group membership may influence hiring decisions rather than focusing exclusively on individual qualifications and demonstrated teaching ability.

incidents.short_description: Home Depot displayed training materials in a Calgary staff room that categorized employees by immutable characteristics including skin colour, religion, and sexual orientation, instructing them to acknowledge corresponding 'privilege.'
incidents.description_of_event: Training materials titled 'Leading Practices: Unpacking Privilege' were displayed in a Calgary Home Depot staff room. The materials divided employees into categories based on skin colour, sex, religion, sexual orientation, physical ability, and socioeconomic background. The policy defined various forms of privilege including labeling confidence in police protection as an indicator of privilege based on skin colour, and expecting time off for religious holidays as Christian privilege. The materials included a checklist for employees to assess their privilege across seven demographic categories. The training defined racism as racial prejudice plus power, and stated that the materials aimed to encourage staff to discuss their privilege with each other. The policy acknowledged that discussing these categories creates discomfort, particularly when individuals are not accustomed to being defined by demographic characteristics.
incidents.expected_harm: Employees are categorized and evaluated based on immutable characteristics rather than their individual qualities, abilities, and character. This approach reduces individuals to demographic proxies and assumes shared experiences, perspectives, and advantages based solely on group membership. Staff members are directed to view themselves and colleagues through the lens of demographic categories, potentially undermining workplace relationships built on individual merit and mutual respect. The training framework establishes a hierarchy of moral standing based on demographic characteristics, where some employees are positioned as inherently privileged and others as inherently disadvantaged, regardless of their individual circumstances, struggles, or achievements. This categorization method discourages genuine understanding between individuals by prioritizing assumed group identities over actual personal experiences and viewpoints.

quotes.text: Societal privileges that benefit white people beyond what is commonly experienced by people of colour under the same social, political and economic circumstances.
quotes.rationale: Definition provided in training materials that explicitly categorizes employees by skin colour and assigns differential treatment based on this immutable characteristic

quotes.text: The policy includes a checkmark list for checking your privilege: white, male, class, Christian, cisgender, able-bodied, and heterosexual.
quotes.rationale: Describes the checklist mechanism requiring employees to self-identify across seven demographic categories

incidents.short_description: The Narwhal, a non-profit journalism organization, posted a job opening for an audience fellow position that accepted applications only from candidates based on their race or ethnicity.
incidents.description_of_event: The Narwhal published a job posting for a one-year audience fellow position with a $50,000 salary. The posting explicitly stated that the organization was accepting applications only from candidates who are Black, Indigenous and People of Colour. The role focused on understanding, expanding and engaging with audiences on social platforms to strengthen the impact of The Narwhal's journalism. The posting indicated that The Narwhal frequently reports on stories impacting Indigenous and racialized communities, and stated these stories weren't reaching people who have been underrepresented in media coverage. The position was described as being at the intersection of journalism and community building, designed to help attract new audiences and deepen engagement. The application deadline was March 29, 2022.
incidents.expected_harm: The hiring restriction excludes qualified candidates from consideration based solely on their race or ethnicity rather than their individual merit, skills, and experience. This practice violates the principle of evaluating people as individuals based on their unique capabilities and demonstrated qualifications. Candidates who do not fall within the specified demographic categories are denied the opportunity to compete for the position regardless of their abilities, experience with the communities The Narwhal covers, understanding of the issues, or capacity to fulfill the role's requirements. This approach reduces individuals to demographic categories and undermines the goal of building a workforce based on individual merit and contribution.

quotes.text: The Narwhal is accepting applications from candidates who are Black, Indigenous and People of Colour for a role focused on understanding, expanding and engaging with audiences on social platforms to help strengthen the impact of The Narwhal's journalism
quotes.rationale: This statement explicitly restricts applications to candidates based on their race or ethnicity, excluding others from applying regardless of their qualifications or individual merit.

incidents.institution_name: Government of Canada > Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation
incidents.short_description: The federal government committed $10 million to a homeownership program exclusively for families where at least one member identifies based on skin colour, restricting eligibility by immutable trait rather than evaluating individual circumstances or demonstrated need.
incidents.description_of_event: In February 2022, Minister of Housing, Diversity and Inclusion Ahmed Hussen announced $10 million in federal funding for the BlackNorth Homeownership Bridge Program through the Shared Equity Mortgage Provider fund. The program provides financial assistance to help 200 families in the Greater Toronto Area purchase their first home by 2027. Eligibility requires that applicants or household members self-identify based on skin colour. Additional requirements include having at least one child under 16, household income between $65,000 and $90,000, full-time employment, and completion of 500 volunteer hours. The program uses a two-mortgage model where families receive a conventional first mortgage and a silent second mortgage from the program, requiring no down payment. Minister Hussen framed the initiative as addressing systemic barriers and lower homeownership rates among certain populations. The BlackNorth Initiative partnered with Habitat for Humanity GTA and Dream Legacy Foundation to administer the program, describing it as both a symbolic and practical approach to mobilize society in working together to eliminate systemic racism.
incidents.expected_harm: This program reduces people to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique circumstances, abilities, and needs. By making skin colour a prerequisite for accessing housing assistance, the government treats immutable traits as a proxy for merit or need, undermining the principle that institutions should assess people based on their individual character, demonstrated financial situation, and specific housing challenges. Families with identical income levels, employment histories, and housing needs will receive different treatment based solely on how they categorize themselves by skin colour. This approach reinforces the practice of dividing Canadians into groups based on immutable characteristics, encouraging people to view themselves and others primarily through demographic labels rather than as distinct individuals. The program normalizes institutional decision-making that privileges demographic classification over individual assessment, setting a precedent that government resources should be allocated based on group membership rather than individual circumstances. It denies opportunity to families who may face identical economic barriers to homeownership but who fall outside the designated demographic category.

quotes.text: The Canadian government is committing $10 million in federal funding to help 200 Black families in the Greater Toronto Area purchase their first home.
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core fact: federal government funding restricted to families categorized by skin colour rather than individual need or circumstances

incidents.short_description: Western University restricted a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair position to applicants who self-identify as having a disability, excluding all other candidates regardless of qualifications.
incidents.description_of_event: Western University posted a job advertisement for a Tier 1 Canada Research Chair position that was restricted exclusively to scholars who self-identify as having a disability. The posting stated that pursuant to Section 14 of the Ontario Human Rights Code, only applicants who self-identify as a person with a disability would be considered for this position. The restriction applied to a prestigious, tenured academic appointment at the rank of Associate or Full Professor across any of Western's eleven faculties, with an anticipated start date of July 1, 2022. The position required world-class researchers with international recognition, superior records of research achievement, and demonstrated excellence in scholarly activities. Western justified this hiring restriction by citing its commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion, and the underrepresentation of scholars with disabilities among Canada Research Chair holders. Candidates were required to complete a specific application form designating the position as restricted to persons with disabilities.
incidents.expected_harm: This hiring restriction excludes qualified scholars from consideration for a prestigious academic position based solely on whether they possess a particular immutable characteristic, rather than evaluating them as individuals based on their research excellence, leadership, and contributions to their field. Scholars without disabilities who may be the most qualified candidates are denied the opportunity to compete for the position regardless of their merit, achievements, or potential contributions. This approach reduces applicants to demographic categories rather than assessing their unique qualifications, research programs, and individual capabilities. The practice undermines the principle that institutions should evaluate people based on their demonstrated abilities and individual qualities. It also creates a system where career opportunities are allocated based on group membership rather than individual merit, potentially affecting hiring practices across Canadian academia and normalizing the use of immutable characteristics as employment criteria.

quotes.text: To address Western's commitment to equity, diversity and inclusion, and in recognition of the underrepresentation of Scholars with a Disability in our chairholders and pursuant to Section 14 of the Ontario Human Rights Code, only applicants who self-identify as a person with a disability will be considered for this CRC opportunity.
quotes.rationale: Explicitly states the hiring restriction, confirming that applicants without disabilities are categorically excluded from consideration regardless of qualifications.

incidents.short_description: The Black Screen Office published comprehensive directives for Canadian film and television that require content creators, broadcasters, and funders to evaluate projects based on creators' race and sex. The framework establishes that certain stories should only be told by people from specific demographic categories and creates hiring and funding preferences based on these immutable characteristics.
incidents.description_of_event: In 2022, the Black Screen Office released a suite of reports titled 'Being Seen: Directives for Creating Authentic and Inclusive Content' following consultations with over 400 participants. The reports provide binding guidance to Canadian film, television, and digital media stakeholders on commissioning and creating content. The directives establish that content authenticity depends substantially on whether creators belong to the same demographic categories as the characters or communities portrayed. The framework instructs broadcasters, funders, and distributors to assess whether creators are 'the best person to write and produce the story' based on their 'lived experience' tied to identity categories. It directs industry gatekeepers to 'ask if the creator has hired a writer/story editor/consultant' with 'the right identity for the story' and to 'ask who is profiting from the story' when someone outside a demographic community tells that community's story. The directives establish detailed protocols for when people may tell stories about communities they do not belong to, requiring consultation, hiring from within communities, and in some cases suggesting certain stories should not be told by outsiders at all. Casting directives require authentic matching between performers' identities and character identities. The framework repeatedly emphasizes hiring based on demographic categories to address historical underrepresentation and explicitly calls for preferential treatment in funding, training, and employment until demographic parity is achieved. Throughout the reports, people are consistently described and sorted by categories like skin colour, sexual orientation, and disability status rather than as distinct individuals.
incidents.expected_harm: The directives will result in Canadian content creators being evaluated primarily through the lens of their demographic categories rather than their individual talents, perspectives, and capabilities. Writers, directors, and producers will face barriers to creative opportunities based on whether their immutable characteristics match those of characters or communities in their proposed projects. The framework creates a system where career advancement and funding access depend significantly on demographic classification rather than individual merit and creative vision. By establishing that story authenticity requires demographic matching between creator and subject, the directives reduce storytelling to identity categories and discourage the development of empathy and understanding across different experiences. The emphasis on hiring and funding based on demographic targets rather than individual evaluation will lead institutions to treat people as representatives of categories rather than as unique individuals with varied perspectives. The protocols create an environment where creators must navigate complex rules about which stories they are permitted to tell based on their backgrounds, chilling creative expression and limiting the breadth of Canadian storytelling. By requiring consultation and approval processes based on demographic membership, the framework establishes a system of creative oversight tied to identity rather than craft. The reports' consistent framing of people through demographic labels rather than as individuals with complex, multifaceted identities will reinforce patterns of categorizing Canadians by immutable traits in an industry context with significant cultural influence.

quotes.text: If the creator has hired a writer/story editor/consultant, ask if that person has the right identity for the story (e.g., Black Nova Scotian for a Black Nova Scotian story)?
quotes.rationale: Explicitly requires demographic matching between creator identity and story subject as criterion for approval

incidents.short_description: An Ontario teachers' union local implemented a voting system that weights votes based on members' self-identified demographic categories, ensuring certain groups always hold 50% of voting power regardless of attendance. Members who questioned the policy faced harassment complaints and workplace discipline.
incidents.description_of_event: In June 2021, the Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation Teachers' Bargaining Unit in Halton region passed a motion with 68% delegate support to implement weighted voting at board of local presidents meetings. The policy requires that members who self-identify as indigenous, black or from other designated groups must collectively represent 50% of votes on all decisions. When fewer than half of voting members present self-identify with these groups, their individual votes are mathematically weighted upward to achieve 50% collective voting power. Under this system, if 4 members from designated groups and 16 non-identifying members attend a meeting, each designated group member's vote counts as 2.5 votes while each non-identifying member's vote counts as 0.625 votes. The policy applies to the board of local presidents who make decisions on behalf of approximately 1,400 teachers and staff. Union materials justify the system by stating that treating all members the same does not constitute equal opportunity, and that amplifying voices of certain demographic groups is necessary to achieve equal outcomes. The system took effect at the start of the 2021-2022 school year. At a November 2021 meeting, some members described the policy as constituting differential treatment based on demographic categories. Union president Cindy Gage subsequently issued communications declaring such characterizations to be inherently harmful language that violates anti-harassment policies. School administrators were involved in enforcing workplace harassment protocols against teachers who questioned the policy. The union adopted a position that intent does not matter, only impact, meaning members who make statements interpreted as harmful are considered to have violated policies regardless of their purpose in speaking.
incidents.expected_harm: The weighted voting system evaluates union members based on their self-identified demographic categories rather than as individuals with unique perspectives and concerns. Teachers' influence in union governance now depends on whether their school representative belongs to designated demographic groups, meaning members with identical professional interests receive unequal political power based on categorical membership rather than the substance of their positions or individual merit of their arguments. This system replaces evaluation of ideas on their merits with a mechanism that assigns voting power based on speakers' demographic characteristics. The policy will suppress open discourse by making demographic identity rather than strength of reasoning the determining factor in decision outcomes. When members raised concerns about differential treatment, they faced institutional punishment through harassment complaints and workplace discipline. The union's framework declaring that intent is irrelevant and that certain characterizations of policies are inherently harmful regardless of accuracy creates conditions where members cannot engage in honest evaluation of union decisions without risking professional consequences. This discourages truthful expression and evidence-based discussion of institutional practices. The system institutionalizes categorical division by sorting members into groups with different political rights based on demographic self-identification. It treats these groups as monolithic voting blocs with uniform interests rather than recognizing that individuals within any demographic category hold diverse perspectives on professional and policy matters. By presuming that certain viewpoints deserve amplified power based on speakers' demographic characteristics, the system undermines reasoned debate where arguments are assessed on their logical coherence and evidentiary support. The chilling effect on dissent, combined with weaponization of harassment policies against members who question institutional practices, will erode trust among colleagues and replace collaborative problem-solving with fear of speaking candidly about union governance.

quotes.text: The new system, aimed at improving the representation of Indigenous, Black and racialized teachers in the union's decision-making process, will ensure that they always represent 50 per cent of the votes. It means that if 15 people voting have not self-identified as racialized, and five have, both groups will be weighted to each represent 50 per cent of the total vote.
quotes.rationale: Describes the core mechanism of the weighted voting system showing how votes are mathematically adjusted based on demographic self-identification rather than one-person-one-vote principle

incidents.institution_name: Government of Ontario > Cabinet Office > Strategy and Innovation Branch
incidents.short_description: Ontario's Policy Innovation Hub issued a 17-page document directing provincial bureaucrats to avoid using common phrases like "low-hanging fruit," "hold down the fort," and "talent pipeline" based on tenuous connections to racial harm or Indigenous oppression.
incidents.description_of_event: Ontario's Policy Innovation Hub, described as an in-house consultancy partnering with ministries to deliver on government priorities, produced a 17-page document titled "rethinking language" that instructed Ontario Public Service employees to avoid numerous common phrases. The document discouraged use of "low-hanging fruit" because the term "can be problematic for those who witnessed lynchings," despite acknowledging "there is no direct connection." It directed staff to stop using "talent pipeline" because "to many Indigenous communities, this term represents oppression" related to oil and gas pipelines built on Indigenous land. Other banned phrases included "hold down the fort" and "cakewalk" due to alleged racist origins. This document emerged from the same organization that previously released a report stating as fact that the Ontario Public Service is a "White supremacist organization." The language restrictions were developed during the COVID-19 pandemic when the civil service faced urgent operational demands.
incidents.expected_harm: This policy will undermine reasoned discourse within Ontario's civil service by substituting invented rationales for genuine analysis. When institutions ban common phrases based on tenuous connections that the policy itself acknowledges lack direct linkage to harm, they train employees to accept unreasoned assertions rather than evaluate claims on evidence. Civil servants required to self-censor everyday language learn to prioritize demographic sensitivities over clarity and truth-seeking. The approach reduces communication to navigating arbitrary restrictions rather than conveying meaning effectively. By treating adults as incapable of distinguishing between a phrase describing an easy task and historical atrocities, the policy infantilizes employees and discourages the intellectual honesty necessary for effective governance. The standards established here will spread beyond initial phrases to progressively restrict more language, creating an environment where fear of giving offence stifles the open dialogue essential to sound policy development. When civil servants must constantly monitor speech for imagined connections to harm, they cannot engage fully with complex policy challenges or have the frank discussions required to serve the public interest.

incidents.short_description: The Canadian Avalanche Association, in collaboration with the Association of Canadian Mountain Guides and Canadian Ski Guides Association, commissioned and published a 2019 study examining diversity, inclusion, and mental health in the avalanche and guiding industry. The study approach and findings emphasize demographic categorization and group-based analysis rather than individual merit and circumstances.
incidents.description_of_event: In 2019, the Canadian Avalanche Association (CAA), Association of Canadian Mountain Guides (ACMG), and Canadian Ski Guides Association (CSGA) jointly commissioned Lotus Mountain Consulting Inc. to conduct a study examining diversity, inclusion, and mental health in Canada's avalanche and guiding profession. The resulting report presents data and recommendations that focus extensively on demographic categories (gender, race, ethnicity, sexual orientation) as primary analytical lenses. The study frames workplace challenges through the lens of group identity rather than individual circumstances, suggesting that certain demographic groups face systemic barriers and calling for demographic-based interventions. The report's methodology surveys members about their experiences, categorizes them by demographic characteristics, and draws conclusions about treatment differences based on these categories. The recommendations include implementing training on concepts such as privilege, intersectionality, and unconscious bias, and establishing accountability mechanisms tied to diversity metrics. The study emphasizes increasing minority representation in leadership positions as a goal, suggesting evaluation criteria beyond individual merit and demonstrated capability.
incidents.expected_harm: This approach will likely reduce guides and avalanche workers to demographic categories rather than evaluating them as individuals with unique qualities, experiences, and capabilities. By framing professional challenges primarily through demographic lenses, the study may obscure individual circumstances that affect worker wellbeing and professional development. The recommended training on concepts like privilege and intersectionality may promote viewing colleagues through demographic categories rather than as individuals. Accountability mechanisms tied to diversity metrics may create pressure to make decisions based on demographic factors rather than individual merit, skills, and professional qualifications. Workers may feel reduced to representatives of demographic groups rather than recognized for their individual contributions. The emphasis on increasing minority representation in leadership could lead to assumptions about individuals based on their demographic characteristics rather than assessment of their unique leadership qualities and professional achievements. Mental health challenges may be incorrectly attributed to demographic factors rather than individual circumstances, limiting effective support. The framework fosters division by emphasizing demographic differences rather than the common humanity shared across the profession.

quotes.text: Guides and avalanche workers indicated a desire for increased minority representation; increased involvement from associations to provide support, education and awareness, and accountability for gender discrimination and sexual harassment issues.
quotes.rationale: Shows recommendations focused on demographic representation rather than individual merit-based advancement.

quotes.text: More active mentorship and leadership from amongst minorities.
quotes.rationale: Recommendation suggests selecting leaders based on demographic minority status rather than individual leadership qualities and demonstrated capabilities.

incidents.institution_name: Association of Management, Administrative and Professional Crown Employees (AMAPCEO)
incidents.short_description: AMAPCEO voted to grant two additional mental health days exclusively to employees identifying as part of a specific racial demographic, beyond the three compassionate leave days available to all members.
incidents.description_of_event: The Association of Management, Administrative and Professional Crown Employees (AMAPCEO), representing 14,000 Ontario Public Service professionals, held its annual conference where members voted on a motion to provide two extra days of mental health leave specifically for employees of one racial demographic to address impacts of perceived discrimination. The chairperson of AMAPCEO's racial caucus argued that members had experienced burnout and trauma from volunteering to combat discrimination within the Ontario Public Service. During debate on a related motion addressing career advancement and pay equity programs, several attendees suggested extending equity initiatives to all minority groups rather than singling out one demographic. One member expressed concern that focusing on a single group limits the ability of all groups to move forward. Another noted that multiple communities face marginalization. When an amendment was proposed to broaden the motion to all equity-seeking groups, the caucus chairperson became agitated and insisted on maintaining the demographic-specific focus, stating that removing the racial designation would undermine the caucus itself. The chairperson characterized the group as "equity-denied" rather than "equity-seeking" and claimed disproportionate negative treatment within the Ontario Public Service. The amendment to include other groups failed with 140 members voting against it. The original motion passed overwhelmingly without debate and with no indication of vote tally. Union leadership committed to prioritizing the two extra days of leave in collective bargaining negotiations.
incidents.expected_harm: This policy reduces individuals to demographic categories rather than evaluating them based on their unique circumstances, lived experiences, and individual needs. Employees facing mental health challenges, burnout, or trauma from workplace issues will receive different levels of institutional support depending on their racial classification rather than their actual circumstances or demonstrated needs. The policy creates a two-tier system where employees with identical mental health needs receive unequal treatment based solely on demographic categorization. Employees from other backgrounds who experience discrimination, marginalization, workplace trauma, or burnout will be denied the same institutional recognition and support, regardless of their individual situations. This approach undermines the principle of singular worth by presuming that demographic membership, rather than individual character, abilities, and circumstances, should determine access to workplace benefits. The policy assumes homogeneous experiences within demographic groups while ignoring the diverse range of individual experiences, challenges, and needs that exist across all employees. By rejecting proposals to extend support based on individual circumstances or demonstrated need, the union has prioritized demographic categorization over meaningful assessment of each person's unique situation, potentially leaving many employees without adequate support while failing to address the actual causes of workplace stress and discrimination that affect individuals across all backgrounds.

quotes.text: An Ontario union has voted overwhelmingly and without debate to allow black employees two extra days of mental health leave to deal with the impact of "anti-black racism."
quotes.rationale: Establishes the core policy decision: differential leave allocation based on racial demographic category rather than individual circumstances or needs.

incidents.short_description: University of Waterloo awards $392,000 in research funding exclusively to four indigenous scholars to integrate traditional knowledge with western science. The program restricts eligibility based on ethnicity rather than evaluating researchers on individual merit.
incidents.description_of_event: The University of Waterloo announced SSHRC awards totaling $392,000 to four students identified as indigenous. The funding supports research combining Traditional Knowledge with western scientific approaches across ecology, climate change, repatriation, and health. The SSHRC Indigenous Scholars Awards program explicitly restricts eligibility to candidates based on their status as First Nations, Inuit, or Métis persons, rather than evaluating all researchers based on their individual qualifications, research merit, or demonstrated need. The university frames this as part of ongoing reconciliation initiatives and decolonization efforts.
incidents.expected_harm: By restricting research funding eligibility based on ethnicity rather than individual merit, the program may exclude qualified researchers who could contribute meaningfully to these fields of study. This approach evaluates candidates through demographic categorization rather than assessing each person's unique abilities, research potential, and individual circumstances. Such eligibility restrictions may normalize institutional practices that prioritize group membership over individual achievement, potentially undermining principles of equal opportunity in academic advancement and research funding allocation.

quotes.text: The SSHRC Canada Graduate Research Scholarship and the SSHRC Indigenous Scholars Awards support exceptional student researchers and are intended to increase participation in academic research and promote work that is informed by the successful candidate's experience as a First Nations, Inuit and/or Métis person.
quotes.rationale: Demonstrates that the awards explicitly restrict eligibility based on ethnic or cultural identity membership rather than evaluating all researchers on individual merit, abilities, or demonstrated need.
